### [1] [PhysX-Omni: Unified Simulation-Ready Physical 3D Generation for Rigid, Deformable, and Articulated Objects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21572)
*Ziang Cao,Yinghao Liu,Haitian Li,Runmao Yao,Fangzhou Hong,Zhaoxi Chen,Liang Pan,Ziwei Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A unified framework, PhysX-Omni, enables simulation-ready physical 3D generation across diverse asset types, with a high-fidelity geometry representation for Vision-Language Models, a general dataset (PhysXVerse), and a comprehensive evaluation suite (PhysX-Bench). It achieves strong generation and understanding, and supports simulation-ready scene generation and robotic policy learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3D generation methods either ignore physical properties or are restricted to a single asset category. A universal, simulation-ready pipeline and evaluation suite is needed for cross-category physical 3D generation and understanding.

Method: (1) Propose a novel, high-resolution geometry representation tailored for Vision-Language Models that encodes 3D structure without compression. (2) Build PhysXVerse, a general simulation-ready 3D dataset spanning diverse indoor/outdoor categories. (3) Develop PhysX-Bench, a six-attribute evaluation suite (geometry, absolute scale, material, affordance, kinematics, function description) for both generative and understanding tasks. (4) Conduct extensive experiments with conventional metrics and PhysX-Bench to demonstrate generation and understanding performance; show potential for simulation-ready scene generation and robotic policy learning.

Result: PhysX-Omni demonstrates strong performance in both generation and understanding. The high-fidelity geometry representation improves generation quality. PhysXVerse provides broad coverage for evaluation, and PhysX-Bench enables flexible, comprehensive assessment of geometric, physical, and functional properties. Together, these enable downstream applications in simulation-ready scenes and robotic policy learning.

Conclusion: PhysX-Omni can significantly advance downstream tasks in embodied AI and physics-based simulation by providing a universal framework, dataset, and evaluation suite for cross-category, simulation-ready 3D generation.

Abstract: Simulation-ready physical 3D assets have emerged as a promising direction owing to their broad applicability in downstream tasks. However, most existing 3D generation methods either neglect physical properties or are limited to a single asset category, e.g., rigid, deformable, or articulated objects. To address these limitations, we introduce PhysX-Omni, a unified framework for simulation-ready physical 3D generation across diverse asset types. Specifically, we develop a novel and efficient geometry representation tailored for Vision-Language Models, which directly encodes high-resolution 3D structures without compression, significantly improving generation performance. In addition, we construct the first general simulation-ready 3D dataset, PhysXVerse, covering diverse indoor and outdoor categories. Furthermore, to comprehensively and flexibly evaluate both generative and understanding capabilities in the wild, we propose PhysX-Bench, which encompasses six key attributes: geometry, absolute scale, material, affordance, kinematics, and function description. Extensive experiments with conventional metrics and PhysX-Bench show that PhysX-Omni performs strongly in both generation and understanding. Moreover, additional studies further validate the potential of PhysX-Omni for applications in simulation-ready scene generation and robotic policy learning. We believe PhysX-Omni can significantly advance a wide range of downstream applications, particularly in embodied AI and physics-based simulation.

</details>


### [2] [Lens: Rethinking Training Efficiency for Foundational Text-to-Image Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21573)
*Dong Chen,Fangyun Wei,Ziyu Wan,Dongdong Chen,Jiawei Zhang,Jinjing Zhao,Sirui Zhang,Yang Yue,Zhiyang Liang,Baining Guo,Chong Luo,Jianmin Bao,Ji Li,Lei Shi,Qinhong Yang,Xiuyu Wu,Xuelu Feng,Yan Lu,Yanchen Dong,Yitong Wang,Yunuo Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: 3.8B parameter text-to-image model Lens achieves competitive or superior performance versus state-of-the-art models with >6B parameters while using only ~19.3% of the training compute of Z-Image. It leverages dense GPT-4.1 captions and multi-resolution batches, architectural choices (semantic VAE, strong language encoder), RL with taxonomy-driven prompts, a reasoner module, and distillation for fast inference. It supports arbitrary aspect ratios up to 1:2–2:1, resolutions up to 1440^2, multilingual prompts, and fast generation (1024^2 image in 3.15s on a single H100; distilled turbo in 0.84s).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce training compute and model size in text-to-image generation while sustaining or surpassing performance, with broader flexibility (aspect ratio, resolution, multilingual support).

Method: Data: Lens-800M, 800M densely captioned image-text pairs with GPT-4.1 generated captions (~109 words per caption). Training batch construction with images of multiple resolutions and diverse aspect ratios to enlarge visual coverage. Architecture: semantic VAE for better latent representations; strong language encoder for faster optimization and multilingual generalization. Training regime: pre-training, followed by RL with taxonomy-driven prompts (Lens-RL-8K) and structured rewards to suppress artifacts; a reasoning module with training-free system prompt search to align user requests; distillation to accelerate 4-step inference.

Result: Model size 3.8B achieves performance competitive with or surpassing SOTA models >6B across benchmarks; training compute ~19.3% of Z-Image. Generalizes to aspect ratios 1:2 to 2:1 and resolutions up to 1440^2. Multilingual prompting support. Inference: 1024^2 image in 3.15s on a single NVIDIA H100; distilled turbo version does 4-step generation in 0.84s.

Conclusion: Efficient training and systematic optimization enable a compact T2I model to attain high-quality, flexible outputs with substantial compute savings, highlighting data-density and architectural design as key drivers of performance and generalization.

Abstract: We introduce Lens, a 3.8B-parameter T2I model that achieves performance competitive with, and in several cases surpassing, state-of-the-art models with more than 6B parameters across various benchmarks, while requiring significantly less training compute. For example, Lens requires only about 19.3% of the training compute used by Z-Image. The training efficiency of Lens stems from two key strategies beyond its compact model size. First, we maximize data information density per training batch by (i) training on Lens-800M, a dataset of 800M densely captioned image-text pairs whose captions are generated by GPT-4.1 and contain approximately 109 words on average, providing richer semantic supervision than conventional short captions, and (ii) constructing each batch from images with multiple resolutions and diverse aspect ratios, thereby enlarging the effective visual coverage of each optimization step. Second, we improve convergence speed through careful architectural choices, including adopting a semantic VAE that provides better latent representations and employing a strong language encoder that accelerates optimization while enabling multilingual generalization from English-only training data. After pre-training, we apply RL with taxonomy-driven prompts (Lens-RL-8K) and structured reward rubrics to suppress artifacts and improve visual quality, a reasoner module with training-free system prompt search to better align user requests with the model, and distillation-based acceleration for 4-step inference. Through efficient training and systematic optimization, Lens generalizes to arbitrary aspect ratios from 1:2 to 2:1 and resolutions up to 1440^2, and supports prompts in several commonly used languages. Thanks to its compact size, Lens generates a 1024^2 image in 3.15 seconds on a single NVIDIA H100 GPU, while its distilled turbo version performs 4-step generation in 0.84 seconds.

</details>


### [3] [GenEvolve: Self-Evolving Image Generation Agents via Tool-Orchestrated Visual Experience Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21605)
*Sixiang Chen,Zhaohu Xing,Tian Ye,Xinyu Geng,Yunlong Lin,Jianyu Lai,Xuanhua He,Fuxiang Zhai,Jialin Gao,Lei Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GenEvolve introduces a self-evolving image-generation agent that uses tool orchestration and visual experience distillation to improve performance, achieving state-of-the-art results on public benchmarks and a dedicated GenEvolve-Bench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-ended image generation increasingly relies on external tools and internal generative ability. A general, self-evolving agent can iteratively improve through trajectories, evidence gathering, and knowledge distillation.

Method: Proposes Tool-Orchestrated Visual Experience Distillation (GenEvolve). Each generation attempt is a tool-driven trajectory where the agent gathers evidence, selects references, invokes generation skills, and composes a prompt-reference program. A privileged teacher branch provides structured visual experiences by distilling best-worst differences. Dense token-level supervision via Visual Experience Distillation guides the student to improve search, knowledge activation, reference selection, and prompt construction. Additional resources GenEvolve-Data and GenEvolve-Bench are created for evaluation.

Result: Empirical results show substantial improvements over strong baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance among current image-generation frameworks on public benchmarks and the GenEvolve-Bench.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates the viability of self-evolving, tool-driven agents for image generation and provides datasets and benchmarks to foster further research, highlighting the benefits of structured visual experience distillation for complex multi-step generation tasks.

Abstract: Open-ended image generation is no longer a simple prompt-to-image problem. High-quality generation often requires an agent to combine a model's internal generative ability with external resources. As requests become more diverse and demanding, we aim to develop a general image-generation agent that can self-evolve through trajectories and use tools more effectively across varied generation challenges. To this end, we propose GenEvolve, a self-evolving framework based on Tool-Orchestrated Visual Experience Distillation. In GenEvolve, each generation attempt is modeled as a tool-orchestrated trajectory, where the agent gathers evidence, selects references, invokes generation skills, and composes them into a prompt-reference program. Unlike existing agentic generation methods that mainly rely on image-level scalar rewards, GenEvolve compares multiple trajectories for the same request and abstracts best-worst differences into structured visual experience, provided only to a privileged teacher branch. Inspired by on-policy self-distillation, Visual Experience Distillation provides dense token-level supervision, helping the student internalize better search, knowledge activation, reference selection, and prompt construction. We further construct GenEvolve-Data and GenEvolve-Bench. Experiments on public benchmarks and GenEvolve-Bench show substantial gains over strong baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance among current image-generation frameworks. Our website is as follows: https://ephemeral182.github.io/GenEvolve/

</details>


### [4] [UniVL: Unified Vision-Language Embedding for Spatially Grounded Contextual Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21611)
*Jiayun Wang,Yu Wang,Weijie Gan,Zhenting Wang,Wei Wei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A unified, spatially grounded conditioning framework (UniVL) for image generation that eliminates a separate text encoder by using an OCR-pretrained backbone to produce spatially aware embeddings, achieving better image quality and lower compute on a large mask-annotated benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the inefficiency and fragmentation of traditional dual-encoder conditioning (vision + language) and enable explicit spatial control with a single unified conditioning channel, reducing inference cost.

Method: Adapt an OCR-pretrained UniVL encoder to read a unified visual+text condition rendered onto a spatial mask, producing a fVIL embedding that fuses visual and semantic intent with spatial locations. A two-stage pipeline aligns UniVL with the VAE embedding space and then conditions a pretrained diffusion backbone entirely on UniVL embeddings, removing the need for a separate text encoder (e.g., T5).

Result: On UniVL-ImgGen (477K mask-annotated images), UniVL improves image quality over text-prompt baselines (FID 14→11; PSNR 16→20). It eliminates the text encoder, reducing inference TFLOPs by up to 52% and runtime by up to 44%. Ablation analyses support the contributions of the proposed components.

Conclusion: Spatially grounded, unified conditioning is viable and more efficient, enabling high-quality image generation with reduced computation and a minimal text interface.

Abstract: We introduce spatially grounded contextual image generation, a controllable image generation task that reframes the conditioning paradigm. Instead of supplying a reference image and a global text prompt through two separate encoders, one for vision and one for language, UniVL is trained to bind semantics to spatial locations directly from a single unified visual input, where the textual instruction is rendered onto the spatial mask. This removes the need for a standalone text encoder at inference time. The resulting model supports contextual image generation by following user-specified instructions about what should appear where, while substantially reducing computation.
  To address this task, we propose a framework in which the UniVL encoder, adapted from an optical-character-recognition-pretrained backbone, reads the unified condition optically and produces a UniVL embedding, fVIL, that fuses visual and semantic intent with spatial locations in a single token sequence. A two-stage pipeline first aligns UniVL with the VAE embedding space and then conditions a pretrained diffusion backbone entirely on UniVL embeddings, eliminating the standalone text encoder, such as T5. Although this reframing uses a deliberately minimal text interface, it yields strong empirical gains. On UniVL-ImgGen, a benchmark of 477K mask-annotated images that we construct for training and evaluation, UniVL improves image quality over text-prompted baselines, reducing FID from 14 to 11 and increasing PSNR from 16 to 20. It also eliminates the text encoder entirely, reducing inference TFLOPs by up to 52% and runtime by up to 44%. Additional ablation studies validate the contributions of the proposed components, paving the way for efficient, spatially grounded image generation with a unified conditioning paradigm.

</details>


### [5] [Flat-Pack Bench: Evaluating Spatio-Temporal Understanding in Large Vision-Language Models through Furniture Assembly](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21625)
*Aditya Chetan,Eric Cai,Peeyush Kushwaha,Bharath Raj Nagoor Kani,Utkarsh Mall,Qianqian Wang,Noah Snavely,Bharath Hariharan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Flat-Pack Bench, a benchmark for LVLMs to perform fine-grained, step-by-step spatio-temporal reasoning in furniture assembly tasks; reveals current LVLMs struggle with temporal ordering, localization, part mating, and tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LVLM benchmarks emphasize coarse-grained tasks and easily named entities, which fails to capture the needs of in-the-wild, procedural tasks like furniture assembly that require precise temporal and spatial understanding.

Method: Proposes a benchmark with multiple-choice questions and visual prompts highlighting relevant parts; evaluates temporal ordering, temporal localization, part mating understanding, and tracking in video contexts.

Result: State-of-the-art LVLMs show significant underperformance on fine-grained spatio-temporal reasoning; limited ability to leverage temporal information, track objects/parts, and understand spatial contacts.

Conclusion: The benchmark exposes critical gaps in current LVLM capabilities for fine-grained, temporally-structured video understanding and motivates future work to enhance temporal reasoning and multi-step spatial interaction modeling.

Abstract: The emergence of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) has significantly advanced video understanding capabilities. However, existing benchmarks focus predominantly on coarse-grained tasks such as action segmentation, classification, captioning, and retrieval. Furthermore, these benchmarks often rely on entities that can be easily identified verbally, like household objects, animals, human subjects, etc., limiting their applicability to complex, in-the-wild video scenarios. But, many applications such as furniture assembly, cooking, etc., require step-by-step fine-grained spatio-temporal understanding of the video, which is not sufficiently evaluated in current benchmarks. To address this gap, we introduce Flat-Pack Bench, a novel benchmark centered on furniture assembly tasks. Our benchmark evaluates LVLMs on nuanced tasks, including temporal ordering of assembly actions, temporal localization of assembly state, understanding part mating, and tracking, using multiple-choice questions paired with visual prompts highlighting relevant parts as references for fine-grained questions. Our experiments reveal that state-of-the-art LVLMs struggle significantly with fine-grained spatio-temporal reasoning, highlighting their limitations in effectively leveraging temporal information from videos, limited tracking ability, and understanding of spatial interactions like physical contact.

</details>


### [6] [Ablate-to-Validate: Are Vision-Language Models Really Using Continuous Thought Tokens?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21642)
*Tianyi Zhang,Mahtab Bigverdi,Ranjay Krishna*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Ablate-to-Validate and Token Replacement Test (TRT) to diagnose whether latent continuous tokens in vision-language models are genuinely used for reasoning, not just present; thorough, controlled ablations show accuracy gains persist even when token content is replaced, implying token presence rather than content drives improvements; recommends TRT as standard diagnostic alongside accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address a key concern: improvements from latent non-textual tokens could stem from confounds (e.g., increased context, anchoring, regularization) rather than true reasoning with token content. Provide a principled diagnostic to verify genuine use of latent content.

Method: Formalize Ablate-to-Validate; instantiate as Token Replacement Test (TRT): fix prompt, image, token budget, decoding; replace intermediate tokens with zero, random, first-repeat, or oracle alternatives. Use a controlled testbed with relative depth reasoning across LLaVA-13B and Qwen2.5-VL-3B, learning to predict depth spans using frozen encoders (SigLIP2, CLIP, DINOv2) and varying token budgets. Extend TRT to three off-the-shelf systems (Mirage, Mull-Tokens, CoVT) on BLINK, VSP, CV-Bench.

Result: Across settings, accuracy gains persist even when latent token content is corrupted or replaced, indicating that performance improvements are not fully dependent on the actual content of latent tokens but on their presence as a channel. Reveals a gap between having a latent channel and using it as an information bottleneck.

Conclusion: Recommend TRT as a standard diagnostic alongside accuracy for any method introducing continuous thought tokens to verify genuine latent-token reasoning.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly augmented with continuous or latent non-textual tokens intended to support "visual thinking." Despite improved task accuracy, this alone does not show that models actually use these tokens for reasoning -- gains may arise from confounds such as added context length, special-token anchoring, or training-time regularization. We formalize a diagnostic principle, Ablate-to-Validate, for testing whether latent-token content is genuinely utilized, and instantiate it as the Token Replacement Test (TRT), a standardized suite of content-replacement ablations. TRT holds the prompt, image, token budget, and decoding fixed while replacing intermediate tokens with zero, random, first-repeat, or oracle alternatives, isolating whether performance depends on token content or merely on token presence. As a controlled testbed, we study relative depth reasoning with LLaVA-13B and Qwen2.5-VL-3B, training models to predict and consume continuous or discrete depth spans across multiple frozen encoders (SigLIP2, CLIP, DINOv2) and token budgets. We additionally apply TRT to three off-the-shelf visual-thinking systems (Mirage, Mull-Tokens, CoVT) on BLINK, VSP, and CV-Bench. Across all settings, accuracy gains are a misleading proxy for latent-token reasoning: VLMs retain most improvement even when token content is corrupted or replaced, revealing a persistent gap between having a latent channel and using it as an information bottleneck. We recommend TRT as a standard diagnostic alongside accuracy for any method introducing continuous thought tokens.

</details>


### [7] [Look-Closer-Then-Diagnose: Confidence-Aware Ultrasound VQA via Active Zooming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21652)
*Yue Zhou,Erxuan Wu,Yikang Sun,Hongjoo Lee,Yuan Bi,Huixiong Xu,Zhongliang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A medical VLM framework for ultrasound that mimics the sonographer's zoom-in search and uses an uncertainty-aware GRPO learning objective, achieving substantial improvements in lesion localization (39.3%) across liver, breast, and thyroid datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current vision-language medical models underperform in ultrasound due to lack of interactive lesion-centric reasoning and the treatment of annotations as flawless ground truths despite inherent subjectivity.

Method: 1) Zoom-then-Diagnose paradigm to simulate interactive lesion-focused search; 2) Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with an uncertainty-aware reward derived from stochastic group-wise rollouts to estimate model confidence and promote reliable predictions, especially under ambiguity.

Result: The framework achieves a 39.3% improvement in lesion localization across liver, breast, and thyroid datasets, indicating the model learns to actively look closer before diagnosing.

Conclusion: Integrating interactive lesion-focused reasoning with uncertainty-aware optimization aligns VLMs with the clinical cognitive workflow, boosting localization accuracy while handling annotation subjectivity.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have significantly advanced medical visual question answering, yet their performance in ultrasound remains suboptimal. In clinical practice, sonographers explicitly focus on lesion regions to formulate reports, though diagnostic interpretations sometimes vary due to inherent subjectivity. However, existing VLMs are not explicitly structured to interactively zoom into lesions prior to diagnosis; moreover, they typically treat annotations as unbiased ground truths, failing to account for their inherent subjectivity and ambiguity. In this paper, we propose a framework specifically designed to consider the sonographer's cognitive workflow. We first introduce a structured Zoom-then-Diagnose paradigm, which replicates the interactive search process to enable lesion-focused reasoning. Furthermore, within the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) framework, we introduce an uncertainty-aware reward derived from stochastic group-wise rollouts to estimate prediction consistency as a proxy for model confidence. Together, these two components encourage the model to reinforce accurate predictions on clear cases while remaining cautious under ambiguity. Experiments across liver, breast, and thyroid datasets show that our framework improves lesion localization by 39.3\%, demonstrating that our model has learned the ability to actively look closer and diagnose.

</details>


### [8] [MRecover: A Conditional Generative Model for Recovering Motion-Corrupted MR images Using AI Generated Contrast](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21669)
*Jinghang Li,Tales Santini,Courtney Clark,Bruno de Almeida,Cong Chu,Salem Alkhateeb,Andrea Sajewski,Jacob Berardinelli,Hecheng Jin,Tobias Campos,Jeremy J. Berardo,Joseph Mettenburg,Ariel Gildengers,Howard J. Aizenstein,Minjie Wu,Tamer S. Ibrahim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MRecover synthesizes TSE from T1w for hippocampal subfield segmentation, improving data usability under motion, with strong 7T training and 3T generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motion artifacts in high-resolution hippocampal TSE MRI cause substantial data loss; a synthesis-based approach using routinely acquired T1w scans could recover usable data for subfield segmentation.

Method: Conditional generative model MRecover with autoregressive slice conditioning to enforce volumetric consistency; trained on 7T MRI data (n=577). In-domain evaluation on 7T holdout (n=148) with SSIM=0.84 and FSIM=0.94. Generalization to out-of-domain 3T data (n=416) showing subfield volumes from synthesized and as-acquired images highly correlated (r=0.87–0.97). Applied to motion-affected ADNI3 dataset, increasing analyzable subjects by 31.8% (593 vs 450). Reported larger diagnostic effect sizes (epsilon^2) in left/right hippocampus (0.121–0.100 vs 0.086–0.062). Project page provided.

Result: High in-domain fidelity (SSIM 0.84, FSIM 0.94) on 7T; strong cross-domain agreement of subfield volumes (r=0.87–0.97) on 3T; increased QC yield in ADNI3 by 593 vs 450; larger diagnostic effect sizes due to larger sample.

Conclusion: MRecover demonstrates effective synthesis of TSE from T1w to enable robust hippocampal subfield segmentation under motion, with good generalization across field strengths and potential to augment datasets and statistical power in atrophy studies.

Abstract: Hippocampal subfield segmentation requires high-resolution T2w turbo spin echo (TSE) MRI, yet this sequence is susceptible to motion artifacts, leading to substantial data loss. We developed a conditional generative model (MRecover) that synthesizes routinely acquired T1w images to create TSE images with autoregressive slice conditioning for volumetric consistency. Trained on 7T MRI data (n=577), the model achieved high in-domain fidelity (n=148, SSIM=0.84, FSIM=0.94) and generalized well to out-of-domain 3T data: subfield volumes from synthesized and the as-acquired images closely matched: (n=416, r=0.87-0.97) and yielded 31.8% more analyzable subjects in the motion-affected ADNI3 dataset after quality control (593 vs 450). The synthesized images also achieved larger effect sizes due to increasing the sample size for diagnostic group differences in hippocampal subfield atrophy (whole hippocampus $ε^2$= 0.121-0.100 vs. 0.086-0.062, left-right hemispheres). Project page: https://jinghangli98.github.io/MRecover/

</details>


### [9] [AVI-HT: Adaptive Vision-IMU Fusion for 3D Hand Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21714)
*Ziyi Kou,Ankit Kumar,Mia Huang,Taylor Niehues,Vatsal Mehta,Ergys Ristani,Li Guan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AVI-HT: an adaptive cross-sensor fusion framework that jointly leverages egocentric vision and on-glove IMU data to track 3D hand poses during hand-object interactions, using a cross-sensor attention mechanism and synchronized training with motion-capture ground truth; evaluated on DexGloveHOI with 100K+ samples and two hand models (UmeTrack, MANO), achieving notable improvements in keypoint error (−16.1% average; −24.2% for wrist-aligned variant) and insightful ablations on per-finger IMU contributions and sensor noise/misalignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome occlusion and modality limitations in 3D hand pose tracking by fusing on-body vision with on-glove IMUs through learned cross-sensor attention, enabling accurate and robust HOI tracking in real-world scenarios.

Method: Synchronized multi-modal training data (vision+6-DoF IMU) paired with ground-truth 3D hand poses from motion capture; a cross-sensor deep attention module that adaptively weighs vision vs. individual IMU signals; evaluation on DexGloveHOI dataset with two hand models (UmeTrack, MANO).

Result: AVI-HT reduces mean keypoint error by 16.1% overall and 24.2% for a wrist-aligned variant compared with baselines; ablation studies reveal per-finger IMU contribution and sensitivity to IMU noise and temporal misalignment.

Conclusion: The proposed cross-modal fusion with adaptive trust weighting improves accuracy and availability of 3D hand pose tracking in HOI scenarios, validated on a large synchronized dataset and across multiple hand models; the work highlights the complementary value of per-sensor IMUs and vision, and clarifies the effects of sensor quality and timing.

Abstract: We present AVI-HT, an adaptive visual-IMU fusion approach for tracking 3D hand poses by jointly modeling the egocentric image with on-glove 6-DoF IMU signals. AVI-HT achieves significantly improved accuracy and availability, particularly in hand-object interaction (HOI) scenarios involving heavy visual occlusion. Two complementary ingredients underpin its success: (1) synchronized multi-modal training data pairing on-body vision-IMU sensor streams with ground-truth 3D hand poses from a motion-capture system, and (2) a cross-sensor deep attention mechanism that adaptively modulates the trust assigned to the vision and individual IMU sensors. To evaluate AVI-HT in real-world settings, we conduct extensive experiments on our DexGloveHOI dataset that consists of 100K+ pairwise vision-IMU samples with synchronized 3D annotated poses, in which users manipulate a variety of objects during daily tasks. We compare against multiple single- and multi-modal tracking approaches under two hand models (UmeTrack, MANO). The results show that AVI-HT reduces mean keypoint error by 16.1% and its wrist-aligned variant by 24.2% over the baselines. Ablation studies further reveal the per-finger contribution of IMU sensors across activity types, and the model's sensitivity to IMU noise and temporal misalignment in vision-IMU fusion.

</details>


### [10] [BEiTScore: Reference-free Image Captioning Evaluation with an Efficient Cross-Encoder Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21728)
*Gonçalo Gomes,Bruno Martins,Chrysoula Zerva*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight cross-encoder-based learned metric for image captioning evaluation that initializes from a visual QA model, trained on a mixture with adversarial LLM augmentations, and accompanied by a new benchmark; achieves state-of-the-art accuracy with efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of current captioning evaluators: high computational cost of LLM judges and CLIP-based metrics' token limits, poor fine-grained sensitivity, and weak compositional generalization.

Method: Develop a cross-encoder metric initialized from a VQA model checkpoint. Train with a carefully constructed data mixture for supervised learning, incorporating adversarial LLM-based data augmentations to improve sensitivity to fine-grained visual-linguistic errors. Introduce a new benchmark assessing detailed captioning evaluation across diverse scenarios.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining computational efficiency, enabling large-scale benchmarking, quality-aware decoding, or reward guidance.

Conclusion: A carefully initialized, data-augmented lightweight cross-encoder can outperform existing metrics on detailed image-caption evaluation without compromising efficiency.

Abstract: Image captioning evaluation remains a significant challenge, as vision-language models evolve toward more challenging capabilities such as generating long-form and context-rich descriptions. State-of-the-art evaluation metrics involve extensive computational costs associated with the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) as judges, or instead suffer from the limitations of standard CLIP-based encoders, such as strict token limits, lack of fine-grained sensitivity, or lack of compositional generalization by treating captions as ``bags-of-words.'' We propose a new learned metric that tackles the aforementioned challenges, based on a lightweight cross-encoder that is initialized from a visual question-answering model checkpoint, balancing a strong weight initialization with computational efficiency. Our training scheme uses a carefully assembled data mixture for supervised learning, featuring adversarial LLM-based data augmentations to enhance model sensitivity to fine-grained visual-linguistic errors. We also introduce a new benchmark designed to assess detailed captioning evaluation across diverse scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed metric achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining the efficiency required for large-scale benchmarking, quality-aware decoding, or reward guidance.

</details>


### [11] [Improving 3D Labeling in Self-Driving by Inferring Vehicle Information using Vision Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21747)
*Steven Chen,Shivesh Khaitan,Nemanja Djuric*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Zero-shot VLM-based vehicle make/model recognition and 3D dimension inference can seed labeling, improving 3D vehicle labeling with less manual effort and higher quality, especially under occlusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce manual labeling burden and improve 3D vehicle annotations in autonomous driving by leveraging zero-shot VMMR and dimension estimation from images, addressing occlusion and labeling inconsistency.

Method: Use Vision Language Models to extract make/model/generation from image crops and predict 3D bounding box dimensions to seed manual labeling. Employ iterative prompt engineering. Evaluate multiple VLMs and compare against strong baselines (lidar-aided human labels). Test on public and proprietary datasets to assess generalizability across labelers and datasets.

Result: Achieves high accuracy in both vehicle bounding box inference and make/model/generation recognition. VLMs can provide more accurate dimensions than lidar-aided initial labels in occluded cases, reducing labeling errors. Demonstrates robustness across baselines, datasets, and labelers, and suggests reduced labeling time with improved label quality.

Conclusion: Integrating Vision Language Models into the labeling workflow improves efficiency and label quality for 3D vehicle annotations. Performance depends on prompt engineering and model choice, but findings generalize across datasets and labeling scenarios.

Abstract: We present an approach to improve 3D vehicle labeling in self-driving applications through zero-shot inference of vehicle information, leveraging Vehicle Make and Model Recognition (VMMR) methods. The proposed approach utilizes a Vision Language Model (VLM) to both infer a vehicle's make, model, and generation from image crops, and output accurate 3D bounding box dimensions to seed manual labeling. We evaluate the impact of iterative prompt engineering and the choice of different VLMs on both vehicle bounding box inference and make/model/generation recognition. When compared to strong baselines, the proposed approach not only shows high accuracy, but also excels in mitigating specific failure modes where VLMs provide better dimensions than initial lidar-aided human annotated labels (e.g., in cases of significant vehicle occlusion). Experiments on both public and proprietary data strongly suggest that our conclusions are generalizable across different labelers and datasets. The results demonstrate that integrating VLMs into the labeling process can reduce manual labeling time while increasing label quality.

</details>


### [12] [BodyReLux: Temporally Consistent Full-Body Video Relighting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21766)
*Li Ma,Mingming He,Xueming Yu,David M. George,Ahmet Levent Taşel,Paul Debevec,Julien Philip*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BodyReLux is a subject-specific video diffusion framework for temporally consistent relighting of full-body human performances, trained on a hybrid OLAT-based dataset with dynamic, strobe-free lighting, using light-token conditioning and masked-attention for dynamic illumination, achieving photorealistic results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Relighting human performances is critical for post-production and content creation. Existing methods struggle with full-body scope, temporal consistency, and dynamic lighting control. A dataset capturing diverse lighting, viewpoints, and performances is needed, together with a model that can harness generative priors and handle changing illumination over time.

Method: 1) Build a hybrid dataset of pixel-aligned video relighting pairs using static One-Light-at-a-Time (OLAT) captures and a novel dynamic capture where two smoothly varying lighting sequences are rapidly interleaved to avoid flicker. 2) Fine-tune a pretrained text-to-video model on this data to leverage generative priors. 3) Introduce lighting conditioning by representing each light source as a token, and condition on sequences of lighting with masked attention to support dynamic lighting. 4) Use careful data augmentation to improve photorealism and temporal robustness. 5) Train as a subject-specific model for relighting performances.

Result: The framework achieves photorealistic, robust, and temporally consistent video relighting of subject-specific human performances across diverse lighting conditions, viewpoints, and motions, with explicit control over individual light sources and dynamic lighting sequences.

Conclusion: BodyReLux demonstrates that video diffusion with light-token conditioning and masked-attention for dynamic lighting, trained on a hybrid OLAT/dynamic dataset, can deliver high-quality, temporally coherent relighting of full-body performances, offering a practical tool for post-production and content creation; future work may explore broader generalization beyond subject-specific models and real-time capabilities.

Abstract: Being able to relight human performance is a fundamental task for post production and content creation. We present BodyReLux, a subject-specific video diffusion-based framework for relighting full-body human performances in a temporally consistent way. Our model is trained on a hybrid dataset of pixel-aligned video relighting pairs, covering a diverse combination of lighting conditions, performances and viewpoints. To acquire such dataset, we combine traditional static One-Light-at-a-Time (OLAT) capture and a novel dynamic performance capture in which two smoothly varying lighting sequences are rapidly interleaved. Because the lighting operates above the human flicker-fusion threshold, the interleaving does not appear to strobe. We train our video relighting model from a pretrained text-to-video model to fully leverage the generative priors for producing high quality videos. To achieve accurate lighting control, we introduce a new lighting conditioning method that represents each light source as a token. We further condition on sequences of lighting using masked attention to support dynamic lighting control. Together with a carefully designed data augmentation pipeline, we achieve photorealistic, robust, and temporally consistent video relighting of subject-specific human performances.

</details>


### [13] [SceneGraphGrounder: Zero-Shot 3D Visual Grounding via Structured Scene Graph Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21788)
*Xuefei Sun,Xujia Zhang,Brendan Crowe,Doncey Albin,Christoffer Heckman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes SceneGraphGrounder, a zero-shot 3D grounding method that converts a 3D scene into a persistent graph and matches a query graph to it via constrained, multi-view aligned graph matching; demonstrates competitive zero-shot results on ScanRefer with RGB-D and real-world robot deployment; code to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current vision-language models rely on view-dependent reasoning or implicit representations, which hinder spatial consistency and interpretability for compositional language queries in 3D grounding. A structured, persistent 3D scene representation can enable more robust, interpretable, and multi-view-consistent grounding.

Method: Reconstructs a 3D scene graph from RGB-D data. Introduces a visual marker prompting strategy to extract object-object relationships from 2D views, then lifts these relations into a persistent 3D scene graph encoding spatial and semantic relations. For a given natural-language query, constructs a query graph and performs constrained alignment with the scene graph to enforce multi-view consistency and interpretable reasoning.

Result: On ScanRefer, achieves competitive zero-shot performance among RGB-D based methods. Demonstrated robust spatial reasoning in long-horizon real-world robot deployment.

Conclusion: A graph-based, zero-shot 3D grounding framework that uses a persistent 3D scene graph and graph matching with visual-marker-derived relations to achieve interpretable, multi-view-consistent grounding, with competitive results and real-world validation; code will be released.

Abstract: Zero-shot 3D visual grounding requires localizing objects in unstructured environments from free-form natural language. Recent vision-language model (VLM) approaches achieve promising results but rely on view-dependent reasoning or implicit representations, limiting spatial consistency and interpretability for compositional queries. We propose SceneGraphGrounder, a framework that reformulates 3D grounding as structured graph matching over a reconstructed 3D scene graph. To enable this formulation, we introduce a visual marker prompting strategy that enables a VLM to infer object-object relationships from 2D views, which are subsequently lifted into a persistent 3D scene graph encoding both spatial and semantic relations. Given a query, we construct a query graph and perform constrained alignment with the scene graph, ensuring multi-view consistency and interpretable reasoning. Experiments on the ScanRefer benchmark demonstrate that our method achieves competitive performance among zero-shot approaches, using only RGB-D inputs. We further validate our framework through real-world deployment on a mobile robot, demonstrating robust spatial reasoning in long-horizon physical environments. We will make our code publicly available upon acceptance.

</details>


### [14] [MM-Conv: A Multimodal Dataset and Benchmark for Context-Aware Grounding in 3D Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21796)
*Anna Deichler,Jim O'Regan,Fethiye Irmak Dogan,Lubos Marcinek,Anna Klezovich,Iolanda Leite,Jonas Beskow*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage referential grounding framework for dynamic 3D environments, with a large VR-based dataset and contextual rewriting that resolves dialogue ambiguity before visual localization, yielding substantial gains over end-to-end baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current vision-language models struggle with referential ambiguity in multi-turn dialogues within 3D environments; there is a need for a dataset and method to ground expressions that emerge in conversation.

Method: Construct a VR-based egocentric dataset (6.7h, 4,200+ expressions, full/partitive/pronominal types) and propose a two-stage grounding pipeline: (a) contextual rewriting to resolve ambiguity using dialogue context, (b) visual localization using a detector (e.g., GroundingDINO) on the rewritten expression.

Result: Contextual rewriting improves grounding by 11–22 percentage points on average; GroundingDINO attains 56.7% accuracy on pronominals after rewriting, nearly double the best end-to-end baseline.

Conclusion: Separating linguistic reasoning from visual perception enhances conversational grounding effectiveness compared to end-to-end models.

Abstract: Grounding language in the physical world requires AI systems to interpret references that emerge dynamically during conversation. While current vision-language models (VLMs) excel at static image tasks, they struggle to resolve ambiguous expressions in spontaneous, multi-turn dialogue. We address this gap by introducing (1) a benchmark for referential communication in dynamic 3D environments, built from 6.7 hours of egocentric VR interaction with synchronized speech, motion, gaze, and 3D scene geometry, and (2) a two-stage grounding pipeline that explicitly resolves conversational ambiguity before visual localization. The benchmark includes over 4,200 manually verified referring expressions spanning full, partitive, and pronominal types. Our contextual rewriting approach improves grounding performance by 11-22 percentage points on average, with a pure detector (GroundingDINO) reaching 56.7% on pronominals after rewriting, nearly double the best end-to-end baseline. Results demonstrate that decoupling linguistic reasoning from visual perception is more effective than end-to-end approaches for conversational grounding.

</details>


### [15] [Seizure-Semiology-Suite (S3): A Clinically Multimodal Dataset, Benchmark, and Models for Seizure Semiology Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21852)
*Lina Zhang,Tonmoy Monsoor,Peizheng Li,Jiarui Cui,Xinyi Peng,Chong Han,Prateik Sinha,Siyuan Dai,Jessica Nichole Pasqua,Colin M McCrimmon,Weiting Liu,Hailey Marie Miranda,Bing Hu,Xiangting Wu,Tengyou Xu,Chunhan Li,Jiaye Tian,Jiarui Tang,Detao Ma,Lingye Kong,Junnan Lyu,Jungang Li,Yan Zan,Junhua Huang,Rajarshi Mazumder,Vwani Roychowdhury*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A dataset and seven-task benchmark for fine-grained seizure semiology understanding in multimodal large language models (MLLMs), featuring 438 videos with 35k dense labels across 20 ILAE-defined features; introduces Seizure-RQI for clinically meaningful report evaluation; demonstrates benefits of seizure-specific fine-tuning and a two-stage neuro-symbolic framework achieving high F1 on epileptic vs non-epileptic classification; establishes a safety-critical medical video understanding benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in evaluating MLLMs on involuntary, pathologic motor behaviors (seizure semiology) and the need for clinically meaningful evaluation metrics and benchmarks to enable reliable, domain-adaptive medical video understanding.

Method: Construct Seizure-Semiology-Suite: 438 seizure videos with >35,000 dense labels across 20 ILAE-defined semiological features; define a seven-task hierarchical benchmark spanning low-level perception to temporal sequencing, narrative reporting, and seizure diagnosis; create the Report Quality Index for Seizure Semiology (Seizure-RQI); evaluate 11 open-weight MLLMs; apply seizure-specific fine-tuning; develop a two-stage neuro-symbolic framework.

Result: Identified systematic weaknesses in laterality reasoning, temporal localization, symptom sequencing, and clinically faithful reporting across current MLLMs; seizure-specific fine-tuning substantially improves performance; two-stage neuro-symbolic framework achieves F1=0.96 on epileptic vs non-epileptic seizure classification; dataset and benchmark enable assessment of clinically safe and domain-adaptive multimodal models.

Conclusion: Seizure-Semiology-Suite provides a rigorous, clinically grounded benchmark for evaluating multimodal models on safety-critical medical video understanding and guides development of domain-adaptive, clinically reliable AI.

Abstract: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated remarkable proficiency in general video understanding, their capacity to interpret involuntary, and spatio-temporally evolving pathologic motor behaviors such as seizure semiology remains largely untested. To address this gap, we introduce Seizure-Semiology-Suite, a clinically grounded dataset and benchmark for fine-grained, structured seizure semiology understanding. The dataset includes 438 seizure videos annotated with over 35,000 dense labels covering 20 ILAE-defined semiological features. Building on this dataset, we propose a seven-task hierarchical benchmark that systematically evaluates MLLMs from low-level visual perception to temporal sequencing, narrative report generation, and seizure diagnosis. To enable clinically meaningful evaluation of generated reports, we further introduce the Report Quality Index for Seizure Semiology (Seizure-RQI). Extensive baselines across 11 open-weight MLLMs reveal systematic weaknesses in laterality reasoning, temporal localization, symptom sequencing, and clinically faithful reporting. We show that seizure-specific fine-tuning substantially improves performance across tasks, and that a two-stage neuro-symbolic framework achieves an F1 score of 0.96 on epileptic versus non-epileptic seizure classification. Seizure-Semiology-Suite establishes a rigorous benchmark for evaluating multimodal models in safety-critical medical video understanding and guides the development of clinically reliable, domain-adaptive multimodal intelligence.

</details>


### [16] [CrossVLA: Cross-Paradigm Post-Training and Inference Optimization for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21854)
*Zhi Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CrossVLA studies cross-paradigm post-training for Vision-Language-Action models, introducing a surrogate log-prob estimator for flow-matching DPO, comparing LoRA and DoRA for parameter efficiency, analyzing inference-time bottlenecks, and releasing a strong retrieval-oriented initialization head with open-source resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models now fall into discrete-token autoregressive and continuous-action flow-matching paradigms. Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO) has been predominantly explored for autoregressive models; there is a need to understand how cross-paradigm post-training performs and what practices transfer.

Method: (i) Develop a surrogate flow-matching log-prob estimator enabling DPO on continuous-action backbones without integrating probability-flow ODEs. (ii) Compare LoRA and DoRA as parameter-efficient adapters for VLA DPO, reporting performance across LIBERO 4-suite with seeds. (iii) Analyze inference-time anatomy to identify latency bottlenecks (denoise loop, caching). (iv) Pretrain a multi-view + temporal projection head on 6000 LIBERO frames and evaluate as a downstream initialization for retrieval tasks. (v) Release code, checkpoints, logs, and scripts publicly.

Result: DoRA surpasses OpenVLA SFT by an average of +10.4 percentage points across the LIBERO 4-suite (600 trials, 3 seeds): Object +20.0, Long-horizon +11.0, Goal +8.0, Spatial +2.7; Object shows zero seed variance (38/50 across seeds). Inference analysis shows denoise loop accounts for 78.6% of sample_actions latency; prefix-K/V caching with VLA-Cache yields up to 21% acceleration but degrades success rate to 0-80%. A multi-view temporal head achieves 99.5% k-NN recall@1 on same-task retrieval (36x over random) on 6000 LIBERO frames.

Conclusion: CrossVLA provides concrete guidance on cross-paradigm DPO for VLA, highlighting DoRA as the stronger parameter-efficient adapter, clarifying latency and cache trade-offs for practical deployment, and offering a strong initialization head for retrieval tasks. All resources are open-sourced for replication.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have rapidly converged on a small set of architectural patterns: discrete-token autoregression (e.g. OpenVLA) and continuous-action flow-matching (e.g. pi-0.5). Yet preference alignment via Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO) -- the de-facto post-training step in language models -- has been studied almost exclusively on autoregressive VLAs. We present CrossVLA, an empirical study of cross-paradigm VLA post-training. Three contributions: (i) a surrogate flow-matching log-probability estimator that lets DPO operate on continuous-action backbones without probability-flow ODE integration; (ii) a head-to-head comparison of LoRA and DoRA as the parameter-efficient layer for VLA DPO, finding DoRA improves over OpenVLA SFT by a mean +10.4 pp across LIBERO 4-suite (600 trials, 3 seeds) -- per-suite +20.0 Object, +11.0 Long-horizon, +8.0 Goal, +2.7 Spatial -- with zero seed variance on Object (38/50 on each of 3 seeds); (iii) an inference-time anatomy showing the denoise loop dominates 78.6% of sample_actions latency and prefix-K/V caching a la VLA-Cache caps at a 21% acceleration ceiling -- both chunk-level and token-level cache strategies degrade success rate to 0-80% in our benchmarks. We further pretrain a multi-view + temporal projection head on 6000 LIBERO frames, achieving 99.5% k-NN recall@1 for same-task retrieval (36x over random), available as a downstream initialisation. All code, ckpts, training logs, and reproduction scripts are open at https://github.com/lz-googlefycy/vla-lab.

</details>


### [17] [Learning Emergent Modular Representations in Multi-modality Medical Vision Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21861)
*Yuting He,Chenyu You,Shuo Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DEX introduces a modular, expert-augmented architecture for multi-modality medical vision models to address non-IID modality statistics, using dynamic expert activation and a director updated by group EMA to fuse cross-modality knowledge; evaluated on a new 4M-image, 10-modality Medical Vision Universe, with improvements in optimization and transfer to 26 downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monolithic self-supervised training on heterogeneous medical modalities leads to conflicting gradients and shortcut representations due to pronounced non-IID statistics across modalities. The work reframes this as imbalance between specialization and coordination in emergent modularity and seeks principled control over this dynamic.

Method: Propose Director-Experts (DEX), a modular network. Each DEX module contains a pool of experts that autonomously specialize in modality-dominant statistics via an image-wise activation strategy. A director, updated via group exponential moving average, distills multi-expert knowledge into a shared space for semantic integration across modalities, enabling modular representations.

Result: The authors curate Medical Vision Universe (~4M images, 10 modalities) for pre-training. Extensive experiments on 26 downstream tasks show improved optimization behavior and transferability, indicating better cross-modality generalization and representation quality.

Conclusion: DEX provides a principled approach to balance specialization and coordination in modular MVFMs, enabling robust multi-modality representations and improved transfer to downstream tasks; the authors plan to release code and dataset.

Abstract: Multi-modality medical vision (MV) foundation models (FM) are fundamentally challenged by pronounced Non-IID feature statistics across heterogeneous imaging modalities. Monolithic self-supervised optimization on such data induces conflicting gradients, driving representations to collapse toward modality-dominant shortcuts. This work reframes this failure as an imbalance between specialization and coordination in emergent modularity, and proposes Director-Experts (DEX), a modular network that explicitly regulates these dynamics in stacked modules. Each DEX module comprises a pool of experts, dynamically adapted by our image-wise activation strategy, autonomously specializing in modality-dominant statistics, together with a director, updated via our group exponential moving average, which distills multi-expert knowledge into a shared space for semantic integration across modalities, thus driving the emergence of modular representations. We curate a new benchmark, Medical Vision Universe, over 4 million images across 10 modalities, which provides a FM-level pre-training with the broadest coverage of distinct imaging modalities to our DEX. Extensive evaluations on 26 downstream tasks demonstrate improved optimization behavior and transferability, indicating DEX as a principled step toward general-purpose multi-modality medical AI. Our code and dataset will be opened at https://github.com/YutingHe-list/DEX.

</details>


### [18] [Two-Stage Multimodal Framework for Emotion Mimicry Intensity Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21869)
*Dinithi Dissanayake,Shaveen Silva,Ovindu Atukorala,Prasanth Sasikumar,Suranga Nanayakkara*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A staged multimodal fusion framework for predicting EMI six emotion intensities from text, audio, visual (with optional motion), achieving 0.4722 Pearson on validation (4:1 split) and 0.57 on test, yielding a practical baseline and third place in EMI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predict continuous emotion intensity dimensions (Admiration, Amusement, Determination, Empathic Pain, Excitement, Joy) from in-the-wild multimodal video. Propose a modular, train-each-modality-then-fuse approach to leverage textual, acoustic, visual cues with an optional motion branch, aiming for improved performance and reproducibility.

Method: Independently train modality-specific encoders (text, audio, visual; optional motion). Fuse learned representations with a lightweight regressor using modality dropout and controlled encoder adaptation. Evaluate on EMI EMI EMI Challenge data, with an expanded 4:1 split; best model uses text–audio–vision–motion fusion.

Result: Best validation Pearson correlation of 0.4722 for the 4:1 split using the text–audio–vision–motion fusion. Motion branch provides only marginal gains. On the test set, the team achieved an average Pearson correlation of 0.57 and placed third.

Conclusion: A practical, reproducible EMI prediction baseline built on staged modality-specific encoders and a lightweight fusion regressor; multimodal fusion improves performance, though the motion branch yields limited gains.

Abstract: We present our submission to the Hume-ABAW10 Emotional Mimicry Intensity (EMI) Challenge, which aims to predict six continuous emotion intensity dimensions: Admiration, Amusement, Determination, Empathic Pain, Excitement, and Joy, from in-the-wild multimodal video clips. We propose a staged multimodal framework that combines textual, acoustic, and visual representations, with an optional motion branch. Our approach first trains modality-specific encoders independently and then fuses their learned representations through a lightweight regressor with modality dropout and controlled encoder adaptation. Across our submitted systems, the best validation performance is obtained by the text--audio--vision--motion fusion model under the expanded 4:1 split, achieving an average Pearson correlation of 0.4722. Although the motion branch yields only very slight gains, its behavior can be interesting to study. Our team was placed third in the EMI challenge, achieving an average Pearson correlation of 0.57 for the test set. Overall, we provide a practical and reproducible baseline for EMI prediction.

</details>


### [19] [Thermo-VL: Extending Vision-Language Models to Thermal Infrared Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21882)
*Rusiru Thushara,Yasiru Ranasinghe,Jay Paranjape,Vishal M. Patel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Thermo-VL presents a wavelength-aware VLM that fuses RGB and thermal information via a trainable thermal encoder and a prompt-guided dual-attention fusion module, improving grounding under low illumination and cross-spectrum reasoning while preserving the frozen RGB-language interface.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RGB-based grounding degrades in low-light environments where RGB cues are weak; thermal infrared provides complementary scene structure that can improve grounding and reasoning in multimodal tasks.

Method: Attach a trainable thermal encoder to a frozen Molmo-7B RGB backbone. Use a text-guided dual-attention fusion module that conditions thermal tokens on language prompts and RGB context, then injects a gated residual into the RGB stream. Train with standard LM objective plus auxiliary alignment and regularization losses. Create a pixel-aligned RGB-thermal instruction-tuning dataset and Thermo-VL-Bench, a manually screened RGB-thermal VQA benchmark.

Result: Thermo-VL achieves strong gains on thermal-only and RGB+thermal reasoning tasks, demonstrating the effectiveness of prompt-conditioned multispectral fusion; code and dataset are publicly available.

Conclusion: Prompt-conditioned multispectral fusion with a wavelength-aware architecture can effectively leverage thermal cues to enhance VLM grounding in low-light and cross-spectrum scenarios without disrupting the pretrained RGB-language interface.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) often fail under low illumination because their visual grounding is learned predominantly from RGB imagery, whereas thermal infrared preserves complementary scene structure when visible cues degrade. We present Thermo-VL, a wavelength-aware VLM that augments a frozen Molmo-7B backbone with a trainable thermal encoder and a text-guided dual-attention fusion module. Given aligned RGB tokens, thermal tokens, and prompt embeddings, the fusion module conditions thermal features on both language and RGB context, then injects a gated residual into the frozen RGB stream so thermal evidence can be incorporated without disrupting Molmo's pretrained RGB-language interface. We train the model with the standard language-modeling objective together with auxiliary alignment and regularization losses that improve cross-modal grounding and reduce over-reliance on RGB. We also introduce a pixel-aligned RGB-thermal instruction-tuning dataset and Thermo-VL-Bench, a manually screened RGB-thermal VQA benchmark for low-light and cross-spectrum reasoning. Experiments show strong gains on challenging thermal-only and RGB+thermal reasoning tasks, highlighting the value of prompt-conditioned multispectral fusion. Our dataset and code are publicly available at: https://thusharakart.github.io/Thermo-VL

</details>


### [20] [Universal CT Representations from Anatomy to Disease Phenotype through Agglomerative Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21906)
*Yuheng Li,Yuan Gao,Haoyu Dong,Yuxiang Lai,Shansong Wang,Mojtaba Safari,James E. Baciak,Xiaofeng Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FlexiCT is a CT foundation model trained with agglomerative continual pretraining on 266k CT volumes from 56 public datasets, enabling multi-task performance and tumor-stage–aware embeddings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CT AI is fragmented across task-specific models; a scalable foundation model and a large public CT representation resource are needed to support diverse tasks and phenotype-aware imaging.

Method: Three-stage agglomerative pretraining: 2D axial pretraining, 3D anatomical pretraining, and report-guided semantic alignment, enabling slice-level, volume-level, and vision-language analysis; trained on 266,227 volumes from 56 datasets.

Result: FlexiCT matches or exceeds prior task-specific approaches across five downstream task families (segmentation, classification, registration, vision-language understanding, clinical retrieval) on multiple benchmarks; embeddings organize CT scans along tumor-stage gradients, indicating phenotype-relevant feature capture.

Conclusion: CT foundation models can capture disease phenotype–relevant imaging features; FlexiCT demonstrates the viability of large-scale CT foundation models and provides a public resource for CT representation learning (code available).

Abstract: Computed tomography (CT) is a central to three-dimensional medical imaging, yet CT-based artificial intelligence remains fragmented across task-specific models for segmentation, classification, registration, and report analysis. Here we present FlexiCT, a family of CT foundation models trained by agglomerative continual pretraining on 266,227 CT volumes from 56 publicly available datasets, forming a large-scale public resource for CT representation learning. FlexiCT uses agglomerative pretraining across three stages: two-dimensional axial pretraining, three-dimensional anatomical pretraining and report-guided semantic alignment. This training strategy supports slice-level, volume-level and vision-language analysis. Across five downstream task families (segmentation, classification, registration, vision-language understanding and clinical retrieval), FlexiCT matches or exceeds prior task-specific approaches on multiple benchmarks. Its embeddings further organize CT scans along gradients associated with various tumor stages, suggesting that CT foundation models can capture imaging features relevant to disease phenotype characterization. Code is available at https://github.com/ricklisz/FlexiCT

</details>


### [21] [Guided Trajectory Optimization with Sparse Scaling for Test-Time Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21907)
*Gang Dai,Yining Huang,Yiming Xia,Guohao Chen,Shuaicheng Niu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RTS (Reward-guided Trajectory Scaling) improves diffusion model generation via reward-guided noise optimization and PCA-based curvature-driven sparse test-time scaling, prioritizing key denoising steps. It claims +15.6% GenEval and +60.4% ImageReward, achieving SOTA and offering practical guidelines for test-time scaling across diffusion architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Test-Time Scaling (TTS) methods rely on a static noise pool and have inflexible exploration along the denoising trajectory. RTS aims to dynamically guide exploration and compress the search space to unlock higher fidelity images.

Method: 1) Reward-guided noise optimization to steer searches toward promising regions in the denoising process. 2) Sparse test-time scaling with a PCA-driven curvature analysis to identify and emphasize key intermediate steps, effectively reducing the search space.

Result: Empirical results show baselines are outperformed by 15.6% on GenEval Score and 60.4% on ImageReward score; RTS sets a new state-of-the-art and provides practical guidelines for diffusion-specific test-time scaling.

Conclusion: RTS delivers refined, high-fidelity image synthesis by both guiding noise exploration and prioritizing critical denoising steps, demonstrating strong gains and a framework for efficient TTS across diffusion architectures.

Abstract: The efficient Test-Time Scaling (TTS) paradigm offers a promising perspective for enhancing the generation performance of diffusion models. However, current solutions are limited to a static, pre-defined noise pool and suffer from inflexible noise exploration across the denoising trajectory. To bridge this gap, we propose RTS, a novel Reward-guided Trajectory Scaling method to fully unlock the generative potential of diffusion models. Unlike existing methods, RTS facilitates the synthesis of refined, high-fidelity images via two core innovations: 1) a reward-guided noise optimization strategy to actively direct the search towards promising regions; and 2) a sparse test-time scaling framework together with a PCA-driven curvature analysis scheme to prioritize key intermediate steps in the entire denoising space, effectively compressing the search space. Experiments show our approach outperforms baselines by 15.6% across GenEval Score, and a 60.4% enhancement in ImageReward score, setting a new SOTA while providing a practical guideline for more effective test-time scaling across diffusion-specific architectures.

</details>


### [22] [Multi-scale interaction network for stereo image super-resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21913)
*Liyi Xu,Lin Qi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces a multi-scale interaction network for stereo image super-resolution featuring two attention modules: a Multi-scale Spatial-Channel Attention (MS-SCA) and a Dual-View Epipolar Attention (DVEA) with optimal transport, achieving competitive performance and outperforming most SOTA methods in extensive experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stereo SR benefits from both intra-view feature extraction and cross-view correspondence, but existing methods underutilize multi-scale spatial cues and precise epipolar-based matching between views.

Method: Proposes a Multi-scale Interaction Network. The Multi-scale Spatial-Channel Attention Module combines multi-scale large separable kernel attention with a simple channel attention component to enhance intra-view features. The Dual-View Epipolar Attention Module uses an optimal transport framework to achieve accurate cross-view matching along the epipolar line. The network jointly fuses intra-view and cross-view information through these modules, with ablation studies validating each component.

Result: Extensive experiments and ablation studies show competitive results that surpass most state-of-the-art methods in stereo image super-resolution.

Conclusion: The proposed MS-SCA and DVEA modules effectively leverage both intra-view and cross-view information, advancing stereo SR performance and highlighting the benefit of cross-view epipolar matching via optimal transport.

Abstract: Stereo image super-resolution aims to generate high-resolution images by leveraging complementary information from binocular systems. Although previous studies have achieved impressive results, the potential of intra-view and cross-view information has not been fully exploited. To address this issue, we propose a novel multi-scale interaction network for stereo image super-resolution. Specifically, we design a Multi-scale Spatial-Channel Attention Module that utilizes multi-scale large separable kernel attention and simple channel attention to improve intra-view feature extraction. Additionally, we propose a Dual-View Epipolar Attention Module, utilizing an optimal transport algorithm to achieve more accurate matching along the epipolar line. Extensive experimental and ablation studies show that our method achieves competitive results that outperform most SOTA methods.

</details>


### [23] [MAVEN: A Multi-stage Agentic Annotation Pipeline for Video Reasoning Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21917)
*Han Zhang,Wanting Jiang,Tomasz Kornuta,Tian Zheng,Vidya Murali*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MAVEN is a multi-stage, agentic pipeline that converts raw videos into multi-task training data with Chain-of-Thought reasoning traces, enabling scalable domain-adaptive video-event QA for vision-language models. It builds a Multi-Scale Spatio-Temporal Event Description (MSTED) from three caption levels and uses an agent-driven prompt redesign plus a hierarchical error-refinement loop to iteratively improve data quality; applied to 5,300 traffic videos with fine-tuning results that outperform multiple Gemini baselines on CCTV and AccidentBench tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality, structured, event-focused annotations capturing when/where/why/consequence are needed for video event reasoning with VLMs, but manual labelling is infeasible at scale. A scalable pipeline that can adapt prompts to new domains and datasets is required to improve downstream VLM performance.

Method: MAVEN comprises: (1) generation of MSTED from three caption levels to describe events; (2) use of Chain-of-Thought reasoning traces as intermediate input to downstream Q&A generation across multiple task formats; (3) agentic, top-down redesign of prompts for new datasets (domain adaptation); (4) a hierarchical refinement loop that classifies annotation errors against a taxonomy, traces root causes to the pipeline stage, and applies targeted edits to prompts or structure; (5) application to 5,300 traffic videos to create training data; (6) fine-tuning Cosmos-Reason2-8B with the MAVEN data; (7) evaluation on private CCTV and AccidentBench datasets with RL post-training for performance gains.

Result: Fine-tuning with MAVEN data beats Gemini baselines on CCTV: +38.8 MCQ points over zero-shot. On AccidentBench, CCTV-only training improves Cosmos-Reason2 by +10.7 MCQ points and matches Gemini 2.5 Pro despite no dashcam data; adding agent-adapted dashcam annotations narrows gap to Gemini 3.1 Flash, and RL post-training surpasses both Gemini baselines. Qualitative results show rapid adaptation to warehouse surveillance and public safety videos.

Conclusion: MAVEN demonstrates a scalable, domain-adaptive, multi-task annotation workflow that yields high-quality event-focused training data with CoT traces, enabling strong, adaptable VLM performance across surveillance and safety domains. The agent-driven prompts and hierarchical refinement loop are key for data quality and cross-domain transfer.

Abstract: Training Vision Language Models (VLMs) for video event reasoning requires high-quality structured annotations capturing not only what happened, but when, where, why, and with what consequence, at a scale manual labelling cannot support. We present MAVEN (Multi-stage Agentic Video Event aNnotation), a multi-stage agentic pipeline that turns raw videos into multi-task training data with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning traces, organized around a designated Event of Focus. At its core, MAVEN synthesizes a Multi-Scale Spatio-Temporal Event Description (MSTED) from three complementary caption levels; this explicit intermediate serves as the sole input to downstream Q&A generation across multiple task formats. Crucially, MAVEN supports agent-driven domain adaptation: given a new video dataset and target question examples, the agent redesigns all prompts top-down without manual re-engineering. A hierarchical refinement loop further classifies annotation errors against a taxonomy, traces root causes to the originating pipeline stage, and applies targeted edits that rewrite prompts or modify the pipeline structure itself, iteratively improving data quality. We apply MAVEN to label over 5,300 traffic videos and fine-tune Cosmos-Reason2-8B on the resulting data. On a private CCTV evaluation set, fine-tuning surpasses both Gemini 2.5 Pro and 3.1 Flash, including a $+38.8$-point gain in MCQ accuracy over zero-shot. On AccidentBench, CCTV-only training lifts Cosmos-Reason2 by $+10.7$ MCQ points and matches Gemini 2.5 Pro despite seeing no dashcam videos; adding agent-adapted dashcam annotations narrows the gap to Gemini 3.1 Flash, and RL post-training pushes overall performance past both Gemini baselines. Qualitative results on warehouse surveillance and public safety videos further show the agentic workflow readily adapts the pipeline to new domains.

</details>


### [24] [SDGBiasBench: Benchmarking and Mitigating Vision--Language Models' Biases in Sustainable Development Goals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21919)
*Zihang Lin,Huaiyuan Qin,Muli Yang,Hongyuan Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SDGBiasBench benchmarks SDG-oriented vision-language reasoning to expose biases where priors overshadow evidence; CADE is a training-free debiasing method that uses modality-specific priors and yields notable gains on the benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill a gap where SDG monitoring combines qualitative and quantitative judgments and models may substitute priors for evidence, causing systematic bias.

Method: Create SDGBiasBench with 500k expert-involved multiple-choice questions and 50k regression tasks to evaluate decision-level and estimation-level bias in VLMs; introduce CADE (Contrastive Adaptive Debias Ensemble) as a plug-in, training-free method leveraging modality-specific answer priors.

Result: Reveals intrinsic SDG bias in current VLMs, with predictions driven by SDG priors rather than multi-modal evidence; CADE improves multiple-choice accuracy by up to 25% and reduces regression MAE by up to 12 points across various VLMs.

Conclusion: Provides a benchmark and a practical debiasing tool to foster fair and reliable AI for sustainable development; CADE's plug-and-play nature facilitates adoption across models.

Abstract: Assessing progress toward the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) requires multi-step reasoning over visual cues, contextual knowledge, and development indicators, where incomplete evidence use and imperfect evidence integration can introduce hidden prediction biases. Real-world SDG monitoring further spans both qualitative judgments and quantitative estimation. However, existing benchmarks typically evaluate these aspects in isolation, obscuring systematic biases that emerge when models substitute priors for evidence. To address this gap, we propose SDGBiasBench, a large-scale benchmark suite for SDG-oriented vision-language reasoning. Spanning 500k expert-involved multiple-choice questions and 50k regression tasks, the benchmark enables comprehensive assessment of both decision-level and estimation-level bias in Vision--Language Models (VLMs). Evaluations on SDGBiasBench reveal an intrinsic SDG bias in current VLMs, where predictions are frequently driven by SDG specific priors rather than reliable multi-modal cues. To mitigate such bias, we propose CADE (Contrastive Adaptive Debias Ensemble), a training-free, plug-and-play method that leverages modality-specific answer priors. CADE yields significant gains on the proposed benchmark, improving multiple-choice accuracy by up to 25% and reducing regression MAE by up to 12 points across multiple VLMs. We hope our work can foster the development of more fair and reliable AI systems for sustainable development.

</details>


### [25] [Visual-Advantage On-Policy Distillation for Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21924)
*Ruiqi Liu,Xiaolei Lv,Gengsheng Li,Ximo Zhu,Zhiheng Wang,Zhengbo Zhang,Junkai Chen,Zhiheng Li,Bo Li,Jun Gao,Shu Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VA-OPD introduces Visual Advantage to identify token-level visual supervision and reweights on-policy distillation for VLMs, improving visual grounding and performance on math and visual tasks across scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard on-policy distillation improves text outputs but often fails to strengthen reliance on visual input; token-level visual signal is diluted.

Method: Define Visual Advantage (VA) as the log-prob difference when the teacher scores a student rollout with vs without fine-grained visuals; perform two-level distillation: rollout-level reweighting by trajectory-averaged VA and token-level KL separation between high-VA and low-VA groups; train on Geometry3K and ViRL39K with Qwen3-VL across 4B/8B/32B teachers; evaluation on eight benchmarks.

Result: VA-OPD outperforms standard on-policy distillation on all benchmarks; gains scale with teacher size and data size, indicating compounding effects.

Conclusion: Accounting for tokenwise visual supervision via VA and separate optimization for high-VA tokens yields more visually grounded models and robust gains across tasks and scales.

Abstract: On-policy knowledge distillation has proven effective for language models, yet its application to vision-language models (VLMs) remains underexplored.
