We observe that standard on-policy distillation can improve a student's output quality while failing to strengthen its reliance on visual input: on vision-critical tokens, the student's predictions remain largely unchanged whether or not fine-grained visual detail is present, even though the teacher's predictions depend heavily on it.To make this difference observable, we introduce visual advantage (VA), the token-level log-probability difference when the teacher scores a student-generated rollout with versus without access to fine-grained visual detail. VA is concentrated in a small minority of tokens, and these high-VA tokens are the ones that actually carry the visual supervision signal. This motivates a distillation objective that treats them differently from language scaffolding, so their contribution is not diluted by the abundant surrounding language tokens.We propose Visual-Advantage On-Policy Distillation (VA-OPD), which uses VA at two granularities: rollout-level reweighting by trajectory-averaged VA, and token-level KL averaged within high-VA and low-VA groups separately. We train on two math datasets (Geometry3K and ViRL39K) and evaluate on eight benchmarks covering both mathematical reasoning and visual understanding, across three teacher sizes (4B, 8B, and 32B) on the Qwen3-VL family. VA-OPD improves over standard on-policy distillation on every benchmark, with the gain growing monotonically along both the teacher-size and data-scale axes, suggesting that these factors compound consistently.

</details>


### [26] [EvoVid: Temporal-Centric Self-Evolution for Video Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21931)
*Shiqi Huang,Ziyue Wang,Zhongrong Zuo,Han Qiu,Qi She,Bihan Wen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EvoVid is a temporal-centric self-evolving framework enabling Video-LLMs to learn from unannotated videos using two temporal rewards to improve temporal reasoning and localization, improving performance over baselines and competitive with supervised methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL pipelines for video-language models rely on costly human annotations and struggle to capture temporal dynamics; self-evolving methods adapted from text/image domains fail to leverage video-specific temporal information.

Method: Proposes EvoVid. Introduces two temporal-centric rewards: (1) temporal-aware Questioner reward that promotes temporally dependent question generation via perturbation sensitivity, and (2) temporal-grounded Solver reward that provides automatic temporal supervision through inherent video segment localization. Evaluated across four base models and six benchmarks.

Result: Observed consistent improvements over base models and existing self-evolving baselines; achieves competitive performance with supervised methods across benchmarks; supports the efficacy of temporal-centric self-evolution for video understanding.

Conclusion: Temporal-centric self-evolution is an effective and scalable paradigm for video understanding and reasoning, enabling Video-LLMs to improve directly from raw, unannotated videos.

Abstract: Recent Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in video reasoning through reinforcement learning (RL). However, existing RL pipelines rely heavily on human-annotated tasks and solutions, making them costly to scale and fundamentally constrained by human expertise. Self-evolving frameworks have recently emerged as a promising alternative through autonomous Questioner-Solver self-play. Unfortunately, these approaches are primarily designed for static modalities such as text and images, fundamentally failing to capture the temporal dynamics that are central to video reasoning. In this work, we propose $\textbf{EvoVid}$, a temporal-centric self-evolving framework that enables Video-LLMs to improve directly from raw, unannotated videos. Specifically, we introduce two complementary temporal-centric rewards: a temporal-aware Questioner reward that encourages temporally dependent question generation through temporal perturbation sensitivity, and a temporal-grounded Solver reward that provides automatic temporal supervision via inherent video segment localization. Extensive experiments across four base models and six benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over both base models and existing self-evolving baselines, achieving competitive performance with supervised methods. These results highlight temporal-centric self-evolution as an effective and scalable paradigm for video understanding and reasoning.

</details>


### [27] [MLLMs Know When Before Speaking: Revealing and Recovering Temporal Grounding via Attention Cues](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21954)
*Dazhao Du,Liao Duan,Jian Liu,Tao Han,Yujia Zhang,Eric Liu,Xi Chen,Song Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An inference-time read-then-regenerate framework leverages TG-Heads during prefill to identify the target interval and constrains MLLM video context to that interval at test time, yielding VTG gains without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a perception-generation gap in multimodal LLMs for video temporal grounding. Current remedies are costly (post-training) or rely on coarse heuristics. The work aims to exploit cross-modal attention to locate the ground-truth interval during prefill and mitigate attention drift during generation.

Method: Identify Temporal Grounding Heads (TG-Heads) in prefill attention, convert their attention into a debiased frame-level relevance signal to extract the high-attention interval, then re-invoke the MLLM with visual context restricted to this interval via video cropping or attention masking—no parameter updates or architectural changes.

Result: Consistent improvements on three VTG benchmarks across MiMo-VL-7B, Qwen3-VL-8B, and TimeLens-8B, with gains up to 3.5 mIoU.

Conclusion: A training-free read-then-regenerate framework that exploits prefill TG attention to produce a more accurate ground-truth interval, demonstrating the viability of inference-time correction for VTG.

Abstract: Video temporal grounding (VTG), which localizes the start and end times of a queried event in an untrimmed video, is a key test of whether multimodal large language models (MLLMs) understand not only what happens but also when it happens. Although modern MLLMs describe video content fluently, their timestamp predictions remain unreliable, while existing remedies either require costly post-training on temporal annotations or rely on coarse training-free heuristics. In this work, we probe the cross-modal attention of MLLMs and uncover a perception-generation gap. Our key finding is that MLLMs often know the target interval during prefill, but lose this signal when generating the final answer. In the prefill stage, a sparse set of attention heads, which we call \emph{Temporal Grounding Heads} (TG-Heads), concentrates query-to-video attention on the ground-truth interval. During autoregressive decoding, however, the answer tokens shift attention away from this interval toward visually salient but query-irrelevant segments. This observation motivates an inference-time read-then-regenerate framework. We first convert TG-Head prefill attention into a debiased frame-level relevance signal and extract the high-attention interval it highlights. We then re-invoke the MLLM with visual context restricted to this interval, using video cropping or attention masking to suppress distractors. Without parameter updates and architectural changes, our framework consistently improves MiMo-VL-7B, Qwen3-VL-8B, and TimeLens-8B on three VTG benchmarks, with gains of up to +3.5 mIoU. The project website can be found at https://ddz16.github.io/mllmsknowwhen.github.io/.

</details>


### [28] [Bounding-Box Trajectories Matter for Video Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21957)
*Inpyo Song,Jangwon Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TrajVAD introduces a trajectory-based anomaly detection framework for videos, modeling multi-class bounding-box trajectories with normalizing flows. A trajectory-only variant (TrajVAD-T) outperforms pose-based methods on ShanghaiTech and MSAD; a pose-augmented variant (TrajVAD-P) further improves ShanghaiTech results, underscoring bounding-box trajectories as a potent, underexplored modality for anomaly detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Challenge in video anomaly detection due to appearance, viewpoint, and scene dynamics variations. Pose-based methods are robust to appearance changes but may neglect bounding-box trajectories that are readily available in pipelines. The work hypothesizes that bounding-box trajectories carry strong, underutilized normal/abnormal motion patterns that can be learned with normalizing flows.

Method: Model multi-class bounding-box trajectories with normalizing flows to learn normal kinematic patterns. TrajVAD-T uses only trajectories (no pose) for anomaly detection. TrajVAD-P extends with pose information to further improve detection performance.

Result: On ShanghaiTech: TrajVAD-T achieves AP 87.7%, surpassing all compared pose-based methods; on MSAD: top results reported. TrajVAD-P achieves 88.6% AUROC and 90.9% AP on ShanghaiTech, indicating additional gains from incorporating pose features.

Conclusion: Bounding-box trajectories constitute an effective and underexplored modality for video anomaly detection. A trajectory-focused approach can outperform pose-based methods, and combining trajectories with pose yields further improvements, suggesting broader applicability of trajectory cues in anomaly detection.

Abstract: Video anomaly detection is critical for public safety and security, yet remains highly challenging despite extensive research due to large variations in appearance, viewpoint, and scene dynamics. Among existing approaches, human pose-based methods have emerged as a major line of research, showing strong performance since many anomalies in public datasets involve humans and pose representations are robust to appearance changes while providing compact motion descriptions. However, these methods often overlook bounding-box trajectories, although such information is inherently available in pose-based pipelines. In this paper, we explicitly leverage these trajectories as a primary anomaly cue. We present TrajVAD, a framework that models multi-class bounding-box trajectories using normalizing flows to learn normal kinematic patterns. Its trajectory-only variant (TrajVAD-T) eliminates pose estimation and surpasses all compared pose-based methods on ShanghaiTech in AP (87.7%), while achieving the best results on MSAD. An extended version (TrajVAD-P) incorporates pose information and further improves performance to 88.6% AUROC and 90.9% AP on ShanghaiTech, highlighting bounding-box trajectories as an effective yet underexplored modality for video anomaly detection.

</details>


### [29] [Dual-Integrated Low-Latency Single-Lens Infrared Computational Imaging for Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21964)
*Xuquan Wang,Guishuo Yang,Dapeng Yan,Yujie Xing,Xuanyu Qian,Kai Zhang,Xiong Dun,Jiande Sun,Zhanshan Wang,Xinbin Cheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A physics-informed dual-integrated network (PDI-Net) enables fast, accurate infrared object detection by sharing semi-reconstructed features with a YOLO detector and using a PSF-prior–driven bridge, achieving large speedups on a single-lens system.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce latency in end-to-end infrared imaging pipelines by incorporating optical priors from the optical path into learning, rather than solely compressing reconstruction networks.

Method: Train a supervised U-Net; during inference, employ a semi-U-Net encoder that shares features with a YOLO-based detector to avoid full image reconstruction. Introduce a physics-aware large-small bridge (PALS-Bridge) that modulates multiscale convolutional branches using field-dependent PSF priors. Develop a physics-informed optical degradation simulation pipeline for training/validation. Deploy on a single-lens infrared camera.

Result: On the M3FD benchmark under low-SNR conditions, PDI-Net reduces inference time by 84.06% compared with Rec+Det with pruning and improves mAP@0.5:0.95 by 5.07%. The single-lens design also reduces system weight by about 50% compared with traditional multi-lens configurations.

Conclusion: The approach delivers compact, low-latency infrared imaging for real-time object detection on resource-constrained platforms while enhancing detection accuracy.

Abstract: Computational imaging enables compact infrared systems, but deep-learning pipelines that combine image reconstruction and object detection often introduce substantial inference latency. Most existing acceleration strategies compress the reconstruction network while overlooking physical priors from the optical path, leaving a trade-off between accuracy and speed. We present Physics-aware Dual-Integrated Network (PDI-Net), a low-latency framework that integrates infrared reconstruction with object detection and further embeds optical priors into the learning process. PDI-Net uses a supervised U-Net during training, while a semi-U-Net encoder shares features directly with a YOLO-based detector during inference, avoiding full image reconstruction. To bridge the gap between fidelity-oriented reconstruction features and detection-oriented semantics, we introduce a physics-aware large-small bridge (PALS-Bridge), which uses field-dependent point spread function priors to adaptively modulate multiscale convolutional branches. A physics-informed optical degradation simulation pipeline is also developed for training and validation. The method is deployed on a single-lens infrared camera, reducing system weight by about 50% compared with traditional multi-lens designs. On the M3FD benchmark under low-SNR conditions, PDI-Net reduces inference time by 84.06% compared with the Rec+Det with pruning strategy while improving mAP@0.5:0.95 by 5.07%. These results demonstrate compact, low-latency computational infrared imaging for real-time object detection on resource-constrained platforms.

</details>


### [30] [Foresee-to-Ground: From Predictive Temporal Perception to Evidence-Driven Reasoning for Video Temporal Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21973)
*Zelin Zheng,Xinyan Liu,Ruixin Li,Antoni B. Chan,Guorong Li,Qingming Huang,Laiyun Qing*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Reframes Video Temporal Grounding (VTG) as an Identify-then-Measure task; uses predictive temporal perception to form boundary-sensitive event representations; constructs a verifiable, evidence-based pool of candidate segments exposed as citable units; decouples event identification from precise boundary measurement, improving stability and transferability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Video-LLM VTG methods rely on direct timestamp generation from an unstructured visual-token stream, leading to brittle numerics and inconsistent boundaries. There is a need for verifiable grounding and stable boundaries by separating identification from measurement.

Method: F2G architecture: Predictive Temporal Perception to learn boundary-sensitive temporal representations; builds a video-wide evidence pool of candidate event segments; exposes segments as citable evidence units to the LLM, linking boundary prediction to explicit event hypotheses; decouples event identification from precise boundary measurement to stabilize grounding.

Result: Empirically improves grounding accuracy across diverse benchmarks, transfers robustly across different Video-LLM backbones, and preserves general video understanding capabilities.

Conclusion: F2G stabilizes VTG grounding by framing it as an Identify-then-Measure problem, enabling verifiable and transferable predictions through an evidence-driven approach.

Abstract: Current Video-LLM approaches for Video Temporal Grounding (VTG) typically rely on direct timestamp generation from an unstructured visual-token stream, often leading to brittle numerics and inconsistent boundaries. To address this, we propose Foresee-to-Ground (F2G), a framework that reformulates VTG as a verifiable Identify-then-Measure problem. F2G integrates Predictive Temporal Perception with Evidence-Driven Reasoning: it learns boundary-sensitive temporal representations to build a video-wide evidence pool of candidate event segments, and exposes these segments to the LLM as citable evidence units that bind boundary prediction to explicit event hypotheses. By decoupling event identification from precise boundary measurement, F2G stabilizes grounding and makes predictions verifiable. Extensive experiments demonstrate that F2G consistently improves grounding accuracy across diverse benchmarks, transfers robustly across different Video-LLM backbones, and preserves general video understanding capabilities.

</details>


### [31] [Video as Natural Augmentation: Towards Unified AI-Generated Image and Video Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21977)
*Zhengcen Li,Chenyang Jiang,Liangxu Su,Tong Shao,Shiyang Zhou,Ming Tao,Jingyong Su*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AIGC detectors trained on images struggle to generalize to video frames. The paper proposes VINA, a joint image–video detector that treats video frames as natural augmentations and uses a cross-modal supervised contrastive objective to align image and video representations, achieving robust, transfer-friendly, state-of-the-art performance across 14 benchmarks without dataset-specific tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-modal distribution shifts cause detectors to fail when applied to video frames. Video processing steps (color, compression, resizing, blur) and generator fingerprints create fingerprinted artifacts that degrade image-trained detectors. A unified detector that generalizes across image and video data is needed.

Method: VINA trains a unified model on both image and video data. Video frames are used as natural augmentations of images. It introduces a cross-modal supervised contrastive objective to align image and video representations on a shared real/fake decision boundary, enabling bidirectional gains in robustness and transferability without heavy dataset-specific tuning.

Result: Extensive experiments on 14 image, video, and in-the-wild benchmarks show bidirectional gains in robustness and transferability and state-of-the-art performance across most evaluated settings, without complex augmentation or dataset-specific tuning.

Conclusion: Jointly training on image and video with natural augmentation and cross-modal alignment yields robust, transferable AIGC detection. Cross-modal alignment reduces the performance gap between image-trained detectors and video frames, enabling generalization to unseen pipelines and modalities.

Abstract: AI-generated content (AIGC) is rapidly improving, creating an urgent need for detectors that generalize across data sources, deployment pipelines, and visual modalities. A strongly generalizable detector should remain robust under distributional variations. However, we identify a consistent failure mode: SOTA AI-generated image detectors often collapse when applied to frames extracted from videos. Through systematic analysis, we show that this cross-modal gap arises from both entangled synthesis-agnostic video processing shifts, including color conversion, codec compression, resizing, and blur, and model-specific fingerprints introduced by modern video generators. Motivated by these findings, we propose VINA (Video as Natural Augmentation), a unified AIGC detection framework that jointly trains on image and video data. VINA uses video frames as physically grounded natural augmentations and further introduces a cross-modal supervised contrastive objective to align image and video representations under a shared real/fake decision boundary. Extensive experiments on 14 image, video, and in-the-wild benchmarks show that VINA delivers bidirectional gains, improves robustness and transferability, and achieves state-of-the-art performance across nearly all evaluated settings without complex augmentation or dataset-specific tuning.

</details>


### [32] [Interpreting and Enhancing Emotional Circuits in Large Vision-Language Models via Cross-Modal Information Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21980)
*Chengsheng Zhang,Chenghao Sun,Zhining Xie,Xinmei Tian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a steering-vector-based causal attribution framework to understand and control how LVLMs convert visual emotion cues into narrative emotion. Reveals a decoupled mechanism: middle-layer sentiment-specific attention aggregates cues; deep-layer emotion-general pathways translate into narrative. Uses inference-time intervention on MER-UniBench to reduce emotional hallucinations and demonstrates causal fidelity with a specialized dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To demystify the internal emotional reasoning of LVLMs and reduce emotional hallucinations caused by diffuse emotional expression and lack of visual counterfactuals.

Method: Introduce steering-vector-based causal attribution; build a specialized dataset to map the Adapt-Aggregate-Execute mechanism; identify a functional decoupling between aggregation (middle layers, sentiment-specific attention) and narrative translation (deep layers, emotion-general pathways); apply inference-time interventions to regulate routing and boost semantic activation; evaluate on MER-UniBench.

Result: Inference-time intervention significantly improves performance and mitigates emotional hallucinations; supports the causal fidelity of the discovered circuits on MER-UniBench.

Conclusion: Provides an interpretable causal account of how visual emotion is processed into narrative and demonstrates practical gains via targeted routing interventions, suggesting a path to more reliable emotional reasoning in LVLMs.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) represent a significant leap towards empathetic agents, demonstrating remarkable capabilities in emotion understanding. However, the internal mechanisms governing how LVLMs translate abstract visual stimuli into coherent emotional narratives remain largely unexplored, primarily due to the scarcity of visual counterfactuals and the diffuse nature of emotional expression. In this paper, we bridge this gap by introducing a steering-vector-based causal attribution framework tailored for descriptive emotional reasoning. To this end, we construct a specialized dataset to demystify the emotional circuits underlying the three-stage ``Adapt-Aggregate-Execute'' mechanism. Crucially, we discover a functional decoupling: visual emotional cues are aggregated in middle layers via sentiment-specific attention heads, but are subsequently translated into narrative generation in deep layers through emotion-general pathways. Guided by these insights, we regulate the emotional information routing to strengthen attention flow and amplify the semantic activation to consolidate expression. Extensive experiments on the comprehensive MER-UniBench demonstrate that our methods significantly improve performance via inference-time intervention, effectively mitigating emotional hallucinations and corroborating the causal fidelity of the discovered circuits.

</details>


### [33] [RiT: Vanilla Diffusion Transformers Suffice in Representation Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21981)
*Le Zhang,Ning Mang,Aishwarya Agrawal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Repurposing fixed pretrained features (DINOv2) for x-prediction flow-matching yields better-conditioned distributions than raw pixels; introduces Representation Image Transformer (RiT) that uses frozen DINOv2 features, a dimension-aware noise schedule, and CLS-patch modeling to achieve strong ImageNet 256x256 FID with fewer parameters and efficient ODE integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether pretrained representation spaces offer more favorable data distributions for flow-matching learning, beyond pixel space; compare pixel, SD-VAE, and DINOv2 representations to understand how representation-learning objectives affect conditioning and generative performance.

Method: 1) Systematically compare intrinsic dimensionality, effective rank, covariance conditioning, excess kurtosis, and on-manifold interpolation error across pixel, SD-VAE, and DINOv2 features. 2) Propose RiT: a vanilla Diffusion Transformer trained with x-prediction on frozen DINOv2 features, augmented with a dimension-aware noise schedule and joint [CLS]-patch modeling. 3) Evaluate on ImageNet 256×256: unconditional and classifier-free guided FID; compare parameter count against DiT-DH-XL; assess ODE solvability with coarse steps.

Result: DINOv2 features show near-identical intrinsic dimensionality to pixel space (~33) but 7.3× higher effective rank, 35× better covariance conditioning, 11.5× lower excess kurtosis, and 1.7× lower on-manifold interpolation error; SD-VAE latents are intermediate. RiT achieves 1.45 FID uncond and 1.14 with guidance on ImageNet-256, outperforming DiT-DH-XL with 19% fewer parameters (676M vs 839M). The associated ODE is tractable: 5 Heun steps reach FID ~2.0, 10 steps reach ~1.25 with guidance; no distillation required.

Conclusion: The benefits originate from representation-learning objectives rather than mere compression; using pretrained representations in flow-matching can yield well-conditioned learning problems and strong generative performance, with efficient ODE inference. RiT demonstrates that frozen DINOv2 features plus a simple diffusion Transformer suffice for high-quality ImageNet synthesis.

Abstract: Flow matching with $x$-prediction -- regressing the clean data point rather than the ambient velocity -- is known to exploit low-dimensional manifold structure effectively in pixel space \cite{li2025back}. We ask whether a pretrained representation space, while containing a low-dimensional data manifold of comparable intrinsic dimensionality, offers a distribution more favorable for flow-matching learning. Comparing pixel, SD-VAE, and DINOv2 features along four geometric axes, we find that pixel and DINOv2 share nearly identical intrinsic dimensionalities (both $\hat{d}\!\approx\!33$) yet DINOv2 exhibits $7.3\times$ higher effective rank, $35\times$ better covariance conditioning, $11.5\times$ lower excess kurtosis, and $1.7\times$ lower on-manifold interpolation error; SD-VAE latents are consistently intermediate, indicating that the advantage stems from representation-learning objectives rather than mere compression. These statistical properties render the flow-matching regression well-conditioned and remove the need for the specialized prediction heads or Riemannian transport used by prior DINOv2 diffusion methods. We propose the \emph{Representation Image Transformer} (RiT): a vanilla Diffusion Transformer trained by $x$-prediction on frozen DINOv2 features, augmented only by a dimension-aware noise schedule and joint \texttt{[CLS]}-patch modeling. On ImageNet $256{\times}256$, RiT attains FID 1.45 without guidance and 1.14 with classifier-free guidance, outperforming DiT$^\text{DH}$-XL with $19\%$ fewer parameters (676M vs.\ 839M). The resulting ODE is efficiently solvable at coarse discretizations: with classifier-free guidance, $5$ Heun steps already reach FID 2.0 and $10$ steps reach 1.25, without distillation or consistency training. Code at https://github.com/lezhang7/RiT.

</details>


### [34] [Learning Spatiotemporal Sensitivity in Video LLMs via Counterfactual Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21988)
*Dazhao Du,Jian Liu,Jialong Qin,Tao Han,Bohai Gu,Fangqi Zhu,Yujia Zhang,Eric Liu,Xi Chen,Song Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CRPO is a dual-branch RL framework that uses counterfactual video perturbations and a Counterfactual Relation Reward to enforce spatiotemporal sensitivity, evaluated on DyBench with notable gains over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video LLMs tend to answer via shortcuts (single-frame cues, language priors) and RL post-training can amplify these shortcuts. A counterfactual evaluation is proposed to force sensitivity to dynamics.

Method: Construct counterfactual videos via horizontal flips and temporal reversals; train original and counterfactual branches jointly; apply a Counterfactual Relation Reward (CRR) that encourages answers to change for dynamic questions and stay the same for static ones, creating cross-branch constraints.

Result: CRPO outperforms prior RL methods on spatiotemporal-sensitive metrics while preserving general video performance. On Qwen3-VL-8B, DyBench P-Acc improves by 7.7 points and TimeBlind I-Acc by 8.2 points over the base model.

Conclusion: Counterfactual relational optimization mitigates shortcut-driven policies and enhances genuine spatiotemporal understanding in video LLMs, as evidenced by DyBench gains and maintained baseline performance.

Abstract: Video large language models (Video LLMs) achieve strong benchmark accuracy, yet often answer video questions through shortcuts such as single-frame cues and language priors rather than by tracking spatiotemporal dynamics. This issue is exacerbated in RL post-training, where correctness-only rewards can further reinforce shortcut policies that obtain high reward without tracking video dynamics. We address this by asking a controlled counterfactual question: if the visual world changed while the question remained fixed, should the answer change or stay the same? Based on this view, we propose \textbf{Counterfactual Relational Policy Optimization (CRPO)}, a dual-branch RL framework for improving \emph{spatiotemporal sensitivity}. CRPO constructs counterfactual videos through horizontal flips and temporal reversals, trains on both original and counterfactual branches, and introduces a \textbf{Counterfactual Relation Reward (CRR)} between their answers. CRR encourages answers to change for dynamic questions and remain unchanged for static questions. This cross-branch constraint makes it difficult for shortcut policies to be consistently rewarded across both branches. To evaluate this property, we introduce \textbf{DyBench}, a paired counterfactual video benchmark with 3,014 videos covering reversible dynamics, moving direction, and event sequence, together with a strict pair-accuracy metric that prevents fixed-answer shortcuts from inflating scores. Experiments show that CRPO outperforms prior RL methods on spatiotemporal-sensitive evaluations while maintaining competitive general video performance. On Qwen3-VL-8B, CRPO improves DyBench P-Acc by +7.7 and TimeBlind I-Acc by +8.2 over the base model, indicating improved spatiotemporal sensitivity rather than stronger reliance on static shortcuts. The project website can be found at https://ddz16.github.io/crpo.github.io/ .

</details>


### [35] [Virtual 3D H&E Staining from Phase-contrast Back-illumination Interference Tomography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22000)
*Anthony Song,Boyan Zhou,Mayank Golhar,Marisa Morakis,Alex Baras,Nicholas Durr*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HistoBIT3D creates voxel-wise BIT–nuclei paired data and a bidirectional multiscale virtual-staining framework to convert BIT volumes to realistic H&E, enabling quantitative validation and improved 3D nuclei segmentation (zero-shot).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome lack of quantitative benchmarks and shift-variant contrast when translating bit volumes to H&E, enabling validated, slide-free volumetric histopathology.

Method: Assemble HistoBIT3D dataset with BIT volumes and fluorescence-labeled nuclei; develop a bidirectional, multiscale content-consistency virtual staining network with cross-domain style reuse; train and evaluate against ground-truth nuclei distributions; assess realism metrics and 3D nuclei segmentation (zero-shot Cellpose).

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art realism metrics and improved 3D nuclei segmentation accuracy and boundary preservation; provides quantitative benchmarks; dataset and code released.

Conclusion: Provides a quantitatively validated, structurally faithful, scalable pipeline for 3D virtual H&E staining, accelerating slide-free volumetric computational histopathology.

Abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) histopathology of unprocessed tissues has the potential to transform disease management by enabling volumetric characterization of tissue microarchitecture and in-vivo assessment. Back-illumination Interference Tomography (BIT) is a new phase microscopy technology that provides rapid, non-destructive volumetric imaging of unprocessed tissues. However, translating BIT volumes into clinically interpretable H&E images remains challenging, particularly due to shift-variant contrast and the absence of quantitative validation benchmarks. We introduce HistoBIT3D, the first voxel-wise paired BIT and fluorescence-labeled nuclei dataset, enabling quantitative evaluation of structural preservation in unsupervised virtual staining against ground-truth nuclear distributions. Using this dataset, we present a novel virtual staining framework that translates BIT volumes with shift-variant contrast into realistic H&E volumes by leveraging bidirectional multiscale content consistency and cross-domain style reuse to enhance structural fidelity and perceptual realism. Our method achieves state-of-the-art realism metrics while significantly improving 3D nuclei segmentation accuracy and boundary preservation under zero-shot Cellpose evaluation. Together, these contributions establish a quantitatively validated, structurally faithful, and scalable pipeline for 3D virtual H&E staining, advancing the paradigm of slide-free, volumetric computational histopathology. Our data and code are available at: https://github.com/aasong113/HistoBIT3D_VirtualStaining.

</details>


### [36] [ConvNeXt-FD: A Fractal-Based Deep Model for Robust Biomedical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22002)
*Joao Batista Florindo,Amanda Pontes de Oliveira Ornelas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ConvNeXt-FD: a U-Net-like biomedical segmentation model using ConvNeXt as encoder with a Dice loss plus a boundary-aware regularization term based on differentiable Fractal Dimension; validates on six datasets, outperforming many baselines, especially with ImageNet pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address variability, noise, and complex morphology in biomedical images by strengthening boundary accuracy and shape fidelity through a strong encoder (ConvNeXt) and a boundary-aware loss.

Method: U-Net–like encoder-decoder. Uses ConvNeXt as encoder backbone. Hybrid loss combines Dice coefficient with a boundary-aware regularization term inspired by differentiable Fractal Dimension. Evaluated on six datasets (BUSI, DDTI, FluoCells, IDRiD, ISIC2018, MoNuSeg). ImageNet-pretrained weights used for initialization.

Result: ConvNeXt-FD achieves competitive to state-of-the-art performance across Dice, Jaccard, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, and False Positive Rate. Boundary-aware term improves boundary delineation; pretraining on ImageNet enhances performance.

Conclusion: A strong encoder plus boundary-aware regularization yields accurate, robust segmentations across diverse biomedical modalities; the approach effectively balances high-level semantics with fine boundary details.

Abstract: Biomedical image segmentation is a critical task in medical diagnosis and treatment planning, enabling precise delineation of anatomical structures and pathological regions. Despite significant advancements, challenges persist due to the inherent variability, noise, and complex morphology present in diverse medical imaging modalities. This paper introduces ConvNeXt-FD, a novel deep learning architecture for robust biomedical image segmentation, built upon a U-Net-like encoder-decoder framework leveraging the powerful ConvNeXt backbone. Our approach integrates a hybrid loss function combining the Dice coefficient with a boundary-aware regularization term inspired by a differentiable formulation of Fractal Dimension, designed to enhance the model's sensitivity to object boundaries and shape fidelity. We rigorously evaluate ConvNeXt-FD across six distinct biomedical datasets: BUSI (Breast Ultrasound Images), DDTI (Thyroid Ultrasound Images), FluoCells (Fluorescent Cell Images), IDRiD (Diabetic Retinopathy Images for Optic Disc Segmentation), ISIC2018 (Skin Lesion Images), and MoNuSeg (Nuclei Segmentation). Experimental results demonstrate that ConvNeXt-FD, particularly when initialized with ImageNet pre-trained weights, achieves competitive and often superior performance compared to existing state-of-the-art methods across various metrics, including Dice, Jaccard, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, and False Positive Rate. The integration of ConvNeXt as a strong encoder, coupled with the boundary-aware regularization, proves effective in capturing both high-level semantic features and fine-grained boundary details, leading to more accurate and reliable segmentations in challenging biomedical contexts.

</details>


### [37] [Rethinking Token Reduction for Diffusion Models via Output-Similarity-Awareness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22011)
*Hangyeol Lee,Hyojeong Lee,Joo-Young Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes DiTo, an output-centric token reduction for Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) that uses cross-timestep output token similarities to establish and reuse token correspondences, with PMR-guided interval scheduling and a frequency-aware penalty, achieving superior efficiency-quality trade-offs (1.6–3.9 dB PSNR gains at similar speedups).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current token reduction methods in diffusion models rely on input-token similarity in reduction-only ViT paradigms, which misalign with the diffusion objective of minimizing recovery error and preserving output token similarity.

Method: Introduce Matching timesteps using prior-step output similarities to establish token correspondences, then reuse these matches across multiple Reduction timesteps. Optimize matching frequency with Pair Match Ratio (PMR)-guided interval scheduling. Mitigate artifacts via Frequency-aware Token Matching with a selection-frequency penalty.

Result: Extensive experiments show that DiTo consistently outperforms existing TR methods, delivering 1.6–3.9 dB higher PSNR at comparable speedups and achieving a superior Pareto frontier.

Conclusion: Output-centric token reduction with inter-timestep matching and scheduling improves DiT efficiency while maintaining high output quality, addressing artifacts through frequency-aware matching.

Abstract: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) achieve superior image generation quality but suffer from quadratic computational complexity relative to token count. While various token reduction (TR) methods have been proposed to mitigate this cost, they overlook the primary objective of generative models: minimizing recovery error, which requires reflecting output token similarity. They rely solely on input token similarity inherited from reduction-only ViT paradigms, leading to a fundamental misalignment with this objective.
  To bridge this gap, we propose DiTo, a novel TR paradigm that shifts the focus toward output-centric token reduction. Based on the observation that output token similarity is consistently preserved across adjacent timesteps, DiTo utilizes prior-step similarities as an effective proxy to establish token correspondences at a Matching timestep, which are then reused across multiple subsequent Reduction timesteps. To optimize this interleaved scheduling, we propose Pair Match Ratio (PMR)-guided Interval Scheduling to determine the optimal matching frequency. Furthermore, to mitigate localized approximation errors and resulting blocking artifacts caused by repeated reuse, we propose Frequency-aware Token Matching by incorporating a selection-frequency penalty. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DiTo consistently outperforms existing TR methods with 1.6-3.9 dB higher PSNR at comparable speedups, achieving a superior Pareto frontier.

</details>


### [38] [PointLLM-R: Enhancing 3D Point Cloud Reasoning via Chain-of-Thought](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22013)
*Chaoqi Chen,Qile Xu,Wenjun Zhou,Hui Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A data-centric two-stage CoT supervision framework for 3D point clouds yields PoCoTI (55k samples) and PointLLM-R, achieving SOTA generative 3D classification/captioning and strong generalization to real scans and multi-turn dialogue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of explicit reasoning and irregular structure of 3D point clouds; extend Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to 3D by constructing large-scale CoT supervision.

Method: Two-stage pipeline: Stage 1 refines point-text instruction data via vision-language-model-based quality evaluation and reference-guided refinement; Stage 2 synthesizes high-quality reasoning paths with Human-in-the-Loop Prompt Optimization (HiLPO). Build PoCoTI with 55k samples; fine-tune PointLLM to obtain PointLLM-R.

Result: PointLLM-R achieves state-of-the-art performance on generative 3D classification and captioning; demonstrates robust generalization to real-world scanned point clouds and multi-turn dialogue scenarios.

Conclusion: A data-centric CoT supervision approach effectively enables reasoning-enabled 3D multimodal LMs; PoCoTI and PointLLM-R showcase strong performance and robustness in 3D understanding tasks.

Abstract: Understanding 3D point clouds through language remains a fundamental challenge in computer graphics and visual computing, due to the irregular structure of point cloud data and the lack of explicit reasoning in existing 3D multimodal models. While Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning has shown strong effectiveness in LLMs and image-based MLLMs, its extension to 3D understanding remains largely underexplored. In this paper, we propose a data-centric framework for constructing large-scale CoT supervision tailored to 3D point cloud understanding. Our framework consists of a two-stage pipeline that first refines point-text instruction data via vision-language-model-based quality evaluation and reference-guided refinement, and then synthesizes high-quality reasoning paths through Human-in-the-Loop Prompt Optimization (HiLPO). Using this approach, we build PoCoTI, a CoT-enhanced point-text instruction-following dataset containing 55K samples with explicit reasoning paths. Fine-tuning PointLLM on PoCoTI yields PointLLM-R, a reasoning-capable 3D multimodal language model. Extensive experiments on generative 3D classification and captioning demonstrate that PointLLM-R achieves state-of-the-art performance and generalizes robustly to real-world scanned point clouds and multi-turn dialogue scenarios.

</details>


### [39] [ORBIS: Output-Guided Token Reduction with Distribution-Aware Matching for Video Diffusion Acceleration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22015)
*Hangyeol Lee,Joo-Young Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ORBIS is a SW-HW co-designed accelerator for video Diffusion Transformer (DiT) that uses a Distribution-Aware Token Matching (DATM) algorithm to improve inter-token matching by leveraging previous timestep activations, enabling higher token reduction and substantial speedups/energy savings over A100.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D spatio-temporal attention in video DiT inflates token length with the number of frames, making computation expensive. Existing token reduction methods rely on inaccurate similarity estimates and lightweight matching, yielding poor matching and limited acceleration.

Method: Introduce ORBIS: a co-designed hardware-software approach. Use previous timestep activations to improve inter-token similarity estimates; develop DATM to capture global token distribution and minimize token-pair loss; implement deeply pipelined hardware and apply quantization to reduce area to 2.4% with negligible accuracy loss.

Result: ORBIS achieves approximately 2x higher token reduction ratio than AsymRnR, with up to 4.5x speedup and 79.3% energy reduction compared to an NVIDIA A100 GPU.

Conclusion: A high-quality token-matching strategy combined with dedicated pipelined hardware and quantization enables substantial reductions in computation, memory traffic, and energy for video DiT, enabling efficient real-time inference.

Abstract: Diffusion Transformer (DiT) has emerged as a powerful model architecture for generating high-quality images and videos. In the case of video DiT, 3D Spatio-Temporal Attention increases token length in proportion to the number of frames, sharply increasing computational cost. Token reduction methods mitigate this cost by exploiting spatial redundancy, but existing approaches rely on inaccurate similarity estimates and lightweight matching algorithms, resulting in poor matching quality and only marginal acceleration.
  To overcome these limitations, we propose ORBIS, an SW-HW co-designed accelerator for video DiT. ORBIS leverages the output activation from the previous timestep to obtain more accurate inter-token similarity, substantially improving matching quality and enabling a higher token reduction ratio. We further introduce a Distribution-Aware Token Matching (DATM) algorithm that captures global token distribution and explicitly minimizes token-pair loss for additional gains. To fully hide DATM latency, we design specialized, deeply pipelined hardware and minimize its hardware cost through quantization, occupying only 2.4% of total area with negligible accuracy loss. Extensive experiments show that ORBIS achieves about 2x higher token reduction ratio than the state-of-the-art approach, AsymRnR, while delivering up to 4.5x speedup and 79.3% energy reduction compared to an NVIDIA A100 GPU.

</details>


### [40] [Diverse Yet Consistent: Context-Guided Diffusion with Energy-Based Joint Refinement for Multi-Agent Motion Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22017)
*Lei Chu,Yuhuan Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-based, context-guided, energy-refined multi-agent motion prediction framework achieving improvements on ADE/FDE and JADE/JFDE on ETH/UCY.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of producing diverse yet jointly consistent predictions for interacting agents, beyond single-agent metrics, by leveraging historical context and a joint refinement mechanism.

Method: Proposes a diffusion-based framework that uses a guidance mechanism to incorporate historical trajectories, improving diversity; introduces an energy-based formulation to refine the joint trajectory distribution while preserving per-agent plausibility.

Result: Extensive experiments on four benchmarks show consistent improvements over baselines; notable gains in both marginal metrics (ADE/FDE) and joint metrics (JADE/JFDE) on ETH/UCY; competitive joint performance relative to prior joint methods.

Conclusion: The combination of diffusion modeling, context-guided generation, and energy-based joint refinement advances multi-agent motion prediction by balancing diversity, realism, and interaction consistency; potential computational trade-offs and generalization to other domains warrant further study.

Abstract: Deepgenerative models havebecomeapromisingapproach for human motion prediction due to their ability to capture multimodal distributions and represent diverse human be haviors. However, generating predictions that are both di verse and jointly consistent among interacting agents re mains challenging. In addition, most existing approaches are primarily evaluated using single-agent (marginal) met rics, which fail to fully reflect the joint dynamics of multi agent interactions. We propose a diffusion-based frame work that improves multi-agent motion prediction by lever aging rich contextual information from historical trajecto ries. This information is incorporated through a guidance mechanism to enhance the diversity and expressiveness of predicted motions. To further enforce interaction consis tency, we introduce an energy-based formulation that re fines the joint trajectory distribution while preserving the plausibility of individual trajectories. Extensive experi ments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms existing methods. No tably, our approach substantially improves both marginal (ADE/FDE) and joint (JADE/JFDE) metrics on ETH/UCY over strong marginal baselines. Compared with prior joint prediction methods, it delivers significant gains in marginal metrics while maintaining competitive joint performance.

</details>


### [41] [FRED: A Multi-Modal Autonomous Driving Dataset for Flooded Road Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22018)
*Connor Malone,Sebastien Demmel,Sebastien Glaser*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FRED is the first multi-modal autonomous-driving dataset focused on water hazards, providing synchronized camera, LiDAR, and IMU/GNSS data across five flood events, with KITTI-style and RTMaps formats, semantic water-hazard labels, and accompanying dry-condition data for localization/SLAM and map-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for dedicated data to develop and evaluate perception, fusion, and localization methods under flooded-road conditions, where water hazards degrade sensing and navigation performance.

Method: Data were collected with a 2.3 MP FLIR USB3 camera, 64-beam 360° LiDAR (OS1-64), and iXblue ATLANS-C IMU corrected by Geoflex RTK GNSS, across five locations during and after flooding. Data are published in KITTI-style and RTMaps formats, with semantic labels for water hazards and additional position/velocity and dry-condition data to support location-based detection, SLAM, and localization research.

Result: The dataset provides synchronized multi-modal data and semantic annotations for water hazard detection, enabling both single-sensor and sensor-fusion methods, plus resources to study localization and SLAM in flood scenarios.

Conclusion: FRED is positioned as the first dataset specifically targeting water-hazard scenarios in autonomous driving, enabling research in perception, fusion, and map-aided localization under flood conditions.

Abstract: The Flooded Road Environments Dataset (FRED) is, to our knowledge, the first multi-modal autonomous driving dataset specifically targeting the collection of data from scenarios involving water hazards on the road. The dataset contains images from a 2.3 MP FLIR Blackfly USB3 camera, 64-beam 360$^\circ$ point clouds from an Ouster OS1-64 LiDAR, and data from an iXblue ATLANS-C IMU corrected by a Geoflex RTK GNSS, from five separate locations captured both during and after flooding events. The data has been released in two formats: a KITTI-style format for easy integration with existing data tools, and the RTMaps format for direct replay of the vehicle's data capture. We provide semantic labels to enable the training and evaluation of both single-sensor and sensor-fusion methods for water hazard detection. Position and velocity, as well as data captured under dry conditions, are provided to enable the development of location-based detection methods that may incorporate maps, and to evaluate other tasks such as localisation and SLAM.

</details>


### [42] [ForeSplat: Optimization-Aware Foresight for Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22020)
*Yuke Li,Weihang Liu,Cheng Zhang,Yuefeng Zhang,Jiadi Cui,Zixuan Wang,Junran Ding,Haoyu Wu,Yujiao Shi,Jingyi Yu,Xin Lou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ForeSplat introduces an optimization-aware training framework for feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) using a lightweight multi-anchor meta-gradient (MetaGrad). It trains initializations that are explicitly designed for rapid, effective refinement, reducing the capacity burden on the network and enabling high-quality reconstruction with compact models. Across backbones (AnySplat, Pi3X, edge-distilled variants), ForeSplat initializations converge faster and achieve higher peak quality than vanilla initializations, with no inference overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard feed-forward 3DGS methods optimize solely for zero-order rendering error and assume the output is a good initialization for downstream optimization. However, limited large-scale 3D annotations constrain per-scene quality. A practical compromise is predict-then-refine, but the predictor must produce initializations that are suitable for quick, effective optimization. Therefore, there is a need for optimization-aware training that aligns the predictor with the downstream optimizer's refinements, enabling high-quality reconstruction with compact networks.

Method: ForeSplat couples MetaGrad, a lightweight optimization-aware training rule, with a short inner-loop refinement unrolled during training. It samples anchor states, back-propagates aggregated first-order gradients to the prediction head to provide an optimization-aware signal, and avoids costly higher-order differentiation by bypassing the 3DGS optimizer. This yields a surrogate signal for training without adding inference cost. The framework is instantiated on diverse backbones (AnySplat, Pi3X) and a distilled edge variant, demonstrating broadly applicable improvements.

Result: ForeSplat-trained initializations require fewer refinement steps to reach high-quality reconstructions and achieve higher peak quality than vanilla initializations. This holds across tested architectures, and also when the optimization fully converges. Importantly, there is no added inference cost.

Conclusion: ForeSplat effectively bridges amortized prediction and per-scene optimization, enabling lightweight yet high-fidelity 3D reconstruction. The optimization-aware training via MetaGrad reduces the burden on the feed-forward model, expands the practicality of 3DGS with compact networks, and provides a generalizable path across backbones and deployment scenarios.

Abstract: Feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) models offer fast single-pass reconstruction,but scaling them to match per-scene optimization quality is fundamentally hindered by the scarcity of large-scale 3D annotations.A practical compromise is predict-then-refine,where post-prediction optimization compensates for the limited capacity of the feed-forward network.However,standard feed-forward 3DGS is trained solely for zero-step rendering error,ignoring whether its output constitutes a good initialization for the downstream optimizer.We present ForeSplat,an optimization-aware training framework that equips feed-forward 3DGS models to produce initializations explicitly designed for rapid,effective refinement.By offloading part of the scene-modeling burden to the optimizer,ForeSplat substantially reduces the capacity pressure on the feed-forward model,making high-quality reconstruction feasible even with compact networks.At its core is MetaGrad,a lightweight multi-anchor meta-gradient training rule that bypasses costly higher-order differentiation through the 3DGS optimizer.MetaGrad unrolls a short inner-loop refinement trajectory,samples anchor states,and back-propagates aggregated first-order gradients to the prediction head as a surrogate optimization-aware signal.This fine-tuning adds no inference cost and enables high-quality reconstruction within seconds after a few refinement steps.We instantiate ForeSplat on diverse backbones,including AnySplat,Pi3X,and a distilled variant tailored for edge deployment.Across all tested architectures,a ForeSplat-trained initialization converges in fewer refinement steps and reaches a higher peak reconstruction quality than its vanilla counterpart,even fully converged.The framework consistently bridges the gap between amortized prediction and per-scene optimization,establishing a practical path toward lightweight,high-fidelity 3D reconstruction.

</details>


### [43] [SO-Mamba: State-Ownership Mamba for Unrolled MRI Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22031)
*Pengcheng Fang,Hongli Chen,Fangfang Tang,Feng Liu,Xiaohao Cai,Shanshan Shan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SO-Mamba is a state-ownership regularizer for unrolled MRI reconstruction that partitions recurrent and non-recurrent evidence via a State-Ownership Router (SOR). It maintains a resident carrier for recurrent content, routes non-resident evidence to affine-modulated state interfaces and an output correction outlet, and introduces an outer-band leakage diagnostic. Empirically, it outperforms CNN-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based baselines across five public MRI benchmarks with competitive efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In data-consistency–coupled unrolled MRI reconstruction, different stages operate on distinct reconstruction iterates. A uniform recurrent path can entangle persistent resident content with stage-dependent non-resident evidence, hindering long-range coherence of anatomical structures. A mechanism to separate resident (recurrent) content from non-resident evidence could improve reconstruction fidelity and stability across large spatial regions.

Method: Propose SO-Mamba, which uses a State-Ownership Router (SOR) to assign reconstruction evidence to three roles: resident carrier for recurrent content, state-interface access via affine modulation, and an output correction outlet. Non-resident evidence is routed to affine-modulated state interfaces and the output outlet, avoiding entry into the recurrent content path. Introduce a two-level outer-band leakage diagnostic that differentiates hidden-state storage from readout by measuring outer-band energy in a selective-scan trajectory and in the post-scan Mamba readout. Evaluate on five public MRI reconstruction benchmarks with diverse anatomies, sampling patterns, and coil configurations, comparing to CNN-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based baselines.

Result: SO-Mamba consistently improves reconstruction quality over CNN-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based baselines across all five benchmarks, while maintaining competitive computational efficiency.

Conclusion: State-ownership routing with the SO-Mamba framework enhances the separation of resident and non-resident evidence in unrolled MRI reconstruction, improving long-range coherence and reconstruction quality without sacrificing efficiency.

Abstract: Accelerated MRI reconstruction requires recovering missing details while preserving anatomically coherent structures across large spatial regions. State-space models such as Mamba provide efficient long-range modeling, making them attractive learned regularizers for unrolled reconstruction. However, in a data-consistency-coupled unrolled solver, different stages operate on different reconstruction iterates, where the resident carrier should preserve coherent reconstruction content across stages while stage-dependent non-resident evidence is tied to the current update. Treating these roles uniformly can place persistent resident-carrier evidence and update-dependent non-resident evidence into the same recurrent content route. We therefore propose SO-Mamba, a state-ownership Mamba regularizer that assigns reconstruction evidence within each Mamba stage to recurrent residency, state-interface access, and non-state output correction. SO-Mamba implements this ownership rule with a State-Ownership Router (SOR), which constructs a resident carrier for recurrent content and routes non-resident evidence to affine modulation of the B/C state interfaces and an output correction outlet. The resident carrier supplies the Mamba content route, while the non-resident evidence stream adapts the state interfaces and contributes through the output outlet without entering the recurrent content route. We further introduce a two-level outer-band leakage diagnostic that separates hidden-state storage from readout expression by measuring outer-band energy in the selective-scan state trajectory and the post-scan Mamba readout. Experiments on five public MRI reconstruction benchmarks spanning diverse anatomies, sampling patterns, and coil configurations show that SO-Mamba consistently improves over CNN-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based baselines with competitive computational efficiency.

</details>


### [44] [AgroVG: A Large-Scale Multi-Source Benchmark for Agricultural Visual Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22034)
*Haocheng Li,Juepeng Zheng,Zenghao Yang,Kaiqi Du,Guilong Xiao,Gengmeng Pu,Haohuan Fu,Jianxi Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AgroVG is a multi-source benchmark framing agricultural visual grounding as generalized set prediction, enabling evaluation of localization, target-set completeness, and existence-aware abstention across six agricultural target families. It reveals substantial gaps in current models, with low performance on multi-target set prediction (Set-F1 ~0.35) and mask grounding (IoU@0.75 < 0.17).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Grounding targets in agricultural imagery is critical for precision ag tasks but is hindered by small, occluded, repetitive targets and variable queries (single/multi/absent). A robust, unified evaluation framework is needed to jointly assess localization accuracy, target-set completeness, and abstention behavior.

Method: Assembles AgroVG from ten source datasets into 10,071 annotation-grounded image-query pairs spanning six target families (crop/weed, fruit, wheat head, pest, plant disease, tree canopy). Casts grounding as generalized set prediction: return all matching targets or abstain if none. Supports bounding-box grounding (T1) across all families and instance-mask grounding (T2) on sources with pixel-precise annotations. Provides task-specific protocols for box-set matching and query-level mask coverage and includes zero-shot evaluation across 26 model configurations (closed/open MLLMs, specialized grounding systems).

Result: The benchmark exposes noticeable gaps in current systems: the best multi-target Set-F1 is 0.35, and the best positive-query mask success rate at IoU@0.75 is below 0.17, underscoring the difficulty of agricultural grounding.

Conclusion: AgroVG offers a challenging, unified evaluation framework for agricultural grounding that surfaces large gaps in existing models and provides resources (data/code) to drive progress toward reliable, abstention-aware grounding in agricultural AI.

Abstract: Visual grounding, the task of localizing objects described by natural-language expressions, is a foundational capability for agricultural AI systems, enabling applications such as selective weeding, disease monitoring, and targeted harvesting. Reliable evaluation of agricultural visual grounding remains challenging because agricultural targets are often small, repetitive, occluded, or irregularly shaped, and instructions may refer to one, many, or no objects in an image. Evaluating this capability therefore requires jointly testing localization accuracy, target-set completeness, and existence-aware abstention. To address these challenges, we introduce \textbf{AgroVG}, a multi-source benchmark that formulates agricultural grounding as generalized set prediction: given an image and a referring expression, a model must return all matching target instances or abstain when no target is present. AgroVG contains 10{,}071 annotation-grounded image-query pairs from ten source datasets across six target families: crop/weed, fruit, wheat head, pest, plant disease, and tree canopy. It supports bounding-box grounding (T1) across all six families and instance-mask grounding (T2) on sources with reliable instance-level pixel annotations, with queries covering single-target, multi-target, and target-absent regimes. AgroVG further provides task-specific protocols for box-set matching and query-level mask coverage. Zero-shot evaluation of 26 model configurations spanning closed-source MLLMs, open-source VLMs, and specialized grounding systems reveals persistent gaps: the best multi-target Set-$F_1$ reaches only 0.35, and the best positive-query mask success rate at IoU@0.75 remains below 0.17. Data and code are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/AgroVG-5172/ .

</details>


### [45] [HyLoVQA: Dynamic Hypernetwork-Generated Low-Rank Adaptation for Continual Visual Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22035)
*Yiran Wang,Chenyi Xiong,Ziyue Qin,Miao Zhang,Kui Xiao,Zhifei Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HyLoVQA provides a drift-resilient memory-based continual VQA approach where a hypernetwork generates task- and object-specific LoRA adapters conditioned on an anchor memory, with an alignment loss to constrain parameter updates; it achieves state-of-the-art results on VQA v2 and NExT-QA in both standard and compositional settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continual VQA must learn from non-stationary streams of visual inputs and questions while preserving past knowledge. Standard methods updating many shared parameters suffer cross-level task interference, limiting adaptation to the current task/object.

Method: Maintain a drift-resilient memory bank of anchors containing visual object contents and textual task representations, updated from current inputs. Retrieve anchors to condition a hypernetwork that generates lightweight Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapters for task/object-specific updates. Introduce an alignment loss that links semantic discrepancies in feature space to functional changes in parameter space to keep adapters focused on the current task/object.

Result: Extensive experiments on VQA v2 and NExT-QA in standard and compositional settings show HyLoVQA outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Drift-resilient anchor memory + hypernetwork-generated LoRA adapters, guided by an alignment loss, yields parameter-efficient continual VQA with reduced interference and superior performance across standard and compositional tasks.

Abstract: Continual Visual Question Answering (VQA) requires learning from non-stationary streams of visual inputs and questions while preserving past knowledge. Most prior methods adapt by updating a largely shared parameter set. This often leads to cross-level task interference, hindering accurate adaptation to the current task and object. To address this limitation, we propose HyLoVQA. It maintains a drift-resilient memory bank of anchors. The bank stores the content of visual objects and textual tasks, and they are updated using current input features. Conditioned on retrieved anchors, a hypernetwork generates lightweight Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapters. This ensures parameter efficiency, allowing the model to adapt to each task and object dynamically. Additionally, we formulate an alignment loss that aligns semantic discrepancies in the feature space with functional changes in the parameter space, thereby constraining LoRA adapters to remain focused on the current task and object. Extensive experiments on VQA v2 and NExT-QA under both standard and compositional settings demonstrate the superiority of HyLoVQA over prior state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [46] [GA-VLN: Geometry-Aware BEV Representation for Efficient Vision-Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22036)
*Jiahao Yang,Zihan Wang,Xiangyang Li,Xing Zhu,Yujun Shen,Yinghao Xu,Shuqiang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GA-BEV introduces a compact 3D-grounded BEV representation for VLN by projecting RGB-D features into an agent-centric 3D space and enriching it with 3D foundation model priors, enabling efficient, geometry-rich multimodal navigation with state-of-the-art results using only navigation data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome heavy tokenization and weak spatial structure in dense RGB-based VLN, which leads to high computational overhead and limited spatial reasoning.

Method: Convert RGB-D inputs into agent-centric Bird-Eye-View (BEV) maps by 3D projection and feature aggregation; integrate explicit depth-based projection with implicit priors from a pretrained 3D foundation model into BEV space; feed the compact BEV representation into an MLLM-based navigation system.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art navigation performance with data efficiency, not requiring DAgger augmentation or mixed VQA training, demonstrating robustness of GA-BEV.

Conclusion: Explicit+implicit geometric fusion in a BEV space yields compact yet expressive representations that improve VLN efficiency and performance, validating 3D-grounded multimodal navigation via BEV and 3D priors.

Abstract: Despite significant progress in Vision-Language Navigation (VLN), existing approaches still rely on dense RGB videos that produce excessive patch tokens and lack explicit spatial structure, resulting in substantial computational overhead and limited spatial reasoning. To address these issues, we introduce the Geometry-Aware BEV (GA-BEV) - a compact, 3D-grounded feature representation that integrates both explicit and implicit geometric cues into multimodal large language model (MLLM) - based navigation systems. We construct BEV spatial maps from RGB-D inputs by projecting visual features into 3D space and aggregating them into an agent-centric layout that preserves geometric consistency while reducing token redundancy. To further enrich geometric understanding, we incorporate features from a pretrained 3D foundation model into the BEV space, injecting structural priors learned from large-scale 3D reconstruction tasks. Together, these complementary cues - explicit depth-based projection and implicit learned priors - yield compact yet spatially expressive representations that substantially improve navigation efficiency and performance. Experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art results using only navigation data, without DAgger augmentation or mixed VQA training, demonstrating the robustness and data efficiency of the proposed GA-VLN framework.

</details>


### [47] [Physiology and Anatomy Aware Inverse Inference of Myocardial Infarction for Cardiac Digital Twin](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22044)
*Mengxiao Wang,Yilin Lyu,Julia Camps,Ching Hui Sia,Mark Yan-Yee Chan,Yanrui Jin,Shuzhi Sam Ge,Chengliang Liu,Lei Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Novel cardiac digital twin framework for noninvasive myocardial infarction localization using anatomy-aware infarct synthesis and a physiology-aware network (PAA-Net), achieving Dice scores of 0.7391 (scar) and 0.5503 (border zone), surpassing existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LGE-MRI is the gold standard but resource-intensive; there is a need for noninvasive, accurate MI localization that integrates cine MRI with ECG. Existing inverse inference methods miss realistic scar morphology and repolarization dynamics, reducing sensitivity to subtle ECG variations and interpretability.

Method: Anatomy-aware stochastic infarct synthesis to generate realistic irregular scars with border zones; construct a virtual cohort to simulate QRS-T waveforms capturing depolarization and repolarization dynamics; Physiology and Anatomy Aware Network (PAA-Net) jointly encodes 3D myocardial geometry and multi-lead ECGs to infer infarct areas across varying localizations, sizes, extents, and transmuralities.

Result: The framework significantly outperforms existing methods in inverse inference, achieving Dice scores of 0.7391 for scar and 0.5503 for border zone; improves interpretability of the ECG-infarct relationship; code will be released upon acceptance.

Conclusion: The proposed integration of anatomy-aware infarct synthesis with a physiology-aware network via cardiac digital twins advances noninvasive MI localization, potentially reducing reliance on LGE-MRI and enhancing interpretability, with open questions about real-world generalization and validation.

Abstract: Accurate localization of myocardial infarction is essential for risk stratification. While LGE-MRI remains the gold standard, it is resource-intensive. Integrating cine MRI with ECG enables a more detailed representation of infarct properties. Existing inverse MI inference methods overlook realistic scar morphology and cardiac repolarization, reducing sensitivity to subtle ECG variations and interpretability of infarct-induced electrophysiological changes. In this paper, we propose a novel framework for noninvasive MI localization using cardiac digital twins. To bridge the domain gap between simulation and reality, we introduce an anatomy-aware stochastic infarct synthesis strategy to synthesize realistic, irregular scars with border zones, mimicking ischemic transmural progression. We then construct a virtual cohort to simulate QRS-T waveforms, capturing both depolarization and repolarization dynamics. Furthermore, we design a Physiology and Anatomy Aware Network (PAA-Net) that jointly encodes 3D myocardial geometry and multi-lead ECGs to infer infarct areas with varying localizations, sizes, spatial extents, and transmuralities. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms existing methods in inverse inference, achieving Dice scores of 0.7391 and 0.5503 for scar and border zone segmentation, respectively, while further enhancing the interpretability of the ECG-infarct relationship. Our code will be released upon acceptance.

</details>


### [48] [Diffusion-guided Generalizable Enhancer for Urban Scene Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22420)
*Henry Che,Jingkang Wang,Yun Chen,Ze Yang,Sivabalan Manivasagam,Raquel Urtasun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GenRe: diffusion-guided, generalizable enhancer for urban scene reconstruction that distills priors into pretrained 3D Gaussian representations, achieving robust, high-fidelity results with minimal per-scene optimization and strong generalization to unseen viewpoints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural rendering for urban scenes suffers from quality loss under large viewpoint shifts, limiting closed-loop simulation; diffusion priors improve quality but require costly per-scene optimization and fragile distilled representations with limited generalization.

Method: GenRe takes any pretrained 3D Gaussian representation and, within minutes, distills generative priors across diverse scenes to fix deficiencies, producing a diffusion-guided, generalizable enhancement that can be applied to existing representations.

Result: GenRe outperforms prior methods in both rendering quality and efficiency and yields robust reconstructions that generalize to challenging unseen viewpoints (e.g., lane changes), facilitating robust, scalable sensor simulation for autonomous driving.

Conclusion: GenRe provides a fast, diffusion-guided, generalizable enhancement for urban scene reconstruction that leverages cross-scene priors to improve quality and robustness, enabling reliable deployment in autonomous-driving simulations.

Abstract: Urban scene reconstruction from real-world observations has emerged as a powerful tool for self-driving development and testing. While current neural rendering approaches achieve high-fidelity rendering along the recorded trajectories, their quality degrades significantly under large viewpoint shifts, limiting the applicability for closed-loop simulation. Recent works have shown promising results in using diffusion models to enhance quality at these challenging viewpoints and distill improvements back into 3D representations. However, they often require costly per-scene optimization, and the distilled representations remain fragile and fail to generalize beyond limited synthesized views. To address these limitations, we propose GenRe, a novel diffusion-guided generalizable enhancer for urban scene reconstruction. GenRe takes as input any pretrained 3D Gaussian representation and fixes the deficiencies within a few minutes. By learning to distill generative priors across diverse scenes, GenRe produces robust and high-fidelity representation efficiently that generalizes reliably to challenging unseen viewpoints (e.g., lane change). Experiments show that GenRe outperforms existing methods in both quality and efficiency and benefits various downstream tasks, enabling robust and scalable sensor simulation for autonomous driving.

</details>


### [49] [Broken Memories: Detecting and Mitigating Memorization in Diffusion Models with Degraded Generations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22050)
*Yuanmin Huang,Mi Zhang,Chen Chen,Feifei Li,Geng Hong,Xiaoyu You,Min Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An on-the-fly framework detects memorization-induced instability in diffusion-model generation via empirical stability regions of latent update norms and adaptively mitigates memorization without changing prompts, achieving near-perfect detection and zero memorization with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Memorization in diffusion models poses privacy and copyright risks. Quantifying and mitigating memorization without sacrificing semantic fidelity is essential.

Method: Define empirical stability regions based on latent update norms to quantify stable generation. Implement step-wise, on-the-fly detection and apply adaptive mitigation during sampling without altering prompts or guidance.

Result: Experiments on Stable Diffusion 1.4 show AUC > 0.999 for memorization detection and 0.0% memorization rate after mitigation, with negligible overhead (~0.01s per image).

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively suppresses memorization while preserving semantic fidelity and image quality, enabling on-the-fly mitigation with minimal computational cost.

Abstract: While diffusion models excel at generating high-quality images, their tendency to memorize training data poses significant privacy and copyright risks. In this work, we for the first time identify that memorization induces internal numerical instability, often manifesting as visually ``broken'' artifacts. Inspired by stability analysis in numerical methods, we introduce empirical stability regions based on latent update norms to quantitatively characterize stable behavior during generation. Leveraging this, we propose a principled, on-the-fly framework for step-wise detection and adaptive mitigation. Our approach suppresses memorization without altering prompts or guidance, thereby preserving semantic fidelity and image quality. Extensive experiments on Stable Diffusion 1.4 demonstrate that our method achieves an AUC $>0.999$ detection performance and a $0.0\%$ memorization rate after mitigation with negligible overhead ($\approx0.01$s per image).

</details>


### [50] [Pre-VLA: Preemptive Runtime Verification for Reliable Vision-Language-Action and World-Model Rollouts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22446)
*Zhen Sun,Yongjian Guo,Haoran Sun,Luqiao Wang,Wei Lu,Jiachi Ji,Shengzhe Ji,Junwu Xiong,Zhijun Meng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Pre-VLA is a runtime verifier that preemptively assesses action validity for VLA models and world models, improving success rates and efficiency by filtering/resampling actions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty in learning-based action generation causes physical failures and costly world-model rollouts; need a lightweight verifier to prune low-quality actions under budget.
