Method: A multimodal backbone with modality-aware pooling and a dual-branch head predicting safety confidence and advantage scores; training with a multi-task objective: focal classification, advantage regression, and soft-threshold calibration; deployment uses a dual-mode preemptive resampling scheduler within a limited computation budget.

Result: On LIBERO benchmark, average closed-loop success rate across four suites increases from 30.79% to 37.62%; reduces task execution steps; 183.9 ms average forward verification time per action chunk; mitigates error accumulation in world-model rollouts.

Conclusion: Pre-VLA provides effective pre-execution action validation, improving success rate and efficiency while limiting computational overhead.

Abstract: While large vision-language-action (VLA) models and generative world models (WM) have advanced long-horizon embodied intelligence, their practical deployment remains challenged by uncertainty in learning-based action generation. Low-quality actions may cause physical failures during execution or lead to misleading world-model rollouts with redundant rendering costs. To address this issue, we propose Pre-VLA, a unified runtime verification architecture that performs preemptive action validity assessment before physical execution or world-model imagination. Pre-VLA leverages an efficient multimodal backbone with modality-aware pooling and a lightweight dual-branch head to predict both safety confidence and critic-derived advantage scores for candidate action chunks. To handle severe class imbalance and unstable boundary decisions, we train Pre-VLA with a multi-task objective combining Focal classification, advantage regression, and soft-threshold calibration. During deployment, a dual-mode preemptive resampling scheduler filters low-quality actions and triggers adaptive resampling under a limited computation budget. Experiments on the LIBERO benchmark show that Pre-VLA improves the average closed-loop success rate across four suites from 30.79\% to 37.62\% over RynnVLA-002, reduces task execution steps, achieves 183.9 ms average forward verification time per action chunk, and mitigates error accumulation in world-model rollouts.

</details>


### [51] [EasyVFX: Frequency-Driven Decoupling for Resource-Efficient VFX Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22051)
*Yue Ma,Xu Ye,Qinghe Wang,Yucheng Wang,Hongyu Liu,Yinhan Zhang,Xinyu Wang,Yuanpeng Che,Shanhui Mo,Paul Liang,Fangneng Zhan,Qifeng Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EasyVFX offers a resource-efficient, frequency-aware VFX synthesis via a two-stage framework: Freq-MoE for spectral priors and a fast Test-Time Training with Frequency-constraint Loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VFX generation traditionally requires large datasets and heavy computation due to coupling of spatial textures and temporal dynamics; need democratized, data-efficient approaches.

Method: Two-stage: (1) Frequency-aware Mixture-of-Experts (Freq-MoE) with soft routing that assigns experts to spectral bands for appearance and motion priors; (2) Test-Time Training with a Frequency-constraint Loss enabling rapid adaptation (~100 steps on a single GPU).

Result: Empirical results show structurally consistent, visually compelling VFX; demonstrates data- and compute-efficiency and supports frequency-aware learning for democratization.

Conclusion: Spectral disentanglement reduces optimization barriers and data dependencies, making professional-grade VFX achievable under stringent resource constraints.

Abstract: Generating high-fidelity visual effects (VFX) typically demands massive datasets and prohibitive computational power due to the intricate coupling of spatial textures and temporal dynamics. In this paper, we introduce EasyVFX, a resource-efficient framework that achieves realistic VFX synthesis under stringent constraints. Our core philosophy lies in frequency-domain decomposition: we observe that the complexity of VFX can be significantly mitigated by decoupling high-frequency components, which represent intricate spatial appearances, from low-frequency components that encapsulate global motion dynamics. This spectral disentanglement transforms a high-dimensional learning problem into manageable sub-tasks, thereby lowering the optimization barrier and reducing data dependency. Building upon this insight, we propose a two-stage training paradigm. First, we design a Frequency-aware Mixture-of-Experts (Freq-MoE) architecture. By utilizing a soft routing mechanism, our model assigns specialized experts to distinct spectral bands, enabling them to cultivate robust priors for appearance and motion dynamics. This specialization allows the model to acquire foundational VFX knowledge with fewer GPU resources. Second, we introduce a Test-Time Training strategy powered by a novel Frequency-constraint Loss. This allows the pre-trained model to swiftly adapt to specific, unseen effects through localized optimizations, requiring only about 100 steps on a single GPU. Experimental results demonstrate that EasyVFX produces structurally consistent and visually stunning effects, proving that frequency-aware learning is a key catalyst for democratizing professional-grade VFX.

</details>


### [52] [Distributed Image Compression with Multimodal Side Information at Extremely Low Bitrates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22061)
*Guojun Xu,Mingyang Zhang,Jianwen Xiang,Cheng Tan,Yanchao Yang,Junwei Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MDIC introduces a multimodal side-information–aware distributed image compression framework that uses a text-conditioned diffusion decoder and a multimodal-guided feature mask to preserve fine details and improve global perceptual quality, achieving state-of-the-art results at ultra-low bitrates on KITTI and Cityscapes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DIC at extremely low bitrates (<0.1 bpp) struggles to utilize global context and object-level details from side information, leading to blur and loss of fine details. There is a need to better exploit side information, including global semantics and fine-grained visual cues.

Method: Propose Multimodal DIC (MDIC) with (1) a text-to-image diffusion-based decoder conditioned on textual side information extracted from correlated images to capture shared global semantics; (2) a feature-mask generator supervised by a multimodal fine-grained alignment task to strengthen exploitation of visual side information. The mask guides extraction of fine-grained details from losslessly transmitted side information and regulates extraction of clustered features from quantized VQ-VAE embeddings.

Result: Extensive experiments on KITTI Stereo and Cityscapes demonstrate that MDIC achieves state-of-the-art perceptual quality at extremely low bitrates.

Conclusion: MDIC effectively leverages multimodal side information to preserve fine-grained local details and enhance global perceptual quality in distributed image compression, addressing key limitations of prior DIC approaches.

Abstract: Distributed Image Compression (DIC) is crucial for multi-view transmission, especially when operating at extremely low bitrates (< 0.1 bpp). Its core challenge is effectively utilizing side information to achieve high-quality reconstruction under strict bitrate budgets. However, existing DIC approaches struggle to exploit global context and object-level details from side information, leading to local blurring and the loss of fine details in the reconstruction. To address these limitations, we propose a Multimodal DIC framework (MDIC), which, for the first time, leverages side information in a multimodal manner into the DIC paradigm, effectively preserving fine-grained local details and enhancing global perceptual quality in reconstructed images. Specifically, we introduce a text-to-image diffusion-based decoder conditioned on textual side information extracted from correlated images to capture shared global semantics. Moreover, we design a feature-mask generator, supervised by a multimodal fine-grained alignment task, to strengthen the exploitation of visual side information. The generated mask serves two purposes: first, it guides the extraction of fine-grained details from losslessly transmitted side information to preserve the semantic consistency of reconstructed details; second, it regulates the extraction of clustered feature representations from the quantized VQ-VAE embeddings, compensating for category information lost under the extreme compression of the primary image. Extensive experiments on the widely used KITTI Stereo and Cityscapes datasets demonstrate that MDIC achieves state-of-the-art perceptual quality at extremely low bitrates.

</details>


### [53] [COCOTree: A Dataset and Benchmark for Open Tree-Structured Visual Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22068)
*Junhyub Lee,Seunghun Chae,Hyosu Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes open tree decomposition as a scalable, open-vocabulary hierarchical image segmentation paradigm; introduces COCOTree benchmark (21k images, 1.8M nodes, 3.5k labels); a fully automated LVLM–SAM generation pipeline; and the Open Tree Quality (OTQ) metric, validated by human judgments; dataset and code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome manual annotation bottlenecks and enable unconstrained granularity in visual scene structuring. Seek a formal basis for hierarchical trees of components with an open vocabulary, leveraging semantic reasoning and precise geometric grounding to capture long-tail assemblies.

Method: Introduce an automated generation pipeline that combines Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) for semantic reasoning with SAM for geometric grounding. Build an open-vocabulary, hierarchical decomposition (trees) with 3.5k labels, producing 1.8M structural nodes across 21k images (COCOTree). Propose Open Tree Quality (OTQ) metric to jointly assess mask precision, label accuracy, and structural consistency.

Result: Construction of the COCOTree benchmark with extensive annotations; validation via rigorous human evaluation showing alignment with human structural judgments; release of dataset and benchmark code. Open-vocabulary approach captures long-tail distributions of complex assemblies.

Conclusion: Offers a standardized evaluation protocol for open-tree decompositions and provides a scalable benchmark to advance open-structural image understanding, enabling flexible, hierarchy-aware scene parsing across a large label space.

Abstract: We formalize and enable the task of open tree decomposition, which segments an image into hierarchical trees of visual components with unconstrained granularity and flexibility. Specifically, we provide the foundation benchmark for this new paradigm with the following three key contributions. First, we overcome the prohibitively high cognitive and physical bottlenecks of manual annotation by developing a fully automated generation pipeline that synergizes the semantic reasoning of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) with the precise geometric grounding of SAM 3. Second, leveraging this pipeline, we construct COCOTree, a massive-scale benchmark featuring over 21K images and 1.8M structural nodes. By embracing an open-vocabulary space of over 3.5K unique labels, it successfully captures the long-tail distribution of complex physical assemblies. Notably, rigorous human evaluation confirms our generated annotations demonstrate strong alignment with human structural judgment. Third, we establish a standardized evaluation protocol by proposing the Open Tree Quality (OTQ) metric, which jointly assesses mask precision, label accuracy, and structural consistency. We release our dataset and benchmark code at https://github.com/melonkick3090/COCOTree.

</details>


### [54] [TWINGS: Thin Plate Splines Warp-aligned Initialization for Sparse-View Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22069)
*Hyeseong Kim,Geonhui Son,Deukhee Lee,Dosik Hwang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TWINGS uses Thin Plate Splines to globally warp backprojected points so they align with triangulated control points, providing a calibrated initialization for 3D Gaussian Splatting that improves detail and color in sparse-view reconstructions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse-view inputs yield sparse, noisy geometry and texture; a globally coherent calibration of backprojected points can enhance initialization and reconstruction quality for point-based volumetric rendering.

Method: Estimate a global warp with Thin Plate Splines from correspondences between backprojected points (from estimated depth) and triangulated 3D control points; compute calibrated backprojected points; sample near control points to initialize 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS); then perform reconstruction with improved detail and color fidelity.

Result: TWINGS consistently outperforms existing methods on DTU, LLFF, and Mip-NeRF360, delivering more detailed and accurate reconstructions under sparse-view conditions.

Conclusion: TPS-based calibration effectively mitigates point sparsity in 3DGS, enabling fast, geometrically accurate initializations and superior reconstructions across diverse sparse-view datasets.

Abstract: Novel view synthesis from sparse-view inputs poses a significant challenge in 3D computer vision, particularly for achieving high-quality scene reconstructions with limited viewpoints. We introduce TWINGS, a framework that enhances 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) by directly addressing point sparsity. We employ Thin Plate Splines (TPS), a smooth non-rigid deformation model that minimizes bending energy to estimate a globally coherent warp from control-point correspondences, to align backprojected points from estimated depth with triangulated 3D control points, yielding calibrated backprojected points. By sampling these calibrated points near the control points, TWINGS provides a fast and geometrically accurate initialization for 3DGS, ultimately improving structural detail preservation and color fidelity in reconstructed scenes. Extensive experiments on DTU, LLFF, and Mip-NeRF360 demonstrate that TWINGS consistently outperforms existing methods, delivering detailed and accurate reconstructions under sparse-view scenarios.

</details>


### [55] [JMed48k: A Multi-Profession Japanese Medical Licensing Benchmark for Vision-Language Model Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22080)
*Yue Xun,Junyu Liu,Qian Niu,Xinyi Wang,Zheng Yuan,Zirui Li,Zequn Zhang,Bowen Zhao,Shujun Wang,Irene Li,Kan Hatakeyama-Sato,Yusuke Iwasawa,Yutaka Matsuo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces JMed48k, a large multimodal benchmark built from Japanese medical licensing exams (48k questions, 20k images) with an 8-type visual taxonomy; defines JMed48k-Eval (12,484 scored questions; 9,905 text-only; 2,579 with images); assesses 21 models with and without images; includes a paired image-removal audit across four answer-transition states; finds images boost proprietary/open-source models more than medical-specific models; offers dataset release for reproducible, profession-stratified evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a profession-stratified, vision-language evaluation benchmark for medical licensing where visual content is integral, using official exam materials to reflect real-world decision-making and ensure reproducibility.

Method: Assembled 48,862 questions and 20,142 images from 11 Japanese national licensing exams (2005–2025). Annotated visuals using an 8-type taxonomy. Created JMed48k-Eval with 12,484 scored items (9,905 text-only; 2,579 with images). Evaluated 21 models (proprietary, open-source, and medical-specific) on text-only and image-inclusive tasks. Implemented a paired image-removal audit to compare answers with and without visual content, defining four answer-transition states.

Result: Proprietary and open-source models gain substantial performance from images; medical-specific models show limited use of visual evidence as many correct answers persist after image removal. Net image-removal effects vary by profession (e.g., +5.7 points for Physician; +39.8 points for Public Health Nurse). Across models, images contribute differently depending on profession; four transition states characterize answer changes.

Conclusion: JMed48k is released to enable reproducible, profession-stratified evaluation of vision-language models in medical licensing contexts and to study how visual content influences model reasoning across professions.

Abstract: We introduce JMed48k, a multi-profession Japanese healthcare licensing benchmark for evaluating vision-language models. Built from official PDF materials released by the Japanese Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare, JMed48k contains 48,862 exam questions and 20,142 images from 11 national licensing examinations between 2005 and 2025, with visual content annotated under an 8-type taxonomy. From this corpus, we derive JMed48k-Eval, a recent five-year evaluation subset with 12,484 scored questions, including 9,905 text-only questions and 2,579 questions with images. We evaluate 21 proprietary, open-source, and medical-specific models, reporting text-only and with-image performance separately. Because these subsets contain different questions, we further introduce a paired image-removal audit that evaluates questions with images before and after removing visual content to explore four answer-transition states. The audit shows that proprietary and open source models gain substantially from images, whereas medical-specific systems show limited observable use of visual evidence, with many correct answers persisting after image removal. Even among proprietary models, the net image-removal effect varies sevenfold across professions, from +5.7 points on Physician questions to +39.8 points on Public Health Nurse questions. We release JMed48k to support reproducible, profession-stratified evaluation of vision-language models in medical licensing settings.

</details>


### [56] [GenHAR: Generalizing Cross-domain Human Activity Recognition for Last-mile Delivery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22086)
*Zhiqing Hong,Zelong Li,Xiubin Fan,Guang Yang,Baoshen Guo,Haotian Wang,Tian He,Desheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GenHAR is a domain-generalizing HAR framework that tokenizes sensor data, learns cross-channel correlations in the frequency domain, and uses selective masking with an efficient attention design to achieve domain-invariant representations, yielding significant accuracy gains and computational savings while enabling real-world deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HAR suffers from distribution shifts across sensor data domains, causing performance drop when deployed in new environments; the goal is to achieve robust generalization to target domains using only source-domain data.

Method: Tokenize sensor data, learn correlations among frequency-domain channel dimensions to build robust representations; incorporate selective masking and an efficient attention mechanism to reduce computation; train to minimize domain discrepancy so representations are domain-invariant; evaluate against state-of-the-art HAR methods on real-world datasets; deploy in industry.

Result: GenHAR outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 9.97% in accuracy and reduces FLOPs by 6.4x on real-world HAR benchmarks; deployed at a leading logistics company across 4 cities with 2.15 billion real-time activities detected; code released at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: GenHAR enhances HAR generalization under domain shift via domain-invariant representations learned from source data, coupled with architecture-level efficiency improvements, demonstrating both strong performance and practical deployment potential.

Abstract: Human Activity Recognition (HAR) has shown remarkable effectiveness in various applications, such as smart healthcare and intelligent manufacturing. However, a major challenge faced by HAR is the distribution shift across different sensor data domains, which often leads to decreased performance when deployed for real-world applications. To address this issue, this paper introduces GenHAR, a novel framework designed to mitigate the domain gap by learning domain-invariant sensor representations. GenHAR aims to enhance the generalization capabilities of HAR on target domains purely with data from the source domain. The key novelty of GenHAR lies in two aspects. Firstly, GenHAR tokenizes sensor data and learns correlations among frequency sensor channel dimensions to improve the robustness of HAR models. Secondly, GenHAR improves the efficiency via selective masking and an efficient attention mechanism. We conduct a systematic analysis of GenHAR by comparing it with state-of-the-art HAR methods on real-world human activity datasets. Results show that GenHAR outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 9.97% in accuracy, and reduces Floating Point Operations by 6.4 times. Moreover, we deploy GenHAR at a leading logistics company in 4 cities, and have detected 2.15 billion real-time activities. We release our code at: https://github.com/Sensor-FoundationModel/GenHAR.

</details>


### [57] [LVDrive: Latent Visual Representation Enhanced Vision-Language-Action Autonomous Driving Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22089)
*Xiaodong Mei,Diankun Zhang,Hongwei Xie,Guang Chen,Hangjun Ye,Dan Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LVDrive uses latent future prediction and a two-stage decoder to enhance Vision-Language-Action (VLA) driving; it achieves strong gains on Bench2Drive in closed-loop tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome underutilization of powerful scene understanding in VLA due to sparse action supervision and the misalignment of dense visual supervision with semantic scene representation. The goal is to learn high-level latent representations of future scenes and motions for better planning.

Method: Introduce a future scene prediction task in a high-level latent space guided by a pretrained vision backbone. Model future scene and motion in a unified embedding space with a single forward pass. Employ a two-stage trajectory decoding strategy that uses latent future representations to refine trajectory generation.

Result: Significant improvements in closed-loop driving performance on the Bench2Drive benchmark, outperforming both action-supervised methods and image-reconstruction-based world model approaches.

Conclusion: Latent future representations in a unified latent space, coupled with a two-stage decoder, improve VLA-based autonomous driving efficiency and effectiveness, demonstrating the value of latent-domain future prediction for planning and control.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising framework for end-to-end autonomous driving. However, existing VLAs typically rely on sparse action supervision, which underutilizes their powerful scene understanding and reasoning capabilities. Recent attempts to incorporate dense visual supervision via world modeling often overemphasize pixel-level image reconstruction, neglecting semantically meaningful scene representation learning. In this work, we propose LVDrive, a Latent Visual representation enhanced VLA framework for autonomous driving. LVDrive introduces a future scene prediction task into the VLA paradigm, where future representations are learned entirely in a high-level latent space under auxiliary supervision from a pretrained vision backbone. Departing from inefficient autoregressive generation, we jointly model future scene and motion prediction within a unified embedding space, processed in a single forward pass to conduct the future-aware reasoning. We further design a two-stage trajectory decoding strategy that explicitly leverages the learned latent future representations to refine trajectory generation. Extensive experiments on the challenging Bench2Drive benchmark demonstrate that LVDrive achieves significant improvements in closed-loop driving performance, outperforming both action supervised methods and image-reconstruction-based world model approaches.

</details>


### [58] [VISTA: Validation-Guided Integration of Spatial and Temporal Foundation Models with Anatomical Decoding for Rare-Pathology VCE Event Detection -- after competition results](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22096)
*Bo-Cheng Qiu,Fang-Ying Lin,Ming-Han Sun,Yu-Fan Lin,Chia-Ming Lee,Chih-Chung Hsu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VISTA is a metric-aligned multi-backbone framework for RAREVISION capsule endoscopy event detection, combining EndoFM-LV temporal context and DINOv3 ViTL/16 frame semantics with Diverse Head Ensemble, Validation-Guided Weighted Fusion, and Anatomy-Aware Temporal Event Decoding; it achieves improved mAP@0.5 and mAP@0.95, and ranks second after post-competition threshold refinement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Event detection in capsule endoscopy is challenging due to sparsity and visual heterogeneity of clinically relevant findings, and evaluation at the event level (not per-frame) requires architectures that align with this metric and capture both temporal context and frame-level semantics.

Method: Utilizes EndoFM-LV to capture temporal context and DINOv3 ViTL/16 for robust frame-level visual representations; aggregates predictions with a Diverse Head Ensemble (DHE), validates and weights fusion with a Validation-Guided Weighted Fusion (VGWF), and decodes events with Anatomy-Aware Temporal Event Decoding (ATED). Post-competition threshold optimization via a global coarse search.

Result: Original submission: mAP@0.5 = 0.3530; mAP@0.95 = 0.3235. After global threshold refinement: mAP@0.5 = 0.3726; mAP@0.95 = 0.3431; ranking second in post-competition evaluation (Team ACVLab).

Conclusion: The approach yields consistent, albeit incremental, gains by combining temporal-context modeling with strong frame-level semantics and robust ensemble/fusion strategies; post-competition threshold tuning further boosts performance, indicating sensitivity to thresholding and potential gains from calibration.

Abstract: Capsule endoscopy event detection is challenging because clinically relevant findings are sparse, visually heterogeneous, and evaluated at the event level rather than by frame accuracy. We propose VISTA, a metric-aligned multi-backbone framework for the RAREVISION task. VISTA combines EndoFM-LV for temporal context and DINOv3 ViTL/16 for frame-level visual semantics, followed by a Diverse Head Ensemble (DHE), Validation-Guided Weighted Fusion (VGWF), and Anatomy-Aware Temporal Event Decoding (ATED). The original official submission achieved hidden-test temporal mAP@0.5 of 0.3530 and mAP@0.95 of 0.3235. After the competition, extending local threshold refinement with a global coarse search improved performance to 0.3726 mAP@0.5 and 0.3431 mAP@0.95, ranking Team ACVLab second in the post-competition evaluation.

</details>


### [59] [TextTeacher: What Can Language Teach About Images?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22098)
*Tobias Christian Nauen,Stanislav Frolov,Brian Bernhard Moser,Federico Raue,Ahmed Anwar,Andreas Dengel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TextTeacher adds a text-embedding auxiliary objective using frozen text encodings from captions to guide Vision Transformer training, improving ImageNet accuracy and transfer with minimal overhead; outperforms distillation and preserves inference simplicity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Emerging idea of shared representation geometry across modalities; question whether language-derived semantic knowledge can improve vision models efficiently.

Method: Introduce TextTeacher: for each image, obtain caption-derived text embedding via a frozen pre-trained text encoder and a lightweight projection to create semantic anchors; incorporate as extra supervision during image classification training; training-time only; no change to inference; uses existing captions and encoders; negligible overhead.

Result: On ImageNet with standard ViT backbones, accuracy gains up to 2.7 percentage points; average transfer gains +1.0 p.p.; outperforms vision knowledge distillation at equivalent compute and is 33% faster; analysis suggests acts as a feature-space preconditioner, shaping early layers and improving generalization.

Conclusion: TextTeacher offers a simple, scalable way to leverage language models to boost vision models without multimodal training or inference-time changes; potential for broader applicability and further exploration.

Abstract: The platonic representation hypothesis suggests that sufficiently large models converge to a shared representation geometry, even across modalities. Motivated by this, we ask: Can the semantic knowledge of a language model efficiently improve a vision model? As an answer, we introduce TextTeacher, a simple auxiliary objective that injects text embeddings as additional information into image classification training. TextTeacher uses readily available image captions, a pre-trained and frozen text encoder, and a lightweight projection to produce semantic anchors that efficiently guide representations during training while leaving the inference-time model unchanged. On ImageNet with standard ViT backbones, TextTeacher improves accuracy by up to +2.7 percentage points (p.p.) and yields consistent transfer gains (on average +1.0 p.p.) under the same recipe and compute. It outperforms vision knowledge distillation, yielding more accuracy at a constant compute budget or similar accuracy, but 33% faster. Our analysis indicates that TextTeacher acts as a feature-space preconditioner, shaping deeper layers in the first stages of training, and aiding generalization by supplying complementary semantic cues. TextTeacher adds negligible overhead, requires no costly multimodal training of the target model and preserves the simplicity and latency of pure vision models.
  Project page with code and captions: https://nauen-it.de/publications/text-teacher

</details>


### [60] [OPERA: An Agent for Image Restoration with End-to-End Joint Planning-Execution Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22104)
*Feng Zhu,Shuyang Xie,Yihan Zeng,Ming Liu,Wangmeng Zuo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OPERA jointly optimizes planning and execution for complex, real-world image restoration by RL-based planning over tool combinations and agent-guided cooperative tool training, outperforming single-model and prior agent-based approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional agent-based restoration suffers from restricted planning spaces and poor coordination among independently pretrained tools, limiting performance on real-world, multi-degradation scenarios.

Method: End-to-end framework with (1) RL-driven planning to compose tools in a combinatorial plan space using final restoration quality as reward, and (2) agent-guided co-training to foster cooperative tool usage during sequential execution.

Result: Extensive experiments on multi-degradation benchmarks and real-world data show OPERA consistently outperforms all-in-one restoration models and existing agent-based methods across diverse degradation settings.

Conclusion: Joint optimization of planning and execution along with cooperative tool learning yields robust, scalable restoration under complex degradations.

Abstract: Real-world image restoration is challenging due to complex and interacting mixed degradations. Recent agent-based approaches address this problem by composing multiple task-specific restoration tools. However, empirical analysis reveals that their performance is fundamentally limited by implicitly constrained planning spaces and the lack of coordination among independently pretrained tools. To address these issues, we propose OPERA (Optimized Planning-Execution Restoration Agent), a framework that jointly optimizes restoration planning and tool execution in an end-to-end manner. On the planning side, OPERA uses reinforcement learning to directly optimize tool composition over a combinatorial plan space, with the final restoration quality as the reward. On the execution side, OPERA introduces agent-guided co-training of restoration tools, enabling them to learn cooperative behaviors under sequential composition. Extensive experiments on multi-degradation benchmarks and real-world datasets demonstrate that OPERA consistently outperforms both all-in-one restoration models and existing agent-based methods across diverse and complex degradation scenarios.

</details>


### [61] [MotionDPS: Motion-Compensated 3D Brain MRI Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22121)
*Antonio Ortiz-Gonzalez,Erich Kobler,Lukas Schletter,Alexander Effland*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Unified Bayesian 3D MRI reconstruction with joint motion and coil estimation, using a pretrained complex-valued score-based diffusion prior; unsupervised, diffusion-based image updates with proximal steps; improves image quality under severe motion and high acceleration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MRI motion causes phase inconsistencies across k-space measurements, leading to artifacts (blur, ghosting, distortions). Retrospective motion compensation is ill-posed, especially in accelerated acquisitions.

Method: Joint Bayesian framework estimating image, rigid-body motion parameters, and coil sensitivity maps from motion-corrupted k-space data. Integrates a pretrained 3D complex-valued score-based diffusion model as an anatomical prior within a physics-based forward model. Inference via alternating diffusion-posterior image updates with efficient proximal optimization for motion and coil estimation, enabling fully unsupervised reconstruction without paired motion-free data.

Result: Experiments on simulated and real-motion brain MRI datasets show improved image quality and motion robustness compared to state-of-the-art classical and learning-based motion correction methods, particularly under severe motion and high acceleration.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effective integration of diffusion priors into a joint forward-model reconstruction for motion-compensated MRI, enabling unsupervised recovery with strong robustness; potential for clinical impact, though computationally intensive and reliant on quality diffusion priors.

Abstract: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is highly susceptible to patient motion due to its relatively long acquisition times and the fact that data are acquired sequentially in k-space. Even small patient movements introduce phase inconsistencies across measurements, leading to severe artifacts such as blurring, ghosting, and geometric distortions that can compromise diagnostic quality. Retrospective motion compensation remains challenging, particularly in accelerated acquisitions, due to the ill-posed nature of the joint reconstruction and motion estimation problem. In this work, we propose a unified Bayesian framework for motion-compensated 3D MRI that jointly estimates the anatomical image, rigid-body motion parameters, and coil sensitivity maps directly from motion-corrupted k-space data. Our approach integrates pretrained 3D complex-valued score-based diffusion models as expressive anatomical image priors within a physics-based forward model. Inference is performed by alternating diffusion posterior image updates with efficient proximal optimization steps for motion and coil sensitivity estimation, enabling fully unsupervised reconstruction without the need for paired motion-free training data. Experiments on simulated and real-motion brain MRI datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves improved image quality and motion robustness compared to state-of-the-art classical and learning-based motion correction techniques, particularly in the presence of severe motion and high acceleration.

</details>


### [62] [AesFormer: Transform Everyday Photos into Beautiful Memories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22126)
*Tianxiang Du,Hulingxiao He,Yuxin Peng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AesFormer introduces a two-stage, aesthetic-aware reconstruction framework for photos, decoupling aesthetic planning from editing, using AesThinker and AesEditor, plus a video-based corpus pipeline (VCMP) and AesRecon benchmark; it claims notable APR improvements and competitiveness with Nano Banana Pro, with code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a gap between semantically plausible edits and aesthetically improved results in APR. Current retouching methods struggle with structural flaws and lack explicit aesthetic understanding, while preserving identity and semantics.

Method: Stage 1: Aesthetic action model (AesThinker) analyzes input along seven progressive photographic dimensions and outputs executable actions; GRPO-A to promote broad exploration beyond supervised fine-tuning. Stage 2: Action-conditioned editor (AesEditor) applies structural edits guided by actions. VCMP mines video corpus; AesRecon provides 9,071 strictly aligned (poor, good) image pairs as a benchmark.

Result: Experiments show substantial APR performance gains and competitive results with Nano Banana Pro; code is released at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: Decoupling aesthetic planning from editing enables more meaningful, diverse edits that improve aesthetic quality while preserving identity/semantics; the VCMP pipeline and AesRecon benchmark offer resources for APR evaluation and progress in the field.

Abstract: In everyday photography, aesthetically appealing moments are often captured with structural flaws (e.g., composition, camera viewpoint, or pose) that existing retouching and portrait enhancement methods cannot fix. We formulate Aesthetic Photo Reconstruction (APR) as improving a photo's aesthetic quality via structural reconstruction while preserving subject identity and scene semantics. Although recent advances in image editing models make APR feasible, they often lack aesthetic understanding, yielding edits that are semantically plausible yet aesthetically weak. To address this, we propose AesFormer, a two-stage framework that decouples aesthetic planning from image editing. In Stage 1, an aesthetic action model (AesThinker) analyzes the input along seven progressive photographic dimensions and outputs executable editing actions; we further apply GRPO-A to encourage broad exploration over diverse action plans beyond SFT. In Stage 2, an action-conditioned editor (AesEditor) performs structural edits guided by these actions. To support APR, we build a video-based corpus-mining pipeline (VCMP) and construct AesRecon, a benchmark of 9,071 strictly aligned (poor, good) image pairs. Experiments show that AesFormer substantially improves APR performance and is competitive with Nano Banana Pro. Code is available at https://github.com/PKU-ICST-MIPL/AesFormer_ICML2026.

</details>


### [63] [Accelerating Vision Foundation Models with Drop-in Depthwise Convolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22132)
*Carmelo Scribano,Mohammad Mahdi,Nedyalko Prisadnikov,Yuqian Fu,Giorgia Franchini,Danda Pani Paudel,Marko Bertogna,Luc Van Gool*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Efficiently speeding up large vision transformers by replacing certain attention heads with depthwise convolution layers, guided by head-selection strategies and followed by fine-tuning to recover performance; yields ~17–20% inference speedup on classification and segmentation with minimal accuracy loss; implementation publicly available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision Transformer backbones are computationally expensive, hindering deployment on resource-constrained devices. The work aims to preserve feature extraction while reducing inference costs by exploiting convolution-like behavior in some attention heads.

Method: Introduce a drop-in depthwise convolution-based layer to substitute selected attention heads. Develop strategies to identify replacable heads. Apply a straightforward fine-tuning procedure to recover downstream performance. Validate across image classification and segmentation with efficiency benchmarks and provide reference implementation.

Result: Achieves 17–20% inference speedup with minimal performance degradation across classification and segmentation tasks. Supported by derivations, extensive experiments, and efficiency benchmarks; code is publicly available.

Conclusion: Replacing a subset of ViT attention heads with depthwise conv layers is a practical, effective way to accelerate large pretrained ViTs while preserving core feature extraction capabilities, enabling faster inference without substantial accuracy loss.

Abstract: Pretrained vision foundation models deliver strong performance across tasks with limited fine-tuning. However, their Vision Transformer (ViT) backbones impose high inference costs, limiting deployment on resource-constrained devices. In this work, we accelerate large-scale pretrained ViTs while preserving their feature extraction capabilities by exploiting the intrinsic convolution-like behavior of some attention heads. Specifically, we introduce an efficient depthwise convolution-based layer that serves as a drop-in replacement for these heads. Additionally, we propose simple strategies to identify which heads can be replaced and introduce a fine-tuning procedure that recovers downstream task performance. Across both image classification and segmentation tasks, our method achieves 17-20\% percent inference speedup with minimal performance degradation. We validate the approach through detailed derivations, extensive experiments, and efficiency benchmarks. The reference implementation is publicly available.

</details>


### [64] [EventGait: Towards Robust Gait Recognition with Event Streams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22139)
*Senyan Xu,Shuai Chen,Chuanfu Shen,Kean Liu,Zhijing Sun,Chengzhi Cao,Xueyang Fu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EventGait proposes an end-to-end dual-stream framework for event-based gait recognition. A dynamic stream uses Mixture of Spiking Experts for robust motion capture, while a static stream uses Cross-modal Structure Alignment with foundation models for dense shape. It introduces a synthesis pipeline and two benchmarks (SUSTech1K-E, CCGR-Mini-E), achieving SOTA and superior low-light performance, with code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gait recognition is sensitive to illumination and motion and is challenged in uncontrolled environments. Event cameras offer microsecond temporal resolution and high dynamic range, which can capture robust gait cues that conventional cameras miss. Existing event-based methods often discard fine-grained motion by aggregating events into long-term images.

Method: Proposes EventGait, a dual-stream architecture: (1) Dynamic stream using Mixture of Spiking Experts (MoSE) with diverse neuron constants to handle varying motion and illumination; (2) Static stream employing Cross-modal Structure Alignment (CroSA) to learn dense shape representations by leveraging large vision foundation models. Addresses data scarcity with a synthesis pipeline and introduces two benchmarks: SUSTech1K-E and CCGR-Mini-E.

Result: Extensive experiments show event-based gait recognition is competitive with camera-based methods under normal conditions and significantly outperforms them in low-light scenarios. The approach achieves state-of-the-art results on both synthesized and real-world event-based gait benchmarks.

Conclusion: Event-driven gait analysis is robust and promising for practical identification, especially in challenging lighting. The work provides a scalable framework and benchmark resources to advance the field, with released code and datasets.

Abstract: Gait recognition enables non-intrusive, privacy-preserving identification but suffers in uncontrolled environments due to illumination and motion sensitivity of conventional cameras. In this work, we explore gait recognition using event cameras, which offer microsecond temporal resolution and high dynamic range, naturally capturing robust dynamic cues and suppressing static noise. Existing event-based approaches typically aggregate event streams into event images over long time windows, thereby discarding fine-grained motion dynamics critical for gait recognition. Therefore, we propose \textbf{EventGait}, an end-to-end dual-stream framework that separately models motion and shape while preserving the advantages of events. Our dynamic stream leverages a Mixture of Spiking Experts (MoSE) with diverse neuron constants for robust dynamic perception across complex motion and illumination scenes, while the static stream learns dense shape representations via Cross-modal Structure Alignment (CroSA) with large vision foundation models. To address the absence of large-scale event-based gait datasets, we introduce a synthesis pipeline and release two new benchmarks: SUSTech1K-E and CCGR-Mini-E. Extensive experiments have shown that event-based gait recognition not only achieves results comparable to camera-based gait recognition under normal conditions but also significantly outperforms it in low-light scenarios. Our approach sets a new state of the art on both synthesized and real-world event-based gait benchmarks, highlighting the robustness and potential of event-driven gait analysis. The code and datasets are released at https://github.com/QUEAHREN/EventGait.

</details>


### [65] [One Sentence, One Drama: Personalized Short-Form Drama Generation via Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22144)
*Yufei Shi,Weilong Yan,Naixuan Huang,Yucheng Chen,Chenyu Zhang,Tao He,Si Yong Yeo,Ming Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A hierarchical, multi-agent framework converts a single user sentence into a produced short drama with pacing, spatial consistency, and production-grade quality control, aided by 3D-grounded initial frames, multi-stage reviewer loops, scene-level BGM/ transitions, and a dedicated Short-Drama-Bench benchmark; reported improvements over pipelines in narrative quality, cross-clip consistency, and viewing experience.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome three main limitations of existing short-drama generation pipelines: (i) weak narrative pacing and endings, (ii) drifting spatial layouts and inconsistency across clips, and (iii) lack of scalable production-quality control across script and visuals.

Method: A three-component architecture: (1) multi-agent debate-based story generation to enforce pacing and coherence; (2) 3D-grounded first-frame generation to establish shared spatial reference for consistent character positioning across clips; (3) multi-stage reviewer loops for error detection and targeted revisions across script, visuals, and video generation; additional scene-level BGM matching and transition planning; evaluation on Short-Drama-Bench with extended metrics.

Result: Experimental results indicate significant improvements over existing pipelines in narrative quality, cross-clip consistency, and overall viewing experience; Short-Drama-Bench provides a standardized, short-drama-specific evaluation framework.

Conclusion: The proposed One Sentence, One Drama framework enables end-to-end, production-like short drama generation from a single sentence through structured intermediate modules and iterative refinement, with a dedicated benchmark to quantify gains in narrative and perceptual quality.

Abstract: Existing approaches for digital short-drama production typically rely on one-shot LLM generated scripts and loosely coupled pipelines, which fail to satisfy three key requirements of short-drama generation: (1) narrative pacing, resulting in weak hooks, insufficient escalation, and unattractive endings; (2) spatial consistency, leading to drifting scene layouts and inconsistent character positions across clips; and (3) production-level quality control, requiring extensive manual review and correction across script and visual stages. We present One Sentence, One Drama, a hierarchical multi-agent framework that transforms a user's single-sentence idea into a fully produced short drama through structured intermediate modules and iterative refinement. Our approach is built upon three key components: (1) a multi-agent debate-based story generation module that enforces short-drama pacing and narrative coherence; (2) a 3D-grounded first-frame generation mechanism that establishes a shared spatial reference for consistent character positioning and scene layout across clips; and (3) multi-stage reviewer loops that perform comprehensive error detection and targeted revision across script, visual, and video generation stages. We also introduce scene-level BGM matching and scene transition planning to improve the audience's immersive experience. To systematically evaluate this task, we introduce Short-Drama-Bench, a benchmark that extends standard video quality metrics with short-drama-specific criteria. Experimental results demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing pipelines in narrative quality, cross-clip consistency, and overall viewing experience.

</details>


### [66] [Flow-based Gaussian Splatting for Continuous-Scale Remote Sensing Image Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22147)
*Jiangwei Mo,Xi Lu,Hanlin Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FlowGS offers arbitrary-scale SR for high-resolution remote sensing images by learning a continuous detail prior via flow matching and shortcut constraints, combined with 2D Gaussian splatting for a continuous feature field, delivering competitive perceptual quality with faster inference than diffusion-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses slow, iterative inference and limited continuous-scale capability of diffusion SR methods for RSIs.

Method: Models high-frequency detail as a continuous flow from noise to detail priors using flow matching with shortcut consistency; builds a continuous feature field via 2D Gaussian splatting to allow query at arbitrary locations.

Result: Achieves competitive perceptual SR against existing methods in both continuous-scale and fixed-scale settings, with substantially improved inference efficiency.

Conclusion: Introduces FlowGS, a generative reconstruction framework that enables efficient, flexible arbitrary-scale SR for RSIs by integrating flow-based priors with continuous feature fields.

Abstract: High-resolution remote sensing images (RSIs) are crucial for Earth observation applications, yet acquiring them is often limited by sensor constraints and costs. In recent years, generative super-resolution (SR) methods, particularly diffusion models, have made significant progress. However, they typically require slow iterative inference with 40--1000 steps and exhibit limited flexibility in continuous-scale SR settings. To address these issues, we propose FlowGS, a generative reconstruction framework for arbitrary-scale SR of RSIs. FlowGS models the high-frequency detail representations between high- and low-resolution images and learns a continuous probability flow from noise to detail priors via flow matching (FM) constrained by shortcut consistency, thereby reducing generative complexity and improving inference efficiency. Additionally, we employ 2D Gaussian splatting to construct a continuous feature field, thereby enabling flexible reconstruction at arbitrary query locations. Experimental results show that FlowGS delivers competitive perceptual quality compared with existing methods in both continuous-scale and fixed-scale SR settings, with substantially improved inference efficiency.

</details>


### [67] [Balancing Uncertainty and Diversity of Samples: Leveraging Diversity of Least, High Confidence Samples for Effective Active Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22169)
*Vipul Arya,S. H. Shabbeer Basha,Srikrishna U N,Sunainha Vijay,Snehasis Mukherjee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes four hybrid active-learning sampling methods that select both easy and hard, and diverse samples; Among them, LCD (Least Confident and Diverse) consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods by leveraging uncertainty and diversity; code to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce labeling cost for data-hungry deep learning by improving pool-based active learning; single-dimension sampling (uncertainty or diversity) may hurt performance.

Method: Introduce four novel hybrid sampling strategies that pair uncertainty with diversity to pool both easy and hard samples; evaluate using high- and low-confidence subsets; identify LCD as the strongest method.

Result: LCD consistently surpasses baseline/state-of-the-art methods across experiments; results indicate that combining uncertainty with diversity yields more informative samples and distinct feature learning.

Conclusion: Uncertainty plus diversity in sampling improves active learning efficiency; practical guidance to select uncertain and diverse instances for better feature learning; code will be available.

Abstract: Deep learning models, including Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs), have achieved state-of-the-art performance on various computer vision tasks such as object classification, detection, segmentation, generation, and many more. However, these models are data-hungry as they require more training data to learn millions or billions of parameters. Especially for supervised learning tasks, curating a large number of labeled samples for model training is an expensive and time-consuming task. Active Learning (AL) has been used to address this problem for many years. Existing active learning methods aim at choosing the samples for annotation from a pool of unlabeled samples that are either diverse or uncertain. Choosing such samples may hinder the model's performance as we pool based on one dimension, i.e., either diverse or uncertain. In this paper, we propose four novel hybrid sampling methods for pooling both easy and hard samples, which are also diverse. To verify the efficacy of the proposed methods, extensive experiments are conducted using high and low-confidence samples separately. We observe from our experiments that the proposed hybrid sampling method, Least Confident and Diverse (LCD), consistently performs better compared to state-of-the-art methods. It is observed that selecting uncertain and diverse instances helps the model learn more distinct features. The codes related to this study will be available at https://github.com/XXX/LCD.

</details>


### [68] [Enhancing Multimodal Large Language Models for Safety-Critical Driving Video Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22185)
*Tomaso Trinci,Henrique Piñeiro Monteagudo,Leonardo Taccari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Pipeline fuses downsampled video frames, high-frequency telematics, and CV-derived semantics to generate pseudo-labels for training MLLMs to recognize safety-critical events in driving; fine-tunes QwenVL-2.5 with DoRA adapters (<50M params) achieving improved SCE detection/explanation with limited compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs struggle with rare, high-stakes dynamic driving events; current perception limits hinder safety-critical reasoning. A robust training signal and parameter-efficient adaptation are needed to improve SCE understanding.

Method: Integrate downsampled video with synchronized IMU/GPS data and semantic CV outputs to produce high-quality pseudo-labels (descriptions and QA pairs) targeting SCEs. Fine-tune the open-source QwenVL-2.5 using DoRA adapters, keeping trainable parameters under ~50 million and constrained compute.

Result: Significant improvements in identifying and explaining safety-critical events; achieving these gains with a parameter-efficient setup (≤50M trainable parameters) and a limited compute budget.

Conclusion: The proposed pipeline efficiently enhances MLLM perception and reasoning for SCEs in real-world driving, via compact adapters and pseudo-label guided training.

Abstract: Recent advancements in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities in general visual understanding. However, their application to safety-critical driving scenarios remains limited by an inability to accurately perceive and reason about rare high-stakes dynamic events, such as collisions or near-collisions. To address this, we introduce a pipeline that enhances MLLM perception by fusing downsampled video frames with synchronized high-frequency telematics data (IMU and GPS) and semantic insights from specialized computer vision models. Our pipeline generates high-quality pseudo-labels, including descriptive captions and question-answer pairs, specifically designed to train MLLMs to identify and describe Safety-Critical Events (SCEs) in real-world driving footage. We show the effectiveness of our approach fine-tuning the open-source QwenVL-2.5 model via DoRA adapters: our experiments demonstrate significant improvements in identifying and explaining safety-critical events, with fewer than 50M trainable parameters and limited computational budget.

</details>


### [69] [Event-Illumination Collaborative Low-light Image Enhancement with a High-resolution Real-world Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22186)
*Senyan Xu,Zhijing Sun,Kean Liu,Xin Lu,Ruixuan Jiang,Mingyang Huang,Xueyang Fu,Zheng-Jun Zha*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes EIC-LIE, a framework for event-based low-light image enhancement that integrates illumination-aware collaboration and noise filtering, plus a synchronized event-image dataset collected with a beam-splitter system, achieving state-of-the-art gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current event-based LIE methods overemphasize HDR information from events while neglecting global illumination context and are sensitive to event noise in real-world scenes; a solution requires illumination-aware integration and noise-robust processing.

Method: Introduce Event-Illumination Collaborative Interaction (EICI) with forward gathering (HDR feature gathering across lighting conditions) and backward injection (complementary illumination and event representations). Develop Illumination-aware Event Filter (IAEF) to dynamically suppress event noise using image brightness statistics. Build a beam-splitter-based hybrid imaging system to capture synchronized high-resolution event-image pairs and construct a real-world LIE dataset.

Result: Extensive experiments on five real-world and synthetic datasets show state-of-the-art performance, with up to 1.24 dB PSNR and 0.069 SSIM gains over prior methods; code and dataset released.

Conclusion: EIC-LIE effectively leverages illumination collaboration and noise-aware event processing to enhance low-light images from events, and provides a high-quality real-world LIE dataset for future research.

Abstract: Event-based low-light image enhancement (LIE) methods mainly focus on incorporating high dynamic range (HDR) information from events while overlooking the essential global illumination in images and the inherent noise sensitivity of event signals in real-world scenarios. To address these issues, we propose EIC-LIE, an event-illumination collaborative LIE framework. Concretely, we first design an Event-Illumination Collaborative Interaction (EICI) module, which contains two key processes: forward gathering, which gathers HDR features across varying lighting conditions, and backward injection, which provides complementary content for illumination and event representations. Next, we introduce an Illumination-aware Event Filter (IAEF) that dynamically reduces event noise based on brightness statistics derived from images. Additionally, we build a beam-splitter-based hybrid imaging system to collect high-quality event-image pairs with temporal synchronization from dynamic scenes, providing the first high-resolution, real-world event-based LIE dataset. Extensive experiments show that our EIC-LIE outperforms state-of-the-art methods on five real-world and synthetic datasets, significantly surpassing previous methods with improvements of up to 1.24dB in PSNR and 0.069 in SSIM. The code and dataset are released at https://github.com/QUEAHREN/EIC-LIE.

</details>


### [70] [No Pose, No Problem in 4D: Feed-Forward Dynamic Gaussians from Unposed Multi-View Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22190)
*Matteo Balice,Yanik Kunzi,Chenyangguang Zhang,Matteo Matteucci,Marc Pollefeys,Sungwhan Hong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NoPo4D is a fast, fully feed-forward 4D Gaussian splatting method that reconstructs dynamic scenes from multi-view input with unknown camera poses by decomposing motion into per-pixel image-plane shifts and depth changes, supervised by pseudo optical flow, augmented with a bidirectional motion encoder and view-dependent opacity; it achieves state-of-the-art speed and competitive quality, surpassing per-scene optimization with optional post-optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches either require known camera poses, are monocular, handle only static scenes, or rely on slow per-scene optimization; there is no single feed-forward pipeline addressing dynamics, multi-view input, and pose-free operation.

Method: Proposes velocity decomposition into 2D flow-like image-plane shifts and depth changes; uses pseudo ground-truth optical flow for 2D component supervision; introduces a bidirectional motion encoder to aggregate features across views and frames; uses view-dependent opacity to reduce misalignment; builds on a pretrained geometry backbone and 4D Gaussian framework; optional post-optimization stage.

Result: Empirically, on four multi-view dynamic benchmarks, NoPo4D outperforms prior feed-forward baselines; with post-optimization, surpasses per-scene optimization methods while remaining orders of magnitude faster.

Conclusion: NoPo4D closes the gap for fast, pose-free, dynamic, multi-view 3D reconstruction in a single feed-forward pass, offering strong accuracy and speed and enabling practical deployment; post-optimization further narrows the gap to optimization-based methods.

Abstract: Recent feed-forward 3D gaussian splatting methods have made dramatic progress on individual aspects of 3D scene reconstruction, but no existing method jointly addresses dynamic content, multi-view input, and unknown camera poses in a single feed-forward pass. Methods that handle dynamics either require accurate camera poses or accept only monocular input; pose-free multi-view methods address only static scenes; and per-scene optimization methods bridge some of these gaps but at minutes-to-hours cost per scene. We introduce NoPo4D, the first feed-forward system that addresses this empty quadrant. Building on a pretrained geometry backbone and recent 4D Gaussian frameworks, NoPo4D introduces a velocity decomposition that splits Gaussian motion into per-pixel image-plane shifts and depth changes, allowing direct supervision from pseudo ground-truth optical flow on the 2D component. This sidesteps both the differentiable rendering that couples prior posed methods to pose accuracy and the 3D motion ground truth that prior pose-free methods require. The system is rounded out by a bidirectional motion encoder for cross-view and cross-frame feature aggregation, and view-dependent opacity that mitigates cross-view and cross-timestep Gaussian misalignments. On four multi-view dynamic benchmarks, NoPo4D consistently outperforms prior feed-forward baselines, and with an optional post-optimization stage surpasses per-scene optimization methods, while running orders of magnitude faster.

</details>


### [71] [Ultra-High-Definition Image Quality Assessment via Graph Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22192)
*Shaode Yu,Enqi Chen,Ming Huang,Xuemin Ren,Songnan Zhao,Zhicheng Zhang,Qiurui Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A graph-based UHD BIQA method (UHD-GCN-BIQA) models region dependencies via residual graph convolution over patches to predict image quality, achieving competitive correlation and low RMSE on UHD-IQA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UHD images are computationally heavy for native-resolution BIQA; aggressive resizing or cropping can suppress distortions and break region-context relationships. Explicitly modeling inter-region dependencies can improve score accuracy.

Method: Sample aspect-ratio-aligned patches from UHD images and encode them as graph nodes. Build a hybrid kNN graph using spatial proximity and feature similarity. Apply residual graph convolution to propagate contextual information across regions. Use gated attention pooling to aggregate patch-level evidence into an image-level quality score. Train with an exponential moving average normalized multiobjective loss that jointly optimizes regression, correlation, and ranking objectives.

Result: On the UHD-IQA benchmark, UHD-GCN-BIQA achieves PLCC=0.7784, SRCC=0.8019, RMSE=0.0519, showing competitive correlation and the lowest RMSE among compared methods.

Conclusion: Graph-based region relation modeling is effective for UHD BIQA and notably improves absolute quality score estimation on high-resolution content.

Abstract: Blind image quality assessment (BIQA) for ultrahighdefinition (UHD) images remains challenging because native-resolution inference is computationally expensive, whereas aggressive resizing or isolated cropping may suppress scale-sensitive distortions and weaken the relationship between local artifacts and global scene context. This paper aims to improve UHD-BIQA by explicitly modeling the structural dependencies among sampled image regions rather than treating them as independent views, and a graph representation learning framework UHD-GCN-BIQA is proposed. The framework samples aspect-ratio-aligned patches from each UHD image, encodes them as graph nodes, and constructs a hybrid k-nearest-neighbor graph using spatial proximity and feature similarity. Residual graph convolution is used to propagate contextual information across regions, and gated attention pooling aggregates patchlevel evidence into an imagelevel quality prediction. An exponential moving average normalized multiobjective loss function is adopted to stabilize the joint optimization of regression, correlation, and ranking objectives. Experiments on the UHD-IQA benchmark show that UHD-GCN-BIQA achieves PLCC = 0.7784, SRCC = 0.8019, and RMSE = 0.0519, obtaining competitive correlation performance and the lowest RMSE among the compared methods. These results indicate that graph-based region relation modeling is effective for UHD image quality assessment, particularly for improving absolute quality score estimation under high-resolution visual content.

</details>


### [72] [OSS: Open Suturing Skills Vision-Based Assessment Challenge 2024-2025](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22200)
*Hanna Hoffmann,Setareh Bady,Claas de Boer,Max Kirchner,Jan Egger,Rainer Röhrig,Frank Hölzle,Lennart Johannes Gruber,Kunpeng Xie,Marlon Neuhaus,Victor Alves,Guilherme Barbosa,Leonardo Barroso,João Carvalho,Hao Chen,Gabriella d'Albenzio,André Ferreira,Nuno Gomes,Yuichiro Hayashi,Kousuke Hirasawa,Rebecca Hisey,Seungjae Hong,Seoi Jeong,Tiago Jesus,Daehong Kang,Satoshi Kasai,Shunsuke Kikuchi,Takayuki Kitasaka,Satoshi Kondo,Hyoun-Joong Kong,Youngbin Kong,Atsushi Kouno,Shlomi Laufer,Kyu Eun Lee,Bining Long,Nooshin Maghsoodi,Hiroki Matsuzaki,Evangelos Mazomenos,Ori Meiraz,Kensaku Mori,Marina Music,Masahiro Oda,Roi Papo,Jieun Park,Rafael Piexoto,Saeid Rezaei,Mariana Ribeiro,Soyeon Shin,Yang Shu,Idan Smoller,Danail Stoyanov,Yihui Wang,Xinkai Zhao,Sebastian Bodenstedt,Isabel Funke,Stefanie Speidel,Behrus Hinrichs-Puladi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Benchmark study on video- and tracking-based skill assessment for open surgery via the OSS Challenge; spatiotemporal video models perform best, OSATS prediction is still difficult, and tracking suffers from occlusions; dataset and tasks aim to advance clinical-impact automated assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in automated skill assessment for open surgery by providing a dedicated benchmark (MICCAI OSS Challenge) and a dataset to accelerate data-driven methods toward clinical impact.

Method: Dataset from open suturing tasks recorded with a GoPro in a dry-lab; trajectories available; two challenges across two and three years with three tasks: (1) four-class skill classification, (2) predicting eight OSATS categories, (3) hand/tool tracking. Participants used deep learning video models, tracking-driven, and hybrid approaches. General spatiotemporal video models yielded strongest results; tracking faced occlusion/out-of-frame issues; more training data improved OSATS prediction.

Result: General-purpose spatiotemporal video models dominated performance; performance improved with more training data for OSATS; fine-grained OSATS scores remain challenging; keypoint tracking hampered by occlusion and out-of-frame instances, limiting motion-based skill analysis.

Conclusion: The study provides a comprehensive benchmark and demonstrates both the promise and current limitations of video-based skill assessment in open surgery; highlights directions for advancement toward clinical impact, including data availability, robust tracking, and integrated approaches.

Abstract: Achieving high levels of surgical skill through effective training is essential for optimal patient outcomes. Automated, data-driven skill assessment holds significant potential to improve surgical training. While machine learning-based methods are increasingly popular for assessing skills in minimally invasive surgery, their application to open surgery remains limited. We present the results of a dedicated MICCAI challenge designed to benchmark and advance vision-based skill assessment in open surgery.
  The challenge dataset comprises videos of an open suturing training task recorded with a static GoPro camera in a dry-lab setting, with instrument trajectories available in addition to the primary video modality. The OSS Challenge was hosted over two consecutive years, comprising two and three independent tasks, respectively: (1) classifying skill level into four classes, (2) predicting the full Objective Structured Assessment of Technical Skills across eight categories, and (3) tracking hands and surgical tools. Participants submitted diverse solutions including deep learning-based video models, tracking-driven methods, and hybrid approaches.
  General-purpose spatiotemporal video models consistently achieved the strongest performance, though conceptually diverse approaches reached competitive levels when well-executed. Predicting fine-grained OSATS scores remains challenging but benefits substantially from increased training data. Keypoint tracking proves difficult given frequent occlusions and out-of-frame instances, limiting current applicability for motion-based skill analysis. This work benchmarks innovative and diverse solutions for surgical skill assessment, highlighting both the promise and current limitations of video-based evaluation in open surgery and identifying critical directions for advancing automated skill assessment toward clinical impact.

</details>


### [73] [Zero-Shot Temporal Action Localization Through Textual Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22201)
*Benedetta Liberatori,Alessandro Conti,Lorenzo Vaquero,Paolo Rota,Yiming Wang,Elisa Ricci*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TEGU introduces textual guidance from large language models and captions to enable finer, zero-shot temporal action localization, reducing reliance on labeled data and achieving state-of-the-art performance among non-training-based ZS-TAL on THUMOS14 and ActivityNet-v1.3.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-shot temporal action localization suffers from fine-grained discrimination challenges and weak transfer when relying solely on visual cues. Large labeled video datasets are costly and may not generalize well; there is a need for data-free or data-light approaches that leverage external textual information to improve localization.

Method: TEGU leverages textual information from large language models and structured caption text as auxiliary cues to refine localization without training data. This textual guidance is integrated into the ZS-TAL pipeline to enhance fine-grained discrimination between action presence and absence.

Result: Empirical results on THUMOS14 and ActivityNet-v1.3 show that TEGU outperforms state-of-the-art ZS-TAL methods that do not involve training, demonstrating the benefit of rich textual information for zero-shot fine-grained localization.

Conclusion: Incorporating rich textual context from LMs and captions can substantially improve fine-grained zero-shot temporal action localization, reducing dependence on annotated data and improving generalization on standard benchmarks.

Abstract: Zero-shot temporal action localization (ZS-TAL) consists of classifying and localizing actions in untrimmed videos, where action classes are unseen at training time. Existing work uses Vision and Language Models (VLMs), taking advantage of their strong zero-shot transfer capabilities. Yet, these models face evident challenges with fine-grained action classification, making it difficult to directly use them to distinguish between the presence and absence of an action. Most current methods for ZS-TAL address these challenges by training models on large-scale video datasets, which require annotated data and often result in limited generalization performance. Recently, approaches discarding the use of labeled data have emerged as an alternative. Following this direction, we propose a novel approach, ``Textual Guidance for finer localization of actions in videos'' (TEGU), that compensates for the lack of supervision from training data by exploiting rich textual information derived from large language models and structured text extracted from captions. This additional linguistic context can improve fine-grained discrimination by providing richer cues about fine-grained action differences within videos. We validate the effectiveness of the proposed method by conducting experiments on the THUMOS14 and the ActivityNet-v1.3 datasets. Our results show that, by exploiting rich textual information for improved action localization, TEGU outperforms state-of-the-art ZS-TAL approaches that do not involve training

</details>


### [74] [EvoIR-Agent: Self-Evolving Image Restoration Agentic System via Experience-Driven Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22208)
*Kailin Zhuang,Jiawei Wu,Zhi Jin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EvoIR-Agent introduces a training-free, self-evolving, hierarchical experience pool to guide MLLM-based image restoration, achieving better full-reference metrics and Pareto-optimal efficiency-compliance than state-of-the-art methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome zero-shot planning failures and high trial-and-error overhead in MLLM-driven restoration by bridging the gap between training-based (efficient but inflexible) and training-free (flexible but costly) approaches.

Method: Formulate the experience components of a training-free agent, build a hierarchical experience pool for coarse-to-fine guidance across tools and deinstallation orders, and implement a self-evolving mechanism that reinitializes the pool from accumulated records to continuously improve performance and efficiency.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows EvoIR-Agent achieves leading performance on full-reference metrics and exhibits a Pareto-optimal balance between restoration quality and computational efficiency compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: EvoIR-Agent effectively resolves the training-free vs training-based dilemma in MLLM-driven image restoration, delivering superior adaptability and efficiency through a self-evolving, hierarchical experience framework.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM)-driven image restoration agent demonstrates effectiveness in degradation coupling scenarios by flexibly selecting tools and determining removal orders. However, their zero-shot planning often fails without experience, necessitating severe trial-and-error overhead to achieve satisfactory outcomes. Currently, two paradigms are employed to address this issue, yet a dilemma persists: Training-based methods embed intrinsic experience into parameters, achieving high inference efficiency but lacking compatibility with new tools or degradation. In contrast, training-free methods utilize explicit experience storage for compatibility but still incur trial-and-error overhead due to naive experience. To resolve the dilemma, we propose EvoIR-Agent, which first systematically formulates the experience components of a training-free image restoration agent. Subsequently, a hierarchical experience pool is constructed, which enables coarse-to-fine guidance for diverse tools and removal orders. Furthermore, a self-evolving mechanism is introduced to update the pool from scratch using accumulated records, thereby greatly improving performance and efficiency. Extensive experiments reveal that EvoIR-Agent achieves a significant lead in the full reference metrics and yields a remarkable Pareto-optimal balance between performance and efficiency compared to the state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [75] [GALAR-TemporalNet v2: Anatomy-Guided Dual-Branch Temporal Classification with Bidirectional Mamba and Dual-Graph GCN for Video Capsule Endoscopy -- after competition results](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22209)
*Jiye Won,Seangmin Lee,Soon Ki Jung*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GALAR-TemporalNet v2 introduces a hierarchical temporal architecture for multi-label vascular endoscopy frame classification, tackling extreme class imbalance, long-range dependencies, and pathology–anatomy entanglement, with substantial performance gains over the competition baseline on RARE-VISION.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video Capsule Endoscopy yields tens of thousands of frames with 8 anatomy regions and 9 pathologies, producing a highly imbalanced and entangled multi-label temporal classification problem requiring both local and global temporal reasoning.

Method: A three-branch hierarchical model: windowed self-attention for local temporal modeling, Dual-Graph GCN for global frame relations, and Bidirectional Mamba for boundary-context encoding; an anatomy prototype residual pathway to decouple pathology deviations from normal anatomy; frame-level GCN skip connections to stabilize training of rare confusable classes; redesigned pathology branch, refined losses, and extended post-processing in v2.

Result: Competition GALAR-TemporalNet achieved mAP@0.5 = 0.2644 and mAP@0.95 = 0.2353 on RARE-VISION. Post-change GALAR-TemporalNet v2 improved to mAP@0.5 = 0.3409 and mAP@0.95 = 0.3333.

Conclusion: The v2 design effectively mitigates the three core challenges, delivering notable improvements in both overall and high-precision mAP metrics on a challenging medical-VCE benchmark.

Abstract: Video Capsule Endoscopy (VCE) poses a challenging multi-label temporal classification problem, requiring simultaneous localization of 8 anatomical regions and detection of 9 pathological findings across tens of thousands of frames. We present GALAR-TemporalNet v2, a hierarchical temporal model that addresses three core challenges: extreme class imbalance, long-range temporal dependencies, and pathology--anatomy entanglement.
