*Xingtian Pei,Yukun Song,Changwei Wang,Shunpeng Chen,Rongtao Xu,Shibiao Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a hierarchical Perception-to-Deliberation Framework (PDF) for zero-shot compositional image retrieval, introducing Intent Routing Manager and a Decision Manager with training-free reasoning policy distillation and tournament-style test-time scaling (TTS); reports SOTA on CIRR, CIRCO, and FashionIQ with self-evolution dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle Perception Myopia in a single-representation space and Logic Drift during iterative retrieval by enabling dynamic, multi-view perception and self-evolving reasoning at test time.

Method: A hierarchical, multi-agent architecture: (1) Intent Routing Manager dynamically routes multi-view perception signals to workers to create a high-recall candidate pool; (2) Decision Manager combines Training-free Reasoning Policy Distillation with a Tournament-style TTS strategy to produce refined retrieval results and enable self-evolution.

Result: Empirical results demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on CIRR, CIRCO, and FashionIQ datasets.

Conclusion: Experience-driven self-evolution and Test-Time Scaling (TTS) constitute a scalable and promising approach for zero-shot, fine-grained multimedia retrieval; code will be released upon acceptance.

Abstract: Zero-Shot Compositional Image Retrieval (ZS-CIR) requires both preserving the visual continuity of the reference image and faithfully executing the semantic variables specified in the modification text, which constitutes the core challenge of the task. Existing methods often suffer from Perception Myopia in a single space, or fall into Logic Drift in iterative collaboration due to the perception ceiling of the underlying retriever. To address this issue, we propose a one-stop hierarchical Perception-to-Deliberation Framework (PDF), which, to the best of our knowledge, is the first to introduce experience self-evolution and Test-Time Scaling Law (TTS) into ZS-CIR. Relying on a hierarchical multi-agent architecture, PDF first utilizes an Intent Routing Manager to dynamically dispatch multi-view Worker perception signals based on modification intents to construct a high-recall candidate pool. Subsequently, the Decision Manager combines a Training-free Reasoning Policy Distillation mechanism with a Tournament-style TTS strategy to achieve self-evolving fine-grained reasoning, yielding the final retrieval results. Experimental results demonstrate that PDF achieves SOTA performance on three benchmark datasets: CIRR, CIRCO, and FashionIQ. This study indicates that experience-driven self-evolution and TTS represent a highly promising and scalable path for achieving zero-shot fine-grained multimedia retrieval. The code will be made publicly available upon acceptance.

</details>


### [102] [Supervised Classification Heads as Semantic Prototypes: Unlocking Vision-Language Alignment via Weight Recycling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22484)
*David Méndez,Roberto Confalonieri,Natalia Díaz Rodríguez*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Repurpose pretrained vision-model classification heads as semantic prototypes to enable zero-shot alignment and data augmentation, boosting cross-modal retrieval and zero-/few-shot classification when combined with post-hoc alignment methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the data and training cost of end-to-end VLMs; reduce paired data needs; leverage discarded pretraining weights as semantic anchors and augmentation to improve post-hoc alignment.

Method: Extract weights from pretrained classification heads; interpret as semantic prototypes; use as anchors for zero-shot alignment; mix prototypes with real image-text pairs for augmentation; evaluate by integrating with several post-hoc alignment techniques.

Result: Consistent accuracy gains in cross-modal retrieval and zero-/few-shot classification across multiple post-hoc alignment baselines.

Conclusion: Prototypical recycling of classifier weights is an effective, lightweight strategy to enhance VLM alignment and can complement existing post-hoc methods.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) excel at tasks like zero-shot classification and cross-modal retrieval by mapping images and text to a shared space, but this requires expensive end-to-end training with massive paired datasets. Current post-hoc alignment methods reduce computational costs by connecting pretrained encoders through lightweight mappings, yet still demand substantial paired data. In this work, we investigate the potential of repurposing the classification heads of pretrained vision models as semantic prototypes. The recycling of these weights, typically discarded after pretraining, unlocks two distinct capabilities: it enables zero-shot alignment by using weights as semantic anchors, and serves as a robust data augmentation strategy by mixing these prototypes with real image-text pairs. We demonstrate that integrating our approach with several state-of-the-art post-hoc alignment techniques consistently boosts accuracy in cross-modal retrieval, zero- and few-shot classification tasks.

</details>


### [103] [Training-Free Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentations in Low Data Regimes: A FungiTastic Baseline](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22492)
*Sebastian Cavada,Francesco Pelosin,Lapo Faggi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free, two-stage framework (SAM3 + DINOv3) for fine-grained semantic segmentation in low-data fungal imagery, decoupling segmentation and classification via prototype matching; scalable and low-cost compared to class-specific prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve precise localization and discrimination in fine-grained segmentation under long-tailed distributions and variable image acquisition, particularly for fungi.

Method: Stage 1 SAM3 outputs class-agnostic mushroom masks using macro-taxonomic prompts; Stage 2 DINOv3 assigns fine-grained labels through prototype matching in embedding space; apply a simple transformation to the DINOv3 feature space to boost prototype-based classification; compare to class-specific prompting; evaluate from one-shot to few-hundred-shot regimes.

Result: Demonstrates effectiveness across data regimes from one-shot to hundreds-shot; provides a first baseline for fine-grained semantic segmentation in low-data settings; shows improved prototype-based classification and scalable, low-cost segmentation.

Conclusion: A training-free, two-stage approach yields scalable, data-efficient fine-grained segmentation by decoupling segmentation and classification, with strong potential for broader applicability beyond fungi.

Abstract: Fine-grained semantic segmentation requires both precise localization and discrimination between visually similar classes. In FungiTastic, this problem is further complicated by a long-tailed distribution and strong variation in image acquisition conditions. We propose a training-free two-stage framework that decouples segmentation from classification. SAM3 first produces class-agnostic mushroom masks using macro-taxonomic prompts, and DINOv3 then assigns fine-grained labels through prototype matching in the embedding space. To improve this stage, we apply a simple transformation of the DINOv3 feature space that improves prototype-based classification. Compared with class-specific prompting, our approach is more scalable and keeps the segmentation cost low. We report results from one-shot to few-hundred-shot regimes, providing, to the best of our knowledge, the first baseline for fine-grained semantic segmentation in low-data settings.

</details>


### [104] [SpaceDG: Benchmarking Spatial Intelligence under Visual Degradation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22536)
*Xiaolong Zhou,Yifei Liu,Ziyang Gong,Jiarui Li,Qiyue Zhao,Muyao Niu,Yuanyuan Gao,Le Ma,Xue Yang,Hongjie Zhang,Zhihang Zhong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SpaceDG provides a degradation-aware spatial reasoning dataset and benchmark for MLLMs, revealing robustness gaps under degraded visuals and showing degradation-aware finetuning improves performance without harming clean-image accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing spatial reasoning benchmarks assume pristine visuals and neglect real-world degradations; assessing and improving MLLMs’ robustness to degraded observations is essential for reliable deployment.

Method: Introduce SpaceDG with a physically grounded degradation synthesis engine integrated into 3D Gaussian Splatting rendering to simulate nine degradation types; generate ~1M QA pairs from ~1,000 indoor scenes. Create SpaceDG-Bench with 1,102 questions across 11 reasoning categories and 9 degradation types (over 10K VQA instances). Evaluate 25 open- and closed-source MLLMs; perform degradation-aware finetuning on SpaceDG.

Result: Visual degradations consistently and substantially degrade spatial reasoning across MLLMs. Finetuning on SpaceDG markedly improves degradation robustness and can exceed human performance under degraded conditions, with no loss of performance on clean images.

Conclusion: Degradation-aware training holds strong promise for robust spatial intelligence in MLLMs; SpaceDG serves as a valuable resource to study and enhance robustness to real-world visual degradations.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have made rapid progress in spatial intelligence, yet existing spatial reasoning benchmarks largely assume pristine visual inputs and overlook the degradations that commonly occur in real-world deployment, such as motion blur, low light, adverse weather, lens distortion, and compression artifacts. This raises a fundamental question: how robust is the spatial intelligence of current MLLMs when visual observations are imperfect? To answer this question, we introduce SpaceDG, the first large-scale dataset for degradation-aware spatial understanding. It is constructed with a physically grounded degradation synthesis engine that embeds degradation formation process into 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) rendering, enabling realistic simulation of nine degradation types. The resulting dataset contains approximately 1M QA pairs from nearly 1,000 indoor scenes. We further introduce SpaceDG-Bench, an human-verified benchmark with 1,102 questions spanning 11 reasoning categories and 9 visual degradation types, yielding over 10K VQA instances. Evaluating 25 open- and closed-source MLLMs reveals that visual degradations consistently and substantially impair spatial reasoning, exposing a critical robustness gap. Finally, we show that finetuning on SpaceDG markedly improves degradation robustness and can even surpass human performance under degraded conditions without any performance drop on clean images, highlighting the promise of degradation-aware training for robust spatial intelligence.

</details>


### [105] [Segment Anything with Motion, Geometry, and Semantic Adaptation for Complex Nonlinear Visual Object Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22538)
*Deyi Zhu,Yuji Wang,Yong Liu,Yansong Tang,Bingyao Yu,Jiwen Lu,Jie Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAMOSA enhances SAM 2-based visual object tracking by injecting explicit motion, geometry, and semantic cues to handle complex VOT scenarios, achieving better generalization and robustness than existing SAM 2-based and supervised trackers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SAM 2 provides strong video priors but lacks explicit modeling of target motion dynamics and cross-frame consistency; this limits tracking under distractors, occlusion, and nonlinear motion.

Method: Introduce a lightweight nonlinear motion predictor to model dynamics and guide mask selection and memory filtering; use semantic cues to detect shifts and recover from failures; integrate geometric constraints as structural regularization; unify implicit priors with explicit tracking components.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art SAM 2-based trackers on general benchmarks; shows stronger generalization than supervised VOT methods; achieves notable gains on anti-UAV datasets; code available.

Conclusion: SAMOSA bridges SAM 2's latent video understanding with explicit tracking-oriented modeling, improving robustness and generalization in VOT.

Abstract: Traditional visual object tracking (VOT) methods typically rely on task-specific supervised training, limiting their generalization to unseen objects and challenging scenarios with distractors, occlusion, and nonlinear motion. Recent vision foundation models, exemplified by SAM 2, learn strong video understanding priors from large-scale pretraining and offer a promising foundation for building more robust and generalizable trackers. However, directly applying SAM 2 to VOT remains suboptimal, as it does not explicitly model target motion dynamics or enforce geometric and semantic consistency across frames, both of which are essential for reliable tracking. To address this issue, we propose SAMOSA, a new tracking framework that adapts SAM 2 to complex VOT scenarios by explicitly leveraging motion, geometry, and semantic cues. Specifically, we introduce a lightweight nonlinear motion predictor to model target dynamics and guide mask selection as well as memory filtering. We further exploit semantic cues to detect target shifts and recover from tracking failures, while geometric cues are incorporated as structural constraints to improve tracking stability. In this way, SAMOSA bridges the gap between the implicit video understanding prior of SAM 2 and explicit tracking-oriented modeling. Extensive experiments show that SAMOSA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art SAM 2--based approaches on general benchmarks, demonstrates stronger generalization than supervised VOT methods, and achieves substantial gains on anti-UAV datasets, which typify complex nonlinear motion scenarios. Our code is available at https://github.com/DurYi/SAMOSA.

</details>


### [106] [Case-Aware Medical Image Classification with Multimodal Knowledge Graphs and Reliability-Guided Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22547)
*Yiming Xu,Yixuan Liu,Yuhang Zhang,Ling Zheng,Yihan Wang,Qi Song*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multimodal knowledge-graph–based, case-aware reasoning framework (MKG-CARE) for explainable medical image diagnosis that retrieves similar cases, propagates knowledge with a graph attention network, injects semantics via cross-modal attention, and performs confidence-calibrated refinement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical diagnosis benefits from historical similar cases and external knowledge; existing DL methods mostly rely on isolated visual evidence and fail to leverage related samples or knowledge, limiting accuracy and explainability.

Method: Construct a multimodal knowledge graph from adaptively retrieved similar cases; use an image-centric Graph Attention Network to propagate knowledge and derive case-based features; apply a bidirectional cross-modal attention to inject these features into visual representations for cross-modal alignment; implement a confidence-calibrated decision refinement that jointly evaluates prediction confidence and case similarity to weight each retrieved case and provide interpretable evidence.

Result: Experiments on multiple medical imaging datasets show consistent improvements over strong baselines; ablation studies validate each component's contribution; code released.

Conclusion: The framework enables effective use of historical cases and external knowledge for explainable, accurate medical image diagnosis, with interpretable case-level evidence and adaptable retrieval reliability.

Abstract: Deep learning has brought significant progress to medical image classification, yet most existing methods still rely on isolated visual evidence and cannot effectively leverage similar cases or external knowledge. In clinical practice, diagnosis is typically supported by historical similar cases and their associated symptoms. To simulate this diagnostic process, we propose a framework that performs case-aware reasoning using multimodal knowledge graphs for explainable medical image diagnosis. Given an input image, our method constructs a multimodal knowledge graph from adaptively retrieved similar cases, enabling more effective utilization of related samples. We further introduce a knowledge propagation and injection mechanism, where an image-centric Graph Attention Network propagates knowledge semantics to obtain case-based features, followed by a bidirectional cross-modal attention mechanism that injects these features into visual representations for cross-modal alignment. To mitigate noisy retrieval, we design a confidence-calibrated decision refinement scheme that estimates the reliability of each retrieved case by jointly considering prediction confidence and sample similarity, adaptively adjusting its contribution to the final prediction and providing interpretable case-level evidence. Extensive experiments on multiple medical imaging datasets show that our approach consistently outperforms strong baselines, and ablation studies validate the effectiveness of each component. The source code is publicly available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/MKG-CARE-8B7B.

</details>


### [107] [MOTOR: A Multimodal Dataset for Two-Wheeler Rider Behavior Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22550)
*Varun A. Paturkar,Shankar Gangisetty,C. V. Jawahar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MOTOR is a large-scale, multimodal dataset for two-wheeler rider behavior, featuring 1,629 sequences (25+ hours) across 16 riders with synchronized front/rear/helmet video, rider eye-gaze, on-road audio, and telemetry. It includes rich annotations for traffic context, rider state, 12 maneuvers, and legality labels (Legal/Illegal/Unspecified). Baselines using CNN/Transformer video backbones with multimodal fusion show that combining RGB, gaze, and telemetry yields the best performance. Provides a benchmark for behavior analysis, legality-aware prediction, and ITS; dataset and code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a critical gap in two-wheeler rider behavior datasets and multimodal resources, hindering advances in safety-critical AI and ADAS for dense, unstructured traffic common in the Global South. A large, diverse dataset with rich modalities is needed to study rider behavior and legality in realistic conditions.

Method: Data collection from 16 riders yielding 1,629 sequences (>25 hours). Multimodal setup with synchronized front, rear, and helmet RGB video, rider eye-gaze from wearables, ambient on-road audio, and telemetry (GPS, accelerometer, gyroscope). Comprehensive annotations capture traffic context, rider state, 12 riding maneuvers (spanning conventional and unconventional), and legality labels (Legal, Illegal, Unspecified). Baseline experiments use state-of-the-art video backbones (CNN, Transformer) with multimodal fusion to assess rider behavior recognition and maneuver legality classification.

Result: Multimodal fusion that includes RGB, gaze, and telemetry consistently achieves the best performance across tasks, outperforming single-modality baselines. This demonstrates the value of integrating visual, gaze, and motion data for two-wheeler behavior analysis and legality prediction.

Conclusion: MOTOR establishes a foundational benchmark and dataset for safety-critical understanding of two-wheeler riding, enabling development and evaluation of behavior analysis, legality-aware prediction, and ITS applications. The dataset and code are publicly available at the provided URL.

Abstract: Two-wheelers account for a disproportionately high share of road fatalities in the Global South. Research on two-wheeler rider behavior, however, lags far behind four-wheelers, where multimodal datasets have driven major advances in Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS). To address this gap, we present the MOtorized TwO-wheeler Rider (MOTOR) dataset, the first large-scale, multi-view, multimodal resource dedicated to two-wheelers in dense, unstructured traffic. MOTOR comprises 1,629 sequences (25+ hours of video data) collected from 16 riders and integrates synchronized front, rear, and helmet videos, rider eye-gaze from wearable trackers, on-road audio, and telemetry (GPS, accelerometer, gyroscope). Rich annotations capture traffic context, rider state, 12 riding maneuvers spanning conventional and unconventional behaviors, and legality labels (Legal, Illegal, Unspecified). We benchmark rider behavior recognition and maneuver legality classification using state-of-the-art video action recognition backbones (CNN and Transformer-based), extended with multimodal fusion, and find that combining RGB, gaze, and telemetry consistently yields the best performance. MOTOR thus provides a unique foundation for advancing safety-critical understanding of two-wheeler riding. It offers the research community a benchmark to develop and evaluate models for behavior analysis, legality-aware prediction, and intelligent transportation systems. Dataset and code is available at https: //varuniiith.github.io/MOTOR-Dataset/

</details>


### [108] [FashionLens: Toward Versatile Fashion Image Retrieval via Task-Adaptive Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22552)
*Haokun Wen,Xuemeng Song,Xinghao Xie,Xiaolin Chen,Xiangyu Zhao,Weili Guan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A unified, multimodal fashion-image retrieval framework (FashionLens) built on a new unified benchmark (U-FIRE) that handles diverse query formats and search intents, with adaptive components (Proposal-Guided Spherical Query Calibrator and Gradient-Guided Adaptive Sampling) to align task-specific metrics and balance learning across tasks; achieves state-of-the-art performance and generalizes to unseen tasks; code and data released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The fashion retrieval landscape is fragmented with tasks requiring different query formats and objectives. A unified framework is needed to support diverse realistic scenarios. A common data foundation (U-FIRE) is proposed to consolidate datasets, while adaptive mechanisms address task heterogeneity and optimization imbalance.

Method: Proposes FashionLens, a Multimodal LLM-based framework. Introduces a Proposal-Guided Spherical Query Calibrator that uses adaptive spherical linear interpolation to map queries into task-aligned metric spaces. Includes Gradient-Guided Adaptive Sampling that re-weights tasks based on real-time learning difficulty and data scale priors. Built atop the U-FIRE benchmark and validated on diverse retrieval tasks.

Result: FashionLens achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse fashion retrieval scenarios on U-FIRE and shows robust generalization to unseen tasks. The authors publicly release data and code at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: A unified, adaptable retrieval framework can effectively handle diverse fashion search tasks and generalize beyond training distributions. The introduced benchmark and adaptive components facilitate broader, versatile fashion image retrieval and provide resources for future research.

Abstract: Fashion image retrieval is a cornerstone of modern e-commerce systems. A unified framework that supports diverse query formats and search intentions is highly desired in practice. However, existing approaches focus on narrow retrieval tasks and do not fully capture such diversity. Therefore, in this work, we aim to develop a unified framework capable of handling diverse realistic fashion retrieval scenarios, achieving truly versatile fashion image retrieval. To establish a data foundation, we first introduce U-FIRE, a comprehensive benchmark that consolidates fragmented fashion datasets into a unified collection, supplemented by two manually curated datasets for testing generalization. Building upon this, we propose FashionLens, a unified framework based on Multimodal Large Language Models. To handle divergent matching objectives, we design a Proposal-Guided Spherical Query Calibrator that dynamically shifts query representations into task-aligned metric spaces via adaptive spherical linear interpolation. Additionally, to mitigate the optimization imbalance caused by varying task complexities and data scales, we develop a Gradient-Guided Adaptive Sampling strategy that automatically re-weights tasks based on realtime learning difficulty and the data scale prior. Experiments on U-FIRE show that FashionLens achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse retrieval scenarios and generalizes robustly to unseen tasks. The data and code are publicly released at https://github.com/haokunwen/FashionLens.

</details>


### [109] [GeoWeaver: Grounding Visual Tokens with Geometric Evidence before Scene Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22558)
*Deshui Miao,Xingsen Huang,Yameng Gu,Xin Li,Haijun Zhang,Ming-Hsuan Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Token-adaptive geometric grounding (GeoWeaver) injects multi-level geometric evidence from a frozen geometry encoder into visual tokens before language reasoning, improving geometry-aware spatio-temporal reasoning without sacrificing multimodal capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current geometry-enhanced multimodal models treat geometry as a shared signal across tokens, neglecting that different visual tokens require distinct geometric cues depending on their spatial roles.

Method: Construct a multi-level geometry bank from a frozen geometry encoder; perform token-adaptive evidence allocation to retrieve relevant geometric abstractions for each token; apply a residual grounding operation to fuse evidence into visual tokens prior to language modeling.

Result: Empirical evaluation on spatial reasoning benchmarks shows consistent gains in geometry-aware reasoning while preserving overall multimodal performance.

Conclusion: Geometry information acts as a representational prerequisite shaping the foundation on which large language models reason; GeoWeaver demonstrates the benefit of token-adaptive geometry grounding, and code/models will be released.

Abstract: Spatio-temporal reasoning in vision-language models requires visual representations that preserve physical geometry rather than merely semantic appearance. Recent multimodal models incorporate geometric information through structural branches, 3D-aware supervision, reasoning-stage fusion, or long-horizon memory. While these approaches demonstrate the importance of geometry for spatial intelligence, they typically treat geometric cues as a shared signal across all visual tokens. We note that this overlooks a finer-grained challenge: different visual tokens require different geometric evidence depending on their spatial roles. To address this limitation, we introduce GeoWeaver, a pre-reasoning geometric grounding framework that treats geometry as a representational prerequisite for spatio-temporal reasoning. GeoWeaver constructs a multi-level geometry bank from a frozen geometry encoder and performs token-adaptive geometric evidence allocation, enabling each visual token to retrieve the most relevant geometric abstractions. The selected evidence is incorporated into visual tokens via a residual grounding operation prior to language modeling, yielding geometry-grounded representations for downstream reasoning. Extensive evaluations on spatial reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that GeoWeaver consistently enhances geometry-aware reasoning while retaining general multimodal capabilities. This indicates that geometric information yields the greatest benefit not as a late-fusion auxiliary signal but as a fundamental prerequisite that shapes the representational foundation on which large language models perform reasoning. All source code and models will be released at https://github.com/yahooo-m/GeoWeaver .

</details>


### [110] [Cell Phantom Video Generation in Elliptical Fourier Descriptor Domain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22563)
*Francesco Benedetto,Roberto Basla,Luca Magri,Giacomo Boracchi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A framework to generate time-consistent cell phantoms for video data by modeling 2D cell contours with Elliptical Fourier Descriptors (EFDs) as a multivariate time series, enabling biologically plausible synthetic videos for annotation-free data augmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the scarcity and high cost of annotated cell-tracking data by creating synthetic yet biologically coherent phantom videos, leveraging a compact contour representation.

Method: Represent cell contours with EFD coefficients and model their temporal evolution as a multivariate time series; impose strong morphological priors to ensure coherent, realistic dynamics; generate sequences that evolve plausibly over time and convert them into synthetic videos with ground-truth annotations.

Result: Experimental validation indicates that temporal evolution in EFD space produces biologically plausible phantom videos, supporting the use of these phantoms in data-generation pipelines for cell tracking; code is released.

Conclusion: The proposed EFD-based temporal model is a viable component for generating annotated synthetic data for cell-tracking tasks, potentially reducing annotation effort and accelerating development of tracking methods.

Abstract: Training Deep Neural Networks for tracking individual cells in biomedical videos requires a large amount of annotated data. The annotation of videos for cell tracking is very time consuming and often requires domain expertise; this explains the limited availability of public annotated data to address important medical problems like tissue repair or cancer treatment. Generating synthetic videos along with their Ground Truth annotations is a promising solution that relies, as a foundational first step, on the synthesis of single cell annotations (or phantoms). Phantoms need to be time consistent, as they have to replicate biological processes that are specific to the cell types. In this work, we propose a novel framework for generating videos of cell phantoms in the Elliptical Fourier Descriptors (EFDs) domain, a compact and geometrically interpretable representation for 2D closed contours. We represent the cell phantom evolution as a multivariate time series of EFD coefficients, introducing a strong prior for cell morphology and enabling the efficient generation of sequences that evolve coherently in time. Our experimental validation proves that modelling the temporal evolution in EFD space enables the generation of biologically plausible phantom videos. Our method can be used in generative pipelines for synthesizing annotated data for cell tracking, thus strongly mitigating the annotation effort for creating new datasets. Our code is available for download here: https://github.com/FrancescoBenedetto99/efd-cell-video-gen.

</details>


### [111] [VGenST-Bench: A Benchmark for Spatio-Temporal Reasoning via Active Video Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22570)
*Jinho Park,Youbin Kim,Hogun Park,Eunbyung Park*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A generative, controllable video benchmark (VGenST-Bench) to evaluate spatio-temporal reasoning in MLLMs, featuring a 3x2x2 taxonomy and hierarchical task suite, built via a multi-agent pipeline with human QC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks rely on static images or passively gathered videos, insufficient for fine-grained reasoning; need actively synthesized, diverse, controlled evaluation scenarios.

Method: Constructs VGenST-Bench via generative models and a multi-agent pipeline with human-in-the-loop QC; defines a 3x2x2 video taxonomy (Spatial Scale, Perspective, Scene Dynamics); designs hierarchical tasks decoupling perception from high-level reasoning.

Result: Enables fine-grained diagnosis of spatio-temporal understanding; provides comprehensive coverage and controlled evaluation; shifts benchmarking paradigm from passive curation to active synthesis.

Conclusion: Shifts the evaluation paradigm toward active synthesis; VGenST-Bench facilitates precise spatio-temporal reasoning assessment in MLLMs and supports more nuanced diagnostics.

Abstract: Spatio-temporal reasoning is a core capability for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) operating in the real world. As such, evaluating it precisely has become an essential challenge. However, existing spatio-temporal reasoning benchmark datasets primarily rely on static image sets or passively curated video data, which limits the evaluation of fine-grained reasoning capabilities. In this paper, we introduce VGenST-Bench, a video benchmark that employs generative models to actively synthesize highly controlled and diverse evaluation scenarios. To construct VGenST-Bench, we propose a multi-agent pipeline incorporating a human quality control stage, ensuring the quality of all generated videos and QA pairs. We establish a comprehensive 3x2x2 video taxonomy, encompassing Spatial Scale, Perspective, and Scene Dynamics to span diverse scenarios. Furthermore, we design a hierarchical task suite that decouples low-level visual perception from high-level spatio-temporal reasoning. By shifting the paradigm from passive curation to active synthesis, VGenST-Bench enables fine-grained diagnosis of spatio-temporal understanding in MLLMs.

</details>


### [112] [SegGuidedNet: Sub-Region-Aware Attention Supervision for Interpretable Brain Tumor Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22572)
*Hasaan Maqsood,Saif Ur Rehman Khan,Sebastian Vollmer,Andreas Dengel,Muhammad Nabeel Asim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SegGuidedNet introduces SegAttentionGate in a 3D residual encoder–decoder for brain tumor sub-region segmentation, delivering state-of-the-art single-model performance with built-in interpretability and minimal parameter overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address morphological variability, severe class imbalance, and overlapping appearances among tumor sub-regions (necrotic core, peritumoral edema, enhancing tumor); provide spatial interpretability without post-hoc methods while maintaining clinical practicality.

Method: A three-dimensional residual encoder–decoder network augmented with a SegAttentionGate module that explicitly supervises decoder-generated spatial attention maps for each tumor sub-region via a lightweight auxiliary loss (<0.2% parameter overhead). This sub-region supervision preserves decoder discriminability and yields free spatial interpretability at inference.

Result: On BraTS2021 and BraTS2023 GLI (251 held-out subjects each), SegGuidedNet achieves mean Dice 0.905 (ET=0.873, TC=0.906, WT=0.935) and 0.897 (ET=0.859, TC=0.902, WT=0.931). It outperforms ensemble nnU-Net and HNF-Netv2 as a single model and approaches Swin UNETR when using a 10-model ensemble, with 2–4 Dice points less at a fraction of the inference cost and shows consistent results across editions.

Conclusion: SegGuidedNet offers competitive segmentation accuracy with built-in sub-region interpretability in a lightweight, clinically practical framework, suggesting strong generalizability across BraTS editions.

Abstract: Accurate segmentation of brain tumour sub-regions from multi-parametric MRI is critical for treatment planning yet remains challenging due to morphological variability, class imbalance, and overlapping appearances of tumour regions across imaging sequences. We propose SegGuidedNet, a three-dimensional residual encoder--decoder network introducing a novel SegAttentionGate module that explicitly supervises the decoder to produce spatially discriminative attention maps for each tumour sub-region necrotic core, peritumoral oedema, and enhancing tumour via a lightweight auxiliary loss, adding less than 0.2% parameter overhead. This sub-region supervision maintains decoder discriminability between visually ambiguous classes while providing free-of-cost spatial interpretability at inference without any post-hoc explanation method. Evaluated independently on BraTS2021 and BraTS2023 GLI across 251 held-out subjects each, SegGuidedNet achieves mean Dice of 0.905 (ET= 0.873, TC=0.906, WT=0.935) and 0.897 (ET=0.859, TC=0.902, WT=0.931) respectively, surpassing ensemble-based nnU-Net and HNF-Netv2 as a single model and approaching Swin UNETR a 10-model ensemble within 2--4 Dice points at a fraction of the inference cost. The consistency of results across two benchmark editions further confirms the generalisability of the proposed approach, offering competitive accuracy with built-in interpretability in a lightweight, clinically practical framework.

</details>


### [113] [Beyond Chamfer Distance: Granular Order-aware Evaluation Metric For Online Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22578)
*Chouaib Bencheikh Lehocine,Adam Lilja,Junsheng Fu,Lars Hammarstrand*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Order-aware and soft multi-instance metrics for online map estimation: SOSPA and PLD; enable fine-grained evaluation and reveal detection bottlenecks not captured by mAP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation relies on mAP with thresholded Chamfer distance, which ignores point order and provides coarse granularity. Need order-sensitive, metric-compliant single-instance and soft, differentiable multi-instance metrics to better assess geometric quality and detection performance.

Method: Introduce SOSPA (Sequence Optimal Sub-Pattern Assignment) as an order-aware metric for single geometries, satisfying metric axioms. Propose PLD (Polyline Localisation and Detection) as a soft multi-instance metric that jointly models detection quality and geometric accuracy without hard thresholding, enabling soft assignment.

Result: On nuScenes, PLD ranks SOTA online mapping methods (MapTRv2, StreamMapNet, MapTracker) and supports decomposed error analysis. Detects detection capability as the primary bottleneck, a trend missed by mAP.

Conclusion: SOSPA and PLD provide principled, fine-grained evaluation for online map estimation, improving method differentiation and bottleneck identification; code for evaluation will be released.

Abstract: Online map estimation is a crucial component of autonomous driving systems that reduces the reliance on costly high-definition maps. State-of-the-art (SOTA) methods commonly predict map elements as ordered sequences of points that form polylines and polygons. The evaluation of these methods relies predominantly on mean average precision (mAP) based on thresholded Chamfer distance (CD). This framework lacks sensitivity to point ordering and provides limited granularity in assessing geometric quality, making it difficult to distinguish which methods truly excel over others. In this work, we address these limitations on two fronts. For the single-instance similarity measure, we introduce sequence optimal sub-pattern assignment (SOSPA), an order-aware metric that enables fine-grained evaluation of individual geometries while satisfying all metric axioms. For the multi-instance evaluation framework, we propose polyline localisation and detection (PLD), a soft metric that jointly captures detection quality and geometric accuracy, replacing the hard thresholding of mAP with a principled soft assignment. Through evaluations on nuScenes, we demonstrate that PLD effectively ranks SOTA online mapping methods (MapTRv2, StreamMapNet, MapTracker) while providing a decomposed error analysis. This analysis identifies detection capability as the dominant bottleneck in current methods, revealing a performance trend that mAP fails to capture. Code for evaluation using our metrics will be released.

</details>


### [114] [SceneAligner: 3D-Grounded Floorplan Localization in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22581)
*Junhyeong Cho,Ruojin Cai,Hadar Averbuch-Elor*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Grounded floorplan localization in the wild via 3D scene reconstruction and a cross-modal 2D foundation model; uses a density-map floorplan proxy and a 2D similarity transform; effective even with sparse data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current floorplan localization methods assume controlled environments and precise vectorized floorplans, limiting scalability to large buildings and rasterized plans. A robust, scalable approach is needed that can operate from unconstrained images and non-vector floorplans.

Method: Reconstruct a gravity-aligned 3D scene from an unconstrained image collection; project this 3D representation into a 2D density map that serves as a floorplan proxy; localize by aligning the proxy with the input floorplan using a 2D similarity transform. Bridge cross-modal gaps by fine-tuning a 2D foundation model to learn cross-modal correspondences, promoting semantically aligned matches while preserving structural consistency.

Result: Extensive experiments report substantial improvements over prior methods, including success with extremely sparse data (as few as a single image). Code and data will be publicly released.

Conclusion: A robust, scalable floorplan localization framework that grounds 2D floorplans in a reconstructed 3D scene and leverages cross-modal learning to bridge modality gaps, with strong empirical performance and practical potential.

Abstract: Many public buildings provide floorplans with a "you are here" indicator to help visitors orient themselves. Floorplan localization seeks to computationally replicate this capability by determining where visual observations were captured within a floorplan. However, existing methods typically assume controlled small-scale environments and precise vectorized floorplans, limiting their ability to operate in large-scale buildings and rasterized floorplans. In this work, we present an approach for performing floorplan localization in the wild by grounding the task in a reconstructed 3D representation of the scene. Given an unconstrained image collection, our method reconstructs a gravity-aligned 3D scene and projects it into a 2D density map that serves as a floorplan proxy. Floorplan localization is then formulated as aligning this proxy with the input floorplan via a 2D similarity transform. To bridge the appearance gap between density maps and architectural floorplans, we adapt a 2D foundation model to learn cross-modal correspondences, introducing a fine-tuning scheme that encourages semantically aligned matches while preserving structural consistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate substantial improvements over prior methods, including in extremely sparse settings with as little as a single input image. Our code and data will be publicly available.

</details>


### [115] [Rethinking Noise-Robust Training for Frozen Vision Foundation Models: A Cross-Dataset Benchmark with a Case Study of Small-Loss Failure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22591)
*Zitong Li,Haoyu Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: No universal best noisy-label method for frozen Vision Foundation Models in medical imaging; performance depends on noise regime; practical guidance requires regime-aware method selection aided by a feature-space selector.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Noisy-label learning in frozen-feature regimes is poorly understood and lacks practical guidance for medical imaging deployments of frozen VFMs; the study tests assumptions (e.g., small-loss) and seeks evidence-based recommendations.

Method: Benchmark eight noisy-label methods across five medical datasets, three backbones, two noise types, five noise rates (150 conditions, ~6k training runs), evaluated with balanced accuracy. Includes Friedman tests and regime-aware analyses; analyses of small-loss assumption under frozen DINOv2 features; ISIC2019 case study with Co-Teaching.

Result: No single method dominates: Friedman χ²=333.2 (p≈4.8e-68); ELR wins most conditions (49/150), CUFIT has best mean rank (2.51). Cost of method choice increases with noise severity (4.5 percentage points on clean data to 18.8pp at asymmetric 40% noise). Under frozen DINOv2 features, clean vs. noisy loss distributions overlap by 53–61%; prediction agreement is more stable than loss ranking under asymmetric noise (3pp vs. 13pp precision drop). On ISIC2019 with 40% asymmetric noise, Co-Teaching reaches 68% overall accuracy but drops to 35.1% balanced accuracy with zero recall on three minority classes. These results suggest a regime-aware approach rather than a search for a single winner; provide evidence-based guidance and a low-regret feature-space selector for practical deployment.

Conclusion: No universal dominant noisy-label method for frozen VFMs in medical imaging; adopt regime-aware method selection guided by a feature-space selector to balance performance across noise regimes; deliver practical recommendations and robust deployment strategies.

Abstract: Frozen Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) with lightweight classification heads are increasingly used in medical imaging because they offer efficient and reproducible deployment. Yet noisy-label learning methods for this frozen-feature regime remain poorly understood, and most existing methods still rely on a small-loss assumption inherited from end-to-end training. We present a controlled benchmark of eight noisy-label methods across five medical datasets, three backbones, two noise types, and five noise rates (150 conditions, 6,000 training runs), evaluated with balanced accuracy. The benchmark shows that there is no universal winner: Friedman ranking over the 150 conditions yields $χ^2 = 333.2$ ($p = 4.77 \times 10^{-68}$), ELR wins the most conditions (49/150), while CUFIT attains the best mean rank (2.51). The practical cost of method choice grows sharply with noise severity, from 4.5pp on clean data to 18.8pp at asymmetric 40\% noise. To explain these benchmark-level patterns, we revisit the small-loss assumption in a representative high-risk regime. Under frozen DINOv2 features, clean and noisy loss distributions overlap by 53--61\%, and matched-rate clean-sample detection shows that prediction agreement is markedly more stable than loss ranking under asymmetric noise (3pp vs.\ 13pp precision drop). On ISIC2019 with asymmetric 40\% noise, Co-Teaching reaches 68\% overall accuracy while collapsing to 35.1\% balanced accuracy with zero recall on three minority classes. Together, these results recast noisy-label learning for frozen VFMs as a regime-aware method-selection problem rather than a search for a single dominant algorithm. We conclude with evidence-based guidance and a low-regret feature-space selector for practical recommendation.

</details>


### [116] [Enhancing Gaze Reasoning in Vision Foundation Models for Gaze Following](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22607)
*Shijing Wang,Yaping Huang,Chaoqun Cui,David Wong,Yihua Cheng,Alexandros Neophytou,Hyung Jin Chang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a training mechanism to boost gaze reasoning in vision foundation models for gaze following, using a head-conditioned local LoRA and an out-of-cone penalty, achieving state-of-the-art results on GazeFollow and VAT, especially when targets are not semantically salient; code to be released after acceptance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision foundation models improve scene understanding but contribute little to gaze reasoning; existing methods often rely on semantically salient objects rather than true gaze cues, leading to poor performance when targets are not salient.

Method: 1) head-conditioned local LoRA to adapt only head tokens for gaze reasoning while preserving scene token learning; 2) out-of-cone penalty to inject gaze cues into head tokens and align them with scene tokens.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on GazeFollow and VAT datasets, with particularly strong improvements when gaze targets lack semantic salience.

Conclusion: The proposed mechanisms advance gaze following by enhancing gaze reasoning in VFMs, offering insights for future work and promising code release after acceptance.

Abstract: Gaze following requires both scene understanding and gaze reasoning to localize the gaze target of an in-scene person. Recently, vision foundation models (VFMs) have demonstrated strong performance on this task, enabling simpler architectures while outperforming prior methods. However, we observe a key limitation of VFM-based approaches: while VFMs substantially improve scene understanding, they contribute little to gaze reasoning. As a result, existing methods often rely on semantically salient objects rather than true gaze cues, leading to degraded performance when targets are not salient. To address this, we propose a novel training mechanism to enhance gaze reasoning in VFMs for gaze following. Our method includes: (1) a head-conditioned local LoRA, which enables localized adaptation to preserve scene token learning while improving head token learning for gaze reasoning; and (2) an out-of-cone penalty, which injects gaze cues into head tokens while aligning them with scene tokens. Experiments on the GazeFollow and VAT datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance, with particularly strong improvements when gaze targets are not semantically salient. Our findings offer valuable insights for advancing future gaze following research. We will release the code once the paper is accepted.

</details>


### [117] [GLeVE: Graph-Guided Lesion Grounding with Proposal Verification in 3D CT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22619)
*Shuo Jiang,Yuhao Hong,Chunbo Jiang,Weihong Chen,Huangwei Chen,Shenghao Zhu,Beining Wu,Mingxuan Liu,Zhu Zhu,Feiwei Qin,Min Tan,Yifei Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GLeVE is a graph-guided lesion grounding framework that anchors lesion descriptions in 3D CT volumes using anatomy-aware proposals and octree-based autoregressive refinement, achieving improved lesion-wise localization and segmentation over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridging the semantic gap between free-text radiology reports and volumetric CT data; existing report-assisted and vision-language methods rely on phrase-level alignment or dense supervision, yielding weak lesion-wise correspondence and localization.

Method: Treat each lesion description as an atomic semantic unit and encode organ attribution, attributes, and inter-lesion relations via relation-aware graph reasoning to generate discriminative lesion queries. Use anatomy-aware proposal generation with region-level verification to enforce one-to-one text-lesion alignment. Apply hierarchical octree-based autoregressive refinement to progressively improve boundary delineation.

Result: On AbdomenAtlas 3.0, GLeVE achieves consistent gains over classical multimodal foundation models and reported baselines in both segmentation accuracy and lesion-level localization.

Conclusion: The framework effectively reduces semantic-spatial misalignment by leveraging anatomical priors, graph reasoning, and multi-scale refinement to deliver accurate, verifiable lesion grounding and delineation in radiology reports.

Abstract: Grounding radiology report descriptions to 3D CT volumes is essential for verifiable clinical interpretation, yet remains challenging due to the semantic-spatial gap between free-text narratives and volumetric anatomy. Existing report-assisted and vision-language grounding methods typically rely on phrase-level alignment or dense pixel supervision, resulting in limited lesion-wise correspondence and suboptimal localization accuracy. We propose GLeVE, a graph-guided lesion grounding framework with anatomical prior verification and octree-based autoregressive refinement. GLeVE treats each lesion description as an atomic semantic unit and encodes organ attribution, attributes, and inter-lesion relations through relation-aware graph reasoning to produce discriminative lesion-wise queries. Anatomy-aware proposal generation with region-level verification enforces one-to-one text-lesion alignment, while hierarchical octree refinement progressively improves boundary delineation. Experiments on AbdomenAtlas 3.0 demonstrate consistent gains over classical multimodal foundation models and report-supervised baselines in both segmentation accuracy and lesion-level localization.

</details>


### [118] [H-Flow: Self-supervised Human Scene Flow via Physics-inspired Joint Multi-modal Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22629)
*Zhanbo Huang,Xiaoming Liu,Yu Kong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A monocular, transformer-based dense human scene flow model (H-Flow) jointly estimates pose, depth, and dense flow, guided by physics priors; introduces DynAct4D synthetic benchmark; achieves state-of-the-art performance and zero-shot generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-rigid surface dynamics of clothing and soft tissue cannot be captured by parametric human models; generic scene flow struggles on articulated bodies due to limited supervision.

Method: A unified multi-head transformer predicts dense flow from monocular video, with joint outputs for pose and depth; training is grounded in cross-modal objectives that encode geometric, structural, and biomechanical priors; DynAct4D provides dense flow annotations for diverse subjects.

Result: H-Flow outperforms scene-flow and parametric baselines on standard benchmarks and generalizes zero-shot to in-the-wild video.

Conclusion: Introduces a physics-informed, dense human scene flow framework and a high-fidelity synthetic benchmark, enabling improved modeling of pose and surface deformation; code, models, and DynAct4D to be released.

Abstract: Parametric human models capture global pose but cannot represent the non-rigid surface dynamics of clothing and soft tissue. Generic scene flow estimates dense motion but breaks down on articulated bodies, where pixel-level supervision is also intractable to acquire. We introduce H-Flow, a dense human scene flow that captures both skeletal kinematics and surface deformation. A unified multi-head transformer estimates flow from monocular video, jointly predicting pose and depth as companion outputs. The challenge lies in the lack of supervision. In place of unattainable labels, we anchor the network in the physics of human motion, encoding geometric, structural, and biomechanical priors as cross-modal training objectives. We further introduce DynAct4D, a high-fidelity synthetic benchmark providing dense flow annotations across diverse subjects, garments, and motions. On standard benchmarks, H-Flow outperforms scene-flow and parametric baselines, and generalizes zero-shot to in-the-wild video. Code, models, and the DynAct4D benchmark will be released upon publication

</details>


### [119] [AtomicMotion: Learning Human Motion From Different Human Parts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22631)
*Runzhen Liu,Chuhua Xian,Fa-Ting Hong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AtomicMotion decouples and re-integrates body dynamics to reconstruct full-body poses from sparse head/hand data by partitioning the skeleton into five functional clusters, using masked pre-conditioning during training, and introducing Kinematic Attention that enforces kinematic tree constraints; it outperforms baselines on AMASS in fidelity and biomechanical realism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current full-body pose reconstruction methods treat the human body monolithically, leading to error accumulation and unnatural joint coordination due to neglecting fine-grained functional intents and the underlying skeletal topology.

Method: 1) Logical body partitioning into five functional clusters to preserve intra-cluster joint synergies while isolating local motion primitives. 2) Masked full-body pre-conditioning during training to map sparse inputs to high-dimensional poses, embedding global skeletal topology and latent kinematic constraints. 3) Kinematic Attention that incorporates the classical kinematic tree into the attention mechanism to ensure biomechanical plausibility.

Result: Extensive evaluations on the AMASS dataset show AtomicMotion significantly outperforms existing baselines, delivering higher reconstruction fidelity and more biomechanically realistic motion.

Conclusion: AtomicMotion demonstrates that partitioning the skeleton by functional intent, augmenting with masked pre-conditioning, and enforcing a kinematic-tree-aware attention framework yields superior full-body pose reconstruction from sparse inputs, with strong biomechanical realism.

Abstract: Accurately reconstructing full-body poses from sparse head and hand trajectories is a foundational challenge for immersive AR/VR telepresence. Current methods often struggle with error accumulation and unnatural joint coordination, primarily because they treat the human body as a monolithic entity, thereby failing to capture the fine-grained ``atomic intents'' embedded in subtle signal variations and overlooking the inherent structural topology. To bridge this gap, we present AtomicMotion, a framework designed to decouple and re-integrate body dynamics through three core innovations. First, we introduce a logical body partitioning scheme that decomposes the skeleton into five distinct clusters based on functional intent; this ensures that each partition preserves internal joint synergies while isolating local motion primitives. Second, to robustly map sparse inputs to high-dimensional poses, we employ a masked full-body pre-conditioning strategy during training, forcing the model to internalize global skeletal topology and latent kinematic constraints. Finally, addressing the limitations of vanilla spatial attention, which often ignores fixed physiological connectivity, we propose Kinematic Attention. By embedding the classical kinematic tree structure into the attention mechanism, we ensure biological plausibility in the synthesized motions. Extensive evaluations on the AMASS dataset demonstrate that AtomicMotion significantly outperforms existing baselines, yielding higher reconstruction fidelity and superior biomechanical realism.

</details>


### [120] [From Baseline to Follow-Up: Counterfactual Spine DXA Image Synthesis in UK Biobank Using a Causal Hierarchical Variational Autoencoder](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22649)
*Yilin Zhang,Nicholas C. Harvey,Nicholas R. Fuggle,Rahman Attar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A metadata-conditioned causal hierarchical variational autoencoder (CHVAE) is proposed to generate causally consistent AP spine DXA images from UK Biobank data, conditioned on participant attributes and lumbar morphometry; evaluated with abduction–action–prediction (AAP) in a baseline-to-follow-up setting; results show strong agreement in key vertebral morphometry under age intervention, supporting plausibly synthesized, intervention-aligned DXA images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning controllable, interpretable, factor-specific anatomical variation in DXA data is challenging; need causal-consistent synthetic generation conditioned on metadata to enable large-scale, interpretable skeletal phenotyping.

Method: Train a metadata-conditioned CHVAE on 3,743 raw AP spine scans from the first UKB imaging visit, conditioned on basic participant attributes and lumbar morphometry. Use abduction–action–prediction (AAP) to assess causal consistency: abduct latent variables from baseline images, intervene age to repeat-imaging value, and compare the counterfactual follow-up morphometry with observed follow-up measurements.

Result: Demonstrates strong absolute-level agreement for key vertebral morphometry variables under age intervention, indicating intervention-aligned synthesis yields anatomically plausible DXA images.

Conclusion: CHVAE enables controllable, causally consistent generation of spinal DXA images conditioned on metadata, with potential for synthetic data generation and causal reasoning in skeletal imaging.

Abstract: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) is widely used for large-scale skeletal assessment, yet learning controllable and interpretable factor-specific anatomical variation remains challenging. We propose a metadata-conditioned causal hierarchical variational autoencoder (CHVAE) for causally consistent generation of anteroposterior (AP) spine DXA images from the UK Biobank (UKB). The model is trained on 3,743 raw AP spine scans from the first imaging visit and conditioned on basic participant attributes and lumbar morphometry. Causal consistency is evaluated in a baseline-to-follow-up setting using abduction--action--prediction (AAP): latent variables are abducted from baseline images, age is intervened to the repeat-imaging value, and the resulting counterfactual follow-up morphometry is compared with observed repeat-imaging measurements. Results show strong absolute-level agreement for key vertebral morphometry variables under age intervention, supporting intervention-aligned synthesis of anatomically plausible DXA images.

</details>


### [121] [What Does the Caption Really Say? Counterfactual Phrase Intervention for Compositional Data Selection in Vision-Language Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22651)
*Hyejin Go,Semi Lee,Hyesong Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CPI adds phrase-level, counterfactual interventions to CLIP-style pretraining to capture compositional signal, yielding data-efficient improvements over alignment-only filtering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global, pair-level filters saturate; they misjudge whether caption phrases (objects, attributes, relations) actually support the image-text match, hindering compositional generalization.

Method: Counterfactual Phrase Intervention (CPI): use controlled nonce-token substitutions to generate image-conditioned phrase-sensitivity scores; perform coarse global alignment for mismatch removal, then rank surviving captions by how phrases affect the image-text score under substitution.

Result: On CC3M-scale data, CPI yields a subset (about 50% data) that improves VL-CheckList-VG Relation by +1.91 over full-data baseline and +1.00 over alignment-only filtering at equal budget; improves SugarCrepe and preserves transfer; when applied to NegCLIP, gains +3.84 on VL-CheckList-VG Relation and further CE-CLIP gains in main text.

Conclusion: Phrase-level, counterfactual, first-order sensitivity signals can capture compositional supervision more faithfully than global filters, enabling data-efficient gains in image-text pretraining without sacrificing transfer, and can complement existing alignment-based methods.

Abstract: CLIP-style contrastive pretraining typically curates web-scale image-text pairs using sample-level filtering signals, often based on pair-level alignment. We show that this signal saturates: once coarse mismatches are removed, stricter global filtering no longer tracks the compositional supervision provided by the retained captions. The reason is structural - a global score conflates whether a pair is broadly plausible with whether the individual object, attribute, and relation phrases inside the caption materially support the image-text match. The latter is what compositional generalization demands, yet pair-level filters are blind to it. We address this with Counterfactual Phrase Intervention (CPI), a phrase-level curation framework that converts controlled nonce-token substitutions into image-conditioned phrase-sensitivity scores. CPI uses global alignment only for coarse mismatch removal, then ranks the surviving pool by whether caption phrases measurably affect the image-text score under controlled substitution. We frame CPI as a first-order phrase-sensitivity signal rather than a grounding or identification result, and evaluate it at CC3M scale. Ranking by this signal yields a 50%-data subset that improves VL-CheckList-VG Relation by +1.91 over the full-data baseline and +1.00 over alignment-only filtering at matched budget, while improving SugarCrepe overall and preserving general transfer. CPI is loss-orthogonal: applied unchanged to NegCLIP, it further improves VL-CheckList-VG Relation by +3.84, with additional CE-CLIP gains in the main text.

</details>


### [122] [SegCompass: Exploring Interpretable Alignment with Sparse Autoencoders for Enhanced Reasoning Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22658)
*Zhenyu Lu,Liupeng Li,Jinpeng Wang,Haoqian Kang,Yan Feng,Ke Chen,Yaowei Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SegCompass uses a Sparse Autoencoder to align chain-of-thought with visual tokens in a shared sparse concept space, enabling an interpretable, differentiable path from reasoning to segmentation via a multi-slot heatmap and mask decoder.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address interpretability gap between reasoning in large language models and visual perception; improve transparency beyond end-to-end latent-query methods and post-hoc textual readouts.

Method: Generate a chain-of-thought (CoT); map CoT and image tokens into a shared high-dimensional sparse concept space via a Sparse Autoencoder (SAE); a learned query codebook selects salient concepts; a slot mapper grounds these into a multi-slot heatmap that guides the final mask decoder; train jointly with reinforcement learning for reasoning and standard segmentation supervision.

Result: Matches or surpasses state-of-the-art on five benchmarks; strong correlation between the quality of learned sparse concepts and final mask accuracy; code is released.

Conclusion: SAE-driven, end-to-end alignment provides a transparent, differentiable, and interpretable bridge between visual perception and reasoning, yielding robust segmentation performance.

Abstract: While large language models provide strong compositional reasoning, existing reasoning segmentation pipelines fail to transparently connect this reasoning to visual perception. Current methods, such as latent query alignment, are end-to-end yet opaque "black boxes". Conversely, textual localization readout is merely readable, not truly interpretable, often functioning as an unconstrained post-hoc step. To bridge this interpretability gap, we propose SegCompass, an end-to-end model that leverages a Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) to forge an explicit, interpretable, and differentiable alignment pathway. Given an image-instruction pair, SegCompass first generates a chain-of-thought (CoT) trace. The core of our method is an SAE that maps both the CoT and visual tokens into a shared, high-dimensional sparse concept space. A query codebook selects salient concepts from this space, which are then spatially grounded by a slot mapper into a multi-slot heatmap that guides the final mask decoder. The entire model is trained jointly, unifying reinforcement learning for the reasoning path with standard segmentation supervision. This SAE-driven interface provides a "white-box" connection that is significantly more traceable than latent queries and more coherent than textual readouts. Extensive experiments on five challenging benchmarks demonstrate that SegCompass matches or surpasses state-of-the-art performance. Crucially, our visual and quantitative analyses show a strong correlation between the quality of the learned sparse concepts and final mask accuracy, confirming that SegCompass achieves superior results through its enhanced and inspectable alignment. Code is available at https://github.com/ZhenyuLU-Heliodore/SegCompass.

</details>


### [123] [SEGA: Spectral-Energy Guided Attention for Resolution Extrapolation in Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22668)
*Javad Rajabi,Kimia Shaban,Koorosh Roohi,David B. Lindell,Babak Taati*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SEGA is a training-free method that adaptively scales RoPE-based attention in diffusion transformers according to the latent's spatial-frequency structure at each denoising step, improving high-resolution synthesis beyond training range.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion transformers exhibit degraded performance at resolutions beyond their training range. Prior training-free methods apply uniform, content-agnostic RoPE scaling, trading off global structure for fine detail.

Method: SEGA dynamically adjusts attention scaling across RoPE components in each denoising step by analyzing the latent's spatial-frequency structure, enabling content-aware extrapolation without training.

Result: Empirical results show SEGA consistently enhances high-resolution image synthesis across multiple target resolutions and surpasses existing training-free baselines.

Conclusion: Adaptive, content-aware attention scaling via SEGA improves both structural coherence and fine-detail fidelity in high-resolution diffusion-based text-to-image generation without requiring additional training.

Abstract: Diffusion transformers (DiTs) have emerged as a dominant architecture for text-to-image generation, yet their performance drops when generating at resolutions beyond their training range. Existing training-free approaches mitigate this by modifying inference-time attention behavior, often through Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) extrapolation combined with attention scaling. However, these strategies apply a uniform and content-agnostic scaling across RoPE components with distinct frequency characteristics, inducing a trade-off between preserving global structure and recovering fine detail. We introduce SEGA, a training-free method that dynamically scales attention across RoPE components according to the latent's spatial-frequency structure at each denoising step. This adaptive scaling improves both structural coherence and fine-detail fidelity. Experiments show that SEGA consistently improves high-resolution synthesis across multiple target resolutions, outperforming state-of-the-art training-free baselines.

</details>


### [124] [From Abstraction to Instantiation: Learning Behavioral Representation for Vision-Language-Action Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22671)
*Bing Hu,Zaijing Li,Rui Shao,Junda Chen,April Hua Liu,Wei-Shi Zheng,Liqiang Nie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BehaviorVLA introduces temporally coherent visuomotor behavior representations for robust VLA manipulation under distribution shifts, using a VBE encoder and a phase-conditioned PBD decoder; achieves state-of-the-art results on RoboTwin 2.0, LIBERO, and CALVIN and improves data efficiency in sim-to-real transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models rely on short-horizon, action-centric latent representations with static execution-alignment, leading to poor generalization under environmental shifts. A temporally coherent behavioral representation can capture long-horizon dependencies and align with task progress.

Method: A symmetric architecture: Visuomotor Behavior Encoder (VBE) with a causal Mamba-based design aggregates long-horizon trajectory information into a unified behavior representation; Phase-conditioned Behavior Decoder (PBD) decodes this representation into actions by dynamically aligning task priors with real-time execution progress.

Result: On RoboTwin 2.0, LIBERO, and CALVIN, the approach achieves state-of-the-art success rates of 58%, 98%, and 4.36 (Avg.Len) respectively. In real-world sim-to-real transfer, BehaviorVLA matches OpenVLA-OFT using only 50% of demonstrations, indicating improved data efficiency and generalization.

Conclusion:  temporally coherent behavior representations via VBE and PBD enable robust manipulation under distribution shifts, with superior data efficiency and generalization across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often suffer from performance degradation under distribution shifts, as they struggle to learn generalized behavior representations across varying environments. While existing approaches attempt to construct behavior representations through action-centric latent variables, they are often limited by short-horizon temporal fragmentation and static execution-alignment, leading to inconsistent behaviors in complex scenarios. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{BehaviorVLA}, a framework that facilitates robust manipulation through the learning of a temporally coherent behavioral representations. Our approach features two symmetric components: (1) the \textbf{Visuomotor Behavior Encoder (VBE)}, which utilizes a causal Mamba-based architecture to aggregate long-horizon trajectory information into a unified behavior representation; and (2) the \textbf{Phase-conditioned Behavior Decoder (PBD)}, which decodes this representation into precise actions by dynamically aligning task-level priors with real-time execution progress. Experiments on RoboTwin 2.0, LIBERO, and CALVIN demonstrate state-of-the-art success rates of 58\%, 98\%, and 4.36 (Avg.Len), respectively. Notably, in real-world sim-to-real transfer, BehaviorVLA matches the performance of OpenVLA-OFT using only 50\% of the demonstration data, showcasing its superior data efficiency and generalization.

</details>


### [125] [Slimmable ConvNeXt: Width-Adaptive Inference for Efficient Multi-Device Deployment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22677)
*Janek Haberer,Jon Eike Wilhelm,Olaf Landsiedel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A Slimmable ConvNeXt enables width-adaptive inference with nested subnetworks, avoiding switchable batch normalization, achieving competitive ImageNet-1k accuracy at multiple compute points and scaling to stronger models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploy vision models across devices with varying resource constraints or fluctuating compute; single model with multiple nested subnetworks to cover a range of capacities. Prior CNN slimmable BN approaches and ViT-based scalable methods have limitations in training complexity or BN overhead.

Method: Introduce Slimmable ConvNeXt using ConvNeXt design elements (LayerNorm and inverted bottlenecks) to enable channel-width slimming without BN switching. Train from scratch on ImageNet-1k for 600 epochs with 3-subnetwork variants; compare to HydraViT, MatFormer-S, and SortedNet-S; scale to ConvNeXt-B for higher capacity.

Result: On ImageNet-1k, Slimmable ConvNeXt-T with 3 subnetworks achieves 80.8% top-1 accuracy at 4.5 GMACs and 77.4% at 1.2 GMACs. It outperforms HydraViT's 6-head subnetwork (78.4% at 4.6 GMACs) and 3-head (73.0% at 1.3 GMACs), as well as MatFormer-S (78.6%) and SortedNet-S (78.2%) at comparable GMACs. Scaling to Slimmable ConvNeXt-B yields 82.8% at 15.35 GMACs.

Conclusion: ConvNeXt’s LayerNorm and inverted bottleneck design make it well-suited for channel-width slimming, removing normalization overhead and enabling a simpler training pipeline than prior CNN and ViT approaches. Slimmable ConvNeXt achieves competitive or superior accuracy across compute points and scales effectively to higher capacity.

Abstract: Deploying vision models across devices with varying resource constraints, or even on a single device where available compute fluctuates due to battery state, thermal throttling, or latency deadlines, typically requires training and maintaining separate models. Width-adaptive inference addresses this by training a single set of shared weights containing multiple nested subnetworks of increasing capacity, but prior CNN-based approaches required switchable batch normalization, while recent scalable methods have focused on Vision Transformers. We present Slimmable ConvNeXt, which shows that ConvNeXt's modern design, specifically LayerNorm and inverted bottlenecks, makes it particularly suited for channel-width slimming, eliminating the normalization overhead of classical slimmable networks and producing a simpler training pipeline than both prior CNN and ViT approaches. On ImageNet-1k, Slimmable ConvNeXt-T with 3 subnetworks achieves 80.8% top-1 accuracy at 4.5 GMACs and 77.4% at 1.2 GMACs, trained from scratch for 600 epochs. At comparable compute, this exceeds HydraViT's 6-head subnetwork (78.4% at 4.6 GMACs) by 2.4 percentage points and its 3-head configuration (73.0% at 1.3 GMACs) by 4.4 percentage points, while also outperforming MatFormer-S (78.6%) and SortedNet-S (78.2%) at the same GMACs. Scaling to Slimmable ConvNeXt-B further improves maximum accuracy to 82.8% at 15.35 GMACs.

</details>


### [126] [Swift Sampling: Selecting Temporal Surprises via Taylor Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22678)
*Dahye Kim,Bhuvan Sachdeva,Karan Uppal,Naman Gupta,Vineeth N. Balasubramanian,Deepti Ghadiyaram*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Swift Sampling is a training-free, lightweight frame selection method that detects temporally surprising frames via latent-space velocity/acceleration and Taylor-based predictions, improving long-video tasks with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce redundancy and improve information efficiency in long-form videos by focusing on moments that deviate from predicted visual evolution.

Method: Model video as a differentiable trajectory in a visual latent space; compute frame velocity and acceleration; apply Taylor expansion to project the expected path of subsequent frames; identify frames that diverge from the predicted manifold as temporally surprising and select them for sampling.

Result: Outperforms uniform sampling and prior query-agnostic baselines on three long-video QA benchmarks and 10 downstream tasks; improves accuracy by up to 12.5 points on long videos with limited frame budgets; adds only 0.02x computational cost over baseline, ~30x cheaper than leading baselines.

Conclusion: Swift Sampling offers a training-free, low-cost, effective frame-selection strategy for long-form video understanding, reducing dependence on auxiliary networks and task-specific hyperparameters.

Abstract: While most frames in long-form video are redundant, the critical information resides in temporal surprises: moments where the actual visual features deviate from their predicted evolution. Inspired by the human brain's predictive coding, we introduce Swift Sampling, an elegant, training-free frame selection algorithm that automatically identifies high-information moments in a video. Specifically, we model a video as a differentiable trajectory in the visual latent space and compute the velocity and acceleration of its features. Then, we apply Taylor expansion to project the expected path of subsequent frames. Frames that diverge sharply from this predicted manifold are identified as temporally surprising frames and selected for sampling. Unlike prior training-free methods that rely on auxiliary networks or video-specific hyperparameter tuning, Swift Sampling is incredibly lightweight, adding only 0.02x additional computational cost over baseline making it 30x cheaper overhead than leading baselines. Across three long-video question answering benchmarks and 10 different downstream tasks, Swift Sampling outperforms uniform sampling and prior query-agnostic baselines. It is especially powerful for long videos with limited frame budgets improving accuracy by up to +12.5 points.

</details>


### [127] [Conceptualizing Embeddings: Sparse Disentanglement for Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22679)
*Piotr Kubaty,Patryk Marszałek,Łukasz Struski,Adam Wróbel,Jacek Tabor,Marek Śmieja*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CEDAR provides a post-hoc, invertible rotation with a top-k sparsity bottleneck to disentangle vision-language embeddings without increasing dimensionality, yielding axis-aligned, interpretable coordinates.
