These coordinates can be linked to textual concepts in CLIP-like models and decoded into natural language in generative models like BLIP, with competitive reconstruction-sparsity performance and improved human-aligned explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-language embeddings are highly entangled and opaque. Traditional sparse autoencoders expand dimensionality, distorting geometry and introducing redundancy. A dimension-preserving method is needed to reveal interpretable, compositional structure.

Method: Train an invertible transformation with a top-k sparsity bottleneck (adaptive rotation) to map pretrained embeddings to axis-aligned, sparsified, disentangled coordinates in a post-hoc manner. Apply to CLIP-like architectures (interpreting coordinates via textual concepts) and to generative models like BLIP (decoding coordinates into natural language). Evaluate reconstruction-sparsity trade-off and interpretability against baselines.

Result: CEDAR achieves a competitive reconstruction-sparsity trade-off while producing explanations that are more interpretable and better aligned with human perception. The axis-aligned coordinates enable textual interpretation in CLIP-like models and natural language decoding in BLIP, supporting the claim that the entanglement in vision-language representations can be resolved by a suitable change of basis without overcomplete expansions.

Conclusion: A basis change via an invertible, sparsity-constrained transformation suffices to disentangle vision-language representations, obviating the need for dimension growth and providing interpretable, human-aligned explanations.

Abstract: Vision-language models learn powerful multimodal embeddings, yet their internal semantics remain opaque. While sparse autoencoders (SAEs) can extract interpretable features, they rely on expanding the representation dimension, which compromises the original geometry and introduces redundancy. We introduce CEDAR (Conceptual Embedding Disentanglement via Adaptive Rotation), a post-hoc method that reveals the compositional structure of pretrained embeddings without increasing dimensionality. By learning an invertible transformation with a top-$k$ sparsity bottleneck, CEDAR concentrates semantic information into axis-aligned disentangled coordinates. In CLIP-like architecture, individual coordinates can be interpreted with textual concepts, while for generative models such as BLIP, they can be decoded into natural language descriptions. Experiments demonstrate that CEDAR achieves a competitive reconstruction-sparsity trade-off while producing explanations that are more interpretable and better aligned with human perception. Our results suggest that the apparent entanglement in vision-language representations can be resolved through a suitable change of basis, eliminating the need for overcomplete expansions.

</details>


### [128] [Improving Viewpoint-Invariance and Temporal Consistency for Action Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22695)
*Yannick Porto,Renato Martins,Thomas Chalumeau,Cedric Demonceaux*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two-stage action detection with training-time viewpoint augmentation and a view-invariant, multi-scale temporal encoder using selective state-space sequence modeling; outperforms SOTA on PKU-MMD and BABEL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need to address view invariance and temporal coherence in untrimmed video action detection; appearance-based methods lack viewpoint diversity; motion-based methods miss fine-grained temporal relations.

Method: Stage 1: extract motion features from augmented virtual viewpoints, used only in training. Stage 2: a view-invariant, multi-scale temporal encoder based on selective state-space sequence modeling to aggregate viewpoint/time-scale information. Datasets PKU-MMD and BABEL; training-time viewpoint augmentation; code at the provided URL.

Result: Significant improvements over state-of-the-art across all splits on PKU-MMD and BABEL; evidence of improved view-invariance and temporal coherence.

Conclusion: HydraView-TAD provides an effective two-stage framework to improve view-invariance and temporal coherence in untrimmed video action detection; code and trained models released.

Abstract: Viewpoint change invariance and action temporal consistency are critical aspects for the effective deployment of human action detection of untrimmed videos. Existing appearance-based video detection methods often struggle with limited viewpoint diversity during training, while motion-based detection approaches frequently fail to model fine-grained temporal relationships across consecutive motion windows. This paper introduces a novel two-stage action detection approach designed to improve both view-invariance and global temporal coherence properties. In the first stage, we extract motion features from augmented virtual viewpoints, solely used at training. Then, the second stage introduces a new view-invariant, multi-scale temporal encoder based on selective state-space sequence modelling to aggregate information across viewpoints and time scales. Experiments on PKU-MMD and BABEL benchmarks demonstrate that this approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in all considered splits. Code and trained models are available at: https://icb-vision-ai.github.io/HydraView-TAD

</details>


### [129] [Cross-Domain Human Action Recognition from Multiview Motion and Textual Descriptions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22697)
*Yannick Porto,Renato Martins,Thomas Chalumeau,Cedric Demonceaux*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Orientation-aware zero-shot action recognition that fuses multi-view motion cues with text prompts; introduces an orientation-aware motion encoding network; achieves consistent state-of-the-art results across NTU-RGB+D, BABEL, NW-UCLA and two surveillance datasets; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-shot action recognition (ZSAR) struggles under domain shifts caused by viewpoint and orientation, and unseen actions. Existing ZSAR often assumes training-like geometric conditions; there is a need for cross-domain generalization without heavy annotation, leveraging multi-view motion and semantic text cues.

Method: Propose an orientation-aware motion encoding network to learn orientation-specific motion features. Train with multi-view motion cues and adapt orientation-aware text prompts to match features at inference. The approach integrates motion information from multiple camera viewpoints with textual action descriptions to improve ZSAR cross-domain performance.

Result: The method consistently improves ZSAR performance across benchmarks (NTU-RGB+D, BABEL, NW-UCLA, and two surveillance datasets), outperforming recent state-of-the-art zero-shot methods. Learned representations show strong transfer capabilities for cross-domain and same-domain recognition of seen actions. Code and trained models are released at the provided URL.

Conclusion: Orientation-aware, multi-view motion features, combined with text prompt alignment, enhance cross-domain ZSAR generalization and transfer learning, with practical benefits demonstrated on multiple benchmarks and accessible code for reproducibility.

Abstract: Robustness to domain changes is a key capability for effective deployment of human action recognition systems in real-world scenarios, where action categories at inference can present important domain shifts or even unseen actions from training. In this context, improving the recognition capabilities of Zero-Shot Action Recognition models (ZSAR), without requiring strong annotation efforts, remains a central challenge. Most ZSAR approaches assume that actions are observed under geometric conditions similar to those seen during training. In practice, variations in human body orientation and camera viewpoint add a significant domain gap in ZSAR, substantially limiting generalization to novel action-motion combinations. In this context, this paper presents a novel orientation-aware action recognition approach with improved cross-domain capabilities. Our approach combines motion cues of multiple camera viewpoints and text descriptions of human actions in the training phase. We present a new orientation-aware motion encoding network to learn different motion features, and adapt a specific orientation-aware text prompt to match the corresponding features at inference. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed method consistently improves ZSAR performance across different recognition benchmarks, outperforming recent state-of-the-art zero-shot approaches on NTU-RGB+D, BABEL, NW-UCLA, and on two surveillance datasets. In addition, the learned representations exhibit strong transfer learning capabilities, yielding competitive performance on both cross-domain and same-domain recognition of seen actions. Code and trained models are available at: https://icb-vision-ai.github.io/OrientationAware-HAR

</details>


### [130] [AnyMo: Geometry-Aware Setup-Agnostic Modeling of Human Motion in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22715)
*Baiyu Chen,Zechen Li,Wilson Wongso,Lihuan Li,Xiachong Lin,Hao Xue,Benjamin Tag,Flora Salim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AnyMo is a geometry-aware, setup-agnostic framework for wearable IMU motion modeling that synthesizes diverse signals via physics-based simulation across dense body placements, learns a graph-encoder from synthetic views, tokenizes multi-position IMUs into full-body motion tokens, and grounds them with an LLM for motion-language tasks, achieving strong zero-shot, cross-modal, and captioning performance across multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inertial sensor signals are highly dependent on sensing setup (location, mounting, orientation, hardware, sampling protocol), hindering cross-device transfer and broad adoption. A setup-agnostic, generalist motion model is needed to enable robust, transferable representations and cross-modal understanding.

Method: 1) Physics-grounded IMU simulation over dense body-surface placements to generate diverse synthetic signals. 2) Pre-train a graph encoder from paired synthetic placement views and masked partial observations. 3) Tokenize multi-position IMU data into full-body motion tokens. 4) Align these tokens with a large language model (LLM) for motion-language understanding, enabling cross-modal tasks.

Result: AnyMo achieves strong zero-shot performance across 14 unseen datasets (e.g., HAR) with improvements of 11.7% in accuracy, 11.6% in F1, and 22.6% in R@2. It also improves cross-modal retrieval (IMU-to-text and text-to-IMU) MRR by 15.9% and 28.6%, respectively, and zero-shot captioning BERT-F1 by 18.8%. These gains support its role as a generalist model for wearable motion understanding in the wild.

Conclusion: AnyMo demonstrates that a physics-grounded, setup-agnostic, multi-view, tokenized IMU framework aligned with an LLM can generalize across datasets and modalities, enabling zero-shot recognition, retrieval, and captioning for wearable motion in unconstrained environments. A project page is provided for reference.

Abstract: As wearable and mobile devices become increasingly embedded in daily life, they offer a practical way to continuously sense human motion in the wild. But inertial signals are highly dependent on the sensing setup, including body location, mounting position, sensor orientation, device hardware, and sampling protocol. This setup dependence makes it difficult to learn motion representations that transfer across devices and datasets, and limits the broader use of wearable IMUs beyond closed-set recognition. We introduce AnyMo, a geometry-aware framework for setup-agnostic human motion modeling. AnyMo uses physics-grounded IMU simulation over dense body-surface placements to generate diverse and plausible synthetic signals, pre-trains a graph encoder from paired synthetic placement views and masked partial observations, tokenizes multi-position IMU into full-body motion tokens, and aligns these tokens with an LLM for motion-language understanding. We evaluate AnyMo on three complementary tasks: zero-shot activity recognition across 14 unseen downstream datasets, cross-modal retrieval, and wearable IMU motion captioning, where it improves average Accuracy/F1/R@2 by 11.7\%/11.6\%/22.6\% on HAR, increases zero-shot IMU-to-text and text-to-IMU retrieval MRR by 15.9\% and 28.6\%, respectively, and improves zero-shot captioning BERT-F1 by 18.8\%. These results support AnyMo as a generalist model for wearable motion understanding in the wild. Project page: https://baiyuchen.com/project/AnyMo.

</details>


### [131] [WorldKV: Efficient World Memory with World Retrieval and Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22718)
*Jung Yi,Minjae Kim,Paul Hyunbin Cho,Wooseok Jang,Sangdoo Yun,Seungryong Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WorldKV is a training-free framework that enables persistent, action-conditioned video generation by extending memory with World Retrieval and compressing history with World Compression to maintain fidelity while achieving ~2x throughput versus full KV-cache.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sustaining long-term consistency in autoregressive video generation requires growing memory and compute; full KV-cache preserves consistency but is too expensive for real-time performance; sliding window fails to preserve long-range consistency.

Method: WorldKV has two components: (1) World Retrieval stores evicted KV-cache chunks in GPU/CPU memory and selectively retrieves scene-relevant chunks via camera/action alignment, injecting them back into the attention window without re-encoding; (2) World Compression prunes redundant tokens within each chunk using key-key similarity to an anchor frame, reducing per-chunk storage by ~50%.

Result: Experiments on Matrix-Game-2.0 and LingBot-World-Fast show WorldKV achieves fidelity comparable to or better than full-KV baselines at ~2x throughput, without any fine-tuning, and is competitive with memory-trained baselines.

Conclusion: WorldKV provides a training-free solution for persistent, action-conditioned world generation with maintained fidelity and improved throughput, enabling closer-to-real-time persistent environments without costly fine-tuning.

Abstract: Autoregressive video diffusion models have enabled real-time, action-conditioned world generation. However, sustaining a persistent world, where revisiting a previously seen viewpoint yields consistent content, remains an open problem. Full KV-cache attention preserves this consistency but breaks real-time constraints: memory footprint and attention cost grow linearly with rollout length. Sliding window inference restores throughput but discards long-term consistency. We propose WorldKV, a training-free framework with two components: World Retrieval and World Compression. World Retrieval stores evicted KV-cache chunks in GPU/CPU memory and selectively retrieves scene-relevant chunks via camera/ action correspondence, inserting them back into the native attention window without re-encoding. World Compression prunes redundant tokens within each chunk via key-key similarity to an anchor frame, halving per-chunk storage to fit 2x more history under a fixed budget. On Matrix-Game-2.0 and LingBot- World-Fast, WorldKV matches or exceeds full-KV memory fidelity at roughly 2x the throughput, and is competitive with memory-trained baselines without any fine-tuning. Project Page: https://cvlab-kaist.github.io/WorldKV/

</details>


### [132] [Spectral Tail Auxiliary Learning for AI-Generated Image Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22751)
*Xingyi Li,Jiahui Zhang,Yiheng Li,Yun Cao,Wenhao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: STAL reveals spectral tail uplift in generated images and uses a tail-aware frequency teacher to inject spectral-tail cues into a spatial detector during training, achieving strong generalization without inference-time costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As generative image quality improves, detection via frequency cues becomes harder; spectral regularities are not well understood. The authors propose spectral tail uplift as a robust, architecture-agnostic cue and aim to leverage it for generalizable detection.

Method: 1) Systematically analyze one-dimensional radial log-power spectra to compare real vs generated images. 2) Identify spectral tail uplift—an anomalous uplift in the ultra-high-frequency tail. 3) Develop Spectral Tail Auxiliary Learning (STAL): a tail-aware frequency teacher provides auxiliary spectral-tail supervision to a spatial detector during training. 4) Discard frequency-domain modules at inference to avoid overhead. 5) Evaluate on 9 public datasets across generators and real-world conditions.

Result: STAL achieves strong generalization and stability across different generators and data distributions, with no inference overhead due to discarding frequency modules at test time; demonstrated on 9 public datasets.

Conclusion: Spectral tail uplift is a robust structural cue for AI-generated image detection. STAL effectively exploits this cue via auxiliary learning, delivering generalizable detection without extra inference cost.

Abstract: As generative image models evolve rapidly, the perceptual gap between generated and real images continues to narrow, making AI-generated image detection increasingly challenging. Many existing methods exploit frequency-domain cues for detection, typically described as frequency-domain artifacts or high-frequency discrepancies. However, the specific and recurring spectral regularities remain insufficiently understood and characterized. In this paper, we systematically analyze the one-dimensional radial log-power spectra of real and generated images. We find that generated images do not necessarily exhibit higher or lower energy across the entire spectrum or high-band range. Instead, their spectra deviate from the power-law decay and show an anomalous uplift in the ultra-high-frequency tail. We term this phenomenon spectral tail uplift. We further attribute this phenomenon to nonlinear harmonic accumulation in trained generative models, suggesting that it can serve as a structural cue across generative architectures. Based on this observation, we propose Spectral Tail Auxiliary Learning (STAL), a frequency-domain auxiliary supervision framework for generalizable AI-generated image detection. STAL transfers spectral-tail cues from a tail-aware frequency teacher to a spatial detector during training, while all frequency-domain modules are discarded at inference time. Consequently, STAL introduces no inference overhead. Extensive experiments on 9 public datasets show that STAL achieves strong generalization and stability across generators, data distributions, and real-world scenarios.

</details>


### [133] [Synthetic Data Alone is Enough? Rethinking Data Scarcity in Pediatric Rare Disease Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22767)
*Ganlin Feng,Yuxi Long,Erin Lou,Lianghong Chen,Zihao Jing,Pingzhao Hu,Wei Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Synthetic-only training for pediatric rare-disease facial recognition can match real-data baselines when scaled, suggesting high-fidelity synthetic data can approximate clinically meaningful distributions and enable privacy-preserving educational resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address extreme data scarcity, privacy constraints, and limited data sharing in pediatric settings that hinder computer-vision-based diagnosis and genetic counseling.

Method: Train models exclusively on phenotype-aware synthetic facial images at increasing scales across multiple backbones; compare against real-data-only baselines for a pediatric rare-disease recognition task; assess performance as a function of synthetic data scale and backbone diversity.

Result: At sufficient scale, synthetic-only training achieves performance comparable to real-data-only baselines across multiple backbones, indicating high-fidelity synthetic data can approximate clinically meaningful distributions.

Conclusion: High-quality synthetic pediatric facial data can serve as privacy-preserving resources for genetic education and counseling, improving data efficiency and expanding accessible visual tools in children’s healthcare.

Abstract: Children with rare genetic diseases often exhibit distinctive facial phenotypes, yet developing computer vision systems for early diagnosis remains challenging due to extreme data scarcity, privacy constraints, and limited data sharing in pediatric settings. These challenges not only hinder automated diagnosis but also restrict the availability of visual resources for clinical genetic counseling. While prior work has shown that synthetic data can augment real datasets and preserve phenotype-level semantics, it remains unclear whether synthetic data alone is sufficient for learning in ultra-low-resource pediatric settings. In this work, we study the synthetic-only regime for pediatric rare disease recognition. Under a controlled experimental setup, models are trained exclusively on phenotype-aware synthetic facial images at increasing scales. We find that synthetic-only training achieves performance comparable to real-data-only baselines at sufficient scale across multiple backbones, suggesting that high-fidelity synthetic data can approximate clinically meaningful distributions. These findings together further enable the use of synthetic pediatric facial images as privacy-preserving resources for genetic education and counseling, supporting clinician training and patient communication. Our results highlight the potential of computer vision to improve data efficiency and expand accessible visual tools in children's healthcare.

</details>


### [134] [DecQ: Detail-Condensing Queries for Enhanced Reconstruction and Generation in Representation Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22777)
*Tianhang Wang,Yitong Chen,Wei Song,Zuxuan Wu,Min Li,Jiaqi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DecQ adds lightweight detail-condensing queries to RAEs to recover fine-grained information from intermediate VFM features, enabling better reconstruction while preserving fast, strong generation with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Freezing vision foundation models (VFMs) preserves semantic space but limits reconstruction; fine-tuning or reconstruction signals typically hurts generative fidelity. The goal is to mitigate the reconstruction–generation trade-off without sacrificing the VFM’s semantic space.

Method: Introduce a set of lightweight detail-condensing queries (8 queries) produced by condenser modules that extract fine-grained information from intermediate VFM features. These queries are integrated into the decoder and are jointly generated with patch tokens during modeling. Information from both shallow and deep VFM layers is aggregated to support both reconstruction and generation.

Result: Reconstruction quality improves from PSNR 19.13 dB to 22.76 dB with only 3.9% extra computation and 8 additional queries. In generative modeling, DecQ attains 3.3× faster convergence, achieving FID 1.41 without guidance and 1.05 with guidance.

Conclusion: DecQ effectively mitigates the reconstruction–generation trade-off in RAEs by injecting minimal, targeted detail information from multiple VFM layers, yielding substantial gains in reconstruction and faster, higher-fidelity generation with modest computational overhead.

Abstract: Representation Autoencoders (RAEs) leverage frozen vision foundation models (VFMs) as tokenizer encoders, providing robust high-level representations that facilitate fast convergence and high-quality generation in latent diffusion models. However, freezing the VFM inherently constrains its spatial reconstruction capacity, limiting fine-grained generation and image editing; in contrast, incorporating reconstruction-oriented signals via fine-tuning disrupts the pretrained semantic space and degrades generative fidelity. To address this trade-off, we propose DecQ, a simple yet effective framework for RAEs. Specifically, DecQ introduces lightweight detail-condensing queries that extract fine-grained information from intermediate VFM features through condenser modules. These queries are incorporated into the decoder to support reconstruction and are jointly generated with patch tokens during generative modeling. By aggregating information from both shallow and deep layers, DecQ effectively mitigates the reconstruction--generation trade-off, improving both reconstruction quality and generative performance. Our experiments demonstrate that: (1) with only 8 additional queries and 3.9% extra computation, DecQ improves reconstruction over the frozen DINOv2-based RAE, increasing PSNR from 19.13 dB to 22.76 dB; and (2) for generative modeling, DecQ achieves 3.3$\times$ faster convergence than RAE, attaining an FID of 1.41 without guidance and 1.05 with guidance.

</details>


### [135] [Sensor2Sensor: Cross-Embodiment Sensor Conversion for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22809)
*Jiahao Wang,Bo Sun,Yijing Bai,Vincent Casser,Songyou Peng,Zehao Zhu,Meng-Li Shih,Xander Masotto,Shih-Yang Su,Kanaad V Parvate,Tiancheng Ge,Linn Bieske,Dragomir Anguelov,Mingxing Tan,Chiyu Max Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-based framework (Sensor2Sensor) translates monocular dashcam video into a high-fidelity, multi-modal AV sensor suite (multi-view images + LiDAR) using 4D Gaussian Splatting for reconstruction and novel-view rendering to create paired training/validation data from in-the-wild footage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome scarcity and limited diversity of proprietary AV data by leveraging large-scale, unstructured in-the-wild dashcam footage, addressing the lack of paired multi-modal training data and protecting long-tail scenario coverage for ADS validation.

Method: Convert real AV logs to dashcam-like videos via 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) reconstruction and novel-view rendering, then apply a diffusion-based generative model to perform the cross-domain translation from monocular dashcam input to a multi-modal sensor output (multi-view camera images + LiDAR point clouds).

Result: Comprehensive quantitative evaluations demonstrate fidelity and realism of the generated sensor data; practical utility shown by converting challenging in-the-wild internet and dashcam footage into realistic, multi-modal formats, enabling use of external data for AV development.

Conclusion: Sensor2Sensor enables leveraging external, large-scale data sources for autonomous-vehicle development by generating realistic, multi-modal training/validation data from monocular dashcam footage.

Abstract: Robust training and validation of Autonomous Driving Systems (ADS) require massive, diverse datasets. Proprietary data collected by Autonomous Vehicle (AV) fleets, while high-fidelity, are limited in scale, diversity of sensor configurations, as well as geographic and long-tail-behavioral coverage. In contrast, in-the-wild data from sources like dashcams offers immense scale and diversity, capturing critical long-tail scenarios and novel environments. However, this unstructured, in-the-wild video data is incompatible with ADS expecting structured, multi-modal sensor inputs for validation and training. To bridge this data gap, we propose Sensor2Sensor, a novel generative modeling paradigm that translates in-the-wild monocular dashcam videos into a high-fidelity, multi-modal sensor suite (AV logs) comprising multi-view camera images and LiDAR point clouds. A core challenge is the lack of paired training data. We address this by converting real AV logs into dashcam-style videos via 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) reconstruction and novel-view rendering. Sensor2Sensor then utilizes a diffusion architecture to perform the generative conversion. We perform comprehensive quantitative evaluations on the fidelity and realism of the generated sensor data. We demonstrate Sensor2Sensor's practical utility by converting challenging in-the-wild internet and dashcam footage into realistic, multi-modal data formats, further unlocking vast external data sources for AV development.

</details>


### [136] [MotiMotion: Motion-Controlled Video Generation with Visual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22818)
*Lee Hsin-Ying,Hanwen Jiang,Yiqun Mei,Jing Shi,Ming-Hsuan Yang,Zhixin Shu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MotiMotion: a reasoning-then-generation framework for motion-controlled image-to-video, using a training-free vision-language reasoner to refine trajectories and hallucinate secondary motions, plus a confidence-aware control scheme and a new benchmark (MotiBench); reports more plausible, interaction-rich videos and human preference over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current motion-controlled image-to-video methods rely on sparse, imprecise, and causally incomplete user-provided trajectories, leading to unnatural outputs that miss secondary causal effects. A causally grounded, commonsense-consistent approach is needed.

Method: Propose MotiMotion with a reasoning-then-generation paradigm. Employ a training-free vision-language reasoner to refine primary image-space trajectories and hallucinate plausible secondary motions. Introduce a confidence-aware control scheme to modulate guidance strength, allowing close following of high-confidence plans while correcting artifacts from low-confidence inputs using internal generative priors. Create MotiBench, an image-to-video benchmark focused on interaction-centric scenes where new events are triggered by motion. Evaluate via VLM-based metrics and a human study.

Result: The approach yields videos with more plausible object behaviors and interactions; VLM-based evaluation and human studies show improvements and a preference over existing methods.

Conclusion: MotiMotion improves motion naturalness and interaction plausibility in image-to-video generation by combining reasoning-based trajectory refinement, hallucination of secondary motions, and confidence-aware guidance, with the MotiBench benchmark enabling systematic evaluation.

Abstract: Current motion-controlled image-to-video generation models rigidly follow user-provided trajectories that are often sparse, imprecise, and causally incomplete. Such reliance often yields unnatural or implausible outcomes, especially by missing secondary causal consequences. To address this, we introduce MotiMotion, a novel framework that reformulates motion control as a reasoning-then-generation problem. To encourage causally grounded and commonsense-consistent interactions, we leverage a training-free vision-language reasoner to refine image-space coordinates of primary trajectories and to hallucinate plausible secondary motions. To further improve motion naturalness, we propose a confidence-aware control scheme that modulates guidance strength, enabling the model to closely follow high-confidence plans while correcting artifacts under low-confidence inputs with its internal generative priors. To support systematic evaluation, we curate a new image-to-video benchmark, MotiBench, consisting of interaction-centric scenes where new events are triggered by motion. Both VLM-based evaluation and a human study on MotiBench demonstrate that MotiMotion produces videos with more plausible object behaviors and interaction, and is preferred over existing approaches.

</details>


### [137] [Cambrian-P: Pose-Grounded Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22819)
*Jihan Yang,Zifan Zhao,Xichen Pan,Shusheng Yang,Junyi Zhang,Bingyi Kang,Hu Xu,Saining Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Cambrian-P: a video MLLM augmented with per-frame learnable camera tokens and a pose regression head, delivering improved spatial reasoning and pose estimation, highlighting camera pose as a fundamental signal for video understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal LLMs for video treat frames as isolated 2D snapshots with weak cross-frame geometry. Incorporating persistent camera pose provides a lightweight supervisory signal to improve reasoning about the physical scene and enable stronger generalization.

Method: Integrate per-frame learnable camera tokens and a pose regression head into a video MLLM (Cambrian-P), employ a careful sampling scheme, and train with both ground-truth and pseudo-annotated poses; evaluate on VSI-Bench and eight other benchmarks; report streaming pose estimation on ScanNet.

Result: Achieves 4.5–6.5% gains on VSI-Bench; generalizes to eight additional spatial/general video QA benchmarks; sets state-of-the-art performance for streaming pose estimation on ScanNet; pseudo-annotated poses from in-the-wild video further improve general video QA performance.

Conclusion: Camera pose serves as a fundamental supervisory signal for video models that reason about the physical world. Incorporating pose into video LLMs yields consistent gains in spatial reasoning and broader QA tasks, suggesting pose-aware architectures can enhance generalization in video understanding.

Abstract: Camera pose matters. The position and orientation of each viewpoint define a shared spatial coordinate frame that relates observations across video frames. Yet this signal is largely absent from multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) for video understanding, which process frames as isolated 2D snapshots, instead of the persistent scene humans perceive. We revisit pose as a lightweight supervisory signal and introduce Cambrian-P, a video MLLM augmented with per-frame learnable camera tokens and a pose regression head. With a carefully designed sampling scheme, the model achieves substantial gains of 4.5-6.5% on spatial reasoning benchmarks such as VSI-Bench, generalizes across eight additional spatial and general video QA benchmarks, and, as a byproduct, achieves state of the art streaming pose estimation on ScanNet. Surprisingly, training on pseudo-annotated poses from in-the-wild video further improves general video QA benchmarks, showing pose helps beyond spatial reasoning. Together, these results position camera pose as a fundamental signal for video models that reason about the physical world.

</details>


### [138] [Which Way Did It Move? Diagnosing and Overcoming Directional Motion Blindness in Video-LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22823)
*Jongseo Lee,Hyuntak Lee,Sunghun Kim,Sooa Kim,Jihoon Chung,Jinwoo Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Video-LLMs struggle with perceiving 2D motion direction in simple videos due to a direction binding gap; the authors diagnose the issue, introduce MoDirect for motion-direction tuning/evaluation, and DeltaDirect, a projector-level objective that predicts 2D motion vectors from feature deltas, yielding large gains on synthetic data and notable gains on real data without sacrificing general video performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diagnose a foundational perceptual failure in Video-LLMs—directional motion blindness—where models cannot bind faint motion-direction signals to the correct verbal answer, even on simple left/right/up/down motions.

Method: Trace motion-direction information through the Video-LLM pipeline to show linear accessibility in vision encoder, projector, and LLM hidden states, but identify a direction binding gap at readout. Propose MoDirect, a dataset family for motion-direction tuning/evaluation, and DeltaDirect, a projector-level objective that predicts normalized 2-D motion vectors from adjacent-frame feature deltas. Evaluate on MoDirect-SynBench and MoDirect-RealBench with instruction tuning.

Result: DeltaDirect substantially closes the binding gap: MoDirect-SynBench accuracy rises from 25.9% to 85.4% after tuning. On MoDirect-RealBench, it improves real-world motion-direction accuracy by 21.9 points over a vanilla baseline without real-world tuning, while preserving standard video-understanding performance.

Conclusion: There exists a direction binding gap in Video-LLMs for motion direction; synthetic instruction tuning helps but generalization to real-world data remains challenging. DeltaDirect provides a targeted, plug-in objective to align perceptual signals with verbal outputs and MoDirect offers standardized tuning/evaluation for motion-direction tasks.

Abstract: Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) have made rapid progress on temporal video understanding, yet many fail at a basic perceptual primitive: signed image-plane motion direction. On simple videos of a single object moving left, right, up, or down, most Video-LLMs perform near chance, with above-chance cases largely attributable to prediction biases rather than genuine direction understanding. We call this failure directional motion blindness. We localize the failure by tracing motion direction information through the Video-LLM pipeline. Motion direction remains linearly accessible from the vision encoder, projector, and LLM hidden states, but the readout fails to bind this signal to the correct verbal answer option, revealing a direction binding gap. Although synthetic motion direction instruction tuning reduces this gap on the source domain, motion direction concept vector analysis shows that visual complexity weakens the signal magnitude and limits out-of-domain generalization. We introduce MoDirect, a dataset family for motion direction instruction tuning and evaluation, and DeltaDirect, a diagnosis-driven, projector-level objective that predicts normalized 2-D motion vectors from adjacent-frame feature deltas. On MoDirect-SynBench, instruction tuning with DeltaDirect improves motion direction accuracy from 25.9% to 85.4%. On MoDirect-RealBench, DeltaDirect improves real-world motion direction accuracy by 21.9 points over the vanilla baseline without real-world tuning data, while preserving standard video-understanding performance. Code: https://github.com/KHU-VLL/DeltaDirect

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [139] [Benchmarking and Improving Monitors for Out-Of-Distribution Alignment Failure in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21602)
*Dylan Feng,Pragya Srivastava,Cassidy Laidlaw*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MOOD benchmark for detecting OOD alignment failures in LLM monitoring; guard models struggle with OOD; combining guard models with Mahalanobis and perplexity-based OOD detectors improves recall; scaling benefits favor OOD detection; conclusion: OOD detection is essential.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OOD failures in LLM safety are common and hard to detect; need robust evaluation of monitoring pipelines; gap: guard models generalize poorly to OOD prompts; propose integrated approach.

Method: Construct MOOD with a restricted training set to train monitors; evaluate seven test sets of diverse alignment failures; test four OOD detectors; analyze guard-only vs guard+OOD detector configurations; assess scaling trends.

Result: Recall improved from 39% to 45% when combining guard with Mahalanobis + perplexity; positive scaling with model size for combined monitors; OOD detection yields larger gains than simply increasing guard model size; robust baseline established.

Conclusion: OOD detection should be a crucial component of LLM monitoring; MOOD provides a foundation for future work in this area.

Abstract: Many safety and alignment failures of large language models (LLMs) occur due to out-of-distribution (OOD) situations: unusual prompt or response patterns that are unforeseen by model developers. We systematically study whether LLM monitoring pipelines can detect these OOD alignment failures by introducing a benchmark called Misalignment Out Of Distribution (MOOD). It is difficult to find failures that are truly OOD for off-the-shelf models trained on vast safety datasets. We sidestep this by including a restricted training set in MOOD that we use to train our own monitors, as well as seven test sets with diverse alignment failures that are outside the training distribution. Using MOOD, we find that guard models (safety classifiers) often fail to generalize OOD. To fix this, we propose combining guard models with OOD detectors. We test four types of OOD detectors and find that a combination of a guard model with Mahalanobis distance and perplexity-based OOD detectors can improve recall from 39% to 45%. We also establish positive scaling trends across model scales for monitors that combine a guard model and OOD detector; we find that incorporating OOD detection into monitoring achieves a higher recall gain than using a guard model with 20 times more parameters. Our work suggests that OOD detection should be a crucial component of LLM monitoring and provides a foundation for further work on this important problem.

</details>


### [140] [TO-Agents: A Multi-Agent AI Pipeline for Preference-Guided Topology Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21622)
*Isabella A. Stewart,Hongrui Chen,Faez Ahmed*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An AI-driven, multi-agent framework (TO-Agents) links natural-language design intent to topology optimization, using an independent judge and iterative revisions to produce preference-aligned designs and enable end-to-end prototyping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between qualitative design goals (style, experience, manufacturability) and solver parameters, improving efficiency, alignment with intent, and enabling end-to-end design-to-prototype workflows.

Method: Convert human problem description into validated solver inputs; run topology optimization; render the 3D topology; apply multi-view vision-language reasoning with an independent judge agent to critique results and revise solver parameters; perform four revision cycles across ten replicates; compare against an ablated pipeline; include a manufacturing agent for post-processing for additive manufacturing.

Result: In two long-horizon tasks (cantilever beam, phone-stand), 60% of trials produced at least one design aligned with the aesthetic preference across four revisions; up to 6x more successful than the ablated pipeline; judge scores and human evaluations indicate effective parameter leverage, recoverability from poor revisions, and expanded exploration; a manufacturing agent enables end-to-end intent-to-prototype design; identified failure modes (overshooting, selective memory, misplaced tools, incorrect parameter reasoning).

Conclusion: Agentic topology optimization can shift designers from low-level parameter tuning toward high-level specification of form and function, while highlighting safeguards needed for reliable autonomous engineering design.

Abstract: Topology optimization can generate efficient structures, but designers often must manually translate qualitative intent, such as desired visual style, product experience, or manufacturability into solver settings that are not directly tied to those preferences. We present TO-Agents, a multi-agent AI framework that connects natural-language design intent with iterative topology optimization. The framework converts a human-provided problem description into validated solver inputs, runs a topology optimization solver, renders the resulting 3D topology, and uses multi-view vision-language reasoning with an independent judge agent to critique each result and revise solver parameters. We evaluate the framework on two long-horizon design tasks: a cantilever beam benchmark and a phone-stand product design. In both tasks, the designer specifies an aesthetic preference for hierarchically branched structures inspired by natural tree morphologies, and the system performs four revision cycles across ten independent replicates. TO-Agents produces at least one preference-aligned design in 60% of trials for each case study, corresponding to up to 6x more successful trials than an ablated pipeline without visual or historical feedback. Judge scores and human evaluations show that the pipeline can identify effective parameter levers, recover from poor revisions, and expand design exploration. A manufacturing agent further post-processes top-ranked designs for additive manufacturing, enabling end-to-end intent-to-prototype design. We also identify failure modes, including overshooting, selective memory, misplaced tools, and incorrect parameter reasoning. These results suggest that agentic topology optimization can shift designers from low-level parameter tuning toward higher-level specification of form and function, while highlighting safeguards needed for reliable autonomous engineering design.

</details>


### [141] [The Shape of Testimony: A Scalable Framework for Oral History Archive Comparison](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21623)
*Itamar Trainin,Renana Keydar,Amit Pinchevski*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Large-scale computational re-evaluation of USC Shoah Foundation vs Yale Fortunoff oral histories; finds overlaps and nuance in the structuredness dichotomy; introduces a scalable framework for digital oral history analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reassess a longstanding dichotomy in Holocaust survivor testimonies; provide a replicable, scalable method to quantify 'structuredness' and compare archives; enable broader applications in digital humanities and oral history.

Method: Analyze over 1,600 testimonies using discourse segmentation, topic modeling, and LLM-based analysis; quantify structuredness via topic coherence, interviewer-survivor dynamics, and distribution of question types; cross-archive comparisons; reproducible, scalable pipeline.

Result: Results largely corroborate prior distinctions, but reveal significant overlaps within and across interviews; the structure vs. free-form dichotomy is not strictly binary; narratives exhibit common patterns across archives.

Conclusion: Proposes a scalable, replicable framework for comparative corpus analysis; demonstrates applicability to digital oral history, narrative analysis, and annotation platform design; encourages broader adoption of computational methods in humanities archives.

Abstract: Researchers in Holocaust studies have often distinguished between two styles of oral survivor testimony: the USC Shoah Foundation's interviews tend to follow a structured, interviewer-guided format, whereas the Yale Fortunoff Video Archive generally favors a more free-form, open-ended style. This distinction has influenced both scholarly research and the development of later archives. In this study, we critically examine that claim by conducting a large-scale computational analysis of more than 1,600 testimonies from both collections. Leveraging discourse segmentation, topic modeling, and large language model (LLM) based analysis, we quantify the "structuredness" level of testimonies through topic coherence, interviewer-survivor dynamics, and the distribution of question types. Our results generally corroborate the structural differences identified in earlier research, while also revealing significant overlaps between the collections, both within individual interviews and across common narrative patterns. This complicates the simple "structured vs. free-form" dichotomy often applied to these oral histories. Beyond revisiting a foundational claim in Holocaust studies, our work provides a scalable, replicable framework for comparative corpus analysis. As a proof of concept, it suggests broader applications for digital oral history, narrative analysis, and the design of citizen-science annotation platforms.

</details>


### [142] [MindLoom: Composing Thought Modes for Frontier-Level Reasoning Data Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21630)
*Haiyang Shen,Taian Guo,Xuanzhong Chen,Mugeng Liu,Weichen Bi,Wenchun Jing,Sixiong Xie,Zhuofan Shi,Yudong Han,Chongyang Pan,Siqi Zhong,Jinsheng Huang,Ming Zhang,Yun Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MindLoom provides a compositional thought-mode engineering framework to synthesize frontier-level reasoning data with controllable difficulty, improving model performance across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle to systematically generate diverse, frontier-level reasoning data with stable difficulty control; existing synthesis methods lack visibility into structural factors driving problem difficulty.

Method: Decompose solutions into chains of thought modes; train a retrieval model to map problem states to compatible thought modes; compose new problems by iteratively applying retrieved thought modes to seed questions with distribution-aligned sampling; roll-out-based judging to label difficulty and provide supervised fine-tuning data.

Result: Fine-tuned models trained on MindLoom data outperform base models, distillation, and external-data baselines across nine benchmarks spanning five STEM disciplines and four mathematical reasoning tasks; ablations confirm contributions of components; MindLoom covers broad reasoning patterns with controllable difficulty; code released at the provided URL.

Conclusion: MindLoom offers a scalable, controllable framework for frontier-level data synthesis that enhances reasoning capabilities of LLMs, with broad applicability across disciplines and model families.

Abstract: Although LLMs have made substantial progress in reasoning, systematically producing frontier-level reasoning data remains difficult. Existing synthesis methods often have limited visibility into the structural factors that govern problem difficulty, which can result in narrow diversity and unstable difficulty control. In this work, we view the difficulty of a reasoning problem as arising from the accumulation of atomic knowledge-reasoning transformations, which we term thought modes. Building on this perspective, we propose MindLoom, a framework for synthesizing frontier-level reasoning data through compositional thought mode engineering. Given a collection of hard problems with verified solutions, MindLoom first decomposes those solutions into thought mode chains that reveal each problem's construction logic. It then trains a retrieval model that matches problem states to compatible thought modes, providing guidance on which reasoning challenges to introduce during synthesis. New problems are composed by iteratively applying retrieved thought modes to seed questions, with distribution-aligned sampling to encourage diverse reasoning coverage. Finally, a rollout-based judging stage labels generated questions by difficulty and supplies judged-correct responses for supervised fine-tuning. We evaluate MindLoom on nine benchmarks covering five STEM disciplines and four mathematical reasoning tasks across multiple model families and sizes. Models fine-tuned on MindLoom-generated data achieves favorable performances over base models, distillation, and external-data baselines across the reported benchmarks. Ablation studies indicate the contribution of each component, and further analysis suggests that MindLoom covers a broad range of reasoning patterns while maintaining useful difficulty control. We have open-sourced our implementation at https://github.com/EachSheep/MindLoom.

</details>


### [143] [AOP-Wiki EMOD 3.0: Data Model Expansions and Content Evaluation Framework for Using Agentic AI to Improve Integration between AOPs and New Approach Methodologies (NAMs)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21645)
*Virginia K. Hench,J. Harry Caufield,Sierra A. T. Moxon,Jason M. O'Brien,Stephen W. Edwards*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AOP-Wiki EMOD 3.0 advances data model expansion to support AI-readiness, evidence quality, and integration with NAMs, enabling computational and quantitative AOPs for next-gen risk assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AOP-Wiki limitations in data model and infrastructure hinder growth and evolution; AI-enabled data modernization and alignment with AOP principles can enhance aggregation, structuring, and regulatory usefulness of AOP information.

Method: Development and demonstration of AOP-Wiki EMOD 3.0 as an evidence model prototype focusing on data model expansion, evidence structuring for FAIRness and AI-readiness, internal quality improvements, and better integration between the AOP framework and NAMs.

Result: Concrete demonstration of expanded data model capabilities and a vision for improved AI-readiness and NAM integration, enabling computational AOPs and qAOPs.

Conclusion: EMOD 3.0 positions the AOP-Wiki to better serve regulatory science and emergent AOP use in biomedical and One Health contexts by supporting computable, AI-friendly evidence architectures and closer NAM alignment.

Abstract: Adverse Outcome Pathways (AOP) are logic models that causally link biological mechanisms that can be measured in a lab to adverse outcomes, relevant to chemical regulatory endpoints. AOPs contextualize new approach methodologies (NAMs), in vitro and in silico methods used as alternatives to animal testing and the sequential events in an AOP serve as multi-scale models spanning biological scales. The AOP-Wiki serves as the global repository for AOPs. While the AOP-Wiki has played a central role in AOP expansion over the past decade, constraints within the current data model and application infrastructure limit the AOP-Wiki from supporting continued AOP growth and evolution. Yet, the transformative power of agentic AI has re-invigorated AOP-Wiki data modernization efforts at a time when core AOP principles can be harnessed to inform use of AI for aggregating and structuring AOP-relevant information. Seizing upon this momentum, we present AOP-Wiki EMOD 3.0, the third in a series of evidence model prototypes, which concretely demonstrates data model expansions and our vision for how the AOP-Wiki might be transformed to better serve regulatory science and emergent use of AOPs in biomedical and One Health contexts. We aim to lay a foundation to support computationally-generated AOPs and quantitative AOPs (qAOPs) by focussing on solutions for AOP-Wiki internal quality improvement, evidence structuring to enhance AOP FAIRness and AI-readiness, and improved integration between the AOP framework and NAMs to better serve next generation risk assessment.

</details>


### [144] [Investigating Concept Alignment Using Implausible Category Members](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21683)
*Sunayana Rane,Brenden M. Lake,Thomas L. Griffiths*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI concept understanding is probed via implausible category members to reveal how model concept boundaries diverge from human norms, with notable misalignments and safety implications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how AI encodes concept boundaries and whether it aligns with human knowledge, using implausible category membership tests as a complementary probe to traditional plausible membership checks.

Method: Evaluate AI systems' assignments of objects to Rosch and Mervis superordinate categories and to mismatched categories, then compare with human judgments across within-category and cross-category tasks.

Result: Finds meaningful misalignments: e.g., treating words as vehicles or clothing, mislabeling veg as fruit, and assigning non-weapon exemplars to weapons; these misalignments correlate with problematic downstream behaviors.

Conclusion: Concept boundaries in AI can diverge from human expectations; implausible-membership testing reveals safety-relevant misalignments and motivates improved evaluative and training strategies to align AI concepts with human semantics.

Abstract: Developing AI systems with a human-like understanding of everyday concepts is a key step towards developing safe, reliable systems whose behavior makes sense to humans. When probing concept understanding, asking questions about plausible category members (e.g., "Is a car a vehicle?") is likely to recall patterns in the model's vast training data. We pursue an alternative strategy, characterizing the boundaries of conceptual categories by asking about implausible category members (e.g., "Is an olive a vehicle?") to probe the kind of concept-level knowledge we take for granted in fellow humans. We characterize concept boundaries for a set of fundamental concepts by studying AI systems' assignments of objects to superordinate categories from a classic psychological study by Rosch and Mervis, as well as their assignments of the same objects to mismatched superordinate categories. We compare these assignments to those made by human participants on the full range of within-category and cross-category assignment tasks. Our results reveal a range of concepts for which which models differ in meaningful and surprising ways from humans, including treating "words" as belonging to categories like "vehicles" and "clothing," identifying several "vegetable" category members as "fruit," and assigning exemplars from non-weapon categories to the "weapons" category. We also demonstrate how these instances of concept misalignment translate into problematic downstream behavior with implications for AI safety.

</details>


### [145] [The Impact of AI Usage and Informativeness on Skill Development in Logical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21695)
*Shang Wu,Hongyu Yao,Catarina Belem,Shuyuan Fu,Mark Steyvers,Padhraic Smyth*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Higher AI usage can hinder skill development when AI informativeness is low; high-information AI can boost short-run performance but with heterogeneous long-run effects; policy implication: regulate AI access to support skill development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve conflicting evidence on whether AI helps or hinders learning by experimentally manipulating AI usage and informativeness in a controlled reasoning task.

Method: Controlled experiment with a logical reasoning task, on-demand AI assistance; manipulated AI usage levels and AI informativeness; measured immediate performance and retention after AI access ends; comparisons among heavy users, light users, and matched non-users.

Result: Heavy AI usage associated with weaker learning; light AI users perform similarly to non-users; low-information AI provides no short-term or long-term benefit and reduces learning; high-information AI improves short-run performance but post-AI outcomes are heterogeneous; informativeness mediates these effects.

Conclusion: AI can both complement and substitute for human skill development depending on context; policy implication is to regulate AI access/usage to promote skill development, with emphasis on the informativeness of AI feedback.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is being increasingly integrated into human problem-solving, yet its effects on individual skill development remain unclear. We examine how both AI usage and informativeness can shape learning in the context of a controlled logical reasoning task with on-demand access to AI assistance. We find that greater AI usage is associated with weaker skill development: heavy AI users underperform relative to comparable peers, whereas light AI users perform similarly to matched users who do not use AI. We also find in our study that these patterns are mediated by AI informativeness. Low-information AI neither improves immediate performance nor preserves performance after AI assistance is removed, and is linked to weaker learning overall. On the other hand, high-information AI was found to improve short-run performance without reducing post-AI outcomes on average in our experiments, but with heterogeneous effects. Our findings in general suggest that AI can, depending on context, either complement human skill development by amplifying independent reasoning or can act as a substitute that undermines such reasoning, with the implication that regulating AI access and usage will be important for promoting skill development in the presence of AI assistance.

</details>


### [146] [Latent-space Attacks for Refusal Evasion in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21706)
*Giorgio Piras,Raffaele Mura,Fabio Brau,Maura Pintor,Luca Oneto,Fabio Roli,Battista Biggio*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Reframing safety-refusal suppression as a latent-space evasion against a linear probe yields a principled view: ablation acts as a minimum-confidence projection, and a stronger attack (Controlled Latent-space Evasion) pushes representations past the decision boundary to induce compliance, achieving state-of-the-art success across many models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current refusal-ablation methods lack a principled, mechanistic account of how latent-space transformations suppress refusals. A formal framework clarifies why ablations work and exposes their limitations, guiding the development of stronger evasion strategies and informing safety-alignment research.

Method: Model refusals are treated as a binary classification by a linear probe trained to separate refused from answered prompts. Ablation corresponds to projecting onto the probe’s decision boundary (minimum-confidence evasion). The authors propose a Controlled Latent-space Evasion (CL-Evasion) attack that projects representations beyond the boundary with optimized confidence, and evaluate this approach on 15 instruction-tuned, multimodal, and reasoning models, comparing to existing refusal-ablation baselines and jailbreaks.

Result: The CL-Evasion attack achieves state-of-the-art success rates across 15 models, outperforming prior refusal-ablation baselines and specialized jailbreak attacks.

Conclusion: A principled latent-space view explains earlier successes and their boundary-limited nature; pushing representations beyond the decision boundary yields stronger refusal evasion. The framework motivates defenses that resist boundary-crossing attacks and highlights the need to move safety alignment beyond simple ablations.

Abstract: Safety-aligned language models are trained to refuse harmful requests, yet refusal behavior can be suppressed by steering their internal representations. Existing methods do so by ablating a refusal direction from model activations, aiming to remove refusal from the model's residual stream. Despite their empirical success, these methods lack a principled account of the latent-space transformation they induce and why it suppresses refusal. In this work, we recast refusal suppression as a latent-space evasion attack against linear probes trained to separate refused from answered prompts. Under this view, prior work's difference-in-means direction naturally defines such a probe, and its ablation is exactly a projection onto its decision boundary, i.e., a minimum-confidence evasion attack. This perspective not only explains the empirical success of prior work but also admits a key limitation: evasion stops at the decision boundary, motivating the need to push representations further into the compliant region, i.e., where the model answers. We leverage this by proposing a Controlled Latent-space Evasion attack that projects representations past the boundary with an optimized confidence. We achieve state-of-the-art attack success rate across 15 instruction-tuned, multimodal, and reasoning models, outperforming existing refusal-ablation baselines and specialized jailbreak attacks.

</details>


### [147] [AttuneBench: A Conversation-Based Benchmark for LLM Emotional Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21739)
*Kate M. Lubrano,Faisal Sayed,Ankita Rathod,Akshansh,Craver Corbyn Thomas-Smith,Mark E. Whiting,Karina Nguyen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AttuneBench offers a real multi-turn EI benchmark for LLMs with per-turn emotional annotations; shows that emotion recognition, behavior classification, preference prediction, and judged response quality form separable capabilities; preference/quality judgments are more discriminative; provides diagnostic framework across 11 models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing emotional intelligence benchmarks rely on synthetic prompts, single-turn cases, or third-party annotation and fail to measure how models infer and respond to a participant's emotional state across an actual conversation.

Method: Curated 200 genuine multi-turn human–model conversations with anonymized LLMs; participants provided turn-by-turn annotations of their emotional state, the model's behavior, and their preferred responses. Evaluated 11 models on emotion recognition, behavioral classification, preference prediction, and judged response quality.

Result: Model rankings across emotion-recognition, behavioral classification, preference prediction, and response-quality judgments are largely independent, indicating emotionally intelligent behavior decomposes into separable capabilities. Preference alignment and response-quality judgments are substantially more discriminating than emotion-label accuracy.

Conclusion: AttuneBench provides a framework to assess each capability and diagnose model-specific strengths and failure modes in emotionally salient conversations, enabling targeted improvements and understanding of how models adapt to individual users in context.

Abstract: Emotional intelligence (EI), the ability to perceive, understand, and respond appropriately to others' emotional states, is central to human communication, and increasingly important to assess as LLMs assume conversational roles in everyday life. Existing EI benchmarks rely on synthetic prompts, single-turn cases, or third-party annotation. These approaches do not directly measure how models infer and respond to a participant's emotional state over the course of a real conversation. We introduce AttuneBench, a benchmark grounded in 200 genuine multi-turn human-model conversations in which participants conversed with anonymized LLMs and provided turn-by-turn annotations of their emotional state, the model's behavior, and their preferred responses. Across 11 evaluated models, we find that model rankings on emotion recognition, behavioral classification, preference prediction, and judged response quality are largely independent, indicating that emotionally intelligent behavior decomposes into separable capabilities. Preference alignment and response-quality judgments are substantially more model-discriminating than emotion-label accuracy. These results indicate that emotionally intelligent behavior requires predicting what kind of response a specific user wants in context, a distinction that aggregate scoring can obscure and that single-turn or synthetic formats cannot directly capture across turns. AttuneBench provides a framework for assessing each of these capabilities and for diagnosing model-specific strengths and failure modes in emotionally salient conversation.

</details>


### [148] [Who Uses AI? Platforms, Workforce, and AI Exposure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21743)
*Michelle Yin,Burhan Ogut*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Platform AI-logs-based occupation exposure proxies partially reflect platform user base, not just the workforce; estimates vary with platform input and channel; reweighting to BLS workforce shares substantially reduces estimates; there is non-classical measurement error with partial-identification bounds; substitution bias likely understates relative to augmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess validity of using AI-chat platform interaction data to measure occupational exposure and quantify measurement biases/identification issues.

Method: Hold outcome, sample, controls, and estimator fixed while varying only the platform input; compare post-ChatGPT employment coefficient; contrast within-vendor consumer vs enterprise channels with sign disagreements; reweight to BLS workforce shares; formalize non-classical measurement error; derive probability limits and partial-identification bounds for employment elasticities.

Result: Coefficient is highly sensitive, changing by a factor of 1.9 when platform input is varied; within-vendor channels yield estimates that disagree in sign; reweighting to BLS workforce shares attenuates estimates by 42–93%; the analysis formalizes non-classical measurement error and derives bounds; bias understates substitution more than augmentation.

Conclusion: Platform-based exposure measures are fragile and biased; non-classical measurement error and channel heterogeneity complicate inference; use bounds and external reweighting, and interpret elasticity estimates with caution, especially regarding substitution versus augmentation effects.

Abstract: A growing literature uses artificial intelligence platform conversation logs to measure occupation exposure. We show that these scores partly measure platform user base rather than the workforce. Holding outcome, sample, controls, and estimator fixed while varying only the platform input changes the post-ChatGPT employment coefficient by a factor of 1.9, and within-vendor consumer-versus-enterprise channels produce estimates that disagree in sign. Reweighting to Bureau of Labor Statistics workforce shares attenuates estimates by 42 to 93 percent. We formalize the non-classical measurement error, derive probability limits and partial-identification bounds for employment elasticities. The bias understates substitution more than augmentation.

</details>


### [149] [A Causal Argumentation Method for Explainability of Machine Learning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21758)
*Henry Salgado,Meagan R. Kendall,Martine Ceberio*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A method integrating causal discovery with bipolar argumentation and semi-stable semantics to explain AI predictions; validated on two benchmarks against standard post-hoc methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: XAI methods identify relevant features but often do not explain why decisions are made; integrating causality with argument-based reasoning aims to reveal the underlying reasons and counterarguments for predictions.

Method: 1) Identify causal relationships among variables via causal discovery methods; 2) Translate the causal graph into a Bipolar Argumentation Framework (BAF) capturing supportive and opposing interactions among features; 3) Apply semi-stable semantics to derive extensions of features that explain why certain outcomes were chosen.

Result: Evaluated on two benchmark datasets with comparisons to standard post-hoc explainability approaches; the proposed approach provides explanations with explicit causal and argumentative structure, indicating improved interpretability over standard methods.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of combining causal discovery with argumentation for explainable AI; semi-stable semantics yield stable, interpretable sets of contributing features and their interactions.

Abstract: Explainable AI (XAI) methods identify which features are relevant to a model's predictions but often fail to clarify why certain decisions are made. In this work, we present a novel method that integrates causality with argument-based reasoning to explain why models may be making predictions. Our approach first identifies causal relationships among variables using causal discovery methods and then translates these into a Bipolar Argumentation Framework (BAF) to represent supportive and opposing interactions among features. By using semi-stable semantics, we find extensions of features that explain why certain outcomes may have been chosen. We demonstrate our method on two benchmark datasets and compare its results against standard post-hoc explainability approaches.

</details>


### [150] [What Counts as AI Sycophancy? A Taxonomy and Expert Survey of a Fragmented Construct](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21778)
*Meryl Ye,Lujain Ibrahim,Jessica Y. Bo,Myra Cheng,Ida Mattsson,Daniel Vennemeyer,Robert Kraut,Steve Rathje*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a taxonomy and expert survey to standardize AI sycophancy definitions and measurements, revealing fragmentation in the field and outlining governance implications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inconsistent use of 'AI sycophancy' across studies leads to non-comparable results and weak transfer of mitigation strategies; a common vocabulary and framework are needed.

Method: (1) Systematic review of 70 papers to build a taxonomy of sycophantic behaviors (target: beliefs vs. traits/emotions; mode: explicit vs. implicit). (2) Large-scale survey of 106 experts to gauge agreement on which behaviors are sycophantic. Mapping of literature to taxonomy.

Result: Taxonomy reveals two core dimensions and the predominance of overt, belief-focused sycophancy in literature; expert survey shows near-unanimous concern (94.3%) about the problem but substantial disagreement on which specific behaviors qualify as sycophantic.

Conclusion: AI sycophancy encompasses a broad family of behaviors with distinct measurement, intervention, and governance needs. The taxonomy offers a shared vocabulary to improve comparability, evaluation, and policy design.

Abstract: AI sycophancy has become a prominent concern in large language model (LLM) research. Yet the term lacks a consistent definition and has been applied to behaviors ranging from agreeing with a user's false claim to excessively praising the user to withholding corrective feedback. When researchers, companies, and policymakers use the same term to describe different behaviors, evaluation results become difficult to compare, mitigation strategies fail to transfer, and systems that are resistant to one form of sycophancy continue exhibiting other forms. To address this, we make two contributions. First, we reviewed 70 papers on AI sycophancy to develop a taxonomy of how the behavior has been defined and measured. The taxonomy distinguishes (1) whether a model is sycophantic toward a user's positions and beliefs, or toward the user's broader personal traits and emotions, and (2) whether this occurs through explicit, direct language or more implicit, subtle behaviors such as framing, omission, or tone. Mapping existing literature to our taxonomy reveals that current research has focused on overt forms of sycophancy toward users' beliefs, leaving more subtle and person-directed behaviors relatively understudied. Second, we surveyed 106 experts in AI sycophancy and related fields to examine whether researchers agree on which model behaviors are sycophantic. While experts are nearly unanimous in believing that sycophancy is a significant problem in current AI systems (94.3% agree), they disagree substantially on which specific behaviors qualify. Together, these findings demonstrate that AI sycophancy is a broad family of behaviors with different measurement challenges, intervention requirements, and governance implications. Our taxonomy provides a shared vocabulary for understanding and addressing these behaviors.

</details>


### [151] [Trace2Skill: Verifier-Guided Skill Evolution for Long-Context EDA Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21810)
*Zijian Du,Nathaniel Pinckney*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Trace2Skill enables test-time skill evolution for hardware LLM agents tackling complex Verilog design problems by mining rollout traces, building dense diagnostics, and iteratively applying an oracle–mutator–selector loop with bounded verifier feedback to guide search, edits, and recovery without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CVDP tasks require locating verifier-relevant RTL, testbenches, and dependencies inside large repos; sparse, opaque failure signals hamper reliable automated solving. A scalable, non-fine-tuned, test-time method is needed to improve agents across hard CVDP tasks.

Method: Trace2Skill mines repeated rollout traces of an agent, converts successes and failures into dense diagnostics and oracle lessons, and uses an iterative oracle–mutator–selector loop to produce task-specific skills. These skills guide later search, edits, validation, and recovery. The framework also uses bounded-runtime dense verifier feedback to supply sanitized functional observations, linking skill text, verifier evidence, and downstream behavior.

Result: Across hard CVDP tasks that defeat the seed agent (and frontier-coding agents), Trace2Skill with dense verifier feedback substantially improves pass rates and achieves breakthrough passes on previously unsolved tasks, without requiring high-quality fine-tuning data, RTL-model training, or weight updates. The approach is generalizable to other verifiable EDA tasks.

Conclusion: Trace2Skill provides a general, test-time scaling strategy for verifiable EDA problems that can extend beyond digital design to a broader set of verifiable hardware task domains.

Abstract: Complex Verilog Design Problems (CVDP) challenge hardware LLM agents because solving them requires localizing verifier-relevant RTL, testbenches, include paths, and build dependencies inside large repository snapshots, making precise edits, and recovering from sparse hidden-verifier failures. We present Trace2Skill, a test-time scaling framework that improves a hardware agent without RTL-specialized model fine-tuning. Rather than training a new model or only sampling more candidate solutions, Trace2Skill treats the agent's natural-language skill as an evolvable policy. It mines repeated rollout traces for success and failure modes, converts them into dense diagnostics and oracle lessons, and uses an oracle, mutator, and selector loop to produce task-specific skills that guide later search, editing, validation, and recovery. Because final pass/fail labels are often too coarse for hard failures, Trace2Skill also supports bounded runtime dense verifier feedback that returns sanitized functional observations while keeping hidden harnesses and reference solutions inaccessible to the agent. This feedback helps guide skill evolution and agent execution by connecting skill text, verifier evidence, and downstream behavior. Across hard CVDP tasks that defeat the seed CVDP agent, including tasks that also defeat frontier coding agents, Trace2Skill with dense verifier feedback substantially improves task pass rates and produces breakthrough passes on previously unsolved tasks, without requiring high-quality fine-tuning data, specialized RTL model training, or model weight updates. The same framework provides a general test-time scaling strategy that can extend beyond digital design to other verifiable EDA tasks.

</details>


### [152] [Implicit Safety Alignment from Crowd Preferences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21822)
*Qian Lin,Daniel S. Brown*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Safe Crowd Preference-based RL: a hierarchical method that learns safety-aligned skills from crowd preferences and composes them with a high-level policy to enforce safety in downstream tasks, outperforming direct reward combination and matching oracle-safe baselines without explicit safety rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Crowd preference datasets encode safety principles but diversity in user goals makes direct reward shaping fragile; need a way to extract shared safety criteria and transfer to RL to reduce safety costs while preserving task performance.

Method: Introduce a hierarchical framework—extract safety-aligned skills from crowd preferences; a high-level policy composes these skills to solve downstream tasks; avoids relying on explicit safety rewards; experiments in safe RL environments and a preliminary LLM-style task.

Result: Significant reduction in safety costs without explicit safety rewards; task performance comparable to oracle methods trained with ground-truth safety signals.
