Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of aligning RL with safety through crowd-preference-derived skills and hierarchical composition, suggesting a practical path to safety-aware RL without explicit risk rewards; broad applicability to diverse user goals with shared safety principles.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) can reveal implicit objectives such as safety considerations that go beyond task completion. In this work, we focus on the common safety criteria embedded in crowd preference datasets, where different users may express distinct preferences or objectives, yet follow similar safety principles. Our aim is to discover shared safety criteria from crowd preferences and then transfer them to downstream RL tasks to regularize agent behavior and enforce safety. We first show that direct reward combination-optimizing a preference-learned reward model together with downstream task rewards-has inherent limitations. Motivated by this, we propose Safe Crowd Preference-based RL, a hierarchical framework that extracts safety-aligned skills from crowd preferences and composes them via a high-level policy to safely solve downstream tasks. Experiments across safe RL environments and a preliminary LLM-style task with diverse user goals and shared safety constraints demonstrate that our approach substantially lowers safety costs without access to explicit safety rewards, while achieving task performance comparable to oracle methods trained with ground-truth safety signals.

</details>


### [153] [Toward AI VIS Co-Scientists: A General and End-to-End Agent Harness for Solving Complex Data Visualization Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21825)
*Haichao Miao,Zhimin Li,Kuangshi Ai,Kaiyuan Tang,Chaoli Wang,Peer-Timo Bremer,Shusen Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An autonomous agentic harness that designs domain-specific VIS apps from data and high-level tasks, coordinating exploration, planning, implementation, and validation to deliver functional single-page visual analysis apps, validated on IEEE SciVis Contests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce reliance on cross-domain expertise for data visualization and enable autonomous, long-horizon data analysis and visualization design via a modular AI co-scientist capable of end-to-end task execution.

Method: An end-to-end system of coordinating agents with specialized skills for exploratory analysis, planning, environment configuration, implementation, interface validation, and task completion evaluation; produces document/instruction artifacts guiding downstream work; iteratively refines; outputs functional single-page VIS Apps with linked-view behavior; validated on IEEE SciVis Contests across science/engineering domains.

Result: System autonomously generates highly customized, functionally verified single-page VIS Apps with linked-view interactions, aligned to target tasks without manual coding.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of an autonomous VIS design co-scientist, a step toward general AI co-scientist capable of long-horizon data analysis and visualization automation; shows that modular agents can coordinate to deliver domain-specific visualization solutions.

Abstract: The ability to inspect, interpret, and communicate complex data is crucial for virtually any scientific endeavor, but often requires significant expertise outside the core domain ranging from data management and analysis to visualization design and implementation. We present an end-to-end agentic harness that, based on only the data and a high level description of the tasks, independently designs custom visual analysis applications (VIS apps). This represents an important step towards a general AI co-scientist envisioned by many as an autonomous system that can autonomously execute long horizon tasks based on high-level directions. Our proposed VIS co-scientist is an essential component of this broader AI co-scientist vision: a harness that can autonomously analyze data and design visualization solutions using a collection of agents and specialized skills that coordinate exploratory analysis, plan, configure the environment, implement, validate the interface, and most importantly evaluate the overall task completion. Each stage produces document and instruction artifacts that guide downstream work and enable iterative refinement. We validate this approach on IEEE SciVis Contests spanning multiple science and engineering fields. These contests serve as ideal proving grounds because they encode real-world complexity: ambiguous requirements, diverse data modalities, design trade-offs, and task-driven validation. Given only the data and target tasks, our system autonomously produces functional single-page VIS Apps with verified linked-view behavior, highly customized to domain experts' specified tasks and needs.

</details>


### [154] [FLUID: From Ephemeral IDs to Multimodal Semantic Codes for Industrial-Scale Livestreaming Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21832)
*Xinhang Yuan,Zexi Huang,Anjia Cao,Xudong Lu,Zikai Wang,Penghao Zhou,Chang Liu,Wentao Guo,Qinglei Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FLUID is an ID-free livestreaming ranker that uses a cross-domain multimodal encoder to produce discrete hierarchical codes (LUCID) and injects slice-level and room-level tokens into a late-fusion ranking model, achieving online gains on a production platform and addressing cold-start issues inherent to ID-based embeddings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ID-based collaborative filtering struggles in livestreaming where item IDs for rooms are sparsely observed due to short broadcast windows, causing cold-start and poor generalization. A production-scale, ID-free solution is needed.

Method: Train a cross-domain multimodal encoder on short videos and livestreams to produce discrete hierarchical codes (LUCID). Use a late-fusion, ID-free ranking that injects slice-level and room-level LUCID as independent tokens, stabilized by staged warmup during online incremental training.

Result:  deployed on industrial livestreaming recommender with >1B users; online gains: +0.55% Quality Watch Duration, +2.05% Cold-Start Room Views, +0.05% Active Hours.

Conclusion: FLUID is the first framework to completely retire candidate-side item IDs in a production livestream ranker, demonstrating that cross-domain discrete representations (LUCID) can effectively support ID-free ranking with measurable online gains and scalable deployment.

Abstract: Modern recommender systems rely heavily on ID-based collaborative filtering: each item is represented by a unique ID embedding that accumulates collaborative signals from user interactions. Livestreaming recommendation, however, faces a unique challenge in this paradigm: a live room typically broadcasts for only tens of minutes, so its item ID remains poorly learned in a persistent cold-start state and ID-centric ranking models fail to generalize. We present FLUID, the first framework to fully retire the candidate-side item ID from a production-scale livestreaming ranker. FLUID couples a cross-domain multimodal encoder, jointly trained on short videos and livestreams to produce discrete hierarchical codes (LUCID), with a late-fusion, ID-free design that injects slice-level and room-level LUCID as independent tokens, stabilized by a staged warmup under online incremental training. Deployed on our industrial livestreaming recommenders with a cross-platform combined user base of over one billion globally, FLUID delivers significant online gains of +0.55% Quality Watch Duration, +2.05% Cold-Start Room Views, and +0.05% Active Hours.

</details>


### [155] [Planning in the LLM Era: Building for Reliability and Efficiency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21902)
*Michael Katz,Harsha Kokel,Kavitha Srinivas,Shirin Sohrabi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Shifts in LLM-based planning from single-shot or hybrid approaches to constructing symbolic solvers at solution time; the paper reviews three planner-generation categories, notes current limitations, and outlines steps for a more reliable, efficient, and maintainable LLM-driven planning paradigm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs in planning are often unsound or resource-intensive. There is a need for reliable, scalable planners that minimize inference-time dependence on LLMs and support maintainable, verifiable solutions.

Method: Literature-based analysis that categorizes planner-generation methods into three families, critiques their limitations (soundness, completeness, resource use), and outlines a research agenda toward more reliable and efficient LLM-based planner generation.

Result: A conceptual synthesis and critique that articulates a research agenda; no empirical results reported, but a framework for evaluating future methods.

Conclusion: The planning field in the LLM era is realigning toward generation of verifiable, efficient planners with reduced runtime reliance on LLMs; the paper maps current approaches and proposes concrete directions for future work.

Abstract: Growing attention to intelligent agents has put a spotlight on one of their central capabilities: planning. Early attempts to leverage large language models (LLMs) for planning relied on single-shot plan generation, followed by hybrid approaches that coupled LLMs with limited external search. These methods, unsound and incomplete by their very nature, often require substantial resources without yielding better solutions on unseen problems. As the limitations of LLMs become clearer, recent work has shifted toward using them at solution construction time -- generating symbolic solvers for a family of problems that can be verified and then used efficiently at inference time. This trend reflects the growing need for agents that are both reliable and resource-efficient. It also offers a path towards generating maintainable planners with minimal dependence on language models at inference time. In this paper, we argue that this shift reflects a broader realignment of the planning field in the LLM era. We examine three major categories of planner-generation methods, discuss their current limitations, and outline research steps towards a more reliable and efficient LLM-based generation of planners.

</details>


### [156] [AI-Enabled Serious Games: Integrating Intelligence and Adaptivity in Training Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21962)
*Priyamvada Tripathi,Bill Kapralos*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A chapter surveys AI-enabled approaches (LLMs, RL, agent architectures) to support real-time instructional adaptation in serious games, differentiating instructional intelligence from adaptivity, providing a historical synthesis, and outlining practical/challenges such as explainability and validation and the need for empirical evidence on long-term learning outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome persistent design and learner-modeling bottlenecks in serious games by leveraging AI for dynamic scenario variation, contextual feedback, adaptive pacing, and improved learner-state modeling, while clarifying core concepts and outlining research gaps.

Method: Historical synthesis and conceptual/analytic review: define instructional intelligence vs adaptivity; trace the evolution from computer-assisted instruction to ITS, dynamic difficulty adjustment, authoring platforms, learning analytics, and AI-enabled architectures; assess potential roles of LLMs, RL, and agent-based systems in integrated intelligence/adaptivity.

Result: A framework for integrating AI into serious games to enable real-time instruction, highlighting potential benefits and pathways for future systems; it also maps challenges—explainability, validation, computational cost—and underscores the current lack of robust long-term learning outcome evidence for AI-enabled serious games.

Conclusion: AI-enabled serious games hold promise for richer instructional intelligence and adaptive capability, but realization requires addressing explainability, validation, cost, and rigorous empirical validation of long-term learning effects.

Abstract: Serious games are widely used for learning and training across domains such as healthcare, defense, and education. Persistent challenges remain, however, including static scenario design, authoring bottlenecks, limited learner modeling, and difficulty implementing meaningful real-time instructional adaptation. Recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) introduce novel capabilities such as dynamic scenario variation, contextual feedback, adaptive pacing, and learner-state modeling that may help address some of these limitations. At the same time, integrating AI into serious games raises important questions related to validity, transparency, system control, and learner trust. This chapter examines how contemporary AI approaches may support real-time instructional adaptation in serious games. It distinguishes between instructional intelligence, defined as a system's capacity to infer learner knowledge and reason about pedagogically appropriate responses, and adaptivity, defined as the ability to modify instructional actions during interaction. A historical synthesis of adaptive learning systems is presented, tracing developments from early computer-assisted instruction through intelligent tutoring systems (ITS), dynamic difficulty adjustment (DDA), authoring platforms, learning analytics, and recent AI-enabled architectures. Building on this perspective, the chapter discusses how large language models (LLMs), reinforcement learning (RL), and agent-based architectures may contribute to more integrated forms of intelligence and adaptivity in serious games. It also highlights practical and research challenges associated with AI-enabled systems, including explainability, validation, computational cost, and the limited empirical evidence regarding long-term learning outcomes in AI-enabled serious games.

</details>


### [157] [Format-Constraint Coupling in Knowledge Graph Construction from Statistical Tables](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21974)
*Jingxuan Qi,Zhiqiang Ye,Yuxiang Feng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Format-constraint coupling in CSV-derived knowledge graphs can degrade fidelity in country-by-year data; serialization format and schema constraints interact super-additively, with mismatched formats causing catastrophic mismatch. The authors provide CSVFidelity-Bench to enable fidelity-aware evaluation, revealing that standard retrieval modes mask construction quality and direct graph access uncovers substantial gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how data serialization formats and schema constraints affect knowledge-graph fidelity for statistical CSV data, particularly for country-by-year time-series matrices commonly found on open-data portals.

Method: Empirical factorial study (2x2) across 6 datasets, assessing the joint effects of format and schema on fidelity; bootstrap 95% CIs; probing and token ablation to examine surface-form anchoring; controlled variants across format-schema pairings, GraphRAG hosts, and LLM families; comparison of retrieval modes; release of CSVFidelity-Bench (15 datasets: 11 Type-II, 4 Type-III; 1,892 Gold Standard facts across 6 domains).

Result: Observed super-additive interaction between format and schema constraints, with joint effect up to +1.180 in fidelity loss (2x2 factorial, 6 datasets); bootstrap CIs positive on 4/6 datasets; strongest evidence on wide Type-II matrices; schema on mismatched formats causes catastrophic mismatch, reducing fact coverage relative to unconstrained baseline via entity inflation or extraction refusal (4/6 datasets); surface-form anchoring mechanism centered on column-name references; results robust across controlled variants and LLM families, though one LLM family shows partial activation; standard retrieval modes largely mask construction quality (delta ≤ 1pp), while direct graph access reveals gaps up to +47.6pp (p < 0.0001).

Conclusion: Format-constraint coupling is a general risk for faithful data extraction from structured CSV sources; fidelity-aware evaluation is necessary. The authors provide CSVFidelity-Bench to enable benchmarking and highlight the need for direct graph access to diagnose construction quality.

Abstract: An extraction schema should not reduce knowledge graph fidelity. On statistical CSV, however, it can. We study country-by-year time-series matrices, a common layout on open-data portals. In this setting, serialization format and schema constraints interact super-additively. Their joint effect exceeds the sum of independent effects by up to +1.180 (2x2 factorial, 6 datasets). Bootstrap 95% CIs are strictly positive on 4/6 datasets, with strongest evidence on wide Type-II matrices. More critically, a schema applied to a mismatched format can trigger catastrophic mismatch. Fact coverage falls below the unconstrained baseline on 4/6 datasets through entity inflation or extraction refusal. We call this observed pattern format-constraint coupling. Probing and token ablation support a surface-form anchoring explanation centred on column-name references. Controlled variants across format-schema pairings, GraphRAG hosts, and LLM families show the same direction within the measured scope; one LLM family shows only partial activation. The observation also has a diagnostic consequence. Three standard retrieval modes largely mask construction quality (delta <= 1pp), whereas direct graph access exposes gaps up to +47.6pp (p < 0.0001). To support fidelity-aware evaluation, we release CSVFidelity-Bench. It contains 15 datasets, 11 Type-II matrices, 4 Type-III tables, and 1,892 Gold Standard facts across 6 domains.

</details>


### [158] [Echo: Learning from Experience Data via User-Driven Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21984)
*Hande Dong,Xiaoyun Liang,Jiarui Yu,Jiayi Lin,Changqing Ai,Feng Liu,Wenjun Zhang,Rongbi Wei,Chaofan Zhu,Linjie Che,Feng Wu,Xin Shen,Dexu Kong,Xiaotian Wang,Qiuyuan Chen,Bingxu An,Yueting Lei,Qiang Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Echo converts noisy real-world interaction logs into high-quality training signals by systematically harvesting user refinements, enabling continuous learning and improved performance in production.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static, human-labeled data are costly and not scalable, while continuous experience data from agent-environment interactions are abundant but noisy. A framework is needed to turn this experience into actionable, learnable knowledge that aligns with real-world needs.

Method: A generalized framework that captures refinement signals from user interactions and feedback, and integrates them into the training loop by echoing environmental feedback into optimization. It is validated in a production code completion setting.

Result: In production code completion, Echo increased the acceptance rate from 25.7% to 35.7%, demonstrating improved alignment with real-world needs.

Conclusion: Echo can transcend static performance ceilings by leveraging user-driven refinements to continuously align agents with real-world requirements.

Abstract: Static "human data" faces inherent limitations: it is expensive to scale and bounded by the knowledge of its creators. Continuous learning from "experience data" - interactions between agents and their environments - promises to transcend these barriers. Today, the widespread deployment of AI agents grants us low-cost access to massive streams of such real-world experience. However, raw interaction logs are inherently noisy, filled with trial-and-error and low information density, rendering them inefficient for direct model training.
  We introduce Echo, a generalized framework designed to operationalize the transition from raw experience to learnable knowledge, effectively "echoing" environmental feedback back into the training loop for model optimization. In today's agent ecosystem, user refinement serves as a primary source of such feedback: driven by responsibility for the outcome, users rigorously transform flawed agent proposals into verified solutions. These user-driven refinement sequences inherently distill agents' crude attempts into high-quality training signals. Echo systematically harvests these signals to continuously align the agent with real-world needs. Large-scale validation in a production code completion environment confirms that Echo effectively harnesses this pipeline, breaking the static performance ceiling by increasing the acceptance rate from 25.7% to 35.7%.

</details>


### [159] [The Log is the Agent: Event-Sourced Reactive Graphs for Auditable, Forkable Agentic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21997)
*Yohei Nakajima*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ActiveGraph inverts the LM-first agent loop by making an append-only event log the source of truth; a deterministic graph projection reacts to log changes, enabling replay, cheap forking, and full lineage from goals to model calls.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide deterministic replay, affordable run-forking, and end-to-end traceability in agent frameworks, addressing limitations of retrieval-memory systems and supporting self-improving agents.

Method: Use an append-only event log as the single source of truth; maintain a deterministic working graph derived from the log; define behaviors (functions, classes, LLM-backed routines, typed-edge logic) that observe graph changes and emit new events; achieve coordination solely through the shared graph under a determinism contract.

Result: Gives three core properties: (1) deterministic replay of any run from its log, (2) cheap forking at any event without re-running shared prefixes, and (3) end-to-end lineage from high-level goals to individual model calls; includes a worked diligence example and discussion on its suitability for self-improving agents and extensions to BabyAGI lineage and prior graph-memory research.

Conclusion: The append-only graph-substrate appears particularly well suited to self-improving agents, offering reproducibility and traceability, and warranting further exploration beyond retrieval-based memory approaches.

Abstract: Most agent frameworks are built around the language model: a conversation loop comes first, then tools, then rules, and finally a logging layer bolted on for observability, with state persisted as retrievable "memory." We describe ActiveGraph, a runtime that inverts this arrangement. The append-only event log is the source of truth; the working graph is a deterministic projection of that log; and behaviors--ordinary functions, classes, LLM-backed routines, or logic attached to typed edges--react to changes in the graph and emit new events. No component instructs another; coordination happens entirely through the shared graph. This single design decision yields three properties that retrieval-and-summarization memory systems do not provide: deterministic replay of any run from its log, cheap forking that branches a run at any event without re-executing the shared prefix, and end-to-end lineage from a high-level goal down to the individual model call that produced each artifact. We present the architecture, a determinism contract that makes replay sound, and a worked diligence example whose full causal structure is reconstructable from the log alone. We discuss--without claiming to demonstrate--why this substrate is unusually well suited to self-improving agents, and how it extends the BabyAGI lineage and prior graph-memory research.

</details>


### [160] [Active Evidence-Seeking and Diagnostic Reasoning in Large Language Models for Clinical Decision Support](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22047)
*Chen Zhan,Xihe Qiu,Xiaoyu Tan,Xibing Zhuang,Gengchen Ma,Yue Zhang,Shuo Li,Peifeng Liu,Xiaoxiao Ge,Liang Liu,Lu Gan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Interacting, iterative evidence-seeking can hurt AI diagnostic performance compared to static full-context benchmarks; an OSCE-like standardized patient benchmark reveals limitations and emphasizes the need for complementary interactive assessment for safer clinical decision support.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess AI diagnostic performance in interactive, uncertain clinical settings and address the gap where static benchmarks may overestimate capability.

Method: Developed an OSCE-inspired standardized patient simulator and a controlled, reproducible benchmark. Evaluated 468 cases across 15 models, comparing multi-turn evidence-seeking (interactive) versus full-context (static) evaluation. Analyzed diagnostic accuracy and supporting-evidence quality, with error analysis on causes of performance drops.

Result: Multi-turn evidence seeking reduced diagnostic accuracy by 12.75% and lowered supporting-evidence quality by 24.36% relative to full-context evaluation. Errors attributed to premature diagnostic closure and inefficient questioning.

Conclusion: Static full-context benchmarks may overestimate performance in interactive settings; complementary interactive assessment is warranted to improve safety of clinical decision support.

Abstract: Large language models perform well on static medical examinations, yet clinical diagnosis often requires iterative evidence gathering under uncertainty. Building on prior interactive evaluation efforts, we introduce an OSCE-inspired standardized patient simulator and a controlled, reproducible benchmark for active diagnostic inquiry. Across 468 cases and 15 models in our protocol, we observe that multi-turn evidence seeking reduces diagnostic accuracy by 12.75% and lowers supporting-evidence quality by 24.36% relative to full-context evaluation; error analyses associate these drops with premature diagnostic closure and inefficient questioning. Together, these results suggest that static full-context benchmarks may overestimate performance in interactive evidence-seeking settings, motivating complementary interactive assessment for safer clinical decision support.

</details>


### [161] [Enhancing Visual Token Representations for Video Large Language Models via Training-Free Spatial-Temporal Pooling and Gridding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22078)
*Bingjun Luo,Tony Wang,Hanqi Chen,Xinpeng Ding*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ST-GridPool is a training-free visual token enhancement method for Video LLMs that improves token representations without retraining. It combines Pyramid Temporal Gridding (PTG) for multi-grained spatiotemporal interactions and Norm-based Spatial Pooling (NSP) to preserve high-information regions, yielding consistent performance gains on video benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To efficiently compress visual tokens in video-Language models while preserving complex spatiotemporal dynamics, addressing the limitations of simple pooling/interpolation methods commonly used in existing MLLMs (e.g., LLaVA).

Method: ST-GridPool is a training-free, plug-and-play approach comprising two components: (1) Pyramid Temporal Gridding (PTG) which captures multi-grained spatiotemporal interactions via hierarchical temporal gridding, and (2) Norm-based Spatial Pooling (NSP) which maintains information-rich regions by leveraging correlations between token norms and semantic content.

Result: Empirical results on diverse benchmarks show consistent performance improvements for Video LLMs with no retraining required; the authors provide code at the project repository (GitHub).

Conclusion: ST-GridPool offers an efficient and practical solution for enhancing visual token representations in Video LLMs, enabling better video understanding without costly retraining and with a plug-and-play integration.

Abstract: Recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have significantly advanced video understanding tasks, yet challenges remain in efficiently compressing visual tokens while preserving spatiotemporal interactions. Existing methods, such as LLaVA family, utilize simplistic pooling or interpolation techniques that overlook the intricate dynamics of visual tokens. To bridge this gap, we propose ST-GridPool, a novel training-free visual token enhancement method designed specifically for Video LLMs. Our approach integrates Pyramid Temporal Gridding (PTG), which captures multi-grained spatiotemporal interactions through hierarchical temporal gridding, and Norm-based Spatial Pooling (NSP), which preserves high-information visual regions by leveraging the correlation between token norms and semantic richness. Extensive experiments on various benchmarks demonstrate that ST-GridPool consistently enhances performance of Video LLMs without requiring costly retraining. Our method offers an efficient and plug-and-play solution for improving visual token representations. Our code is available in https://github.com/bingjunluo/ST-GridPool.

</details>


### [162] [A Camera-Cooperative ISAC Framework for Multimodal Non-Cooperative UAVs Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22090)
*Wenfeng Wu,Luping Xiang,Kun Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Camera-cooperative ISAC (CC-ISAC) combines coarse camera monitoring with ISAC for fine sensing; introduces V2EDA and MMFE to align visual and echo features and fuse multimodal data; shows large reductions in beam steering and tracking overhead on DeepSense 6G while maintaining angular accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of single-modal sensing and resource contention between sensing and communication in ISAC for non-cooperative UAV detection; need efficient, robust beam steering and tracking.

Method: Vision-to-Echo Data Alignment uses cross-attention to align visual features with echo-domain cues; Multimodal Fusion-Based Estimation combines historical multimodal data with current observations for state estimation.

Result: Average 71% reduction in beam steering overhead; 1.69-11.15% reduction in tracking overhead; maintains high angular accuracy; demonstrates practical resource efficiency and reduced sensing-communication contention on DeepSense 6G.

Conclusion: CC-ISAC provides a viable multimodal ISAC framework for UAV surveillance, improving sensing robustness and resource efficiency; potential for broader ISAC deployment with multimodal perception.

Abstract: The detection of non-cooperative unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) presents significant challenges for Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) systems due to the inherent limitations of single-modal perception and the competition for shared communication and sensing resources. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel Camera-Cooperative ISAC (CC-ISAC) framework that employs multimodal sensing to enable efficient UAV beam steering and tracking. The proposed framework employs cameras for coarse-grained airspace monitoring and utilizes ISAC for fine-grained, high-precision sensing, forming a complementary perception loop that enhances both sensing accuracy and resource efficiency. Within this framework, two key modules are developed: (1) a Vision-to-Echo Data Alignment (V2EDA) model that aligns visual and echo-domain features through cross-attention mechanisms, and (2) a Multimodal Fusion-Based Estimation (MMFE) model that integrates historical multimodal data with current observations for robust state estimation. Extensive evaluations conducted on the DeepSense 6G dataset demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves an average reduction of 71% in beam steering overhead and 1.69-11.15% in tracking overhead while maintaining high angular estimation accuracy. The CC-ISAC framework effectively mitigates resource contention between sensing and communication, enabling reliable UAV surveillance while freeing substantial system resources for additional communication tasks, thereby representing a practical advancement in ISAC system design.

</details>


### [163] [Knowledge Graph Re-engineering Along the Ontological Continuum (extended version)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22093)
*Enrico Daga,Valentina Tamma,Terry Payne*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes the ontological continuum to unify diverse knowledge-graph modelling practices and enable principled integration, especially for neuro-symbolic AI and GenAI automation. It defines two orthogonal axes (semantics vs pragmatics; properties vs affordances) to describe, compare, navigate, and transform KGs, grounded empirically via Formal Concept Analysis and a provenance knowledge case study; outlines five open challenges and invites community engagement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KGs vary from lightweight vocabularies to richly axiomatized ontologies, making integration/reuse brittle. In neuro-symbolic AI, reengineering KGs for new tasks is costly, and GenAI-driven automation lacks principled grounding in the KG space.

Method: Introduce a theoretical construct—the ontological continuum—characterized by two orthogonal distinctions (semantics vs pragmatics, properties vs affordances). Adopt an empirical stance that derives theory from observation of real-world KG engineering practices, with formalization potential via Formal Concept Analysis (FCA). Ground the vision through a provenance knowledge case study to illustrate variance along the continuum, and articulate five open research challenges to guide future work.

Result: A formal, empirically grounded framework (the ontological continuum) for describing, comparing, and transforming knowledge graphs across modelling practices. Provides a vocabulary and structure to navigate the KG space and enables principled reengineering aligned with current and future KG engineering practices.

Conclusion: The ontological continuum offers a shared research agenda for the KG community, outlining five open challenges and inviting collaborative development to systematize knowledge-graph modelling across the full spectrum of practices.

Abstract: Knowledge graphs have become the primary vehicle for data integration and are critical to the success of modern AI, but the diversity of KG modelling practices, from lightweight vocabularies to richly axiomatised ontologies, makes integration and reuse expensive and brittle. This challenge is particularly acute in neuro-symbolic AI, where bridging neural and symbolic components depends on the ability to reengineer KGs to fit new requirements; GenAI now offers unprecedented automation capability, but without a principled understanding of the KG space, such automation remains conceptually ungrounded. We introduce the ontological continuum as that missing conceptualisation, a theoretical construct a theoretical construct whose characterisation framework is defined by two orthogonal distinctions: semantics vs pragmatics, and properties vs affordances; together these define a vocabulary to describe, compare, navigate, and transform KGs across the full range of modelling practices. The methodological stance is empirical: rather than prescribing how KGs should be modelled, the continuum aims to define a theory of the existent, derived from observation of real-world KG engineering practices and whose structure can be made formally explicit, for example, through Formal Concept Analysis (FCA). We ground the vision through a case study on provenance knowledge, showing how a single concern manifests differently across the continuum. We articulate five open research challenges and invite the community to develop the ontological continuum as a shared research agenda.

</details>


### [164] [MPDocBench-Parse: Benchmarking Practical Multi-page Document Parsing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22100)
*Bangbang Zhou,Hangdi Xing,Yifan Chen,Jianjun Xu,Qi Zheng,Feiyu Gao,Zhibo Yang,Shuai Bai,Ming Yan,Jieping Ye,Hongtao Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MPDocBench-Parse is a new multi-page document parsing benchmark with 433 annotated documents (3246 pages) across 15 document types in English and Chinese, enabling end-to-end evaluation and a comprehensive content/structure protocol; baseline models struggle with semantic continuity and hierarchical/visual fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks for document parsing are typically single-page or task-specific and fail to capture realistic multi-page scenarios, semantic continuity across pages, and faithful preservation of visual content and structure.

Method: Construct MPDocBench-Parse by collecting and annotating 433 documents (3246 pages) spanning 15 types in English and Chinese, with diverse layouts. Develop an end-to-end evaluation protocol covering text, tables, and formulas; truncated text and table merging; figure extraction; reading order; and heading hierarchy recovery for cross-page document parsing.

Result: Experimental results show that while existing models achieve reasonable performance on basic text extraction, they exhibit clear deficiencies in semantic continuity integration, visual content parsing, and hierarchical structure recovery in multi-page documents.

Conclusion: MPDocBench-Parse provides a unified benchmark and evaluation framework to advance document parsing toward realistic, multi-page scenarios, enabling more robust assessments of semantic, visual, and structural fidelity.

Abstract: Document parsing converts visually rich documents into machine-readable structured representations, forming a crucial foundation for information systems. Although many benchmarks have been proposed for document parsing, they remain inadequate for realistic scenarios. Existing benchmarks either focus on specific tasks or assess only single-page, text-centric settings, making them insufficient for practical multi-page parsing. Moreover, they lack fine-grained evaluation of semantic continuity, hierarchical structure recovery, and visual content preservation. To address these gaps, we propose MPDocBench-Parse, a benchmark for multi-page document parsing in real-world applications. It contains 433 manually annotated documents with 3,246 pages, covering 15 document types in English and Chinese, with diverse layout styles, and supports document-level end-to-end evaluation. We further design a comprehensive protocol for content fidelity and logical structure, covering text, table, and formula recognition, truncated text and table merging, figure extraction, reading order, and heading hierarchy recovery. Experiments show that, while existing models perform well on basic text extraction, they still suffer clear limitations in semantic continuity integration, visual content parsing, and hierarchical structure recovery. MPDocBench-Parse provides a unified foundation for advancing document parsing toward more realistic scenarios.

</details>


### [165] [ExComm: Exploration-Stage Communication for Error-Resilient Agentic Test-Time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22102)
*Woomin Song,Beomjun Kim,Daewon Choi,Sai Muralidhar Jayanthi,Saket Dingliwal,Jinwoo Shin,Aram Galstyan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ExComm proposes a cross-agent feedback protocol for exploration-stage test-time scaling to curb error propagation via periodic belief audits, verification loops, soft updates, and trajectory diversification; shows consistent gains on AIME/GAIA benchmarks with specified models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address error propagation and limited control in current test-time scaling by leveraging cross-agent conflicts and iterative belief refinement.

Method: Periodic belief audits for cross-agent conflicts; tool-based verification loop; soft belief updates; trajectory diversification to maintain diversity; operates within parallel agentic workflows.

Result: ExComm yields average gains of ~5.7% and 5.0% over baselines across AIME 2024/2025/GAIA using Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite and Qwen3.5-4B; improved error recovery, scaling, diversity, and cost-performance.

Conclusion: ExComm effectively mitigates error propagation and improves performance and diversity with a principled feedback protocol.

Abstract: A common failure mode in long-horizon agentic test-time scaling is error propagation, where factual errors or invalid deductions introduced at intermediate steps persist in the agent's belief state and contaminate later reasoning. Existing test-time scaling methods provide limited control over this process, as they often rely on agents to detect their own mistakes, select among flawed trajectories, or refine solutions only after errors have already shaped the reasoning path. We propose ExComm, a communication protocol for exploration-stage agentic test-time scaling. ExComm is motivated by the empirical observation that the majority of intermediate errors in parallel agentic reasoning produce detectable cross-agent factual conflicts. Leveraging the iterative structure of agentic workflows, ExComm periodically audits agent belief states to detect such conflicts, resolves them through a dedicated tool-based verification loop, and returns concise, targeted feedback to the involved agents. Corrections are incorporated through soft belief updates, which append verified feedback rather than overwriting existing beliefs. Furthermore, to prevent collapsing trajectory diversity due to communication, ExComm further introduces a trajectory diversification module that redirects redundant trajectories toward orthogonal strategies. Experiments on AIME 2024, AIME 2025, and GAIA with Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite and Qwen3.5-4B show that ExComm consistently outperforms strong test-time scaling baselines, achieving average performance gains of 5.7% and 5.0% over the best-performing baselines, respectively. Further analyses demonstrate improved error recovery, favorable scaling behavior, stronger diversity than adapted communication baselines, and the best performance-cost trade-off among the evaluated methods.

</details>


### [166] [ArborKV: Structure-Aware KV Cache Management for Scaling Tree-based LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22106)
*Yeqiu Chen,Ziyan Liu,Zhenxin Huang,Runquan Gui,Hong Wang,Lei Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ArborKV reduces KV memory in Tree-of-Thought (ToT) reasoning by ~4x while maintaining accuracy, using a structure-aware eviction policy that combines a lightweight value estimator with tree-aware allocation and lazy rehydration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ToT-style inference stores KV states for many partial trajectories; memory bottlenecks limit search depth/width under fixed hardware budgets. A memory-efficient mechanism is needed that supports revisits.

Method: Introduce ArborKV: a structure-aware eviction framework; token-extractive eviction with lazy rehydration; uses a lightweight value estimator and tree-aware allocation to decide which KV entries to keep. Prioritizes the active branch and its ancestors for near-term decoding; inactive subtrees are evicted but can be rehydrated on backtracking.

Result: Empirical results on ToT-style reasoning benchmarks show up to ~4x peak KV-memory reduction, with near-full-retention accuracy; enables larger search configurations within fixed device budgets.

Conclusion: Structure-aware KV management with lazy rehydration enables ToT-scale reasoning by mitigating memory bottlenecks while preserving performance, enabling deeper/wider search.

Abstract: Recent progress in LLM reasoning has increasingly shifted from single-pass generation to explicit search over intermediate reasoning states. Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) organizes inference to tree-structured search with branching and backtracking, but it substantially amplifies the Key--Value (KV) cache: retaining KV states for a frontier of partial trajectories quickly becomes a memory bottleneck that limits throughput and constrains search depth and width under fixed hardware budgets. We address this challenge by observing that KV reuse in ToT-style inference is governed by search dynamics: near-term decoding depends primarily on the active branch and its ancestors, whereas inactive subtrees have low short-term reuse probability yet must remain recoverable for backtracking. Motivated by this, we propose ArborKV, a structure-aware eviction framework that couples a lightweight value estimator with a tree-aware allocation policy, and performs purely token-extractive eviction with lazy rehydration to support revisits. Experiments on ToT-style reasoning benchmarks show that ArborKV achieves up to ~4x peak KV-memory reduction while preserving near-full-retention accuracy, enabling larger search configurations under fixed device budgets that would otherwise run out of memory.

</details>


### [167] [Perception or Prejudice: Can MLLMs Go Beyond First Impressions of Personality?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22109)
*Caixin Kang,Tianyu Yan,Sitong Gong,Mingfang Zhang,Liangyang Ouyang,Ruicong Liu,Bo Zheng,Huchuan Lu,Kaipeng Zhang,Yoichi Sato,Yifei Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: New task Grounded Personality Reasoning (GPR) requiring MLLMs to anchor Big Five ratings to observable cues via a chain of rating, reasoning, and grounding; MM-OCEAN dataset with 1,104 videos and 5,320 MCQs; three-tier evaluation plus four failure-mode metrics; benchmark across 27 MLLMs; findings show widespread grounding gaps and low holistic grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses whether MLLMs truly perceive personality through behavioral understanding or rely on superficial pattern matching; existing benchmarks only predict numerical Big Five scores without justification.

Method: Formalize GPR task; release MM-OCEAN dataset with timestamped behavioral observations and evidence-grounded trait analyses; seven cue-grounding MCQ categories; three-tier evaluation (rating, reasoning, grounding) plus four failure-mode metrics (Prejudice Rate, Confabulation Rate, Integration-failure Rate, Holistic-grounding Rate); benchmark 27 MLLMs (13 closed, 14 open).

Result: Across models, 51% of correct ratings are not grounded in retrieved cues; Holistic-Grounding Rate ranges from 0 to 33.5%, revealing a disconnect between getting the right score and right-for-the-right-reason grounding.

Conclusion: Introduces a principled framework and dataset for grounded social cognition in MLLMs; highlights significant grounding gaps and outlines a roadmap to improve interpretable, evidence-based personality reasoning in multimodal models.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are increasingly deployed in human-facing roles where personality perception is critical, yet existing benchmarks evaluate this capability solely on numerical Big Five score prediction, leaving open whether models truly perceive personality through behavioral understanding or merely prejudge through superficial pattern matching. We address this gap with three contributions. (i) A new task: we formalize Grounded Personality Reasoning (GPR), which requires MLLMs to anchor each Big Five rating in observable evidence through a chain of rating, reasoning, and grounding. (ii) A new dataset: we release MM-OCEAN (1,104 videos, 5,320 MCQs), produced by a multi-agent pipeline with human verification, with timestamped behavioral observations, evidence-grounded trait analyses, and seven categories of cue-grounding MCQs. (iii) Benchmark and analysis: we design a three-tier evaluation (rating, reasoning, grounding) plus four sample-level failure-mode metrics: Prejudice Rate (PR), Confabulation Rate (CR), Integration-failure Rate (IR), and Holistic-grounding Rate (HR), and benchmark 27 MLLMs (13 closed, 14 open). The analysis uncovers a striking Prejudice Gap: across the field, 51% of correct ratings are not grounded in retrieved cues, and the Holistic-Grounding Rate spans only 0-33.5%. These findings expose a disconnect between getting the right score and reasoning for the right reason, charting a roadmap for grounded social cognition in MLLMs.

</details>


### [168] [Efficient Agentic Reasoning Through Self-Regulated Simulative Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22138)
*Mingkai Deng,Jinyu Hou,Lara Sá Neves,Varad Pimpalkhute,Taylor W. Killian,Zhengzhong Liu,Eric P. Xing*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes decomposing agentic reasoning into three systems—System II simulative reasoning (world-model-based future-state predictions), System III self-regulation (deciding when/how deeply to plan), and System I reactive execution. It implements SR^2AM as two stages within an LLM's chain-of-thought, with the LLM acting as the world model. Two instantiations are explored: v0.1 (recorded decisions from a prompted multi-module system) and v1.0 (structured plans reconstructed from traces of pretrained reasoning LLMs), trained via supervised learning then RL. Results show competitive Pass@1 with far larger models, reduced reasoning tokens, and longer planning horizons; RL increases horizon significantly with minimal increase in planning frequency. Broadly, self-regulation appears as a general principle for agents’ learning and adaptation beyond planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end reactive policies with emergent planning can be highly inefficient and may not reliably improve accuracy. There is a need to constrain and structure planning—in terms of presence, horizon, and depth—to achieve efficient and reliable agentic reasoning across domains.

Method: Propose SR^2AM architecture: decouple planning (System II) from decision of when/how to plan (System III) and execution (System I). Implemented as latent stages within an LLM's chain-of-thought, using the LLM as the world model. Two instantiations: v0.1 (recorded decisions from a multi-module prompted system) and v1.0 (reconstructed plans from traces of pretrained reasoning LLMs), trained first with supervised learning and then reinforcement learning to optimize planning behavior.

Result: Across math, science, tabular analysis, and web information seeking, v0.1-8B and v1.0-30B achieve Pass@1 competitive with much larger models (120–355B and 685B–1T parameters, respectively). v1.0-30B attains 25.8–95.3% fewer reasoning tokens. RL increases average planning horizon by 22.8% while planning frequency grows only 2.0%. Demonstrates that learned self-regulation can drive more effective and efficient planning.

Conclusion: Learned self-regulation is a general principle for when and how to plan and can extend to broader aspects of agent learning and adaptation beyond planning.

Abstract: How should an agent decide when and how to plan? A dominant approach builds agents as reactive policies with adaptive computation (e.g., chain-of-thought), trained end-to-end expecting planning to emerge implicitly. Without control over the presence, structure, or horizon of planning, these systems dramatically increase reasoning length, yielding inefficient token use without reliable accuracy gains. We argue efficient agentic reasoning benefits from decomposing decision-making into three systems: simulative reasoning (System II) grounding deliberation in future-state prediction via a world model; self-regulation (System III) deciding when and how deeply to plan via a learned configurator; and reactive execution (System I) handling fine-grained action. Simulative reasoning provides unified planning across diverse tasks without per-domain engineering, while self-regulation ensures the planner is invoked only when needed. To test this, we develop SR$^2$AM (Self-Regulated Simulative Reasoning Agentic LLM), realizing both as distinct stages within an LLM's chain-of-thought, with the LLM as world model. We explore two instantiations: recording decisions from a prompted multi-module system (v0.1) and reconstructing structured plans from traces of pretrained reasoning LLMs (v1.0), trained via supervised then reinforcement learning (RL). Across math, science, tabular analysis, and web information seeking, v0.1-8B and v1.0-30B achieve Pass@1 competitive with 120-355B and 685B-1T parameter systems respectively, while v1.0-30B uses 25.8-95.3% fewer reasoning tokens than comparable agentic LLMs. RL increases average planning horizon by 22.8% while planning frequency grows only 2.0%, showing it learns to plan further ahead rather than more often. More broadly, learned self-regulation instantiates a principle we expect to extend beyond planning to how agents govern their own learning and adaptation.

</details>


### [169] [Ratchet: A Minimal Hygiene Recipe for Self-Evolving LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22148)
*Xing Zhang,Yanwei Cui,Guanghui Wang,Ziyuan Li,Wei Qiu,Bing Zhu,Peiyang He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Ratchet is a closed-loop, single-agent system that lets a frozen LLM author, retrieve, curate, and retire its own natural-language skills, using four hygiene mechanisms to manage skill lifecycles; it yields substantial performance gains over no-skill baselines and transfers to agentic solvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing frozen-LLM skill libraries underperform due to lifecycle management bottlenecks rather than skill authoring; a unified loop that governs creation, retention, and retirement of skills can unlock reusable knowledge.

Method: Introduce Ratchet: a single-agent loop where a frozen LLM writes, retrieves, curates, and retires NL skills; integrate four hygiene mechanisms—outcome-driven retirement, bounded active-cap, meta-skill authoring guidance, and pattern canonicalisation; evaluate on MBPP+ hard-100 with Claude Opus 4.7 and on SWE-bench Verified, plus eight ablations (A1–A8).

Result: On MBPP+ hard-100 with Claude Opus 4.7, Ratchet raises held-out pass@1 from 0.258 ± 0.047 to a late-window rolling mean of 0.584 (peak 0.658 ± 0.042) across 100 rounds and 3 seeds; a +0.328 ± 0.018 rolling-mean gain versus a no-skill baseline drifting at +0.002 ± 0.005. The same approach transfers to an agentic solver on SWE-bench Verified with a +0.22 peak lift over 20 rounds. Ablations show retirement and meta-skill guidance are load-bearing; explicit deduplication is largely subsumed by the meta-skill; a bounded cap with retirement prevents drift below the no-skills floor.

Conclusion: Lifecycle management via Ratchet yields substantial performance gains, with retirement and meta-skill authoring as key drivers; deduplication is effectively handled by the meta-skill; the bounded-cap and retirement mechanisms stabilize performance and support transfer across benchmarks.

Abstract: Self-evolving skill libraries, pioneered by Voyager, let frozen LLM agents accumulate reusable knowledge without weight updates, yet recent evaluation shows that LLM-authored skills deliver $+0.0$pp over no-skill baselines while human-curated ones deliver $+16.2$pp: the bottleneck is not skill authoring but lifecycle management. We introduce \textbf{Ratchet}, a single-agent loop in which a frozen LLM writes, retrieves, curates, and retires its own natural-language skills. Ratchet integrates four candidate hygiene mechanisms: outcome-driven retirement, a bounded active-cap, meta-skill authoring guidance, and pattern canonicalisation. On MBPP+ hard-100 with Claude Opus 4.7, Ratchet lifts held-out pass@1 from a $0.258 \pm 0.047$ baseline to a late-window rolling mean of $0.584$ (peak $0.658 \pm 0.042$) across 100 rounds and 3 seeds, a $+0.328 \pm 0.018$ rolling-mean gain where the no-skill control drifts at $+0.002 \pm 0.005$; the same recipe transfers to an agentic solver on SWE-bench Verified ($+0.22$ peak lift over 20 rounds). Eight ablations (A1--A8) reveal that the minimal working recipe is smaller than our design suggests: retirement and the meta-skill authoring prior are load-bearing, while explicit deduplication (canonicalisation, cover-guard) is subsumed by the meta-skill itself. A non-divergence proposition shows that bounded cap and retirement threshold together prevent expected performance from drifting below the no-skills floor.

</details>


### [170] [IdleSpec: Exploiting Idle Time via Speculative Planning for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22154)
*Daewon Choi,Kyunghyun Park,Woomin Song,Saket Dingliwal,Sai Muralidhar Jayanthi,Jinwoo Shin,Aram Galstyan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: IdleSpec uses idle time to generate and refine planning candidates for LLM-based agents, enabling improved performance with minimal latency by aggregating plan candidates after observations become available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Idle time is a prevalent yet underutilized resource in multi-step LLM-agent reasoning. Treating idle time as pure overhead ignores varying computational budgets and uncertainty in future observations, leading to suboptimal use of idle cycles.

Method: IdleSpec continuously generates multiple plan candidates during idle periods. Upon receiving new observations, it aggregates these candidates to inform the next reasoning step. It samples between complementary drafting strategies (progressive and recovery) from a learned distribution that is updated via posterior feedback to handle observation uncertainty.

Result: Across GAIA and FRAMES with Gemini-2.5-Flash, IdleSpec attains 55.6% average accuracy, surpassing the vanilla baseline by 5.1%. On MLE-Bench, it yields up to 9.1% improvement in Any Medal rate, demonstrating effectiveness on long-horizon tasks.

Conclusion: IdleSpec offers a scalable, general-purpose inference technique that leverages idle time to improve LLM-based agent performance under observation uncertainty and varying computational budgets.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based agents solve complex tasks by leveraging multi-step reasoning with iterative tool calls and environment interactions, which incur idle time while waiting for observations. Despite the prevalence of idle time in most agentic scenarios, existing works treat it as an unavoidable overhead or propose restricted solutions that overlook varying computational budgets across different tool calls and future observation uncertainty, thereby leading to suboptimal utilization of idle time. In this paper, we introduce IdleSpec, a scalable and generic inference approach that leverages idle-time computation to improve agent performance while minimizing latency overhead. Specifically, IdleSpec iteratively generates plan candidates during idle periods and, once observations become available, aggregates them to guide the next reasoning step. For effective plan generation under observation uncertainty, IdleSpec samples between complementary drafting strategies (i.e., progressive and recovery) from a learned distribution that is updated via posterior feedback. Our experiments demonstrate that IdleSpec significantly improves agent performance in various agentic scenarios by effectively utilizing idle time. In particular, on the GAIA and FRAMES, IdleSpec achieves 55.6% average accuracy with Gemini-2.5-Flash, surpassing the vanilla baseline without idle-time usage by 5.1%. Furthermore, for MLE-Bench, which involves substantial delay from code executions, IdleSpec achieves performance gains of up to 9.1% on the Any Medal rate, highlighting its generalizability to long-horizon tasks.

</details>


### [171] [ST-SimDiff: Balancing Spatiotemporal Similarity and Difference for Efficient Video Understanding with MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22158)
*Bingjun Luo,Tony Wang,Chaoqi Chen,Xinpeng Ding*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A training-free framework ST-SimDiff for efficient video processing in Multimodal LLMs that preserves both static and dynamic content by dual-token selection on a spatio-temporal graph: similarity-based pruning and temporal difference-based event preservation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs incur high computational costs when processing long videos due to large numbers of visual tokens. Existing redundancy-pruning methods mainly remove similar tokens and often fail to capture content changes and turning points, lacking a unified spatio-temporal relational approach.

Method: Build a spatio-temporal graph from visual tokens to model their complex relations. Apply a parallel dual-selection strategy: (1) similarity-based selection using community detection to retain representative tokens and compress static information; (2) temporal difference-based selection to locate content-changing points and preserve tokens capturing dynamic shifts. The framework is training-free.

Result: Extensive experiments show ST-SimDiff significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods while substantially reducing computational costs.

Conclusion: ST-SimDiff preserves both static and dynamic content with a minimal token budget, offering improved efficiency for MLLMs on long videos. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) face significant computational overhead when processing long videos due to the massive number of visual tokens required. To improve efficiency, existing methods primarily reduce redundancy by pruning or merging tokens based on importance or similarity. However, these approaches largely overlook a critical dimension of video content, i.e., changes and turning points, and they lack a collaborative model for spatio-temporal relationships. To address this, we propose a new perspective: similarity is for identifying redundancy, while difference is for capturing key events. Based on this, we designed a training-free framework named ST-SimDiff. We first construct a spatio-temporal graph from the visual tokens to uniformly model their complex associations. Subsequently, we employ a parallel dual-selection strategy: 1) similarity-based selection uses community detection to retain representative tokens, compressing static information; 2) temporal difference-based selection precisely locates content-changing points to preserve tokens that capture key dynamic shifts. This allows it to preserve both static and dynamic content with a minimal number of tokens. Extensive experiments show our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches while substantially reducing computational costs. Our code is available in https://github.com/bingjunluo/ST-SimDiff.

</details>


### [172] [Adapting the Interface, Not the Model: Runtime Harness Adaptation for Deterministic LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22166)
*Tianshi Xu,Huifeng Wen,Meng Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Life-Harness is a lifecycle-aware runtime harness that improves frozen LLM agents by adapting the interaction interface without changing model weights, achieving broad improvements across multiple backbones and environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deterministic, rule-governed domains often fail due to mismatches at the model–environment interface. Most prior work adjusts model weights; Life-Harness targets environment-side adaptation by evolving from training trajectories to fix recurring interaction failures.

Method: From training trajectories, Life-Harness converts recurring interaction failures into reusable interventions across four facets—environment contracts, procedural skills, action realization, and trajectory regulation—while remaining fixed during evaluation. Harnesses learned from Qwen3-4B-Instruct trajectories transfer to 17 other models, indicating capture of reusable environment-side structure rather than model-specific behavior.

Result: On seven deterministic environments from τ-bench, τ²-bench, and AgentBench, Life-Harness improves 116 of 126 model–environment settings across 18 model backbones, with an average relative improvement of 88.5%. Code (GitHub) demonstrates transferability of harnesses across models.

Conclusion: Runtime interface adaptation offers a complementary approach to model-centric training, enabling performance gains without weight updates or changing evaluation environments, and highlighting reusable environment-side structure; code available.

Abstract: LLM agents are shaped not only by their language models, but also by the runtime harness that mediates observation, tool use, action execution, feedback interpretation, and trajectory control. While existing agent adaptation methods mainly update model parameters, many failures in deterministic, rule-governed domains stem from mismatches at the model--environment interface. We propose Life-Harness, a lifecycle-aware runtime harness that improves frozen LLM agents without changing model weights or evaluation environments. Life-Harness evolves from training trajectories by converting recurring interaction failures into reusable interventions across environment contracts, procedural skills, action realization, and trajectory regulation, and remains fixed during held-out evaluation. On seven deterministic environments from $τ$-bench, $τ^2$-bench, and AgentBench, Life-Harness improves 116 out of 126 model--environment settings across 18 model backbones, with an average relative improvement of 88.5%. Harnesses evolved only from Qwen3-4B-Instruct trajectories transfer to 17 other models, showing that Life-Harness captures reusable environment-side structure rather than model-specific behavior. These results position runtime interface adaptation as a complementary alternative to model-centric agent training. Code is available at GitHub.

</details>


### [173] [Measuring Cross-Modal Synergy: A Benchmark for VLM Explainability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22168)
*Joël Roman Ky,Salah Ghamizi,Maxime Cordy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Synergistic Faithfulness (F_syn) for evaluating cross-modal faithfulness in Vision-Language Models by isolating the joint synergy between image and text via the Shapley Interaction Index, addressing the evaluation collapse caused by language priors in unimodal metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current post-hoc explainers and unimodal perturbation metrics fail for VLMs due to language priors and modality biases, leading to cross-modal redundancy where text alone can answer visual queries. There is a need for a scalable, faithful evaluation of cross-modal explanations.

Method: Introduce F_syn based on the Shapley Interaction Index to capture the joint Harsanyi dividend between modalities. Provide a scalable computation with 24× speedup while maintaining high fidelity (proxy ρ=0.92). Empirically evaluate 8 XAI methods across 3 VLM architectures and 3 benchmark datasets to compare cross-modal faithfulness vs. visual salience.

Result: F_syn closely tracks true cross-modal synergy and reveals that many VLM explainers over-emphasize visual salience and underperform adapted attention-based methods in capturing cross-modal interactions. The approach decouples visual plausibility from cross-modal faithfulness, yielding a robust assessment of VLM reasoning.

Conclusion: The proposed framework offers a rigorous, scalable evaluation tool for auditing VLM reasoning in high-stakes contexts, mitigating evaluation collapse and enabling safer deployment by accurately measuring cross-modal synergy.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) map complex visual inputs to semantic spaces, but interpreting the cross-modal reasoning of VLMs currently relies on post-hoc explainers evaluated via unimodal perturbation metrics. We expose a limitation in this paradigm: because multimodal datasets contain language priors and modality biases, VLMs frequently exhibit cross-modal redundancy, allowing them to answer visual queries using text alone. Consequently, unimodal metrics penalize faithful explainers, triggering an evaluation collapse where visual and textual rankings fundamentally contradict each other. %(Kendall's $τ= -0.06$). To resolve this, we introduce Synergistic Faithfulness ($\mathcal{F}_{syn}$), a scalable metric rooted in the Shapley Interaction Index that strictly isolates the joint Harsanyi dividend between modalities, serving as a highly accurate surrogate ($ρ= 0.92$) while achieving a $24\times$ computational speedup. Evaluating 8 distinct XAI methods across 3 VLM architectures and 3 benchmark datasets, reveals that explainers proposed for VLMs heavily over-index on visual salience and significantly underperform adapted attention-based methods in capturing true cross-modal synergy. By decoupling visual plausibility from cross-modal faithfulness, this work provides a rigorous evaluation framework required to safely audit VLM reasoning in high-stakes deployments.

</details>


### [174] [LLM-Metrics: Measuring Research Impact Through Large Language Model Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22176)
*Si Shen,Wenhua Zhao,Danhao Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A novel, memory-based metric (LLM-Metrics) uses LLM exposure/memory to estimate research impact, showing modest positive correlation with citations across multiple models, with strongest signals from author-recognition probes and non-monotonic scaling (smaller models sometimes outperform larger ones).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of citation counts (lag, disciplinary bias, Matthew effect) by leveraging LLMs' parametric memory as a real-time, cross-disciplinary proxy for paper impact.

Method: Four types of multiple-choice probes (title, author, method, venue recognition) evaluated on 549 computer science papers from 2023–2024 across 17 LLMs (0.5B–72B params) from six vendors. Predictions analyzed via Spearman correlation with citation counts; significance tested; subset analysis for 2024 papers; exploration of model scale effects.

Result: 15 of 17 models yielded positive predictions; 9 significant at p<0.05; overall Spearman rho=0.1495, p=0.0004; 2024 papers: rho=0.1880; author-recognition probes had strongest discriminative power; a 3B model (Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct) outperformed many larger models (rho=0.1829); memory effect suggested to be exposure-driven and non-monotonic with model size.

Conclusion: LLM-Metrics provides a real-time, cross-disciplinary, citation-independent measure of research impact, consistent with a memory-based exposure mechanism and selective-memory hypothesis, though with modest predictive strength and potential model-data leakage concerns.

Abstract: Citation counts remain the dominant metric for assessing research impact, yet they suffer from well-documented limitations: temporal lag, disciplinary bias, and Matthew effects. Here we propose LLM-Metrics, a research-impact assessment metric derived from the parametric memory of large language models (LLMs). The central hypothesis is that high-impact papers receive greater exposure in the academic community, that this exposure enters LLM training data in textual form, and that models consequently form stronger parametric memory of these papers. We designed four types of multiple-choice probes, covering title recognition, author recognition, method recognition, and venue recognition, and evaluated 549 computer science papers published in 2023-2024 across 17 LLMs spanning 0.5B to 72B parameters from six vendors. Of the 17 models, 15 produced positive predictions, 9 of which were significant at p less than 0.05, with an overall Spearman correlation of rho = 0.1495 and p = 0.0004 against citation counts. Three additional findings support the proposed mechanism. First, the predictive signal was stronger for 2024 papers, rho = 0.1880, whose citation counts were near zero at model-training time, reducing the plausibility of a simple reverse-causality explanation. Second, author-recognition probes showed the strongest discriminative power, consistent with an exposure-driven memory mechanism. Third, model scale and predictive power were non-monotonic: a 3B-parameter model, Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, with rho = 0.1829, outperformed most larger models, supporting a selective-memory hypothesis in which the limited capacity of smaller models can serve as an effective information filter. LLM-Metrics offers a real-time, cross-disciplinary, citation-independent paradigm for research assessment.

</details>


### [175] [Skill Weaving: Efficient LLM Improvement via Modular Skillpacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22205)
*Zhuo Li,Guodong Du,Zesheng Shi,Weiyang Guo,Weijun Yao,Yuan Zhou,Jiabo Zhang,Jing Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillWeave modularizes a general LLM into lightweight, domain-specific delta modules called skillpacks, compressed with SkillZip to fit fixed memory budgets. It enables strong multi-domain performance with low latency, outperforming baselines (including larger monolithic models) and achieving up to 4x speedups in benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve robust multi-domain capabilities in LLMs under strict memory and latency constraints, addressing the challenge that existing approaches trade off domain specialization against resource efficiency.

Method: Partition the model's capabilities into skillpacks (domain-specific delta modules) that reorganize and refine internal knowledge, then compress them with SkillZip into an inference-ready format. Evaluate the integrated system (SkillWeave) on multi-task and agentic benchmarks, comparing against baselines and larger monolithic models.

Result: A 9B SkillWeave model outperforms several baselines and even surpasses a 32B monolithic LLM, with up to 4x speedup.

Conclusion: SkillWeave provides a modular pathway to specialize LLMs within fixed memory budgets, delivering strong multi-domain performance and efficient, low-latency inference.

Abstract: Large language models increasingly require specialization across diverse domains, yet existing approaches struggle to balance multi-domain capacities with strict memory and inference constraints. In this work, we introduce SkillWeave, a modular improvement framework that enables LLMs to specialize under fixed memory budgets. SkillWeave partitions full capabilities of a general-purpose model into skillpacks -- lightweight, domain-specific delta modules -- that reorganize and refine the model's internal knowledge. For efficient deployment, SkillWeave integrates SkillZip to compress skillpacks into compact and inference-ready format, enabling strong multi-domain performance with low-latency execution. On multi-task and agentic benchmarks, a 9B SkillWeave model outperforms several baselines and even surpasses a 32B monolithic LLM, while achieving up to 4x speedup.

</details>


### [176] [CLORE: Content-Level Optimization for Reasoning Efficiency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22211)
*Yuyang Wu,Qiyao Xue,Guanxing Lu,Weichen Liu,Zihan Wang,Manling Li,Olexandr Isayev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CLORE is a content-level post-training optimization that edits on-policy rollouts by deleting unnecessary reasoning content to improve the accuracy-efficiency trade-off, preserving the final answer and compatible with multiple existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inefficiency and opacity of chain-of-thought in RL-finetuned LLMs by regulating content quality rather than solely response length; reduce repetitive, illegible, and post-answer exploration while maintaining correctness.

Method: Apply an external augmentation model to delete repetitive, illegible, or task-irrelevant segments from correct on-policy rollouts after the solution is established. Create augmented–original pairs and train with a reference-free DPO objective alongside standard policy gradient, restricting augmentation to correct trajectories and performing local deletions to keep edits near the policy distribution and reduce off-policy mismatch.

Result: CLORE yields improved accuracy–efficiency trade-offs across five mathematical reasoning benchmarks on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B and Qwen2.5-Math-7B, and remains compatible with GRPO, DAPO, Training Efficient, and ThinkPrune. Content-level analyses show reductions in repetitive reasoning, illegible content, and post-answer exploration.

Conclusion: Content-level editing of correct on-policy traces is a viable complement to length-based controls, offering improved efficiency without sacrificing correctness and enabling integration with existing RL and reasoning-acceleration methods.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning post-training has improved the reasoning ability of large language models, but often produces unnecessarily long, repetitive, or semantically opaque reasoning traces. Existing efficient reasoning methods mainly regulate response length through explicit budgets or length-aware rewards, leaving intermediate reasoning content weakly supervised. We propose CLORE, a content-level optimization framework that improves reasoning efficiency by editing correct on-policy rollouts. CLORE uses an external augmentation model to delete repetitive segments, illegible or task-irrelevant content, and superfluous reasoning after the solution is established, while preserving the final answer. The resulting augmented--original pairs are optimized with an auxiliary reference-free DPO objective alongside standard policy-gradient training. By restricting augmentation to correct trajectories and performing local deletion, CLORE keeps edited rollouts close to the policy distribution and mitigates off-policy mismatch. Experiments on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B and Qwen2.5-Math-7B across five mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that CLORE improves the accuracy--efficiency trade-off and remains compatible with GRPO, DAPO, Training Efficient, and ThinkPrune. Content-level analyses further show that CLORE reduces repetitive reasoning, illegible content, and post-answer exploration, supporting content-level supervision as a complementary direction to length-level control.

</details>


### [177] [Towards a compositional semantics for quantitative confidence assessment in assurance arguments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22213)
*Benjamin Herd,Jessica Kelly,Jan Sabsch,Lydia Gauerhof*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Confidence semantics for GSN-based assurance arguments using Subjective Logic (SL) to model confidence propagation through a structured argument graph.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GSN and similar notations lack an operational, compositional semantics for confidence; stakeholders need quantifiable trust in claims, not just truth values.

Method: Represent argument elements as SL opinions; map argument relations to SL operators to model confidence flow; maintain provenance and context; show compatibility with GSN; illustrate with an exemplar assessment.

Result: A proposed confidence semantics forming a confidence network over a GSN argument, enabling explicit warrants, context handling, and evidence-based confidence propagation.

Conclusion: The approach offers a principled, analyzable framework for assurance confidence, enabling better trust and traceability; further validation and full formalization across all argument constructs are needed.

Abstract: Assurance arguments provide a clear and structured way to explain why stakeholders should trust that a system satisfies certain properties, yet widely used notations, e.g.Goal Structuring Notation (GSN), typically lack an operational semantics for deriving assurance confidence. Existing approaches address structure and soundness but largely reason over truth values, not over confidence in the justification of claims. Subjective Logic (SL) offers a calculus of belief, disbelief, and uncertainty with operators for combining opinions, enabling confidence propagation under incomplete, conflicting, or subjective evidence. However, existing SL-based approaches do not provide a uniform, compositional semantics that covers all argument elements and relations to enable overall confidence assessment.
