We propose a confidence semantics that represents argument elements as SL opinions and maps relations between elements to SL operators modelling how confidence flows, effectively turning the argument into an analyzable confidence network. The approach provides explicit warrants, principled handling of context, preserved provenance, and compatibility with GSN, along with practical guidance using an exemplary assurance confidence assessment.

</details>


### [178] [SGR-Bench: Benchmarking Search Agents on State-Gated Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22219)
*Ningyuan Li,Haiyang Shen,Mugeng Liu,Yudong Han,Zhuofan Shi,Sixiong Xie,Yun Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SGR-Bench: a benchmark for state-gated retrieval on specialized web sources; 100 tasks across multiple ecosystems; analyzes ability to discover sites and configure site-specific retrieval state; shows current systems struggle, with max 66% item-level F1.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To characterize an underexplored retrieval class where answers depend on proper site-wide retrieval state (filters, views, scopes). LLMs with tool-use often bypass this requirement; need controlled benchmarks to study explicit vs implicit guidance.

Method: Construct 100 expert-curated tasks across six source families and 12 ecosystems; pair constraint-guided and goal-oriented formulations; evaluate 8 CLI-based agentic LLMs and 3 commercial search agents; conduct manual audit of 156 failed CLI trajectories to diagnose failure modes.

Result: Best system achieves 66.18% item-level F1; row-level F1 much lower; major failure modes: retrieval-scope drift (37.2%), criterion mismatch (27.6%), final answer composition (10.3%).

Conclusion: SGR-Bench provides dataset and evaluation protocol; reveals substantial gaps in current state-gated retrieval; enables controlled comparisons between guidance forms; resources available at HuggingFace dataset page.

Abstract: Recent advances in large language models and tool-using agents have expanded the range of benchmarked web tasks. Yet an important class of specialized retrieval tasks remains undercharacterized. On many specialized data-retrieval websites, answer-bearing evidence becomes accessible only after establishing the correct site-specific retrieval state through filters, views, hierarchies, or scopes. We term this capability state-gated retrieval (SGR). We introduce SGR-Bench, a benchmark for this setting containing 100 expert-curated tasks spanning six source families and 12 public data ecosystems. Each task requires discovering the appropriate website and configuring its site-specific retrieval state to produce a structured answer. SGR-Bench pairs constraint-guided and goal-oriented formulations of the same underlying problems, enabling controlled comparisons between explicit and implicit guidance for state-gated retrieval. We evaluate eight CLI-based agentic LLM systems and three commercial search-agent products. On SGR-Bench, the strongest system reaches only 66.18% item-level F1, while row-level F1 remains much lower. A manual audit of 156 analyzable failed CLI trajectories shows why: agents often reach a relevant web source, but establish the wrong site-specific retrieval state. Retrieval-scope drift (37.2%) and criterion mismatch (27.6%) dominate, whereas final answer composition accounts for only 10.3%. The dataset and single-case evaluation instructions are available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/PKUAIWeb/SGR-BENCH.

</details>


### [179] [Evaluating Large Language Models as Live Strategic Agents: Provider Performance, Hybrid Decomposition, and Operational Gaps in Timed Risk Play](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22238)
*H. C. Ekne*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: In dynamic, bounded-task settings, live-agent performance of LLMs depends on planning-execution dynamics, cost, and reliability; Gemini-3.1-pro-preview outperformed peers in a 32-game cross-provider event, but planning-alone parity suggests earlier gaps were due to end-to-end system behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess how LLMs behave in repeated, constrained decision loops and isolate factors driving performance beyond static benchmarks.

Method: Run a 32-game, cross-provider championship under fixed rules; separate planning and execution using a cheaper scaffold to compare planning quality; analyze saved traces to identify mechanism; use p-values to compare distributions against null; investigate objective tracking and conversion to action sequences.

Result: Gemini-3.1-pro-preview won 20/32 vs GPT-5.1, Claude-opus-4-7, Kimi-k2.6; p ~ 1.5e-5 for winner distribution; planner bakeoff shows near equality p ~ 0.821, suggesting end-to-end system behavior explains much of earlier spread; Gemini emphasizes terminal objectives and builds deep conquest chains; live-agent performance influenced by objective tracking, execution conversion, cost, runtime reliability.

Conclusion: Evaluate LLMs as components in bounded workflows; measure performance with timing/formatting constraints; separate planning/execution to diagnose bottlenecks; use traces to understand mechanics.

Abstract: Static benchmarks capture only part of how large language models behave in practice. Real systems place models inside repeated loops with time limits, formatting constraints, and failure modes. We study this setting in a timed multi-phase Risk environment with explicit victory targets and repeated planning and execution cycles. In a replicated 32-game cross-provider championship under frozen rules, gemini-3.1-pro-preview won 20 of 32 games against gpt-5.1, claude-opus-4-7, and kimi-k2.6, and the pooled winner distribution differs strongly from an equal-strength null (p approx 1.5 x 10^-5). We then separate planning from execution by standardizing execution on a cheaper Gemini Flash scaffold. Under this design, a pooled 32-game planner bakeoff is consistent with near-equality (p approx 0.821), which indicates that much of the earlier provider spread came from end-to-end system behavior rather than planning alone. To study mechanism, we analyze saved planning and execution traces from the provider championship. Gemini refers to the terminal objective far more often than the other models and increases that focus as victory approaches. Gemini also converts more turns into deep conquest chains, even though it is not the cleanest runtime. These results show that live-agent performance depends on objective tracking, execution conversion, cost, and runtime reliability, and they support evaluating LLMs as components in bounded workflows rather than as isolated benchmark respondents.

</details>


### [180] [Unlocking Proactivity in Task-Oriented Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22240)
*Hongbin Zhang,Ning Gao,Yuqin Dai,Ruiyuan Wu,Jinpeng Wang,Rena Wei Gao,Bingdong Tan,Shuzheng Gao,Zongjie Li,Chaozheng Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Conditioning on user latent concerns enables proactive task-oriented dialogue; introduces Cognitive User Simulator and Simulator-Induced Asymmetric-View Policy Optimization with two training objectives to transfer concern-aware behavior and refine state transitions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-trained LLMs are conservative; reward shaping struggles because sampling cannot reveal proactive behavior; latent concerns provide a pivotal training-time signal for proactive persuasion.

Method: Develop Cognitive User Simulator (CUS) modeling users as personas with observable traits and hidden concerns; simulator outputs per-turn dynamics; propose two objectives: (1) Asymmetric On-Policy Self-Distillation (privileged view → deployable view) and (2) State-Transition Policy Refinement to leverage simulation state transitions.

Result: Abstract claims that conditioning on concerns unlocks proactive capability; CUS yields faithful and diverse interactions; two training objectives enable concern-aware behavior transfer and policy refinement; specific empirical results are not provided in the excerpt.

Conclusion: Training-time signals in the form of user concerns enable proactive task-oriented dialogue; the proposed Cognitive User Simulator and Simulator-Induced Asymmetric-View Policy Optimization provide a framework for more proactive and effective dialogue policies.

Abstract: Proactive task-oriented dialogue (TOD), such as outbound sales, demands a persuasive agent that actively probes the user's concerns and steers the conversation toward acceptance within a bounded number of turns. Yet post-trained LLMs are inherently conservative, and reward-shaping RL (e.g., GRPO) struggles since it only re-weights what an already passive policy samples. We show that conditioning on the user's latent concerns unlocks proactive capability that no amount of sampling can undermine, establishing these concerns as a pivotal training-time signal. To operationalize this finding, we build the \textbf{Cognitive User Simulator}, which models each user as a stratified persona comprising observable external traits and hidden internal concerns. The simulator produces faithful and diverse interactions, while emitting per-turn state dynamics that track persuasion progress. We then introduce \textbf{Simulator-Induced Asymmetric-View Policy Optimization}, which converts the modeled concerns and the simulation state transition into complementary training objectives: (1) \emph{Asymmetric On-Policy Self-Distillation} that transfers concern-aware behavior from a privileged view of the same policy into its deployable, conversation-only view; and (2) \emph{State-Transition Policy Refinement} ...

</details>


### [181] [SciCore-Mol: Augmenting Large Language Models with Pluggable Molecular Cognition Modules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22287)
*Yuxuan Chen,Changwei Lv,Yunduo Xiao,Zhongjing Du,Daquan Zhou,Yukun Yan,Zheni Zeng,Zhiyuan Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SciCore-Mol: a modular, topology-aware, diffusion-based molecular generation framework that augments LLMs with three pluggable cognitive modules to bridge discrete text and continuous chemical data; achieves competitive results with an 8B open-source model across molecular understanding, generation, reaction prediction, and chemistry knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcoming the information loss and semantic noise when translating continuous molecular/topological data into text; the gap between discrete linguistic symbols and scientific data impedes effective reasoning in LLM-enabled chemistry tasks.

Method: Integrate three pluggable modules with the LLM backbone via learned representation interfaces: (1) topology-aware perception module to capture molecular topology; (2) latent diffusion-based molecular generation module for molecule design and generation; (3) reaction-aware reasoning module to handle chemical reactions. These modules exchange richer information with the LLM than text-only tool feedback.

Result: Experimental evaluation across diverse chemical tasks shows strong, comprehensive performance. An 8B-parameter open-source system matches or surpasses proprietary models in several dimensions, demonstrating the viability of decoupled, modular augmentation for scientific LLMs.

Conclusion: A systematic blueprint for empowering LLMs with scientific expertise through decoupled, pluggable modules that can be orchestrated flexibly, with broad implications for drug design, synthesis, and general scientific discovery.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are central to the one-for-all intelligent paradigm, but they face a fundamental challenge when dealing with heterogeneous scientific data such as molecules: the inherent gap between discrete linguistic symbols and topological molecular or continuous reaction data leads to significant information loss and semantic noise in text-based reasoning. We propose SciCore-Mol, a modular framework that bridges this gap through three deeply integrated pluggable cognitive modules: a topology-aware perception module, a latent diffusion-based molecular generation module, and a reaction-aware reasoning module. Each module is coupled to the LLM backbone through learned representation interfaces, enabling richer information exchange than is possible with text-only tool feedback. Our experiments on diverse chemical tasks demonstrate that SciCore-Mol achieves strong comprehensive performance across molecular understanding, generation, reaction prediction, and general chemistry knowledge, with an 8B-parameter open-source system that is competitive with and in several dimensions surpasses proprietary large models. This work provides a systematic blueprint for equipping LLMs with scientific expertise through decoupled, pluggable, and flexibly orchestrated modules, with direct implications for drug design, chemical synthesis, and broader scientific discovery.

</details>


### [182] [Cross-domain benchmarks reveal when coordinated AI agents improve scientific inference from partial evidence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22300)
*Fiona Y. Wong,Markus J. Buehler*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Cross-domain benchmark shows when coordinating AI agents adds value in scientific tasks; three regimes determine usefulness; gains strongest when signals are complementary across channels, limited when a dominant single signal exists; provenance layer is essential for auditable evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify the value of coordinating AI agents versus single-channel scientific workflows across diverse tasks.

Method: Four tasks across disciplines with frozen evaluation panels, predefined scoring, baselines, ablations, controls; explicit limitations; define three operating regimes; provide auditable provenance via ScienceClaw x Infinite.

Result: Notable AUROC gains in cross-channel composites: climate-vector emergence AUROC 0.944; exoplanet vetting AUROC 0.955. In cases where one signal dominates (paradigm-shift detection) coordination mainly aids interpretability; for molecular sonification gains are representational, not predictive; exoplanet workflow shows decomposition doesn't always improve top-line.

Conclusion: Coordination adds value only under explicit comparative claims; not universally beneficial; provenance and representation claims require explicit comparators; the benchmark's value depends on rigorous evaluation protocols.

Abstract: Scientific evidence often spans instruments, databases, and disciplines, so no single source records the full phenomenon. This makes it difficult to determine when coordinated AI agents add value over simpler scientific workflows. We evaluate this question with a cross-domain benchmark spanning four scientific tasks: mapping molecular structure into musical representations, detecting historical paradigm shifts in science, identifying vector-borne disease emergence, and vetting transiting-exoplanet candidates. Each case uses a frozen evaluation panel, predefined scoring protocols, explicit baselines, ablations or null controls, and stated limitations. The results define three operating regimes. When different disciplines each capture only part of the phenomenon, cross-channel composites improve over single-channel baselines: climate-vector emergence reaches AUROC 0.944 and exoplanet vetting reaches AUROC 0.955. However, the exoplanet workflow is effectively tied with a strong combined-summary baseline, showing that decomposition does not always improve top-line performance. When one signal dominates, as in paradigm-shift detection, coordination mainly improves interpretation and traceability. For molecular sonification, the gain is representational rather than predictive. ScienceClaw x Infinite provides the auditable artifact and provenance layer for this evaluation. The benchmark therefore assigns value to coordination only when the corresponding performance, provenance, or representation claim is supported by explicit comparators.

</details>


### [183] [Evaluation of Pipelines for Data Integration into Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22304)
*Marvin Hofer,Erhard Rahm*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces KGI-Bench, a benchmark to evaluate knowledge-graph integration pipelines across input data formats, assessing coverage, correctness, and consistency, with seed/ground-truth datasets in the movie domain and 12 pipelines analyzed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no general, standardized method to assess the overall quality and performance of knowledge-graph integration pipelines, hindering fair comparison and optimization.

Method: Develop benchmark datasets: start from a seed KG, provide overlapping input data in three formats, and a reference KG as ground truth. Define quality metrics (coverage, correctness, consistency) to evaluate the updated KG. Conduct a comparative evaluation of 12 pipelines across input formats and design choices.

Result: The benchmark and datasets enable systematic evaluation of integration pipelines. The study demonstrates how pipelines differ in output quality across formats and design choices, providing empirical insights into pipeline behavior.

Conclusion: KGI-Bench provides a standardized framework for evaluating KG integration pipelines, facilitating methodological comparisons and guiding pipeline design. The datasets and metrics can be extended to other domains beyond movies.

Abstract: Integrating new data into knowledge graphs (KG) typically involves different tasks that are executed within workflows or pipelines There are many possible pipelines for a specific integration problem but there is not yet a general approach to evaluate the overall quality and performance of such pipelines to be able to determine the best choices. We therefore propose a new benchmark KGI-Bench to evaluate integration pipelines that ingest different kinds of input data into an existing KG. We evaluate pipelines by analyzing their output, i.e., the updated KG, with the three complementary quality metrics coverage, correctness and consistency. We also provide benchmark datasets (seed KG, overlapping input data of three formats, reference KG as a ground truth) for the movie domain. To demonstrate the applicability and usefulness of the proposed benchmark, we comparatively evaluate 12 pipelines and analyze their behavior across different input data formats and design choices.

</details>


### [184] [Meta-Soft: Leveraging Composable Meta-Tokens for Context-Preserving KV Cache Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22337)
*Wei Luo,Yi Huang,Songchen Ma,Huanyu Qu,Jiang Cai,Mingkun Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Meta-Soft for dynamic KV-cache eviction in LLMs by learning an orthogonal basis and a differentiable sparse selector to synthesize targeted Soft Tokens, coupled with an attention-flow mechanism to preserve dropped context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV caches in LLMs grow linearly with context length, causing memory and decoding inefficiencies. Fixed eviction strategies (e.g., Judge Q) use static tokens and cannot adapt to varying prompts, risking suboptimal retention and irreversible information loss when tokens are evicted.

Method: Construct a meta-library with a learnable orthogonal basis L. Use a selector network with Gumbel-Softmax to generate differentiable sparse weights, selecting k Soft Tokens from input prompt features. Append these Soft Tokens to the input. Employ an attention-flow based integration to redistribute information from removed tokens into retained tokens, preserving context.

Result: Empirical evaluation on multiple datasets shows Meta-Soft outperforms existing state-of-the-art eviction methods, offering improved efficiency and better preservation of task-relevant information for KV-cache compression.

Conclusion: Dynamic, probe-driven context integration with differentiable soft-token synthesis provides an effective and adaptable solution for KV-cache compression in long-context LLMs, with strong empirical gains over prior eviction methods.

Abstract: The KV cache used in large language models has linearly growing time complexity, so LLMs face memory blow-up and reduced decoding efficiency when they process long contexts.Current KV Cache eviction has become an important research direction; however, existing methods based on fixed Soft Tokens (e.g., Judge Q) rely on a static parameter set as the query to evaluate the importance of KV pairs, so they cannot adapt dynamically to different input prompts, and they cannot precisely capture complex and changing task relevance.Also, evicted KV pairs are discarded permanently, so this causes irreversible information loss and context breaks. To address this problem, we propose Meta-Soft, a dynamic compression framework based on probe-driven context integration. Specifically, we build a meta-library with a learnable orthogonal basis matrix $\mathcal{L}$, and we use a selector network with Gumbel-Softmax to produce differentiable sparse combination weights, so we dynamically synthesize the most targeted $k$ Soft Tokens from the input prompt features.We append these Soft Tokens to the end of the input sequence to probe key information. We also introduce an attention-flow based integration mechanism, which redistributes the semantic information of removed tokens into retained tokens, and this keeps the dropped context information effectively.Experiments on multiple datasets show that our method outperforms existing state-of-the-art eviction methods and provides a new solution for KV Cache compression.

</details>


### [185] [Scaling Observation-aware Planning in Uncertain Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22364)
*Adrian Zvizdenco,Arthur Conrado Veiga Bosquetti,Alberto Lluch Lafuente,Christoph Matheja*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A decomposition-based method identifies observation functions in POMDPs to solve SSP/POP, achieving 3-order-of-magnitude reduction in instance size and 5-order-of-magnitude reduction in runtime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deciding which sensing capabilities to deploy under uncertainty is costly; formalize as the Optimal Observability Problem (OOP) using POMDPs, with scalable solutions for its decidable fragments SSP and POP.

Method: Develop a new solving approach via decomposition of POMDPs to identify sensible observation functions, improving upon the original parameter-synthesis method and enabling scalable solving of SSP and POP.

Result: Significant scalability gains: approximately 3 orders of magnitude smaller problem instances and 5 orders of magnitude faster runtimes compared to the baseline approach.

Conclusion: Decomposition-based identification of observation functions markedly improves the scalability of solving SSP and POP within the OOP, advancing practical applicability in uncertain domains.

Abstract: Deciding which sensing capabilities to deploy on an agent in uncertain domains is a fundamental engineering challenge, in which one balances task achievability against the high costs of hardware and processing. This problem has previously been formalized as the Optimal Observability Problem (OOP), based on the well-known Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) model for decision-making. This work studies (sub-)symbolic techniques to scale solving of decidable fragments of the OOP, namely the Sensor Selection Problem (SSP) and the Positional Observability Problem (POP). Besides improving the original approach based on parameter synthesis, we develop a new solving method that identifies sensible observation functions via decomposition of POMDPs, improving performance by 3 and 5 orders of magnitude for instance size and runtime, respectively.

</details>


### [186] [Epicure: Navigating the Emergent Geometry of Food Ingredient Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22391)
*Jakub Radzikowski,Josef Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Epicure: multilingual recipe-ingredient embeddings from 4.14M recipes; three Metapath2Vec variants (Cooc, Chem, Core) built on ingredient co-occurrence and compound graphs, with LLM-assisted normalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Create multilingual, linguistically robust ingredient representations that capture both culinary context and chemistry, enabling cross-lingual culinary analytics.

Method: Collect 4.14M recipes from 11 sources across 7 languages; normalize ingredients to 1,790 canonical entries via LLM-augmented pipeline; build 203,508-edge ingredient-ingredient NPMI graph and 80,019-edge typed ingredient-compound graph with 2,247 compound nodes across 15 categories; seed three Metapath2Vec variants (Cooc, Chem, Core) with identical architectures/hyperparameters but different random-walk schemas; Core injects ingredient-ingredient walks into the chemistry-oriented graph.

Result: Produces three embeddings located at different points along the chemistry-vs-recipe-context spectrum, enabling analysis across culinary context and chemical relationships.

Conclusion: Epicure provides a scalable, multilingual, chemistry-aware ingredient-embedding framework that unifies recipe-context and compound knowledge in a single family of models, with potential downstream tasks in culinary analytics and cross-lingual retrieval.

Abstract: We present Epicure, a family of three sibling skip-gram ingredient embeddings retrained from scratch on a multilingual recipe corpus. We aggregate 4.14M recipes from 11 sources spanning seven languages, English, Chinese, Russian, Vietnamese, Spanish, Turkish, Indonesian, German, and Indian-English, and normalise the raw ingredient strings to 1,790 canonical entries via an LLM-augmented pipeline. A 203,508-edge ingredient-ingredient NPMI graph and an 80,019-edge typed FlavorDB ingredient-compound graph, 2,247 typed compound nodes across 15 categories, seed three Metapath2Vec variants that share architecture and hyperparameters and differ only in the random-walk schema: Cooc walks the co-occurrence graph only, Chem walks the typed compound metapaths only, and Core blends both via injected ingredient-ingredient walks at controlled mixing, placing each model at a distinct point on the chemistry-vs-recipe-context spectrum.

</details>


### [187] [S2ED: From Story to Executable Descriptions for Consistency-Aware Story Illustration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22448)
*Sijing Yin,Jiamou Liu,Xiao Tang,Yaser Shakib,Qian Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: S2ED is a training-free, model-agnostic framework that converts a full narrative into executable descriptions to enable long-horizon coherence and editable state propagation in multi-frame story illustration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address long-horizon coherence, persistent character identity, layout, and affect across frames in multi-frame narrative illustration, beyond single-image prompts.

Method: Prompt-layer, training-free framework with three agents: narrative segmentation, canonical attribute grounding, and enriched spatial/affective cues; outputs explicit executable descriptions that can be edited and propagated.

Result: Improves sequence-level consistency and character fidelity vs strong prompts, large-model planning, and a training-based method; validated on Flintstones and Shakoo Maku with automatic metrics and human judgments.

Conclusion: S2ED enables repair of drift via local edits without retraining and can be integrated into end-to-end story-to-storybook systems for children's illustrated stories.

Abstract: Multi-frame story illustration requires long-horizon coherence beyond single-image text-to-image generation, including narrative decomposition and persistent character identity, layout, and affect across frames. We propose Story-to-Executable Descriptions (S2ED), a training-free, model-agnostic, prompt-layer framework that converts a full story into a sequence of explicit, editable executable descriptions for more consistent rendering. S2ED coordinates three agents to segment the narrative, ground canonical character attributes, and enrich spatial and affective cues, enabling interpretable prompt-carried state propagation and local edits to repair drift without retraining the generator. Experiments on Flintstones and Shakoo Maku show that S2ED improves sequence-level consistency and character fidelity over strong prompting, large-model planning, and a reference training-based method, under both automatic metrics and human judgments. We also deploy S2ED in an end-to-end story-to-storybook system for children's illustrated stories, with a supplementary video.

</details>


### [188] [KAPPS: A knowledge-based CPPS Architecture for the Circular Factory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22457)
*Etienne Hoffmann,Jan-Felix Klein,Sören Weindel,Max Goebels,Sebastian Behrendt,Daniel Hernández,Ratan Bahadur Thapa,Jürgen Fleischer,Kai Furmans,Steffen Staab*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Presents KAPPS, a knowledge-based CPS for circular manufacturing using an ontology-grounded knowledge graph as a unified data backbone to enable reasoning, integration, and adaptive planning under uncertainty; validated via two use cases and 14 requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcomes limitations of conventional, deterministic manufacturing IT that cannot represent unique, heterogeneous, and uncertain product states or support human–machine knowledge exchange in circular manufacturing.

Method: Design science research deriving 14 requirements from five perspectives; implements KAPPS with an ontology-grounded knowledge graph, a semantic interface layer, constraint enforcement, and event-driven planning; demonstrates with anomaly-detection/learning and runtime constraint enforcement use cases; evaluates against the requirements.

Result: A unified, knowledge-driven architecture that enables data integration, reasoning, and adaptive execution planning under uncertainty; two implemented use cases validate applicability; architecture evaluated against the 14 derived requirements.

Conclusion: Knowledge graphs can serve as the factory’s authoritative write-time state in circular manufacturing, enabling dynamic, modular, and human–machine-informed operations and highlighting the viability of a knowledge-based CPS for managing variability and heterogeneity.

Abstract: While linear manufacturing relies on homogeneous materials and predefined process sequences, circular manufacturing reintroduces used products with heterogeneous and uncertain conditions. This shift demands manufacturing systems capable of handling variable product states, dynamically reconfigurable processes, and the integration of human and machine knowledge. Conventional manufacturing IT architectures, designed for stable structures and deterministic execution, are unable to meet these requirements, as they cannot adequately represent and manage the uniqueness of individual components at runtime. Following a design science methodology for developing a Cyber Physical Production System for circular manufacturing, we derive 14 requirements from five complementary perspectives. Based on these requirements, we design KAPPS, a knowledge-based architecture that uses an ontology-grounded knowledge graph as a unifying data backbone, combined with a semantic interface layer to enable consistent data and information integration, reasoning, and communication across heterogeneous systems and services, turning the knowledge graph from an integration layer into the factories authoritative write-time state. KAPPS incorporates modules for constraint enforcement and event-driven planning, enabling incremental adaptation of execution plans under uncertainty and human-machine knowledge exchange. The applicability of KAPPS is demonstrated through two implemented use cases: (i) Anomaly detection and learning through knowledge graph mediated services and (ii) runtime constraint enforcement in a modular conveyor system. Subsequently, the architecture is evaluated against the 14 requirements (ed. abstract shortened)

</details>


### [189] [Compiling Agentic Workflows into LLM Weights: Near-Frontier Quality at Two Orders of Magnitude Less Cost](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22502)
*Simon Dennis,Rivaan Patil,Kevin Shabahang,Hao Guo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Fine-tuning small models with embedded procedural knowledge (subterranean agents) can address core drawbacks of external agent orchestration, but real-world adoption remains hindered by three perceived barriers; empirical evaluation across multiple domains demonstrates feasibility and trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce reliance on external orchestrators by embedding procedures into model weights to avoid context-window bloat, avoid per-conversation frontier models, and protect proprietary procedures; assess whether the subterranean approach can match or surpass orchestration in practical tasks.

Method: Three empirical case studies: travel booking (14 nodes), Zoom support (14 nodes with product-specific knowledge), and insurance claims (55 nodes, 6 decision hubs). Compare a subterranean-agent (fine-tuned with embedded procedures) against an orchestration baseline; evaluate task success, efficiency, data exposure, and barrier-related metrics; analyze adoption barriers empirically.

Result: Demonstrates that embedding procedures into model weights can mitigate the three barriers and achieve competitive performance across the tested tasks; however, adoption remains dominated by orchestration due to tooling, maintenance, and generalization considerations; scale and domain-coverage challenges persist for the subterranean approach.

Conclusion: Procedural-weighted agents offer a viable path to address major drawbacks of external orchestration, but broad adoption requires improved tooling, scalable fine-tuning pipelines, and robust domain-knowledge coverage; hybrid or staged deployment may be practical in many settings.

Abstract: Agent orchestration frameworks have proliferated, collectively exceeding 290,000 GitHub stars across LangGraph, CrewAI, Google ADK, OpenAI Agents SDK, Semantic Kernel, Strands, and LlamaIndex. All follow the same pattern: an external orchestrator above the LLM, injecting instructions and routing decisions every turn. Recent work has shown this architecture is dominated for procedural tasks by simply providing the procedure in a frontier model's system prompt [Dennis et al., 2026a], at the cost of consuming the context window, requiring a frontier model for every conversation, and exposing proprietary procedures to third-party providers. Compiling the procedure into the weights of a small fine-tuned model -- creating a subterranean agent -- should resolve all of these concerns, and prior work (SimpleTOD, FireAct, SynTOD, WorkflowLLM, Agent Lumos) has shown the technique works. Yet developer adoption has overwhelmingly favored orchestration. We identify three perceived barriers and address each empirically across travel booking (14 nodes), Zoom support (14 nodes, product-specific knowledge), and insurance claims (55 nodes, 6 decision hubs).

</details>


### [190] [LACO: Adaptive Latent Communication for Collaborative Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22504)
*Tianhao Chen,Yuheng Wu,Dongman Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LACO is a training-free latent communication framework for collaborative driving that reduces communication and inference latency while preserving performance by operating on pretrained driving models. It introduces Iterative Latent Deliberation (ILD), Cross-Horizon Saliency Attribution (CHSA), and Structured Semantic Knowledge Distillation (SSKD) and validates in CARLA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome high latency from autoregressive language-based coordination and information loss from token discretization, and to mitigate agent identity confusion arising from fusing latent states, enabling efficient, end-to-end adaptation of pretrained driving models to coordination tasks.

Method: Proposes LACO: a training-free latent communication paradigm that leverages pretrained models. ILD for latent reasoning, CHSA for selecting salient information across horizons, SSKD to stabilize ego-centric decisions. Evaluated in closed-loop CARLA experiments.

Result: Significant reductions in communication and inference latency with robust collaborative driving performance maintained, across simulated scenarios in CARLA.

Conclusion: LACO offers a practical, training-free path to latent coordination in multi-agent driving by addressing latent reasoning, communication efficiency, and stability, enabling seamless adaptation of pretrained models to collaborative tasks.

Abstract: Collaborative driving aims to improve safety and efficiency by enabling connected vehicles to coordinate under partial observability. Recent approaches have evolved from sharing visual features for perception to exchanging language-based reasoning through foundation models for behavioral coordination. Though communicating in language provides intuitive information, it introduces two challenges: high latency caused by autoregressive decoding and information loss caused by compressing rich internal representations into discrete tokens. To address these challenges, we analyze latent communication in collaborative driving under inherent limitations of multi-agent settings. Our analysis reveals agent identity confusion, where direct fusion of latent states entangles decision representations across vehicles. Motivated by this, we propose LACO, a training-free \textbf{LA}tent \textbf{CO}mmunication paradigm that seamlessly adapts pretrained driving models to collaborative settings. LACO introduces Iterative Latent Deliberation (ILD) for latent reasoning, Cross-Horizon Saliency Attribution (CHSA) for communication-efficient information selection, and Structured Semantic Knowledge Distillation (SSKD) to stabilize ego-centric decision making. Closed-loop experiments in CARLA show that LACO notably reduces communication and inference latency while maintaining strong collaborative driving performance.

</details>


### [191] [Towards Direct Evaluation of Harness Optimizers via Priority Ranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22505)
*Kai Tzu-iunn Ong,Minseok Kang,Dongwook Choi,Junhee Cho,Seungju Kim,Seungwon Lim,Geunha Jang,Minwoo Oh,Bogyung Jeong,Sunghwan Kim,Taeyoon Kwon,Jinyoung Yeo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes priority ranking as a low-cost, direct evaluation for harness optimizers by having them rank harness components by their potential to improve/hinder performance; shows ranking correlates with real multi-step optimization gains using the Shor dataset; provides code/data for replication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation of harness optimizers relies on indirect, end-task performance gains, ignoring intermediate, possibly erroneous actions; direct, low-cost evaluation is needed due to the lack of oracle harnesses.

Method: Introduce a priority ranking task where harness optimizers score components (e.g., tools) in a given harness by their potential impact when updated. Use Shor, a collection of 182 human-verified optimization scenarios across domains, designs, and time stages, to assess ranking ability. Compare ranking performance with actual multi-step harness optimization outcomes.

Result: Rankings produced by harness optimizers show a positive correlation with their effectiveness in subsequent multi-step harness optimization, establishing priority ranking as a reliable predictor of optimization ability across scenarios.

Conclusion: Priority ranking provides a practical, scalable proxy for evaluating harness optimizers. The Shor dataset enables broad, cross-domain assessment. Code and data are published at the provided GitHub link to support replication and comparison.

Abstract: Harness optimization enables automated agent creation by having an optimizer agent iteratively update the harness of target agents. Despite its success, current studies evaluate optimizers solely by observing target agents' performance gains. This indirect end-improvement evaluation neglects optimizers' actions at intermediate steps, which are often erroneous and hinder agent performance. Therefore, it is unclear whether harness optimization is driven by optimizers' informed update actions or simply trial-and-error. This necessitates direct evaluation of harness optimizers. However, evaluating harness optimizers directly is non-trivial and costly due to the lack of oracle harnesses. To address this, we present a simple, low-cost design to directly evaluate them, namely priority ranking. By asking harness optimizers to rank components (e.g., tools) in a given harness by their potential to improve/hinder agent performance when updated, our design quantifies optimizer ability at the step level without expensive rollouts or manual examination. More importantly, optimizers' ranking performance correlates with their ability to improve agents in actual multi-step harness optimization, establishing priority ranking as a reliable predictor of optimization ability. Priority ranking is enabled by Shor, a collection of 182 human-verified optimization scenarios spanning across domains, designs, and time stages. Codes and data can be found at https://github.com/k59118/Harness_Optimizer_Evaluation.

</details>


### [192] [Search-E1: Self-Distillation Drives Self-Evolution in Search-Augmented Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22511)
*Zihan Liang,Yufei Ma,Ben Chen,Zhipeng Qian,Xuxin Zhang,Huangyu Dai,Lingtao Mao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Search-E1 is a minimal self-evolution method that combines vanilla GRPO with offline self-distillation to improve a search-augmented reasoning agent, achieving strong QA performance without external supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many post-training augmentation strategies add external supervision, modules, or sophisticated rollout schemes, but their necessity is unclear. This work asks whether a simpler approach can suffice.

Method: Interleave vanilla GRPO rounds with offline self-distillation (OFSD). After each GRPO round, the policy rollouts are performed on its own training questions. A token-level forward KL objective aligns the policy's inference-time distribution to its own distribution under a privileged context that reveals a more efficient sibling trajectory, providing dense per-step supervision.

Result: On seven QA benchmarks, Search-E1 achieves 0.440 average EM with Qwen2.5-3B, surpassing all open-source baselines at both scales.

Conclusion: A simple pipeline with offline self-distillation can match or surpass heavier, externally-supervised search-enhanced methods, suggesting a minimal, robust path for improving search-augmented reasoning agents; code will be released.

Abstract: Post-training has become the dominant recipe for turning a language model into a competent search-augmented reasoning agent. A line of recent work pushes its performance further by adding elaborate machinery on top of this standard pipeline. These augmentations import external supervision from stronger external systems, attach auxiliary modules such as process reward models or retrospective critics, restructure the rollout itself with tree search or multi-stage curricula, or shape the reward with hand-crafted bonuses and penalties. Each addition delivers a measurable gain, but each also inflates the training pipeline and ties the recipe to resources or designs that may not always be available. We take a step back and ask whether any of this machinery is actually necessary, and propose Search-E1, a self-evolution method that lets a search-augmented agent improve through only vanilla GRPO interleaved with offline self-distillation (OFSD). After each GRPO round, the policy rolls out on its own training questions. A token-level forward KL objective then aligns the policy's inference-time distribution to its own distribution under a privileged context that exposes a more efficient sibling trajectory. Despite this simplicity, the procedure naturally provides dense per-step supervision. On seven QA benchmarks, Search-E1 reaches $0.440$ average EM with Qwen2.5-3B, surpassing all open-source baselines at both scales. Code and complete version will be made public soon.

</details>


### [193] [Meta-Learning for Rapid Adaptation in Reference Tracking of Uncertain Nonlinear Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22513)
*Jiaqi Yan,Ankush Chakrabarty,Niklas Schmid,John Lygeros,Alisa Rupenyan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A meta-learning control framework based on iMAML for uncertain nonlinear systems, combining offline meta-training on source data with online meta-adaptation on the target, using bi-level optimization; implemented with two instantiations (neural state-space model and DQN); demonstrated gains in simulations and hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Limited target-system data and difficulty of collecting data from the target; leverage data from source systems with similar dynamics through meta-learning to accelerate training and improve control performance.

Method: Tailors iMAML to control: offline meta-training learns a shared representation of system dynamics from source data; online meta-adaptation fine-tunes on target with few samples and few steps; bi-level optimization with an efficient solution and reduced storage; framework is general and can incorporate different learning algorithms; two instantiations: neural state-space model and deep Q-network, differing on whether explicit system identification is needed.

Result: Numerical simulations and hardware experiments show enhanced control performance and consistent improvement over baseline approaches.

Conclusion: The framework provides a flexible, data-efficient, transferable meta-learning approach for nonlinear control, validated across modeling paradigms and demonstrating practical viability for data-limited target systems.

Abstract: In this paper, we address the problem of reference tracking for uncertain nonlinear systems. Since collecting data from the target system (i.e., the system of interest) is often challenging, our objective is to design optimal controllers using limited target system data. Meta-learning provides a promising paradigm by leveraging offline data from source systems (systems sharing structural similarities with the target system) to accelerate training and enhance control performance. Motivated by this idea, we propose a meta-learning-based control framework that tailors the implicit model-agnostic meta-learning (iMAML) algorithm to the control setting. The framework operates in two phases: an (offline) meta-training phase, where an aggregated representation is learned from source data to capture the shared system dynamics among similar systems, and an (online) meta-adaptation phase, where this representation is fine-tuned on the target system using only a few data samples and limited adaptation steps. We formulate this framework as a bi-level optimization problem and provide an efficient solution with reduced storage complexity and few approximations. The proposed framework is general, allowing various learning algorithms to be integrated. To demonstrate this flexibility, we propose two specific learning algorithms that can be incorporated into our framework based on a neural state-space model and a deep Q-network, respectively. The primary distinction between these approaches is whether explicit system identification is required. Numerical simulations and hardware experiments demonstrate that the proposed methods enhance control performance and consistently outperform baseline approaches.

</details>


### [194] [A Subjective Logic-based method for runtime confidence updates in safety arguments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22530)
*Benjamin Herd,Jessica Kelly,Clarissa Heinemann,João-Vitor Zacchi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A dynamic, runtime-driven method for updating safety-case confidence using Subjective Logic; it fuses design-time evidence with windowed runtime SPIs; updates claims at runtime via rules that boost confidence when no violations occur and penalize on violations, demonstrated on an ML cone-detection component in a construction-zone simulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static safety cases do not reflect evolving runtime evidence; there is a need for continuous, quantitative assurance that adapts to observed runtime evidence.

Method: Integrate design-time evidence and windowed runtime Safety Performance Indicators (SPIs) within a single Subjective Logic–based assurance case; at runtime, SPI evidence is evaluated and targeted claims are updated using rules that increase confidence in the absence of violations and impose penalties when violations occur, prioritizing safety-relevant responsiveness over exact Bayesian posterior updates.

Result: Simulation-based demonstration focusing on an ML-based construction cone detection component within a construction-zone assist function; shows how confidence evolves as SPI evidence is observed in operation.

Conclusion: Supports dynamic, runtime-driven quantitative assurance by propagating confidence through a Subjective Logic–based assurance framework, emphasizing responsiveness to safety-relevant signals over precise posterior values.

Abstract: We present a method for dynamic quantitative assurance that enhances static safety cases with continuous, runtime-driven confidence updates. The method quantifies and propagates confidence across the development lifecycle by integrating design-time evidence and windowed runtime Safety Performance Indicators (SPIs) within a single Subjective Logic (SL)-based assurance case. At runtime, SPI evidence is continuously evaluated, and targeted claims are updated using a rule that increases confidence in the absence of violations and imposes prompt penalties when violations occur. This design prioritizes safety-relevant responsiveness over exact classical Bayesian posterior updates. We demonstrate the method using a simulation-based construction zone assist function, focusing on an ML-based construction cone detection component, and show how confidence evolves as SPI evidence is observed in operation.

</details>


### [195] [TerminalWorld: Benchmarking Agents on Real-World Terminal Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22535)
*Zhaoyang Chu,Jiarui Hu,Xingyu Jiang,Pengyu Zou,Han Li,Chao Peng,Peter O'Hearn,Earl T. Barr,Mark Harman,Federica Sarro,He Ye*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TerminalWorld automatically reverse-engineers real-world terminal tasks from 80k+ recordings into 1.53k tasks, with a 200-task Verified subset; benchmarking shows models struggle with authentic terminal workflows (max pass rate 62.5%); data/code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for authentic, scalable benchmarks of terminal reasoning and action in realistic workflows. Existing expert-curated benchmarks may not reflect real-world usage and can be weakly correlated with actual performance.

Method: Process 80,870 terminal recordings to extract 1,530 validated tasks spanning 18 categories and 1,280 unique commands; automatically curate a Verified subset of 200 tasks with manual review; benchmark eight frontier models and six agents on TerminalWorld-Verified across real-world terminal environments; compare against existing benchmarks (e.g., Terminal-Bench).

Result: Max pass rate on TerminalWorld-Verified tasks is 62.5%. TerminalWorld measures capture terminal capabilities distinct from existing benchmarks, with only a weak correlation to Terminal-Bench scores (Pearson r=0.20). The dataset and code are publicly available at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: TerminalWorld offers an authentic, scalable evaluation framework for real-world terminal workflows, enabling ongoing benchmarking as developer practices evolve and highlighting remaining gaps in current models when confronted with authentic terminal tasks.

Abstract: We introduce TerminalWorld, a scalable data engine that automatically reverse-engineers high-fidelity evaluation tasks from "in-the-wild" terminal recordings. Processing 80,870 terminal recordings, the engine yields a full benchmark of 1,530 validated tasks, spanning 18 real-world categories, ranging from short everyday operations to workflows exceeding 50 steps, and covering 1,280 unique commands. From these, we curate a Verified subset of 200 representative, manually reviewed tasks. Comprehensive benchmarking on TerminalWorld-Verified across eight frontier models and six agents reveals that current systems still struggle with authentic terminal workflows, achieving a maximum pass rate of only 62.5%. Moreover, TerminalWorld captures real-world terminal capabilities distinct from existing expert-curated benchmarks (e.g., Terminal-Bench), with only a weak correlation to their scores (Pearson r=0.20). The automated engine makes TerminalWorld authentic and scalable by construction, enabling it to evaluate agents in real-world terminal environments as developer practices evolve. Data and code are available at https://github.com/EuniAI/TerminalWorld.

</details>


### [196] [Think Thrice Before You Speak: Dual knowledge-enhanced Theory-of-Mind Reasoning for Persuasive Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22602)
*Minghui Ma,Bin Guo,Runze Yang,Mengqi Chen,Yan Liu,Jingqi Liu,Yahan Pei,Xuehao Ma,Qiuyun Zhang,Zhiwen Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces ToM-PD for modeling sequential latent mental states in persuasive dialogue, along with a large-scale ToM-BPD dataset and the TTBYS reasoning framework; reports Qwen3-8B with TTBYS outperforming GPT-5 on desires, beliefs, and persuasive strategies; claims improved interpretability and consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-driven persuasive dialogue often relies on simplistic prompting and insufficient Theory of Mind (ToM) knowledge, yielding fragmented representations and unstable reasoning. There is a need to explicitly model belief-desire-intention (BDI) dependencies and to provide a rich, labeled dataset to support multi-turn ToM reasoning.

Method: Define a ToM-based persuasive dialogue (ToM-PD) task grounded in the BDI framework to capture sequential dependencies among mental states. Build ToM-based Broad Persuasive Dialogues (ToM-BPD) dataset with fine-grained annotations for desires, beliefs, intentions, and persuasive strategies. Propose Think Thrice Before You Speak (TTBYS), a knowledge-enhanced stepwise reasoning framework that leverages explicit and implicit prior experiences to improve LLM inference of desires, beliefs, and persuasive strategies. Evaluate Qwen3-8B with TTBYS against GPT-5 and analyze interpretability.

Result: Empirical results show Qwen3-8B with TTBYS outperforms GPT-5 by 1.20% (desires), 22.80% (beliefs), and 16.97% (persuasive strategies); case studies indicate improved interpretability and consistency in reasoning.

Conclusion: The ToM-PD task, ToM-BPD dataset, and TTBYS framework together advance multi-turn ToM reasoning for persuasive dialogue, enabling more reliable inference of latent mental states and strategies; results suggest potential for broader applicability with larger models and further ablations.

Abstract: Persuasive dialogue requires reasoning about others' latent mental states, a capability known as Theory of Mind (ToM). However, due to reliance on simple prompting strategies and insufficient ToM knowledge, existing LLMs often fail to capture the intrinsic dependencies among mental states, leading to fragmented representations and unstable reasoning. To address these challenges, we introduce the ToM-based Persuasive Dialogue (ToM-PD) task, grounded in the Belief-Desire-Intention (BDI) framework, which explicitly models the sequential dependencies among mental states in multi-turn dialogues. To facilitate research on this task, we construct a large-scale annotated dataset, ToM-based Broad Persuasive Dialogues (ToM-BPD), capturing fine-grained mental states and corresponding persuasive strategies. We further propose Think Thrice Before You Speak (TTBYS), a knowledge-enhanced stepwise reasoning framework that leverages both explicit and implicit prior experiences to improve LLMs' inference of desires, beliefs, and persuasive strategies. Experimental results demonstrate that Qwen3-8B equipped with TTBYS outperforms GPT-5 by 1.20%, 22.80%, and 16.97% in predicting desires, beliefs, and persuasive strategies, respectively. Case studies further show that our approach enhances interpretability and consistency in reasoning.

</details>


### [197] [Spreadsheet-RL: Advancing Large Language Model Agents on Realistic Spreadsheet Tasks via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22642)
*Banghao Chi,Yining Xie,Mingyuan Wu,Jingcheng Yang,Jize Jiang,Zhaoheng Li,Shengyi Qian,Minjia Zhang,Klara Nahrstedt,Rui Hou,Xiangjun Fan,Hanchao Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RL fine-tuning framework and environment (Spreadsheet-RL) for specialized spreadsheet agents, plus Domain-Spreadsheet benchmark and Spreadsheet Gym; yields notable Pass@1 gains on general and domain tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing prompt-driven spreadsheet agents struggle with multi-step, real-world workflows; a scalable RL-based approach with realistic environment is needed.

Method: Develop a full RL fine-tuning pipeline (Spreadsheet-RL) including: automated collection of paired start-goal spreadsheets, a Domain-Spreadsheet benchmark, and a Spreadsheet Gym environment with a Python sandbox and tool-routing rules to train agents across multi-turn spreadsheet tasks.

Result: Significant performance gains: Pass@1 on SpreadsheetBench from 12.0% to 23.4%, and Pass@1 on Domain-Spreadsheet from 8.4% to 17.2%, indicating improved generalization and potential for real-world adoption.

Conclusion:  RL-based training in a realistic spreadsheet environment substantially improves AI spreadsheet agents, supporting broader LLM-based interactions with data interfaces.

Abstract: Spreadsheet systems (e.g., Microsoft Excel, Google Sheets) play a central role in modern data-centric workflows. As AI agents grow increasingly capable of automating complex tasks, such as controlling computers and generating presentations, building an AI-driven spreadsheet agent has emerged as a promising research direction. Most existing spreadsheet agents rely on specialized prompting over general-purpose LLMs; while this design has potentials on simple spreadsheet operations, it struggles to manage the complex, multi-step workflows typical of real-world applications.
  We introduce Spreadsheet-RL, a reinforcement learning (RL) fine-tuning framework designed to train specialized spreadsheet agents within a realistic Microsoft Excel environment. Spreadsheet-RL features an automated pipeline for scalable collection of paired start-goal spreadsheets from online forums, as well as domain-specific evaluation tasks in areas such as finance and supply chain management, which we compile into the new Domain-Spreadsheet benchmark dataset. It also includes a Spreadsheet Gym environment designed for multi-turn RL: Spreadsheet Gym exposes extensive Excel functionality through a Python sandbox, along with a refined harness that incorporates a comprehensive tool set and carefully designed tool-routing rules for spreadsheet tasks. Through comprehensive experiments, we show that Spreadsheet-RL substantially enhances AI agent's performance on both general and domain-specific spreadsheet tasks: it improves Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507's Pass@1 on SpreadsheetBench from 12.0% to 23.4%, and raises Pass@1 from 8.4% to 17.2% on our curated Domain-Spreadsheet dataset. These results highlight Spreadsheet-RL's strong potential for generalization and real-world adoption in spreadsheet automation, and broadly, its promise for advancing LLM-based interactions with data interfaces in everyday work.

</details>


### [198] [AtelierEval: Agentic Evaluation of Humans & LLMs as Text-to-Image Prompters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22645)
*Hanjun Luo,Zhimu Huang,Sylvia Chung,Yiran Wang,Yingbin Jin,Jialin Li,Jiang Li,Xinfeng Li,Hanan Salam*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces AtelierEval and AtelierJudge—first unified benchmark to quantify prompting proficiency of upstream prompters for text-to-image, across 360 tasks, evaluating humans and MLLMs; reports strong correlation with human experts and insights such as mimicry over planning; includes broad experiments across 8 MLLMs, 48 humans, and 4 backends; released for future research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation fixes prompts and only assesses T2I models, leaving upstream prompting proficiency unmeasured. A cognitive-grounded, scalable benchmark is needed to quantify prompting across real-world challenges for both humans and MLLMs.

Method: Propose AtelierEval with three task categories instantiated from a real-world challenge taxonomy, with a dual human/MLLM interface. Introduce AtelierJudge, a memory-augmented agentic evaluator that outputs subjective and objective scores for prompt–image pairs. Evaluate 8 MLLMs vs 48 humans across 4 T2I backends on 360 tasks.

Result: AtelierEval achieves a Spearman correlation of 0.79 with human experts, approaching human performance. Experimental results validate the benchmark as a robust diagnostic tool, reveal the superiority of mimicry over planning, and show image-augmented prompting as a favorable direction for prompters.

Conclusion: The framework provides a first unified, scalable tool to diagnose prompting proficiency; supports future research; demonstrations across multiple models/users and backends; release to the community.

Abstract: Text-to-image (T2I) systems increasingly rely on upstream prompters, either humans or multimodal large language models (MLLMs), to translate user intent into detailed prompts. Yet current benchmarks fix the prompt and only evaluate T2I models, leaving the prompting proficiency of this upstream component entirely unmeasured. We introduce AtelierEval, the first unified benchmark that quantifies prompting proficiency across 360 expert-crafted tasks. Grounded in a cognitive view, it spans three task categories and instantiates tasks using a taxonomy of real-world challenges, with a dual interface for both humans and MLLMs. To enable scalable and reliable evaluation, we propose AtelierJudge, a skill-based, memory-augmented agentic evaluator. It produces subjective and objective scores for prompt-image pairs, achieving a Spearman correlation of 0.79 with human experts, approaching human performance. Extensive experiments benchmark 8 MLLMs against 48 human users across 4 T2I backends, validate AtelierEval as a robust diagnostic tool, and reveal the superiority of mimicry over planning, advocating for an image-augmented direction for future prompters. Our work is released to support future research.

</details>


### [199] [Claw AI Lab: An Autonomous Multi-Agent Research Team](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22662)
*Fan Wu,Cheng Chen,Zhenshan Tan,Taiyu Zhang,Xinzhen Xu,Yanyu Qian,Dingcheng Gao,Lanyun Zhu,Qi Zhu,Yi Tan,Deyi Ji,Guosheng Lin,Tianrun Chen,Deheng Ye,Fayao Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Claw AI Lab provides a lab-native autonomous research platform that enables forming a full research team from a single prompt, with a Claw-Code Harness to connect local assets to runnable experiments. Evaluated on five AI case studies, it is preferred by experts for idea novelty, experiment completeness, and paper presentation, signaling a move toward steerable, reliable autonomous research infrastructure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To shift from single-agent or fixed serial research pipelines to interactive, multi-agent, lab-like autonomous research platforms that improve efficiency, reproducibility, and result integrity.

Method: Design and implementation of Claw AI Lab: a platform that supports customizable roles, collaborative workflows, real-time monitoring, artifact inspection, rollback/resume via a unified dashboard; supports exploration, multi-agent discussion, and reproduction modes; Claw-Code Harness connects local codebases, datasets, and checkpoints to runnable experiments and feeds artifacts back into the research loop.

Result: Internal evaluation on five AI research case studies shows Claw AI Lab is consistently preferred by AI expert judges over AutoResearchClaw in terms of idea novelty, experiment completeness, and paper presentation quality.

Conclusion: Claw AI Lab is an early step toward autonomous research as usable, interactive, and reliability-aware scientific infrastructure, signaling a new paradigm of lab-like autonomous scientific workflows.

Abstract: We present Claw AI Lab, a lab-native autonomous research platform that advances automated research from a hidden prompt-to-paper pipeline into an interactive AI laboratory. Rather than centering the system around a single agent or a fixed serial workflow, we allow users to instantiate a full research team from one prompt, with customizable roles, collaborative workflows, real-time monitoring, artifact inspection, and rollback/resume control through a unified dashboard. The platform also supports distinct research modes for exploration, multi-agent discussion, and reproduction, making autonomous research substantially more steerable and laboratory-like in practice. A key practical contribution of Claw AI Lab lies in its Claw-Code Harness, which connects local codebases, datasets, and checkpoints to runnable experiments and feeds execution artifacts back into the research loop. As a result, the harness improves not only execution integration, but also experimental completion and result integrity: experiments are easier to inspect, iterate on, and faithfully transfer into final papers, reducing common failure modes such as partial runs and malformed result reporting. In our internal evaluation on five AI research case studies, using AutoResearchClaw as the baseline, Claw AI Lab is consistently preferred by AI expert judges on idea novelty, experiment completeness, and paper presentation quality. We view Claw AI Lab as an early step toward a new paradigm: autonomous research as usable, interactive, and reliability-aware scientific infrastructure.

</details>


### [200] [WorkstreamBench: Evaluating LLM Agents on End-to-End Spreadsheet Tasks in Finance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22664)
*Thomson Yen,Julian Poeltl,Harshith Srinivas Gear,Yilin Meng,Joshua Fan,Adam Shen,Yili Liu,Ali Bauyrzhan,Siri Du,Haoyang Liu,Daniel Guetta,Hongseok Namkoong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: First evaluation of LLM agents on end-to-end spreadsheet tasks for financial workflows; introduces a three-dimensional evaluation taxonomy (Accuracy, Formula, Format); Claude family performs best but overall outputs fall short of professional finance standards, especially as task complexity grows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses a gap in benchmarks for end-to-end spreadsheet capabilities of AI agents within finance, where outputs must be accurate, modifiable, and well-formatted for professional review.

Method: Develop a multidimensional evaluation taxonomy (Accuracy, Formula, Format) and apply it to end-to-end spreadsheet tasks (modeling, forecasting, scenario analysis) using existing agents (notably Claude family). Outputs are evaluated qualitatively for professional standards, including readability and ease of modification; complexity is increased with chained calculations to assess robustness.

Result: Claude agents produce the most professional-looking spreadsheets among evaluated agents, but even the strongest fall short of professional finance standards; performance deteriorates as task complexity (e.g., chained calculations) increases.

Conclusion: Current AI agents are not yet reliable for producing professional-quality, end-to-end spreadsheets at realistic financial workflow scales; the benchmark exposes gaps in accuracy, formula reliability, and formatting, guiding future research toward more robust, modifiable, and standards-compliant outputs.

Abstract: LLM agents are increasingly expected to carry out end-to-end workflows, producing complete artifacts from high-level user instructions. To meet enterprise needs, frontier AI labs have developed agents that can construct entire spreadsheets from scratch. This is especially relevant in finance, where core workflows such as financial modeling, forecasting, and scenario analysis are commonly conducted through spreadsheets. Yet, existing spreadsheet benchmarks do not measure this advanced capability, focusing instead on question-answering or single-formula edits. To address this gap, we provide one of the first evaluations of agents on end-to-end spreadsheet tasks, focusing on economically critical financial workflows such as modeling and scenario analysis. Since deliverables therein are routinely reviewed and revised by multiple stakeholders, judging their quality necessarily involves high-level criteria such as readability or ease of modification. To reflect the multidimensional nature of solution quality, we develop an evaluation taxonomy comprising three dimensions: Accuracy, Formula, and Format, each comprising fine-grained criteria that reflect professional standards. The Claude family leads the benchmark and produces the most professional-looking outputs in our qualitative review, but even the strongest agents frequently fall short of professional finance standards and degrade sharply as the difficulty increases beyond a few chained calculations. This suggests that current agents are not yet able to reliably produce professional-quality spreadsheets at the level of complexity real-world workflows demand.

</details>


### [201] [Is Capability a Liability? More Capable Language Models Make Worse Forecasts When It Matters Most](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22672)
*Nick Merrill,Jaeho Lee,Ezra Karger*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: More capable LLMs exhibit worse upper-tail forecast calibration (inverse scaling) on forecasting tasks involving superlinear growth and regime-change risk; this occurs across synthetic and real data; tail-focused evaluation is necessary.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether scaling up LLMs improves distributional forecast accuracy in domains with rapid growth and tail risk, and whether standard single-threshold metrics capture this behavior.

Method: ForecastBench-Sim (FBSim) for forecasting synthetic SIR epidemics with a matched linear control; replication on real-world datasets (COVID-19, measles, housing markets, hyperinflation); per-quantile decomposition to analyze tails; within-family study of Llama-3.1 to separate scale vs post-training effects; comparison of tail-inclusive vs single-threshold metrics.

Result: Inverse scaling: more capable models yield worse upper-tail forecasts; lower tail remains similar; both model scale and post-training contribute; domain knowledge does not reliably rescue calibration; single-threshold metrics obscure the effect, while tail-inclusive scoring reveals the reversed capability–accuracy relationship.

Conclusion: Evaluation of LLM forecasting should use continuous, unbounded accuracy measures alongside bounded binary thresholds to properly capture tail costs and avoid misleading conclusions about model capability.

Abstract: We document inverse scaling in LLMs on forecasting problems whose underlying time series exhibit superlinear growth and tail risk of regime change, a structure common in finance and epidemiology. On these tasks, more capable models produce worse distributional forecasts. The pattern appears on ForecastBench-Sim (FBSim), a contamination-free, simulated-world benchmark we release, in forecasting synthetic SIR epidemics with a matched linear control, and replicates in real-world datasets on COVID-19, measles, housing markets, and hyperinflation. A per-quantile decomposition shows the failure concentrates at the upper tail, which more capable models shift upward to track aggressive extrapolations of growth, while the lower tail stays put. A within-family study of Llama-3.1 shows that both model scale and post-training independently contribute to this effect. Domain knowledge does not reliably rescue calibration. This inverse scaling does not appear on single-threshold metrics common in LLM forecasting benchmarks, reversing the sign of the capability--accuracy relationship on identical outputs. Single-threshold scoring at conventional cutoffs misses the upper-tail cost; tail-inclusive scoring reverses the sign of the capability--accuracy relationship on the same outputs. We recommend that LLM forecasting evaluations use continuous (and unbounded) measures of accuracy alongside bounded binary threshold metrics.

</details>


### [202] [Forecasting Scientific Progress with Artificial Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22681)
*Sean Wu,Pan Lu,Yupeng Chen,Jonathan Bragg,Yutaro Yamada,Peter Clark,David Clifton,Philip Torr,James Zou,Junchi Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Current frontier AI models struggle to forecast scientific progress with reliability; they identify plausible directions but poorly predict occurrence and timing, showing domain-dependent performance and overconfidence under controlled knowledge constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically evaluate whether AI can forecast scientific progress by constructing a temporally grounded, multi-domain benchmark under controlled information access, addressing the gap between plausible research directions and realized advances.

Method: Introduce CUSP (Cutoff-conditioned Unseen Scientific Progress), a benchmark with 4,760 scientific events and four evaluation axes (feasibility assessment, mechanistic reasoning, generative solution design, and temporal prediction). Assess AI forecasting under varying information access (pre- and post-cutoff) and across domains (biology, chemistry, physics, etc.).

Result: Models can identify plausible directions from competing candidates but fail to reliably predict realization and timing of advances; performance is highly domain-dependent and timing of progress is more predictable than domain-specific scientific advances. Gains from pre-cutoff knowledge do not close the gap to full-information settings, and high-citation advances are harder to predict. Models exhibit systematic overconfidence and response biases, limiting reliable uncertainty estimation.

Conclusion: AI systems currently are not reliable predictors of scientific progress; access to prior knowledge does not guarantee improved forecasting, and post-event information provides more benefit than forward-looking predictions.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly embedded in scientific discovery, yet whether it can anticipate scientific progress remains unclear. To study this question, we introduce a temporally grounded evaluation framework for forecasting scientific progress under controlled knowledge constraints. We present CUSP (Cutoff-conditioned Unseen Scientific Progress), a multi-disciplinary and event-level benchmark that evaluates scientific forecasting in AI systems through feasibility assessment, mechanistic reasoning, generative solution design, and temporal prediction. Across 4,760 scientific events, we observe systematic and domain-dependent limitations in current frontier models. While models can identify plausible research directions from competing candidates, they fail to reliably predict whether scientific advances will be realized and systematically misestimate when they will occur. Performance is highly heterogeneous across domains, with the timing of AI progress more predictable than advances in biology, chemistry, and physics. Performance is largely insensitive to whether events occur before or after the training cutoff, suggesting these limitations cannot be explained solely by knowledge exposure in training data. Under controlled information access, additional pre-cutoff knowledge improves performance but does not close the gap to full-information settings, which becomes more pronounced for high-citation advances. Models also exhibit systematic overconfidence and strong response biases, indicating unreliable uncertainty estimation. Taken together, current AI systems fall short as predictive tools for scientific progress. Access to prior knowledge does not translate into reliable forecasting, and performance benefits more from post-event information than from forward-looking prediction.

</details>


### [203] [Beyond the Org Chart: AI and the Transformation of Invisible Work](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22707)
*Stephanie Rosenthal,Shamsi Iqbal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI adoption reshapes formal roles and informal practices in a large tech firm; qualitative study of 24 product professionals reveals both collaboration gains and risks to career visibility, with actionable steps to surface invisible work and sustain culture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how AI-forward work environments transform job boundaries, collaborations, and informal practices like mentoring, and to guide organizational and leadership responses.

Method: Qualitative interview study with 24 product-focused professionals at a large technology firm; analysis likely thematic to examine impact on individual work, team dynamics, and professional interactions.

Result: AI alters formal responsibilities and collaborations and also shifts informal cultural practices such as mentoring; some changes improve peer collaboration, while others threaten career growth opportunities and access to feedback networks; the authors propose steps to make invisible work visible and to support colleagues during AI transformation while preserving diverse thinking and collaboration.

Conclusion: Offers practical recommendations for organizations and leaders to manage AI-driven changes without eroding mentorship, informal networks, or inclusive culture, emphasizing visibility of invisible work and preservation of healthy organizational norms.

Abstract: An increasing number of news and research articles report that AI adoption is allowing professionals to blur and extend the boundaries of their corporate roles. With the goal of understanding how work processes might be changing in an AI-forward company, we interviewed 24 product-focused individuals at a large technology firm about how AI has impacted their own work, their work within their product team, and their professional interactions. Our conversations suggest that AI is not only changing formal role responsibilities and collaborations between those roles, but also changing informal cultural practices like professional mentoring that are key to helping professionals settle in their positions, stay engaged with their work, and grow their careers. Some of these changes are positive, such as smoother collaboration between peers, but other changes are more nuanced and put the typical career growth opportunities, like receiving feedback from professional networks and promoting leadership and mentorship, at risk. We propose steps that AI companies can take to make the invisible work more visible. Additionally, we propose efforts that individuals and leaders can take to support their colleagues through AI transformation while preserving healthy company cultures that support diverse thinking, collaboration, and informal interactions.
