</details>


### [204] [AMEL: Accumulated Message Effects on LLM Judgments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22714)
*Sid-ali Temkit*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Negative conversation history biases LLM judgments (AMEL); bias is stronger for uncertain items, does not depend on history length, and is larger for negative histories. Mitigation: use fresh context per item or balanced histories during batching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify whether the polarity of prior conversation history biases subsequent LLM judgments and to characterize the AMEL phenomenon across models and providers.

Method: Analysis of 75,898 API calls across 11 models from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and four open-source providers. Identical test items were presented in isolation or after histories saturated with predominantly positive or negative evaluations. Examined varying context lengths (up to 50 turns), assessed per-item baseline uncertainty (entropy), and conducted follow-up analyses on token-level distributions and position effects.

Result: Models shift toward the conversation's polarity (overall d = -0.17, p < 1e-46). Stronger bias for high-entropy items (d = -0.34) than for deterministic items (d = -0.15). Bias does not grow with context length; 5 vs 50 turns yield similar shifts. Negative histories induce 1.62x more bias than positive histories. Scaling varies by model family but bias persists. Token distributions shift continuously; the negativity asymmetry has both token-level and semantic components. Position within history does not matter. Practical fix: use fresh context per item; if batching is necessary, balance the history.

Conclusion: To mitigate AMEL in evaluation pipelines, reset context for each item or ensure histories are balanced when batching; otherwise biased judgments will persist across models and providers.

Abstract: Large language models are routinely used as automated evaluators: to review code, moderate content, or score outputs, often with many items passing through one conversation. We ask whether the polarity of prior conversation history biases subsequent judgments, an effect we call the accumulated message effect on LLM judgments (AMEL). Across 75,898 API calls to 11 models from 4 providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and four open-source models), we present identical test items in isolation or following histories saturated with predominantly positive or negative evaluations.
  Models shift toward the conversation's prevailing polarity (d = -0.17, p < 10^-46). The effect concentrates on items where the model is genuinely uncertain at baseline (d = -0.34 for high-entropy items, vs d = -0.15 when the baseline is deterministic). Bias does not grow with context length: 5 prior turns and 50 produce the same shift (Spearman |r| < 0.01; OLS slope p = 0.80). And there is a negativity asymmetry: paired per item, negative histories induce 1.62x more bias than positive (t = 13.46, p < 10^-39, n = 2,481). Scaling helps but does not solve it (Anthropic: Haiku -0.22 to Opus -0.17; OpenAI: Nano -0.34 to GPT-5.2 -0.17).
  Three follow-ups narrow the mechanism. The token probability distribution shifts continuously, not at a threshold. The negativity asymmetry has both token-level and semantic components, though attributing the balance is exploratory at our sample sizes. Position does not matter: five biased turns anywhere in a 50-turn history produce the same shift. The simplest fix for evaluation pipelines is a fresh context per item; when batching is unavoidable, balancing the history helps.

</details>


### [205] [Parametric Modular Answer Set Programs Made Declarative](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22716)
*Jorge Fandinno,Yuliya Lierler,Torsten Schaub*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces parametric modular logic programs to model modular first-order ASP, enabling subprograms with parameters and intensionality; shows semantics align with clingo's collective control and links modular/non-modular ASP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modularity in ASP supports structuring, reuse, and scalable reasoning. The work is motivated by the need to formalize subprograms with parameters and to capture clingo's collective control semantics.

Method: Define parametric modular logic programs, establish formal semantics, show how to encode clingo-programs with collective control, provide theoretical foundations, and connect to traditional non-modular ASP.

Result: A sound and expressive theory of modular ASP is presented; the formalism can capture the semantics of clingo's collective control and demonstrates the utility of modularization while bridging to standard ASP.

Conclusion: Modular ASP via parametric modular logic programs is viable and beneficial, providing a rigorous framework that unifies modular and non-modular ASP and supports structured program composition.

Abstract: In this paper, we explore the concept of modularity in first-order answer set programming (ASP). We introduce a new formalism called parametric modular logic programs, which allows defining subprograms with parameters and intensionality statements. We demonstrate how this formalism can capture the semantics of clingo-programs with collective control, a feature that enables structuring and instantiating subprograms. We provide theoretical foundations for modular ASP, illustrate its usefulness, and connect to traditional non-modular ASP.

</details>


### [206] [Can AI Make Conflicts Worse? An Alignment Failure in LLM Deployment Across Conflict Contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22720)
*Andrii Kryshtal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Nine-model evaluation in conflict contexts reveals significant misalignment risks; 6-47% failure; first domain-specific evaluation framework suggested.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prevent AI outputs from worsening armed conflict by evaluating misalignment in journalism/humanitarian contexts; fill gap in alignment practice.

Method: Test nine model configurations from four providers on 90 multi-turn scenarios designed to surface misalignment: atrocities, genocide denial, ethnic slurs, among others.

Result: Failure rates range from 6% to 47% across configurations; five of nine configurations failed 80-100% of the time under balancing prompts; highlights model-choice safety implications.

Conclusion: Introduces the first evaluation framework for this domain and proposes integrating it into alignment evaluation portfolios.

Abstract: AI models are already deployed in societies affected by armed conflict, and journalists, humanitarian workers, governments and ordinary citizens rely on them for information or for their work processes. No established practice exists for checking whether their outputs can make those conflicts worse. We tested nine model configurations from four providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepSeek, xAI) on 90 multi-turn scenarios designed to surface misaligned behaviour in conflict contexts: false equivalence between documented atrocities, denial of genocide, and failure to recognise ethnic slurs, among others. When such outputs feed into journalism, humanitarian reporting, or public debate, they can deepen divisions in fragile societies. Failure rates span 6\% to 47\% between the best and worst performing models, which makes model choice a safety question in its own right and when users pushed for ``balance'' in cases where international courts have already assigned responsibility, five of nine configurations failed 80 to 100 percent of the time. We release the first evaluation framework for this domain and propose adding it to alignment evaluation portfolios.

</details>


### [207] [Beyond Acoustic Emotion Recognition: Multimodal Pathos Analysis in Political Speech Using LLM-Based and Acoustic Emotion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22732)
*Juergen Dietrich*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-based multimodal analysis better captures semantically defined political emotion (Pathos) than acoustic SER in a parliamentary speech; Valence correlates with TRUST-Pathos, while acoustic Valence does not; EMO-DB benchmarks reveal biases; Arousal can be partially retrieved from acoustic signals; future work includes video-based analysis (facial expression, gaze).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate whether acoustic emotion recognition can proxy political emotion (Pathos) in the TRUST LLM pipeline and compare with multimodal LLM analysis to identify strengths, limitations, and biases.

Method: Case study on Felix Banaszak's Bundestag speech (51 segments, 245 s). Compare three modalities: (1) emotion2vec_plus_large SER with Arousal/Valence mapped via Russell circumplex; (2) Gemini 2.5 Flash LLM analyzing audio+transcript; (3) TRUST-Pathos scores from a three-advocate LLM supervisor ensemble. Compute Spearman correlations between Valence/Arousal estimates and TRUST-Pathos; perform quality evaluation of EMO-DB using Gemini in open-ended annotation.

Result: Gemini Valence shows a strong positive correlation with TRUST-Pathos (rho = +0.664, p < 0.001); emotion2vec Valence shows no meaningful correlation (rho = +0.097, p = 0.499). EMO-DB quality assessment with Gemini in open-ended annotation reveals biases in standard SER benchmarks (acted speech, cultural bias, category incompatibility). Overall, LLM-based multimodal analysis captures semantically defined political emotion more effectively than acoustic models for Valence; acoustic features remain informative for low-level Arousal.

Conclusion: LLM-based multimodal approaches better capture semantically defined political emotion in speech than acoustic-only models, though acoustic cues retain value for Arousal estimation. Future work should extend to video-based analysis incorporating facial expressions and gaze to enrich Pathos assessment.

Abstract: We investigate whether acoustic emotion recognition models can serve as proxies for the Pathos dimension in political speech analysis, as operationalised by the TRUST multi-agent large language model (LLM) pipeline. Using a Bundestag plenary speech by Felix Banaszak (51 segments, 245 s) as a case study, we compare three analysis modalities: (1) emotion2vec_plus_large, an acoustic speech emotion recognition (SER) model whose continuous Arousal and Valence values are derived via post-hoc Russell Circumplex projection; (2) Gemini 2.5 Flash, an LLM analysing the full speech audio together with its transcript in an open-ended, context-aware fashion; and (3) TRUST-Pathos scores from a three-advocate LLM supervisor ensemble. Spearman rank correlations reveal that Gemini Valence correlates strongly with TRUST-Pathos (rho = +0.664, p < 0.001), whereas emotion2vec Valence does not (rho = +0.097, p = 0.499). We further demonstrate, via a systematic quality evaluation of the Berlin Database of Emotional Speech (EMO-DB) using Gemini in an open-ended annotation paradigm, that standard SER benchmark corpora suffer from acted speech, cultural bias, and category incompatibility. Our results suggest that LLM-based multimodal analysis captures semantically defined political emotion substantially better than acoustic models alone, while acoustic features remain informative for low-level Arousal estimation. Future work will extend this approach to video-based analysis incorporating facial expression and gaze.

</details>


### [208] [Towards a General Intelligence and Interface for Wearable Health Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22759)
*Girish Narayanswamy,Maxwell A. Xu,A. Ali Heydari,Samy Abdel-Ghaffar,Marius Guerard,Kara Vaillancourt,Zhihan Zhang,Jake Garrison,Levi Albuquerque,Dimitris Spathis,Hong Yu,Hamid Palangi,Xuhai "Orson" Xu,David G. T. Barrett,Joseph Breda,Jed McGiffin,Yubin Kim,Yuwei Zhang,Naghmeh Rezaei,Samuel Solomon,Karan Ahuja,Tim Althoff,Jake Sunshine,Ming-Zher Poh,Benjamin Yetton,Ari Winbush,Nicholas B. Allen,James M. Rehg,Isaac Galatzer-Levy,Yun Liu,John Hernandez,Anupam Pathak,Conor Heneghan,Yuzhe Yang,Ahmed A. Metwally,Pushmeet Kohli,Mark Malhotra,Shwetak Patel,Xin Liu,Daniel McDuff*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A population-scale wearable health foundation model pretrained on over 1e12 minutes of unlabeled sensor data from 5 million participants improves 35 health tasks, enables few-shot and generative capabilities, uses an LLM-based search over predictive heads, and is integrated into a clinician-validated Personal Health Agent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome heterogeneity in phenotypes and scarcity of labeled wearable-health data by learning a large, generalized representation from unlabeled sensor signals that can support diverse downstream health predictions.

Method: Pretrain a foundation model on more than one trillion minutes of unlabeled sensor data from five million participants; evaluate on 35 health-prediction tasks across cardiovascular, metabolic, sleep, mental health, lifestyle, and demographic domains; deploy a classroom of LLM agents to explore downstream heads built on the embeddings; integrate predictors into a Personal Health Agent validated by clinicians.

Result: Model capacity and data scale yield systematic performance gains across tasks; the approach enables label-efficient few-shot learning and robust daily metric estimation; LLM-guided discovery of downstream heads improves performance with larger LLMs; clinician ratings (n=1,860) support relevance and safety.

Conclusion: Population-scale wearable representations enable richer, context-aware health insights, improving robustness and safety of automated health predictions and supporting clinically validated deployment.

Abstract: While ubiquitous wearable sensors capture a wealth of behavioral and physiological information, effectively transforming these signals into personalized health insights is challenging. Specifically, converting low-level sensor data into representations capable of characterizing higher-level states is difficult due to high phenotypic diversity and variation in individual baseline health, physiology, and lifestyle factors. Moreover, collecting wearable data paired with health outcome annotations is laborious and expensive, and retrospective annotation remains practically unfeasible, contributing to a scarcity of data with high-quality labels. To overcome these limitations, we propose a foundation model for wearable health that is pretrained on more than one trillion minutes of unlabeled sensor signals drawn from a large cohort of five million participants. We demonstrate that the joint scaling of model capacity and pretraining data volume leads to systematic improvements in performance, as evaluated on a diverse set of 35 health prediction tasks, spanning cardiovascular, metabolic, sleep, and mental health, as well as lifestyle choices and demographic factors. We find that this population scale representation unlocks label-efficient few-shot learning and generative capabilities for robust daily metric estimation. To further leverage this learned representation, we deploy a classroom of LLM agents to autonomously search the space of downstream predictive heads built on the model embeddings, showing broad performance improvements that increase with LLM model capacity. Finally, we show how integrating these downstream predictors into a Personal Health Agent can support model responses that are more relevant, contextually aware, and safe, and we validate this via 1,860 ratings from a cohort of clinicians.

</details>


### [209] [Advancing Mathematics Research with AI-Driven Formal Proof Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22763)
*George Tsoukalas,Anton Kovsharov,Sergey Shirobokov,Anja Surina,Moritz Firsching,Gergely Bérczi,Francisco J. R. Ruiz,Arun Suggala,Adam Zsolt Wagner,Eric Wieser,Lei Yu,Aja Huang,Miklós Z. Horváth,Andrew Ferrauiolo,Henryk Michalewski,Codrut Grosu,Thomas Hubert,Matej Balog,Pushmeet Kohli,Swarat Chaudhuri*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Autonomous LLM-based formal proof search solves a notable subset of open math problems and conjectures, with efficiency depending on agent design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address LLM unreliability in mathematical reasoning by combining natural-language generation with formal verification, enabling scalable AI-assisted proof search for mathematical research.

Method: Large-scale evaluation of LLM-driven proof generation with Lean verification. Compared an autonomous, capable agent to a basic agent that alternates LLM generation with Lean verification. Assessed performance on open Erdős problems (353) and OEIS conjectures (492) across domains including combinatorics, optimization, graph theory, algebraic geometry, and quantum optics.

Result: Autonomous agent resolved 9 of 353 Erdős problems; proved 44 of 492 OEIS conjectures; cost per problem was a few hundred dollars. A basic agent replicates Erdős successes but is costlier on harder problems. Demonstrated deployment potential in multiple research areas and informed guidance on effective agent designs.

Conclusion: AI-aided formal proof search is a viable strategy for tackling open mathematical problems; agent design choices substantially influence cost-efficiency and success on difficult problems, with potential for broader deployment in mathematics and related fields.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly excel at mathematical reasoning, but their unreliability limits their utility in mathematics research. A mitigation is using LLMs to generate formal proofs in languages like Lean. We perform the first large-scale evaluation of this method's ability to solve open problems. Our most capable agent autonomously resolved 9 of 353 open Erdős problems at the per-problem cost of a few hundred dollars, proved 44/492 OEIS conjectures, and is being deployed in combinatorics, optimization, graph theory, algebraic geometry, and quantum optics research. A basic agent alternating LLM-based generation with Lean-based verification replicated the Erdős successes but proved costlier on the hardest problems. These findings demonstrate the power of AI-aided formal proof search and shed light on the agent designs that enable it.

</details>


### [210] [LCGuard: Latent Communication Guard for Safe KV Sharing in Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22786)
*Sadia Asif,Mohammad Mohammadi Amiri,Momin Abbas,Prasanna Sattigeri,Karthikeyan Natesan Ramamurthy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LCGuard provides a privacy-preserving framework for latent KV-based communication in multi-agent LLM systems by learning representation-level transformations to prevent reconstruction of sensitive inputs while maintaining task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV caches used for latent communication can encode sensitive agent inputs and states, enabling leakage across agents; a mechanism is needed to limit information leakage without degrading task efficiency.

Method: Adversarial training: a decoder (adversary) attempts to reconstruct agent-specific sensitive inputs from shared KV artifacts; LCGuard learns transformations on the representations prior to sharing to reduce reconstructability while preserving task-relevant semantics. Leakage is formalized as a reconstruction problem and the system is trained jointly with an adversary.

Result: Empirical evaluations across multiple model families and multi-agent benchmarks show reduced reconstruction-based leakage and lower attack success rates, with task performance comparable to or near standard KV-sharing baselines.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a practical approach to safe latent communication in LLM-based multi-agent systems; LCGuard can be extended to other latent channels and supports privacy-preserving coordination without major performance loss.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems increasingly rely on intermediate communication to coordinate complex tasks. While most existing systems communicate through natural language, recent work shows that latent communication, particularly through transformer key-value (KV) caches, can improve efficiency and preserve richer task-relevant information. However, KV caches also encode contextual inputs, intermediate reasoning states, and agent-specific information, creating an opaque channel through which sensitive content may propagate across agents without explicit textual disclosure. To address this, we introduce \textbf{LCGuard} (Latent Communication Guard), a framework for safe KV-based latent communication in multi-agent LLM systems. LCGuard treats shared KV caches as latent working memory and learns representation-level transformations before cache artifacts are transmitted across agents. We formalize representation-level sensitive information leakage operationally through reconstruction: a shared cache artifact is unsafe if an adversarial decoder can recover agent-specific sensitive inputs from it. This leads to an adversarial training formulation in which the adversary learns to reconstruct sensitive inputs, while LCGuard learns transformations that preserve task-relevant semantics and reduce reconstructable information. Empirical evaluations across multiple model families and multi-agent benchmarks show that LCGuard consistently reduces reconstruction-based leakage and attack success rates while maintaining competitive task performance compared to standard KV-sharing baselines.

</details>


### [211] [Gated DeltaNet-2: Decoupling Erase and Write in Linear Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22791)
*Ali Hatamizadeh,Yejin Choi,Jan Kautz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Gated DeltaNet-2 introduces channel-wise erase and write gates to decouple forgetting and content writing in linear attention, improving long-context memory editing and achieving strong results across NLP tasks; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Linear attention replaces softmax cache with fixed-size memory, but editing compressed memory is constrained by a single gate; need separate erase/write controls per channel to preserve associations and improve retrieval.

Method: Proposes Gated Delta Rule-2 with two channel-wise gates b_t and w_t; fast-weight update perspective; chunkwise WY algorithm with asymmetric erase; gate-aware backward pass; channel-wise decay absorbed into erase factors; efficient parallel training.

Result: Outperforms Mamba-2, Gated DeltaNet, KDA, and Mamba-3 variants on language modeling, commonsense reasoning, and retrieval; 1.3B parameters trained on 100B FineWeb-Edu tokens; notable gains on long-context RULER needle-in-a-haystack benchmarks; effective in recurrent and hybrid settings; code at NVlabs.

Conclusion: Gated DeltaNet-2 generalizes prior approaches by separating forgetting and writing; enables efficient training and better memory editing in linear attention; establishes new state-of-the-art among the compared variants; code available.

Abstract: Linear attention replaces the unbounded cache of softmax attention with a fixed-size recurrent state, reducing sequence mixing to linear time and decoding to constant memory. The hard part is not just what to forget, but how to edit this compressed memory without scrambling existing associations. Delta-rule models subtract the current read before writing a new value, and Kimi Delta Attention (KDA) sharpens forgetting with channel-wise decay. But the active edit still uses a single scalar gate to control two different things: how much old content to erase on the key side and how much new content to commit on the value side. We introduce Gated DeltaNet-2, which generalizes both Gated DeltaNet and KDA by inheriting adaptive forgetting and channel-wise decay while addressing their shared limitation, the scalar tie between erasing and writing. Gated Delta Rule-2 separates these roles with a channel-wise erase gate b_t and a channel-wise write gate w_t, reducing to KDA when both gates collapse to the same scalar and to Gated DeltaNet when the decay also collapses. We derive a fast-weight update view, a chunkwise WY algorithm with channel-wise decay absorbed into asymmetric erase factors, and a gate-aware backward pass that preserves efficient parallel training. At 1.3B parameters trained on 100B FineWeb-Edu tokens, Gated DeltaNet-2 achieves the strongest overall results among Mamba-2, Gated DeltaNet, KDA, and Mamba-3 variants across language modeling, commonsense reasoning, and retrieval. Its advantage is most pronounced on long-context RULER needle-in-a-haystack benchmarks, where it improves the evaluated multi-key retrieval setting and remains strong in both recurrent and hybrid settings. Code is available at https://github.com/NVlabs/GatedDeltaNet-2.

</details>


### [212] [MOSS: Self-Evolution through Source-Level Rewriting in Autonomous Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22794)
*Qianshu Cai,Yonggang Zhang,Xianzhang Jia,Wei Xue,Jun Song,Xinmei Tian,Yike Guo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MOSS enables self-rewriting at the source level for production agentic systems, using a deterministic, multi-stage pipeline with external coding-agent edits, validated by replay in ephemeral trial workers and safe in-place updates; achieves notable performance gains (OpenClaw: 0.25 to 0.61) without human intervention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current self-evolving agents modify only text-mutable artifacts (skill files, prompts, memory schemas), leaving core code (routing, hook order, state invariants, dispatch) inaccessible and prone to structural failures. A Turing-complete, source-level adaptation medium can address these deeper failures, provide deterministic behavior, and resist long-context drift.

Method: Self-rewriting at the source level anchored to automatically curated production-failure evidence, executed via a deterministic multi-stage pipeline. Code edits are delegated to a pluggable external coding-agent CLI; MOSS retains stage ordering and verdicts. Candidate changes are verified by replaying the failure batch on ephemeral trial workers; promoted through in-place container swap gated by user consent and health probes, with rollback if probes fail.

Result: On OpenClaw, MOSS raises the four-task mean grader score from 0.25 to 0.61 in a single cycle without human intervention.

Conclusion: Source-level adaptation is a more general, robust medium for evolving agentic systems than text-only artifacts. MOSS demonstrates feasibility of autonomous, staged, verifiable self-rewriting at the code level, with potential for broader applicability, tempered by safety and reliability considerations.

Abstract: Autonomous agentic systems are largely static after deployment: they do not learn from user interactions, and recurring failures persist until the next human-driven update ships a fix. Self-evolving agents have emerged in response, but all confine evolution to text-mutable artifacts -- skill files, prompt configurations, memory schemas, workflow graphs -- and leave the agent harness untouched. Since routing, hook ordering, state invariants, and dispatch live in code rather than in any text artifact, an entire class of structural failure is physically unreachable from the text layer. We argue that source-level adaptation is a fundamentally more general medium: it is Turing-complete, a strict superset of every text-mutable scope, takes effect deterministically rather than through base-model compliance, and does not erode under long-context drift. We present MOSS, a system that performs self-rewriting at the source level on production agentic substrates. Each evolution is anchored to an automatically curated batch of production-failure evidence and proceeds through a deterministic multi-stage pipeline; code modification is delegated to a pluggable external coding-agent CLI while MOSS retains stage ordering and verdicts. Candidates are verified by replaying the batch against the candidate image in ephemeral trial workers, then promoted via user-consent-gated, in-place container swap with health-probe-gated rollback. On OpenClaw, MOSS lifts a four-task mean grader score from 0.25 to 0.61 in a single cycle without human intervention.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [213] [Flying Together: Human-Guided Immersive Shared Control for Aerial Robot Teams in Unknown Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21680)
*Lou De Bel-Air,Luca Morando,Ruitao Chen,Keru Wang,Benjamin Jarvis,Charbel Toumieh,Yang Zhou,Ken Perlin,Dario Floreano,Giuseppe Loianno*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A VR-based shared-control approach enables human-guided, multi-drone navigation in constrained/unknown environments using a motion-primitive planner integrated with an admittance controller, plus mixed-reality visualization and bilateral VR interfaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous multi-robot teams struggle to adapt to unforeseen conditions and to align with operator-driven objectives in unstructured environments; human-in-the-loop guidance could enhance safety, adaptability, and mission relevance.

Method: Introduce a novel motion-primitive-based planner that yields continuous, collision-free trajectories while integrating operator input in real time; couple it with an admittance controller; support mixed-reality with physical and simulated drones; provide a bilateral VR interface for operator-guided migration points and real-time team-state feedback.

Result: Experimentation indicates that shared control improves obstacle avoidance, preserves inter-agent spacing, and reduces operator effort, demonstrating feasibility and advantages of immersive, human-in-the-loop multi-robot navigation.

Conclusion: VR-based shared control is a feasible and advantageous paradigm for driven multi-robot navigation in constrained/unknown environments, enabling operator guidance to complement autonomous planning and improving safety and efficiency.

Abstract: While autonomous multi-robots can achieve safe and coordinated navigation, they often struggle to adapt to unforeseen conditions and to capture operator-driven objectives in unstructured environments. We present a Virtual Reality (VR)-based shared control framework for teams of drones operating in constrained and unknown environments, enabling real-time, user-guided exploration. At the core of our approach is a novel, user-guided motion-primitive-based planner that computes continuous, collision-free trajectories while continuously integrating operator input. This planner is coupled with an admittance controller, allowing the operator to flexibly influence team behavior and guide drones toward regions of interest that autonomous planners may overlook. The system supports mixed-reality operations with both physical and simulated drones, and implements a bilateral VR-based interface, allowing the operator to guide the robot team via migration points while receiving immediate visual feedback of the team state. Experimental results show that shared control improves obstacle avoidance, maintains inter-agent spacing, and reduces operator effort, demonstrating the feasibility and advantages of immersive, human-in-the-loop multi-robot navigation.

</details>


### [214] [Distributed Multi-Coverage for Robot Swarms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21686)
*Mariem Guitouni,Aaron T. Becker*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Distributed multicoverage algorithm for drone swarms using local sensing/communication to maintain redundancy without global coordination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Centralized multicoverage via integer programming is optimal but impractical for real-world swarms due to limited communication, onboard computation, and failures; need distributed, robust solutions.

Method: Develops a distributed algorithm that operates with local sensing and communication, ensuring multicoverage where each asset is observed by multiple robots; accounts for asset importance and resilience to partial failures; no global coordinator.

Result: The abstract asserts feasibility of distributed multicoverage; methods likely show correctness and robustness via analysis or simulations; concrete results not provided in abstract.

Conclusion: Proposes scalable, robust, decentralized coverage maintenance suitable for real deployments with limited communication and computation; avoids mission aborts under partial failures.

Abstract: Autonomous drone swarms deployed for surveillance, environmental monitoring, and infrastructure inspection must maintain reliable coverage of critical assets despite robot failures. This requires multicoverage: each asset must be observed by multiple robots for redundancy, with coverage requirements varying by asset importance. While recent work has solved the centralized problem optimally using integer programming, practical deployments face constraints that demand distributed solutions: robots operate with limited communication ranges, onboard computation restricts global planning, and partial system failures must not cause mission abort. We present a distributed multicoverage algorithm for robot swarms operating with local sensing, local communication, and no global coordination.

</details>


### [215] [Closed-Loop Sim-to-Real Reinforcement Learning for Deformable Microfiber Shape Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21688)
*Alessandro Amici,Houari Bettahar,Veeti Jaakkola,Quan Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A sim-to-real RL policy trained in a frictionless simulator can regulate microfiber shapes on a surface with real-time visual feedback, achieving sub-millimeter accuracy across multiple diameters/lengths without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Microscale surface and interfacial interactions complicate modelling and hinder conventional model-based control and sim-to-real learning; robust control under unmodeled friction is needed.

Method: Train a reinforcement learning policy in a simplified frictionless simulator for geometric shape regulation; deploy on a real dual-gripper micromanipulation system at 40 Hz; rely on real-time visual feedback to iteratively correct unmodeled surface interactions; no retraining or domain adaptation.

Result: Mean point-wise shape error of 270 ± 80 μm over 24 initial configurations; across 9 specimens with diameters 50, 80, 120 μm and lengths 10, 15, 20 mm, achieving sub-millimeter final shape error without retraining.

Conclusion: Policy learned in a simplified simulator can achieve repeatable real-world microfiber shape regulation under surface contact when the task-relevant sim-to-real mismatch effects are observable and correctable within the closed-loop feedback.

Abstract: Autonomous contact-based micromanipulation is challenging because surface and interfacial interactions at the microscale are difficult to model accurately, limiting the use of conventional model-based control and sim-to-real learning. We present a closed-loop sim-to-real reinforcement learning (RL) approach for microfiber shape control on a surface. The central idea is to train geometric shape regulation in a simplified frictionless simulator and rely on real-time visual feedback during deployment to iteratively correct the observed effects of unmodeled surface interactions. An RL policy trained entirely in simulation is transferred directly to a physical dual-gripper micromanipulation system operating at 40 Hz, without retraining or domain adaptation. Using silk microfibers as a testbed, the policy achieves a mean point-wise shape error of 270 $\pm$ 80 $μ$m across twenty-four diverse initial configurations. Across nine specimens covering all combinations of three fiber diameters (50, 80, and 120 $μ$m) and three manipulated lengths (10 mm, 15mm, and 20 mm), the same policy achieves sub-millimeter final shape error without any retraining or retuning. These results show that a policy learned in a simplified simulator can achieve repeatable real-world microfiber shape regulation under surface contact, provided that the task-relevant effects of the sim-to-real mismatch remain observable and correctable within the closed feedback loop.

</details>


### [216] [Motion Design for Grasp-Based Dynamic Locomotion in Microgravity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21704)
*Chaerim Moon,Joohyung Kim,Justin K. Yim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Parameterizable planning framework for grasp-based dynamic locomotion in microgravity with multi-limbed robots; simulation-based evaluation across two quadruped morphologies; findings show larger feasible contact wrench space and reduced impulsive dynamics improve performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable robust, dynamic mobility in microgravity where anchors are sparse, requiring coordinated 6D limb contacts and whole-body motion.

Method: Develop a parameterizable locomotion planning framework; explore gait patterns, stride length, speed, and nominal posture; test on two quadruped morphologies in physics-based simulation; assess stability and actuation demand; analyze contact wrench space and impulsive dynamics.

Result: Enlarged feasible contact wrench space and attenuated impulsive whole-body dynamics improve locomotion performance; results guide contact configuration and whole-body coordination strategies.

Conclusion: The framework informs design choices for contact configuration and coordination in microgravity multi-limb locomotion; simulation-supported insights; additional real-world validation and broader morphology studies recommended.

Abstract: Locomotion in microgravity often relies on sparsely and irregularly arranged anchors, motivating grasp-based mobility with multiple limbs. In this setting, dynamic locomotion is feasible only through deliberate regulation of both anchored interactions and whole-body coordination under coupled dynamic and kinematic constraints. This paper presents design insights for grasp-based dynamic locomotion with multi-limbed robotic systems in microgravity, targeting scenarios that require 6D limb manipulation to establish contacts with candidate anchors. The investigated design parameters include gait pattern, stride length, locomotion speed, and nominal posture. A parameterizable locomotion planning framework is proposed to support variations of these parameters and to evaluate the resulting locomotion performance in terms of stability and actuation demand. Two representative quadruped morphologies are adopted for evaluation in physics-based simulation. The results demonstrate that enlarging the feasible contact wrench space and attenuating impulsive whole-body dynamics improve locomotion performance. These findings inform strategies for contact configuration selection and whole-body coordination in microgravity locomotion with multi-limbed systems.

</details>


### [217] [PGDG: Physically Grounded Data Generation for Robust Bimanual Policy Learning from a Single Demonstration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21710)
*Cunxi Dai,Haoran Chang,Aditya Nisal,Rahul Kumar,Guofei Chen,Tao Chen,Yuzhe Qin,Guanya Shi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PGDG is a data-generation framework that expands a single demonstration into a diverse, recoverable dataset for contact-rich bimanual manipulation via zero-shot, physics-grounded curation and short-horizon control, improving sim-to-real transfer and enabling better foundation-model fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training robust policies for contact-rich, bimanual manipulation is hard because collecting diverse demonstrations is expensive and off-manifold disturbances lack recovery supervision; a zero-shot method to generate recoverable data is desirable.

Method: Iterate between a physics-grounded sampler and a dataset curator. The curator selects informative, non-redundant, recoverable behaviors to steer the sampling distribution toward under-covered recovery modes. The sampler draws physically plausible rollout candidates from the updated distribution and retains successful trajectories. Short-horizon sampling-based control relabels risky states with corrective actions. Evaluated on four bimanual tasks, with zero-shot real-world transfer.

Result: PGDG outperforms spatial-only augmentation in simulation and real-world transfer. RotateBox-Pitch: sim success 38%→93%; real 35%→82%. Additionally, zero-shot fine-tuning of a foundation model (GR00T) improves success from 46%→77%.

Conclusion: PGDG provides a practical, data-efficient approach to generate diverse, recoverable demonstrations, enabling stronger sim-to-real transfer and effective foundation-model fine-tuning for bimanual manipulation.

Abstract: Behavior cloning for contact-rich bimanual manipulation remains challenging because diverse demonstrations are expensive to collect, and even small disturbances can push the system into off-manifold states where no recovery supervision is available. We propose PGDG, a data generation framework with zero-shot curation that expands a single demonstration into a compact dataset of physically plausible, successful, and diverse recovery behaviors without additional human labeling. PGDG iterates between a physics-grounded sampler and a dataset curator, where the curator selects informative, non-redundant, and recoverable behaviors to update the sampling distribution toward under-covered recovery modes, and the sampler draws physically plausible rollout candidates from this updated distribution and retains successful trajectories. To further improve data quality, PGDG applies short-horizon sampling-based control to relabel selected risky states with corrective actions. Across four bimanual manipulation tasks, PGDG consistently outperforms spatial-only augmentation in both simulation and zero-shot real-world transfer. On RotateBox-Pitch, success improves from 38% to 93% in simulation and from 35% to 82% in the real world. PGDG also enables effective foundation models fine-tuning such as GR00T, increasing success from 46% to 77%. Additional results are available in our website: https://cunxid.github.io/PGDG/.

</details>


### [218] [Mind the Gaps: Multi-Robot Feedback-Driven Ergodic Coverage in Unknown Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21719)
*Thales Costa Silva,Nora Ayanian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Adaptive ergodic coverage for multi-robot exploration uses online-updated parametric environmental models to form a dynamic target distribution, guiding ergodic trajectory optimization under unknown priors; validated by simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ergodic search prescribes exploration proportional to information, but performance degrades when the environment’s prior is unknown or inaccurate. An adaptive, real-time updating approach can better allocate robots to high-information regions.

Method: Enhance ergodic trajectory optimization by constructing a target spatial information distribution from parametric environmental models updated online with real-time feedback. The framework assumes static or slowly changing environments and permits dynamic prioritization of regions; yields control policies for individual agents and improved resource use.

Result: Simulation experiments demonstrate improved coverage efficiency and resource allocation compared to non-adaptive ergodic strategies, with better targeting of high-information areas.

Conclusion: Adaptive ergodic coverage with online-updated models effectively handles unknown priors and improves multi-robot sampling efficiency in static/slowly varying environments; future work includes broader validation and robustness to faster environmental changes.

Abstract: In this work, we address the problem of multi-robot adaptive coverage, where teams of robots perform dynamic sampling by continuously adjusting their positions to collect data in an environment. This task can be challenging, particularly when robots must be efficiently allocated to new sampling locations over time. Ergodic search methods optimize robot trajectories by ensuring that the robots' time-averaged spatial distribution aligns with the spatial distribution of environmental information. While these methods promote effective exploration provided a target distribution, they often fail to account for unknown prior distributions of the environment. To overcome this limitation, we propose an adaptive coverage strategy that utilizes real-time feedback from an environmental model to adjust robot sampling behavior in response to unknown conditions. Our approach enhances traditional ergodic trajectory optimization by constructing a target spatial information distribution based on parametric models of the environment, which are updated online. This strategy assumes that the environment is either static or changes slowly compared to the robot's motion. Our framework allows robots to dynamically prioritize regions of high interest, improving coverage efficiency, synthesizing effective control policies for individual agents, and optimizing resource use in settings with unknown prior distributions. We validate our approach through simulations, demonstrating its effectiveness in enhancing coverage and resource allocation.

</details>


### [219] [Learning Altruistic Collaboration in Heterogeneous Multi-Team Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21723)
*Riwa Karam,Ruoyu Lin,Brooks A. Butler,Magnus Egerstedt*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Altruistic, Hamilton's-rule-guided allocation for heterogeneous multi-team robot collaboration; NP-hard combinatorial problem; uses a graph neural network with centralized training and decentralized execution to approximate allocations; validated in firefighting scenarios with near-optimal performance and scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle scalable, heterogeneous, dynamic resource sharing across multiple teams of robots, considering transfer costs and contribution heterogeneity, by leveraging ecological altruism principles.

Method: Formulate a combinatorial allocation problem incorporating Hamilton's rule (altruistic transfers based on relatedness, benefits, and costs). Develop a graph neural network policy that operates on the team interaction graph to predict robot-level transfers and next robot-to-team assignments. Use centralized training to enforce altruistic behavior and decentralized execution for real-time deployment.

Result: The learned policy achieves near-optimal performance in simulations and experiments and scales to larger systems.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that ecological altruism-inspired rules can guide scalable, multi-team robotic resource sharing; the GNN-based policy provides a practical approximation to a hard allocation problem with real-world applicability in dynamic, heterogeneous settings.

Abstract: This paper studies heterogeneous multi-team collaboration through dynamic robot allocation, where robots are treated as transferable resources. Leveraging Hamilton's rule from ecology as an altruistic decision-making mechanism, we propose a multi-team collaborative resource allocation framework with heterogeneous capabilities, transfer costs, and capability-dependent contributions. The resulting allocation problem is combinatorial and is shown to be NP-hard. To address scalability, we develop a graph neural network policy under centralized training and decentralized execution that approximates the altruistic allocations based on Hamilton's rule. The model operates over the team interaction graph and predicts robot-level transfer decisions and next robot-to-team assignments. The proposed approach is validated in a firefighting scenario through simulations and experiments, demonstrating that the learned policy achieves near-optimal performance while scaling to larger systems.

</details>


### [220] [Safe and Steerable Geometric Motion Policies for Robotic Dexterous Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21811)
*Albert Wu,Riccardo Bonalli,Thomas Lew,C. Karen Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SafePBDS provides a geometry‑consistent framework to compute optimal, certifiably safe configuration‑space accelerations for heterogeneous task manifolds; introduces pullback control barrier functions and a task‑manifold action interface for residual high‑level policies; validated on a 23‑DOF Panda‑Allegro setup with high grasp success and innovative palm‑down in‑hand reorientation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dexterous manipulation involves coordinating objectives on heterogeneous geometric spaces (e.g., R^7 configuration, SE(3) end-effector pose, obstacle margins in R^n). There is a need for a unifying, certifiably safe framework that preserves autonomous behavior while allowing high‑level policy inputs.

Method: SafePBDS combines task-manifold dynamical systems with a pullback control barrier function to convert task‑manifold safety into linear constraints on configuration‑space accelerations. It also introduces a task-manifold action interface enabling high‑level policies to inject low‑dimensional residual motions while preserving safety; autonomy remains the baseline when inputs are zero. Validation is performed in simulation and on a 23‑DOF Franka Panda‑Allegro Hand platform.

Result: On dexterous grasping, SafePBDS achieves 92.5% success across 20 household objects (120 trials). With the action interface, excluding one finger via a 1D action yields 94.4% 3‑finger grasp across 3 objects (36 trials). The method enables the first model‑based, fully actuated palm‑down in‑hand reorientation, exceeding 360° yaw rotation in both directions under varying object weight and wrist motion.

Conclusion: SafePBDS provides efficient, certifiably safe planning for dexterous manipulation in heterogeneous task spaces, while allowing high‑level residual control without sacrificing safety. It demonstrates the first palm‑down in‑hand reorientation in a model-based setting and shows strong empirical performance.

Abstract: Robotic dexterous manipulation requires continuously reconciling objectives and constraints defined on heterogeneous geometric spaces: a robot controlled on a $\mathbb{R}^7$ configuration manifold may need to track end effector poses on $\mathrm{SE}(3)$ while satisfying obstacle avoidance margins in $\mathbb{R}$. We present Safe Pullback Bundle Dynamical Systems (SafePBDS), a geometrically consistent framework that computes optimal, certifiably safe configuration manifold accelerations from objectives and safety requirements on arbitrary task manifolds. SafePBDS builds on prior work that combines predefined task manifold dynamical systems to produce autonomous motion. Its first innovation is a pullback control barrier function construction, which converts task manifold safety conditions into linear constraints on configuration manifold accelerations. The second innovation is a task manifold action interface that allows a high-level policy to inject low dimensional residual motions; zero input recovers the autonomous behavior, while safety is preserved under arbitrary inputs. This lets high-level policies efficiently steer exploration while leaving precise motion to the autonomous behavior. We validate SafePBDS in simulation and on a 23-DOF Franka Panda-Allegro Hand platform. On dexterous grasping, SafePBDS achieves a $92.5\%$ success rate across 20 household objects and 120 trials. Using the action interface, the method can exclude any one of the four fingers during grasping via a one-dimensional action, achieving $94.4\%$ 3-finger grasp success across 3 objects and 36 trials. The efficient planning and safety guarantee of SafePBDS also enables the first model-based, fully actuated palm-down in-hand reorientation, exceeding $360^\circ$ of yaw rotation in both directions under varying object weight and wrist motion. Demo video and details: https://tml.stanford.edu/safe-pbds

</details>


### [221] [Analytical and Experimental Force Analysis of a Soft Linear Pneumatic Actuator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21836)
*Mohammed Abboodi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A quasi-static analytical model plus experiments quantify how a linear soft sleeve actuator generates force, showing that output is governed by pressure, geometry, extension, loading, and axial stiffness; at 125 kPa the force falls from ~112 N at zero extension to ~0 at 40 mm, with static loading reducing force especially at low/intermediate pressures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address incomplete understanding of soft sleeve actuators' force-generation behavior, particularly dependence on extension, external load, and axial stiffness, to enable better design and control.

Method: Develop a quasi-static analytical model where net axial force equals pressure-generated force from cap and folded walls minus the axial-stiffness resistance; incorporate internal pressure, projected areas, folded-wall geometry, axial displacement, and an experimentally fitted axial stiffness relation. Validate with prescribed-extension and static-load experiments.

Result: Quantitative force response: at 125 kPa, force decreases from ~112 N at zero extension to nearly zero at 40 mm; static loading delays force generation and reduces force output, especially at low/intermediate pressures; overall force is governed by coupled effects of pressure, geometry, displacement, loading, and axial stiffness.

Conclusion: LSSA force generation is governed by coupled effects of pressure, geometry, displacement, loading, and axial stiffness; the analytical model captures these interactions and, together with experiments, provides insight for design and control of soft sleeve actuators.

Abstract: Soft sleeve actuators (SSAs) have recently been developed as a pneumatic actuation approach for wearable and assistive robotic systems. By integrating the actuation structure into a sleeve-like geometry, these actuators can reduce reliance on external attachment layers and transmission mechanisms while maintaining compliance with limb-shaped surfaces. However, the force-generation behavior of SSAs remains insufficiently explained, particularly with respect to the variation of output force during extension, the influence of external loading, and the mechanical role of axial stiffness. This paper presents an analytical and experimental force analysis of a linear soft sleeve actuator (LSSA). A quasi-static analytical model was developed by expressing the net axial force as the pressure-generated contribution from the cap and folded walls, reduced by the force associated with axial stiffness. The model incorporates internal pressure, projected pressure areas, folded wall geometry, axial displacement, and an experimentally fitted axial stiffness relation. Prescribed-extension and static-load experiments were conducted to evaluate the actuator response. At 125 kPa, the generated force decreased from approximately 112 N at zero extension to nearly zero at 40 mm. Static loading delayed measurable force generation and reduced force output, particularly at low and intermediate pressures. The results show that LSSA force generation is governed by coupled effects of pressure, geometry, displacement, loading, and axial stiffness.

</details>


### [222] [EvoScene-VLA: Evolving Scene Beliefs Inside the Action Decoder for Chunked Robot Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21862)
*Chushan Zhang,Ruihan Lu,Jinguang Tong,Xuesong Li,Yikai Wang,Hongdong Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: EvoScene-VLA adds a persistent, action-updated scene state across control chunks in chunked VLA policies via a recurrent scene prefix; the VLM uses current observation and prior scene state to predict next action and a scene update, with training-time Scene Predictor and Geometric Anchor, discarded at deployment; yields consistent performance gains on RoboTwin and strong real-robot results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional chunked VLA policies rely on independent per-chunk updates and cannot maintain a scene prior that accounts for action-driven scene changes (contact, occlusion, motion). A persistent, action-updated scene state could improve geometry consistency and decision quality across updates.

Method: Introduce EvoScene-VLA: a recurrent scene prefix that carries geometry-aware state across chunks. At each VLM call, fuse the current observation with the action-updated prior from the previous chunk; the action decoder outputs the next action chunk and a compact scene update, which becomes the next prior. Training uses Scene Predictor to provide future scene-token targets and Geometric Anchor to align scene slots with frozen depth and 3D teachers; deployable model discards these modules.

Result: On 31 RoboTwin tasks, average success increases from 87.2% to 89.1% in fixed evaluation and from 86.1% to 88.5% in randomized evaluation. On the Galaxea R1-Lite real robot, EvoScene-VLA outperforms all baselines.

Conclusion: Maintaining a persistent, action-updated scene state across control calls improves VLA policy performance, validated by simulation benchmarks and real-robot experiments; training-time aids enhance scene understanding while enabling lightweight deployment by discarding auxiliary modules.

Abstract: Chunked vision-language-action (VLA) policies predict multi-step robot controls, conditioning each update on the current visual observation alone. Yet robot actions cause contact, occlusion, and object motion, and the geometry that later decisions depend on can change before the next visual update arrives. Spatial VLAs improve current-frame geometry. Temporal VLAs aggregate past frames. Neither maintains an action-updated scene prior across chunks. We argue for a persistent action-updated scene state across control calls, and introduce EvoScene-VLA. Its recurrent scene prefix carries a geometry-aware scene state across chunks. At each vision-language model (VLM) call, the VLM combines scene information from the current observation with the action-updated prior from the previous chunk; the action decoder outputs both the next action chunk and a compact scene update. This update becomes the next prior, which the VLM corrects against the new observation when the next call arrives. Each control call therefore starts from a scene prior that reflects both recent actions and fresh visual evidence. During training, \textbf{Scene Predictor} supplies future scene-token targets, and Geometric Anchor aligns scene slots with frozen depth and 3D teachers. We discard both modules at deployment. On 31 RoboTwin tasks, EvoScene-VLA raises average success from 87.2% to 89.1% in fixed evaluation and from 86.1% to 88.5% in randomized evaluation. On the Galaxea R1-Lite real robot, EvoScene-VLA outperforms all baselines.

</details>


### [223] [Higher Order Reasoning for Collaborative Communicationless Mobile Robot Operations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21901)
*Jonathan Reasoner,Nicola Bezzo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Dynamic epistemic planning enables implicit, long-horizon coordination in communication-constrained multi-robot systems using higher-order belief particles, Bayesian updates, behavior trees, and MPPI for robust interception and trajectory adaptation; empirically better than first-order baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address coordination under limited communication and partial observability by enabling higher-order reasoning about teammates, enabling implicit coordination without constant information exchange.

Method: Introduce a dynamic epistemic planning framework with higher-order belief particles; update beliefs via Bayesian inference; select actions with a behavior tree that anticipates teammates' decisions; integrate a temporally aware MPPI controller for low-level execution and trajectory planning.

Result: Simulation and physical experiments show consistent reduction in task completion time compared to a first-order baseline, demonstrating epistemic logic as a robust coordination foundation in communication-restricted domains.

Conclusion: Epistemic logic offers a robust foundation for resilient, long-horizon coordination in multi-robot systems operating without constant communication.

Abstract: In communicationless environments, multi-robot systems must operate without the constant information exchange that many coordination strategies typically assume. This paper presents a novel dynamic epistemic planning framework that enables implicit coordination and long horizon planning through higher-order reasoning among robots. With our approach, robots form and propagate higher-order belief particles, update world beliefs using Bayesian inference, and select actions via a behavior tree that anticipates teammates' likely decisions. A temporally aware Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) controller integrates this reasoning into low-level execution, allowing robots to plan intercepts and adapt trajectories under partial observability. The proposed framework is evaluated in both simulations and physical experiments, where it consistently reduces task completion time compared to a first-order baseline, demonstrating that epistemic logic can serve as a robust foundation for resilient coordination in communication-restricted domains.

</details>


### [224] [Non-Contact Vibration-Based Damage Detection of Civil Structures Using a Cost-Effective Autonomous UAV](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21914)
*Javier Becerril,Maximiliano Vargas,Jennifer Herrera,Joanna Gutierrez,Jorge Rios,Mohsen Amjadian,Constantine Tarawneh,Jinghao Yang,Qi Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Non-contact, low-cost UAV-based SHM using vision-based displacement from video to detect damage via natural frequency shifts; validated on a lab-scale frame with consumer hardware; comparable to contact sensing at lower cost with some UAV-induced error (~5.7%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for scalable, cost-effective SHM that avoids contact sensors, enabling operation in GPS-denied environments and potentially deploying multiple cooperative UAVs for enhanced coverage.

Method: Capture video of a frame structure under healthy and simulated-damage states; extract displacement time histories through vision-based motion tracking using a low-cost UAV with onboard camera and autonomous alignment; perform frequency-domain analysis of displacement and compare to reference accelerometer data and a finite element model.

Result: Fundamental frequency and its damage-induced shift are detectable by all platforms; UAV shows up to 5.7% error due to disturbances and sensing limits but reliably indicates damage; performance is competitive with commercial UAVs at a fraction of the cost; supports deployment of multiple cooperative UAVs for improved inspection.

Conclusion: Low-cost autonomous UAVs provide a practical, flexible, and scalable option for structural health monitoring, particularly where contact methods are impractical, with potential for enhanced coverage via multi-UAV collaborations.

Abstract: This paper presents a non-contact approach for vibration-based structural damage detection using an autonomous and customized cost-effective unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV). Vibration signals are extracted from video recordings through vision-based motion tracking to identify shifts in natural frequencies indicative of structural degradation. A laboratory-scale frame structure is evaluated under healthy and simulated-damage conditions. The proposed system is validated through an experimental study involving two smartphones, a USB camera, and a custom-built low-cost UAV equipped with an onboard camera and an autonomous alignment system for operation in GPS-denied environments. The displacement time is extracted and analyzed in the frequency domain and compared to reference measurements from contact accelerometers and a finite element model. Experimental results show that all platforms successfully capture the fundamental frequency and its shift due to damage. Although the UAV exhibits slightly higher errors (up to 5.7%) due to platform-induced disturbances and sensing limitations, it reliably detects damage-induced frequency changes. Compared to commercial UAV systems, the proposed platform achieves comparable inspection performance at significantly lower cost. These results demonstrate that low-cost autonomous UAVs provide a practical, flexible, and scalable solution for structural health monitoring, particularly in scenarios where contact-based sensing is impractical. The findings also support the potential for the deployment of multiple cooperative UAVs to further enhance inspection coverage and robustness.

</details>


### [225] [Auction-Consensus Algorithm with Learned Bidding Scheme for Multi-Robot Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21932)
*Jose Rodriguez,Constantine Tarawneh,Sven Koenig,Wenjie Dong,Qi Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Learning-enhanced auction-consensus for multi-robot task allocation (MRTA) uses neural bidding policies trained with reinforcement learning to replace CBBA's greedy bidding. Centralized training with decentralized execution yields higher-quality allocations while preserving coordination structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CBBA provides scalable decentralized MRTA with convergence guarantees but relies on hand-crafted scoring, which often yields suboptimal allocations. There is a need for improved, scalable methods that preserve decentralization and performance.

Method: Replace CBBA's deterministic bidding with a neural bidding policy trained via Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) under centralized training and decentralized execution. Agents bid from partial local observations, retaining auction and consensus phases. Evaluate multiple architectures (Neural Additive Model, LSTM, Set Transformer) using rewards shaped toward global optima obtained by MILP.

Result: Learned bidding policies improve solution quality over classical CBBA across varying swarm sizes, while maintaining decentralized execution. Neural architectures offer varying performance, with PPO-trained policies approaching or surpassing MILP-informed baselines and demonstrating robustness to observation limits.

Conclusion: Integrating reinforcement learning with classical distributed coordination (CBBA) is a viable, scalable path to higher-quality decentralized MRTA, enabling agents to learn bidding strategies from local observations while preserving the established auction-consensus framework.

Abstract: Multi-Robot Task Allocation (MRTA) is a central challenge in decentralized multi-agent systems, where teams of robots must cooperatively assign and execute tasks under limited communication while optimizing global performance objectives. Auction-consensus algorithms, such as the Consensus-Based Bundle Algorithm (CBBA), provide scalable decentralized coordination with provable convergence, but rely on hand-crafted greedy scoring functions that often lead to suboptimal task allocations. This paper proposes a learning-enhanced auction-consensus framework in which CBBA's deterministic bidding mechanism is replaced by a neural bidding policy trained using reinforcement learning. Under a centralized training and decentralized execution paradigm, agents learn to compute task bids from partial local observations while retaining the standard auction and consensus phases for decentralized coordination. The learned bidding policy is trained using Proximal Policy Optimization with rewards shaped by proximity to globally optimal solutions obtained via mixed-integer linear programming. Multiple neural architectures are evaluated, including a Neural Additive Model, the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model, and the Set Transformer Model. Experimental results across varying swarm sizes demonstrate that learned bidding policies can improve solution quality over classical CBBA while preserving decentralized execution. The proposed approach highlights the effectiveness of integrating reinforcement learning with classical distributed coordination algorithms, offering a scalable pathway toward higher-quality decentralized multi-robot task allocation.

</details>


### [226] [Learning to Evolve: Multi-modal Interactive Fields for Robust Humanoid Navigation in Dynamic Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21935)
*Peifeng Jiang,Hong Liu,Jin Jin,Wenshuai Wang,Xia Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MIF is a multi-modal field-based framework for robust scene memory and safe manipulation in dynamic humanoid robotics, reducing gait-induced perception errors and updating only inconsistent regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safe manipulation requires reliable scene memory under locomotion-induced perceptual distortion, environmental changes, and interaction-level safety constraints. Existing semantic mapping and scene-graph systems struggle in dynamic, non-static environments with unstable camera trajectories.

Method: Introduce the Multi-modal Interactive Field (MIF) integrating three fields: (1) an uncertainty-aware 3DGS Appearance Field to suppress gait-induced blur, (2) a Spatial Field for topological memory, and (3) a Geometry Field for Interaction Pose Safety (IPS). A discrepancy-detection score separates locomotion-induced false positives from persistent changes, updating only locally inconsistent regions within a closed-loop perception-adaptation pipeline. The system combines these within a dynamic memory-update framework and employs feature distillation to reduce semantic memory footprint.

Result: On a Unitree-G1 humanoid in a dynamic office, relocation success in non-static environments improved from 12% to 94% compared with static scene-graph memory, while semantic memory footprint was reduced by 91.4% through feature distillation for online operation.

Conclusion: MIF enables reliable, memory-based navigation for safe manipulation in dynamic humanoid settings by coupling uncertainty-aware appearance, spatial, and geometry fields with discrepancy-based local updates, achieving robust perception and practical online deployment.

Abstract: Safe manipulation-oriented navigation for humanoid robots requires scene memory that remains reliable under locomotion-induced perceptual distortion, environmental changes, and interaction-level geometric safety constraints. Existing semantic mapping and scene-graph systems are difficult to deploy directly in this setting because they often assume stable camera trajectories, static environments, or coarse object geometry. We introduce the Multi-modal Interactive Field (MIF), a humanoid-oriented system that integrates confidence-aware semantic 3D Gaussian Splatting, discrepancy-triggered spatial memory updates, and task-driven geometric reconstruction within a closed-loop perception-adaptation pipeline. MIF couples three fields: an uncertainty-aware 3DGS Appearance Field that suppresses gait-induced blur, a Spatial Field that maintains topological memory, and a Geometry Field that supports Interaction Pose Safety (IPS) before manipulation. A discrepancy detection score is introduced to separate locomotion-induced false-positive changes from persistent changes and updates only locally inconsistent regions. On a Unitree-G1 humanoid in a real dynamic office, MIF improves relocation success in non-static environments from 12% to 94% compared with static scene-graph memory, while reducing semantic memory footprint by 91.4% through feature distillation for practical online operation. Project page and code: https://ziya-jiang.github.io/MIF-homepage/

</details>


### [227] [A Visitation Grid for Complete Coverage Foraging in Robot Swarms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21947)
*Qi Arturo Gonzalez,Yifeng Gao,Li Zhang,Qi Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Grid-based stochastic foraging in swarm robotics reduces late-stage collection time and boosts end-stage efficiency versus CPFA, with scalable memory-light server coordination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-stage inefficiency in swarm foraging: most prior work concentrates on early-phase resource collection; need methods that rapidly finish collecting all resources in unknown environments.

Method: An unknown area is partitioned into a grid map managed by a lightweight central server. Robots and server operate under limited memory/computation. Server updates grid visitation counts from robot locations to estimate exploration density. For each new foraging trip, a robot probabilistically selects its next search area from the 3x3 neighborhood with the lowest visitation counts, biasing exploration toward under-visited regions while preserving stochasticity.

Result: Simulation experiments show the proposed strategy consistently outperforms CPFA, achieving up to 33% reduction in total collection time and over 48% improvement in end-stage collection efficiency. The approach is robust, flexible, and scalable for near-complete/complete resource collection under limited onboard resources.

Conclusion: The grid-based stochastic foraging strategy offers a general enhancement for stochastic swarm foraging methods, improving end-stage efficiency and overall resource completion without requiring extensive onboard resources or centralized control.

Abstract: The complete collection of sparse resources in large, unknown environments remains a challenging problem for autonomous robot swarms. Previous studies have shown that a substantial portion of total mission time is consumed during the final stage of collection, where only a small fraction of randomly scattered resources remain. Consequently, many existing swarm foraging algorithms (search and collection) focus on collecting most resources within a limited time window, rather than improving end-stage efficiency for collecting all resources. We propose a grid-based stochastic foraging strategy that explicitly reduces redundant visits and accelerates late-stage collection. The unknown search area is partitioned into a grid map, which is maintained by a lightweight central server. To maintain scalability, both robots and the server operate within limited memory and computational constraints. The server updates the grid-level visitation counts based on robot-reported locations, producing a global estimate of the exploration density. For each new foraging trip, a robot selects its next search area from a local 3 X 3 neighborhood of grids probabilistically with the lowest visitation count, thus biasing exploration toward under-visited regions while maintaining stochasticity. Extensive simulation experiments demonstrate that the proposed strategy consistently outperforms the canonical centrally placed baseline foraging algorithm (CPFA). Compared to CPFA, the proposed method reduces the total collection time by up to 33% and improves collection efficiency by more than 48% during the final stage of the mission. These results indicate that the proposed strategy is robust, flexible, and scalable for near-complete and complete resource collection in robot swarms and can serve as a general enhancement for stochastic swarm foraging methods under limited onboard resources.

</details>


### [228] [TacO: Benchmarking Tactile Sensors for Object Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21976)
*Anya Zorin,Zilin Si,Myungsun Park,Junsung Park,Alexiy Buynitsky,Sachin Bhadang,Taejun Park,Sohee John Yoon,Yong-Lae Park,Oliver Kroemer,Zeynep Temel,Michael T. Tolley,Sha Yi,Xiaolong Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Systematic, task-driven evaluation of tactile sensors across four modalities (visual, acoustic, magnetic, resistive) for three manipulation tasks; finds that sensor usefulness depends on modality, material properties, and task; proposes a framework to select/evaluate sensors by manipulation policy performance; data/code/hardware to be released publicly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of empirical guidance on which tactile sensors are best for specific manipulation tasks and to provide a framework for sensor selection based on task demands.

Method: Train separate manipulation policies for four tactile sensor modalities (visual, acoustic, magnetic, resistive) across three tasks: pick-and-place with unknown mass, object reorientation, and plug insertion. Analyze how sensor properties (spatial resolution, shear sensing, tactile representation) and material friction affect performance.

Result: Show that usefulness of tactile information is not universal but depends on sensor modality, material properties, and task. Propose a task-driven framework for sensor selection; all related code, data, and hardware setup will be released publicly.

Conclusion: Tactile sensing offers benefits only when aligned with task and material characteristics; a systematic framework is essential for selecting sensors based on manipulation policy performance rather than assuming universal gains from tactile data.

Abstract: Vision-based learning from demonstrations has achieved remarkable success in enabling robots to perform manipulation tasks and high-level semantic reasoning, yet it remains insufficient for complex, contact-rich manipulation. While there is broad agreement that tactile sensing improves manipulation, there is no empirical guidance on which tactile sensors are best suited for which manipulation tasks. In this paper, we provide a systematic, task-driven evaluation of tactile sensors for robot manipulation and propose a framework for selecting and evaluating sensors based on manipulation policy performance. Separate manipulation policies are trained for tactile sensors of four distinct modalities: visual, acoustic, magnetic, and resistive, across three tasks: pick-and-place with unknown mass, object reorientation, and plug insertion. For each task, an analysis of how sensor properties such as spatial resolution, shear sensing, and tactile representation, and the inherent material friction affect task performances is done. Rather than tactile sensing being universally beneficial in the same way, our results show that the usefulness of tactile information depends strongly on sensor modality, material properties, and the specific manipulation tasks. All of the tactile sensors, code, data, and hardware setup will be publicly available on the project website.

</details>


### [229] [Industrial Dual-Arm Box Handling via Online Inertial Estimation and Convex Wrench Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22021)
*Kenzhi Iskandar Wong,Lin Yang,Qian Ying Lee,Domenico Campolo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Friction-aware dual-arm framework estimates unknown object inertial properties online from contact wrenches and uses a second-order cone program with ellipsoidal friction limits to compute feasible contact forces and torques, with offline trajectory refinement; demonstrates stable lifting under unknown COM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unknown mass and center of mass create unstable force-moment balance; slip, drop, misalignment, and squeezing pressures arise; need integrated approach that enforces friction feasibility rather than treating slip avoidance and squeezing separately.

Method: Online estimation of mass and COM from measured contact wrenches; compute friction-feasible contact forces and torsional moments via SOCP under ellipsoidal friction-limit-surface constraints; offline trajectory refinement to reduce undesired contact; friction feasibility enforced as hard constraint; minimize contact effort within feasible region.

Result: Experimental validation on a real dual-arm robot across different COM configurations showing stable lifting with unknown inertial properties and maintained frictional contact.
