Conclusion: The approach provides a practical, robust method for dual-arm manipulation under inertial uncertainty by combining online inertial estimation, hard-friction feasibility constraints, and optimization to reduce contact effort; effective in real experiments.

Abstract: Industrial robotic object handling often involves boxes and packages whose mass and center of mass are not known in advance. These uncertainties affect the force--moment balance required for stable lifting, and improper regulation of contact wrenches can lead to slip, object drop, orientation deviation, or excessive squeezing. This paper presents a friction-aware dual-arm box-handling framework for objects with unknown inertial properties. The proposed approach estimates the object mass and center of mass online from measured contact wrenches, and computes friction-feasible contact forces and torsional moments through a second-order cone program (SOCP) under ellipsoidal friction-limit-surface constraints. An offline trajectory refinement stage is also included to reduce undesired object--environment contact when geometric constraints are present. By enforcing friction feasibility as a hard constraint and minimizing contact effort within the feasible region, the framework achieves stable lifting without treating slip avoidance and excessive squeezing as separately tuned objectives. Experiments on a real dual-arm robotic system under different center-of-mass configurations demonstrate that the method lifts objects with unknown inertial properties while maintaining stable frictional contact.

</details>


### [230] [CoRMA: Contrastive RMA for Contact-Rich Meta-Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22082)
*Wentian Wang,Chutong Wen,Hongxu Ma,Wuhao Wang,Zhexiong Xue,Abdul Haseeb Nizamani,Dandi Zhou,Xinhai Sun,Jianqiao Zhu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CoRMA replaces raw simulator-parameter adaptation with a compact 6D semantic contact context to enable online, gradient-free within-episode adaptation for force-dominant assembly tasks using a deployable Transformer adapter that infers context from force/proprioception/action histories. Evaluated on PegInsert, GearMesh, NutThread in simulation and on a real Marvin arm, it maintains higher real success than FORGE baselines under pose noise, suggesting semantic contact inference as a reusable adaptation interface; future work includes broader unseen-task generalization and Real2Sim calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Achieve robust sim-to-real assembly adaptation without costly offline tuning, demonstrations, privileged inputs, or online gradient updates, particularly for force-dominated assembly where contact dynamics are critical.

Method: Introduce CoRMA with a 6D semantic contact context describing contact onset, lateral engagement, guided transition, contact direction, and jamming. Use a deployable causal Transformer adapter to infer this context online from histories of force, proprioception, and actions via semantic regression and a force-regime contrastive objective. At deployment, replace oracle context with inferred context to enable within-episode adaptation without demonstrations or gradient updates. Evaluate on PegInsert, GearMesh, NutThread in Isaac Lab/Isaac Sim 5.0 and on a real Marvin arm.

Result: CoRMA retains higher verified real success under target-pose noise compared to FORGE baselines, which perform well in simulation but degrade on hardware.

Conclusion: Semantic contact inference can serve as a reusable adaptation interface for related assembly tasks; broader unseen-task generalization and Real2Sim calibration remain open areas for future work.

Abstract: We present CoRMA(Contrastive Robotic Motor Adaptation), a context-based meta-adaptation framework that modifies RMA for force-dominant assembly. CoRMA replaces raw simulator-parameter adaptation with a compact 6D simulator-only semantic contact context describing contact onset, lateral engagement, guided transition, contact direction, and jamming. A deployable causal Transformer adapter infers this context online from force, proprioceptive, and action histories using semantic regression and a force-regime contrastive objective. At deployment, oracle context is removed and replaced by the inferred context, enabling within-episode adaptation without demonstrations, privileged inputs, or gradient updates. We evaluate CoRMA on PegInsert, GearMesh, and NutThread in Isaac Lab / Isaac Sim~5.0 and on a real Marvin arm. Compared with FORGE baselines that achieve high simulation success but degrade substantially on hardware, CoRMA retains higher verified real success under controlled target-pose noise. These results support semantic contact inference as a reusable adaptation interface within a related assembly task family, while broader unseen-task generalization and Real2Sim calibration remain future work.

</details>


### [231] [Beyond Pixels: Learning Invariant Rewards for Real-World Robotics From a Few Demonstrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22123)
*Tengye Xu,Yangting Sun,Ziju Shen,Guanqi Chen,Zhen Fu,Chen yizhou,Hua Chen,Jia Pan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes invariant symbolic reward learning from few demonstrations to generalize open-world manipulation, shifting from pixel-based fitting to task-level invariants; combines structural reward formulation with a hybrid symbolic–numerical invariant distillation, enabling zero-shot generalization and improved policy learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward generalization in robotics RL is limited by overfitting to training visuals; open-world tasks yield many variants (instances, positions, viewpoints). A reward that ignores appearance and captures constant task-level strategies is needed to enable robust, reusable guidance.

Method: Two coupled components: (1) a structural reward formulation encoding task-level strategies and physical constraints while preserving optimal policy invariance; (2) a hybrid symbolic–numerical procedure that distills invariants from as few as five demonstrations without online interaction. The approach emphasizes invariant, abstracted behavior over surface-level visuals.

Result: Empirical evaluation on eight Meta-World tasks and three Franka manipulation tasks shows improved process alignment and policy rollout ranking versus baselines, accelerating downstream RL. Real-world out-of-distribution tests demonstrate zero-shot generalization to variations in position, viewpoint, and objects, enabling a single reward representation to cover diverse task variants.

Conclusion: Shifting from visual feature-fitting to invariant, task-level rewards enables generalization across wide visual variants and reuse across task variants; the approach improves data efficiency and robustness of learned policies in open-world manipulation.

Abstract: Designing reward functions that generalize beyond controlled laboratory settings remains a fundamental challenge in reinforcement learning for robotics. In open-world manipulation problems, a single task can appear in numerous variants through different object instances, positions, and camera viewpoints. Recent vision-based reward models tend to memorize specific pixel distributions and fail to generalize beyond their training conditions. To address this, we propose a framework that learns invariant symbolic reward functions from as few as five demonstrations. The insight is to shift from visual feature-fitting to the discovery of behavioral invariants: task-level properties that remain constant across diverse visual instantiations. The framework has two coupled components: a structural reward formulation that encodes task-level strategies and physical constraints while preserving optimal policy invariance, and a hybrid symbolic-numerical procedure that distills these invariants from demonstrations without online interaction. Experiments on eight Meta-World tasks and three Franka manipulation tasks demonstrate that our method achieves stronger process alignment and policy rollout ranking abilities compared to baselines, accelerating downstream policy learning. Three real-world out-of-distribution experiments further show that the same learned reward generalizes zero-shot to position, viewpoint, and object variations, enabling a single reward representation to be reused across diverse task variants in practice.

</details>


### [232] [Action with Visual Primitives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22183)
*Weilong Guo,Yuchen Wang,Renping Zhou,Yunfeng Zhang,Rui Fang,Yue Meng,Wenda Xu,Yuan He,Gao Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AVP introduces Action with Visual Primitives, a VLA architecture where a vision-language model outputs visual-primitive tokens that condition a flow-matching action expert. It yields a 27.61% higher success rate over pi_0.5 and improves data efficiency and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To disentangle instruction understanding, scene understanding, and motor control in Vision-Language-Action models, enabling better learning efficiency and generalization by leveraging pretrained VLM perceptual/cognitive capabilities rather than relearning them in the action module.

Method: An end-to-end AVP architecture where the VLM infers the next-stage target and emits visual-primitive tokens that condition a flow-matching action expert. Supervision is derived from end-effector kinematics, enabling end-to-end training.

Result: Real-robot experiments on general pick-and-place tasks show 27.61% higher success rate than pi_0.5 and outperforms recent methods, with gains in data efficiency, spatial-compositional generalization, and object-level transfer.

Conclusion: A visual-primitive-centric interface effectively decouples perception from motor control in VLA models, improving data efficiency and generalization in real-robot manipulation.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising paradigm for generalist robotic manipulation. A common design in current architectures maps language instructions and visual observations to actions in a single forward pass. While conceptually simple, this formulation entangles instruction comprehension, spatial scene understanding, and motor control within a single learning objective. As a result, the action expert must implicitly relearn cognitive and perceptual capabilities already present in the pretrained VLM, which can limit both learning efficiency and generalization. We introduce AVP (Action with Visual Primitives), an end-to-end architecture that implements this visual-primitive-centric interface: the VLM infers the next-stage target and emits visual-primitive tokens that condition a flow-matching action expert, with supervision derived from end-effector kinematics. Real-robot experiments on general pick-and-place tasks show that AVP improves the success rate by 27.61% over pi_0.5 and outperforms other recent methods, with consistent gains in data efficiency, spatial-compositional generalization, and object-level transfer.

</details>


### [233] [Learning A Unified Risk Map for Autonomous Driving in Partially Observable Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22189)
*Jie Jia,Yaofeng Su,Zeyu Bao,Yun Hong,Bingzhao Gao,Zhongxue Gan,Wenchao Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Unified risk-map framework for occlusion-aware planning, integrating traffic-flow and collision risk via spatiotemporal modeling, plus diffusion-based scenario generation for occluded interactions; validated on Waymo Open Motion Dataset with significant TTC improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address occlusion-induced uncertainty in autonomous driving, where existing methods either overestimate risk based on reachable states or struggle to predict accurate trajectories under high occlusion.

Method: Develop a unified risk map combining traffic-flow risk and collision risk through spatiotemporal modeling; introduce a diffusion-based scenario generation framework to produce realistic yet adversarial occluded scenarios; integrate the modeling into a planning framework that supports risk-aware decisions under partial observability.

Result: On Waymo Open Motion Dataset, the method outperforms state-of-the-art occlusion-aware baselines, with minimum time-to-collision improved by 0.78x and average time-to-collision improved by 1.67x.

Conclusion: Provides a comprehensive and practical framework for risk-aware planning under partial observability in autonomous driving.

Abstract: Occlusion-aware prediction remains a critical challenge in autonomous driving due to the inherent uncertainty of unobserved regions. Existing approaches either overestimate risk based on reachable states or struggle to predict accurate trajectories under high occlusion uncertainty. To address these limitations, we propose a unified risk map modeling and learning framework for partially observable environments. Our method integrates traffic flow risk and collision risk through spatiotemporal modeling, enabling fine-grained assessment of occlusion-induced hazards. To address the scarcity of scenarios involving occluded interactions, we introduce a diffusion-based scenario generation framework that produces realistic yet adversarial scenarios. We integrate the modeling and learning of a unified risk map into a framework that supports risk-aware planning under partial observability. Experiments on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset show that our method significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art occlusion-aware baseline, improving minimum time-to-collision by 0.78 times and average time-to-collision by 1.67 times. The proposed framework offers a comprehensive and practical solution for risk-aware planning in partially observable environments.

</details>


### [234] [Imagine2Real: Towards Zero-shot Humanoid-Object Interaction via Video Generative Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22272)
*Jiahe Chen,ZiRui Wang,Feiyu Jia,Xiao Chen,Xiaojie Niu,Weishuai Zeng,Tianfan Xue,Xiaowei Zhou,Jiangmiao Pang,Jingbo Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Zero-shot, geometry-free whole-body HOI using sparse keypoints and 4D trajectories guided by a Behavior Foundation Model, enabling mocap-ready deployment without CAD priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D HOI data is scarce; relying on geometric priors causes representation misalignment; retargeting to different bodies/devices introduces complexity and error amplification.

Method: Represent robot and object motions as unified 4D point trajectories; track only sparse keypoints (base, hands, object) with a Keypoints Tracker; use latent space of a Behavior Foundation Model as search domain; progressive training with simple rewards; achieves zero-shot deployment in mocap.

Result: Shows robust behaviors learned from sparse signals and enables zero-shot physical deployment within a mocap system; bypasses heavy retargeting and CAD priors.

Conclusion: The framework provides a geometry-free, misalignment-tolerant HOI solution, reducing data and prior requirements and facilitating real-world deployment.

Abstract: Whole-body Humanoid-Object Interaction (HOI) is bottlenecked by the scarcity of high-fidelity 3D data. While video generative priors offer a promising alternative, existing methods suffer from \textit{Representation Misalignment} due to their reliance on geometric priors (e.g., explicit CAD models), and \textit{Retargeting Complexity} arising from intensive morphing and morphological mismatch. We propose Imagine2Real, a zero-shot HOI framework for flexible, geometry-free interaction. To resolve misalignment, we formulate robot and object motions as unified 4D point trajectories. To overcome retargeting complexity, our Keypoints Tracker tracks only sparse critical points (base, hands, and object), entirely bypassing the error-amplifying retargeting process. To maintain natural gaits despite these sparse signals, we utilize the latent space of a Behavior Foundation Model (BFM) as the tracker's search domain. Using a progressive training strategy, Imagine2Real learns robust behaviors with simple tracking rewards, enabling zero-shot physical deployment within a motion capture(mocap) system.

</details>


### [235] [Spatial Memory for Out-of-Vision Manipulation in Vision-Language-Action](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22283)
*Pengteng Li,Weiyu Guo,He Zhang,Tiefu Cai,Xiao He,Yandong Guo,Hui Xiong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SOMA introduces a persistent spatial memory for vision-language-action models to reason beyond the current view, significantly improving out-of-vision manipulation by constructing, refining, and retrieving spatial-semantic memories from multi-view observations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLAs struggle when targets are not visible; brittle, reactive behaviors arise due to implicit assumption of always-visible task objects. A persistent spatial memory from multi-view observations can enable reasoning beyond the current visual frustum.

Method: Spatial Memory Construction aggregates angular observations into a unified spatial-semantic map via scanning. Dynamic Memory Refinement maintains global temporal consistency. Contextual Memory Retrieval activates instruction-relevant spatial cues during manipulation.

Result: Empirical evaluation on five real-world out-of-vision manipulation tasks (including multi-step and dual-arm setups) shows higher task success, faster target localization, reduced viewpoint search, and near one-shot grasping under partial observability. Additional tests on RoboCasa GR1 and SimplerEnv validate effectiveness under conventional fully observable settings.

Conclusion: A memory-based framework for VLA enables robust out-of-vision reasoning and improves performance while remaining effective in fully observable scenarios; code release is forthcoming.

Abstract: We introduce SOMA, the Spatial Memory framework for Out-of-Vision Manipulation in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. Most existing VLAs implicitly assume that task-relevant objects are always visible, leading to brittle and reactive behaviors when targets fall outside the camera's field of view. SOMA addresses this limitation by equipping VLAs with a persistent spatial memory constructed from multi-view observations acquired via a movable head camera, enabling reasoning beyond the current visual frustum. The framework consists of three components: Spatial Memory Construction, which aggregates angular-wise observations into a unified spatial-semantic representation through scanning; Dynamic Memory Refinement, which maintains global consistency over time; and Contextual Memory Retrieval, which activates instruction-relevant spatial cues during manipulation. We evaluate SOMA on five challenging real-world out-of-vision manipulation tasks, including multi-step and dual-arm scenarios where target objects are initially invisible. Experimental results show that SOMA not only improves task success rates, but also induces qualitatively different manipulation behaviors, with faster target localization, reduced viewpoint search, and near one-shot grasping under partial observability. Additional experiments on RoboCasa GR1 and SimplerEnv further validate the effectiveness of SOMA's memory design under conventional fully observable settings. Code will be released soon.

</details>


### [236] [How can reasoning capability empower the AI copilot robot in endoscopic surgery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22322)
*Guankun Wang,Long Bai,Hongliang Ren*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Reasoning-enabled Vision-Language-Action (VLA) AI copilot could enhance endoscopic surgery by fusing multimodal cues, inferring tissue dynamics, and reducing surgeon cognitive load, turning copilot into a cognitive collaborator.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses intraoperative uncertainty and cognitive burden; gaps in current AI copilots' reasoning capabilities in endoscopic surgery.

Method: Proposes a reasoning-driven autonomy framework within a VLA model, integrating multimodal inputs, reconstructing surgical intent, and inferring hidden tissue dynamics to plan and adapt actions.

Result: The abstract presents a conceptual framework and potential benefits; no empirical results reported.

Conclusion: Reasoning-driven autonomy can transform AI copilot robots into cognitive collaborators, improving precision, safety, and sustainability in clinical practice.

Abstract: Reasoning capability has significantly advanced complex logical inference and robotic decision-making in general domains. However, its potential in the Artificial Intelligence (AI) copilot robot-particularly implemented based on the Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model-remains unexplored in endoscopic surgery. Effective reasoning should enable AI copilot robots to integrate multimodal cues, interpret surgical intent, and infer hidden tissue dynamics, thereby alleviating intraoperative uncertainty and cognitive burden on surgeons. Properly implemented, reasoning-driven autonomy can transform AI copilot robots from reactive executors into cognitive collaborators, enhancing precision, safety, and sustainability in clinical practice.

</details>


### [237] [Real-Time Auto-Optimization in Unknown Environments via Structure-Exploiting Dual Control for Exploration and Exploitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22431)
*Shiying Dong,Haoyang Yang,Qiwei Liu,Wen-Hua Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A structure-exploiting dual control method (DCEE) for unknown-environment auto-optimization that linearizes a nonlinear residual while preserving a convex outer loss, yielding a PSD generalized Gauss-Newton Hessian for fast, reliable online subproblems; demonstrates ~10x speedup with 83 μs runtime on embedded CPU in vehicle auto-optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computational burden of dual control for active learning in auto-optimization under unknown, potentially changing environments; existing DCEE methods rely on standard optimization or gradient updates and may not exploit problem structure, limiting real-time applicability.

Method: Identify that the DCEE reward decomposes into a unified nonlinear residual map with a convex outer loss (convex-over-nonlinear structure). Linearize the nonlinear residual only, keep the convex outer loss, and solve each subproblem as a structured convex program. Use a generalized Gauss-Newton Hessian approximation that is positive semidefinite and depends only on first-order derivatives. This yields a fast, online-capable solution with a per-iteration convex-form subproblem.

Result: Applied to a vehicle cruising auto-optimization problem. Through simulation and hardware-in-the-loop experiments, the method achieves about an order of magnitude speedup and maximum computation time of 83 microseconds on a typical vehicle embedded CPU while improving control performance compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The structure-exploiting DCEE offers fast, reliable real-time dual control in unknown environments by leveraging a convex-over-nonlinear formulation and Gauss-Newton-type Hessian approximations. It reduces computational burden without sacrificing performance, as demonstrated in vehicle auto-optimization tests; suggests applicability to broader DCEE settings and potential for further efficiency gains.

Abstract: This paper develops a fast numerical dual control for exploration and exploitation (DCEE) method to address auto-optimization problems in unknown environments. In auto-optimization problems, the optimal operating condition is unknown a priori and may vary with the environment. As in classical dual control techniques, computational burden remains a major concern in DCEE for active learning. Existing DCEE methods provide a principled exploration-exploitation objective, but mainly realized through standard optimization packages or explicit gradient-type update laws, where the numerical structure of the DCEE has not been fully exploited. This paper shows that the reward function in DCEE has an inherent convex-over-nonlinear structure, where the exploitation and exploration terms form a unified nonlinear residual map equipped with a convex outer loss. Benefiting from this structure, a structure-exploiting numerical method is developed by linearizing only the nonlinear residual map while preserving the convex outer loss. Thus, each subproblem is transformed into a structured convex form that can be solved reliably. The resulting generalized Gauss-Newton Hessian approximation is positive semidefinite and depends only on first-order derivatives, thereby supporting fast online computation. The proposed method is evaluated on a vehicle cruising auto-optimization problem and compared with existing methods. Simulation and hardware-in-the-loop experimental results show that the proposed method improves control performance and achieves a speedup of approximately one order of magnitude, with a microsecond-level maximum computation time of only 83 μs on a typical vehicle embedded CPU.

</details>


### [238] [Terminal Constraint Model Predictive Control for Image-Based Visual Servoing of UAVs with Kalman Filter-Based Moment Loss Compensation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22443)
*X. Wang,Y. Cao,W. L. W. Leong,Y. R. Tan,S. Huang,S. H. R. Teo,C. Xiang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A terminal-constraint MPC for IBVS with KF-based moment prediction improves stability and continuity under input/state constraints and intermittent visual features in UAVs; validated in real-time experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional IBVS lacks guarantees under input/state constraints and is vulnerable to moment loss during aggressive UAV motion; there is a need for robust, feasible control with feature-loss compensation.

Method: Embed terminal-state constraints and a terminal cost into IBVS within a model predictive control framework; integrate a Kalman filter to predict image moments during partial observability; aim for recursive feasibility and closed-loop stability.

Result: The framework yields improved recursive feasibility, convergence, and stability under constraints; preserves control continuity during moment dropouts; validated with real-time UAV visual servoing experiments.

Conclusion: TC-MPC with KF-based state prediction robustly enhances IBVS performance under constraints and partial measurements, enabling reliable real-time UAV visual servoing.

Abstract: Image-Based Visual Servoing (IBVS) provides an efficient vision-guided control paradigm for unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) by directly regulating image-space errors. However, conventional IBVS controllers are vulnerable to two critical issues: loss of closed-loop stability near the target due to input and state constraints, and control failure caused by intermittent loss of moment-based visual features under aggressive motion. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a terminal-constraint model predictive control (TC-MPC) framework for IBVS, integrated with a Kalman filter (KF)-based state-prediction mechanism. The TC-MPC explicitly incorporates terminal-state constraints and a terminal cost into the IBVS error dynamics, ensuring recursive feasibility, improved convergence behavior, and closed-loop stability under control and state constraints. In parallel, the Kalman filter predicts the temporal evolution of image moments during short-term visual degradation, enabling the controller to preserve control continuity when moment measurements are partially unavailable. The proposed approach is validated through real-time UAV visual servoing experiments.

</details>


### [239] [Steins;Gate Drive: Semantic Safety Arbitration over Structured Futures for Latency-Decoupled LLM Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22456)
*Anjie Qiu,Hans D. Schotten*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Latency-decoupled planner-runtime architecture for cloud-hosted LLM driving using worldline forecasts; reduces effective lag while preserving safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome inference latency and tight coupling between future prediction and action in autonomous driving; enable safe, programmable forecasts.

Method: SteinsGateDrive architecture; worldline generator produces three roles: alpha nominal ego-conditioned futures, beta interaction counterfactuals around nearby vehicles, gamma hazard-stress futures (e.g., braking, cut-ins). The selected branch becomes a typed StrategicForecast with horizon, validity/abort conditions, fallback, and authority. Runtime uses atom-predicate checks for safety; drift score acts as a refresh-frequency knob.

Result: In a within-subject protocol (10 seeds, 20 steps), GPT-5.4 mini reduced effective lag from +3.07 s at 1-second horizon to -0.01 s at 4-second horizon while maintaining the no-collision safety boundary.

Conclusion: Safety arises from the atom-predicate runtime check rather than the drift score, which is used primarily as a refresh-frequency knob; the architecture decouples planning to achieve latency reduction while preserving safety.

Abstract: Cloud-hosted LLM driver agents provide useful semantic judgments, but their inference latency exceeds stepwise vehicle-control windows. Learned world models predict futures, but they usually keep future generation and action selection inside large coupled loops. We present SteinsGateDrive, a latency-decoupled planner-runtime architecture in which the worldline metaphor from the eponymous story names one plausible consequence of an intervention: the LLM selects counterfactual driving futures before the final control instant, and a runtime reuses the selected forecast only while safety contracts remain valid. The generator builds three world-line roles: alpha nominal ego-conditioned futures, beta interaction counterfactuals around nearby vehicles, and gamma hazard-stress futures such as braking, cut-ins, or blocked corridors. The selected branch becomes a typed StrategicForecast with horizon, validity/abort conditions, fallback, and authority. On a within-subject, matched-seed normal-highway protocol with 10 seeds and 20 steps, GPT-5.4 mini reduces effective lag from +3.07 s at 1-second horizon to -0.01 s at 4-second horizon while preserving the measured no-collision safety boundary. The architecture's safety contribution comes from the atom-predicate runtime check, not from the drift score, which functions as a refresh-frequency knob.

</details>


### [240] [Quantifying Full-Body Immersion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22521)
*Alihan Bakir,Ekrem Yüksel,Fabio Zuliani,Neil Chennoufi,Francesco Bruno,Jamie Paik*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A scalable, modular, nature-inspired robotic surface platform enabling full-body immersive experiences (FBI) in virtual environments via distributed haptic, shape, and motion feedback for real-time, multi-user interaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Push immersion beyond traditional VR by providing distributed, adaptive feedback across space, enabling seamless whole-body interaction and scalable multi-user experiences; address the gap between real and virtual environments with a bio-inspired modular approach.

Method: Design and deploy modular robotic surface units that can be tiled to render immersive environments at any scale; distribute force, shape, and motion feedback through the space; enable real-time adaptation to user interactions and support multi-user coordination within a scalable platform.

Result: Conceptual framework and proposed architecture with a scalable, distributable platform; demonstrates potential to achieve audio-visual, physical, and full-body immersion and multi-user engagement, though empirical results are not reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: A foundational framework for a new era of human–environment synthesis, enabling symbiotic, whole-body interactions with virtual spaces and scalable, adaptive immersive experiences across varying scales and user counts.

Abstract: Humanity is at the forefront of yet another digital revolution, where the lines between real and virtual worlds are dissolving, reshaping how we perceive and interact with our surroundings. In this context, we introduce a transformative paradigm for immersive virtual experiences centered around whole-body kinetic interactions. Our approach redefines immersion through three distinct levels: audio-visual immersion, capturing sensory realism; physical immersion, delivering haptic feedback; and full-body immersion (FBI), where dynamic bodily interaction integrates seamlessly with virtual environments. At the core of this innovation lies a scalable, distributable platform based on modular robotic surface units inspired by the adaptive designs of nature. These units enable the rendering of immersive environments at any scale, from intimate personal experiences to expansive multi-user settings, dynamically adapting to interactions in real-time. The modular system distributes force, shape, and motion feedback throughout entire spaces, replicating the physical characteristics of the environment and enabling new depth of engagement through FBI. By combining scalability, adaptability, and dynamic physical engagement, this framework bridges the gap between real and virtual worlds. It offers an unprecedented level of immersion where users can engage their entire bodies in symbiotic interactions with the virtual space. This work not only advances immersive technology but also redefines how humans and virtual environments coexist, setting a foundation for a new era of human-environment synthesis.

</details>


### [241] [Decoupling Ego-Motion from Target Dynamics via Dual-Interval Motion Cues for UAV Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22605)
*Liuyang Wang,Feitian Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A vision-only motion-guided detection framework for UAVs that decouples target motion from camera motion using GMC, dual-interval motion extraction, and MGA within an FPN, showing improvements over YOLOv8 on VisDrone-VID under severe ego-motion; includes ablations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UAV video suffers from ego-motion, jitter, and severe scale variation; existing motion methods are either too costly (optical flow) or brittle (single-interval differencing).

Method: Apply homography-based Global Motion Compensation to align adjacent frames; introduce Dual-Interval Motion Extraction to capture short-term and long-term motion cues; integrate cues via a lightweight Motion-Guided Attention module within a Feature Pyramid Network to enhance feature representations.

Result: Experiments on VisDrone-VID show consistent improvements over a strong YOLOv8 baseline under severe ego-motion; ablation studies confirm effectiveness of dual-interval design and motion-guided attention.

Conclusion: A viable vision-only approach for UAV object detection in dynamic scenes by decoupling camera-induced motion from target motion, leveraging multi-timescale motion cues and attention-guided feature fusion.

Abstract: Object detection from Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) is challenged by severe ego-motion, camera jitter, and large scale variations. While modern detectors perform well on static images, their direct application to UAV video often fails, particularly for small objects in dynamic scenes. Existing motion-based methods either rely on computationally expensive optical flow or use single-interval differencing, which is sensitive to jitter and limited in capturing diverse motion patterns. We propose a vision-only motion-guided detection framework that decouples target motion from camera-induced disturbances. A homography-based Global Motion Compensation (GMC) first aligns adjacent frames. We then introduce a Dual-Interval Motion Extraction strategy that captures both short-term and long-term motion cues. To integrate these cues, a lightweight Motion-Guided Attention (MGA) module enhances feature representations within a Feature Pyramid Network. Experiments on the VisDrone-VID dataset demonstrate consistent improvements over a strong YOLOv8 baseline under severe ego-motion. Ablation studies further confirm the effectiveness of the dual-interval design and the proposed motion-guided attention mechanism.

</details>


### [242] [SE3Kit: A Lightweight Python Library for Specialized Geometric Primitives in Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22633)
*Daniyal Maroufi,Omid Rezayof,Farshid Alambeigi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SE3Kit is a lightweight, pure-Python NumPy-based library for Lie group operations on SE(3) and SO(3), aimed at embedded deployment, rapid prototyping, and education, with rigorous mathematics and no heavy dependencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the gap between lightweight, mathematically strict Lie-group implementations and heavy robotics frameworks; enables embedded deployment, rapid prototyping, and educational use without heavy dependencies.

Method: Pure-Python, NumPy-only implementation of Lie Group operations for SE(3) and SO(3); no deep learning or visualization dependencies; designed for lightweight, robotics-specific features with rigorous mathematics.

Result: Introduces SE3Kit, a lightweight Python library providing efficient Lie group operations on SE(3) and SO(3) with a pure-Python/NumPy implementation and minimal dependencies.

Conclusion: SE3Kit fills the niche between heavy frameworks and general tools, offering robotics-focused Lie group operations with strict mathematical implementation and suitability for embedded deployment, prototyping, and education.

Abstract: The Python robotics ecosystem faces a challenge: while many libraries exist for rigid body transformations, few are both lightweight and mathematically strict. This paper introduces SE3Kit, a lightweight Python library efficient operations on the Special Euclidean Group SE(3) and the Special Orthogonal Group SO(3). Unlike established frameworks that require heavy dependencies (e.g., SpatialMath, PyPose) or general tools that lack robotics-specific features (e.g., SciPy), SE3Kit targets the gap between these extremes. It is designed for embedded deployment, rapid prototyping, and education while providing rigorous mathematical implementation. It provides a pure-Python, NumPy-only implementation of Lie Group operations, without the overhead of deep learning or other visualization software.

</details>


### [243] [Symmetries Here and There, Combined Everywhere: Cross-space Symmetry Compositions in Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22639)
*Loizos Hadjiloizou,Rodrigo Pérez-Dattari,Noémie Jaquier*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A framework for learning robot policies that are jointly equivariant to multiple symmetries across configuration and task spaces, using cross-space symmetry composition via the differential-geometric forward kinematics, showing improved generalization on a dual-arm robot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotics problems often contain multiple symmetries that are treated in isolation; jointly exploiting them could enhance data efficiency and generalization across tasks and configurations.

Method: Introduce cross-space symmetry compositions that descend symmetries from configuration to task space and lift symmetries from task to configuration space using the differential-geometric structure of forward kinematics, enabling a unified representation with joint equivariance to multiple symmetries across spaces.

Result: Validation on both simulated and real-world experiments with a dual-arm robot, demonstrating improved generalization when leveraging multiple symmetries jointly.

Conclusion: Jointly exploiting multiple symmetries through cross-space composition yields a more powerful and generalizable policy representation, suggesting broad applicability beyond the tested setup.

Abstract: Robots exhibit a rich variety of symmetries arising from their mechanical structure and the properties of their tasks. Although many robotics problems exhibit several symmetries simultaneously, existing approaches typically treat them in isolation, failing to exploit their combined potential. This paper introduces cross-space symmetry compositions, a framework for learning robot policies that are jointly equivariant to multiple symmetries across configuration and task spaces. Leveraging the differential-geometric structure of the forward kinematics map, we both descend symmetries from configuration to task space and lift symmetries from task to configuration space, enabling their composition within a unified representation space. We validate our framework on simulated and real-world experiments on a dual-arm robot, demonstrating that jointly leveraging multiple symmetries yields improved generalization.

</details>


### [244] [Scout-Assisted Planning for Heterogeneous Robot Teams under Partially Known Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22693)
*Hoang-Dung Bui,Abhish Khanal,Raihan Islam Arnob,Gregory J. Stein*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SAP introduces scout-assisted planning with Information Gain-based Action Pruning, using a Graph Neural Network to predict information gain and enable real-time, high-quality planning in partially known environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous ground robots incur costly backtracking when blocked routes are only revealed through traversal; proactive scouting aims to reduce uncertainty and travel costs by focusing on the most impactful edges.

Method: A heterogeneous planner (Scout-Assisted Planning, SAP) uses UAV scouts to gather environment data. It employs Information Gain-based Action Pruning to score scouting actions by their expected impact on ground robot behavior. To manage computational cost, a Graph Neural Network predicts information gain directly from graph structure and belief state, enabling real-time planning.

Result: SAP with information gain-based pruning reduces ground robot travel cost by 31.9–37.7% relative to the Canadian Traveler Problem baseline, and outperforms proximity-based scouting guidance by 8–14%. The approach maintains solution quality while meeting real-time feasibility.

Conclusion: Principled information-gain-guided scouting is both more effective and computationally feasible for real-world deployment of autonomous robot teams in partially known environments.

Abstract: Autonomous robot teams navigating partially known environments face costly backtracking when ground robots encounter blocked roads that are only revealed upon physical traversal. We address this with Scout-Assisted Planning, a heterogeneous planning framework in which scouting Unmanned Aerial Vehicles proactively gather environmental information to improve Unmanned Ground Vehicle navigation. To focus scouting on the most consequential edges, we propose Information Gain-based Action Pruning, which scores candidate scouting actions by their expected impact on ground robot behavior. Since exact Information Gain-based Action Pruning computation is prohibitively expensive, we develop a Graph Neural Network based model that predicts information gain values directly from graph structure and belief state, reducing planning time to real-time levels without sacrificing solution quality. Experiments across three environment types show that SAP with Information Gain Action Pruning reduces ground robot travel cost by 31.9--37.7% over the Canadian Traveler Problem baseline, and outperforms proximity-based scouting guidance by an additional 8--14%, confirming that principled information-gain-guided scouting is both more effective and computationally feasible for real-world deployment

</details>


### [245] [Superhuman Safe and Agile Racing through Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22748)
*Ismail Geles,Leonard Bauersfeld,Markus Wulfmeier,Davide Scaramuzza*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Multi-agent reinforcement learning enables safe, high-speed quadrotor racing with multiple racers; league-based self-play yields anticipatory, cooperative/competitive behaviors; outperforms humans and reduces collisions; zero-shot safe human interaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address brittleness of single-agent control in real-world dynamic multi-agent spaces; provide safety scaffold via multi-agent coordination; enable robust robotic co-existence.

Method: Use high-speed quadrotor racing as testbed; train with variable number of racers; league-based self-play to foster anticipatory behaviors; address aerodynamic interactions (downwash); evaluate against human pilot and single-agent baselines.

Result: Agents outperform champion human pilot in multi-player races above 22 m/s; collisions reduced by 50% vs strong single-agent baselines; zero-shot generalization to safer human interaction.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that multi-agent interaction with diverse agents is key for robustness in real-world robotic co-existence; empirical demonstration that learning to coordinate in MARL can surpass single-agent safety constraints.

Abstract: Autonomous systems have achieved superhuman performance in isolation or simulation, yet they remain brittle in shared, dynamic real-world spaces. This failure stems from the dominant single-agent paradigm for physical applications, where other actors are ignored or treated as environmental noise, preventing effective coordination. Here we show that multi-agent reinforcement learning provides the essential safety scaffolding required for real-world interaction. Using high-speed quadrotor racing as a high-stakes testbed, we train agents to navigate complex aerodynamic interactions and strategic maneuvering with a variable number of racers. Through league-based self-play, agents evolve sophisticated anticipatory behaviors, including proactive collision avoidance, overtaking, and handling multi-agent physical interactions, including aerodynamic downwash. Our agents outperform a champion-level human pilot in multi-player races at speeds exceeding 22 m/s, while simultaneously reducing collision rates by 50 % compared to state-of-the-art single-agent baselines. Crucially, training with diverse artificial agents enables zero-shot generalization to safer human interaction. These results suggest that the path to robust robotic co-existence lies not in isolated safety constraints, but in the rigorous demands of multi-agent interaction. Multimedia materials are available at: https://rpg.ifi.uzh.ch/marl

</details>


### [246] [GesVLA: Gesture-Aware Vision-Language-Action Model Embedded Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22812)
*Wenxuan Guo,Ziyuan Li,Meng Zhang,Yichen Liu,Yimeng Dong,Chuxi Xu,Yunfei Wei,Ze Chen,Erjin Zhou,Jianjiang Feng*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GesVLA introduces gesture as a parallel instruction modality for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) robots, enabling gesture features to participate in high-level reasoning and policy generation via a dual-VLM architecture. A scalable synthetic gesture data pipeline (hand rendering on real scenes) and a two-stage training regime improve grounding and action in cluttered real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA systems relying on textual instructions face spatial ambiguity in scenes with multiple similar objects; introducing gestures can disambiguate targets and guide actions more effectively.

Method: Encode gesture features into the latent space and couple them with action policies using a dual-vision-language model (VLM) architecture. Build a gesture data pipeline by rendering hand models on real images to generate diverse gestures with pointing annotations. Employ a two-stage training strategy to learn gesture perception and action prediction.

Result: The inclusion of gesture consistently improves target grounding accuracy and human-robot interaction efficiency, particularly in complex and cluttered environments, demonstrated on tasks like controlled block manipulation and product/produce selection on real robots.

Conclusion: Gesture-aware VLA models with tight gesture-action coupling and scalable gesture data generation enhance grounding and control in real-world manipulation tasks, addressing spatial ambiguity in cluttered scenes.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown strong potential for general-purpose robot manipulation by unifying perception and action. However, existing VLA systems primarily rely on textual instructions and struggle to resolve spatial ambiguity in complex scenes with multiple similar objects. To address this limitation, we introduce gesture as a parallel instruction modality and propose a Gesture-aware Vision-Language-Action model (GesVLA). Our approach encodes gesture features directly into the latent space, enabling them to participate in both high-level reasoning and low-level action generation, and adopts a dual-VLM architecture to achieve tight coupling between gesture representations and action policies. At the data level, we construct a scalable gesture data generation pipeline by rendering hand models onto real-world scene images. This reduces the sim-to-real visual gap while producing rich data with diverse motion patterns and corresponding pointing annotations. In addition, we employ a two-stage training strategy to equip the model with both gesture perception and action prediction capabilities. We evaluate our approach on multiple real-world robotic tasks, including a controlled block manipulation task for validation and more practical scenarios such as product and produce selection. Experimental results show that incorporating gesture consistently improves target grounding accuracy and human-robot interaction efficiency, especially in complex and cluttered environments. Project page: https://gwxuan.github.io/GesVLA/.

</details>


### [247] [AwareVLN: Reasoning with Self-awareness for Vision-Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22816)
*Wenxuan Guo,Xiuwei Xu,Yichen Liu,Xiangyu Li,Hang Yin,Huangxing Chen,Wenzhao Zheng,Jianjiang Feng,Jie Zhou,Jiwen Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AwareVLN introduces a self-aware, end-to-end VLN framework with a structural reasoning module and a progress-driven data engine, achieving state-of-the-art results on Habitat without relying on explicit 3D scene maps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between end-to-end vision-language navigation and explicit agent–instruction–scene understanding, while avoiding reliance on additional 3D sensors and keeping end-to-end trainability.

Method: (1) Structural reasoning module that enforces spatial and task-oriented self-awareness of the agent; (2) Automatic data engine that divides training into progress-based stages to improve data efficiency and learning signal quality; both components operate within a fully end-to-end, data-driven framework.

Result: Extensive experiments on Habitat-based datasets show that AwareVLN significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-art vision-language navigation methods.

Conclusion: A self-aware, end-to-end VLN framework with structural reasoning and progress-aware data training can achieve superior navigation performance without external 3D mapping, suggesting better grounding of instructions and progress awareness in VLM-based agents.

Abstract: Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) requires an agent to ground language instructions to its own movement within a visual environment. While state-of-the-art methods leverage the reasoning capabilities of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for end-to-end action prediction, they often lack an explicit and explainable understanding of the relationships between the agent, the instruction, and the scene. Conversely, explicitly building a scene map for heuristic planning is intuitively appealing but relies on additional 3D sensors and hinders large-scale vision-language pre-training. To bridge this gap, we propose AwareVLN, a novel framework that equips the navigation model with a self-aware reasoning mechanism, enabling it to understand the agent's state and task progress in a fully end-to-end and data-driven manner. Our approach features two key innovations: (1) a structural reasoning module that fosters spatial and task-oriented self-awareness, and (2) an automatic data engine with progress division for effective training. Extensive experiments on various datasets in Habitat simulator show our AwareVLN significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-art vision-language navigation methods. Project page: https://gwxuan.github.io/AwareVLN/.

</details>


<div id='cs.CG'></div>

# cs.CG [[Back]](#toc)

### [248] [Bifunction and Interlevel Delaunay Trifiltrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21636)
*Ángel Javier Alonso,Michael Kerber,Tung Lam,Michael Lesnick,Abhishek Rathod*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: A 3-parameter extension of the Delaunay filtration (trifiltration) for point clouds X ⊂ R^d with an R^2-valued function is introduced. It preserves a weak topological equivalence with a corresponding 3-parameter offset filtration, and comes with size and time bounds: size O(|X|^{⌈(d+1)/2⌉+1}) and compute time O(|X|^{⌈d/2⌉+2}). An implementation is reported that handles thousands of points in R^3 with nearly linear memory growth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivated by time-varying data and the desire to extend Delaunay-type filtrations to multi-parameter settings, achieving a tractable weak equivalence for higher-dimensional/parameterized filtrations.

Method: Define a 3-parameter trifiltration for X ⊂ R^d using an R^2-valued function, analogous to the 2-parameter extension of the Delaunay/offset filtrations. Prove a weak (homotopy-like) equivalence to the corresponding 3-parameter offset filtration and derive complexity bounds. Implement the algorithm and provide empirical evaluation.

Result: Establishes a computable trifiltration with size and time bounds as above, and demonstrates practical feasibility via implementation and experiments on 3D data with thousands of points and near-linear memory growth.

Conclusion: The trifiltration extends the Delaunay-filtration toolkit to a 3-parameter, R^2-valued setting, preserving a weak equivalence to an offset filtration and enabling scalable computation with practical performance on moderate-sized 3D datasets.

Abstract: A key property of the Delaunay filtration is that it is topologically (i.e., weakly) equivalent to the offset (union-of-balls) filtration. Recently, this filtration has been extended to point clouds equipped with an $\mathbb{R}$-valued function, yielding a computable 2-parameter filtration that satisfies an analogous weak equivalence. Motivated in part by the study of time-varying data, we introduce a 3-parameter extension of the Delaunay filtration for point clouds equipped with an $\mathbb{R}^2$-valued function, also satisfying an analogous weak equivalence. For a point cloud $X \subset \mathbb{R}^d$, our trifiltration has size $O\bigl(|X|^{\lceil(d+1)/2\rceil+1}\bigr)$. We present an algorithm that computes this trifiltration in time $O\bigl(|X|^{\lceil d/2\rceil+2}\bigr)$, together with an implementation. Our experiments demonstrate that implementation can handle thousands of points in $\mathbb{R}^3$, with memory growth that is nearly linear.

</details>


### [249] [A geometric modelling framework to support the design of heterogeneous lattice structures with non-linearly varying geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21971)
*Nikita Letov,Yaoyao Fiona Zhao*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: Introduces a function-representation based modelling method for heterogeneous lattice structures in AM to overcome parameter-driven model limits; includes a software prototype and use-cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AM enables manufacturing of complex heterogeneous lattices, but existing geometry modelling cannot flexibly handle non-linear parameter variation or a wide number of controllable parameters, creating a gap between manufacturing capabilities and modelling tools.

Method: Proposes replacing manual geometric parameters with mathematical functions to control geometry, leveraging function representation for design; implements a software prototype and analyzes multiple use-cases to demonstrate feasibility.

Result: Demonstrates feasibility of non-linear and richly parameterized lattice modelling via function-based representation; software prototype validates concept across several practical use-cases.

Conclusion: The function-representation approach narrows the divide between AM manufacturing capabilities and geometric modelling, enabling more flexible, non-linear control of lattice structures; sets the stage for broader adoption and further refinement.

Abstract: Geometric modelling has been a crucial component of the design process ever since the introduction of the first Computer-Aided Design (CAD) systems. Additive Manufacturing (AM) pushes design freedom to previously unachievable limits. AM allows the manufacturing of lattice structures which are otherwise close to impossible to be manufactured conventionally. Yet, the geometric modelling of heterogeneous lattice structures is still greatly limited. Thus, the AM industry is now in a situation where the manufacturing capabilities exceed the geometric modelling capabilities. While there have been advancements in the modelling of heterogeneous lattice structures, the review of relevant literature revealed critical limitations of the existing approaches. These limitations include their inability to model non-linear variation of geometric parameters, as well as the limited amount of controllable geometric parameters. This work presents a novel geometric modelling methodology based on function representation as an attempt to bridge this gap. The proposed approach avoids the manual definition of geometric parameters and provides a method to control them with mathematical functions instead. A software prototype implementing the proposed approach is presented, and several use-cases are analysed.

</details>


### [250] [Maximum-Weight Two Boxes Symmetric Difference Problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22690)
*José Fernández Goycoolea,Luis H. Herrera,Pablo Pérez Lantero,Carlos Seara*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: Given n weighted points in the plane (weights may be positive or negative), the paper presents an exact algorithm running in O(n^4 log n) time and O(n) space to select two axis-aligned rectangles A and B (possibly overlapping) that maximize the total weight of points in their symmetric difference. The framework extends naturally to maximize the symmetric-difference weight for k≥3 boxes and/or the union weight for k≥2 boxes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Maximizing weighted coverage by geometric ranges is a fundamental problem in computational geometry with applications in data analysis, GIS, and spatial querying. Using the symmetric difference allows the objective to reward points covered by exactly one rectangle, penalizing overlap, and accommodating negative weights.

Method: The algorithm constructs an optimization framework based on axis-aligned rectangles and the symmetric-difference objective. It reduces the search to a finite set of candidate rectangle placements (determined by input coordinates) and efficiently evaluates the objective for pairs (A,B) using a sweep-like or subproblem decomposition that can be updated incrementally. The approach yields a combinatorial search with O(n^4) candidates for rectangle configurations and a log n factor from data-structure operations, giving O(n^4 log n) time and O(n) space.

Result: An exact, polynomial-time algorithm is obtained for two-rectangle symmetric-difference maximization with provable optimality under the given model; the same optimization framework can be adapted to solve related problems such as maximizing the symmetric-difference total weight for k≥3 boxes and/or the union total weight for k≥2 boxes.

Conclusion: The work provides a scalable exact framework for multi-rectangle coverage problems under the symmetric-difference and union objectives, with a clear path to generalization to more boxes. While the O(n^4 log n) time bound is substantial, the O(n) space usage is efficient and the method exposes a structure that could inspire improvement or specialization (e.g., for fixed k or restricted instances).

Abstract: Let $P$ be a set of $n$ points in the plane, where each element of $P$ is assigned a weight $ω(p)$, positive or negative. In this paper, we present an algorithm that runs in $O(n^4\log n)$ time and $O(n)$ space to find two possibly overlapping axis-aligned rectangles $A$ and $B$ so as to maximize the total weight of the points contained in the symmetric difference of $A$ and $B$. The same optimization framework can easily be adapted to solve related problems such as to maximize the total weight in the symmetric difference of $k \geq 3$ boxes and/or in the union of $k \geq 2$ boxes.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [251] [Temporal Contrastive Transformer for Financial Crime Detection: Self-Supervised Sequence Embeddings via Predictive Contrastive Coding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21490)
*Danny Butvinik,Yonit Marcus,Nitzan Tal,Gabrielle Azoulay*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Temporal Contrastive Transformer (TCT) learns self-supervised, temporal representations of transaction sequences for fraud detection; embeddings show predictive value, but provide limited additive benefit over strong engineered features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce reliance on manual feature engineering by learning representations that capture temporal behavioral patterns in financial transactions for fraud detection.

Method: Train a Transformer-based encoder using a self-supervised contrastive objective to produce embeddings; evaluate by feeding embeddings into a gradient boosting classifier; compare performance of embeddings alone versus embeddings plus domain-engineered features.

Result: Embeddings alone achieve AUC 0.8644; embeddings plus engineered features do not improve over baseline (0.9205 vs 0.9245), indicating the learned representations overlap substantially with existing features; the approach captures non-trivial temporal structure but offers limited additive value over strong domain features.

Conclusion: TCT is a promising direction for temporal representation learning in financial crime detection, but faces challenges in delivering additive performance over engineered features; the work underscores early-stage development and potential to reduce manual feature engineering with further architectural and objective refinements.

Abstract: We introduce the Temporal Contrastive Transformer (TCT), a representation learning framework designed to capture contextual temporal dynamics in sequences of financial transactions. The model is trained using a self-supervised contrastive objective to produce embeddings that encode behavioral patterns over time, with the goal of supporting downstream fraud detection tasks. We evaluate TCT in a realistic setting by using the learned embeddings as input features to a gradient boosting classifier. Experimental results show that embeddings alone achieve meaningful predictive performance (AUC 0.8644), indicating that the model captures non-trivial temporal structure. However, when combined with domain-engineered features, no measurable improvement is observed over the baseline (AUC 0.9205 vs. 0.9245), suggesting that the learned representations largely overlap with existing feature abstractions. These findings position TCT as a promising representation learning approach that captures relevant behavioral signal, while highlighting the challenges of achieving additive value over strong domain features. The results reflect an intermediate stage in the development of temporal representation learning for financial crime detection and motivate further research on model architecture, training objectives, and integration strategies. At this early stage, achieving performance comparable to a strong feature-engineered baseline is itself a meaningful outcome, indicating that learned representations approximate domain-specific features without manual engineering. While not yet production-ready, these results point to a promising direction for reducing reliance on feature engineering in financial crime detection.

</details>


### [252] [Teaching Language Models to Forecast Research Success Through Comparative Idea Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21491)
*Srujan P Mule,Aniketh Garikaparthi,Manasi Patwardhan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Small language models, after supervised fine-tuning (and with RL-based prompts), can predict which research ideas will perform better, serving as scalable verifiers for autonomous discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The bottleneck is evaluating hundreds of AI-generated hypotheses; need objective, scalable forecasting of empirical success before experiments.

Method: Create 11,488 idea pairs from PapersWithCode outcomes; evaluate 8B LMs; apply supervised fine-tuning (SFT); frame evaluation as Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR); perform ablations and cross-domain/time-split tests.

Result: Off-the-shelf 8B models ~30% accuracy; SFT yields 77.1% accuracy, surpassing GPT-5's 61.1%; RLVR achieves 71.35% accuracy with interpretable justifications; robust to surface heuristics and transferable to time-split and independent test sets.

Conclusion: Compute-efficient small LMs can function as effective, objective verifiers to accelerate autonomous scientific discovery.

Abstract: As language models accelerate scientific research by automating hypothesis generation and implementation, a new bottleneck emerges: evaluating and filtering hundreds of AI-generated ideas without exhaustive experimentation. We ask whether LMs can learn to forecast the empirical success of research ideas before any experiments are run. We study comparative empirical forecasting: given a benchmark-specific research goal and two candidate ideas, predict which will achieve better benchmark performance. We construct a dataset of 11,488 idea pairs grounded in objective outcomes from PapersWithCode. While off-the-shelf 8B-parameter models struggle (30% acc.), SFT dramatically boosts performance to 77.1%, outperforming GPT-5 (61.1%). By framing evaluation as a reasoning task via Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), we train models to discover latent reasoning paths, achieving 71.35% acc. with interpretable justifications. Through additional ablations and out-of-distribution tests, we show robustness to surface-level heuristics and transfer to both a cross-domain time-split test set and an independently constructed test set. Our results demonstrate that compute-efficient small language models can serve as effective, objective verifiers, offering a scalable path for autonomous scientific discovery.

</details>


### [253] [The Attribution Impossibility: No Feature Ranking Is Faithful, Stable, and Complete Under Collinearity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21492)
*Drake Caraker,Bryan Arnold,David Rhoads*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Impossibility result for feature attribution under collinearity; introduces DASH ensemble to achieve stability; formal verification in Lean 4; two-family design space; empirical evidence of instability; implications for fairness auditing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explainable AI attribution under collinearity; provide quantitative theory, a practical stable method, and formal verification; assess limitations of SHAP and conditional SHAP; contribute to auditing reliability.

Method: Theoretical impossibility proofs; quantitative characterization across four model classes; propose DASH ensemble; formal verification in Lean 4 (305 theorems); practical diagnostics (Z-test, single-model screening).

Result: Attribution design space consists of two families only; unstable faithful-complete methods vs stable DASH-like ensembles; DASH Pareto-optimal among unbiased aggregations; variance bound; ensemble size formula; empirical survey shows 68% instability; conditional SHAP does not escape; 77 datasets; 305 Lean theorems.

Conclusion: Directed implications: SHAP-based proxy discrimination audits unreliable under collinearity; need careful method choice; formal impossibility established; DASH provides practical and provable improvements; first formally verified impossibility in explainable AI.

Abstract: No feature ranking can be simultaneously faithful, stable, and complete when features are collinear. For collinear pairs, ranking reduces to a coin flip. We prove this impossibility, quantify it for four model classes, resolve it via ensemble averaging (DASH), and machine-verify it with 305 Lean 4 theorems. We characterize the complete attribution design space: exactly two families of methods exist -- faithful-complete methods (unstable, with rankings that flip up to 50% of the time) and ensemble methods like DASH (stable, reporting ties for symmetric features) -- and no method lies outside this dichotomy. The impossibility is quantitative: the attribution ratio diverges as 1/(1-rho^2) for gradient boosting, is infinite for Lasso, and converges for random forests. DASH (Diversified Aggregation of SHAP) is provably Pareto-optimal among unbiased aggregations, achieving the Cramer-Rao variance bound with a tight ensemble size formula. In a survey of 77 public datasets, 68% exhibit attribution instability. Switching to conditional SHAP does not escape the impossibility when features have equal causal effects. The framework includes practical diagnostics -- a Z-test workflow and single-model screening tool -- and has direct consequences for fairness auditing: SHAP-based proxy discrimination audits are provably unreliable under collinearity. The design space theorem, diagnostics, and impossibility are mechanically verified in Lean 4 (305 theorems from 16 axioms, 0 sorry) -- to our knowledge, the first formally verified impossibility in explainable AI.

</details>


### [254] [Don't Collapse Your Features: Why CenterLoss Hurts OOD Detection and Multi-Scale Mahalanobis Wins](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21493)
*Rahul D Ray*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GOEN is a geometry-focused OOD detection pipeline that optimizes epistemic uncertainty rather than classification accuracy. It combines multi-scale features, L2 normalization, Mahalanobis distance, and a calibration head trained on hard OOD examples. CenterLoss hurts OOD performance; GOEN-NoCenterLoss achieves state-of-the-art OOD AUROC (~0.948) on CIFAR-10, beating deep ensembles, KNN, and ODIN, with efficient training (<20 minutes on a single GPU).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classification-optimized feature geometry may distort covariance structures and inter-class margins that are crucial for reliable epistemic uncertainty estimation. There is a need for representations and procedures specifically tuned for OOD detection.

Method: Build GOEN by aggregating multi-scale features, applying L2 normalization, and using Mahalanobis distance with a calibration head trained on real hard OOD examples. Conduct ablations showing CenterLoss degrades OOD performance. The best variant, GOEN-NoCenterLoss, excludes CenterLoss and achieves state-of-the-art OOD AUROC.

Result: GOEN-NoCenterLoss attains an average OOD AUROC of 0.9483 on CIFAR-10, surpassing baselines: deep ensembles 0.8827, KNN 0.8967, ODIN 0.8870; in-distribution accuracy remains competitive. CenterLoss degrades OOD AUROC from 0.9483 to 0.9366.

Conclusion: The work challenges the assumption that improved classification geometry improves epistemic uncertainty. Overly tight feature clustering can compress inter-class margins and distort covariance essential for OOD detection. GOEN provides an efficient, practical blueprint for reliable OOD detection (training under 20 minutes on a single GPU).

Abstract: The ability to detect out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs is fundamental to safe deployment of machine learning systems. Yet, current methods often rely on feature representations that are optimised solely for classification accuracy, neglecting the distinct requirements of epistemic uncertainty. We introduce GOEN (Geometry-Optimised Epistemic Network), a simple pipeline that combines multi-scale features, L2 normalisation, Mahalanobis distance, and a calibration head trained with real hard OOD examples. Through systematic ablation we uncover a counter-intuitive finding: CenterLoss, a popular regulariser for feature compactness, significantly degrades OOD detection performance, reducing average OOD AUROC from 0.9483 to 0.9366 despite improving classification accuracy. The best variant, GOEN-NoCenterLoss, achieves an average OOD AUROC of 0.9483, surpassing all baselines including deep ensembles (0.8827), KNN (0.8967), and ODIN (0.8870) on CIFAR-10 benchmarks, while maintaining competitive in-distribution accuracy. Our results challenge the prevailing assumption that better classification geometry automatically leads to better epistemic uncertainty. Instead, we show that overly tight feature clusters compress inter-class margins and distort the covariance structure needed for effective OOD detection. GOEN is efficient, training in under 20 minutes on a single GPU, and provides a practical blueprint for building AI systems that reliably recognise their own limitations.

</details>


### [255] [Double descent for least-squares interpolation on contaminated data: A simulation study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21494)
*Tino Werner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: In a linear regression setting with contaminated training data, the double descent phenomenon can occur with high overparameterization, and a highly overparameterized least-squares interpolator can generalize very well, outperforming several robust alternatives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether the double descent phenomenon persists under data contamination and how robust estimators compare to simple LS interpolation in an overparameterized regime.

Method: Empirical/analytic comparison across varying model complexity in linear regression with contaminated data, contrasting least-squares interpolation with multiple robust estimators to assess generalization performance.

Result: Finding: large overparameterization yields a double descent in generalization error, with the least-squares interpolator achieving very good generalization, surpassing the robust alternatives.

Conclusion: Double descent persists under data contamination in this linear setting, and interpolation-based LS can outperform robust methods in terms of generalization when overparameterization is large; robustness advantages may be outweighed in highly overparameterized regimes depending on contamination level.

Abstract: Overparametrized models can exhibit an excellent generalization performance, although they should be prone to overfitting according to classical statistical theory. The discovery of the "double descent", indicating that the generalization error decreases after a certain model complexity has been reached, opened a new line of research. Robust statistics considers statistical estimation on contaminated data, which, due to assumptions that do not hold on real data, let data points appear as outliers w.r.t. the assumed "ideal" distribution, potentially severely distorting any classical estimator. We address the question whether a double descent phenomenon can be observed in a linear regression setting with contaminated training data. We compare the performance of the highly non-robust least-squares interpolation estimator with several robust alternatives. It turns out that large overparametrization indeed allows for a double descent phenomenon, resulting in a very good generalization performance of the least-squares interpolator, surpassing that of the robust alternatives.

</details>


### [256] [HealthCraft: A Reinforcement Learning Safety Environment for Emergency Medicine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21496)
*Brandon Dent*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HealthCraft: a public RL environment to evaluate trajectory-level safety of frontier LMs in emergency medicine; reveals substantial safety failures and large drops in multi-step workflows; shows infrastructure fidelity strongly affects evaluation and training signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static medical QA benchmarks miss critical failure modes in emergency care (trajectory-level safety, tool misuse, sustained clinical pressure). A realistic, scalable evaluation is needed to push frontier LMs toward safe clinical deployment.

Method: Adapt Corecraft into healthcare: FHIR R4 world state (14 entity types, 3,987 seed entities), 24 MCP tools; dual-layer safety rubric; 195 tasks across six categories (2,255 binary criteria; 515 safety-critical). Post-hoc slate adds 10 negative-class tasks (total 205 tasks, 2,337 criteria). Deterministic LLM-judge overlay; 60-run negative-class smoke pilot; 2 frontier-model evaluation (Claude Opus 4.6, GPT-5.4). Results reported with Pass@1 and safety-failure rates; multi-step workflows show near-zero performance; infrastructure bugs fixed between pilots v2 and v8; coupling to Megatron+SGLang+GRPO loop per Corecraft. Environment, tasks, rubrics, harness released under Apache 2.0.

Result: Claude Opus 4.6 Pass@1 = 24.8% [21.5–28.4], GPT-5.4 Pass@1 = 12.6% [10.2–15.6]; safety-failure rates = 27.5% and 34.0%. In multi-step workflows, Claude ~1.0%, GPT-5.4 ~0.0%. Infrastructure fixes reordered perceived model strength; deterministic judge bounds evaluator noise; 60-run smoke pilot shows reward signal is not training-safe (restraint criteria pass at 0.929 prevalence).

Conclusion: HealthCraft provides a scalable, Apache-licensed RL evaluation platform for trajectory-level safety in emergency medicine. Findings show substantial safety risks and collapse under complex workflows, highlighting the importance of infrastructure fidelity and the need to design rewards that do not leak into training. The work lays groundwork for integration with Corecraft-style loops and motivates safer deployment practices in frontline clinical AI.

Abstract: Frontier language models are being deployed into clinical workflows faster than the infrastructure to evaluate them safely. Static medical-QA benchmarks miss the failure modes that matter in emergency medicine: trajectory-level safety collapse, tool misuse, and capitulation under sustained clinical pressure. We present HealthCraft, the first public reinforcement-learning environment that rewards trajectory-level safety under realistic emergency-medicine conditions, adapted from Corecraft. It is built on a FHIR R4 world state with 14 entity types and 3,987 seed entities, exposes 24 MCP tools, and defines a dual-layer rubric that zeroes reward whenever any safety-critical criterion is violated. We release 195 tasks across six categories, graded against 2,255 binary criteria (515 safety-critical); a post-hoc 10-task negative-class slate extends this to 205 tasks and 2,337 criteria. V8 results on two frontier models show Claude Opus 4.6 at Pass@1 24.8% [21.5-28.4] and GPT-5.4 at 12.6% [10.2-15.6], with safety-failure rates of 27.5% and 34.0%. On multi-step workflows - the closest proxy to real emergency care - performance collapses to near zero (Claude 1.0%, GPT-5.4 0.0%) despite partial competence on individual steps.
