Six infrastructure bugs fixed between pilots v2 and v8 re-ordered which model "looks stronger," evidence that infrastructure fidelity is part of the measurement. A deterministic LLM-judge overlay bounds evaluator noise, and a 60-run negative-class smoke pilot shows the reward signal is not drop-in training-safe: restraint criteria pass at 0.929 prevalence, a gameability an eval harness can tolerate but a training reward cannot. We scaffold coupling to a Megatron+SGLang+GRPO loop per Corecraft Section 5.2 and leave training-reward ablations as future work. Environment, tasks, rubrics, and harness are released under Apache 2.0.

</details>


### [257] [Predicting Performance of Symbolic and Prompt Programs with Examples](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21515)
*Chengqi Zheng,Keya Hu,Shuzhi Liu,Tao Wu,Kevin Ellis,Yewen Pu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Summary generation failed


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable

Method: Method extraction failed

Result: Result analysis unavailable

Conclusion: Conclusion extraction failed

Abstract: LLM prompting is widely used for naturally stated tasks, yet it is unreliable it may succeed on a few test cases but fail at deployment time. We study performance prediction: given a program, either symbolic (e.g. Python) or a prompt executed on an LLM, and a few in-domain examples, predict its performance on unseen tasks from the same domain. We use a simple coin-flip model, treating each pass/fail program execution as a Bernoulli random variable, whose success probability is the programs unknown performance. In this model, performance depends entirely on: 1) the observed execution outcomes on test cases, and 2) a prior over performances. We compile empirical performance priors from a corpus of diverse programs and tasks, and find that performance for symbolic programs (e.g., Python) are all or nothing, while prompt programs have a diffuse prior with many nearly-correct programs. This difference explains why a few passing tests can certify symbolic programs but not prompt programs. Building on this insight, we develop RAP (Retrieved Approximate Prior), which retrieves similar tasks and prompt programs from an existing corpus to construct a proxy prior, which is then used to predict performance. We show RAP achieves solid performances.

</details>


### [258] [Harnesses for Inference-Time Alignment over Execution Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21516)
*Boyuan Wang,Bochao Li,Minghan Wang,Yuxin Tao,Fang Kong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Harness design for LLM agents balances task decomposition and guided execution; more structured approaches can help or hurt, depending on granularity and guidance. Partial harnesses—specifying only the initial steps—can outperform fully structured workflows by preserving agent autonomy. Key failure modes include over-decomposition, over-pruning, and hallucinated execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how inference-time harnesses constrain and enable long-horizon performance, and to characterize the limits and failure modes of task decomposition and guided execution in LLM-driven agents.

Method: Proposes an inference-time trajectory alignment framework that separates harness design into two mechanisms: task decomposition (sub-goal structuring) and guided execution (action distribution shaping). Quantifies effects of workflow granularity, retry budgets, and guidance-induced action reweighting. Validates predictions with controlled synthetic experiments and real terminal-agent benchmarks.

Result: Identifies concrete failure modes (over-decomposition, over-pruning, hallucinated execution) and shows that partial harnesses can achieve higher pass rates than fully structured workflows, supported by synthetic and real-world experiments.

Conclusion: Harness design is regime-dependent; careful calibration of decomposition and guidance can improve reliability. Partial specification—leaving later steps to the agent—can be advantageous. The work offers design principles and empirical validation for constructing effective harnesses in inference-time LLM systems.

Abstract: Harness engineering has emerged as an important inference-time technique for large language model (LLM) agents, aiming to improve long-term performance through task decomposition and guided execution. However, more elaborate harnesses are not uniformly better: increasing decomposition or guidance can sometimes improve execution, but can also reduce final task success. We study harness design through the lens of inference-time trajectory alignment. This perspective separates harness into two mechanisms: task decomposition, which structures a task into sub-goals, and guided execution, which reshapes local action distributions during execution. This decomposition allows us to quantify how workflow granularity, retry budgets, and guidance-induced action reweighting shape the performance limits of harness design. It further reveals concrete failure modes, including over-decomposition, over-pruning, and hallucinated execution. We validate these predictions through controlled synthetic experiments and real terminal agent benchmarks. Inspired by the theory, we further show that effective harnesses can be partial: specifying only the initial steps and leaving the remaining execution to agent can achieve higher pass rate than fully structured workflows.

</details>


### [259] [A Reproducible Log-Driven AutoML Framework for Interpretable Pipeline Optimization in Healthcare Risk Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21528)
*Rui Huang,Lican Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A deterministic, log-driven AutoML framework (yvsoucom-iterkit) analyzes 18k pipeline configurations for disease risk prediction on Pima and Stroke datasets, revealing a small set of high-impact components (augmentation, model choice, imbalance handling) and strong ensemble performance with dataset-specific trade-offs due to class imbalance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address reproducibility and interpretability in AutoML for heterogeneous biomedical data with limited samples and imbalanced classes; provide component-level attribution and redundancy insights.

Method: Proposes yvsoucom-iterkit: a fully reproducible, configuration-level AutoML framework; each pipeline encoded as a log entry; uses Random Forest importance, component similarity analysis, cross-seed robustness analysis; experiments across 18k configurations on two datasets; ensemble models; analysis of augmentation, imbalance handling, feature selection variants, mixup, TomekLinks, Gaussian noise; cross-seed analysis.

Result: Strong and stable performance: Pima Weighted-F1 0.89, Macro-F1 0.88; Stroke Weighted-F1 0.94; Macro-F1 0.67 due to imbalance; redundancy in components; key drivers quantified; low RMS distances indicate redundancy; cross-seed robustness 0.023-0.026 for ensembles vs SVM; concluded that optimizing AutoML can focus on a reduced set of high-impact components.

Conclusion: AutoML optimization can focus on a small subset of high-impact components to achieve robust, reproducible performance; ensemble models provide stability; dataset imbalance affects dataset-specific macro performance; the traceable log framework enables component-level attribution and insight into interactions.

Abstract: Accurate and reproducible disease risk prediction remains challenging due to heterogeneous features, limited samples, and severe class imbalance. This study introduces yvsoucom-iterkit, a deterministic and log-driven automated machine learning framework that formulates pipeline optimization as a fully reproducible, configuration-level system. Each pipeline is encoded as a traceable log entity, enabling analysis of component attribution, interactions, similarity, and cross-seed robustness. Experiments on the Pima Indians Diabetes and Stroke datasets across more than 18,000 pipeline configurations reveal a structured and partially redundant search space, where performance is governed by a small subset of interacting components. Random Forest importance analysis identifies augmentation (0.454), model choice (0.198), and imbalance handling (0.101) as key drivers on Pima, while imbalance handling dominates Stroke (0.406). Component similarity analysis shows strong redundancy, with feature selection variants (biMax-biMean) exhibiting low RMS distance (0.0252), mixup closely matching no augmentation (0.0279), and TomekLinks aligning with no imbalance handling (0.0325), whereas Gaussian noise shows greater divergence from no augmentation (0.10). The framework achieves strong and stable performance using ensemble models (Weighted-F1 0.89, Macro-F1 0.88 on Pima; Weighted-F1 0.94 on Stroke), while Macro-F1 remains lower on Stroke (0.67) due to class imbalance. Cross-seed analysis reveals a performance-robustness trade-off, with ensembles showing lower variability (0.023-0.026) than SVM. These results indicate that effective AutoML optimization can focus on a reduced set of high-impact components.

</details>


### [260] [DualOptim+: Bridging Shared and Decoupled Optimizer States for Better Machine Unlearning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21539)
*Xuyang Zhong,Qizhang Li,Yiwen Guo,Chen Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DualOptim+ is a hierarchical optimization framework for machine unlearning in LLMs, using a base state for shared representations and delta states for objective-specific residuals, with a quantized 8-bit variant. It yields improved trade-offs across forgetting and retaining objectives, with public code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve machine unlearning in large language models by balancing forgetting and retaining objectives, while reducing memory overhead; existing methods struggle with gradient conflicts and memory costs.

Method: Introduce a base state capturing shared representations; delta states capturing objective-specific residuals; adaptively bridge shared and decoupled states based on directional conflict between forgetting and retaining gradients; add DualOptim+8bit as a quantized variant; evaluate across fictitious and real-world unlearning, safety alignment, and multi-task learning.

Result: Demonstrates superior trade-offs across unlearning and related objectives; DualOptim+8bit reduces memory overhead without compromising performance; code available at the provided GitHub repository.

Conclusion: DualOptim+offers an effective multi-objective optimization framework for machine unlearning in LLMs, with an efficient 8-bit variant that improves practicality; public implementation supports reproducibility.

Abstract: We propose DualOptim+, a novel optimization framework for improving machine unlearning in large language models. It introduces a base state to capture common representations shared by forgetting and retaining objectives and delta states to preserve objective-specific residuals. This architecture allows the optimizer to adaptively bridge shared and decoupled states based on the directional conflict between forgetting and retaining gradients. We further introduce DualOptim+ 8bit, a quantized variant that reduces memory overhead without compromising performance. Extensive experiments across fictitious and real-world unlearning, safety alignment, and multi-task learning tasks demonstrate that DualOptim+ consistently achieves a superior trade-off between different objectives. Codes are available at https://github.com/CityU-MLO/DualOptimPlus.

</details>


### [261] [Discovering Entity-Conditioned Lag Heterogeneity: A Lag-Gated Neural Audit Framework for Panel Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21542)
*Andi Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AC-GATE introduces an adaptive-conditioning encoder with a scale-invariant lag gate to discover entity-specific, heterogeneous lags in country-level panel data, producing auditable lag outputs rather than post-hoc explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Researchers need auditable, entity-specific lag summaries in temporal panels to understand how different entities respond to historical signals across various time horizons; current methods offer limited, non-auditable lag discovery.

Method: Propose AC-GATE (Adaptive-Conditioning Encoder with a Scale-Invariant Lag Gate) that uses observable entity-level proxies to condition lag-weight distributions over historical observations, realizing conditional Moderated Distributed Lag as a structural model output. Evaluation uses a layered audit protocol with a synthetic panel (ground-truth lags) and two real-world country panels for external audit and stress testing.

Result: AC-GATE successfully recovers heterogeneous lag structure in synthetic data and yields non-degenerate, externally structured effective lags in real data.

Conclusion: AC-GATE offers structural, auditable lag discovery in panel data by turning lag weights into model outputs governed by entity conditioning, validated through mechanism recovery and external audits on synthetic and real panels.

Abstract: Country-level temporal panels are widely used in empirical analysis. Researchers often need to audit how different entities respond to historical signals over different time horizons. Current approaches typically do not provide directly auditable entity-specific lag summaries. We formulate entity-conditioned heterogeneous lag discovery as a temporal panel mining task and propose AC-GATE, an Adaptive-Conditioning Encoder with a Scale-Invariant Lag Gate. It instantiates conditional Moderated Distributed Lag by using observable entity-level proxies to condition lag-weight distributions over historical observations, thereby making effective lags structural outputs of the model rather than post-hoc explanations. The evaluation is based on a layered audit protocol that separates predictive calibration from lag discovery. A synthetic panel with known ground-truth lags is used for mechanism recovery testing, and two real-world country-level panels are used for external audit and stress testing. The results show that AC-GATE can recover heterogeneous lag structure in synthetic data, and generates non-degenerate, externally structured effective lags in real data.

</details>


### [262] [Provable Joint Decontamination for Benchmarking Multiple Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21543)
*Zhenlong Liu,Hao Zeng,Hongxin Wei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: JECS: a joint conformal selection framework to control global contamination rate in multi-model benchmarks by aggregating per-model p-values into a max-p envelope and applying false-discovery control, yielding higher power than a simple max-p baseline while preserving GCR control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmark data contamination inflates reported LLM performance and undermines cross-model fairness. Existing per-model conformal methods lack multi-model global guarantees. A joint method is needed to provide provable control of contamination across models.

Method: Compute per-model conformal p-values for each item, aggregate with the per-item maximum, reconstruct a conservative max-p null distribution envelope from right-tail data above a data-driven threshold, and apply adaptive Benjamini-Hochberg to envelope-rescaled p-values to select benchmarks with a provable global contamination rate (GCR) guarantee.

Result: JECS achieves greater statistical power than a max-p baseline in identifying contaminated items while maintaining the target GCR across diverse models and benchmarks in extensive experiments.

Conclusion: JECS provides a principled, multi-model benchmark decontamination procedure with provable GCR control, improving cross-model fairness and reliability of LLM benchmark evaluations by enabling tighter, globally controlled contamination screening.

Abstract: Benchmark data contamination has become a central challenge in LLM evaluation: when evaluation examples appear in the training data of one or more audited models, reported performance can be inflated and cross-model comparisons become unreliable. A broad line of training-data detection work designs scores to quantify how strongly a model memorizes a given data point, but these score-based methods lack theoretical guarantees. Recent conformal approaches provide provable false-identification control for a single model; however, applying them separately to each model can produce model-specific benchmarks, undermining fair comparison across models. In this work, we formalize multi-model benchmark decontamination as a joint selection problem and propose Joint Envelope Conformal Selection (JECS), a conformal procedure that enables global contamination rate (GCR) control under stated assumptions. Specifically, JECS computes per-model conformal p-values, aggregates them by the per-item maximum, and reconstructs a conservative envelope of the max-p null distribution from right-tail observations above a data-driven threshold. By applying the adaptive Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) procedure to the envelope-rescaled values, we select a benchmark with provable GCR control. Extensive experiments across various models and benchmarks demonstrate that JECS achieves higher power than the max-p baseline while consistently maintaining the target GCR control.

</details>


### [263] [Tabular foundation models for robust calibration of near-infrared chemical sensing data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21544)
*Robin Reiter,Denis Cornet,Fabien Michel,Lauriane Rouan,Gregory Beurier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TabPFN-based tabular models show competitive calibration performance for NIR spectroscopy across regression and classification, with preprocessing giving an edge in regression and raw spectra giving an edge in classification; promising complement to chemometrics in small-to-medium datasets, but robustness to outliers/extrapolation remains a caveat.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of calibrating NIR sensors—high-dimensional, collinear spectra, limited samples, preprocessing dependence, spectral outliers, and extrapolation—by testing whether tabular foundation models can offer a new calibration strategy.

Method: Benchmark on 66 NIR datasets (54 regression, 12 classification). Compare direct inference on raw spectra vs preprocessing-optimized inference against PLS/PLS-DA, Ridge, CatBoost, and CNN-1D. A unified validation framework ensures preprocessing and model selection are trained only on calibration data before external test evaluation; include robustness analyses for spectral outliers and extrapolated samples.

Result: In regression, preprocessing-optimized TabPFN achieves the best average rank and significantly outperforms PLS, CatBoost, TabPFN on raw spectra, and CNN-1D, while remaining statistically comparable to Ridge. In classification, TabPFN applied to raw spectra provides the best average rank, with performance close to the optimized variant. Robustness analyses show strong average predictive performance but diminished advantage on spectral outliers and extrapolation, where classical chemometric models remain competitive.

Conclusion: Tabular foundation models can complement established chemometric workflows for NIR chemical sensing, particularly in small- to medium-sized calibration settings, while underscoring the need for spectroscopy-specific priors and uncertainty-aware deployment strategies.

Abstract: Near-infrared spectroscopy is increasingly used as a rapid, non-destructive chemical sensing technology for the analysis of food, pharmaceutical, biological, and environmental samples. However, the practical deployment of NIR sensors still depends on calibration models able to handle high-dimensional, collinear spectra, limited sample sizes, preprocessing dependence, spectral outliers, and extrapolation beyond the calibration domain. Here, we evaluate whether tabular foundation models can provide a new calibration strategy for NIR chemical sensing. We benchmark TabPFN on 66 NIR datasets covering 54 regression and 12 classification tasks, and compare direct inference on raw spectra with preprocessing-optimized inference against PLS/PLS-DA, Ridge, Catboost, and one-dimensional convolutional neural networks. The study uses a unified validation framework in which preprocessing and model selection are performed exclusively on calibration data before external test evaluation. In regression, preprocessing-optimized TabPFN achieves the best overall average rank and significantly outperforms PLS, CatBoost, TabPFN on raw spectra, and CNN-1D, while remaining statistically comparable to Ridge. In classification, TabPFN applied directly to raw spectra provides the best average rank, with performance close to the optimized variant. Robustness analyses show that TabPFN provides strong average predictive performance but that its advantage decreases on spectral outliers and extrapolated samples, where classical chemometric models remain competitive. These results suggest that tabular foundation models can complement established chemometric workflows for NIR chemical sensing, especially in small- to medium-sized calibration settings, while highlighting the need for spectroscopy-specific priors and uncertainty-aware deployment strategies.

</details>


### [264] [Expectation Consistency Loss: Rethink Confidence Calibration under Covariate Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21552)
*Jinzong Dong,Zhaohui Jiang,Bo Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces an Expectation consistency condition for confidence calibration under covariate shift, and an unsupervised domain adaptation loss (ECL) that calibrates target-domain confidence with compatibility to canonical, class-wise, and top-label calibration. Proves ECL has the same sample complexity as ECE and provides a mini-batch trainable scheme; validated on simulated and real covariate-shift datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Calibration methods typically assume i.i.d. data; under covariate shift they degrade, and existing covariate-shift calibration methods struggle with class-wise and canonical calibrations and rely on unstable density ratio estimates. A weaker, necessary-and-sufficient condition for calibration under covariate shift is sought to enable practical, unsupervised domain adaptation.

Method: Derive a necessary and sufficient condition, named Expectation consistency condition, for confidence calibration under covariate shift. Propose Expectation consistency loss (ECL) as an unsupervised domain adaptation objective to calibrate the target domain’s confidence, compatible with canonical, class-wise, and top-label calibrations. Establish that computing ECL has the same sample complexity as Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and provide a theoretically grounded mini-batch training scheme. Validate on simulated and real-world covariate-shift datasets.

Result: The paper theoretically grounds confidence calibration under covariate shift via the Expectation consistency condition, introduces ECL for unsupervised domain adaptation, demonstrates parity in sample complexity with ECE, and empirically validates effectiveness on both synthetic and real covariate-shift datasets.

Conclusion: Expectation consistency provides a weaker yet sufficient criterion for calibration under covariate shift, enabling a practical ECL approach that is calibration-compatible and sample-efficient; the method is validated empirically and offers a principled framework for confidence calibration under distributional shifts.

Abstract: Confidence calibration for classification models is vital in safety-critical decision-making scenarios and has received extensive attention. General confidence calibration methods assume training and test data are independent and identically distributed, limiting their effectiveness under covariate shifts. Previous calibration methods under covariate shift struggle with class-wise or canonical calibrations and often rely on unstable importance weighting when density ratios are large or unbounded. Given the above limitations, this paper rethinks confidence calibration under covariate shifts. First, we derive a necessary and sufficient condition for confidence calibration under covariate shifts, named Expectation consistency condition, which reveals covariate shifts do not necessarily lead to uncalibrated confidence and provides a weaker condition for confidence calibration than global covariate distribution alignment. Then, utilizing Expectation consistency condition, this paper proposes an unsupervised domain adaptation loss to calibrate confidence of the target domain, named Expectation consistency loss (ECL), which is compatible with canonical calibration, class-wise calibration, and top-label calibration. Third, we prove that computing ECL loss has the same sample complexity as Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and provide a theoretically grounded mini-batch trainable scheme for ECL loss. Finally, we validate the effectiveness of our method on both simulated and real-world covariate shift datasets.

</details>


### [265] [Beyond Single Slot: Joint Optimization for Multi-Slot Guaranteed Display Advertising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21556)
*Zhaoqi Zhang,Jiaming Deng,Miao Xie,Linyou Cai,Qianlong Xie,Xingxing Wang,Siqiang Luo,Gao Cong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Joint optimization framework for multi-slot guaranteed display advertising using offline bipartite matching with a contract roulette mechanism and Page View constraints, enabling scalable deployment and improved revenue metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-slot GD methods fail to optimize across multi-slot pages, leading to slot-level redundancy, contract imbalance, and exposure concentration. A multi-slot, constraint-aware framework is needed for better merchant ROI and platform revenue robustness.

Method: Formulates allocation as an offline bipartite matching problem. Introduces a contract roulette mechanism to enforce slot exclusivity and Page View constraints to control impressions. Develops a scalable allocation optimization algorithm for large-scale deployment. Validates via online experiments on Meituan's platform.

Result: Significant online improvements: 28.99% increase in Average Revenue Per User (ARPU) under 70% traffic. Difference-in-differences analysis shows improved contract stability and robustness of contract fulfillment, along with enhanced merchant ROI and platform revenue efficiency.

Conclusion: The proposed joint optimization framework effectively handles multi-slot GD allocation, with strong empirical support in real-world deployments, indicating wide applicability and scalability for platform monetization.

Abstract: Guaranteed display advertising is crucial for platform monetization, yet existing methods often operate under a single-slot assumption, limiting their ability to optimize allocation across multi-slot page views. In this paper, we propose a novel joint optimization framework for multi-slot GD allocation, addressing key challenges such as slot-level redundancy, contract imbalance, and exposure concentration. Our approach formulates the allocation as an offline bipartite matching problem with a contract roulette mechanism for slot exclusivity and Page View constraints for impression control, and incorporates a scalable allocation optimization algorithm for efficient large-scale deployment. Extensive online tests on the Meituan advertising platform demonstrate that our method significantly improves merchant ROI, platform revenue efficiency, and contract fulfillment robustness. Specifically, online A/B tests show a 28.99% increase in Average Revenue Per User under 70% traffic, and DID analysis further indicates improved contract stability, demonstrating the strong applicability and effectiveness of our framework in real-world advertising deployments.

</details>


### [266] [From Parameters to Data: A Task-Parameter-Guided Fine-Tuning Pipeline for Efficient LLM Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21558)
*Hao Chen,Qi Zhang,Liyao Li,Zhanming Shen,Wentao Ye,Lirong Gao,Ningtao Wang,Xing Fu,Xiaoyu Shen,Junbo Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: P2D unifies data selection and parameter-efficient fine-tuning by using task-sensitive attention heads as a dual filter, enabling data mining and pruning with limited updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient domain adaptation of LLMs is hampered by high data/computational costs; treating data selection and fine-tuning as separate tasks may miss synergies.

Method: The Strong Map Hypothesis posits a sparse set of attention heads dominates task adaptation. P2D identifies these heads via a lightweight proxy, then uses them to curate high-affinity data and prune the model; introduces Alignment Efficiency Ratio (AER) to quantify end-to-end pipeline cost; demonstrates updating 10% heads on 10% data.

Result: Achieves 8.3 percentage-point improvement over strong baselines and 7x end-to-end time speedup in a limited setting.

Conclusion: Parameter-data synchronization can eliminate redundancy and enable efficient alignment; P2D offers a unified, joint approach to data selection and pruning.

Abstract: Adapting Large Language Models (LLMs) to specialized domains typically incurs high data and computational overhead. While prior efficiency efforts have largely treated data selection and parameter-efficient fine-tuning as isolated processes, our empirical analysis suggests they may be intrinsically coupled. We posit the Strong Map Hypothesis: a sparse subset of attention heads plays a dominant role in task-specific adaptation, acting as keys that unlock specific data patterns. Building on this observation, we propose From Parameters to Data (P2D), a unified framework that leverages these task-sensitive attention heads as a dual compass for both sample mining and structural pruning. To rigorously quantify the total pipeline cost, we introduce the Alignment Efficiency Ratio (AER) metric for both selection latency and training time. Mechanistically, P2D identifies critical heads via a lightweight proxy and uses them as a functional filter to curate high-affinity data, establishing a synergistic pipeline. Empirically, by updating merely 10% of attention heads on 10% of the data, P2D achieves an 8.3 pp performance gain over strong baselines and delivers a 7.0x end-to-end time speedup. These results validate that precise parameter-data synchronization eliminates redundancy, offering a new paradigm for efficient alignment.

</details>


### [267] [AutoMCU: Feasibility-First MCU Neural Network Customization via LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21560)
*Penglin Dai,Zijie Zhou,Xincao Xu,Junhua Wang,Xiao Wu,Lixin Duan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AutoMCU is an LLM-driven multi-agent system that automates neural network customization for MCU constraints, prioritizing feasibility via hardware-in-the-loop checks and deployability analysis. It reduces customization time from GPU-hours to 1–2 hours and achieves competitive accuracy on CIFAR-10/100, validated on real STM32 MCUs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between architecture design and verified deployment for MCU-scale edge intelligence; existing model compression and HW-NAS rely on proxy metrics and are costly, leaving deployment feasibility uncertain.

Method: Iterative, LLM-guided generation of architecture candidates; hardware-in-the-loop screening against RAM/Flash constraints; vendor toolchain feedback before training; controlled evaluation protocol; deployment analysis via backend-grounded verification; state-isolated multi-agent scheduling for proposal, training, evaluation, deployment phases.

Result: Competitive accuracy on CIFAR-10/100 under strict MCU constraints; customization time ~1–2 hours vs hundreds GPU hours for HW-NAS baselines; outruns ColabNAS and GENIUS on NAS-Bench-201 in stability; validated by real-device deployments on STM32 MCUs.

Conclusion: Feasibility-first, LLM-based multi-agent NAS can effectively tailor models to MCU constraints, delivering deployable, accurate networks with substantial time savings; the approach is validated through both simulations and real hardware deployments.

Abstract: Deploying neural networks on microcontroller units (MCUs) is critical for edge intelligence but remains challenging due to tight memory, storage, and computation constraints. Existing approaches, such as model compression and hardware-aware neural architecture search (HW-NAS), often depend on proxy metrics, incur high search cost, and do not fully bridge the gap between architecture design and verified deployment. This paper presents AutoMCU, a feasibility-first large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent system for automated neural network customization under MCU constraints. Given natural-language task requirements and hardware specifications, AutoMCU iteratively generates structured architecture candidates, filters infeasible designs through vendor toolchain feedback before training, evaluates feasible models under a controlled protocol, and verifies deployability through backend-grounded deployment analysis. AutoMCU includes two key mechanisms: 1) hardware-in-the-loop architecture generation for early elimination of undeployable candidates under RAM and Flash constraints, and 2) state-isolated multi-agent scheduling for stable coordination of proposal, training, evaluation, and deployment stages. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 under strict MCU constraints show that AutoMCU achieves competitive accuracy while reducing customization time to about 1--2 hours, compared with hundreds of GPU hours for representative MCU-oriented HW-NAS baselines. Comparisons with ColabNAS and the LLM-based NAS method GENIUS on NAS-Bench-201 further demonstrate the effectiveness and stability of AutoMCU. Real-device deployments on multiple STM32 microcontrollers validate its practical applicability to MCU-scale edge intelligence.

</details>


### [268] [Objective-Induced Bias and Search Dynamics in Multiobjective Unsupervised Feature Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21561)
*Mathieu Cherpitel,Thomas Bäck,Martijn R. Tannemaat,Anna V. Kononova*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Objective design governs performance in multiobjective unsupervised feature selection; a PCA reconstruction-loss objective yields compact, predictive subsets, while silhouette-based objectives bias toward trivial, low-cardinality solutions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Systematically assess how evaluation objectives, subset-size regularisation direction, and initialisation impact search dynamics and Pareto fronts in unsupervised multiobjective feature selection, using controlled synthetic data with known informative, redundant, and irrelevant features.

Method: Six formulations: combine three objectives (accuracy, silhouette score, PCA reconstruction loss) with subset-size minimisation or maximisation; use a synthetic dataset with known feature types; compare search dynamics, Pareto-front quality, and subset performance on test data.

Result: Formulation choice strongly shapes search dynamics and Pareto-front quality. Silhouette-based objectives bias toward trivial, low-cardinality solutions and are weak proxies for predictive performance. The PCA reconstruction-loss objective yields compact subsets with test accuracy comparable to subsets obtained by supervised accuracy optimization.

Conclusion: Objective design is central to effective multiobjective unsupervised feature selection; selecting a PCA-based objective can produce compact, predictive subsets, whereas silhouette-based objectives may mislead optimization.

Abstract: Unsupervised feature selection is commonly formulated as a multiobjective optimisation problem that jointly optimises subset quality and subset size. Yet the behaviour of this formulation depends critically on the choice of evaluation objective, the direction of subset-size regularisation, and the initialisation strategy. We study these factors in a controlled setting using a synthetic dataset with known informative, redundant, and irrelevant feature types. Six formulations are compared by combining three evaluation objectives: accuracy, silhouette score, and PCA reconstruction loss with subset-size minimisation or maximisation. The results show that formulation strongly affects both search dynamics and the quality of the resulting Pareto front. Silhouette-based formulations exhibit a strong bias toward trivial low-cardinality solutions and remain weak proxies for predictive performance. In contrast, the proposed PCA loss objective produces compact subsets with test accuracy comparable to subsets obtained by directly optimising supervised accuracy. Overall, the study shows that objective design is central to effective multiobjective unsupervised feature selection.

</details>


### [269] [Leveraging Self-Paced Curriculum Learning for Enhanced Modality Balance in Multimodal Conversational Emotion Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21565)
*Phuong-Anh Nguyen,The-Son Le,Duc-Trong Le,Cam-Van Thi Nguyen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a Self-Paced Curriculum Learning (SPCL) framework for Multimodal Emotion Recognition in Conversations (MERC) with a dual-level difficulty measurer and a dynamic learning scheduler, yielding consistent accuracy gains across architectures on IEMOCAP and MELD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address modality misalignment and imbalanced learning in MERC to better leverage multimodal information and improve robustness.

Method: A plug-and-play SPCL module with a dual-level Difficulty Measurer (utterance-level and conversation-level) guiding training from easy to hard via a Learning Scheduler; integrates with existing MERC architectures.

Result: Achieves consistent improvements across architectures and modality settings on IEMOCAP (+1.2% to +6.6% weighted F1) and MELD (up to +10.4%).

Conclusion: SPCL is an effective, lightweight plug-and-play enhancement for MERC, improving modality balance and robustness with good generalization.

Abstract: Multimodal Emotion Recognition in Conversations (MERC) is a crucial task for understanding human interactions, where multimodal approaches integrating language, facial expressions, and vocal tone have achieved significant progress. However, modality misalignment and imbalanced learning remain major challenges, limiting the effective utilization of multimodal information. To address this issue, we propose a plug-and-play framework based on Self-Paced Curriculum Learning (SPCL) for MERC. We introduce a dual-level Difficulty Measurer that captures both utterance-level and conversation-level challenges. The utterance-level score models fine-grained modality-specific difficulty, while the conversation-level score captures broader dialogue structures, including emotional dependencies and modality coherence. Based on these scores, the Learning Scheduler dynamically guides training from easier to more difficult instances. By integrating SPCL into existing MERC architectures, our method alleviates modality imbalance and improves model robustness. Extensive experiments on the IEMOCAP and MELD datasets demonstrate consistent improvements across different architectures and modality settings. On IEMOCAP, SPCL improves weighted F1-score by approximately +1.2% to +6.6% over baseline models, while on MELD, gains reach up to +10.4%. These results highlight the effectiveness and generalizability of SPCL as a lightweight plug-and-play module for multimodal emotion recognition.

</details>


### [270] [Calibration, Uncertainty Communication, and Deployment Readiness in CKD Risk Prediction: A Framework Evaluation Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21566)
*Michael O. Eniolade*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Internal CKD risk models show perfect AUROC on training data but poor external calibration and uncertainty; deployment readiness is low due to degraded calibration and conformal coverage on external data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Demonstrate that high internal performance does not guarantee reliable probabilistic outputs or uncertainty estimates for CKD risk prediction; emphasize external validation for calibration and conformal prediction reliability.

Method: Train five classifiers (logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, SVM with Platt scaling, Gaussian naive Bayes) on the UCI CKD dataset (400 patients, 62.5% CKD). Evaluate calibration (ECE, Brier Score), conformal prediction coverage (split conformal with 90% target), and an eight-criterion deployment readiness framework. Apply best-calibrated variant to the MIMIC-IV demo cohort (97 patients, 23.7% CKD) to test under prevalence shift and missingness.

Result: AUROC = 1.00 on UCI test for all models; isotonic recalibration reduces internal ECE to 0.000–0.022. On MIMIC-IV: AUROC 0.48–0.58; ECE 0.68–0.76; conformal coverage 0.21–0.25 (vs a 0.90 target). Deployment readiness scores ≤4/16 for all models.

Conclusion: Excellent internal performance does not translate to external data. Calibration stability and conformal coverage should be validated on external datasets before clinical deployment.

Abstract: Machine learning models for chronic kidney disease (CKD) risk prediction often post strong discrimination scores on internal test sets. Calibration and uncertainty quantification get far less attention, leaving clinicians without reliable information about whether the probability outputs are accurate.
  We trained five classifiers on the UCI CKD dataset (400 patients, 62.5% CKD prevalence): logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, SVM with Platt scaling, and Gaussian naive Bayes. We evaluated each across calibration quality, conformal prediction coverage, and an eight-criterion deployment readiness framework. A distributional stress-test applied the best-calibrated variant of each model to the open-access MIMIC-IV demo cohort (97 patients, 23.7% CKD) to assess behaviour under prevalence shift and feature missingness. We measured calibration before and after Platt scaling and isotonic regression using Expected Calibration Error and Brier Score, and quantified uncertainty through split conformal prediction targeting 90% marginal coverage.
  All five models reached AUROC 1.00 on the UCI test set. Isotonic recalibration reduced internal ECE to 0.000-0.022. On MIMIC-IV, AUROC fell to 0.48-0.58, ECE rose to 0.68-0.76, and conformal coverage dropped from 0.80-0.98 to 0.21-0.25 against a 90% target. No model scored above 4 out of 16 on the deployment readiness checklist.
  Near-perfect internal performance did not transfer. Calibration stability and conformal coverage should be evaluated on external data before any clinical prediction model moves toward deployment.

</details>


### [271] [Equilibrium Propagation and Hamiltonian Inference in the Diffusive Fitzhugh-Nagumo Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21568)
*Jack Kendall*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends Equilibrium Propagation to skew-gradient systems, linking deep Energy-Based Models with Hamiltonian neural networks via Fitzhugh-Nagumo networks, and derives a layer-wise Hamiltonian recurrence for inference in stationary deep networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unify energy-based and Hamiltonian approaches to learning by establishing theoretical equivalence under specific neural dynamics, enabling efficient credit assignment and principled inference in deep networks with residual-like topologies.

Method: Theoretically extend equilibrium propagation to skew-gradient dynamics; show stationary Fitzhugh-Nagumo solutions correspond to self-adjoint operators; prove Hamiltonian structure for deep residual-like topologies; derive Hamiltonian Echo Backpropagation applicability; obtain a layer-wise Hamiltonian recurrence for inference in both deep Fitzhugh-Nagumo networks and deep Energy-Based Models.

Result: Demonstrates an equivalence between deep Energy-Based Models and Hamiltonian neural networks under the stated conditions; provides an explicit layer-wise Hamiltonian recurrence guiding inference for stationary states across the considered architectures; validates the applicability of equilibrium-propagation-based credit assignment and Hamiltonian-based backpropagation in this setting.

Conclusion: The work offers a unified, dynamics-grounded framework for credit assignment and inference across energy-based and Hamiltonian-inspired architectures, with practical implications for designing deep networks that exploit stationarity and residual-like topologies.

Abstract: In this work, we extend the Equilibrium Propagation framework to skew-gradient systems and show an equivalence between deep Energy-Based Models and Hamiltonian neural networks. We focus on networks of diffusively coupled Fitzhugh-Nagumo neurons as a prototypical example. We show that since stationary solutions of the Fitzhugh-Nagumo model are described by self-adjoint operators, the methods of equilibrium propagation for performing credit assignment can be applied. Furthermore, for Fitzhugh-Nagumo networks with the topology of a deep residual network, we show that the steady state solutions admit a (spatial) Hamiltonian, and thus the methods of Hamiltonian Echo Backpropagation can be applied. We end by deriving an explicit layer-wise Hamiltonian recurrence relation governing inference for stationary solutions of both deep Fitzhugh-Nagumo networks and deep Energy-Based Models.

</details>


### [272] [ConTact: Contact-First Antibody CDR Design via Explicit Interface Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21600)
*Mansoor Ahmed,Spencer VonBank,Nadeem Taj,Sujin Lee,Naila Jan,Murray Patterson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces ConTact, a contact-then-act CDR design architecture that decouples contact prediction from sequence design via a three-stage process, yielding superior structure, epitope awareness, and competitive sequence recovery on CHIMERA-Bench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing computational antibody CDR design methods conflate contact identification with sequence optimization, diluting antigen signal and hindering targeted binding judgments.

Method: Three cascaded stages: (1) learn surface complementarity fingerprints, (2) predict CDR–antigen contacts, (3) inject contact-gated antigen features into the sequence head. Uses a distance-biased cross-attention module to encode geometric priors and a contact-weighted cross-entropy loss to focus gradients on binding-critical positions.

Result: On CHIMERA-Bench, achieves best structural quality with ~7% RMSD improvement over the next-best baseline, best epitope awareness with ~10% F1 gain over GNN baselines, and competitive sequence recovery (AAR 0.38) among several CDR-H3 design baselines.

Conclusion: Decoupling contact reasoning from sequence design and incorporating explicit geometric priors improves CDR-H3 design performance, particularly in structure quality and epitope-aware binding predictions.

Abstract: Computational antibody CDR design methods condition on antigen structure to generate binding loops, yet existing architectures conflate two fundamentally distinct sub-problems: identifying which CDR positions will contact the antigen, and selecting amino acids at those positions. This conflation forces models to learn contact reasoning implicitly through uniform message passing, diluting antigen signal across all positions equally. We introduce ConTact, a contact-then-act architecture that explicitly decomposes CDR design into three cascaded stages: learning surface complementarity fingerprints, predicting CDR-antigen contacts, and injecting contact-gated antigen features into the sequence head. A distance-biased cross-attention module encodes geometric priors favoring spatial neighbors, while a contact-weighted cross-entropy loss concentrates gradient signal on binding-critical positions. On CHIMERA-Bench dataset, ConTact achieves the best structural quality (7% RMSD improvement over the next-best baseline), best epitope awareness (10% F1 score over GNN baselines), and competitive sequence recovery (AAR 0.38) among several CDR-H3 design baselines.

</details>


### [273] [When Are Teacher Tokens Reliable? Position-Weighted On-Policy Self-Distillation for Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21606)
*Xiaogeng Liu,Xinyan Wang,Yingzi Ma,Yechao Zhang,Chaowei Xiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Position-Weighted On-Policy Self-Distillation (PW-OPSD) guided by a branch-viability diagnostic to weight teacher tokens by trajectory position, improving AIME Avg@12 on multiple models without extra teacher computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard on-policy self-distillation (OPSD) treats all teacher tokens equally, while entropy-based relaxations conflate non-viable uncertainty with benign diversity. A trajectory-structured reliability signal is needed to distinguish when teacher targets are trustworthy during reasoning.

Method: Introduce a branch-viability diagnostic that, for each student prompt, evaluates next-token alternatives from the privileged teacher and tests whether the resulting continuation recovers the correct answer, revealing that within-sequence position is a strong reliability predictor (AUROC ~0.83 on Qwen3-4B). Build PW-OPSD by applying an increasing position weight during the same student rollout, keeping the privileged teacher pass and clipped forward-KL target unchanged.

Result: PW-OPSD shows improved effectiveness: AUROC for the position predictor is 0.83; local uncertainty scores are limited (~0.57). In randomized runs, PW-OPSD improves AIME 2024 Avg@12 by +1.0 and AIME 2025 Avg@12 by +1.1. Generalization to larger models (DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B, Olmo-3-7B-Think) also yields consistent Avg@12 gains.

Conclusion: Teacher-token reliability in reasoning distillation is trajectory-structured and exploitable without extra teacher computation, enabling more effective OPSD through position-weighted targeting.

Abstract: On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) trains a student on its own rollouts using a privileged teacher, but its standard objective weights all generated tokens equally, implicitly treating the privileged teacher target as equally reliable at every student-visited prefix. Existing entropy-based OPD methods relax this uniformity by modulating token-level supervision with teacher entropy, but high teacher entropy in reasoning has an ambiguous reliability meaning: it can reflect either non-viable uncertainty or benign solution diversity. To identify this phenomenon, we introduce a branch-viability diagnostic. Specifically, we record next-token alternatives from the privileged-answer teacher prompt, force each alternative after the student prompt plus its on-policy spine prefix, and test whether the resulting student-template continuation recovers the correct answer. On Qwen3-4B, we find that an oriented within-sequence position score is the strongest tested predictor of teacher-token reliability, reaching an area-under-ROC-curve (AUROC) of 0.83; local uncertainty scores are at most 0.57. Motivated by this trajectory-level structure, we propose Position-Weighted On-Policy Self-Distillation (PW-OPSD), which applies an increasing position weight while keeping the same student rollout, privileged teacher pass, and clipped forward-KL target as OPSD. In our comprehensive evaluations with different random seeds, the diagnostic-derived PW-OPSD improves AIME 2024 and AIME 2025 Avg@12 by +1.0 and +1.1 points, and a generalization evaluation on two larger-scale models from different families, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B and Olmo-3-7B-Think, also demonstrates consistent aggregate Avg@12 improvements. These results show that teacher-token reliability in reasoning distillation is trajectory-structured and can be utilized without additional teacher computation.

</details>


### [274] [AgForce Enables Antigen-conditioned Generative Antibody Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21610)
*Mansoor Ahmed,Murray Patterson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AgForce is a graph neural network (GNN) encoder–decoder designed for antibody design that addresses antigen blindness, vocabulary collapse, and cross-entropy-induced marginalization by using an MDN head with Potts-like coupling, annealed MCL, and antigen cycle consistency; it achieves superior binding and sequence recovery on CHIMERA-Bench and substantially increases the effective vocabulary.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Baseline antibody design methods conditioned on antigen input often ignore antigen information, leading to three failure modes: antigen blindness (predictions derive from antibody framework rather than antigen), vocabulary collapse (limited amino-acid diversity per position), and convergence to positional marginals with standard cross-entropy, hindering antigen-specific predictions.

Method: Introduce AgForce: a GNN encoder with decoders for sequence-structure co-design. Employ framework dropout, gated bottlenecks, and hyperbolic cross attention to prevent shortcut paths. Use an MDN sequence head with Potts-like pairwise coupling and annealed multiple-choice learning (aMCL) to avoid marginal predictions. Add an antigen cycle consistency head to route gradients through the sequence decoder, forcing antigen identity encoding. Train/evaluate on CHIMERA-Bench; compare against baselines across interface metrics; code at GitHub.

Result: AgForce achieves best binding quality and sequence recovery on CHIMERA-Bench, improving amino acid recovery by about 8% over the strongest sequence baseline and outperforming baselines on all interface metrics; nearly doubles the effective vocabulary of GNN-based methods.

Conclusion: AgForce demonstrates a viable, antigen-aware sequence–structure co-design approach that overcomes key limitations of conventional methods and sets new benchmarks for antibody design metrics; source code is available.

Abstract: Antibody design methods condition on antigen structure to generate complementarity-determining regions (CDR), yet a systematic evaluation of baseline methods reveals that they largely ignore the antigen input. We identify three failure modes that explain this behavior. Antigen blindness arises because models derive predictions from antibody framework context rather than antigen information, producing nearly identical CDRs regardless of the target. Vocabulary collapse reduces predicted amino acids to three to five per position, far below the ground truth distribution in native sequences. Moreover, any model trained with standard per-position cross-entropy converges to the positional marginal distribution, making it provably unable to produce antigen-specific sequence predictions. We propose a novel encoder-decoder architecture called AgForce, that uses a graph neural network (GNN) as the encoder and specialized decoders for sequence-structure co-design. Specifically, we apply framework dropout, gated bottlenecks, and hyperbolic cross attention that prevent the antibody shortcut path. In the decoder, a Mixture Density Network (MDN) sequence head with Potts-like pairwise coupling and annealed Multiple Choice Learning (aMCL) replaces the cross-entropy objective with a multi-component distribution whose optimal solution differs from the positional marginal. An antigen cycle consistency head routes gradients through the sequence decoder, forcing predicted distributions to encode antigen identity. AgForce achieves the best binding quality and sequence recovery simultaneously on the CHIMERA-Bench dataset, improving amino acid recovery by 8% over the strongest sequence baseline while surpassing the baselines across all interface metrics, and nearly doubling the effective vocabulary of GNN methods. The source code is available at: https://github.com/mansoor181/ag-force.git

</details>


### [275] [$\textit{BlockFormer}$ : Transformer-based inference from interaction maps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21617)
*Eloïse Touron,Pedro L. C. Rodrigues,Julyan Arbel,Nelle Varoquaux,Michael Arbel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformer-based, data-driven method for centromere localization from Hi-C interaction maps using synthetic data to handle variable numbers/sizes of entities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Centromere identification from genome-wide interaction maps (e.g., Hi-C) is an ill-posed inverse problem with variable entity counts and sizes; robust, scalable methods are needed to exploit shared global-structure across species.

Method: A transformer architecture designed to handle variable numbers/sizes of blocks, trained with a custom simulator that generates abundant, inexpensive synthetic Hi-C-like maps; leverages global alignment of localized interaction patterns to infer centromere positions.

Result: The method accurately localizes centromeres across a wide range of species and genome sizes, demonstrating robustness to genome-scale variability.

Conclusion: A data-driven transformer-based framework, coupled with synthetic data generation, can effectively solve inverse problems on interaction maps like centromere localization and may generalize to other related tasks with shared structure and variable block dimensions.

Abstract: Inference from interaction maps, such as centromere identification from genome-wide chromosome conformation capture techniques -- notably Hi-C -- can be formulated as a generic inverse problem: infer a set of parameters given a map summarizing pairwise interactions between entities through blocks of variable numbers and sizes. In this work, we introduce a data-driven approach that leverages shared structure between these maps, such as global alignment between localized patterns, while handling the variability in number and size of entities arising in real-world data. Our approach relies on a transformer architecture capable of handling such variability and a custom simulator to generate abundant, yet computationally cheap synthetic data for training. Applied to the problem of centromere localization, the method accurately recovers their genomic positions across a wide range of species of various genome sizes.

</details>


### [276] [Alike Parts: A Feature-Informed Approach to Local and Global Prototype Explanations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21646)
*Jacek Karolczak,Jerzy Stefanowski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A two-level feature-importance framework for prototype explanations: locally, 'alike parts' highlights shared, important features between an instance and its nearest prototype; globally, a feature-importance term in prototype selection promotes attribution diversity. Empirical tests on six datasets show maintained or improved surrogate fidelity with diverse explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prototype-based explanations often lack feature-level granularity. Integrating feature importance at both local and global levels aims to provide more informative, diverse, and faithful explanations.

Method: Local: 'alike parts' identifies and highlights the most relevant shared feature subsets between the instance and its nearest prototype using feature importance scores. Global: adds a feature-importance term to the prototype selection objective to encourage diversity in the feature attributions across the selected prototypes.

Result: Experiments on six benchmark datasets show that the augmented prototype selection maintains or increases the prediction fidelity of the surrogate model, indicating that feature diversity does not compromise fidelity.

Conclusion: Integrating feature importance at both local and global levels yields more informative, diverse, and faithful prototype-based explanations without sacrificing surrogate fidelity.

Abstract: Prototype-based explanations offer an intuitive, example-based approach to support the interpretability of machine learning black box classifiers but often lack feature-level granularity. We introduce a framework that integrates feature importance at two levels to address this gap. First, for local explanations, we propose \textit{alike parts}: a method that uses feature importance scores to highlight the most relevant, shared feature subsets between a classified instance and its nearest prototype, guiding user attention. Second, we augment the global prototype selection objective function with a feature importance term to actively promote diversity in the feature attributions of the selected prototypes. Experiments on six benchmark datasets show that this augmented selection process maintains or, in some cases, increases the prediction fidelity of the surrogate model, suggesting that feature diversity does not compromise model fidelity.

</details>


### [277] [Dropout Universality: Scaling Laws and Optimal Scheduling at the Edge-of-Chaos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21648)
*Lucas Fernandez Sarmiento*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Mean-field dropout is analyzed as a perturbation at the edge of chaos, shifting the perfect-alignment fixed point and making the depth-scale finite at critical initialization. Smooth and kinked activations form distinct universality classes with universal two-parameter scaling in detuning and dropout strength; practical front-loaded dropout schedules reduce held-out loss with no extra cost, validated on MLPs and Vision Transformers; extensions to CNN/ResNet discussed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how dropout interacts with critical signal propagation and activation nonlinearity, deriving universal scaling laws and providing predictive guidance for dropout scheduling across architectures.

Method: Mean-field analysis of signal propagation near the perfect-alignment fixed point; study of correlation maps under dropout; classification of activations into smooth and kinked (ReLU-like) to uncover different critical exponents; derivation of crossover scaling laws and two-parameter scaling collapse in detuning and dropout strength; derivation of saturated dropout profiles and a rank-flow tie-breaker for front-loaded schedules; empirical validation in MLPs and Vision Transformers; discussion of extensions to CNN/ResNet.

Result: Dropout shifts the perfect-alignment fixed point, yielding a finite depth scale for information propagation at critical initialization. Smooth activations and kinked (ReLU-like) activations belong to distinct universality classes with different critical exponents, and a universal two-parameter scaling collapse is obtained in detuning and dropout strength. Non-analyticity for kinked activations arises from a branch point in the correlation map. Saturated dropout profiles emerge under a fixed budget, and a rank-flow tie-breaker selects front-loaded schedules that substantially reduce held-out test loss with no extra computational cost; accuracy gains are a consistent secondary effect. Predictions are tested in MLPs and Vision Transformers, with discussion of CNN/ResNet extensions.

Conclusion: The mean-field dropout framework provides predictive, architecture-spanning insights into how dropout interacts with edge-of-chaos dynamics, enabling principled dropout scheduling and revealing the pivotal role of activation nonlinearity in determining scaling behavior and performance gains; extensions to broader architectures are feasible.

Abstract: We develop a mean-field theory of dropout as a perturbation of critical signal propagation at the edge of chaos. Dropout shifts the perfect-alignment fixed point, making the depth scale for information propagation finite even at critical initialization. We derive critical and crossover scaling laws for correlation decay and establish that smooth activations and kinked, ReLU-like activations constitute distinct universality classes, with different critical exponents and a universal two-parameter scaling collapse in detuning and dropout strength. The distinction traces to the analytic structure of the correlation map: smooth activations admit a Taylor expansion near perfect alignment, while kinked activations develop a branch point with universal non-analyticity. As a corollary, the framework yields saturated dropout profiles under fixed budget; a rank-flow tie-breaker then selects front-loaded schedules, substantially reducing held-out test loss at no extra computational cost, with accuracy gains as a consistent secondary effect. We test the predictions in MLPs and Vision Transformers and discuss CNN/ResNet extensions.

</details>


### [278] [EntmaxKV: Support-Aware Decoding for Entmax Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21649)
*Gonçalo Duarte,Miguel Couceiro,Marcos V. Treviso*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EntmaxKV introduces an entmax-native sparse decoding framework for long-context generation that sparsifies attention before KV pages load, combining query-aware page scoring, support-aware candidate selection, and sparse entmax attention, with a Gaussian-aware selector to adapt budgets. It preserves accuracy while significantly reducing KV traffic and increasing speed; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context decoding is bottlenecked by KV-cache memory traffic that grows with context length. Softmax-based truncation loses probability mass; entmax yields exact zeros, enabling true sparse decoding, but previous entmax works focus on training, not autoregressive decoding with pre-fetch sparsity. A method that exploits entmax sparsity before cache loading is needed.

Method: EntmaxKV combines: (1) query-aware page scoring to rank KV pages for loading, (2) support-aware candidate selection to choose tokens within entmax support, (3) sparse entmax attention to preserve exact sparse structure. It analyzes truncation error via dropped probability mass δ and introduces a Gaussian-aware entmax selector that estimates the entmax threshold from lightweight page statistics to adapt the budget. 

Result: Empirically, EntmaxKV drops less probability mass, preserves more support tokens, and achieves lower output error than softmax-based sparse decoding at the same KV budget. On long-context and language-modeling benchmarks, it closely matches full-cache entmax while using a small fraction of the KV cache, achieving up to 3.36× speedup over full attention baselines with softmax and up to 5.43× speedup with entmax at 1M context length. Code available at the linked repository.

Conclusion: EntmaxKV demonstrates that entmax-native sparse decoding can be effectively realized in autoregressive settings, enabling substantial reductions in KV traffic while maintaining near-full entmax performance. This approach yields practical, scalable long-context decoding with significant speedups.

Abstract: Long-context decoding is increasingly limited by KV-cache memory traffic since each generated token attends over a cache whose size grows linearly with context length. Existing sparse decoding methods reduce this cost by selecting subsets of tokens or pages, but are designed for softmax attention, whose dense tails make any truncation discard nonzero probability mass. In contrast, $α$-entmax produces exact zeros, turning sparse decoding from dense-tail approximation into support recovery: if the selected candidates contain the entmax support, sparse decoding remains exact. While recent entmax kernels enable efficient training, they do not address the autoregressive decoding bottleneck, where dense inference still streams the full KV cache before sparsity is known. In this work, we introduce EntmaxKV, an entmax-native sparse decoding framework that exploits sparsity before KV pages are loaded. EntmaxKV combines query-aware page scoring, support-aware candidate selection, and sparse entmax attention. We analyze truncation error through the dropped probability mass $δ$, showing that output error is controlled by $δ$ and vanishes when the entmax support is recovered. We further introduce a Gaussian-aware entmax selector that estimates the entmax threshold from lightweight page statistics, adapting the selected budget to the score distribution. Empirically, EntmaxKV drops less probability mass, retains more support tokens, and achieves lower output error than softmax-based sparse decoding at matched KV budgets. On long-context and language modeling benchmarks, it closely matches full-cache entmax while using a small fraction of the KV cache, achieving up to $3.36\times$ (softmax) and $5.43\times$ (entmax) speedup over full attention baselines at 1M context length. Code available at: https://github.com/deep-spin/entmaxkv.

</details>


### [279] [Amplifying, Not Learning: Fine-Tuned AI Text Detectors Amplify a Pretrained Direction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21653)
*Alexander Smirnov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AI detectors leverage a pretrained typicality axis rather than forming a strict AI-vs-human boundary; projecting onto AI vs HC3 centroids yields strong discrimination across encoders, with some architectures showing raw projections exceeding fine-tuned performance; ESL writing inverts the axis, a falsifiable unique prediction; a frozen probe matches full fine-tuning; a closed-form Jacobian predictor models axis interventions and generalizes across detectors; multiple probes converge, suggesting observer-invariance; calibration shifts account for most LoRA->full-FT bias, not learned representations, supporting the central claim.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify the mechanism by which AI text detectors distinguish AI-generated text from human text, test whether detection rests on a boundary or a pretrained typicality axis, assess robustness across encoder architectures and populations, and provide interpretable tools (Jacobian predictor and probes) to quantify axis-based interventions.

Method: Project raw encoder representations (pre-task supervision) onto centroid(AI) - centroid(HC3) axis; evaluate NYT vs HC3 AUROC across three architectures; compare raw projection to full fine-tuning; assess RoBERTa-base on fluent-formal vs ESL writing; use a 24-example frozen probe; develop a closed-form Jacobian predictor to parameterize axis-manipulating interventions; test interventions on ELECTRA-CE and three third-party RoBERTa detectors; use three probes (text-surface caps_rate residualisation, geometric signed-epsilon ablation, closed-form text-pair predictor); perform matched-TPR0.90 evaluation and compare with published intervention zoo; analyze LoRA->full-FT bias.

Result: AUROC on NYT vs HC3 using raw projection: 0.806/0.944/0.834 (three architectures), attaining 86-106% of the fine-tuned discrimination ceiling; on RoBERTa-base, raw projection can exceed fine-tuning; full fine-tuning on RoBERTa-base reduces discrimination below raw for fluent-formal populations; ESL writing axis inverted (AUROC 0.06-0.20); 24-example frozen probe matches full fine-tuning (0.900 vs 0.895); closed-form Jacobian predictor achieves R^2 = 1.000 universally, lifts ELECTRA-CE deployment TPR from 0.000 to 0.904 at FPR = 1%, transfers to three RoBERTa detectors with 16/16 oracle-equivalence and 57% NYT-FPR reduction on OpenAI detector; three operational probes agree (cosine similarities 0.74, 0.81, 1.00 across architectures); under matched-TPR=0.90 evaluation, published interventions (CC, dealign-f2c) are calibration-equivalent across 27 cells (|Delta AUROC| ≤ 0.0081); ≥97% of LoRA->full-FT bias gap on ELECTRA is calibration shift, not learned representation; central claim predictions confirmed.

Conclusion: The evidence supports that AI detectors operate by amplifying a pretrained typicality axis rather than constructing a distinct AI-vs-human boundary. The axis is largely architecture- and population-shared, with per-text variability. A universal, closed-form Jacobian predictor effectively models axis interventions and transfers across detectors. Most observed biases stem from calibration shifts, not changes in learned representations, validating the central claim and its falsifiable predictions (e.g., ESL inversion).

Abstract: AI text detectors amplify a pretrained typicality axis; they do not construct an AI-vs-human boundary. On raw encoders before any task supervision, projecting onto centroid(AI)-centroid(HC3) achieves NYT-vs-HC3 AUROC 0.806/0.944/0.834 across three architectures (86-106% of the fine-tuned discrimination ceiling: on RoBERTa-base, raw projection exceeds fine-tuning); on RoBERTa-base, full fine-tuning reduces discrimination below raw on both fluent-formal populations tested. The same axis inverts on non-native ESL writing (AUROC 0.06-0.20) -- a falsifiable prediction unique to the typicality reading. A 24-example frozen probe matches full fine-tuning (0.900 vs 0.895). A closed-form Jacobian predictor parameterises axis-manipulating interventions with R^2 = 1.000 universal, lifts ELECTRA-CE deployment TPR from 0.000 to 0.904 at FPR = 1%, and transfers to three independently-trained third-party RoBERTa detectors at 16/16 oracle-equivalence (57% NYT-FPR reduction on the OpenAI detector). Scope: encoder family; mechanism magnitude HC3-anchored; population-level shared axis with per-text mechanisms varying across architectures. Three operationally distinct probes -- text-surface caps_rate residualisation, geometric signed-epsilon ablation, closed-form text-pair predictor -- agree at cos 0.74/0.81/1.00 across three architectures, confirming observer-invariance. Under matched-TPR-0.90 evaluation, the published intervention zoo (CC, dealign-f2c) is calibration-equivalent across 27 cells (|Delta AUROC| <= 0.0081), and >= 97% of the LoRA->full-FT bias gap on ELECTRA is calibration shift, not learned representation -- the central claim's prediction confirmed.

</details>


### [280] [Value-Gradient Hypothesis of RL for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21654)
*Arip Asadulaev,Daniil Ognev,Karim Salta,Martin Takac*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretical framing of critic-free RL for LLM post-training via value-gradient signals; shows policy gradient aligns with value gradient under differentiable rollout; attention autodiff yields approximate costates; provides a criterion to predict RL effectiveness during pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why critic-free methods like PPO/GRPO perform well in language model RL, quantify when and why they yield gains, and connect practical algorithms to a value-gradient perspective.

Method: 1) Analyze differentiable rollout with additive-noise parameterization to show the actor update is value-gradient-like in expectation. 2) For discrete transformer policies, show autodifferentiation through attention yields empirical costates that approximate the value signal, with error bounded by sampling gap and policy entropy.

Result: A decomposition of RL impact into a value-gradient signal and reachable reward headroom, leading to a criterion for when RL should be most effective along a pretraining trajectory.

Conclusion: RL benefits are maximized when the value-gradient signal is strong and there is sufficient reachable reward headroom; this framework provides a practical criterion to time critic-free RL during LLM pretraining and explains why attention-based autodiff can approximate the value signal in discrete transformer policies.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning substantially improves pretrained language models, but it remains understudied why critic-free methods such as PPO and GRPO work as well as they do, and when they should provide the largest gains. We develop a value-gradient perspective of critic-free RL for LLM post-training. First, under a differentiable rollout and additive-noise parameterization, we show that the actor update is value-gradient-like in expectation: the backward pass propagates costates whose conditional expectation equals the value gradient. Second, for discrete transformer policies, we show that autodifferentiation through attention produces empirical costates that approximate this value signal, with an error controlled by the sampling gap and policy entropy. These results motivate a decomposition of RL impact into value gradient signal and reachable reward headroom, yielding a criterion for when RL should be most effective along a pretraining trajectory.

</details>


### [281] [Hierarchical Variational Policies for Reward-Guided Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21661)
*Kushagra Pandey,Farrin Marouf Sofian,Jan Niklas Groeneveld,Felix Draxler,Stephan Mandt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hierarchical variational framework for test-time adaptation of pretrained diffusion models using an amortized policy, enabling few-step sampling with improved speed and perceptual quality for inverse problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computational burden of test-time guidance/optimization when adapting diffusion models to downstream inverse problems, while preserving or improving sample quality.

Method: Formulate test-time adaptation as a hierarchical variational model; learn a lightweight stochastic policy that amortizes control; enable few-step diffusion with large steps and per-step control; fully amortized sampler; also semi-amortized regime combining cheap proposals with limited optimization.

Result: Achieves strong quality–speed tradeoffs; matches/exceeds test-time scaling baselines with much less compute; e.g., 4x super-resolution shows higher perceptual quality with >5x faster inference; semi-amortized regime yields state-of-the-art perceptual quality across several inverse problems.

Conclusion: Efficient framework for reward-aligned sampling in diffusion models; scalable test-time adaptation; potential applicability to various inverse problems with improved inference efficiency.

Abstract: Adapting pretrained diffusion models to downstream objectives such as inverse problems often requires expensive test-time guidance or optimization. We propose a principled framework for generating high-quality reward-aligned samples at substantially reduced inference cost. Our approach formulates test-time adaptation as a hierarchical variational model, where control is amortized into a lightweight yet expressive stochastic policy. This formulation naturally supports few-step diffusion sampling: large step sizes enable fast inference, while the learned policy maintains sample quality by providing structured per-step control. The resulting fully amortized sampler achieves a strong quality--speed tradeoff, matching or exceeding recent test-time scaling baselines while requiring significantly less compute. For example, on 4x super-resolution, our method achieves better perceptual quality with more than 5x faster inference compared to the best-performing baseline. We further extend our approach to a semi-amortized regime that combines cheap amortized proposals with limited test-time optimization, achieving state-of-the-art perceptual quality across several challenging inverse problems.

</details>


### [282] [Representation Gap: Explaining the Unreasonable Effectiveness of Neural Networks from a Geometric Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21692)
*David Perera,Victor Moura,Lais Isabelle Alves dos Santos,Michel F. C. Haddad,Flavio Figueiredo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RepGap offers a precise asymptotic description of generalization error via intrinsic dimension, in equivariant diffusion models, with broad applicability and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
