Motivation: Generalization error in neural networks is hard to quantify and often relies on heuristics. A well-behaved, estimable metric is needed; RepGap provides such a quantity connected to the model's equivariances and trained via diffusion models.

Method: Define Representation Gap and derive its precise asymptotic equivalent using results from optimal quantization and point-process theory. Show that the dynamics are governed by a single parameter, the intrinsic dimension, linked to architecture equivariances. Extend the analysis to other tasks and training algorithms; validate empirically on synthetic datasets (where quantities are known) and on realistic datasets.

Result: Derived a precise asymptotic law for the Representation Gap; intrinsic dimension suffices to describe the asymptotic dynamics; estimation of intrinsic dimension is accurate; empirical results align with known quantities on synthetic data and corroborate literature on real data.

Conclusion: The asymptotic dynamics are broadly applicable beyond the studied setting; intrinsic dimension is interpretable and efficiently estimable and relates to network equivariances, offering a practical metric for analyzing and designing neural nets. The framework extends to a wider class of tasks and optimization procedures.

Abstract: Characterizing precisely the asymptotic generalization error of neural networks using parameters that can be estimated efficiently is a crucial problem in machine learning, which relies heavily on heuristics and practitioners' intuition to make key design choices. In order to mitigate this issue, we introduce the Representation Gap, a metric closely related to the generalization error, but admitting better-behaved asymptotic dynamics. Focusing on equivariant diffusion models and leveraging results from optimal quantization and point-process theory, we derive a precise asymptotic equivalent of the Representation Gap and show that it is governed by a single parameter, the \textit{intrinsic dimension} of the task, which is easy to interpret, efficient to estimate, and can be linked to the equivariances of common neural network architectures. We show that this asymptotic dynamic also extends to a broader range of tasks and training algorithms. Finally, we demonstrate empirically that our asymptotic law and intrinsic dimension estimation are accurate on a wide range of synthetic datasets, where these quantities are known, as well as on more realistic datasets, where we obtain results consistent with the related literature.

</details>


### [283] [TBP-mHC: full expressivity for manifold-constrained hyper connections through transportation polytopes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21724)
*Anton Lyubinin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TBP/RTBP provide exact doubly stochastic mixing matrices via new parameterizations for hyper-connection architectures, avoiding iterative normalization and combinatorial explosion, while maintaining expressivity; shows competitive language-model pre-training results with improved stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unconstrained mixing in Hyper-Connections (HC) destabilizes training; existing constraints (mHC, mHC-lite, KromHC) trade off between relaxation, factorial cost, and restricted submanifolds. A need for expressive, stable, and scalable mixing in residual streams.

Method: Introduce Transportation Birkhoff Polytope (TBP) parameterizations and Recursive TBP (RTBP) to construct exactly doubly stochastic mixing matrices with (n-1)^2 degrees of freedom, avoiding iterative Sinkhorn normalization and combinatorial explosion; preserve full expressivity of the Birkhoff polytope.

Result: Empirical results on language model pre-training show competitive performance with improved stability and scalability.

Conclusion: TBP/RTBP provide exact doubly stochastic mixing within HC variants, achieving stable training and scalable model capacity while maintaining the full expressivity of the Birkhoff polytope.

Abstract: Hyper-Connections (HC) improve residual networks by introducing learnable mixing across multiple residual streams, but unconstrained mixing leads to training instability. Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections (mHC) address this by enforcing approximate double stochasticity via Sinkhorn normalization, while mHC-lite ensures exact constraints through convex combinations of permutation matrices at the cost of factorial complexity. KromHC reduces this cost using Kronecker-product parameterizations, but restricts the mixing matrices to a structured submanifold of the Birkhoff polytope .
  We propose Transportation Birkhoff Polytope (TBP) parameterizations and their Recursive variants (RTBP), which construct exactly doubly stochastic mixing matrices with $(n-1)^2$ degrees of freedom. Our approach avoids iterative normalization and combinatorial explosion while preserving full expressivity of the Birkhoff polytope. Empirical results on language model pre-training' demonstrate competitive performance with improved stability and scalability.

</details>


### [284] [I-SAFE: Wasserstein Coherence Metrics for Structural Auditing of Scientific AI Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21731)
*Barbara Tarantino,Gennaro Auricchio,Paolo Giudici*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduce I-SAFE, a post-hoc auditing framework using Wasserstein Coherence Metrics to assess distributional coherence under structure-guided perturbations; demonstrates on drug–target interaction models showing insights beyond accuracy; framework is model-agnostic.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accuracy alone can be brittle due to shortcut features, dataset biases, or distributional quirks. A distributional audit aligned with domain structure, via an external prior, can reveal scientifically relevant model behavior.

Method: Define I-SAFE with three coherence metrics: Quantile-Based Metric (QBM) for location-level coherence, Wasserstein Coherence Metric (WCM) for ordinal coherence, and a translation-invariant WCM variant for shape coherence. Apply post-hoc perturbations guided by an external structural prior to a trained black-box predictor. Evaluate output distribution coherence. Instantiate on drug–target interaction (DTI) with Davis kinase benchmark, KLIFS binding-pocket annotations, and three sequence-based DTI models (DeepConvDTI, DeepDTA, TAPB).

Result: I-SAFE yields substantially different distributional response profiles across models that are invisible to accuracy-based evaluation. The framework is model-agnostic and applicable to domains where inputs admit a structured decomposition and an external prior is available.

Conclusion: I-SAFE provides a robust, post-hoc audit of scientifically meaningful behavior in scientific AI, enabling detection of distributional regularities or biases beyond what accuracy captures; broadly applicable to domains with structured inputs and external priors.

Abstract: Deep learning models are increasingly used in scientific prediction tasks where strong benchmark performance is often interpreted as evidence of scientifically meaningful behavior. This interpretation is fragile, as models may exploit shortcut features, dataset-specific regularities, or distributional biases that are predictive on held-out data but not aligned with domain-relevant structure. To address this limitation, we introduce the \textsc{I-SAFE} (Interventional Secure, Accurate, Fair and Explainable) framework, a post-hoc distributional auditing framework for scientific AI models centered on the Wasserstein Coherence Metric (WCM). Given a trained black-box predictor and an external structural prior encoding domain knowledge about task-relevant input structure, \textsc{I-SAFE} evaluates raw model outputs under structurally guided perturbations of the input. The proposed audit measures output-distribution coherence through three complementary metrics: a Quantile-Based Metric (QBM) for location-level coherence, the WCM for ordinal coherence, and a translation-invariant WCM variant for shape coherence. We instantiate \textsc{I-SAFE} on drug--target interaction (DTI) prediction using the Davis kinase benchmark, KLIFS (Kinase--Ligand Interaction Fingerprints and Structures) binding-pocket annotations, and three sequence-based DTI models: DeepConvDTI, DeepDTA, and TAPB. Although the models operate in a comparable predictive regime, \textsc{I-SAFE} reveals substantially different distributional response profiles, a distinction invisible to accuracy-based evaluation. The framework is model-agnostic and applicable to any domain where inputs admit a structured decomposition and an external prior is available.

</details>


### [285] [Correcting Class Imbalance in Prior-Data Fitted Networks for Tabular Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21742)
*Samuel McDowell,Nathan Stromberg,Lalitha Sankar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PFNs for tabular data with class imbalance benefit from simple imbalance remedies: thresholding excels due to calibration, and downsampling is competitive while reducing inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Class-imbalanced tabular classification degrades performance; loss-based balancing is infeasible with in-context learning (ICL) PFNs, necessitating alternative techniques.

Method: Adapted classical class-imbalance techniques to PFNs and evaluated their performance. Focused on thresholding and downsampling, leveraging PFN calibration and limited-data strengths to assess effectiveness and computational trade-offs.

Result: Thresholding shows exceptional performance thanks to PFN calibration; downsampling performs comparably and reduces inference computation.

Conclusion: PFNs’ calibration and data-efficient nature amplify simple imbalance remedies. Thresholding is highly effective; downsampling is a viable, computation-saving alternative. Further work could explore combining techniques or extending to other modalities.

Abstract: Prior-data fitted networks (PFNs) have achieved exceptional performance on tabular classification tasks. However, like other classifiers, their performance can suffer under the effect of class imbalance, resulting in poor performance for rare classes. Several techniques exist which attempt to mitigate the deleterious effect of class imbalance on classification performance, but the in-context learning (ICL) dynamic of PFNs means that loss-based strategies are impossible, and other techniques are unproven. We have adapted several classical techniques addressing class imbalance and analyzed their performance on PFN classification. We observe that thresholding performs exceptionally well because of the calibration characteristics of PFNs, and downsampling performs comparably because of PFNs exceptional limited-data performance, with the additional benefit of reduced computation cost for inference.

</details>


### [286] [Quantitative coronary calcification analysis for prediction of myocardial ischemia using non-contrast CT calcium scoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21745)
*Juhwan Lee,Sadeer Al-Kindi,Ammar Hoori,Tao Hu,Hao Wu,Justin N. Kim,Robert Gilkeson,Sanjay Rajagopalan,David L. Wilson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A ML framework using non-contrast CT calcium scoring (including calcium-omics features) to predict myocardial ischemia from regadenoson PET MPI; SHAP-guided feature selection; 5-fold CV; final model with Agatston score, eight calcium-omics features, and age; high precision and competitive sensitivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leverage routine CT calcium scoring to predict myocardial ischemia, enabling accessible risk stratification and reducing need for stress testing.

Method: Retrospective analysis of 1,375 patients with non-contrast CTCS and regadenoson stress PET MPI within one year. Evaluated 74 variables (clinical, Agatston score, calcium-omics). Feature selection via XGBoost with SHAP. 5-fold cross-validation. Final model includes Agatston, eight calcium-omics features, and age.

Result: Among 987 evaluable patients, 89 ischemia-positive. Final model achieves precision 98.9±3.0%, sensitivity 79.2±8.4%, F1 87.7±5.3%. Calcium-omics features provide incremental predictive value over clinical factors or clinical + Agatston (p<0.05). In logistic regression, number of calcified arteries shows strong association with ischemia (OR 3.63; 95% CI 2.80–4.77; p<1e-5).

Conclusion: Calcium-omics features from routine non-contrast CTCS offer incremental predictive value for myocardial ischemia beyond conventional risk factors and Agatston scoring, supporting accessible cardiovascular risk stratification.

Abstract: Non-contrast computed tomography calcium scoring (CTCS) is widely recognized as an effective tool for cardiovascular risk stratification. This study aimed to develop a novel machine learning framework for predicting myocardial ischemia from routine non-contrast CTCS scans using quantitative coronary calcium assessment. This study analyzed 1,375 patients who underwent both non-contrast CTCS and regadenoson stress cardiac positron emission tomography myocardial perfusion imaging within one year at University Hospitals Cleveland Medical Center. A total of 74 variables, including clinical variables, Agatston score, and calcium-omics features, were evaluated. Relevant features were identified using XGBoost with Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP). Predictive models were trained and evaluated using 5-fold cross-validation. Among 987 patients, 89 (9%) were positive for myocardial ischemia. The final model incorporated the Agatston score, eight calcium-omics features, and age. The proposed model achieved a precision of 98.9+/-3.0%, sensitivity of 79.2+/-8.4, and F1 score of 87.7+/-5.3%. The addition of calcium-omics features significantly improved predictive performance compared with models using clinical variables alone or clinical variables with the Agatston score (p<0.05). Interestingly, the number of calcified arteries, despite being the lowest-ranked feature based on SHAP analysis, showed the strongest association with myocardial ischemia in logistic regression analysis (odds ratio: 3.63, 95% confidence interval: 2.80-4.77, p<0.00001). We developed a machine learning approach for predicting myocardial ischemia using routinely acquired non-contrast CTCS scans. Calcium-omics features provided incremental predictive value beyond conventional risk factors and Agatston scoring and may support more accessible cardiovascular risk stratification.

</details>


### [287] [Models Can Model, But Can't Bind: Structured Grounding in Text-to-Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21751)
*Zhiqi Gao,Albert Ge,Alexander Berenbeim,Nathaniel D. Bastian,Frederic Sala*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A scalable benchmark reveals a binding bottleneck in text-to-optimization; introduces BIND to externalize numeric data, yielding large accuracy gains; finetuning on binding outperforms end-to-end methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To disentangle modeling from binding, quantify how data scale affects accuracy, and improve grounding of numeric problem data in natural-language to optimization pipelines.

Method: Define Text2Opt-Bench with 12 problem categories; evaluate 10+ models; propose BIND, an inference-time scheme to place numeric data in structured files and have the model bind data programmatically; also finetune a binding-focused model on binding tasks.

Result: BIND raises GPT-5-Nano accuracy from 59.1% to 82.4% (near pass@5 = 82.0%); raises GPT-5 accuracy from 86.2% to 95.8%; a 1.5B binding specialist, finetuned on binding, matches a 7B end-to-end baseline across three categories.

Conclusion: Binding data grounding is a critical bottleneck; separating binding from modeling improves performance with lower token costs; binding-focused training is a promising direction for scalable text-to-optimization.

Abstract: Text-to-optimization requires two separable capabilities: modeling -- choosing the right optimization structure -- and binding -- grounding every coefficient, index, and parameter in the concrete problem data. We study this via Text2Opt-Bench, a scalable benchmark of solver-verified optimization problems spanning 12 categories, from textbook linear programs to stochastic and multi-objective formulations with up to thousands of variables. Across 10+ models, we find that accuracy collapses as instance data grows, even when the formulation itself is simple. We call this the effective binding limit. We address this via a simple inference-time approach, BIND, which externalizes numeric data to structured files so the model binds data programmatically rather than transcribing from the prompt. BIND improves GPT-5-Nano from 59.1% to 82.4% accuracy, matching pass@5 (82.0%) at lower token cost than pass@1, and GPT-5 from 86.2% to 95.8%. Furthermore, we validate our hypothesis by finetuning a model exclusively on binding and show that it outperforms end-to-end SFT and RL across three structurally distinct optimization categories, with a 1.5B binding specialist alone matching a 7B end-to-end baseline.

</details>


### [288] [PEARL: Unbiased Percentile Estimation via Contrastive Learning for Industrial-Scale Livestream Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21752)
*Blake Gella,Wei Wu,Yuhao Yin,Zexi Huang,Zikai Wang,Emily Liu,Junlin Zhang,Wentao Guo,Qinglei Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PEARL is a nonparametric contrastive percentile framework that mitigates behavioral intensity imbalance in recommender systems by modeling relative preferences, using percentile-based signals from real contrastive samples, with bootstrapping for sparse feedback and co-training for robust representations; validated offline and with large-scale online gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Behavioral intensity imbalance causes skewed feedback signals, favoring highly active users and degrading recommendation quality and robustness at scale.

Method: Nonparametric contrastive percentile approximation that uses real contrastive interaction samples to approximate percentile relationships (relative advantage debiasing); avoids auxiliary distribution estimation; includes prediction-based bootstrapping for percentile smoothing; generalized value-weighted formulation; co-training to enhance representation learning.

Result: Offline experiments show effective mitigation of behavioral bias and consistent performance gains across ranking targets; online A/B in a production livestream platform with billions of users yields gains: +2.10% Watch Duration, +0.80% Consumption Amount, +1.49% Interaction Rate, and −6.91% Report Rate.

Conclusion: PEARL reduces behavioral bias and improves recommendation quality and robustness at scale, with demonstrated practical impact in production.

Abstract: Recommender systems trained on user interaction data are susceptible to behavioral intensity imbalance--a systematic distortion arising from heterogeneous engagement patterns across users. This imbalance skews feedback signals such that observed interactions no longer faithfully reflect true preferences, causing models to disproportionately amplify signals from highly active users while underrepresenting others, which ultimately degrades recommendation quality and robustness at scale. To address this issue, we propose a nonparametric contrastive percentile approximation framework, PEARL, that models relative preference signals instead of absolute engagement magnitudes. Building upon relative advantage debiasing, PEARL leverages real contrastive interaction samples to approximate percentile relationships directly, without relying on auxiliary distribution estimation models. We provide theoretical justification demonstrating that such pairwise comparisons yield unbiased estimates of percentile-based preference signals. For broader applicability, we introduce a prediction-based bootstrapping mechanism for percentile smoothing to handle sparse and discrete feedback, alongside a generalized value-weighted formulation and a co-training strategy to enhance both modeling flexibility and representation learning. Extensive offline experiments demonstrate that PEARL effectively mitigates behavioral bias and consistently improves recommendation performance across multiple ranking targets. Deployed in a production livestream platform with a combined user base of billions, online A/B testing confirms substantial real-world gains: +2.10% Watch Duration, +0.80% Consumption Amount, +1.49% Interaction Rate, and -6.91% Report Rate.

</details>


### [289] [stable-worldmodel: A Platform for Reproducible World Modeling Research and Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21800)
*Lucas Maes,Quentin Le Lidec,Luiz Facury,Nassim Massaudi,Ayush Chaurasia,Francesco Capuano,Richard Gao,Taj Gillin,Dan Haramati,Damien Scieur,Yann LeCun,Randall Balestriero*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces swm, an open-source platform for standardized, reproducible world-modeling research, featuring a Lance-based data layer, ready-made baselines/solvers, and a broad, controllable evaluation suite.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: World-model research is fragmented across codebases, data pipelines, and evaluation protocols, hindering reproducibility and fair comparison; key bottlenecks include fragile codebases, slow video data loading, and lack of standardized generalization benchmarks.

Method: (swM provides) (1) a Lance-based high-performance data layer with native support/conversion for MP4, HDF5, LeRobot datasets; (2) clean, well-tested implementations of modern world-model baselines and planning solvers; (3) a broad suite of environments/tasks with controllable visual, geometric, and physical factors for systematic in-silico evaluation of dynamics understanding, control performance, representation quality, and out-of-distribution generalization; integrates the full pipeline under a single scalable framework.

Result: Delivers a unified platform that reduces research overhead and accelerates trustworthy progress toward reliable world models by enabling standardized data handling, reusable baselines/solvers, and comprehensive evaluation.

Conclusion: Unifying the full world-model research pipeline under swm enables reproducibility, fair benchmarking, and scalable experimentation for dynamics, control, and generalization studies.

Abstract: World models are central to building agents that can reason, plan, and generalize beyond their training data. However, research on world models is currently fragmented, with disparate codebases, data pipelines, and evaluation protocols hindering reproducibility and fair comparison. Current practice is further limited by three key bottlenecks: fragile one-off codebases, slow video data loading, and the lack of standardized generalization benchmarks. We present stable-worldmodel (swm), an open-source platform for standardized and reproducible world modeling research and evaluation. It delivers (1) a high-performance Lance-based data layer with native support and conversion tools for MP4, HDF5, and LeRobot datasets, (2) clean, well-tested implementations of modern world model baselines and planning solvers, and (3) a broad suite of environments and tasks extended with controllable visual, geometric, and physical factors of variation for systematic in-silico evaluation of dynamics understanding, control performance, representation quality, and out-of-distribution generalization. By unifying the full pipeline under a single, scalable framework, \texttt{swm} dramatically reduces research overhead and accelerates trustworthy progress toward reliable world models.

</details>


### [290] [Machine learning prediction of obstructive coronary artery disease using opportunistic coronary calcium and epicardial fat assessments from CT calcium scoring scans](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21762)
*Juhwan Lee,Ammar Hoori,Tao Hu,Justin N. Kim,Mohamed H. E. Makhlouf,Michelle C. Williams,David E. Newby,Robert Gilkeson,Sanjay Rajagopalan,David L. Wilson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ML framework using calcium-omics and epicardial fat-omics from non-contrast CT calcium scoring to predict obstructive CAD; CatBoost-SHAP selected 14 features; achieved high specificity and sensitivity, including cases with zero calcium score; may reduce need for CTA/invasive testing in low-to intermediate-risk patients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve non-invasive prediction of obstructive CAD using CTCS-derived omics features to reduce reliance on contrast-enhanced or invasive diagnostics, especially in low-to intermediate-risk patients.

Method: Retrospective analysis of 1,324 SCOT-HEART participants who underwent CTCS and coronary CT angiography. Extracted 24 clinical variables, 189 calcium-omics, and 211 epicardial fat-omics from CTCS images. Feature selection via CatBoost with SHAP; model built with CatBoost gradient boosting. From 424 candidates, 14 features identified as most predictive; top two from fat-omics, 12 from calcium-omics.

Result: Sensitivity 83.1±4.6%, specificity 93.8±1.7%, accuracy 85.3±2.0%, F1 73.9±3.3%. Calcium-omics and fat-omics together improved performance. Model maintained predictive accuracy across a range of CAC scores, including obstructive CAD with zero CAC. Suggests potential to reduce reliance on contrast-enhanced or invasive testing, especially in low-to intermediate-risk groups.

Conclusion: The approach is promising for non-invasive CAD risk stratification and decision-making, with potential to decrease CTA/invasive testing. External validation and assessment of generalizability are needed, along with integration considerations into clinical workflows.

Abstract: Non-contrast computed tomography calcium scoring (CTCS) is a cost-effective imaging modality widely used to detect coronary artery calcifications. This study aimed to develop an advanced machine learning framework that utilizes quantitative analyses of coronary calcium and epicardial fat from CTCS images to predict obstructive coronary artery disease (CAD). The study population consisted of 1,324 patients from the SCOT-HEART clinical trial who underwent both CTCS and coronary CT angiography. We extracted and analyzed a broad range of features, including 24 clinical variables, 189 calcium-omics, and 211 epicardial fat-omics features from the CTCS images. Feature selection was conducted using the CatBoost algorithm combined with SHapley Additive exPlanation (SHAP) values. Predictive modeling utilized the CatBoost gradient boosting method, focusing on the most informative features. From an initial set of 424 candidate features, 14 were identified as most predictive through the CatBoost-SHAP method. The top two predictive features originated from fat-omics, with the remaining 12 features derived from calcium-omics. The optimized model achieved robust predictive capabilities, demonstrating a sensitivity of 83.1+/-4.6%, specificity of 93.8+/-1.7%, accuracy of 85.3+/-2.0%, and an F1 score of 73.9+/-3.3%. Inclusion of calcium-omics and fat-omics data significantly improved predictive performance. Notably, the model also showed reliable predictive accuracy in patients with diverse coronary calcium scores, including cases with obstructive CAD despite a zero-calcium score. This innovative approach holds promise for improving clinical decision-making and potentially reducing dependence on contrast-enhanced or invasive diagnostic procedures, particularly within low-to intermediate-risk patient groups.

</details>


### [291] [On the Sample Complexity of Discounted Reinforcement Learning with Optimized Certainty Equivalents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21763)
*Oliver Mortensen,Mohammad Sadegh Talebi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Characterizes PAC-learnability of risk-sensitive RL under optimized certainty equivalents (OCE) in finite discounted MDPs with a generative model; gives upper and lower bounds on sample complexity for value and policy learning; non-PAC-learnability when utility domain is not all real numbers; derives tight SA-dependent lower bounds and explicit 1/τ^2 dependence for CVaR, with suboptimal 1/(1−γ) horizon dependence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand when risk-sensitive RL with OCE objectives is learnable in a PAC sense, and quantify sample complexity for value and policy learning under a generative model, covering CVaR, entropic risk, and mean-variance.

Method: Analyze a simple model-based learning approach under recursive OCE; derive necessary and sufficient conditions on utility function domain for PAC-learnability; establish upper PAC bounds; derive matching lower bounds for value and policy learning; specialize to CVaR to obtain tau-dependent bounds; derive SA-sized tightness and explicit horizon effects for restricted utilities.

Result: Exact characterization: PAC-learnability of OCE objectives depends on u’s domain; non-PAC-learnable when dom(u) ≠ R. Upper bounds via a simple model-based learner; lower bounds matching size SA; for CVaR_tau, correct tau-dependence 1/τ^2; bounds show tightness in SA and, for restricted utilities, explicit 1/(1−γ) dependence (though suboptimal).

Conclusion: Risk-sensitive RL with OCE is PAC-learnable only for utilities with full real-domain; practical learning guarantees require such utilities. The work provides tight sample-complexity benchmarks and highlights a notable improvement for CVaR in tau scaling, while revealing horizon-related limitations that invite further refinement.

Abstract: We study risk-sensitive reinforcement learning in finite discounted MDPs, where a generative model of the MDP is assumed to be available. We consider a family or risk measures called the optimized certainty equivalent (OCE), which includes important risk measures such as entropic risk, CVaR, and mean-variance. Our focus is on the sample complexities of learning the optimal state-action value function (value learning) and an optimal policy (policy learning) under recursive OCE. We provide an exact characterization of utility functions $u$ for which the corresponding OCE defines an objective that is PAC-learnable. We analyze a simple model-based approach and derive PAC sample complexity bounds. We establish that whenever $u$ does not have full domain $\text{dom}(u)\neq \mathbb{R}$, the corresponding problem is not PAC-learnable. Finally, we establish corresponding lower bounds for both value and policy learning, demonstrating tightness in the size $SA$ of state-action space, and for a more restricted class of utilities, we derive lower bounds that makes the dependence on the effective horizon $\frac{1}{1-γ}$ explicit. Specifically, for $\text{CVaR}_τ$ we show that the correct dependence on $τ$ is $\frac{1}{τ^2}$, thus improving by a factor of $\frac{1}τ$ over state-of-the-art although our bound has a suboptimal dependence on $\frac{1}{1-γ}$.

</details>


### [292] [Position: The Time for Sampling Is Now! Charting a New Course for Bayesian Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21765)
*Emanuel Sommer,David Rügamer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAI in Bayesian neural networks is reaching parity with optimization-based methods and may supersede them; success hinges on practical adoption through better exploration of the posterior and high-fidelity distillation of samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To dispel misconceptions about sampling-based inference, promote principled uncertainty quantification in neural networks, and unlock benefits of model averaging and downstream tasks.

Method: Conceptual position paper that argues for SAI's viability, outlines two central research challenges (posterior exploration and distillation), and proposes a roadmap for realigning research efforts.

Result: The paper contends that SAI can match optimization methods and improve performance via model averaging; no empirical results are reported, but a roadmap and stance are presented.

Conclusion: Address exploration and distillation challenges to unlock SAI's full potential and establish it as a central tool in Bayesian deep learning.

Abstract: The practical adoption of sampling-based inference (SAI) in Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) remains limited, partly due to persistent misconceptions about the feasibility and efficiency of sampling. This position paper argues that SAI has achieved computational parity with optimization-based methods and is at the verge of superseding such methods for effective and efficient inference in BNNs. This development should be in the interest of the whole community, promoting BNNs as a principled paradigm with its long-standing yet unfulfilled promise of providing principled uncertainty quantification for neural networks. SAI can even do more -- yielding superior prediction performance through model averaging, serving as the foundation for a plethora of possible downstream tasks, and providing crucial insights into the landscape of BNNs. In order to make such a change happen and unfold the potential of sampling, overcoming current misconceptions is a necessary first step. The next step is to realign research efforts toward addressing remaining challenges in SAI. In particular, the community must focus on two core problems: sufficient exploration of the posterior landscape and high-fidelity distillation of posterior samples for efficient downstream inference. By addressing conceptual and practical obstacles, we can unlock the full potential of SAI and establish it as a central tool in Bayesian deep learning.

</details>


### [293] [Beyond Euclidean Proximity: Repairing Latent World Models with Horizon-Matched Trajectory Reachability Metrics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22164)
*Liangyu Li,Shengzhi Wang,Qingwen Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-hoc terminal-ranking metric (TRM) for latent world models improves long-horizon planning by training a small pairwise head on trajectory structure; it replaces/augments raw latent distance and yields large gains, with mechanistic insights into why latent representations misrank candidates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In latent MPC, raw latent proximity at the terminal state can leak irrelevant information and misrank candidate action sequences. A horizon-aware, task-aligned terminal metric is needed to reliably reflect reachability to the goal over long horizons without changing the underlying model.

Method: Train a compact pairwise head on logged trajectory structure to predict candidate terminal reachability; use this metric as a replacement or augmentation for the terminal cost while keeping encoder, dynamics, sampler, optimizer, and evaluation unchanged; supervise with broad, balanced temporal separations to align with long-horizon ranking.

Result: On TwoRoom hard: LeWM baseline 7.0% vs full-horizon TRM 97.0%; shuffled-label control 0.0%. With PLDM baseline, TRM raises from 32.7% to 84.0% across three seeds; short-horizon TRM with 100k pairs yields 35.0%. Mechanistic evidence: XY position is linearly decodable (R^2=0.998); raw latent MSE misranks despite small signal; the XY-probe rowspace carries most candidate-quality signal; SCSA audits show improved ordering and endpoints. In PushT go50/go75, TRM-style task-state metrics improve SCSA ranking and final distance more cleanly than closed-loop success, motivating auxiliary hybrid costs in continuous manipulation.

Conclusion: TRM acts as a planner-facing repair for latent MPC, indicating when terminal reachability metrics should replace or augment raw latent proximity. It provides both empirical gains and mechanistic insight into why long-horizon terminal cues are misrepresented by raw latent distances, and supports hybrid-cost strategies in continuous tasks.

Abstract: Latent world models can contain the state needed for control, yet their terminal-cost interface can expose the planner to the wrong decision-relevant information. In common latent MPC, candidate sequences are ranked by Euclidean distance between predicted terminal and goal latent states; this assumes that raw latent distance weights reachability-relevant variables correctly. We propose trajectory reachability metrics (TRM), a post-hoc terminal-ranking method for fixed latent world models. TRM trains a small pairwise head from logged trajectory structure and uses it as a replacement or hybrid cost; the encoder, dynamics, sampler, optimizer, and evaluation manifests remain fixed. The key design choice is horizon-aware supervision: the metric is trained on broad, balanced temporal separations to match the long-horizon terminal candidate ranking problem. On a hard TwoRoom benchmark, raw latent planning with LeWorldModel (LeWM) reaches 7.0% success, while full-horizon TRM reaches 97.0%; shuffled temporal-label controls stay at 0.0%. The same recipe improves a PLDM baseline from 32.7% to 84.0% across three seeds, and a short-horizon TRM variant reaches only 35.0% with the 100,000 pair budget. In TwoRoom, we provide mechanistic evidence for why TRM works: XY position is linearly decodable (R^2=0.998), yet raw latent MSE misranks candidates; the XY-probe rowspace accounts for less than 1% of terminal-goal latent MSE but carries most candidate-quality signal; and SCSA audits show that TRM improves the ordering and selected endpoint seen by the planner. On PushT go50/go75, TRM-style task-state metrics improve SCSA ranking and selected final distance more cleanly than closed-loop success, motivating auxiliary hybrid costs in continuous manipulation. TRM is the planner-facing repair, and audits explain when terminal reachability metrics should replace or augment raw latent proximity.

</details>


### [294] [Memory-R2: Fair Credit Assignment for Long-Horizon Memory-Augmented LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21768)
*Sikuan Yan,Ahmed Bahloul,Ercong Nie,Susanna Schwarzmann,Riccardo Trivisonno,Volker Tresp,Yunpu Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Memory-R2 enables long-horizon RL for memory-augmented LLM agents by combining global end-to-end rewards with local, same-state memory-operation comparisons; adds co-learning of memory components and a curriculum to extend horizon.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses non-stationary, memory-induced environment changes in multi-session RL, which bias trajectory-level credit and violate group-relative assumptions like GRPO.

Method: Introduces Memory-R2 with LoGo-GRPO: a dual optimization approach that preserves end-to-end rewards (global) while performing fair local reroll comparisons from the same intermediate memory state (local). Employs shared-parameter co-learning by training a fact extractor and a memory manager from a single LLM backbone with role-specific prompts. Uses a progressive curriculum to grow the horizon from 8 to 16 to 32 sessions.

Result: Claims to provide an effective training paradigm for memory-augmented LLM agents in long-horizon multi-session settings, yielding fairer group comparisons, more precise memory construction supervision, and stabilized training via the curriculum.

Conclusion: Memory-R2 offers an integrated framework for simultaneous memory formation and evolution in LLMs, enabling robust long-horizon RL with memory through LoGo-GRPO and curriculum-based stabilization.

Abstract: Memory-augmented LLM agents enable interactions that extend beyond finite context windows by storing, updating, and reusing information across sessions. However, training such agents with reinforcement learning in multi-session environments is challenging because memory turns the agent's past actions into part of its future environment. Once different rollouts write, update, or delete different memories, they no longer share the same intermediate memory state, making trajectory-level comparisons fundamentally unfair. This violates a key assumption behind group-relative methods such as GRPO, where rollouts are compared as if they were sampled from the same effective environment. Consequently, trajectory-level rewards provide noisy or biased credit signals for long-horizon memory operations. To address this challenge, we introduce Memory-R2, a training framework for long-horizon memory-augmented LLM agents. Its core algorithm, LoGo-GRPO, combines local and global group-relative optimization. The global objective preserves end-to-end learning from long-horizon trajectory-level rewards, while local rerollouts compare different memory-operation outcomes from the same intermediate memory state, yielding fairer group comparisons and more precise supervision for memory construction. Beyond credit assignment, Memory-R2 jointly optimizes memory formation and memory evolution with a shared-parameter co-learning design, where a fact extractor and a memory manager are instantiated from the same LLM backbone through role-specific prompts. To stabilize multi-step RL over long memory horizons, we adopt a progressive curriculum that increases the training horizon from 8 to 16 to 32 sessions. Together, these components provide an effective training paradigm for memory-augmented LLM agents in long-horizon multi-session settings.

</details>


### [295] [An Evidence Hierarchy for Bayesian Object Classification via OSINT-Aided Heterogeneous Sensor Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22259)
*Jan Nausner,Michael Hubner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A context-aware, domain knowledge-enhanced fusion framework for heterogeneous sensor data to detect, localize, and classify CBRNE threats, using an evidence hierarchy, OSINT-based environment context, and Bayesian threat-type classification with domain-informed priors; evaluated in simulations with robust performance (up to 95% accuracy).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenges of heterogeneous sensors that detect only subsets of threats with varying reliability, high clutter, and scarce high-quality datasets, which hinder learning-based detection and classification.

Method: Propose a three-layer approach: (1) an evidence hierarchy that models direct, indicative, and contextual information; (2) incorporation of contextual environment data via OSINT into the fusion process; (3) a Bayesian threat-type classifier that uses priors informed by domain knowledge across all evidence levels.

Result: Evaluated in simulated scenarios, showing improved robustness to clutter and prior mismatch, achieving up to 95% overall classification accuracy.

Conclusion: The framework enhances multimodal threat detection/classification by leveraging structured evidence, external contextual data, and domain-informed priors; results are promising in simulations, warranting validation on real data and exploration of dataset requirements and scalability.

Abstract: Heterogeneous sensor fusion is vital for detecting, localizing, and classifying CBRNE threats. However, individual sensors are often only capable of detecting a subset of relevant threats with varying reliability or can even provide only indirect threat indications, making threat classification challenging. Furthermore, high clutter rates on the sensor side present a great challenge for fusion systems. Additionally, the limited availability of high quality datasets hinders the advancement of learning-based detection and classification models in smart sensors. To mitigate these sensor related shortcomings, a context-aware and domain knowledge-enhanced fusion process is proposed. First, a novel evidence hierarchy is established that enables modeling of direct, indicative, and contextual information. Second, contextual information about the environment is introduced into the fusion process, by collecting, processing, and exploiting OSINT inputs. Third, all levels of the evidence hierarchy are used to craft a Bayesian threat type classification mechanism with domain knowledge-informed priors. The proposed methodology is evaluated in simulated scenarios, and the results demonstrate the benefit of the proposed fusion approach in terms of robustness to clutter and prior mismatch, with an overall classification accuracy of up to 95%.

</details>


### [296] [Provable Robustness against Backdoor Attacks via the Primal-Dual Perspective on Differential Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21780)
*Aman Saxena,Jan Schuchardt,Yan Scholten,Stephan Günnemann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A modular, end-to-end framework for certifying robustness against backdoor attacks by unifying randomized smoothing with differential privacy via privacy profiles, enabling joint training-time and inference-time robustness certificates through composed mechanisms (DP-SGD and Deep Partition Aggregation with inference-time smoothing).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Backdoor attacks perturb training and test data jointly, requiring end-to-end robustness certificates. Existing smoothing methods address either training-time or inference-time perturbations in isolation, but not their combination within a single certificate.

Method: Frame randomized smoothing as a dual view of differential privacy through privacy profiles, which provide a numerical, composable procedure for heterogeneous mechanisms. Instantiate the framework with DP-SGD and Deep Partition Aggregation augmented by inference-time smoothing, and derive joint robustness guarantees. Use these compositions to obtain end-to-end certificates and validate them on standard benchmarks.

Result: A principled, modular framework that enables end-to-end robustness certificates against training-time and inference-time attacks by composing DP-based mechanisms with smoothing. Experimental results on MNIST and CIFAR-10 demonstrate effective joint robustness guarantees and practical viability of the approach.

Conclusion: The work delivers a general, principled method for certifying robustness under complex threat models (backdoor attacks) by leveraging composable DP-based mechanisms and existing analyses, with broad applicability and potential for tighter, end-to-end guarantees.

Abstract: Randomized smoothing is a powerful tool for certifying robustness to adversarial perturbations, including poisoning attacks via randomized training and evasion attacks via randomized inference. Extending these guarantees to backdoor attacks, where training and test data are jointly perturbed, remains challenging because training- and test-time randomized mechanisms must be analyzed within a single robustness certificate. We address this by connecting randomized smoothing to the dual view of differential privacy through privacy profiles, which provide a numerical procedure for composing heterogeneous mechanisms. The resulting framework enables tight, modular, end-to-end certification of complex, composed mechanisms while leveraging existing analyses of differentially private mechanisms. We instantiate the framework for DP-SGD and Deep Partition Aggregation with inference-time smoothing, deriving joint robustness guarantees against both training-time and inference-time attacks. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework. Overall, we provide a principled and general framework for using composite mechanisms to certify robustness under complex threat models that better capture the capabilities of real-world adversaries.

</details>


### [297] [MMD-Balls as Credal Sets: A PAC-Bayesian Framework for Epistemic Uncertainty in Test-Time Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21783)
*Ahanaf Hasan Ariq*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A PAC-Bayesian framework for test-time adaptation (TTA) with an MMD-based shift penalty, interpreting MMD-balls around the source as Walley-style credal sets to quantify epistemic uncertainty, yielding explicit bounds and geometric insights for when adaptation is warranted.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fill the gap between distribution-shift magnitude and prediction reliability in TTA by providing formal guarantees and a principled uncertainty quantification framework.

Method: Derive a PAC-Bayesian bound with an MMD-dependent shift penalty under an RKHS-Lipschitz loss; obtain a finite-sample bound via MMD concentration; prove a uniform worst-case risk bound over all distributions in the credal set with a lower/upper-risk decomposition; establish geodesic preservation bounds; interpret the credal set using Walley’s imprecise probability theory to separate epistemic from aleatoric uncertainty.

Result: Theoretical guarantees: (i) MMD-aware PAC-Bayesian bound; (ii) finite-sample bound; (iii) uniform risk bound over the credal set with lower/upper decomposition; (iv) geodesic preservation insights; a credal-set view enabling principled when-to-adapt decisions and a separation of epistemic/aleatoric uncertainty.

Conclusion: The credal-set interpretation provides a principled, uncertainty-aware criterion for TTA—quantifying how much shift is tolerable before adaptation, and delivering formal guarantees that connect shift magnitude to prediction reliability while preserving local feature geometry.

Abstract: Test-time adaptation (TTA) methods improve model performance under distribution shift but lack formal guarantees connecting shift magnitude to prediction reliability. We develop a PAC-Bayesian framework yielding generalization bounds explicitly parameterized by the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between source and target distributions. Our principal contribution is interpreting MMD-balls around the source distribution as credal sets in Walley's imprecise probability theory, yielding natural epistemic uncertainty quantification. We establish: (i) a PAC-Bayesian bound with an MMD-dependent shift penalty under an RKHS-Lipschitz loss assumption; (ii) a finite-sample version via MMD concentration; (iii) a uniform worst-case risk bound over all distributions in the credal set, with a lower-upper risk decomposition; and (iv) geodesic preservation bounds explaining why kernel-guided adaptation protects local feature geometry. The credal set interpretation separates epistemic from aleatoric uncertainty and provides a principled decision criterion for when adaptation is warranted.

</details>


### [298] [Understanding Multimodal Failure in Action-Chunking Behavioral Cloning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22493)
*Lorenzo Mazza,Massimiliano Datres,Ariel Rodriguez,Sebastian Bodenstedt,Gitta Kutyniok,Stefanie Speidel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multimodal imitation learning reveals distinct failure modes for latent-variable vs. action-space policies: regularization trades off preserving mode information and prior coverage, while transport smoothness constrains multimodality; empirical validation on synthetic tasks and robotics supports the mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Behavioral cloning can be ambiguous: the same observation can correspond to multiple valid actions. The paper investigates why two popular multimodal policy families fail differently and what design choices are needed to cover multiple demonstrated modes robustly.

Method: 1) Analyze latent-variable policies with posterior-prior regularization and its effect on deployment-time sampling and mode information. 2) Analyze action-space generative policies under base-to-action transport smoothness (Lipschitz constraints) and the need for sharp base-space transitions or off-support action-space bridges. 3) Empirical evaluation on synthetic multimodal tasks and robotic simulation benchmarks to validate the mechanisms.

Result: For latent-variable policies, posterior-prior regularization improves deployment-time sampling reliability but excessive regularization erases action-conditioned information needed to distinguish demonstrated modes; reducing regularization preserves mode information but then success depends on whether the prior covers the relevant latent regions. For action-space generative policies, multimodality is constrained by the smoothness of the base-to-action map: a small Lipschitz constant makes it hard to assign substantial probability to many well-separated modes. To cover many modes, one must have sharp transitions in base space or off-support bridge regions in action space. Experiments on synthetic multimodal tasks and robotic simulations support these mechanisms.

Conclusion: Multimodal imitation learning is constrained by regularization and transport properties: achieving robust coverage of multiple demonstrated modes requires balancing prior coverage with regularization in latent-variable policies, or shaping the base-to-action mapping to allow multiple modes (via sharp base transitions or bridge regions) in action-space policies. The reported experiments corroborate these insights and offer design guidance for multimodal behavioral cloning.

Abstract: Behavioral cloning becomes difficult when the same observation admits several valid actions. We study this problem for action-chunking policies and show that different multimodal parameterizations fail in different ways. For latent-variable policies, posterior-prior regularization makes deployment-time sampling more reliable, but excessive regularization removes the action-conditioned information needed to distinguish demonstrated modes. Reducing this regularization can preserve mode information, but then success depends on whether the prior covers the relevant latent regions. For action-space generative policies, multimodality is constrained by the smoothness of the base-to-action transport: a map with small Lipschitz constant cannot assign substantial probability to many well-separated modes. Covering many modes therefore requires either sharp transitions in base space or off-support bridge regions in action space. Experiments on synthetic multimodal tasks and robotic simulation benchmarks support these mechanisms.

</details>


### [299] [Three Costs of Amortizing Gaussian Process Inference with Neural Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21798)
*Robin Young*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Amortized neural processes approximate GP posteriors with O(n) inference; the KL divergence to GP predictions decomposes into three interpretable sources (label contamination, information bottleneck, amortization error). Bottleneck terms decay with representation dimension differently for SE and Matérn kernels; label contamination is O(1) in general while observation noise decays as O(1/n). Architectural guidance: size the representation to match kernel smoothness and input dimension, and employ second-order pooling instead of mean aggregation to close the amortization gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify the costs of amortizing Gaussian process inference via neural processes, and relate those costs to representation size, kernel smoothness, and input dimension; provide actionable architectural recommendations to improve variance prediction from context locations.

Method: Derives a KL-divergence bound between GP and latent neural process (LNP) predictives for a class of latent neural processes; decomposes the bound into three terms (label contamination, information bottleneck, amortization error); analyzes their dependence on representation dimension d and the kernel (squared-exponential and Matérn ν); derives decay rates (e^{-c d^{2/d_x}} for SE, d^{-2ν/d_x} for Matérn); discusses implications for architecture sizing and pooling strategies; proposes replacing mean aggregation with second-order pooling to mitigate amortization gap.

Result: A three-term KL decomposition for GP-LNP approximations with explicit decay rates for the bottleneck term under SE and Matérn kernels; identification of persistent label contamination cost and a diminishing amortization error with larger encodings; practical guidelines suggesting targeted representation sizing and second-order pooling to predict variance from context locations in the GP-amortization regime.

Conclusion: Characterizes the costs of GP amortization within the studied class, linking architectural choices to kernel smoothness and input dimensionality; provides concrete recommendations to reduce amortization gaps and improve variance prediction by transitioning from mean to second-order pooling.

Abstract: Neural processes amortize Gaussian process inference, replacing the exact $O(n^3)$ posterior with a learned $O(n)$ map from context sets to predictive distributions. For a class of latent neural processes, we bound the Kullback--Leibler (KL) divergence between the GP and LNP predictives, decomposing it into three interpretable sources, namely label contamination as the neural process uses label values to estimate a quantity that is label-independent in the exact GP, an information bottleneck because the finite-dimensional representation cannot resolve the full context geometry, and amortization error from a single encoder network shared across all contexts. The bottleneck truncation term decays in the representation dimension $d$ as $O(e^{-cd^{2/d_x}})$ for squared-exponential kernels on $\mathbb{R}^{d_x}$ where $c > 0$ is a kernel-dependent constant and as $O(d^{-2ν/d_x})$ for Matérn-$ν$ kernels, directly linking architecture sizing to kernel smoothness and input dimension. The label contamination term is $O(1)$ in general, with only the observation-noise component decaying as $O(1/n)$, identifying a persistent cost of routing uncertainty estimation through a label-dependent representation. These results characterize the costs of amortization within the analyzed class and yield architectural recommendations to predict variance from context locations alone in the GP-amortization regime, and replace mean aggregation with second-order pooling to close the dominant amortization gap.

</details>


### [300] [MoSA: Motion-constrained Stress Adaptation for Mitigating Real-to-Sim Gap in Continuum Dynamics via Learning Residual Anisotropy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22597)
*Jiaxu Wang,Junhao He,Jingkai Sun,Yi Gu,Yunyang Mo,Jiahang Cao,Qiang Zhang,Renjing Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MoSA introduces a physics-informed approach to model residual stress-driven anisotropy and heterogeneity on top of an isotropic backbone, using microplane-constrained redistribution in a cascaded network with motion supervision to improve real-to-sim dynamics and sim-to-real transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world objects exhibit mild anisotropy and heterogeneity that are not captured by homogeneous, isotropic physical models. After calibrating an near-isotropic backbone, residual effects become the main bottleneck, and end-to-end NN models lack strong physical priors, leading to inefficiency and overfitting.

Method: Use an isotropic physics backbone as a prior and learn residual stress operators that redistribute stiffness via microplane constraints in a cascaded, physics-informed network. Train with supervision on temporal and spatial derivatives of the deformation field to enforce motion consistency.

Result: Achieves superior accuracy, generalization, and robustness in learned dynamics, with physically meaningful residual anisotropy; improves real-to-sim dynamics and translates to more reliable sim-to-real transfer in a robot manipulation setting.

Conclusion: MoSA effectively targets residual anisotropy/heterogeneity beyond near-isotropic calibration, delivering a practical framework that enhances data efficiency and transfer performance while preserving physical interpretability of learned residuals.

Abstract: Learning real-world dynamics from visual observations is crucial for various domains. A common strategy is to calibrate simulators by estimating physical parameters, yet accuracy is ultimately bounded by the underlying physical models, which often assume materials are homogeneous and isotropic. Even if reasonable, real-world objects typically exhibit mild anisotropy and heterogeneity. After the near-isotropic backbone is well calibrated, these residual effects become the key bottleneck for further closing the real-to-sim gap. Although neural networks can fit dynamics end-to-end, such black-box modeling discards strong physical priors, leading to poor data efficiency and overfitting. Therefore, we propose MoSA, a motion-constrained stress adaptation framework that targets these residual effects to further improve real-to-sim dynamics learning. MoSA uses an isotropic model as a physics prior and learns residual stress operators to capture mild anisotropy and heterogeneity. It progressively adapts stresses via microplane-constrained redistribution in a physics-informed cascaded network. We further impose motion constraints by supervising temporal and spatial derivatives of the deformation field. Experimentally, our learned dynamics achieves superior accuracy, generalization, and robustness, while learning physically meaningful residual anisotropy. Finally, we validate MoSA in a robot manipulation setting, showing that better real-to-sim dynamics modeling translates into more reliable sim-to-real transfer. Project Page is available at https://mercerai.github.io/MoSA/.

</details>


### [301] [Why Semantic Entropy Fails: Geometry-Aware and Calibrated Uncertainty for Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21801)
*Zheyuan Zhang,Kaiwen Shi,Han Bao,Zehong Wang,Tianyi Ma,Yanfang Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GCPO introduces a geometry-aware, calibrated uncertainty framework to regulate gradient variance in post-training LLMs, addressing anisotropic and calibration gaps in entropy-based estimators and improving gradient tracking and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-training with critic-free models relies on uncertainty signals to balance informative versus noisy signals. Entropy-based estimators used in methods like GRPO suffer from instability and misalignment with optimization dynamics, motivating a principled, geometry-aware approach.

Method: Propose Geometric-aware Calibrated Policy Optimization (GCPO), which combines geometry-aware measures of semantic disagreement with reward-based calibration to align uncertainty with learning signal strength. The approach formalizes two gaps—anisotropic gap and calibration gap—and analyzes them both empirically and theoretically.

Result: Experiments across multiple benchmarks show GCPO more faithfully tracks gradient variability and yields consistent post-training performance gains, demonstrating robust alignment between uncertainty and optimization dynamics.

Conclusion: Design uncertainty signals that faithfully reflect optimization dynamics; geometry-aware calibrated uncertainty provides a principled framework for robust post-training of LLMs.

Abstract: Post-training has become central to improving reasoning and alignment in large language models, where critic-free models enable scalable learning from model-generated outputs but lack principled mechanisms to distinguish informative from noisy signals. Recent approaches leverage response-level measures as uncertainty signals to regulate group-based optimization methods such as GRPO. Yet their empirical success remains unstable and unclear in how they influence optimization dynamics. In this paper, we provide, to our knowledge, the first principled formulation that interprets uncertainty signals as mechanisms for characterizing and regulating gradient variance and learning signal quality. Based on both empirical and theoretical analysis, we identify two critical gaps of current entropy-based estimators: The anisotropic gap and The calibration gap. Motivated by this analysis, we propose Geometric-aware Calibrated Policy Optimization (GCPO), a novel framework integrating geometry-aware measures to capture semantic disagreement with reward-based calibration to align uncertainty with learning signal strength. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that our approach more faithfully tracks gradient variability and consistently improves post-training performance. Our results highlight the importance of designing uncertainty signals that are aligned with optimization dynamics, offering a principled perspective for robust post-training.

</details>


### [302] [Same Architecture, Different Capacity: Optimizer-Induced Spectral Scaling Laws](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21803)
*Nandan Kumar Jha,Brandon Reagen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Optimizer choice dramatically affects representation scaling in transformers; AdamW vs Muon yield different spectral scaling, showing optimization is a separate axis of scaling beyond architecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prevailing scaling laws treat the optimizer as a fixed training detail. This work investigates whether optimization dynamics influence how spectral capacity scales with model width, and how this interacts with architecture.

Method: Compute eigenspectra of feed-forward representations using soft and hard spectral ranks across different optimizers (AdamW vs Muon) and architectures/width schedules; quantify spectral scaling via a beta exponent in the relationship between width and rank; compare optimizer effects to architectural interventions (attention rank, positional encoding).

Result: The same Transformer architecture shows markedly different spectral scaling under different optimizers. For rare-token representations, AdamW shows weak hard-rank scaling (beta=0.44) while Muon achieves near-linear scaling (beta=1.02), a 2.3x increase in the scaling exponent. This divergence is not reducible to validation loss: AdamW configs can match low-rank perplexity with extended training but have different spectral geometry. Hard-soft rank asymmetry indicates optimizers shape capacity distribution across eigenmodes. Optimizer-induced spectral shifts often exceed architectural effects.

Conclusion: Optimization is a first-class axis of representation scaling; optimization–architecture co-design is warranted to fully leverage spectral capacity in Transformer representations.

Abstract: Scaling laws have made language-model performance predictable from model size, data, and compute, but they typically treat the optimizer as a fixed training detail. We show that this assumption misses a fundamental axis of representation scaling: how effectively the optimizer converts added FFN width into utilized spectral capacity. Using eigenspectra of feed-forward network representations, measured through soft and hard spectral-ranks, we find that \emph{the same Transformer architecture realizes markedly different spectral scaling laws when trained with different optimizers}. Holding architecture and width schedule fixed, AdamW exhibits weak hard-rank scaling ($β$=0.44) on rare-token (TAIL) representations where learning is known to be hardest, whereas Muon achieves linear scaling ($β$=1.02) in the same regimes, a $2.3\times$ increase in the scaling exponent. This difference is not reducible to validation loss: AdamW configurations can match low-rank Dion variants in perplexity, under extended training, while exhibiting sharply different spectral geometry, demonstrating that matched loss does not imply matched representation structure. Hard--soft rank asymmetry further reveals that optimizers differ not only in how much capacity is realized, but also in how that capacity is structured across eigenmodes. To disentangle optimizer effects from architectural ones, we compare against architectural interventions (e.g., attention rank and positional encoding), and find that optimizer-induced spectral shifts often exceed the architectural effects. These results suggest optimization as a first-class axis of representation scaling, motivating optimizer--architecture co-design.

</details>


### [303] [Symbolic Density Estimation for Discrete Distributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21813)
*Ziwen Liu,Meng Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Symbolic Density Estimation (SDE), an unsupervised method to discover closed-form discrete distributions by composing elementary operations in a search space; supports zero-inflation and finite mixtures; provides a benchmark dataset; shows accurate recovery of benchmark families and improved goodness-of-fit on real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete probability laws are numerous but interpretable closed-form distributions are scarce; there is a need for automatic, interpretable, parametric forms without manual derivations.

Method: Symbolic density estimation via a structured search space of analytic operations, guided by domain-specific priors and evolutionary search; includes a validity-aware inference stage; extends framework to zero-inflated and finite mixture distributions; provides a benchmark dataset for evaluation.

Result: The algorithm recovers all benchmark distribution families with accurate parameter estimates; in a real-data application it yields concise, interpretable mixture models that improve goodness-of-fit relative to standard models.

Conclusion: SDE enables automated discovery of interpretable discrete distributions, expanding the set of usable families and providing resources for evaluation; it demonstrates practical gains in model interpretability and fit.

Abstract: Discrete probability laws underpin statistical modeling, yet the catalog of interpretable distributions has expanded only gradually through centuries of case-by-case mathematical derivations. We introduce symbolic density estimation (SDE), an unsupervised framework that automatically recovers closed-form probability mass functions by composing elementary analytic operations within a structured search space. Our method integrates domain-specific structural priors with evolutionary search and a validity-aware inference stage, and it extends to richer distribution families such as zero inflation and finite mixtures. To support systematic evaluation and future research, we contribute a benchmark dataset spanning a broad collection of commonly used discrete distributions. The proposed algorithm recovers all benchmark families with accurate parameter estimates. A real data application shows that it identifies concise and interpretable mixture models that improve goodness-of-fit over standard models.

</details>


### [304] [Beyond Scalar Objectives: Expert-Feedback-Driven Autonomous Experimentation for Scientific Discovery at the Nanoscale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21820)
*Ralph Bulanadi,Jefferey Baxter,Arpan Biswas,Hiroshi Funakubo,Dennis Meier,Jan Schultheiß,Rama Vasudevan,Yongtao Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deep-Kernel Pairwise Learning (DKPL) integrates expert pairwise judgments into autonomous microscopy experiments to learn a latent utility function and guide active learning, removing reliance on predefined scalar descriptors. It demonstrates accurate nanoscale structure learning and domain-wall characterization in ferroelectric materials.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bayesian optimization with fixed scalar descriptors may miss scientifically important signals; expert interdisciplinary knowledge can reveal more informative cues. There is a need for autonomous systems that can leverage such expertise directly in the loop.

Method: DKPL uses a deep kernel framework to learn a latent utility from expert pairwise comparisons of experiment outputs. This latent utility guides subsequent experiments in an active learning loop without requiring explicit scalar objectives.

Result: The approach learns physically meaningful nanoscale structures and efficiently prioritizes high-information measurement regions on a model dataset with ground truth. It also distinguishes high vs. low characteristic domain-wall angles in BiFeO3 and discovers head-to-head vs. tail-to-tail domain walls in ErMnO3, demonstrating expert-guided autonomy in microscopy.

Conclusion: DKPL provides a pathway to integrate expert knowledge into autonomous microscopy, enabling self-driving laboratories to tackle problems beyond scalar-metric-driven learning and broadening the applicability of autonomous experimentation in materials science.

Abstract: Self-driving laboratories or autonomous experimentation are emerging as transformative platforms for accelerating scientific discovery. Bayesian optimization (BO) is among the most widely used machine learning frameworks for these purposes, but these BO-based frameworks rely on predefined scalar descriptors to guide experimentation. In many situations, the determination of an appropriate scalar descriptor can be challenging, and may fail to capture subtle yet scientifically important phenomena apparent to experts with interdisciplinary insight. To overcome this limitation, here we develop deep-kernel pairwise learning (DKPL), an approach for autonomous microscopy experiments which incorporates human expertise and interdisciplinary scientific knowledge into an active learning loop. Instead of relying on explicit scalar objectives, DKPL enables experts to directly evaluate which experimental output is more promising using interdisciplinary knowledge. DKPL then learns a latent utility function from these expert judgements to guide subsequent autonomous microscopy experiments. We demonstrate DKPL's performance in learning physically meaningful nanoscale structures while effectively prioritizing high-information measurement regions using an experimental model dataset with known ground truth. We further apply DKPL to analyze the character of ferroelectric domain walls, where we find DKPL capable of distinguishing between high and low characteristic domain-wall angles in bismuth ferrite, and able to discover both head-to-head and tail-to-tail domain-wall character in erbium manganite. This development establishes an approach to integrate expert knowledge into autonomous microscopy experiments and demonstrates a pathway toward expert-guided self-driving laboratories capable of addressing scientific problems beyond the limits of scalar-metrics-driven learning.

</details>


### [305] [On-Policy Consistency Training Improves LLM Safety with Minimal Capability Degradation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21834)
*Andy Han,Kristina Fujimoto,Avidan Shah,Kiet Nguyen,Kai Xu,Chen Yueh-Han,Ilia Sucholutsky,Rico Angell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OPCT (On-Policy Consistency Training) improves alignment by training the model on its own outputs under contrastive prompts, instead of offline supervised fine-tuning on fixed data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline SFT with consistency training tends to memorize training surface forms, hindering generalization and enabling regressions. A on-policy, self-supervised consistency approach could yield better generalization and robustness across safety challenges.

Method: OPCT computes the training objective over the model's own responses to prompts, supervised by itself conditioned on corresponding contrastive prompts. Evaluated across three safety axes (sycophancy, jailbreaking, safety awareness) and three model families, compared to SFT-based consistency.

Result: OPCT outperforms SFT on all safety desiderata: sycophancy reduced from 15.4% baseline to 8.1% (SFT 11.2%); jailbreaking defense near 99% vs. 87% for SFT; safety awareness better on 2/3 models and on par on the third; OPCT avoids major capability regressions seen with SFT (e.g., no 28-point drop on MATH-500).

Conclusion: Consistency training is more effective when implemented as OPCT, particularly for generalization beyond the training distribution; on-policy, self-supervised consistency yields stronger safety and robustness than offline SFT-based approaches.

Abstract: Aligned models can misbehave in several ways: they are often sycophantic, fall victim to jailbreaks, or fail to include appropriate safety warnings. Consistency training is a promising new alignment paradigm to mitigate such failures by training invariants into the model using contrastive input pairs. Existing consistency training procedures generate the supervision signal once, offline, and use supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to update the model. Unfortunately, the resulting models tend to merely memorize the surface forms of the training distribution and thus generalize poorly and regress in their capabilities. We introduce On-Policy Consistency Training (OPCT), a new consistency training approach where the objective is computed over the model's own responses to prompts, supervised by itself conditioned on corresponding contrastive prompts. We evaluate OPCT on three safety axes: sycophancy, jailbreaking, and safety awareness. Across three model families, OPCT outperforms its SFT counterpart on all safety desiderata. It nearly halves the sycophancy rate relative to baseline (8.1% vs. 15.4%, compared to 11.2% for SFT). Under an adaptive per-target attacker, OPCT holds jailbreak defense success near 99% on held-out jailbreak behaviors, whereas SFT achieves 87% on average. On safety awareness, OPCT outperforms SFT in two out of three models, and matches it on the other. OPCT also largely avoids the capability regressions that SFT induces, such as a 28-point drop on MATH-500. Our results suggest that consistency training is best implemented as OPCT rather than as SFT, especially when generalization beyond the training distribution is desired.

</details>


### [306] [Energy-Gated Attention: Spectral Salience as an Inductive Bias for Transformer Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21842)
*Athanasios Zeris*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Energy-Gated Attention (EGA) biases transformer attention toward tokens with higher spectral energy, as determined by a learned projection of key embeddings. It yields consistent, dataset-independent gains on language tasks with minimal overhead, and shows that energy directions are data-adaptive and non-sinusoidal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard transformer attention treats tokens as equally salient. Analogous to turbulence where coherent structures dominate energy, informative positions in text (content words, syntactic heads, discourse markers) may concentrate spectral energy. EGA gates value aggregation by this energy signal, aiming to amplify informative tokens in attention.

Method: Compute spectral energy of key token embeddings via a single learned linear projection to identify the dominant spectral mode. Gate the value aggregation by this energy. Evaluate on TinyShakespeare and Penn Treebank; perform ablations across wavelet families (fixed Morlet, Daubechies db2/db4, parametric Morlet); analyze data-adaptive energy directions and learn an energy threshold tau (found near 0.35).

Result: On TinyShakespeare, +0.103 validation loss improvement with +12,480 parameters (<0.26% overhead) and no measurable extra compute. On Penn Treebank, +0.101 improvement, suggesting dataset independence. Ablations indicate fixed bases are suboptimal; learned wavelet packets are promising. Learned energy threshold tau ≈ 0.35, corresponding to ~36% of tokens with above-average spectral energy in English text.

Conclusion: Energy-Gated Attention demonstrates that token-level spectral energy can effectively guide attention, with a data-adaptive energy direction outperforming fixed bases. This opens avenues for learned spectral bases (e.g., wavelet packets) and further exploration of energy-based gating in NLP.

Abstract: Standard transformer attention computes pairwise similarity between queries and keys, treating all tokens as equally salient regardless of their intrinsic informational content. In turbulent fluid dynamics, coherent structures -- the energetically dominant, spatially organized patterns that persist amid background chaos -- carry a disproportionate fraction of total energy and govern all transport.
