We propose that tokens play an analogous role in transformer attention: informationally dense positions (morphological boundaries, syntactic heads, discourse markers) concentrate spectral energy and should attract proportionally more attention than background tokens (function words, repeated patterns, low-information filler). We propose Energy-Gated Attention (EGA): a simple modification that gates value aggregation by the spectral energy of key token embeddings, computed by a single learned linear projection that discovers the dominant spectral mode of the embedding field. On TinyShakespeare, EGA achieves +0.103 validation loss improvement with only 12,480 additional parameters (<0.26% overhead) and no measurable computational cost. The result is consistent on Penn Treebank (+0.101), demonstrating dataset independence. A systematic ablation across three wavelet families (fixed Morlet, Daubechies db2/db4, and a parametric Morlet) establishes that fixed structured bases are suboptimal -- the optimal energy direction is data-adaptive and non-sinusoidal -- while identifying learned wavelet packets as a promising open direction. The learned energy threshold converges to tau ~= 0.35 independently of initialization, corresponding to the fraction (~36%) of tokens carrying above-average spectral energy in English text, a stable linguistic property consistent with the fraction of content words in running English text.

</details>


### [307] [Geometry-Adaptive Explainer for Faithful Dictionary-Based Interpretability under Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21849)
*Sungjun Lim,Heedong Kim,Andrew Lee,Kyungwoo Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Distribution shift rotates the model's active subspace, misaligning in-distribution (ID) dictionary explainers with the OOD subspace; Geometry-Adaptive Explainer (GAE) realigns the dictionary to the OOD-active subspace using unlabeled activations and no gradient updates, improving causal faithfulness and often beating training-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mechanistic interpretability relies on dictionary-based explainers, but their faithfulness under out-of-distribution (OOD) shifts is under-studied. The work investigates how subspace rotation under shift degrades faithfulness and aims to provide a robust, data-efficient adaptation.

Method: Formalize faithfulness gap as a geometric distance between the ID dictionary and the OOD-active subspace. Introduce Geometry-Adaptive Explainer (GAE) that realigns the dictionary with the OOD-active subspace while preserving the original feature structure. The method uses only unlabeled OOD activations and requires no gradient updates.

Result: The excess loss due to using the ID explainer after OOD shift is bounded quadratically by the second-moment shift. Empirically, GAE matches or surpasses training-based baselines in causal faithfulness across multiple models and OOD settings.

Conclusion: GAE provides a simple, label-free, and effective approach to maintain causal faithfulness of dictionary-based explainers under OOD shifts by realigning the explainer to the OOD-active subspace.

Abstract: Mechanistic interpretability aims to explain a model's behavior by identifying causally responsible internal structures. Dictionary-based explainers such as sparse autoencoders and transcoders are a primary tool, but their faithfulness under out-of-distribution (OOD) shift has received little systematic attention. We show that distribution shift rotates the subspace that the model actively uses, misaligning the explainer's dictionary trained on in-distribution (ID) activations. We formalize this misalignment as the faithfulness gap, a geometric distance between the ID dictionary and the OOD-active subspace, and show that it controls OOD faithfulness degradation. To reduce this gap, we propose the Geometry-Adaptive Explainer (GAE), which realigns the explainer's dictionary with the OOD-active subspace while preserving the original feature structure. This requires only unlabeled OOD activations and no gradient updates. We prove that GAE improves over the unadapted ID explainer, with excess loss bounded quadratically by the second-moment shift. Empirically, GAE even matches or surpasses all training-based baselines in causal faithfulness across multiple models and OOD settings.

</details>


### [308] [OPPO: Bayesian Value Recursion for Token-Level Credit Assignment in LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21851)
*Yu Li,Rui Miao,Tian Lan,Zhengling Qi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OPPO: a trajectory-aware, oracle-guided per-token RLHF method that updates a running success probability along the sequence to produce token-level advantages, enhancing reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RLHF methods either assign a single trajectory-level advantage (GRPO) or rely on isolated per-token signals without trajectory context. There is a need for informative, stable credit assignment that emphasizes pivotal reasoning steps and reduces variance.

Method: Accumulate the oracle signal along the trajectory to yield a running, closed-form Bayesian update of the model's probability of eventual success. This provides token-level advantages with no learned value network or extra rollouts and only one extra forward pass. Two estimators are offered: self-oracle (reuses the student, recovering on-policy distillation reward as a special case) and teacher-oracle (uses a stronger frozen model for scoring).

Result: Empirically improves over GRPO, DAPO, and SDPO by up to +6.0 points on AMC'23 and +5.2 points on AIME'24 across seven math/science/code benchmarks; gains widen with longer responses.

Conclusion: OPPO delivers a principled, efficient approach to per-token credit assignment in RLHF for LLM reasoning, combining Bayesian-style trajectory updates with discriminative signals and variance reduction, yielding consistent performance gains.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has become the standard recipe for improving LLM reasoning, but the dominant algorithm GRPO assigns a single trajectory-level advantage to every token, diluting the signal at pivotal reasoning steps and injecting noise at uninformative ones. Critic-free alternatives derived from on-policy distillation supply per-token signals through oracle-conditioned likelihood ratios, yet apply each signal in isolation from the trajectory-level evidence accumulated up to that position. We propose Oracle-Prompted Policy Optimization (OPPO), which rests on a single observation: the oracle signal used by prior distillation-style methods for local discrimination is also the natural Bayesian update of the model's belief about eventual success. Accumulating the signal along a trajectory yields, in closed form and at the cost of one extra forward pass, a running estimate of the success probability at every position, together with a token-level advantage that requires no learned value network and no additional rollouts. A first-order analysis factorizes the advantage into the per-token discrimination signal used by distillation methods modulated by a state weight that concentrates credit on genuinely pivotal tokens, with a directional variance-reduction guarantee. The framework admits two estimators differing only in which model scores the evidence: a \textit{self-oracle} that reuses the student and recovers the on-policy distillation reward as a strict special case, and a \textit{teacher-oracle} that delegates scoring to a stronger frozen model. On two base LLMs across seven mathematics, science, and code reasoning benchmarks, OPPO improves over GRPO, DAPO, and SDPO by up to $+6.0$ points on AMC'23 and $+5.2$ points on AIME'24, with gains that widen monotonically with response length.

</details>


### [309] [The Illusion of Reasoning: Exposing Evasive Data Contamination in LLMs via Zero-CoT Truncation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21856)
*Yifan Lan,Yuanpu Cao,Hanyu Wang,Lu Lin,Jinghui Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Zero-CoT Probe (ZCP) to detect data contamination in LLMs by truncating chain-of-thought, exposing memorization, and comparing zero-CoT performance on original and isomorphically perturbed data, plus a Contamination Confidence metric; demonstrates robustness against direct and evasive contamination; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Objective evaluation of LLM reasoning is compromised by data contamination, including stealthy, paraphrased data; existing detectors struggle to identify such contamination.

Method: Zero-CoT Probe (ZCP) operates as a black-box detector by truncating the full Chain-of-Thought to reveal latent shortcut mappings. It compares zero-CoT performance on the original benchmark with an isomorphically perturbed reference dataset. Introduces Contamination Confidence to quantify both likelihood and severity of contamination.

Result: ZCP robustly detects both direct and evasive data contamination across contaminated and fine-tuned contaminated models; demonstrates that model-generated reasoning can mask memorization; code available at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: ZCP helps separate memorization from genuine reasoning, enabling more reliable contamination detection beyond binary labeling and offering a practical tool for evaluating LLMs in contaminated environments.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive reasoning abilities across a wide range of tasks, but data contamination undermines the objective evaluation of these capabilities. This problem is further exacerbated by malicious model publishers who use evasive, or indirect, contamination strategies, such as paraphrasing benchmark data to evade existing detection methods and artificially boost leaderboard performance. Current approaches struggle to reliably detect such stealthy contamination. In this work, we uncover a critical phenomenon: a model's generated reasoning steps actively mask its underlying memorization. Inspired by this, we propose the Zero-CoT Probe (ZCP), a novel black-box detection method that deliberately truncates the entire Chain-of-Thought (CoT) process to expose latent shortcut mappings. To further isolate memorization from the model's intrinsic problem-solving capabilities, ZCP compares the model's zero-CoT performance on the original benchmark against an isomorphically perturbed reference dataset. Furthermore, we introduce Contamination Confidence, a metric that quantifies both the likelihood and severity of contamination, moving beyond simple binary classifications. Extensive experiments on both previously identified contaminated models and specially fine-tuned contaminated models demonstrate that ZCP robustly detects both direct and evasive data contamination. The code for ZCP is accessible at https://github.com/Yifan-Lan/zero-cot-probe.

</details>


### [310] [When to Switch, Not Just What: Transition Quality Prediction in Clash Royale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21868)
*Heeyun Heo,Huy Kang Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces TQP (Transition Quality Predictor) to address zero switching cost in strategy recommendations for competitive games. Proposes Who->When->What pipeline with PersonaGate, TimingGate, and ScoreFusion to account for switching costs and individual propensities. Demonstrates improved discriminative power using SwitchGap (+10.4 pp at 5.4% recommendation rate) and notes loss-triggered switchers benefit most from subtype-conditioned guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Empirical finding that switching frequency correlates inversely with win rate and that traditional recommendations overlook switching costs and individual heterogeneity, leading to suboptimal guidance.

Method: Three-stage pipeline (Who -> When -> What): PersonaGate suppresses recommendations for consistent players; TimingGate identifies moments when switching is beneficial; ScoreFusion combines adoptability with predicted transition quality (delta WR). Introduces SwitchGap metric to evaluate policy discriminability without treating observed switches as optimal ground truth. Uses subtype/state matching baselines to control for natural recovery.

Result: SwitchGap of +10.4 percentage points at 5.4% recommendation rate; loss-triggered switchers, although lowest performers, gain the most from subtype-conditioned guidance; full pipeline demonstrates improved discrimination and targeted guidance over naive strategies.

Conclusion: Accounting for switching costs and individual differences via a transition-level decision framework can yield substantially better, targeted strategy recommendations in dynamic environments, with potential applicability beyond Clash Royale.

Abstract: In competitive games, players frequently switch strategies after losing streaks, yet our analysis of 926,334 match records from 34,619 Clash Royale players reveals a counterintuitive pattern: switching frequency is inversely associated with the win rate, with effects that vary substantially across players and situational contexts. We attribute this to a limitation common in many prior recommendation systems, which evaluate strategies by expected quality while overlooking the behavioral cost of switching and individual differences in switching propensity. We refer to this implicit premise as the Zero Switching Cost Assumption. To address this, we reformulate strategy recommendation as a transition-level decision problem and instantiate it as TQP (Transition Quality Predictor), a three-stage pipeline structured as Who -> When -> What. PersonaGate suppresses recommendations for players whose strategic consistency is empirically associated with superior outcomes. TimingGate identifies moments when switching is likely to yield a net benefit over staying, using a subtype- and state-matched baseline to control for natural win-rate recovery. ScoreFusion ranks candidate strategies by combining an adoptability signal with predicted transition quality (delta WR). We further introduce SwitchGap, an evaluation metric that measures a policy's discriminative quality without treating observed player choices as optimal ground truth. This property is particularly important because the most frequent switchers record the lowest win rates. The full pipeline achieves a SwitchGap of +10.4 percentage points at a recommendation rate of 5.4%, and loss-triggered switchers, despite being the lowest-performing group, benefit the most from subtype-conditioned guidance.

</details>


### [311] [Noise Schedule Design for Diffusion Models: An Optimal Control Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21911)
*Seo Taek Kong,Weina Wang,R. Srikant*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Treats noise scheduling in diffusion models as an optimal control problem where the Fisher information is the state and the noise schedule is the control; derives sufficient conditions for achieving the commonly observed d/n sampling error scaling; introduces a parametric family of noise schedules that generalizes exponential and sigmoid schedules and yields closed-form expressions under a data-distribution assumption, with tuning improving FID scores.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-model noise schedules are central to performance but current theory only covers a few specific schedules; a principled framework could unify design, provide guarantees, and guide practical schedule selection.

Method: Model the forward diffusion as an ODE with control input equal to the noise schedule; track Fisher information as the state; define an objective functional that upper-bounds the KL sampling error; solve the resulting optimal control problem to obtain sufficient conditions for schedulers; under a parametric data distribution, derive closed-form noise schedules; generalize exponential and sigmoid schedules with extra parameters, and tune these parameters to improve empirical metrics (FID).

Result: Yielded sufficient conditions under which the diffusion process attains the tilde-O(d/n) sampling error bound; introduced a parametric family of noise schedules that includes standard exponential/sigmoid shapes and provides closed-form expressions; demonstrated that systematic parameter tuning produces improved FID scores on image-generation benchmarks.

Conclusion: Provides a principled framework linking noise schedules, Fisher information dynamics, and KL sampling error, enabling design of practical schedules with provable guarantees and empirical benefits. The approach relies on an upper bound argument and a parametric data-distribution assumption; further work may explore necessity of conditions, robustness, and applicability across datasets.

Abstract: We develop a principled framework for analyzing and designing noise schedules in diffusion models. We show that one can recast this design problem as an optimal control problem, whose state is the Fisher information of the diffusion process which evolves according to an ODE and the control input is the noise schedule. The objective of the optimal control problem is a functional involving the Fisher information, which is shown to be an upper bound on the Kullback-Leibler sampling error. By solving this optimal control problem, we obtain sufficient conditions on noise schedules under which state-of-the-art $\tilde{\mathcal{O}} (d/n)$ sampling error is achievable, where $d$ is the data dimension and $n$ is the number of discretization steps. While existing theoretical work also prove that $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d/n)$ sampling error bounds are achievable, these results hold for specific noise schedules, which do not include the schedules used in practice. Under a further parametric assumption on the data distribution, we show that one can obtain closed-form expressions for the noise schedules. These noise schedules generalize standard empirical schedules such as exponential and sigmoid schedules by allowing additional parameters that can be tuned. Systematically tuning the parameters of these schedules yields new schedules that achieve superior FID scores on image generation benchmarks.

</details>


### [312] [CausalGuard: Conformal Inference under Graph Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21928)
*Vikash Singh,Weicong Chen,Debargha Ganguly,Yanyan Zhang,Nengbo Wang,Sreehari Sankar,Mohsen Hariri,Alexander Nemecek,Chaoda Song,Shouren Wang,Biyao Zhang,Van Yang,Erman Ayday,Jing Ma,Vipin Chaudhary*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CausalGuard: a structure-weighted conformal method for estimating conditional average treatment effects under unknown causal graphs by integrating LLM priors, CI pruning, and BIC reweighting to calibrate graph-conditional pseudo-outcomes, achieving finite-sample coverage and robust CATE estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph misspecification jeopardizes validity and graph-agnostic conformal methods require large padding; need distribution-free finite-sample coverage while preserving identification-based CATE accuracy.

Method: Generate candidate DAGs from LLM edge priors; prune via conditional independence tests; reweight by BIC; compute graph-conditional doubly robust pseudo-outcomes; aggregate using posterior weights; combine into a composite nonconformity score; calibrate to obtain distribution-free marginal coverage for the aggregated pseudo-outcome; under identification assumptions, converge of conditional mean to true CATE.

Result: Across five benchmarks, achieves mean coverage above nominal 90% for target; reduces interval width compared to graph-agnostic baselines with large padding; stress tests show suppression of invalid collider adjustment and stability to misspecified priors when candidates are data-supported.

Conclusion: With causal identification, overlap, nuisance-stability, and concentration on target-aligned valid adjustment strategies, CausalGuard achieves finite-sample validity and consistent CATE estimation; it provides robust calibration under plausible model misspecification.

Abstract: Estimating treatment effects from observational data requires choosing an adjustment set, but valid adjustment depends on an unknown causal graph. Graph misspecification can cause under-coverage, while graph-agnostic conformal wrappers may regain nominal coverage only through large padding. We introduce CausalGuard, a structure-weighted conformal framework that calibrates after aggregating graph-conditional doubly robust pseudo-outcomes. Candidate DAGs are proposed from an LLM-derived edge prior, pruned by conditional-independence tests, and reweighted by Bayesian Information Criterion. A composite nonconformity score then calibrates the posterior-weighted pseudo-outcome. CausalGuard provides distribution-free finite-sample marginal coverage for this aggregated pseudo-outcome; under causal identification, overlap, conditional-mean nuisance stability, and concentration on target-aligned valid adjustment strategies, its conditional mean converges to the true Conditional Average Treatment Effect. Across five benchmarks, CausalGuard attains mean coverage above the nominal 90% level for the directly evaluable target and reduces width when graph-agnostic conformal baselines require large padding. Stress tests show that CausalGuard suppresses invalid collider adjustment and remains stable under misspecified priors when the retained candidate set is data-supported.

</details>


### [313] [Optimal Guarantees for Auditing Rényi Differentially Private Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21938)
*Benjamin D. Kim,Lav R. Varshney,Daniel Alabi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A black-box auditing framework for Rényi DP using Donsker-Varadhan DV estimators, providing non-asymptotic confidence intervals, minimax-optimal sample complexity, and strong DP-SGD auditing on MNIST/CIFAR-10.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Auditability of Rényi DP in practical ML deployments where only black-box access is available. The work separates statistical estimation error from the algorithmic privacy leakage and establishes information-theoretic optimal guarantees for RDP auditing.

Method: A hypothesis-testing-based framework employing class-restricted Donsker-Varadhan variational estimators to estimate Rényi divergence between neighboring executions, yielding non-asymptotic confidence intervals and enabling separation of estimation error from privacy leakage; proves minimax lower bounds and applies to DP-SGD in a black-box setting.

Result: Derives explicit, non-asymptotic confidence intervals for RDP auditing; demonstrates information-theoretic optimality (up to log factors) via matching minimax lower bounds; empirically yields stronger lower bounds on RDP than prior black-box methods, especially at small/moderate Rényi orders across MNIST and CIFAR-10.

Conclusion: First set of optimal guarantees for auditing RDP with DV estimators; validates practical black-box auditing of DP-SGD and suggests broad applicability to Rényi-DP auditing in machine learning.

Abstract: We study black-box auditing for machine learning algorithms that claim R \ 'enyi differential privacy (RDP) guarantees. We introduce an auditing framework, based on hypothesis testing, that directly estimates Rényi divergence between neighboring executions using the Donsker-Varadhan (DV) variational estimator. Our analysis yields explicit and non-asymptotic confidence intervals for RDP auditing via class-restricted DV estimators, separating statistical estimation error from algorithmic privacy leakage. We prove matching minimax lower bounds showing that, up to logarithmic factors, our sample-complexity guarantees are information-theoretically optimal, thereby establishing the first optimal guarantees for auditing RDP via DV estimators. Empirically, we instantiate our framework for auditing DP-SGD in a fully black-box setting. Across MNIST and CIFAR-10, and over a wide range of privacy regimes, our auditors produce a strong overall improvement on empirical RDP lower bounds compared to prior state-of-the-art black-box methods especially at small and moderate Rényi orders where accurate auditing is most challenging.

</details>


### [314] [SCI-Defense: Defending Manipulation Attacks from Generative Engine Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21948)
*Xucheng Yu,Haibo Jin,Huimin Zeng,Haohan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCI-Defense is a three-component framework (Perplexity detection, Semantic Integrity Scoring, Inter-Candidate Detection) that defends LLM-based ranking systems from GEO attacks by evaluating manipulation across four semantic axes, achieving perfect precision and zero FPR on Amazon data and strong recall against multiple attack types, while exposing weaknesses in existing defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GEO attacks inject semantic signals into product descriptions to unfairly boost rankings in LLM-based systems. Existing defenses (perplexity filters, safety classifiers, paraphrasing) fail to detect semantic manipulation, necessitating a robust, multi-faceted defense that can withstand adaptive adversaries.

Method: Three components: PPL detects unusual language perplexity patterns; SIS scores semantic integrity across four manipulation dimensions (Authority Attribution, Narrative Purposiveness, Comparative Claims, Temporal Claims); ICD analyses cross-candidate signals to detect inconsistencies. Evaluated on 600 Amazon product descriptions across 6 categories and 600 MS MARCO web passages, with attacks including String, Reasoning, and Review. Compared against baseline defenses; introduces new attack vectors like Specification Amplification and Use-Case Saturation to reveal blind spots.

Result: SCI-Defense achieves Precision=1.000 and FPR=0.000 on Amazon data, with Recall of 1.000, 0.952, 0.830 for String, Reasoning, and Review attacks. On MS MARCO web passages, String attacks are blocked with perfect recall; Review attacks yield near-zero recall. Demonstrates that existing defenses have zero recall against semantic manipulation; reveals vulnerabilities to new attack vectors and suggests directions for future research.

Conclusion: A promising multi-component defense against GEO attacks in LLM ranking, combining surface-level and semantic checks plus cross-candidate analysis. Demonstrates strong empirical performance on two datasets but requires broader validation, ablation studies, and assessment against adaptive attackers and different languages/domains to establish generalizability and robustness.

Abstract: LLM-based ranking systems are vulnerable to Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) attacks, where adversaries inject semantic signals into product descriptions to artificially boost rankings. We propose SCI-Defense, a three-component defense framework combining Perplexity detection (PPL), Semantic Integrity Scoring (SIS), and Inter-Candidate Detection (ICD). SIS evaluates four manipulation dimensions: Authority Attribution (AA), Narrative Purposiveness (NP), Comparative Claims (CA), and Temporal Claims (TC). Evaluated on 600 Amazon product descriptions across 6 categories, SCI-Defense achieves Precision=1.000 and FPR=0.000, with Recall of 1.000, 0.952, and 0.830 against String, Reasoning, and Review attacks respectively. On 600 MS MARCO web passages, String attacks are blocked with perfect recall while Review attacks yield near-zero recall, as web passages lack the persuasion-oriented signals that SIS targets in product descriptions. We demonstrate that existing defenses -- PPL-only filters, SafetyClf content classifiers, and paraphrasing -- achieve zero recall against semantic manipulation attacks. We further demonstrate new attacks such as Specification Amplification and Use-Case Saturation can expose semantic relevance manipulation as a structural defense blind spot that suggests directions for future research.

</details>


### [315] [Dynamic Mixture of Latent Memories for Self-Evolving Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21951)
*Dianzhi Yu,Vireo Zhang,Hongru Wang,Yanyu Chen,Minda Hu,Wanghan Xu,Siki Chen,Philip Torr,Zhenfei Yin,Irwin King*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MoLEM is a generative latent-memory system using a dynamic mixture-of-experts to accumulate knowledge across continual learning while keeping a frozen base model. It achieves improved performance by internalizing memory into modular experts and routing that memory into reasoning, yielding about 10.4% average accuracy gain over a vanilla pretrained baseline across math, science, and code tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable continual learning without catastrophic forgetting by internalizing knowledge in dedicated memory modules rather than updating the base model, and to surpass external-memory approaches that do not enhance intrinsic capabilities.

Method: MoLEM employs a dynamic mixture of latent memory experts. Multiple independent experts generate memory; a router uses key-query matching to select/weight experts; the aggregated latent memory is injected into reasoning. The base model remains frozen, with experiential knowledge stored in the auxiliary modules. For continual learning, each training stage is paired with a lightweight autoencoder to select the routing group during inference, with inputs not matching any stage falling back to the pretrained model.

Result: Empirical evaluation on continual-learning sequences in math, science, and code shows the method improves average accuracy by 10.40% over the Vanilla pretrained baseline; competing methods do not consistently surpass this baseline across different training orders.

Conclusion: MoLEM demonstrates effective continual learning by internalizing memory in latent experts and routing it to the frozen base model, achieving significant accuracy gains and preserving prior knowledge across task sequences.

Abstract: Achieving self-evolution in intelligent agents requires the continual accumulation of new knowledge across changing task sequences without forgetting previously acquired abilities. Existing approaches either internalize knowledge by updating model parameters, which induces catastrophic forgetting, or rely on external memory, which fails to genuinely enhance the model's intrinsic capabilities. We propose MoLEM, a generative mixture of latent memory framework based on a dynamic mixture-of-experts (MoE). We treat multiple experts as independent carriers to generate memory. A router selects and weights experts through key-query matching, and the aggregated latent memory is injected into the reasoning process. The base model for reasoning remains entirely frozen, with all experiential knowledge internalized into the additional modules, avoiding catastrophic forgetting. For continual learning, each training stage is paired with a lightweight autoencoder that selects the appropriate routing group at inference, and inputs that match no stage fall back to the pretrained model. Experiments train the framework on continual-learning sequences spanning math, science, and code domains. After training, we evaluate the framework on the corresponding test sets to measure task learning and competence preservation across continual adaptation stages. After the full continual-learning sequence, our method improves the average accuracy by 10.40% over the Vanilla pretrained baseline, while none of the competing methods consistently exceed this baseline across different training orders.

</details>


### [316] [ChronoMedicalWorld: A Medical World Model for Learning Patient Trajectories from Longitudinal Care Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21963)
*Jiangyuan Wang,Xuyong Chen,Junwei He,Xu Xu,Shasha Xie,Fuman Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CMWM is an action-conditioned latent world-model for long-horizon patient trajectory forecasting that outperforms a GPT-5.5 baseline on CKD eGFR trajectories; architecture generalizes to other chronic conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the gap between discriminative EHR models and the drift of general LLMs under repeated interventions; aims to model long-horizon physiology under interventions with both structured and conversational components.

Method: An action-conditioned latent world-model with a joint-embedding state encoder and a wide action encoder that handles structured intervention indicators and free-text dialogue embeddings. It trains a recurrent latent transition module with a six-term objective: next observation, next latent, SIGReg latent regularization, and three physiology-aware shape priors (slope, continuity, large-jump penalty). A closed-loop rollout-prefix training protocol aligns optimization with deployment, ensuring multi-step error exposure during training. Case study instantiated for annual eGFR trajectory forecasting in CKD on a 2,232-patient cohort; beat a tuned GPT-5.5 structured-prompting baseline.

Result: CKD-specific instantiation achieves dynamic-50% history rollout MAE 7.384 and RMSE 10.256, versus baseline 7.964 MAE and 11.069 RMSE (-7.28% MAE, -7.35% RMSE). Gains largely driven by the dialogue portion of patient–health-coach communication.

Conclusion: The framework is not CKD-specific; its architecture, loss design, and training protocol are applicable to any chronic condition that can be cast as periodic clinical state interleaved with structured and conversational interventions.

Abstract: Long-horizon clinical simulation -- predicting how a patient's physiology evolves over years under specified interventions -- is central to chronic-disease care, yet existing electronic health record (EHR) models are predominantly discriminative, and general-purpose large language models drift under repeated interventions. We propose the \textbf{ChronoMedicalWorld Model (CMWM)}, an action-conditioned latent world-model framework for learning patient trajectories from longitudinal care data. CMWM couples a joint-embedding state encoder with a wide action encoder that admits both structured intervention indicators and free-text communication embeddings, and trains a recurrent latent transition module under a six-term objective: next-observation supervision, next-latent prediction, SIGReg latent regularisation, and three physiology-aware shape priors (slope, continuity, large-jump penalty). A closed-loop rollout-prefix protocol matches training to deployment, so the model is optimised against the same multi-step error it exhibits at inference. As a concrete case study, we instantiate CMWM for annual estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) trajectory forecasting in chronic kidney disease (CKD). On a 2{,}232-patient nephrology cohort, the CKD instantiation achieves a dynamic-50\% history rollout test mean absolute error (MAE) of 7.384 and root-mean-square error (RMSE) of 10.256, against 7.964 and 11.069 for a tuned GPT-5.5 structured-prompting baseline ($-7.28\%$ MAE, $-7.35\%$ RMSE), with the gain dominated by the dialogue portion of patient--health-coach communication. The framework is not CKD-specific: its architecture, loss design, and training protocol apply to any chronic condition that can be cast as periodic clinical state interleaved with structured and conversational interventions.

</details>


### [317] [An Improved Adaptive PID Optimizer with Enhanced Convergence and Stability for Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21968)
*Saurabh Saini,Kapil Ahuja,Thomas Wick,Saurav Kumar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IAdaPID-ADG: a new optimizer that fuses AdaPID with AMSGrad-style non-increasing learning rate and DiffGrad-style gradient-difference modulation. It outperforms competing optimizers on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and real-world datasets (IARC, AnnoCerv), with ablation confirming component contributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address two critical Adam-derived drawbacks in AdaPID—convergence instability due to a non-convergent step size and stability issues due to gradient coupling—by integrating AMSGrad-style non-increasing effective learning rates and DiffGrad-style gradient-difference modulation within the AdaPID framework.

Method: Extend AdaPID by incorporating (1) AMSGrad-inspired non-increasing effective learning rate to ensure convergence, and (2) DiffGrad-inspired gradient-difference modulation to improve stability. Build IAdaPID-ADG as the combined optimizer and evaluate on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and real-world datasets IARC and AnnoCerv; perform ablation studies on MNIST to quantify each addition.

Result: IAdaPID-ADG substantially outperforms competing optimizers across benchmark and real-world datasets. Ablation on MNIST confirms that both the non-increasing learning rate and the gradient-difference modulation contribute to performance gains.

Conclusion: The proposed IAdaPID-ADG effectively mitigates the convergence and stability drawbacks inherited from AdaPID while delivering superior optimization performance across diverse tasks, with empirical evidence from both standard benchmarks and real-world datasets and supported by targeted ablations.

Abstract: Optimization is essential in deep learning. The foundational method upon which most optimizers are built is momentum-based stochastic gradient descent. However, it suffers from two key drawbacks. First, it has noisy and varying gradients, and second, it has an overshoot phenomenon. To address noisy gradients, Adam was proposed, which remains the most widely used adaptive optimizer. To address the overshoot phenomenon, a control-theory-based PID optimizer was proposed. To tackle both the limitations within a single framework, several variants of Adaptive PID (AdaPID) have recently been proposed.
  Although AdaPID performs well, it still inherits two critical drawbacks from Adam, namely convergence and stability issues. In this work, we address both these limitations. To fix the convergence issue, we uniquely integrate the idea of using a non-increasing effective learning rate into AdaPID (originally proposed in AMSGrad, an extension of Adam). To fix the stability issue, we innovatively integrate a gradient difference based modulation factor into AdaPID (originally proposed in DiffGrad, another extension of Adam). Combining both these ideas in AdaPID, results in our novel IAdaPID-ADG optimizer.
  We evaluate our proposed optimizer on multiple datasets, including benchmark datasets (MNIST and CIFAR10) and real-world datasets (IARC and AnnoCerv). The IAdaPID-ADG substantially outperforms all competing optimizers. Additionally, we perform an ablation study on the MNIST dataset to demonstrate the contribution of each added component.

</details>


### [318] [How Sparsity Allocation Shapes Label-Free Post-Pruning Recoverability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21972)
*Qishi Zhan,Minxuan Hu,Liang He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Allocation of pruning sparsity (ERK vs LAMP) substantially affects the success of label-free post-pruning repair, with the optimal choice depending on architecture, dataset difficulty, and sparsity; there exists a repair-sensitive regime where BatchNorm recalibration fails but activation-statistic repair remains effective; results generalize from CIFAR/Imagenette to ImageNet-100 and DenseNet-121, suggesting joint optimization of pruning allocation and repair.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how sparsity allocation affects the recoverability of highly pruned networks when label-free post-pruning repair is the only repair mechanism available, and to identify regimes where repair is feasible or fails.

Method: Empirical study comparing two pruning allocation schemes—ERK and LAMP—under a fixed label-free activation-statistic repair backend across datasets (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, Imagenette) and architectures (ResNet-18, ResNet-34, ResNet-50) at high sparsities (90% to 95.5%). Additional validation on ImageNet-100 and DenseNet-121 to probe generality; analysis of where BatchNorm recalibration fails and where activation-statistic repair remains effective.

Result: Sparsity allocation markedly changes post-repair accuracy at the same global sparsity. The preferred allocation depends on architecture, dataset difficulty, and sparsity level. A repair-sensitive transition regime is identified: BatchNorm recalibration fails while activation-statistic repair still recovers nontrivial accuracy. Validation shows the location and width of the recoverable regime depend on data scale and connectivity (ImageNet-100, DenseNet-121). Overall, pruning allocation and post-pruning repair interact significantly, since the allocation preserves activation signal useful for label-free recovery.

Conclusion: Pruning allocation plays a crucial role in determining recoverability under label-free post-pruning repair. The study advocates joint optimization of pruning allocation and repair strategy, as the upstream allocation constrains the activation signal available for downstream recovery.

Abstract: Unstructured magnitude pruning at high sparsity can reduce neural network accuracy to near-random performance, while labeled retraining may be unavailable in practical deployment settings. Label-free post-pruning repair methods can partially recover collapsed sparse models, but their effectiveness depends on the sparse model left by the upstream pruning allocation. This paper studies how sparsity allocation shapes post-repair recoverability under a fixed activation-statistic repair backend. We compare ERK and LAMP allocations under the same label-free repair protocol across CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Imagenette with ResNet-18, ResNet-34, and ResNet-50 at sparsities from 90% to 95.5%. The results show that allocation choice can substantially change post-repair accuracy at the same global sparsity, and that the preferred allocation varies with architecture, dataset difficulty, and sparsity level. We identify a repair-sensitive transition regime in which BatchNorm recalibration begins to fail, while activation-statistic repair still recovers nontrivial accuracy. Additional validation on ImageNet-100 and DenseNet-121 shows that the location and width of this recoverable regime depend on data scale and connectivity structure. These findings suggest that pruning allocation and post-pruning repair should be studied jointly, since the allocation determines how much activation signal remains available for label-free recovery.

</details>


### [319] [Reasoning through Verifiable Forecast Actions: Consistency-Grounded RL for Financial LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21975)
*Jialin Chen,Aosong Feng,Harshit Verma,Siyi Gu,Haiwen Wang,Ali Maatouk,Yixuan He,Yifeng Gao,Leandros Tassiulas,Rex Ying*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: StockR1 introduces a time-series–enhanced LLM that combines forecasting with financial reasoning via a verifiable forecast action. It uses a tool-call architecture: first outputs a structured action, then a time-series decoder generates distributional trajectories conditioned on that action. Reinforcement learning optimizes answer validity, forecast accuracy, and action–data consistency, with uncertainty weighting to handle market dynamics. Evaluated on a 10-year benchmark, it outperforms baselines, notably improving reasoning accuracy by 17.7% (4B) and 25.9% (8B).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Finance markets exhibit non-stationarity, low signal-to-noise, and heavy dependence on external information. Existing work either abstracts time-series into text or decouples forecasting from language reasoning, causing a misalignment between qualitative reasoning and quantitative outcomes. The paper motivates a unified, verifiable approach that tightly integrates numerical forecasting with linguistic reasoning.

Method: A tool-call design where the model first outputs a forecast action (structured, interpretable representation of market outlook). It then invokes a time-series decoder conditioned on this action to generate distributional future trajectories. The full pipeline is trained with reinforcement learning, with rewards for answer validity, forecast accuracy, and consistency between actions and observed data, reweighted by a sample-level uncertainty scalar.

Result: StockR1 consistently outperforms time-series baselines and general-purpose LLMs on financial question answering and stock forecasting over a 10-year benchmark, with significant gains in reasoning accuracy.

Conclusion: Structuring forecast actions creates a strong synergy between language-based reasoning and temporal prediction, enabling verifiable, interpretable, and numerically grounded decision-making in finance.

Abstract: Financial markets are characterized by extreme non-stationarity, low signal-to-noise ratios, and strong dependence on external information such as news, company fundamentals, and macroeconomic signals. Yet, existing approaches either abstract time-series into text or decouple forecasting from language-based reasoning, leading to a fundamental mismatch between qualitative reasoning and quantitative outcomes. To address this, we introduce StockR1, a time-series-enhanced LLM that unifies stock forecasting and financial reasoning through a verifiable forecast action. Based on a tool-call design, the model first emits a forecast action, which is a structured and interpretable representation of its qualitative market outlook. It then invokes a time-series decoder conditioned on this action to generate distributional future trajectories, leading to more informed question answering and financial reasoning. We optimize the full pipeline with reinforcement learning, where rewards jointly reflect answer validity, forecast accuracy, and consistency between generated actions and observed time-series dynamics. In addition, rewards are reweighted by a sample-level uncertainty scalar, encouraging the model to accommodate varying uncertainty in market dynamics. We evaluate StockR1 on financial question answering and stock forecasting over a large-scale 10-year benchmark. Our method consistently outperforms time-series baselines and general-purpose LLMs, improving reasoning accuracy by 17.7% (4B) and 25.9% (8B). These findings demonstrate that structuring the forecast actions establishes a powerful synergy between language reasoning and temporal prediction, enabling LLMs to reason through verifiable, interpretable, and numerically grounded decisions.

</details>


### [320] [Ex-GraphRAG: Interpretable Evidence Routing for Graph-Augmented LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21994)
*Yoav Kor Sade,Arvindh Arun,Rishi Puri,Steffen Staab,Maya Bechler-Speicher*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Auditable GraphRAG with a Multivariate Graph Neural Additive Network (M-GNAN) yields exact per-node and per-feature contributions in graph-augmented LLMs, preserving performance while revealing semantic–structural mismatches that affect retrieval and multi-hop QA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable faithful auditing of how retrieved subgraphs influence the encoder output, addressing the entanglement from iterative neighborhood aggregation in standard GNNs.

Method: Replace the GNN encoder with M-GNAN, an additive graph model extended to high-dimensional embeddings that provides exact decomposition of the encoder output across individual nodes and feature groups; evaluate on STaRK-Prime.

Result: Auditable encoder matches black-box performance on STaRK-Prime; audits reveal a semantic–structural mismatch where dominant nodes are structurally disconnected, held together by low-attribution intermediaries whose removal degrades multi-hop QA by up to 28%; semantic importance and structural connectivity are governed by disjoint sets of nodes.

Conclusion: Demonstrates decoupling of semantic and structural signals in graph-augmented LLMs; implications for retrieval pruning, context construction, and failure diagnosis.

Abstract: GraphRAG conditions language models on subgraphs retrieved from knowledge graphs, encoded via message-passing GNNs. Because these encoders entangle node contributions through iterated neighborhood aggregation, there is no closed-form way to determine how much each retrieved entity influenced the encoder's output, and therefore no way to faithfully audit what structural evidence actually reached the model. We introduce Ex-GraphRAG, which replaces the GNN encoder with a Multivariate Graph Neural Additive Network (M-GNAN), an extension of additive graph models to high-dimensional embedding spaces that yields an exact decomposition of the encoder's output across individual nodes and feature groups, without post-hoc approximation. On STaRK-Prime, this auditable encoder matches black-box performance. Using it to audit evidence routing, we uncover a semantic-structural mismatch: the nodes that dominate the encoder's output are structurally disconnected in the retrieved subgraph, held together by low-attribution intermediaries whose removal degrades multi-hop QA by up to 28%. This mismatch, invisible to any opaque encoder, reveals that semantic importance and structural connectivity are governed by disjoint sets of nodes, with direct implications for retrieval pruning, context construction, and failure diagnosis in graph-augmented LLMs.

</details>


### [321] [Toward Understanding Adversarial Distillation: Why Robust Teachers Fail](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21999)
*Hongsin Lee,Hye Won Chung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adversarial Distillation's effectiveness hinges on how a robust teacher treats unlearnable data: a misalignment between the teacher's confident supervision and the student's limited representational capacity on a Robustly Unlearnable Set can cause robust overfitting, while a teacher's high uncertainty on those samples mitigates noise memorization and enhances robust generalization. Predictive entropy of the teacher on unlearnable samples is a strong indicator of student robustness and guides robust teacher selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explain why stronger teacher robustness can paradoxically harm student robustness, identify the underlying mechanism, and provide a principled criterion for selecting robust teachers in adversarial distillation.

Method: The paper builds a theoretical framework around a two-layer neural network, defining the Robustly Unlearnable Set. It analyzes feature learning dynamics to show a dichotomy: confident supervision on unlearnable samples drives memorization of spurious noise and robust overfitting, while uncertainty on these samples suppresses noise memorization. The theory is complemented by empirical validation on synthetic data and real-image datasets, examining the relation between teacher confidence (predictive entropy) and student robustness.

Result: Key findings include: (1) a mismatch between teacher confidence and the student's learnable signal on unlearnable samples causes robust overfitting; (2) a high-uncertainty teacher on those samples prevents noise memorization and supports robust generalization; (3) predictive entropy on unlearnable samples correlates with student robustness; (4) empirical results on synthetic and real datasets validate the theory.

Conclusion: Predictive entropy of the teacher on unlearnable samples serves as a principled, practical criterion for robust teacher selection in adversarial distillation. Favoring teachers with higher uncertainty on unlearnable data can mitigate robust overfitting and improve robust generalization.

Abstract: Adversarial Distillation aims to enhance student robustness by guiding the student with a robust teacher's soft labels within the min-max adversarial training framework, yet its success is notoriously inconsistent: a more robust teacher often fails to improve, or even harms, the student's robust generalization. In this paper, we identify a key mechanism of this teacher dependency: the misalignment between the teacher's supervisory confidence and the student's representational limitations on a consistent subset of training data -- the Robustly Unlearnable Set. We present a theoretical framework analyzing the feature learning dynamics of a two-layer neural network, demonstrating that this mismatch creates a dichotomy in distillation outcomes. We prove that when a teacher provides confident supervision on unlearnable samples, it compels the student to memorize spurious noise patterns that eventually overpower the learned robust signal, thereby driving robust overfitting. Conversely, a teacher that exhibits high uncertainty on these samples effectively suppresses noise memorization, allowing the student to rely solely on the learnable signal for robust generalization. We empirically validate our theory across both synthetic simulations and real-image classification datasets, confirming that robust overfitting is driven by the teacher's interaction with unlearnable samples. Finally, we demonstrate that a teacher's predictive entropy on unlearnable samples serves as a strong indicator of student robustness, validating our theoretical framework and offering a principled guideline for robust teacher selection.

</details>


### [322] [Check Your LLM's Secret Dictionary! Five Lines of Code Reveal What Your LLM Learned (Including What It Shouldn't Have)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22005)
*Hisashi Miyashita*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SVD of the lm_head weight matrix reveals interpretable semantic subspaces and clustering across models; introduces VCS and WPS; claims pretraining origins for objectionable subspaces; advocates SVD-based safety auditing and tokenizer optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To audit and interpret large language models by inspecting weight-space structure, linking singular directions to vocabulary semantics and potential safety risks, without runtime inference.

Method: Compute left singular vectors of the lm_head weight matrix for several models (GPT-OSS-120B, Gemma-2-2B, Qwen2.5-1.5B); inspect top-token clusters per singular direction; quantify subspace coherence with Vocabulary Cluster Score (VCS); detect potential glitches with Weighted Projection Score (WPS); compare pretraining/alignment effects across models; assess ethical content subspaces.

Result: Found model-specific subspace structures: GPT shows a graduated hierarchy of functionally differentiated subspaces; Gemma dominated by pre-nineteenth-century English orthography, yielding stepwise clusters linked to controllability; Qwen shows broad multilingual coverage with subspaces containing ethically inappropriate vocabulary. Ethically concerning subspaces originate in pretraining and persist post-alignment. WPS recovers a known glitch token in GPT-OSS-120B without inference. VCS quantifies subspace coherence; proposed taxonomy of root causes for problematic vocabulary; calls for SVD-based safety auditing and tokenizer optimization.

Conclusion: SVD analysis of the lm_head offers a practical pre-release safety auditing tool and a path toward more controllable LLMs. Recommends adopting SVD-based checks in standard workflows and using the insights to guide tokenizer design and model alignment. Notes limitations include reliance on static weight structure and need for broader validation across architectures.

Abstract: We show that singular value decomposition of the lm_head} weight matrix of a transformer-based large language model -- requiring only five lines of PyTorch and no model inference -- reveals interpretable semantic subspaces directly from the model weights. Each left singular vector identifies the vocabulary tokens most readily selected when the hidden state aligns with the corresponding singular direction; inspecting these clusters exposes the model's training data composition and curation philosophy.
  Analysing GPT-OSS-120B, Gemma-2-2B, and Qwen2.5-1.5B, we find that singular value spectra and vocabulary cluster structures differ systematically across models: GPT exhibits a graduated hierarchy of functionally differentiated subspaces; Gemma is dominated by pre-nineteenth-century English orthography, forming a stepwise clustering structure that may contribute to high output controllability; and Qwen exhibits broad multilingual coverage alongside subspaces whose vocabulary the authors have determined to be ethically inappropriate for direct publication.
  Base-instruct comparison reveals that ethically concerning subspaces originate in pretraining and are not removed by post-training alignment. We introduce the Vocabulary Cluster Score (VCS) to quantify subspace coherence, and the Weighted Projection Score (WPS) as a static glitch token detector; applying WPS to GPT-OSS-120B recovers shokubutsu-hyakka-tsu (ID 137606), a well-known glitch token widely reported in the CJK language community, without any model inference. We propose a taxonomy of root causes for problematic vocabulary content and call for lm_head} SVD analysis to be adopted as a standard pre-release safety auditing step. Our findings further suggest directions toward SVD-guided tokenizer optimisation and more controllable LLM design.

</details>


### [323] [CASE-NET: Deep Spatio-Temporal Representation Learning via Causal Attention and Channel Recalibration for Multivariate Time Series Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22043)
*Fan Zhang,Yating Cui,Hua Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CASE-NET combines a Causal Temporal Encoder with adaptive channel recalibration to address temporal non-causality and noise in MTS classification, achieving SOTA across six domains and 98.6% on AWR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify two bottlenecks: temporal non-causality causing confounding in non-stationary dynamics; absence of channel saliency allowing noise to contaminate latent representations.

Method: Causal Temporal Encoder uses masked self-attention and causal convolutions to enforce arrow-of-time constraints; Adaptive Channel Recalibration module acts as an information bottleneck to suppress detrimental noise; architecture enables structural manifold pre-conditioning; evaluated on six heterogeneous domains.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art on four tasks; peak accuracy 98.6% on the AWR dataset; demonstrates robustness in non-stationary regimes.

Conclusion: CASE-NET advances MTS classification by enforcing temporal causality and channel saliency, improving representation fidelity and cross-domain robustness.

Abstract: Multivariate time series (MTS) classification is foundational to pervasive computing and financial analysis, yet existing multi-scale paradigms are often constrained by suboptimal representation fidelity. We identify two critical bottlenecks: temporal non-causality in standard encoders that induces temporal confounding in non-stationary dynamics, and the absence of explicit channel saliency mechanisms that allows noise to contaminate the latent space. To address these challenges, we propose the Causal Attention and Spatio-temporal Encoder Network (CASE-NET), an architecture designed for structural manifold pre-conditioning. CASE-NET synergizes a Causal Temporal Encoder, which enforces physical arrow-of-time constraints via masked self-attention and causal convolutions, with an Adaptive Channel Recalibration module functioning as an information bottleneck to suppress detrimental noise. Comprehensive evaluations across six heterogeneous domains demonstrate that CASE-NET establishes new state-of-the-art benchmarks on four tasks, achieving a peak accuracy of 98.6% on the AWR dataset and superior robustness in non-stationary regimes.

</details>


### [324] [LABO: LLM-Accelerated Bayesian Optimization through Broad Exploration and Selective Experimentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22054)
*Zhuo Chen,Xinzhe Yuan,Jianshu Zhang,Jinzong Dong,Ruichen Zhou,Yingchun Niu,Tianhang Zhou,Yu Yang Fredrik Liu,Yuqiang Li,Nanyang Ye,Qinying Gu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework (LABO) for integrating LLMs into Bayesian optimization by dynamically gating between cheap LLM predictions and costly experiments, improving sample efficiency with a regret-bound theorem and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High cost and scarcity of data in scientific experiments motivates leveraging cheap, broad LLM evaluations to supplement BO, reducing reliance on expensive experiments.

Method: LABO integrates LLM predictions and real observations in a single BO loop, using a gating criterion to switch emphasis between LLM-based exploration and experimental data. The framework uses inexpensive LLM evaluations to explore, reserving costly experiments for high-uncertainty regions.

Result: Theoretical analysis provides a cumulative regret bound, and empirical experiments across diverse scientific tasks show LABO outperforms existing methods under the same budget.

Conclusion: LABO is a practical, theoretically grounded approach for incorporating LLMs into scientific discovery workflows, achieving improved sample efficiency.

Abstract: The high cost and data scarcity in scientific exploration have motivated the use of large language models (LLMs) as knowledge-driven components in Bayesian optimization (BO). However, existing approaches typically embed LLMs directly into the sampling or surrogate modeling pipeline, without fully leveraging their significantly lower evaluation cost compared to real-world experiments. To address this limitation, we propose LLM-Accelerated Bayesian Optimization (LABO), a framework that combines LLM predictions with experimental observations within a single BO loop. LABO employs a gating criterion to dynamically balance the reliance on LLM predictions versus actual experiments. By leveraging inexpensive LLM evaluations to broadly explore the search space and reserving costly real experiments only for regions with high uncertainty, LABO achieves more sample-efficient optimization. We provide a theoretical analysis with a cumulative regret bound that formalizes this efficiency gain. Empirical results across diverse scientific tasks demonstrate that LABO consistently outperforms existing methods under identical experimental budgets. Our results suggest that LABO offers a practical and theoretically grounded approach for integrating LLMs into scientific discovery workflows.

</details>


### [325] [Prototype-Guided Classification Sub-Task Decoupling Framework: Enhancing Generalization and Interpretability for Multivariate Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22055)
*Xianhao Song,Yuang Zhang,Yuqi She,Liping Wang,Xuemin Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PDFTime reframes time series classification as a multi-stage, prototype-guided, similarity-based process, decoupling feature extraction from decision and achieving SOTA on UEA/UCR datasets (80/128 top-1 on UCR).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional TSC models rely on direct feature-to-label mappings via a single linear projection after pooling, entangling feature extraction with decision, hindering interpretability and generalization; a decoupled, multi-stage approach with prototypes can approximate class-conditional latent distributions and enable progressive discrimination.

Method: PDFTime introduces learned prototypes to represent class-conditional distributions in latent space, and performs multi-stage decision with varying granularity through similarity-based reasoning, decoupling representation learning from final classification.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on UEA and UCR benchmarks, including top-1 accuracy on 80 of 128 UCR datasets, with improved consistency and generalization over recent strong baselines.

Conclusion: Recasting TSC as a decoupled, multi-stage, prototype-guided similarity-based process offers improved interpretability and performance, establishing a new paradigm for TSC architecture design.

Abstract: Time Series Classification (TSC) is a long-standing research problem that has gained increasing attention in recent years with the rapid growth of large-scale temporal data. Despite substantial progress enabled by deep learning, designing TSC models that are both accurate and interpretable remains a challenging task. Many existing approaches adopt a direct feature-to-label classification paradigm, by collapsing high-dimensional temporal embeddings into class logits via a single linear projection (often after global pooling), the paradigm conflates feature extraction and decision logic into an inseparable mapping.
  To address these limitations, we propose PDFTime, a prototype-guided framework that reformulates time series classification as a multi-stage decision process. Instead of direct feature-to-label mapping, PDFTime leverages learned prototypes to approximate class-conditional feature distributions in the latent space, enabling progressive discrimination through classification sub-tasks of varying granularity. To our knowledge, PDFTime is the first framework to reformulate time series classification as a decoupled, multi-stage similarity-based reasoning process, breaking the long-standing paradigm of direct, black-box feature-to-label mapping. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that PDFTime achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance across UEA and UCR benchmarks. Notably, it secures the top-$1$ accuracy on 80 out of 128 datasets in the UCR archive, significantly outperforming recent strong baselines in both consistency and generalization.

</details>


### [326] [From Reasoning Chains to Verifiable Subproblems: Curriculum Reinforcement Learning Enables Credit Assignment for LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22074)
*Xitai Jiang,Zihan Tang,Wenze Lin,Yang Yue,Shenzhi Wang,Gao Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCRL introduces subproblem-based curriculum reinforcement learning with per-subproblem reward normalization to enable fine-grained credit assignment, boosting hard mathematical reasoning performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outcome-based RLVR is inefficient on hard problems due to sparse correct final-answer rollouts and inability to credit partial progress; finer learning signals from verifiable subproblems are needed.

Method: Derive verifiable subproblems from reference reasoning chains and keep the final subproblem as the original problem. Apply subproblem-level normalization to rewards, assigning advantages to corresponding answer spans for fine-grained credit assignment without external reward models or rubrics.

Result: SCRL outperforms strong curriculum baselines across seven mathematical reasoning benchmarks, with average gains of +4.1 points in accuracy over GRPO on Qwen3-4B-Base and +1.9 points on Qwen3-14B-Base. On AIME24, AIME25, and IMO-Bench, pass@1 improves by +3.7 points and pass@64 by +4.6 points on Qwen3-4B-Base, indicating better exploration on hard problems.

Conclusion: Subproblem curricula alleviate gradient dead zones, yielding larger gains as the original problem difficulty increases. SCRL demonstrates robust improvements across multiple hard reasoning benchmarks and enables fine-grained credit assignment without external reward models, suggesting broad applicability to complex reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) has shown strong promise for LLM reasoning, but outcome-based RLVR remains inefficient on hard problems because correct final-answer rollouts are rare and sample-level credit assignment cannot use partial progress in failed attempts. We introduce SCRL (Subproblem Curriculum Reinforcement Learning), a curriculum RL framework that derives verifiable subproblems from reference reasoning chains and fixes the final subproblem as the original problem. This turns partial progress on hard problems into verifiable learning signals. Algorithmically, SCRL uses subproblem-level normalization, which normalizes rewards independently at each subproblem position and assigns the resulting advantages to the corresponding answer spans, enabling finer-grained credit assignment without external rubrics or reward models. Our analysis shows that subproblem curricula lift hard problems out of gradient dead zones, with larger relative gains as the original problem becomes harder. Across seven mathematical reasoning benchmarks, SCRL outperforms strong curriculum-learning baselines, improving average accuracy over GRPO by +4.1 points on Qwen3-4B-Base and +1.9 points on Qwen3-14B-Base. On AIME24, AIME25, and IMO-Bench, SCRL further improves pass@1 by +3.7 points and pass@64 by +4.6 points on Qwen3-4B-Base, indicating better exploration on hard reasoning problems.

</details>


### [327] [Can Breath Biomarkers Causally Influence Blood Glucose? Investigating VOC-Mediated Modulation in Diabetes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22075)
*Varsha Sharma,Prasanta K. Guha,Avik Ghose*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Non-invasive diabetes risk assessment using VOCs and lifestyle data with causal inference and ML classification, plus clustering for risk stratification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diabetes imposes a global health burden; early detection is crucial. A non-invasive approach using VOC profiles and lifestyle factors could enable scalable screening and a better understanding of causal relationships between VOCs and glucose.

Method: Apply causal inference to VOCs (acetone, isopropanol, isoprene, ethanol) to estimate their impact on blood glucose. Develop a classifier to distinguish diabetics from non-diabetics using non-invasive markers. Create a risk-based ranking for individuals in the “gray zone.” Use Gaussian Mixture Models to identify natural clusters in the population.

Result: Some VOCs exhibit a strong causal influence on glucose levels, and machine learning classifiers effectively distinguish diabetics from non-diabetics and stratify high-risk individuals. The approach supports non-invasive early screening.

Conclusion: An integrated causal-explainable framework can facilitate the development of non-invasive tools for early diabetes screening and risk stratification.

Abstract: Diabetes is a global health burden, and early detection is critical for timely intervention. This study explores a non-invasive, data-driven framework to identify individuals at risk of diabetes using Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) and lifestyle variables. We use causal inference techniques to estimate the impact of VOCs such as acetone, isopropanol, isoprene, and ethanol on blood glucose levels. Additionally, we designed a classifier to distinguish diabetics from non-diabetics using non-invasive markers. We created a risk-based ranking system for individuals in the "gray zone," and identified natural clusters in the population using Gaussian Mixture Model. Our results suggest that specific VOCs exhibit a strong causal influence on glucose levels and that machine learning models can reliably classify and stratify individuals at high risk. This integrated causal-explainable analysis can support the development of tool for non-invasive early screening of diabetes.

</details>


### [328] [Aerodynamic force reconstruction using physics-informed Gaussian processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22111)
*Gledson Rodrigo Tondo,Igor Kavrakov,Guido Morgenthal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A probabilistic physics-informed ML framework reconstructs aerodynamic loads from noisy structural responses without regularization, leveraging multi-fidelity data; validated on linear unsteady model of the Great Belt East Bridge, showing accurate load reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need to infer true aerodynamic loads from indirect, noisy measurements for validation and prognosis. Traditional models rely on simplifications and often require regularization; a probabilistic PINN approach can robustly infer loads from limited, noisy data while accommodating heterogeneous data sources.

Method: A probabilistic physics-informed machine learning approach (likely Bayesian PINN style) that reconstructs underlying aerodynamic loads directly from structural dynamic responses. The model mitigates overfitting, does not rely on explicit regularization schemes, and supports heterogeneous and multi-fidelity training data.

Result: Successful reconstruction of aerodynamic loads with strong agreement to the true loads in terms of RMSE, magnitude, phase angle, and peak values, demonstrated on the Great Belt East Bridge under a linear unsteady assumption.

Conclusion: The approach offers broad applicability for model validation, future load estimation, and structural damage prognosis, with potential for extension to other aero-structural problems and data regimes.

Abstract: Accurate modeling of aerodynamic loads is essential for understanding and predicting the responses of complex structural systems. However, these models often rely on simplifications of the true physical forces, introducing assumptions that can limit their accuracy. Validating such models becomes particularly challenging in the presence of noisy or incomplete data. To address this, we introduce a probabilistic physics-informed machine learning approach designed to reconstruct the underlying aerodynamic loads from noisy measurements of structural dynamic responses. The model avoids overfitting, eliminates the need for regularization schemes, and allows for the use of heterogeneous and multi-fidelity data during the training process. The efficacy of the approach is demonstrated through the reconstruction of aerodynamic loads on the Great Belt East Bridge, simulated under a linear unsteady assumption. Results show a strong agreement between true and predicted loads, particularly related to root mean squared errors, magnitude, phase angle and peak values of the signals. The method for load reconstructing holds broad applicability, such as modeling validation, future load estimation, and structural damage prognosis.

</details>


### [329] [Short-Term-to-Long-Term Memory Transfer for Knowledge Graphs under Partial Observability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22142)
*Taewoon Kim,Vincent François-Lavet,Michael Cochez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Per-item Q-learning for memory transfer in a neuro-symbolic temporal KG setting; improves long-term memory decisions on RoomKG; short-term-only policy often best; results are interpretable under memory constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Partial observability in RL requires selective retention of observations. Existing memory models lack explicit short-term-to-long-term transfer modeling for symbolic observations; propose a per-item transfer policy for memory management.

Method: Per-item Q-learning with shared parameters to manage a variable-sized short-term buffer; TD updates over matched items across consecutive steps; memory is a temporal knowledge graph. Action: keep or drop each observed triple before insertion; evaluated on RoomKG with long-term capacity 128; comparisons to symbolic and neural baselines including temporal annotations and LSTM/Transformer; ablations including local short-term-only variant.

Result: Transfer decisions outperform baselines at memory capacity 128; best across transfer-policy ablations is the local short-term-only variant; policy preserves navigation- and query-relevant facts while discarding low-value candidates; results imply explicit, interpretable memory management under constraints.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates effective short-term-to-long-term transfer for symbolic observations in a neuro-symbolic memory setting, yielding interpretable memory decisions and improved performance under memory constraints; short-term signals suffice for strong transfer decisions.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning under partial observability requires deciding what information to retain, yet most memory-based approaches do not explicitly model short-term-to-long-term transfer of symbolic observations. We study this transfer process in a temporal knowledge-graph memory setting and cast it as a neuro-symbolic value-based decision problem: for each observed triple, the agent chooses whether to keep or drop it before long-term insertion. To handle variable-sized short-term buffers, we use a per-item Q-learning design with shared parameters and a practical temporal-difference update over matched items across consecutive steps. On the RoomKG benchmark at long-term memory capacity 128, learned transfer decisions outperform symbolic and neural baselines, including symbolic baselines with temporal annotations and history-based LSTM/Transformer baselines. Across transfer-policy ablations, a lightweight local short-term-only variant performs best, and step-level behavior shows that the policy keeps navigation- and query-relevant facts while discarding lower-value candidate facts, supporting explicit and interpretable memory decisions under memory constraints.

</details>


### [330] [Algebraic Machine Learning for Small-to-Medium Datasets Is Competitive against Strong Standard Baselines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22155)
*David Mendez,Fernando Martin-Maroto,Gonzalo G. de Polavieja*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Algebraic Machine Learning (AML) leverages subdirect decomposition of algebraic structure to learn without optimization; it outperforms cross-validated baselines on small to medium image data and is competitive with bias-rich methods on tabular data, without cross-validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether a symbolic/algebraic learner can compete with modern learners in realistic supervised tasks and across modalities, especially with limited data and without hyperparameter tuning.

Method: AML uses subdirect decomposition of algebraic structure as inductive bias; trained solely on labeled data with no validation or cross-validation; compares with CNNs for images and XGBoost/LightGBM/RF for tabular data; no task-dependent hyperparameters.

Result: On image datasets with 50–2000 training examples, AML outperforms cross-validated baselines including CNNs. On tabular data, XGBoost generally performs best, but AML is comparable to LightGBM and RF; AML is effective across modalities using a generic algebraic bias and requires no cross-validation.

Conclusion: AML demonstrates that a generic algebraic inductive bias can deliver competitive performance across diverse tasks without hyperparameter tuning, challenging the view that symbolic methods are inferior to modern learners in practical settings.

Abstract: Symbolic methods are generally not considered competitive with strong modern learners on realistic supervised tasks. We evaluate Algebraic Machine Learning (AML), a framework that learns through subdirect decomposition of algebraic structure rather than numerical optimization, against standard baselines on image and tabular classification across varying training-set sizes. We find that AML trained only on training data without using validation or cross-validation outperforms a family of cross-validated baseline methods including CNNs on small to medium image datasets (50--2000 training examples). On tabular datasets in the same size range, XGBoost is overall the best performing method, but AML is nonetheless comparable to methods incorporating task-specific biases such as LightGBM and random forests. AML achieves this competitive performance across two very different types of datasets using a generic algebraic inductive bias, rather than the modality-specific biases built into standard baselines like CNNs for images or XGBoost for tabular data, and requires no cross validation because it has no task-dependent hyperparameters to tune.

</details>
