### [331] [One-Way Policy Optimization for Self-Evolving LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22156)
*Shuo Yang,Jinda Lu,Kexin Huang,Chiyu Ma,Shaohang Wei,Yuyang Liu,Guoyin Wang,Jingren Zhou,Li Yuan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: One-Way Policy Optimization (OWPO) decouples update direction from update magnitude in RL with verifiable rewards, using asymmetric reweighting (Accelerated Alignment and Gain Locking) and iterative reference updates to create a Ratchet Effect, yielding improved stability and performance without external references.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Binary verifier rewards are sparse, and existing token-level constraint methods penalize deviations indiscriminately, which can reverse progress when the policy outperforms a reference, leading to inefficiency and unstable learning.

Method: OWPO: verifier dictates update direction; a reference policy scales the update magnitude. Uses asymmetric reweighting: Accelerated Alignment for inferior deviations (policy behind reference) and Gain Locking for superior deviations (policy ahead). Iterative reference updates create a Ratchet Effect that consolidates gains.

Result: OWPO outperforms strong baselines (DAPO, OPD, MOPD) on RL with verifiable rewards, enabling continuous self-evolution without reliance on external reference models.

Conclusion: Decoupling optimization direction from magnitude improves stability and efficiency; the Ratchet Effect from iterative references supports continual self-improvement without fixed priors.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become a promising paradigm for scaling reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, the sparsity of binary verifier rewards often leads to low efficiency and optimization instability. To stabilize training, existing methods typically impose token-level constraints relative to a reference policy. We identify that such constraints penalize deviations indiscriminately; this can flip verifier-determined direction when the policy attempts to outperform the reference, thereby suppressing gains. To resolve this, we propose One-Way Policy Optimization (OWPO), a method based on the principle of decoupling optimization direction from update magnitude. In OWPO, the verifier dictates the update direction, while the reference policy serves only to adjust the magnitude. Specifically, OWPO applies asymmetric reweighting: it performs Accelerated Alignment for inferior deviations (where the policy lags behind the reference) and Gain Locking for superior deviations (where the policy surpasses the reference). Furthermore, by incorporating iterative reference updates, OWPO creates a ``Ratchet Effect'' that continuously consolidates gains. Experimental results demonstrate that OWPO outperforms strong baselines, including DAPO, OPD, and MOPD, breaking the bottleneck of fixed priors to enable continuous self-evolution without reliance on external reference models.

</details>


### [332] [Maestro: Reinforcement Learning to Orchestrate Hierarchical Model-Skill Ensembles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22177)
*Jinyang Wu,Guocheng Zhai,Ruihan Jin,Yuhao Shen,Zhengxi Lu,Fan Zhang,Haoran Luo,Zheng Lian,Zhengqi Wen,Jianhua Tao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Maestro is an RL-driven orchestration framework that coordinates ensembles of frozen expert models and a two-tier skill library to handle multimodal tasks as sequential decisions, achieving strong performance with a small orchestrator and generalizing to unseen models/skills.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current frameworks rely on monolithic LLMs and fixed interfaces, failing to exploit complementary strengths across diverse models and skills; there is a need for flexible, scalable orchestration of heterogeneous components.

Method: Maestro formulates multimodal tasks as sequential decisions over a hierarchical model–skill registry. It trains a lightweight policy via outcome-based reinforcement learning to decide at each step whether to invoke an external expert, which model–skill pair to use, and when to terminate, without step-level supervision. The system uses a two-tier skill library and frozen expert models to compose ensembles.

Result: On ten multimodal benchmarks (math reasoning, chart understanding, high-resolution perception, domain-specific analysis), a 4B orchestrator achieves 70.1% average accuracy, surpassing GPT-5 (69.3%) and Gemini-2.5-Pro (68.7%). Generalization to unseen models/skills yields 59.5% average on four challenging benchmarks, outperforming all closed-source baselines, with low latency.

Conclusion: Maestro demonstrates effective RL-based coordination of heterogeneous models and skills, with strong cross-model generalization and efficiency; the approach is open-sourced, enabling broader adoption and extension.

Abstract: The proliferation of large language models (LLMs) and modular skills has endowed autonomous agents with increasingly powerful capabilities. Existing frameworks typically rely on monolithic LLMs and fixed logic to interface with these skills. This gives rise to a critical bottleneck: different LLMs offer distinct advantages across diverse domains, yet current frameworks fail to exploit the complementary strengths of models and skills, thereby limiting their performance on downstream tasks. In this paper, we present Maestro (Multimodal Agent for Expert-Skill Targeted Reinforced Orchestration), a Reinforcement Learning (RL)-driven orchestration framework that reframes heterogeneous multimodal tasks as a sequential decision-making process over a hierarchical model-skill registry. Rather than consolidating all knowledge into a single model, Maestro trains a lightweight policy to dynamically compose ensembles of frozen expert models and a two-tier skill library, deciding at each step whether to invoke an external expert, which model-skill pair to select, and when to terminate. The policy is optimized via outcome-based RL, requiring no step-level supervision. We evaluate Maestro across ten representative multimodal benchmarks spanning mathematical reasoning, chart understanding, high-resolution perception, and domain-specific analysis. With only a 4B orchestrator, Maestro achieves an average accuracy of 70.1%, surpassing both GPT-5 (69.3%) and Gemini-2.5-Pro (68.7%). Crucially, the learned coordination policy generalizes to unseen models and skills without retraining: augmenting the registry with out-of-domain experts yields a 59.5% average on four challenging benchmarks, outperforming all closed-source baselines. Maestro further maintains high computational efficiency with low latency. The source code is available at https://github.com/jinyangwu/Maestro.

</details>


### [333] [IKNO: Infinite-order Kernel Neural Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22182)
*Pengyuan Zhu,Ivor W. Tsang,Yueming Lyu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Infinity-order Kernel Neural Operator (IKNO) to overcome expressivity limits of first-order kernel operators by using infinite-order kernel integrals with a finite closed-form approximation. Introduces two variants: IKNO-Vanilla (full-kernel resolvent on a product grid via Kronecker eigendecomposition) and IKNO-TP (tensor-product per-axis resolvents); offers fast computation schemes and demonstrates SOTA performance on diverse time-dependent/independent benchmarks, including large-scale industrial datasets and point clouds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural operators rely on first-order kernel integral approximations, which constrain expressivity and global information aggregation. There is a need for higher-order, more expressive kernels that remain computationally efficient and handle arbitrary input shapes across time-dependent and time-independent problems.

Method: 1) Formulate neural operators using infinite-order kernel integrals with a closed-form finite approximation (IKNO). 2) IKNO-Vanilla: apply the full-kernel resolvent on the product grid using Kronecker eigendecomposition. 3) IKNO-TP: construct a tensor-product operator by composing per-axis resolvents. 4) Develop fast computational schemes to enable scalability to large inputs and arbitrary shapes.

Result: Empirical evaluation on time-dependent and time-independent benchmarks, including large-scale industrial datasets and point clouds, shows IKNO achieves state-of-the-art accuracy across most benchmarks with significant improvements, while maintaining scalability and computational efficiency.

Conclusion: IKNO introduces a principled infinite-order kernel framework for neural operators, yielding enhanced expressivity and global aggregation without prohibitive cost. The two variants offer design flexibility (full-grid vs. tensor-product) and practical efficiency. Further work could address theoretical guarantees, error bounds of the finite approximation, and broader applicability across PDE regimes and input geometries.

Abstract: Neural operators have achieved significant success in modern scientific computing due to their flexibility and strong generalization capabilities. Existing models, however, primarily rely on first-order kernel integral approximations, which severely limit their expressivity. To address this, we propose the Infinite-order Kernel Neural Operator (IKNO), which constructs neural operators via infinite-order kernel integrals and admits an elegant closed-form finite approximation. We develop two complementary infinite-order neural operator constructions: IKNO-Vanilla, which applies the full-kernel resolvent on the product grid via Kronecker eigendecomposition, and IKNO-TP, an alternative tensor-product operator that composes per-axis resolvents. Furthermore, we develop fast computation schemes for both variants of IKNO, which achieve outstanding global information aggregation while maintaining high computational efficiency. Empirically, we evaluate our IKNO on both time-dependent and time-independent benchmarks with arbitrary input shapes, including large-scale industrial datasets. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the IKNO method consistently achieves the SOTA accuracy with significant improvements on nearly all benchmark datasets while maintaining scalability to very large point clouds.

</details>


### [334] [From Sequential Nodes to GPU Batches: Parallel Branch and Bound for Optimal $k$-Sparse GLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22188)
*Jiachang Liu,Andrea Lodi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A modular CPU-GPU framework accelerates branch-and-bound for cardinality-constrained generalized linear models by batching nodes on GPUs, achieving up to 100x speedups with exact (zero-gap) solutions and enabling construction of the Rashomon set for downstream analyses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete optimization with branch-and-bound faces severe CPU-GPU data movement bottlenecks and sequential heterogeneity; accelerating BnB on GPUs and enabling exploration of multiple optimal or near-optimal models is valuable for exact solutions and model selection.

Method: A simple, generic, and modular CPU–GPU framework processes multiple BnB nodes in batches on GPUs. It relies on a small set of GPU-efficient routines and uses padding plus lightweight custom kernels to handle irregular node data structures, minimizing data movement and synchronization.

Result: Experiments report one- to two-orders-of-magnitude speedups and zero optimality gap on challenging instances of cardinality-constrained GLMs.

Conclusion: The framework not only speeds up exact solutions but also supports collecting the entire Rashomon set, enabling downstream statistical analyses such as variable-importance assessment and model selection under user-specific metrics (e.g., AUC).

Abstract: GPUs have significantly accelerated first-order methods for large-scale optimization, especially in continuous optimization. However, this success has not transferred cleanly to problems with discrete variables, combinatorial structure, and nonlinear objectives, such as certifying optimal solutions for cardinality-constrained generalized linear models. Major challenges include the sequential processing of heterogeneous nodes in branch and bound (BnB) and frequent data movement between the CPU and GPU. We propose a simple, generic, and modular CPU--GPU framework that processes multiple BnB nodes in batches on GPUs. The framework is built around a small set of GPU-efficient routines and uses padding together with lightweight custom kernels to handle irregular node data structures. Experiments show one to two orders of magnitude speedups and zero optimality gap on challenging instances. The framework can also be extended to collect the entire Rashomon set, enabling downstream statistical analysis such as variable-importance analysis and model selection under secondary user-specific measures (e.g., AUC in classification).

</details>


### [335] [Bandit Convex Optimization with Gradient Prediction Adaptivity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22191)
*Shuche Wang,Adarsh Barik,Vincent Y. F. Tan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Optimistic gradient predictions in bandit convex optimization can help only with two-point feedback; variance limits single-point feedback; TP-VR-OPT achieves near-optimal prediction-adaptive regret depending on S_T (O(√(d E[S_T]))) and is optimal up to √d; adaptive and non-stationary extensions provided.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether gradient predictions can reduce worst-case regret in BCO and how the estimation variance interacts with prediction error.

Method: Introduce TP-VR-OPT with a variance-reduced gradient estimator whose variance scales with prediction error; establish upper bounds; prove information-theoretic lower bound; develop adaptive strategies and dynamic regret analysis in non-stationary settings.

Result: Lower bound Ω(√T) for single-point feedback even if S_T=o(T); two-point feedback yields O(√(d E[S_T])) regret; lower bound Ω(√(E[S_T])); TP-VR-OPT near-optimal up to √d; adaptive horizon-free variants; dynamic regret bounds depending on S_T and path length.

Conclusion: Two-point, variance-reduced optimistic gradient descent enables prediction-adaptive regret that is near-optimal; optimistic predictions help when feedback is two-point; single-point feedback cannot overcome variance; extensions to adaptive and non-stationary environments.

Abstract: Bandit convex optimization (BCO) is a fundamental online learning framework with partial feedback, where the learner observes only the loss incurred at the chosen decision point in each round. In this work, we investigate whether optimistic gradient predictions can improve worst-case regret guarantees in a prediction-adaptive manner. Specifically, given gradient predictions $m_t$, we seek regret bounds that scale with the cumulative prediction error $S_T=\sum_{t=1}^T \|\nabla f_t(x_t)-m_t\|^2.$ We first establish a negative result: under the single-point feedback protocol, an unavoidable $Ω(\sqrt{T})$ regret lower bound persists even when $S_T=o(T)$, showing that the variance of gradient estimation fundamentally obscures the benefit of accurate predictions. To overcome this barrier, we propose \emph{Two-Point Variance-Reduced Optimistic Gradient Descent} (TP-VR-OPT) for the two-point feedback setting. The key idea is a novel variance-reduced gradient estimator whose variance scales with the prediction error rather than the gradient norm. This yields a regret bound of $O\big(\sqrt{d\,\mathbb{E}[S_T]}\big),$ where $d$ is the decision dimension. Complementing this result, we establish an information-theoretic lower bound that scales as $Ω(\sqrt{\mathbb{E}[S_T]})$, providing a fundamental characterization of the best achievable prediction-adaptive regret and showing that TP-VR-OPT is optimal up to a factor of $\sqrt d$. We further develop adaptive variants that eliminate the need for prior knowledge of $\mathbb{E}[S_T]$ or the horizon $T$, and extend our framework to non-stationary environments, establishing dynamic regret guarantees that adapt simultaneously to the cumulative prediction error and the comparator path length.

</details>


### [336] [Reinforced Graph of Thoughts: RL-Driven Adaptive Prompting for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22195)
*Manuel Noah Riesen,Peter Alfred von Niederhäusern*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RGoT automates the GoT prompting framework by using reinforcement learning to adaptively generate a graph of operations from a predefined set, addressing rigidity of manually defined graphs and enabling task-dependent complexity handling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GoT relies on a manually defined, static graph of operations, which is rigid and may not scale to varying problem complexity. An adaptive method could tailor the graph to the task.

Method: Apply reinforcement learning to choose/compose operations from a human-defined set to build a graph of thoughts (GoT) for an LLM, effectively learning a policy that assembles problem-solving steps.

Result: Preliminary results suggest that, under certain constraints, the adaptive graph can match or better handle task complexity by varying the graph structure automatically.

Conclusion: Reinforcement learning can automate and tailor the GoT graph to task requirements, potentially improving problem solving with more scalable and flexible prompting; further work needed on details, evaluation, and generalization.

Abstract: Graph of Thoughts (GoT), a generalized form of recent prompting paradigms for large language models (LLMs), has been shown to be useful for elaborate problem solving. By executing a graph of operations, thoughts of the LLM are structured as an arbitrary graph, forming the actual graph of thoughts. Originally, the graph of operations is defined manually, which requires in-depth knowledge about the solution of the problem to solve. Such a static graph of operations is rigid and therefore lacks adaptability. We propose Reinforced Graph of Thoughts (RGoT), an automated approach to the GoT prompting paradigm that leverages reinforcement learning (RL) to adaptively generate a graph of operations from a human-defined set. Results indicate that, under certain constraints, it is possible to construct graphs of operations adaptively to the task's complexity in an automated way.

</details>


### [337] [Survive or Collapse: The Asymmetric Roles of Data Gating and Reward Grounding in Self-Play RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22217)
*Sophia Xiao Pu,Zhaotian Weng,Chengzhi Liu,Jayanth Srinivasa,Gaowen Liu,William Yang Wang,Xin Eric Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Self-play stability in language-model training is governed primarily by a data-level gate that selects which proposals’ tasks enter training, not by reward shaping. A strict gate ensures stability across various rewards, while rewards cannot compensate for the absence of gating; a continuous gate parameter reveals a two-stage decoupling and a Grounded Proposer Paradox where grounded proposers collapse faster with a self-consistency solver. Data gating, not reward calibration, is the binding constraint on stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To disentangle the effects of data selection (gate) and reward signals on the stability and collapse of self-play RL in language models, and to identify fundamental levers for robust training.

Method: Controlled experiments on a Python output-prediction task and a deterministic-DSL twin task that removes pretraining priors, output ambiguity, and executor noise. Compare strict binary gating vs various reward variants (including self-consistency) and analyze the effect of a continuous gate parameter epsilon. Examine proposer with/without ground-truth access paired with a self-consistency solver.

Result: A strict data gate is sufficient for stability under every tested reward variant; no reward variant stabilizes training in the absence of gating. The Grounded Proposer Paradox shows that ground-truth access accelerates collapse when paired with a self-consistency solver by biasing training toward clean tasks that form a fast path to a spurious self-consistent attractor. Introducing a continuous gate (epsilon) reveals a two-stage phase transition: training-side metrics decouple at low epsilon, while validation accuracy remains stable until very high epsilon.

Conclusion: Data-level gating is the primary constraint on self-play stability; reward calibration cannot substitute for proper task admission control. The findings reveal a critical asymmetry and a coupling between data curation and solver signals that informs design of stable self-play systems.

Abstract: Self-play reinforcement learning trains language models on their own generated tasks, co-evolving a proposer and solver without human labels. Recent systems report strong reasoning gains, but collapse and instability are widely observed and poorly understood. The dominant response treats this as a reward-design problem. We argue instead that self-play stability is governed by two distinct levers: a data-level gate that decides which proposer-generated tasks enter the training pool, and the reward signal that updates the policy on tasks already admitted. Through controlled experiments on a Python output-prediction task and a deterministic-DSL twin task that strips pretraining priors, output ambiguity, and executor noise, we find the two levers are asymmetric. A strict gate is sufficient for stability under every reward variant we test, including a self-consistency reward with no access to ground truth; while no reward variant is sufficient once the gate is removed. This asymmetry exposes a counter-intuitive coupling we call the Grounded Proposer Paradox: a proposer with ground-truth access accelerates collapse faster than an ungrounded one when paired with a self-consistency solver, by concentrating training on clean tasks that form the fastest path to a spurious self-consistent attractor. Replacing the binary gate with a continuous strictness parameter $\varepsilon$ further reveals a two-stage phase transition: training-side metrics decouple at low $\varepsilon$, while validation accuracy holds until $\varepsilon$ is much higher. Data-level gating, not reward calibration, is the binding constraint on self-play stability.

</details>


### [338] [Can Transformers Learn to Verify During Backtracking Search?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22221)
*Yin Jun Phua,Tony Ribeiro,Tuan Nguyen,Katsumi Inoue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decoder-only transformers trained on cumulative solver traces can misalign decisions with the current search state due to scattered state features and history entanglement; two fixes are proposed—localizing state features in traces (localization) and enforcing state-focused attention (Selective State Attention, SSA).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diagnose why autoregressive models that predict continue/backtrack decisions on serialized search traces fail to generalize across same-state trajectories differing by history; improve reliability of learned search control in transformers.

Method: Introduce trace-localization to rewrite each decision block exposing state features locally; apply a fixed attention mask (SSA) to enforce state-based decisions without altering data, objectives, or parameters. Evaluate on reactive verification tasks (3-SAT, graph coloring, Blocks World, backtracking parsing) comparing same-state pairs with different histories.

Result: SSA yields identical continue/backtrack decisions for same-state pairs that differ only in history, addressing the scatter and history-entanglement issues that plague cumulative-trace training; causal baselines fail on these pairs.

Conclusion: Transformers trained on cumulative traces can misuse history; locality and fixed-state attention provide an effective structural remedy without retraining, with implications for inference-time context clearing and broader search-based reasoning in language models.

Abstract: Backtracking search underlies classical constraint solvers, planners, and theorem provers. Recent transformer-based reasoning systems explore search trees over their own intermediate steps. A common training recipe fits an autoregressive next-token loss on offline solver traces. The model's input at each step is a cumulative trace of all prior decisions. The optimal continue-or-backtrack predictor depends only on the current search state, since two trajectories reaching the same state admit the same viable continuations. We show that decoder-only transformers trained on cumulative traces fail this requirement in two ways: the trace can scatter state features across many positions (scattered retrieval), and the predictor can condition on the trajectory rather than the state (history entanglement). We address scattered retrieval with localization, a trace-level fix that rewrites each decision block to expose state features locally. We address history entanglement with Selective State Attention (SSA), a fixed attention mask that enforces state-based decisions structurally without modifying training data, objective, or parameters. We focus on reactive verification, after propagation has exposed a contradiction. We test SSA on 3-SAT, graph coloring, Blocks World, and backtracking parsing. On same-state pairs that differ only in prior history, SSA emits identical decisions while a cumulative-trained causal baseline does not. Our contribution is a diagnostic of transformer behavior on serialized trajectory data, paired with a structural fix. Pretrained language models that search over their own reasoning steps may face the same failure. Our analysis opens up inference-time context clearing as a candidate way to apply the same isolation without retraining.

</details>


### [339] [ARC-STAR: Auditable Post-Hoc Correction for PDE Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22222)
*Chengze Li,Lingwei Wei,Li Sun,Hongbo Lv,Jie Yang,Hongrong Zhang,Kening Zheng,Wei-Chieh Huang,Enze Ma,Philip S. Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ARC-STAR is a three-stage, frozen-solver post-hoc correction framework for PDE foundation models that achieves large error reductions without retraining, by global correction, blockwise local refinement, and budget-aware risk routing to high-risk regions. It preserves the pretrained solver (frozen-host), is auditable (separable stages), and allows budget-constrained deployment. It shows strong results across multiple flow benchmarks, notably >36x error reduction on all cells and up to 99% global error reduction, with a local residual reduction up to 94.4%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unfamiliar flow regimes cause error drift in PDE foundation models; errors concentrate spatially, and retraining destabilizes models while uniform post-hoc corrections miss localized concentration. A post-hoc, auditable, budget-aware correction that preserves the original solver is needed.

Method: A three-stage post-hoc correction pipeline: (1) a global corrector to remove broad solver bias, (2) a blockwise local refiner to clean the remaining post-global residual, and (3) a deployment-time, label-free score that routes refinement to high-risk blocks under a compute budget. The solver is frozen (no fine-tuning), and stages are trained/evaluated separately for auditability; a blockwise interface enables full-field or selective refinement depending on budget.

Result: Across five flow benchmarks spanning ten regime cells, ARC-STAR is the only method to reduce velocity rollout error by at least 36x over raw Poseidon on every cell. The global stage reduces raw host error by 91-99%, and the local stage reduces the remaining post-global residual by up to 94.4%. Code available at the provided URL.

Conclusion: ARC-STAR demonstrates effective, auditable, budget-aware post-hoc refinement for PDE foundation models without retraining, achieving substantial error reductions and offering practical applicability and generalizability across flow regimes.

Abstract: Partial differential equation (PDE) foundation models are pretrained networks that forecast how physical fields like velocity and pressure evolve from a single reusable solver. On unfamiliar flows their predictions drift step by step, errors concentrate in a few regions, yet retraining destabilizes the network and uniform post-hoc correction overlooks this spatial concentration. To address this, we propose a frozen-solver post-hoc correction framework, Adaptive Risk-Calibrated Spatial Triage for Auditable Refinement (ARC-STAR). ARC-STAR organizes correction into three stages: a global corrector removes broad solver bias, a blockwise local refiner cleans the post-global residual, and, at deployment, a label-free score routes refinement to high-risk blocks under a compute budget. The framework is designed to be (i) frozen-host, preserving the pretrained solver without fine-tuning; (ii) auditable, with global and local stages trained and evaluated separately for measurable contributions; and (iii) budget-aware, using a blockwise interface that either refines the full field or routes limited compute to high-risk regions. Across five flow benchmarks spanning ten regime cells, ARC-STAR is the only method that cuts velocity rollout error by at least 36x over raw Poseidon on every cell. The global stage reduces raw host error by 91-99%, and the local stage further reduces the remaining post-global residual by up to 94.4%. Our code implementation is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/arc_star.

</details>


### [340] [How Many Different Outputs Can a Transformer Generate?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22223)
*Maxime Meyer,Mario Michelessa,Caroline Chaux,Vincent Y. F. Tan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Upper bound on the number of sequences a transformer can output is governed by prompt length; accessible sequence length grows linearly; after a threshold, accessible proportion decays exponentially; bound is near tight (factor <10); holds even with unbounded context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain observed empirical behaviors and failures of transformers on simple tasks by deriving fundamental combinatorial bounds on output diversity from architectural constraints.

Method: Provide rigorous bounds on maximal accessible sequence length as a function of prompt length; establish phase transition threshold; derive proportionality and bound constants; validate empirically across architectures and model sizes.

Result: Upper bound is tight up to factor <10; linear growth of accessible sequences with prompt length; after threshold, fraction decays exponentially; bound on the linear coefficient; results hold with unbounded context and computation time.

Conclusion: Theoretical framework explains why transformers struggle on simple tasks despite large context; informs expectations on output diversity; improvements may require changing architectural constraints or training objectives.

Abstract: We study how we can leverage only a handful of characteristics of a transformer's architecture to closely predict the number of different sequences it can output, both qualitatively and quantitatively. We provide an upper bound depending on the length of the prompt, which we show empirically to be tight up to a factor less than 10, across architectures and model sizes. Our analysis also provides a theoretical explanation for previously observed empirical failures of transformers on simple sequence tasks, such as copying and cramming. Formally, we prove that (i) the maximal length of accessible sequences (those that the transformer can output for some prompt) grows linearly with the prompt length, (ii) beyond a critical threshold, the proportion of accessible sequences decays exponentially with sequence length, and (iii) the linear coefficient relating prompt length to accessible sequence length admits a theoretical upper bound. Notably, these results hold even with unbounded context and computation time.

</details>


### [341] [Holomorphic Neural ODEs with Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks for Interpretable Discovery of Complex Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22235)
*Bhaskar Ranjan Karn,Dinesh Kumar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Holomorphic KAN-ODE introduces a Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) with edge-activated B-splines and differentiable Cauchy–Riemann regularization to enforce holomorphic structure in neural ODEs, achieving high accuracy, symbolic discovery, and fractal-boundary reconstruction with far fewer parameters than MLP baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard MLP-based neural ODEs lack complex-analytic priors and CR-consistency, yielding opaque models and poor robustness for holomorphic dynamics; there is a need for interpretable, physics-informed discovery that preserves holomorphic structure and provides symbolic equations.

Method: Replace the MLP component in Neural ODEs with a Kolmogorov-Arnold Network whose learnable activations are B-splines located on network edges; enforce Cauchy–Riemann equations as a differentiable regularizer; validate on six complex dynamical systems (polynomial and transcendental); perform automatic spline-to-formula fitting to recover governing equations; achieve parsimonious parameter count (280 parameters).

Result: The approach yields velocity-field R^2 > 0.95 on all six systems; correctly identifies all six symbolic governing families via spline-to-formula fitting; reconstructs Julia set fractal boundaries with up to 98.0% agreement; under 10% observation noise, MSE degrades by 4% (vs 15.2× for MLP baselines); exhibits a 90.4% improvement in transfer learning from quadratic to cubic dynamics; despite MLPs having lower pointwise error due to higher capacity, KANs provide interpretable symbolic equations, holomorphic enforcement, and superior noise resilience.

Conclusion: KANs offer a parameter-efficient, interpretable alternative to MLPs for physics-informed discovery of holomorphic dynamics, combining holomorphic priors with data-driven learning to recover governing equations and fractal structures while maintaining robustness to noise.

Abstract: Complex dynamical systems governed by holomorphic maps such as $z^2 + c$ exhibit fractal boundaries with extreme sensitivity to initial conditions. Accurately modelling these structures from data requires methods that respect the underlying complex-analytic geometry, yet Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs) within Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (Neural ODEs) lack complex-analytic priors, violate the Cauchy--Riemann conditions, and function as opaque approximators incapable of yielding governing equations. We introduce Holomorphic KAN-ODE, a framework that replaces the MLP with a Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) whose learnable B-spline activations reside on network edges, and incorporates Cauchy--Riemann equations as a differentiable regularization to preserve holomorphic structure. We evaluate on six families of complex dynamical systems spanning polynomial and transcendental classes. With only 280 parameters ($16\times$ fewer than the MLP baseline), the network achieves velocity-field $R^2 > 0.95$ on all six systems, correctly identifies all six governing symbolic families through automatic spline-to-formula fitting, and reconstructs Julia set fractal boundaries with up to 98.0\% agreement. Crucially, the model exhibits only 4\% MSE degradation under 10\% observation noise versus $15.2\times$ for MLPs, and achieves 90.4\% improvement in transfer learning from quadratic to cubic dynamics. While the MLP attains lower pointwise reconstruction error due to its larger capacity, the KAN uniquely provides interpretable symbolic equations, enforced holomorphic structure, and superior noise resilience, capabilities that are entirely absent in black-box architectures. These results establish KANs as a parameter-efficient, interpretable alternative to MLPs for physics-informed discovery of holomorphic dynamics.

</details>


### [342] [Decomposing Ensemble Spread in Lorenz '96 With Learned Stochastic Parameterizations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22242)
*Birgit Kühbacher,Daan Crommelin,Niki Kilbertus*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Systematic study using the two-scale Lorenz 1996 system to disentangle intrinsic variability, initial-condition perturbations, and stochastic model uncertainty; stochastic parameterizations (notably temporally persistent) improve early spread growth and spread-error consistency, while perturbations mainly affect decorrelation and exploration of the invariant measure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Weather and climate forecasts are uncertain due to chaotic dynamics, imperfect initial conditions, and incomplete representation of physical processes. Ensembles often underdisperse, so it is essential to understand how different uncertainty sources interact and how to design effective stochastic parameterizations.

Method: Use the two-scale Lorenz 1996 system as a controlled testbed. Systematically compare multiple ensemble configurations and parameterization strategies, including deterministic, autoregressive, Bayesian, and flow-based approaches. Decompose uncertainty into intrinsic variability, initial-condition perturbations, and stochastic model uncertainty; evaluate impact on spread, error growth, decorrelation, and invariant-measure exploration.

Result: Ensemble perturbations do not increase the system’s long-term variance; instead, they regulate how quickly trajectories decorrelate and explore the invariant measure. Stochastic parameterizations with temporally persistent structure enhance early spread growth and improve spread-error consistency.

Conclusion: Provides a clearer understanding of how different sources of uncertainty interact in chaotic systems and offers guidance for designing and evaluating stochastic parameterizations in weather and climate models.

Abstract: Weather and climate forecasts are inherently uncertain due to chaotic dynamics, imperfect initial conditions, and incomplete representation of the underlying physical processes. Operational ensemble forecasts aim to represent these uncertainties through forecast spread, yet many approaches yield underdispersive estimates, with spread that grows too slowly relative to forecast error. Using the two-scale Lorenz 1996 system as a widely used, controlled testbed, we design a systematic approach to disentangle intrinsic variability, initial-condition perturbations, and stochastic model uncertainty. We compare multiple ensemble configurations and parameterization strategies, including existing deterministic and autoregressive as well as novel Bayesian and flow-based approaches. Our results show that ensemble perturbations do not increase the system's long-term variance; rather, they regulate how rapidly trajectories decorrelate and explore the invariant measure. Stochastic parameterizations, particularly those with temporally persistent structure, enhance early spread growth and improve spread-error consistency. Overall, we bring clarity to how different sources of uncertainty interact in a chaotic system and provide guidance for the design and evaluation of stochastic parameterizations in weather and climate models.

</details>


### [343] [Explainable AI for Data-Driven Design of High-Dimensional Predictive Studies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22243)
*Junyu Yan,Damian Machlanski,Kurt Butler,Panagiotis Dimitrakopoulos,Ewen M Harrison,Bruce Guthrie,Sotirios A Tsaftaris*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Exploratory AI Recommender (EAR) to enhance interpretable survival models by data-driven recommendations; tested on a Cox PH model predicting time to first fall/injury in a large cohort; improves predictive performance and calibration; validated on two public datasets; demonstrates potential for explainable AI to guide high-dimensional model design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Health data often involve many features with non-linearities and interactions. Manual feature selection and transformation for interpretable models is challenging; an explainable AI approach can automate and justify design choices while maintaining transparency.

Method: Develop EAR that uses flexible AI modeling to capture complex patterns and translate them into three recommendation types: feature exclusion, non-linear terms, and feature interactions. Evaluate by comparing a baseline Cox Proportional Hazards (CPH) model to an augmented CPH incorporating EAR recommendations. Primary outcome: time to first fall/injury in 245,614 patients. EAR recommended excluding 23 features, adding non-linear terms for two features, and including 221 feature interactions. Evaluation via C-index (with 95% CI) and calibration metrics (intercept, slope).

Result: C-index improved from 0.805 (95% CI 0.798-0.812) to 0.815 (95% CI 0.809-0.822); calibration intercept shifted from -0.006 to 0.003 and slope from 1.063 to 0.950. EAR proposed 23 feature exclusions, non-linear terms for two features, and 221 interactions; all recommendations aligned with existing literature. Method also demonstrated effectiveness on two public datasets, indicating wider applicability.

Conclusion: EAR demonstrates the potential of explainable AI and data-driven study design to improve development and performance of high-dimensional, yet interpretable, predictive models in health.

Abstract: Predictive modelling is important for health data analysis and data-driven clinical decision-making. However, predictive studies are challenging to design optimally by hand when tens or even hundreds of features require selection, transformation, or interaction modelling. While complex machine learning models offer high performance, their "black-box" nature limits the clinical trust, transparency, and interpretability required for decision-making. We developed and evaluated an Exploratory AI Recommender that provides data-driven recommendations to improve predictive performance of existing interpretable statistical models. The developed framework uses flexible AI modelling to capture complex data patterns and explainable AI techniques to translate the patterns into three recommendation types: feature exclusion, non-linear terms, and feature interactions. We evaluated the framework by comparing predictive performance of a baseline (i.e., no interactions or non-linear terms) Cox Proportional Hazards (CPH) model against an augmented CPH incorporating recommendations suggested by our method. The primary analysis predicts the time to the first occurrence of a fall or related injury in 245,614 patients. Our method recommended excluding 23 features, including non-linear terms for two features, and including 221 suggested feature interactions. The C-index improved from 0.805 (95% CI 0.798-0.812) to 0.815 (95% CI 0.809-0.822), and so did calibration (intercept: -0.006 to 0.003; slope: 1.063 to 0.950). All recommendations were supported by existing literature. The method also proved effective on two additional public datasets, demonstrating wider applicability. The proposed Exploratory AI Recommender demonstrates the potential of explainable AI and data-driven study design to improve the process of developing, and the performance of high-dimensional transparent predictive models.

</details>


### [344] [No Epoch Like the Present: Robust Climate Emulation Requires Out-of-Distribution Generalisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22248)
*Bradley Stanley-Clamp,Anson Lei,Hannah M. Christensen,Ingmar Posner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Seasonal climate variation serves as a real-world, zero-overhead OOD testbed for climate emulators, revealing that state-of-the-art hybrid-ML models degrade under such shifts; incorporating physics-informed, compositional decompositions improves robustness with modest IID trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ML-based climate emulators trained on present-day data often fail under distribution shifts caused by climate change. Standard IID evaluation underestimates this risk. The work seeks to quantify climate-induced distribution shifts, validate seasonal variation as a proxy, and push toward robust emulator design.

Method: Empirical analysis shows climate-change driven shifts in atmospheric state distributions; uses seasonal variation as a proxy for long-term shifts; proposes a zero-overhead seasonal-shift evaluation framework; conducts a systematic characterization of hybrid-ML emulators under these shifts; investigates compositional generalisation and physics-informed decompositions to improve OOD robustness.

Result: Hybrid-ML climate emulators exhibit significant degradation under seasonal shifts, indicating poor OOD performance. Physically motivated, decomposed representations substantially improve OOD performance with only modest reductions in in-distribution accuracy.

Conclusion: A principled path to robust climate emulation lies in compositional generalisation and physics-informed decompositions, enabling ML-driven climte emulators that are more resilient to unknown future distribution shifts.

Abstract: Climate emulation is an out-of-distribution (OOD) projection task. This is precisely the challenge where modern Machine Learning (ML) methods are most prone to failure. Consequently, while current ML emulators trained on present climate achieve high in-distribution performance, their future reliability under the inevitable distribution shifts of a changing climate remains a critical, poorly understood blind spot. Addressing this challenge requires a fundamental shift in how we understand, evaluate, and design climate emulators. In this work, we first confirm that climate change drives a statistically significant and progressively growing shift in atmospheric state distributions, rendering standard evaluation protocols insufficient. We empirically establish that seasonal variation serves as an effective proxy for these long-term climate shifts, providing access to $\textit{real-world}$ distribution shifts without recourse to heuristics like synthetic perturbations. Motivated by this link, we introduce a novel evaluation framework that leverages seasonal shifts as a rigorous, zero-overhead testbed for emulator robustness. Our systematic characterisation confirms that current state-of-the-art hybrid-ML emulators degrade significantly under these realistic shifts. Finally, we chart a path forward by identifying compositional generalisation, the ability to form novel combinations from observed elementary components, as a principled route towards robust climate emulation. We demonstrate that physically motivated decompositions substantially improve OOD performance with only modest trade-offs against in-distribution performance, providing an avenue towards ML-driven climate emulators robust to an unknown future.

</details>


### [345] [What are the Right Symmetries for Formal Theorem Proving?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22257)
*Krzysztof Olejniczak,Radoslav Dimitrov,Xingyue Huang,Bernardo Cuenca Grau,Jinwoo Kim,İsmail İlkan Ceylan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes rewriting categories to model non-invertible proof transformations, defines proof equivariance and success invariance, analyzes current provers, and presents test-time aggregation over rewritings to recover invariance and boost robustness under fixed budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the sensitivity of LLM-driven formal theorem provers to superficial problem representations and identify symmetry-based inductive biases essential for robust proof search.

Method: Introduce rewriting categories as a category-theoretic framework capturing proof-tactic transformations; formalize two symmetries (proof equivariance and success invariance); compare state-based next-tactic provers with LLM-based provers; propose test-time aggregation over equivalent rewritings; provide theoretical justification (invariance in sampling limit) and empirical evaluation.

Result: Found that state-based provers satisfy proof equivariance; LLM-based provers satisfy neither symmetry and show large performance variance across equivalent formulations; test-time aggregation over rewritings approximates success invariance in the sampling limit and improves robustness and performance under fixed inference budgets.

Conclusion: Symmetry is a key missing inductive bias in LLM-based theorem proving; test-time computation over equivalent rewritings offers a practical approach to approximate this symmetry and enhance robustness.

Abstract: Formal theorem provers based on large language models (LLMs) are highly sensitive to superficial variations in problem representation: semantically equivalent statements can exhibit drastically different proof success rates, revealing a failure to respect structural symmetries inherent in formal mathematics. This raises a central question: what are the right symmetries for formal theorem proving? We introduce rewriting categories, a category-theoretic framework capturing the compositional, generally non-invertible transformations induced by proof tactics, and use it to formalize two symmetry notions: proof equivariance, governing how proof distributions transform under rewrites, and success invariance (i.e., invariance of success probability), requiring equivalent statements to be solved with the same probability. We observe that state-based next-tactic provers naturally satisfy proof equivariance by operating on proof states. In contrast, state-of-the-art LLM-based provers satisfy neither property, exhibiting large performance variation across equivalent formulations. To mitigate this, we propose test-time methods that aggregate over equivalent rewritings of the input, showing theoretically that they recover success invariance in the sampling limit, and empirically, that they improve robustness and performance under fixed inference budgets. Our results highlight symmetry as a key missing inductive bias in LLM-based theorem proving and suggest test-time computation as a practical route to approximate it.

</details>


### [346] [Tailoring Teaching to Aptitude: Direction-Adaptive Self-Distillation for LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22263)
*Hongbin Zhang,Chaozheng Wang,Kehai Chen,Youcheng Pan,Yang Xiang,Jinpeng Wang,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DASD is an entropy-aware, direction-adaptive self-distillation method for on-policy LLM post-training that improves reasoning by routing supervision based on token uncertainty, balancing exploration and step accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OPSD uses a uniform teacher signal that suppresses exploration for high-entropy tokens and may degrade step-level execution for low-entropy tokens, harming complex reasoning.

Method: Introduce entropy-routed directional supervision (DASD): high-entropy tokens are pushed away from the privileged teacher; low-entropy tokens are pulled toward the teacher. Evaluate on six math reasoning benchmarks against RLVR and self-distillation baselines.

Result: DASD achieves the best macro Avg@16 over baselines; Pass@k, reasoning-health, and generalization analyses indicate gains derive from preserving exploration while maintaining step-level execution.

Conclusion: Directional, entropy-aware self-distillation improves complex reasoning under OPSD and offers a robust approach to balance exploration and execution in LLM post-training.

Abstract: On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) is an emerging LLM post-training paradigm in which the model serves as its own teacher: conditioned on privileged information such as a reference trace or hint, the same policy provides dense token-level supervision on its own rollouts. However, recent studies show that OPSD degrades complex reasoning by suppressing predictive uncertainty, which supports exploration and hypothesis revision. Our token-level analysis shows that this failure arises from applying a uniform direction of teacher supervision across tokens with different uncertainty levels: conformity to the privileged self-teacher suppresses exploration at high entropy, while deviation from the teacher degrades step accuracy at low entropy. Accordingly, we propose \textbf{Direction-Adaptive Self-Distillation} (\textbf{DASD}), which reframes privileged self-distillation from uniform teacher imitation into entropy-routed directional supervision: high-entropy tokens are pushed away from the privileged teacher to preserve exploration, while low-entropy tokens are pulled toward the teacher to stabilize step-level execution. Across six mathematical reasoning benchmarks, DASD achieves the best macro Avg@16 over strong RLVR and self-distillation baselines. Pass@$k$, reasoning-health, and generalization analyses show that these average gains come from preserving exploration without sacrificing step-level execution.

</details>


### [347] [Detecting Atypical Clients in Federated Learning via Representation-Level Divergence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22266)
*Cristian Pérez-Corral,Jose I. Mestre,Alberto Fernández-Hernández,Manuel F. Dolz,Enrique S. Quitana-Ortí*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A lightweight geometric signal quantifies functional deviation of federated clients by examining how local training alters the activation-induced input-space partition relative to a shared probe set; permutation-invariant and interpretable, it detects atypical functional changes and supports risk-aware aggregation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning faces data heterogeneity and potential distributional shifts/anomalies causing unstable updates; a robust, interpretable measure of client behavior is needed to assess reliability beyond parameter/gradient comparisons.

Method: Compute a probe-set-based analysis of the activation-induced partitioning produced by each client's local training, yielding a permutation-invariant metric of client-global divergence that reflects how the model processes data differently after local updates.

Result: The signal effectively identifies clients causing atypical functional changes, distinguishing stable yet heterogeneous clients from those diverging from the global regime; provides a simple tool for monitoring behavior.

Conclusion: Supports risk-aware aggregation in federated systems by enabling monitoring and selective weighting based on functional deviation rather than raw parameters or gradients.

Abstract: Federated learning enables collaborative training across distributed clients with heterogeneous data, but such heterogeneity often leads to unstable updates and degraded global performance. Moreover, in practical deployments, client updates may deviate from the expected behavior not only due to benign not i.i.d. distributions, but also due to distributional shifts or anomalous inputs, raising concerns about the reliability of the aggregation process. In this work, we propose a lightweight geometric signal to quantify the functional deviation of a client with respect to the global model. Instead of comparing model parameters or gradients, our approach measures how the local training of each client alters the activation-induced partition of the input space, evaluated on a shared probe set. This yields a permutation-invariant, interpretable metric of client--global divergence that captures differences in how data is processed by the model. We show that this signal effectively identifies clients that induce atypical functional changes, distinguishing stable yet heterogeneous clients from those whose updates significantly diverge from the global regime. As a result, the proposed metric provides a simple tool for monitoring client behavior and enabling risk-aware aggregation strategies in federated learning systems.

</details>


### [348] [Adaptive Measurement Allocation for Learning Kernelized SVMs Under Noisy Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22275)
*Artur Miroszewski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive, task-aware measurement allocation for noisy kernel learning improves SVM performance over uniform allocation by focusing measurements on decision-critical kernel entries using geometric sensitivity and active-set instability; theoretical regime characterization; empirical gains and early stopping; applicable to quantum kernels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Kernel methods assume exact Gram access, but in settings like quantum machine learning kernel entries must be inferred under budget constraints. Uniform allocation ignores nonuniform kernel importance, potentially wasting measurements on entries with little impact on the decision boundary.

Method: Introduce two signals—geometric sensitivity (how kernel-entry perturbations affect the SVM margin) and active-set instability (probability of support-vector changes due to measurement noise). Combine these into a task-aware allocation that concentrates measurements on kernel entries most critical for the decision function. Provide theoretical analysis showing regime-dependent gains, and a dual-coefficient stability criterion for early stopping.

Result: Adaptive allocation yields improved support-vector recovery, margin estimation, and decision-function accuracy under fixed budgets in synthetic data. The dual-coefficient stability criterion enables early stopping with reduced measurement cost, approaching near-optimal performance. Experiments on quantum kernels derived from real data show regime-dependent behavior consistent with kernel concentration.

Conclusion: Task-aware, adaptive measurement allocation leverages heterogeneity in kernel importance to outperform uniform schemes in noisy Gram settings. The approach yields theoretical and empirical gains, with practical relevance for quantum kernels and other applications where measurement budgets are limited.

Abstract: Kernel methods are typically formulated under the assumption of exact, noise-free access to the Gram matrix. However, in emerging settings such as quantum machine learning, each kernel entry must be inferred from noisy observations, and its accuracy depends on how a limited measurement budget is allocated. Despite this, existing approaches overwhelmingly rely on uniform allocation, which equalizes estimator variance but ignores the highly non-uniform dependence of kernelized classifiers on the Gram matrix. In this work, we introduce an adaptive measurement-allocation strategy for learning kernelized Support Vector Machines (SVMs) from noisy Bernoulli observations. Our approach combines two complementary principles: (i) geometric sensitivity, capturing how perturbations of individual kernel entries affect the classifier margin, and (ii) active-set instability, quantifying the probability of discrete changes in support-vector membership induced by measurement noise. These signals define a task-aware allocation scheme that concentrates measurements on the most decision-critical regions of the kernel matrix. We provide a theoretical analysis showing that the benefit of adaptive allocation is governed by the heterogeneity of the induced kernel importance structure, leading to distinct regimes in which adaptive or uniform strategies are preferable. Empirical evaluations on synthetic datasets demonstrate that adaptive allocation significantly improves support-vector recovery, margin estimation, and decision-function accuracy under fixed measurement budgets. A dual-coefficient stability criterion further enables early stopping, achieving near-optimal performance while using only a fraction of the measurement cost. Additional experiments on quantum kernels derived from real-world data reveal a regime-dependent behavior aligned with known phenomena such as kernel concentration. Together...

</details>


### [349] [EmoTrack: Robust Depression Tracking from Counseling Transcripts across Session Regimes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22286)
*Zhaomin Wu,Jiayi Li,Bingsheng He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EmoTrack improves PHQ-8 prediction in counseling transcripts by fusing LLM-extracted signals with frozen turn-level embeddings and cross-session memory, achieving strong single-session gains and competitive longitudinal performance on DAIC-WOZ and LongCounsel.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for robust depression tracking across single- and multi-session regimes. Fine-tuning methods may overfit under data scarcity; prompt-based LLMs lack longitudinal context. A hybrid approach exploiting both rich supervision and data efficiency can bridge this gap.

Method: The approach combines LLM-extracted clinical signals with frozen turn-level semantic embeddings to form a transcript representation; symptom-specific predictors are trained on this representation; when available, compact cross-session memory integrates prior sessions; evaluated on LongCounsel (new multi-session dataset) and DAIC-WOZ (single-session benchmark).

Result: EmoTrack yields a clear gain on the real single-session benchmark, with a 13.5% relative MAE reduction over the strongest DAIC-WOZ baseline, and remains competitive with the strongest longitudinal baseline on LongCounsel.

Conclusion: EmoTrack demonstrates improved robustness and effectiveness for PHQ-8 prediction across session regimes by combining multimodal transcript representations with cross-session memory, validated on two benchmarks.

Abstract: Text-based counseling is an important interface for AI mental-health support, where transcripts may be used to monitor depression severity and flag sessions requiring timely human review. However, robust PHQ-8 prediction across session regimes remains challenging: fine-tuning-based methods can exploit richer supervision but may generalize poorly under data scarcity, while prompt-based LLM methods are data-efficient but usually treat each transcript holistically and provide limited support for longitudinal context. We study robust depression tracking from counseling transcripts across single-session and multi-session regimes. We introduce LongCounsel, a multi-session counseling dataset with session-level PHQ-8 supervision for evaluating repeated-session tracking under partial symptom disclosure and cross-session continuity. We further propose EmoTrack, a PHQ-8 prediction framework that combines LLM-extracted clinical signals with frozen turn-level semantic embeddings and trains symptom-specific predictors over the resulting transcript representation. When prior sessions are available, EmoTrack can further incorporate them through compact cross-session memory. Experiments on LongCounsel and DAIC-WOZ show that EmoTrack achieves a clear gain on the real single-session benchmark, including a 13.5% relative MAE reduction over the strongest DAIC-WOZ baseline, and remains competitive with the strongest longitudinal baseline on LongCounsel.

</details>


### [350] [Long-term Fairness with Selective Labels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22291)
*Giovani Valdrighi,Isabel Valera,Marcos Medeiros Raimundo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework for long-term fairness with selective (censored) labels, decomposing true fairness into observed fairness and label-prediction bias; derives sufficient conditions and a RL algorithm; shows semisynthetic results close to oracle.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Selective labels (e.g., loan repayment) bias fairness evaluation; naive methods fail to guarantee true long-term fairness; need to leverage label predictors to estimate true fairness.

Method: Theoretically decompose fairness into observable component plus bias from predictions; derive sufficient conditions using predictor confidence to guarantee true fairness from observable quantities; develop RL algorithm for long-term fair decision-making under selective labels.

Result: In semisynthetic environments, algorithm matches fairness and performance of oracle-access baseline.

Conclusion: Provides a principled approach to long-term fair decision-making with selective labels; bridges theory and RL algorithm; practical potential contingent on predictor reliability.

Abstract: Long-term fairness algorithms aim to satisfy fairness beyond static and short-term notions by accounting for the dynamics between decision-making policies and population behavior. Most previous approaches evaluate performance and fairness measures from observable features and a label, which is assumed to be fully observed. However, in scenarios such as hiring or lending, the labels (e.g., ability to repay the loan) are selective labels as they are only revealed based on positive decisions (e.g., when a loan is granted). In this paper, we study long-term fairness in the selective labels setting and analytically show that naive solutions do not guarantee fairness. To address this gap, we then introduce a novel framework that leverages both the observed data and a label predictor model to estimate the true fairness measure value by decomposing it into the observed fairness and bias from label predictions. This allows us to derive sufficient conditions to satisfy true fairness from observable quantities by using the confidence in the predictor model. Finally, we rely on our theoretical results to propose a novel reinforcement learning algorithm for effective long-term fair decision-making with selective labels. In semisynthetic environments, the proposed algorithm reached comparable fairness and performance to an agent with oracle access to the true labels.

</details>


### [351] [One LR Doesn't Fit All: Heavy-Tail Guided Layerwise Learning Rates for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22297)
*Di He,Songjun Tu,Keyu Wang,Lu Yin,Shiwei Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Layerwise Learning Rate (LLR) adaptively assigns per-layer learning rates to Transformer layers using Heavy-Tailed Self-Regularization (HT-SR) theory, balancing training across layers. It achieves faster convergence and better generalization, with reported up to 1.5x training speedups and a +1.93 percentage point gain in zero-shot accuracy; LR settings transfer from a uniform baseline."


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard practice of using a uniform learning rate ignores the structural heterogeneity of Transformers; HT-SR describes the empirical spectral density of weight correlations to quantify heavy-tailedness, which can guide per-layer LR to balance training and improve efficiency.

Method: Compute the empirical spectral density (ESD) of weight correlation matrices per Transformer layer to quantify heavy-tailedness. Assign larger learning rates to layers with weaker heavy-tailedness and smaller learning rates to layers with stronger heavy-tailedness. Apply this per-layer LR schedule across architectures (LLaMA to GPT-nano), optimizers (AdamW, Muon), and parameter scales (60M–1B). The approach transfers LR settings from a uniform baseline with low tuning overhead. Code is released.

Result: LLR enables faster training (up to 1.5x speedup) and improved generalization. It raises average zero-shot accuracy from 47.09% to 49.02% across evaluated settings, with consistent benefits across architectures and optimizers and parameter scales.

Conclusion: LLR provides a theoretically grounded, practically light-hearted per-layer LR heuristic that balances training dynamics in Transformers, yielding faster convergence and improved generalization while requiring minimal tuning; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Learning rate configuration is a fundamental aspect of modern deep learning. The prevailing practice of applying a uniform learning rate across all layers overlooks the structural heterogeneity of Transformers, potentially limiting their effectiveness as the backbone of Large Language Models (LLMs). In this paper, we introduce Layerwise Learning Rate (LLR), an adaptive scheme that assigns distinct learning rates to individual Transformer layers. Our method is grounded in Heavy-Tailed Self-Regularization (HT-SR) theory, which characterizes the empirical spectral density (ESD) of weight correlation matrices to quantify heavy-tailedness. Layers with weaker heavy-tailedness are assigned larger learning rates to accelerate their training, while layers with stronger heavy-tailedness receive smaller learning rates. By tailoring learning rates in this manner, LLR promotes balanced training across layers, leading to faster convergence and improved generalization. Extensive experiments across architectures (from LLaMA to GPT-nano), optimizers (AdamW and Muon), and parameter scales (60M-1B) demonstrate that LLR achieves up to 1.5x training speedup and outperforms baselines, notably raising average zero-shot accuracy from 47.09% to 49.02%. A key advantage of LLR is its low tuning overhead: it transfers nearly optimal LR settings directly from the uniform baseline. Code is available at https://github.com/hed-ucas/Layer-wise-Learning-Rate.

</details>


### [352] [Chebyshev Policies and the Mountain Car Problem: Reinforcement Learning for Low-Dimensional Control Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22305)
*Stefan Huber,Hannes Unger,Georg Schäfer,Jakob Rehrl*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Analytical Mountain Car solution yields a simple optimal policy; introduces Chebyshev policies as a dense universal policy class that outperforms neural nets with far fewer parameters and less regret in low-dimensional control tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between optimal control and RL by deriving the exact solution, reveal the simplicity of optimal policies, and provide a universal, efficient policy class (Chebyshev) to improve sample efficiency and explainability.

Method: Analytical derivation of the Mountain Car optimal control; formulation and construction of Chebyshev policies from first principles; trainable as drop-in replacements for neural networks; extensive empirical evaluation on additional RL tasks including a real-world nonlinear motion control testbed; comparison with PPO, ARS, and REINFORCE.

Result: The optimal control for Mountain Car is surprisingly simple; modern RL agents exhibit a substantial gap to optimality. Chebyshev policies, trained as neural-network drop-ins, reduce regret by 4.18x and use 277x fewer parameters. They improve performance across additional RL tasks, including real-world tests, outperforming NN baselines (PPO, ARS, REINFORCE).

Conclusion: Chebyshev policies offer a compelling, lightweight alternative or complement to neural nets for low-dimensional control tasks, enhancing explainability, sample efficiency, and real-time capability.

Abstract: We analytically solve the Mountain Car problem, a canonical benchmark in RL, and derive an optimal control solution, closing a gap after 36 years. This enables us to reveal two surprising insights: The optimal control is quite simple, yet modern RL agents display a large gap to optimality. Motivated by the analysis of the optimal control, we introduce Chebyshev policies as a universal (i.e. dense) class of RL policies from first principles. They can be trained as drop-in replacements of neural nets, reducing the regret by a factor of 4.18, while requiring 277 times fewer parameters, fostering sample efficiency, explainability and realtime capability. Chebyshev policies are evaluated on further RL tasks, including a real-world nonlinear motion control testbed. They consistently improve performance over neural nets with PPO, ARS and REINFORCE. Our results demonstrate how Chebyshev policies offer a compelling and lightweight alternative or addition to neural nets for low-dimensional control tasks.

</details>


### [353] [SepsisAI Orchestrator: A Containerized and Scalable Platform for Deploying AI Models and Real-Time Monitoring in Early Sepsis Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22331)
*Santiago Ospitia,John Sanabria,John Garcia-Henao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Open-source SepsisAI-Orchestrator demonstrates a deployment-focused platform for early sepsis detection, revealing a U-shaped scaling behavior for LightGBM inference under load and providing actionable guidance for aligning replicas with CPU threads; no prospective clinical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between high predictive accuracy and bedside deployment by addressing heterogeneous data representations, standardized deployment workflows, and latency constraints in hospital environments.

Method: Modular platform integrating CDA preprocessing, NoSQL storage, a containerized LightGBM classifier served via REST APIs, and a Streamlit clinical dashboard, orchestrated with Docker and Kubernetes; load testing with k6 across 50–1000 concurrent virtual users to quantify performance as replica counts scale.

Result: Replica count must match host CPU threads; scaling from 3 to 12 replicas on a 12-thread CPU reduced p95 latency from 3.3s to 1.41s (57.3% reduction) and eliminated all request failures; over-provisioning to 24 or 48 replicas caused scheduler contention and degraded performance; the U-shaped scaling behavior under clinical AI inference workloads is quantified; the model itself was previously validated (F1 0.87–0.94 on PhysioNet 2019).

Conclusion: Infrastructure-centric contributions plus empirical load characterization advance deployment practices for clinical AI; code and manifests are open-source; prospective clinical validation is not claimed.

Abstract: Despite strong predictive results in the clinical machine learning literature, the translation of these models into bedside use remains limited by systems-level barriers: heterogeneous data representations, the absence of standardized deployment workflows, and a mismatch between research prototypes and the concurrency and latency requirements of hospital environments. We present the SepsisAI-Orchestrator, an open-source modular platform that addresses this deployment gap for early sepsis detection. The platform integrates HL7 FHIR-inspired Clinical Document Architecture (CDA) preprocessing, NoSQL storage, a containerized LightGBM classifier served via REST APIs, and a Streamlit clinical dashboard, orchestrated with Docker and Kubernetes. A previously validated LightGBM model (F1 0.87-0.94 on PhysioNet 2019) is reused without modification; the contribution lies in the surrounding infrastructure and its empirical characterization under load. Using k6 with 50-1000 concurrent virtual users, we find that replica count must be matched to the physical CPU thread count of the host: scaling from 3 to 12 replicas on a 12-thread CPU reduces p95 latency from 3.3s to 1.41s (57.3% reduction) and eliminates all request failures, while over-provisioning to 24 or 48 replicas degrades performance due to scheduler contention. To our knowledge this U-shaped scaling behavior has not been quantified previously for clinical AI inference workloads. We do not claim prospective clinical validation. Source code and deployment manifests are available at https://github.com/nucleusai/sepsisai-orchestrator.

</details>


### [354] [Riemannian geometry meets fMRI: the advantages of modeling correlation manifolds and eigenvector subspaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22334)
*Mario Severino,Manuela Moretto,Robert A. McCutcheon,Mattia Veronese*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A scalable geometric framework for brain-network analysis based on correlation matrices. It introduces the Off-log metric, which maps correlation matrices to symmetric zero-diagonal matrices to enable closed-form distances, Frechet means, and linear models, and a Grassmannian subspace discrimination method that compares subjects via principal-angle distances between eigenvector subspaces. Together they integrate into standard ML workflows and improve sensitivity and predictive performance across multiple fMRI cohorts, outperforming Euclidean baselines and matching or exceeding Riemannian methods in various tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Correlation matrices capture functional brain networks but lie on a curved manifold, making standard Euclidean analyses inappropriate. Existing geometric approaches often lack closed-form operations or depend on arbitrary region ordering, hindering scalability to large datasets and routine ML workflows.

Method: Two components: (i) Off-log metric that transforms correlation matrices into symmetric zero-diagonal matrices, yielding closed-form expressions for distances, Frechet means, and linear models, enabling conventional statistical modeling without complex manifold optimization; (ii) Grassmannian subspace discrimination that uses principal-angle distances between eigenvector subspaces to compare subjects, addressing sign and basis ambiguities. These are integrated into standard ML pipelines for inference, regression, and classification.

Result: Empirical validation across two clinical cohorts (Parkinson's disease and psychosis) and three aging fMRI datasets showed that the Off-log metric increased sensitivity in permutation tests and matched or surpassed Riemannian and Euclidean baselines for classification. Brain-age predictions were comparable to baselines, with Riemannian metrics outperforming in two of three cohorts. The Grassmannian method consistently outperformed Euclidean baselines, highlighting networks relevant to disease. Overall, geometry-aware representations improved sensitivity and predictive performance while remaining scalable and easy to deploy.

Conclusion: The proposed Off-log and Grassmannian components offer a scalable, geometry-aware framework for functional- connectivity analysis that enhances statistical sensitivity and predictive accuracy without demanding complex optimization, making it suitable for large-scale neuroimaging studies.

Abstract: Correlation matrices are fundamental summaries of functional brain networks, yet standard analyses often treat entries independently, ignoring the curved geometry of correlation space. Existing geometric methods frequently lack closed-form operations or depend on arbitrary region ordering, limiting scalability. We introduce a scalable geometric framework with two components: (i) the Off-log metric, a smooth transformation mapping correlation matrices to symmetric zero-diagonal matrices. This enables closed-form expressions for distances, Frechet means, and linear models, allowing standard statistical modeling without complex manifold optimization. (ii) Grassmannian subspace discrimination, which compares subjects via principal-angle distances between eigenvector subspaces, resolving inherent sign and basis ambiguities. Both components integrate into standard machine-learning workflows for inference, regression, and classification. Validated across two clinical cohorts (Parkinson's and psychosis) and three ageing fMRI datasets, the Off-log metric increased sensitivity in permutation tests and matched or exceeded Riemannian and Euclidean baselines in classification. Brain-age prediction performance was comparable, with Riemannian metrics excelling in two of three cohorts. The Grassmannian method consistently outperformed Euclidean baselines, highlighting disease-relevant networks. Overall, geometry-aware representations improve sensitivity and predictive performance while remaining straightforward to deploy at scale.

</details>


### [355] [Learning Causal Orderings for In-Context Tabular Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22335)
*Sascha Xu,Sarah Mameche,Jilles Vreeken*

Main category: cs.LG
