TL;DR: Proposes TabOrder: learns causal topological orderings and uses order-constrained attention for tabular prediction; jointly infers structure and predicts/imputes; robust to missingness and distribution shift; validated on real data with interventions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge causal discovery and predictive modeling in tabular data; address distribution shift, interventions, and missing data by integrating causal order into prediction.

Method: Introduce TabOrder: a model that enforces causal order constraints in attention so predictions depend only on predecessors in the learned order; learns the optimal variable ordering via a likelihood-based objective in an unsupervised fashion; justifies under standard functional model assumptions; analyzes missingness interactions; empirical evaluation on ordering accuracy and downstream tasks (prediction, imputation, interventions).

Result: TabOrder recovers accurate variable orderings; effective for prediction and imputation; yields insight into real-world biological data under intervention.

Conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of simultaneous causal order inference and enforcement within tabular prediction; TabOrder can recover orderings and improve task performance; supports use of causal structure as a predictive inductive bias.

Abstract: In-context learning for tabular data sets strong predictive standards in observational settings; it however primarily relies on correlational structure, which becomes unreliable under distribution shift or intervention. While established methods to discover causal structure exist, they are often focused on structure identifiability and decoupled from the predictive architectures that could benefit from them.
  To bridge these perspectives, we study how to simultaneously infer and enforce causal structure in the form of topological variable orderings into tabular prediction. Unlike standard architectures, our model TabOrder uses causal order-constrained attention, basing predictions only on features that precede a target under a learned causal order. Similar to causal discovery methods, TabOrder learns the optimal variable ordering in an unsupervised manner through a likelihood-based objective. We justify this choice under standard functional model classes and also study how sample missingness, a common challenge in tabular data, interacts with causal direction identification. Empirically, we confirm that TabOrder recovers accurate variable orderings while addressing prediction and imputation tasks, as well as gives insight into real-world biological data under intervention.

</details>


### [356] [From Snapshots to Trajectories: Learning Single-Cell Gene Expression Dynamics via Conditional Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22340)
*Siyu Pu,Qingqing Long,Xiaohan Huang,Haotian Chen,Jiajia Wang,Meng Xiao,Xiao Luo,Hengshu Zhu,Yuanchun Zhou,Xuezhi Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: scFM introduces a flow-matching framework with entropically regularized OT couplings and bidirectional velocity fields to model time-dependent gene expression dynamics from sparse, unpaired scRNA-seq snapshots, addressing long-horizon drift and instability and enabling coherent trajectory interpolation/extrapolation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Time-resolved scRNA-seq often provides only a few discrete, unpaired snapshots, causing ambiguity in local transitions and drift in long-range predictions; there is a need for a method that yields temporally coherent dynamics and stable long-horizon forecasting.

Method: Compute entropically regularized OT couplings between adjacent snapshots to form soft, weighted flow-matching targets for learning time-dependent velocity fields; learn bidirectional velocity fields with consistency enforcement to refine couplings; integrate distribution-level alignment and latent dynamic regularization to anchor long rollouts and reduce drift.

Result: Empirical results on real scRNA-seq time-series datasets show improved distributional prediction for both temporal interpolation and extrapolation; more accurate trajectory reconstruction and temporally coherent visualizations in regions with missing intermediate time points.

Conclusion: scFM provides a coupling-conditioned flow-matching approach that stabilizes inference from sparse time-series data, improves temporal coherence, and mitigates drift in long-horizon predictions while yielding better trajectory reconstruction.

Abstract: Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) provides high-dimensional profiles of cellular states, enabling data-driven modeling of cellular dynamics over time. In practice, time-resolved scRNA-seq is collected at only a few discrete time points as unpaired snapshot populations, leaving substantial temporal gaps. This motivates trajectory inference at unmeasured time points. Existing methods mainly follow two directions, optimal-transport (OT) alignment provides distribution-level matching between observed snapshots, while continuous-time generative models support forecasting via learned dynamics. However, two challenges remain: (i) unpaired snapshots render local transitions between adjacent time points ambiguous, leading to unstable supervision; and (ii) long-horizon prediction relies on repeated integration, where small modeling errors compound and cause distribution drift. To address these challenges, we propose single-cell Flow Matching (scFM), a latent generative framework based on coupling-conditioned flow matching. First, we compute entropically regularized OT couplings between adjacent snapshots and use them to construct soft, weighted flow-matching targets for learning time-dependent velocity fields. Second, we learn bidirectional velocity fields and leverage their consistency to refine couplings and improve temporal coherence under sparse supervision. Third, we introduce distribution-level alignment and latent dynamic regularization to anchor long rollouts and mitigate drift. Experiments on real-world time-series scRNA-seq datasets show that scFM consistently improves distributional prediction performance for both temporal interpolation and extrapolation. Moreover, scFM yields more accurate trajectory reconstruction and temporally coherent visualizations where intermediate time points are absent, indicating a more faithful recovery of underlying temporal gene expression dynamics.

</details>


### [357] [A Boundary-Layer Mechanism for One-Third Scaling in Online Softmax Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22341)
*Marcel Kühn,Yoon Thelge,Bernd Rosenow*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: In an online teacher-student model with hard labels and smooth surrogates, late-time dynamics yield a α^{-1/3} learning curve for test loss and generalization error due to boundary-layer dynamics; learning-rate schedules can push toward α^{-1/2}; data structure affects transients; results complement spectral explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why smooth surrogate losses create mismatch with discrete labels and how this mismatch shapes long-time learning curves; explore asymptotic dynamics in the thermodynamic limit; investigate how boundary layers near decision boundaries govern learning.

Method: Theoretical analysis of an online teacher-student model, subtract mean logit, study centered dynamics with alignment D and variance Δ; derive late-time boundary-layer solution with width O(D^{-1}); analyze effect of fixed-learning-rate stochastic gradient descent; perform simulations with correlated Gaussian inputs and whitened pretrained features.

Result: Late-time α scaling α^{-1/3} for test loss and ε_g; Bayes-optimal α^{-1} not achieved; learning-rate schedules improve to α^{-1/2}; simulations validate predictions; data structure can dominate transients.

Conclusion: This is an asymptotic, complementary mechanism to spectral explanations of neural scaling laws; boundary-layer dynamics under hard-label vs soft surrogate losses offers insight into slow learning curves and the role of data structure; practical implication: schedule tuning can mitigate the slower power-law.

Abstract: Hard-label classification is usually trained with smooth surrogate losses, most prominently softmax cross-entropy. We isolate an asymptotic mechanism by which this mismatch between smooth surrogate and discrete labels produces power-law learning curves in an online teacher-student model. After subtracting the mean logit, the thermodynamic-limit dynamics close in centered variables: a growing centered student-teacher alignment $D$ and the residual student variance $Δ$. At late times, examples away from teacher decision boundaries are already classified confidently and contribute exponentially little. Only boundary layers of width $O(D^{-1})$ remain active, while the noise of fixed-learning-rate online gradient descent maintains a nonzero $Δ$. As a function of the training time $α$ the late-time solution yields a $α^{-1/3}$ power law not only for the test loss but also for the generalization error $ε_g$, i.e., one minus test accuracy. This is much slower than the $α^{-1}$ Bayes-optimal reference for the same model. We further show that learning-rate schedules can improve the generalization error towards a $ε_g \sim α^{-1/2}$ power law. Simulations support the predicted order parameter dynamics and learning curves. Controlled experiments with correlated Gaussian inputs and whitened pretrained features show that data structure can dominate transients. Therefore, our result is an asymptotic, complementary mechanism rather than an alternative to spectral explanations of neural scaling laws.

</details>


### [358] [Partial Fusion of Neural Networks: Efficient Tradeoffs Between Ensembles and Weight Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22350)
*Fabian Morelli,Stephan Eckstein*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Partial fusion of neural networks interpolates between ensembles and weight aggregation by matching and aggregating similar neurons, enabling a tunable accuracy–cost tradeoff via partial optimal transport; it generalizes to pruning across ensembles and within a single network; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ensembles typically improve accuracy but are costly; weight-aggregation methods are cheaper but lose accuracy. A flexible framework is needed to balance performance and computational resources and to unify ensemble pruning approaches.

Method: 1) Extend weight-aggregation to operate at neuron level by identifying similarity across networks; 2) Use partial optimal transport to jointly identify and match similar neurons for aggregation (partial fusion); 3) Generalize the concept as pruning of ensemble models where neurons can be deleted or linearly combined; 4) Apply the generalized pruning idea to a single network to mimic partial fusion benefits.

Result: Shows that partial fusion provides a controllable tradeoff between accuracy and computation, with the ability to recover ensemble-like performance at higher cost and more compact models at lower cost. Generalized pruning yields similar benefits when applied to a single network. Code is released.

Conclusion: Partial fusion offers a practical, unified framework that bridges ensembling and weight-aggregation, providing a flexible approach to model compression and resource-constrained deployment.

Abstract: Ensembles of neural networks typically outperform individual networks but incur large computational costs, whereas weight aggregation produces less costly, yet also less accurate, aggregate models. We introduce partial fusion of networks, which interpolates between ensembles and weight aggregation and thus allows for a flexible tradeoff between computational cost and performance. A direct way to achieve this is to extend existing weight aggregation methods based on neuron-level similarity between different networks, where partial fusion then only aggregates weights of neurons which are most similar. We showcase one particular method to jointly identify which neurons are most similar and match them via partial optimal transport. Further, we consider the more general perspective of weight aggregation and partial fusion as generalized pruning of ensemble models, where neurons cannot just be deleted, but also linearly combined. Finally, we show that generalized pruning applied to a single network yields similar benefits as partial fusion by allowing for a tradeoff between isolating, deleting, and linearly combining neurons based on similarity. Our code is available at https://github.com/Fabian-Mor/partial_fusion_nn.

</details>


### [359] [VeriScale: Adversarial Test-Suite Scaling for Verifiable Code Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22368)
*Yifan Bai,Xiaoyang Liu,Zihao Mou,Guihong Wang,Jian Yu,Shuhan Xie,Yantao Li,Yangyu Zhang,Jingwei Liang,Tao Luo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VeriScale uses adversarial implementations to expand and prune test suites for LLM software engineering benchmarks, revealing weaknesses not exposed by prior benchmarks; instantiated as VerinaPlus (83x expansion) and VerinaLite (14x) with strong discriminative power across eight LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks suffer from limited positive/negative test cases and lack of verifiable evaluation, leading to inflated perceptions of LLM capabilities in generating specs and implementations.

Method: A two-stage framework: (1) test-suite expansion via adversarial implementations to create diverse, challenging cases; (2) test-suite reduction to produce compact, discriminative suites. Instantiated on Verina to create VerinaPlus (83x expansion) and VerinaLite (14x).

Result: VerinaPlus expands test suites by over 83x; VerinaLite by 14x. Across eight state-of-the-art LLMs, significant performance drops observed on SpecGen and CodeGen tasks when evaluated with the expanded/discounted benchmarks, exposing weaknesses hidden in the original benchmarks.

Conclusion: Public benchmarks and source code released at the project repository; VeriScale improves benchmarking for LLM-based software engineering by emphasizing verifiability and discriminative evaluation.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed for software engineering, constructing high-quality benchmarks is crucial for evaluating not just the functional correctness, but also the formal verifiability of generated code. However, existing benchmarks are limited by the quantity and quality of positive and negative test cases, leading to an overestimation of model capabilities in generating specifications and implementations. To address this, we propose VeriScale, a novel framework driven by the adversarial implementations. It consists of two stages: test-suite expansion to construct diverse and challenging test cases, and test-suite reduction to distill them into compact yet discriminative suites. While VeriScale is general, we instantiate it on Verina to construct VerinaPlus, which expands the original test suites by over 83$\times$, and VerinaLite, a lightweight 14$\times$ variant. Our experiments across eight state-of-the-art LLMs demonstrate that VerinaPlus exposes substantial model weaknesses hidden by the original benchmark, evidenced by sharp score drops on both SpecGen and CodeGen tasks, whereas VerinaLite maintains this discriminative power at a fraction of the evaluation cost. The enhanced benchmarks and source code are publicly available at https://github.com/XiaoyangLiu-sjtu/VeriScale.

</details>


### [360] [ASAP: Attention Sink Anchored Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22372)
*Jaehyuk Lee,Hanyoung Kim,Yanggee Kim,Donghun Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ASAP is a training-free token pruning framework for ViTs that repurposes the attention sink as a feature by modeling information flow as a Lazy Random Walk, using diffusion distance to a sink to cluster and prune tokens, achieving up to 48% throughput gains with comparable or better accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ViTs suffer quadratic self-attention complexity at high resolutions; existing token pruning relies on local cues and is vulnerable to the attention sink where salient tokens are preserved poorly. A training-free, globally informed pruning method is needed.

Method: Model ViT information flow as Lazy Random Walk; identify sink as dominant mass accumulator; compute diffusion distance to sink in cumulative transition matrix; perform Radial Diffusion Clustering to partition tokens; apply Transition Weight Pooling to compress background in a single shot.

Result: Empirical evaluation across image, video, and vision-language tasks shows ASAP outperforms state-of-the-art pruning methods; throughput speedups up to 48% with accuracy on par or better than baselines.

Conclusion: ASAP provides a training-free, diffusion-based token pruning mechanism that mitigates the attention sink and yields significant computational savings without sacrificing performance, with demonstrated cross-domain efficacy.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) face severe computational bottlenecks due to the quadratic complexity of self-attention at high resolutions. Existing token reduction methods rely on local metrics - such as single-layer attention scores - that are inherently vulnerable to the attention sink phenomenon, where uninformative tokens are paradoxically preserved over salient foreground objects. We propose ASAP (Attention Sink Anchored Pruning), a training-free framework that recasts this sink as a feature. Modeling ViT information flow as a Lazy Random Walk, ASAP identifies the sink as a dominant accumulator of probability mass. By computing the diffusion distance to the sink within the cumulative transition matrix, ASAP partitions tokens via Radial Diffusion Clustering and compresses background redundancy through Transition Weight Pooling in a single shot. Extensive experiments across image, video, and vision-language tasks demonstrate ASAP outperforms state-of-the-art methods, accelerating throughput by up to 48% while maintaining - or even exceeding - baseline accuracy.

</details>


### [361] [Boundary-targeted Membership Inference Attacks on Safety Classifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22373)
*Anthony Hughes,Alexander Goldberg,Prince Jha,Adam Perer,Nikolaos Aletras,Niloofar Mireshghallah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A privacy risk for safety classifiers: a boundary-targeted membership inference attack recovers a notable fraction of distress-related conversations with low false positives, outperforming existing MIA methods. Content filtering alone is ineffective; targeted noise can mitigate some risk, but protection remains imperfect.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Protect user privacy in safety classifiers trained on sensitive conversations. MIAs threaten membership privacy; identifying low-confidence, boundary examples may amplify leakage and undermine defenses.

Method: Propose a boundary-targeted selection strategy that picks low-confidence examples to amplify their membership signal. Evaluate MIAs on a safety classifier fine-tuned to detect users in distress. Compare against state-of-the-art MIAs. Analyze boundary-laying examples and test defense avenues, including content-based filtering and noise strategies.

Result: Adversaries can recover 19% of conversations flagged as indicating distress at a 5% false-positive rate (FPR), 3.5x higher than state-of-the-art MIA methods. Content-based filtering is ineffective for protection; some noise strategies can mitigate susceptibility for these boundary examples.

Conclusion: Boundary-focused leakage reveals a practical privacy vulnerability in safety classifiers. Defenses must go beyond content filtering; effective mitigation may require stronger privacy techniques (e.g., differential privacy, regularization) and targeted defense strategies against boundary examples.

Abstract: Safety classifiers are essential safeguards within generative AI systems, filtering harmful content or identifying at-risk users when interacting with large language models. Despite their necessity, these models are trained on sensitive datasets including discussions of self-harm and mental health, raising important, yet poorly understood, privacy concerns. Membership inference attacks (MIAs) allow adversaries to infer membership of examples used to train models. In this work, we hypothesize that identifying the examples on which the classifier is least confident are informative for an adversary to infer membership. This reflects a localized failure of generalization, where the model relies on memorization to resolve ambiguity in the training set. To investigate this, we introduce a new boundary-targeted selection strategy that identifies low confidence examples that amplify the signal of an examples membership within a training set. Our experimental results show that an adversary can recover 19\% of the conversations a safety classifier flagged as indicating user distress, at a 5\% false-positive rate, on a classifier fine-tuned for detecting a user who may require emotional support. This is $3.5$ times more than attacking using state-of-the-art MIA methods alone. Finally, we characterize the boundary laying examples and show that content-based filtering is ineffective for protection, and existing noise strategies can effectively mitigate susceptibility of these examples.

</details>


### [362] [Target-Aligned Bellman Backup for Cross-domain Offline Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22376)
*Wei Liu,Ting Long*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TABB: a target-aligned Bellman backup method for cross-domain offline RL that selects source-domain transitions by their contribution to accurate Bellman target estimation in the target domain, addressing limitations of transition-level similarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transition-level similarity can be misleading for long-term returns; policy learning relies on Bellman targets, so transfer should be judged by alignment with target Bellman backups rather than superficial similarity.

Method: Introduce Target-Aligned Bellman Backup (TABB) which evaluates and selects source-domain data based on their contribution to accurate Bellman target estimation in the target domain, thereby leveraging data more effectively for target policy learning.

Result: Empirical evaluation across diverse cross-domain offline RL settings with highly limited target data shows that TABB consistently achieves strong performance.

Conclusion: Aligning transferability with target Bellman backup accuracy improves cross-domain offline RL; TABB provides a robust data selection mechanism that enhances policy learning when target data is scarce.

Abstract: Cross-domain offline reinforcement learning (CDRL) aims to improve policy learning in a target domain by leveraging data collected from a source domain. Existing works typically assess the transferability of source-domain data by measuring its similarity to target-domain transitions, and implicitly perform transition-level selection. Transitions that are considered similar are assigned higher weights or rewards, while dissimilar ones are down-weighted. However, transition-level similarity does not necessarily imply consistency in long-term returns. Even visually or dynamically similar transitions may lead to significantly different outcomes in the target domain, which can mislead policy learning and degrade performance. To address this issue, we revisit the fundamental objective of policy learning. Since policy optimization ultimately relies on Bellman targets to evaluate the quality of decisions, we propose to assess the transferability of source-domain transitions based on their alignment with target-domain Bellman targets, rather than superficial transition similarity. Based on this insight, we propose a method termed Target-Aligned Bellman Backup (TABB), which selectively leverages source-domain data by measuring their contribution to accurate Bellman target estimation in the target domain. We evaluate TABB across a broad range of cross-domain offline RL settings with highly limited target-domain data. Experimental results show that TABB consistently achieves strong performance.

</details>


### [363] [Towards Explainability of SLMs by investigating Token Level Activation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22377)
*Sayantani Ghosh,Rajashik Datta,Amit Kumar Das,Amlan Chakrabarti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Activation Flow Network (AFN) identifies token-level semantic importance in BERT by measuring Layer 8 hidden-state magnitudes; provides a lightweight, model-agnostic alternative to attention-based interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address opacity of transformer models and the limitations of attention-centric explanations that emphasize structurally important but semantically weak tokens (e.g., punctuation), by quantifying token activation strengths and revealing semantic consolidation in Layer 8.

Method: Compute Token Activation Strength as the L2-norm of Layer-8 hidden representations; rank tokens by this strength; use an empirical upper-quartile activation boundary to form HIGH-activation and LOW-activation buckets; analyze which tokens populate each bucket across semantic vs structural categories.

Result: Semantically meaningful content words consistently fall into the HIGH-activation bucket and drive representational shifts; structurally supportive tokens contribute comparatively less; Layer 8 appears to serve as a semantic consolidation zone, offering an interpretable and efficient alternative to attention-centric analysis.

Conclusion: Activation magnitudes cluster around semantically informative tokens, enabling a transparent, glass-box view of BERT and supporting more efficient interpretability methods over traditional attention-based approaches.

Abstract: Transformer-based language models such as BERT having 110M+ parameters have revolutionized natural language understanding, yet their internal mechanisms remain largely opaque to researchers and practitioners. Traditional attention-based interpretability methods often emphasize structurally important but semantically weak tokens such as punctuation marks rather than meaningful semantic relationships. This work introduces a lightweight and model-agnostic framework for quantifying token-level representational importance using hidden-state activation strengths at Layer 8 of BERT. The proposed Activation Flow Network (AFN) framework computes Token Activation Strength using the L2 norm of Layer-8 hidden representations, enabling direct ranking of semantically salient tokens. The study further introduces a threshold-based activation bucket formulation that partitions tokens into HIGH-activation and LOW-activation groups using an empirical upper-quartile activation boundary. Experimental observations demonstrate that semantically meaningful content words consistently occupy the HIGH-activation bucket and dominate representational activation shifts, while structurally supportive tokens contribute comparatively less. The results suggest that Layer 8 acts as a critical semantic consolidation zone balancing structural and semantic information processing. By revealing how activation magnitudes concentrate around semantically informative tokens, this work provides an interpretable and computationally efficient alternative to attentioncentric analysis, contributing toward transforming BERT from a "black box" into a more transparent "glass box" model for natural language understanding.

</details>


### [364] [Efficient Higher-order Subgraph Attribution via Message Passing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22385)
*Ping Xiong,Thomas Schnake,Grégoire Montavon,Klaus-Robert Müller,Shinichi Nakajima*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes linear-time subgraph attribution for GNN-LRP by exploiting distributive properties in a message-passing framework, enabling scalable higher-order explanations; extends to neighbor-feature-aware subgraph attributions; validated by experiments showing speedups and usefulness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the exponential complexity of existing GNN-LRP higher-order explanations (_walk-based subgraph attributions) and make GNN interpretability scalable to larger networks and graphs.

Method: Develops linear-time algorithms for subgraph attribution in GNN-LRP using message passing and distributive properties to aggregate contributions over walks. Extends the framework to a generalized subgraph attribution that incorporates neighboring graph features.

Result: Significant computational acceleration of GNN-LRP-based attributions, demonstrating scalability and practical usefulness of the generalized subgraph attribution method on graphs with features.

Conclusion: The proposed linear-time, distributive-property-based approach enables practical, higher-order interpretability for GNNs via GNN-LRP and its generalized variant, broadening the applicability of subgraph attributions to larger graphs and richer feature contexts.

Abstract: Explaining graph neural networks (GNNs) has become more and more important recently. Higher-order interpretation schemes, such as GNN-LRP (layer-wise relevance propagation for GNN), emerged as powerful tools for unraveling how different features interact thereby contributing to explaining GNNs. GNN-LRP gives a relevance attribution of walks between nodes at each layer, and the subgraph attribution is expressed as a sum over exponentially many such walks. In this work, we demonstrate that such exponential complexity can be avoided. In particular, we propose novel algorithms that enable to attribute subgraphs with GNN-LRP in linear-time (w.r.t. the network depth). Our algorithms are derived via message passing techniques that make use of the distributive property, thereby directly computing quantities for higher-order explanations. We further adapt our efficient algorithms to compute a generalization of subgraph attributions that also takes into account the neighboring graph features. Experimental results show the significant acceleration of the proposed algorithms and demonstrate the high usefulness and scalability of our novel generalized subgraph attribution method.

</details>


### [365] [Hybrid Kolmogorov-Arnold Network and XGBoost Framework for Week-Ahead Price Forecasting in Australia's National Electricity Market](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22387)
*Houxuan Zhou,Sriram Prasad,Chenghao Huang,Jiajie Feng,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid KAN+XGBoost framework improves week-ahead electricity price forecasting in NEM by combining global nonlinear representation (KAN) with local robustness (XGBoost), outperforming standard baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Electricity price forecasting is highly challenging due to volatility, nonlinear dynamics, and extreme spikes, worsened by high renewable penetration in the NEM; accurate week-ahead forecasts are valuable for planning and risk management.

Method: Proposes a hybrid model that integrates Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN) with XGBoost. The KAN provides global nonlinear representation while XGBoost offers local robustness. Evaluated with expanding window on real NEM data.

Result: Outperforms SARIMAX, LSTM, standalone KAN, and XGBoost. MAE reduced by ~12% vs XGBoost and by >50% vs a naive baseline.

Conclusion: Hybrid learning strategies are effective for EPF in dynamic electricity markets; KAN+XGBoost is a promising approach for week-ahead forecasting in high-renewable contexts.

Abstract: Accurate electricity price forecasting (EPF) is essential for market participants to support operational planning and risk management, yet remains challenging due to strong volatility, nonlinear dynamics, and frequent extreme price spikes. These challenges are particularly pronounced in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM), where high renewable penetration further increases uncertainty. This paper investigates week-ahead electricity price forecasting and proposes a hybrid KAN+XGBoost framework that integrates Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN) with tree-based learning. The proposed approach combines the global nonlinear representation capability of KAN with the local robustness of XGBoost to capture both long-term dependencies and short-term price fluctuations. Experiments are conducted on real-world NEM data using an expanding window evaluation strategy. The results demonstrate that the proposed model outperforms benchmark methods, including SARIMAX, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), standalone KAN, and XGBoost, reducing MAE by approximately 12% compared to XGBoost and by over 50% compared to a naive baseline. The results suggest that hybrid learning strategies provide an effective and robust solution for electricity price forecasting in highly dynamic electricity markets.

</details>


### [366] [A Posterior-Predictive Variance Decomposition for Epistemic and Aleatoric Uncertainty in Wind Power Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22390)
*Yinsong Chen,Samson S. Yu,Kashem M. Muttaqi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A principled decomposition of total predictive uncertainty into aleatoric and epistemic components for wind power forecasting, using the law of total variance within a joint heteroscedastic NN regression and Bayesian posterior framework, with estimators compatible with standard posterior approximations and beta-NLL training; validated via a wind-specific evaluation protocol on synthetic and real data, showing consistent AU/EU behavior under noise, distribution shift, and data-size effects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable uncertainty quantification in wind power forecasts is hampered by conflating epistemic and aleatoric sources. Disentangling these components enables better calibration, risk assessment, and decision-making in wind energy operations.

Method: Derive an explicit AU/EU decomposition of total uncertainty (via the law of total variance) within a joint heteroscedastic neural network regression and Bayesian posterior approximation setting. Develop estimators compatible with standard posterior-approximation methods and with beta-NLL training to balance mean–variance learning. Propose a wind-specific evaluation framework with three modules: (i) controlled synthetic experiments to test responses to heteroscedastic noise and distribution shift; (ii) data-property–driven validation on a real wind turbine SCADA dataset; (iii) dataset-size scaling to study EU’s asymptotic behavior.

Result: The AU and EU estimators exhibit theoretically consistent responses to noise structure, distributional shift, and training-scale variation across synthetic and real data. The wind-focused evaluation protocol effectively validates disentanglement without ground-truth uncertainty labels, and EU shows expected scaling with dataset size, supporting both theoretical soundness and practical utility.

Conclusion: The proposed TU→AU/EU decomposition is theoretically well-founded and operationally valuable for wind power uncertainty quantification. The accompanying evaluation protocol provides a robust framework for validating epistemic and aleatoric contributions in real-world regression tasks.

Abstract: Accurate wind power forecasting requires reliable uncertainty quantification, yet most existing methods report a single predictive uncertainty that conflates epistemic and aleatoric sources. This paper applies the law of total variance to the joint setting of heteroscedastic neural network regression and Bayesian posterior approximation, deriving an explicit decomposition of total uncertainty (TU) into aleatoric (AU) and epistemic (EU) components. The resulting estimators are compatible with standard posterior-approximation methods and with $β$-NLL training to regulate the mean--variance learning trade-off. A wind power--specific evaluation framework is proposed to validate disentanglement without access to ground-truth uncertainty labels, comprising three modules: controlled synthetic experiments to verify responses to heteroscedastic noise and distribution shift; data-property--driven validation on a real-world wind turbine SCADA dataset; and dataset-size scaling experiments to examine the predicted asymptotic behavior of EU. Across synthetic and real-world experiments, the decomposed AU and EU components respond in theoretically consistent directions to noise structure, distributional shift, and training-scale variation, supporting the theoretical consistency and operational utility of the proposed decomposition and evaluation protocol.

</details>


### [367] [Cross-Species RSA Reveals Conserved Early Visual Alignment but Divergent Higher-Area Rankings Across Human fMRI and Macaque Electrophysiology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22401)
*Nils Leutenegger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cross-species test of learning-rule–to-brain alignment in CNNs using macaque electrophysiology. STDP and predictive coding give strongest V1/V2 alignment; pretrained networks outperform custom CNNs at IT; early visual alignment is robust across species, higher-area alignment limited by capacity and stimuli.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To generalize the relation between learning rules and brain alignment across species by evaluating five learning rules (BP, FA, PC, STDP, random) in macaque data and comparing to human data, thereby testing the robustness and limits of alignment in early versus higher visual areas.

Method: Replicate the human-study weights in five CNN variants corresponding to BP, FA, PC, STDP, and random initialization. Evaluate on two macaque electrophysiology datasets (MajajHong2015 for V4/IT; FreemanZiemba2013 for V1/V2) using representational similarity analysis (RSA). Compare alignment to macaque data versus human fMRI and include a pretrained ResNet-50 (ImageNet) as an additional benchmark. Report noise ceilings, multi-seed variability (5 seeds), and a stimulus-control analysis.

Result: STDP and PC yield the highest alignment in macaque V1/V2 (rho ~0.30 and 0.28). All models align better with macaque V1/V2 than with human fMRI (rho 0.15–0.30 vs 0.01–0.08). At IT, no cross-species ranking detectable (Kendall’s tau ≈ 0, p=1.0) due to limited power and stimulus differences. A pretrained ResNet-50 achieves rho 0.25 at IT, higher than custom CNNs (0.07–0.14), suggesting capacity/training data limits. Conclusion notes robustness of early visual alignment across species; higher areas depend on model capacity and stimulus domain. Noise ceilings and seed/stimulus-control analyses provided.

Conclusion: Early visual alignment with learning-rule CNNs generalizes across species; higher-area alignment is constrained by model capacity and stimulus domain rather than by the specific learning rule.

Abstract: Does the relationship between learning rules and brain alignment generalize across species? We extend our prior finding that untrained CNNs match backpropagation at human V1 by testing the same five learning rules against macaque electrophysiology. The rules are backpropagation (BP), feedback alignment (FA), predictive coding (PC), spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP), and an untrained random-weights baseline. The macaque data come from two datasets: MajajHong2015 (V4/IT, 3,200 stimulus presentations, 88/168 neurons) and FreemanZiemba2013 (V1/V2, 135 stimuli, 102/103 neurons). Using RSA with identical model weights from our human study, we find: (1) all models achieve higher alignment with macaque early visual cortex (rho = 0.15-0.30 at V1/V2) than with human fMRI (rho = 0.01-0.08), consistent with the higher signal-to-noise ratio of electrophysiology; (2) STDP and PC produce the highest macaque V1/V2 alignment (rho ~ 0.30 and 0.28), consistent with their leading position among trained rules in human V1; (3) at IT, learning rule rankings show no detectable correlation across species (Kendall's tau = 0.00, p = 1.00), though this null result is expected given that n = 5 provides power only at tau = +/-1.0, and is further confounded by stimulus set differences; (4) a pretrained ResNet-50 (ImageNet) achieves rho = 0.25 at macaque IT, substantially above all custom CNN conditions (rho = 0.07-0.14), suggesting IT alignment is limited by model capacity and training data rather than by the learning rule. Noise ceilings, multi-seed variability (5 seeds), and a stimulus-control analysis are reported. These results demonstrate that early visual alignment is robust across species, while higher-area alignment is modulated by model capacity and stimulus domain.

</details>


### [368] [AMUSE: Anytime Muon with Stable Gradient Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22432)
*Jueun Kim,Baekrok Shin,Jihun Yun,Beomhan Baek,Minhak Song,Chulhee Yun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AMUSE combines Muon-style momentum orthogonalization with Schedule-Free averaging to achieve schedule-free, anytime training by interpolating between fast, bulk progress and stable gradient averages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Muon's momentum orthogonalization accelerates progress along a fast bulk subspace but amplifies noise in dominant high-curvature directions, causing oscillations; Schedule-Free optimization avoids explicit learning-rate schedules via iterate averaging but lacks the stability of full averaging. There is a need for a method that preserves Muon's acceleration while mitigating valley-wall oscillations without schedules.

Method: Introduce AMUSE (Anytime MUon with Stable gradient Evaluation): use a time-varying interpolation between Muon's update sequence and a stable, averaged gradient sequence. Early iterations rely on fast Muon updates for rapid adaptation; gradually shift toward the averaged sequence to suppress oscillations while maintaining bulk progress. Analysis is framed via river-valley loss landscape to justify the interpolation strategy.

Result: Empirically, AMUSE achieves a better performance-iteration Pareto frontier than both Schedule-Free AdamW and Muon across multiple vision tasks and large language model pretraining, demonstrating effective schedule-free, anytime training.

Conclusion: AMUSE successfully unites rapid bulk optimization with stabilization via averaging, enabling schedule-free, anytime training with improved performance-efficiency across diverse tasks; the framework generalizes the benefits of Muon and Schedule-Free optimization.

Abstract: Modern deep learning commonly relies on AdamW with prescribed learning rate schedules, but recent works challenge both components: Schedule-Free optimization removes explicit schedules via iterate averaging, and Muon improves the update geometry by orthogonalizing momentum for matrix parameters. Despite Muon's strong empirical performance, its underlying mechanism remains partially understood. We study Muon through the river-valley loss landscape, where useful training progress occurs along a flat, low-curvature bulk subspace (the river), while high-curvature dominant directions form steep valley walls that induce oscillations. We empirically show that while Muon's orthogonalization accelerates river progress by increasing the bulk component, it also amplifies dominant-direction noise, causing oscillatory trajectories. Building on this, we propose Anytime MUon with Stable gradient Evaluation (AMUSE), which integrates Muon's rapid bulk progress with the stabilizing effect of Schedule-Free averaging. AMUSE uses a time-varying interpolation coefficient that initially evaluates gradients near the fast Muon sequence for rapid adaptation, then gradually shifts toward the stable averaged sequence to suppress valley-wall oscillations. As a result, AMUSE requires no learning rate schedules and supports anytime training. Across vision tasks and large language model pretraining, AMUSE consistently improves the performance-iteration Pareto frontier over (Schedule-Free) AdamW and Muon.

</details>


### [369] [Don't Forget the Critic: Value-Based Data Rehearsal for Multi-Cyclic Continual Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22454)
*Benjamin Poole,Andrew Quinn,Li Yang,Minwoo Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Qreg+NWLU extends data rehearsal to value-based continual RL by applying continuous Q-value rehearsal and immediate (No-Wait) regularization in Deep Q-Networks, improving learning efficiency, forgetting mitigation, and knowledge transfer in multi-cyclic task sequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses a gap in CRL: most prior work focuses on actor regularization in policy-gradient methods, neglecting critic/value-function regularization. Multi-cycle (repeating) task sequences are common in real-world settings and exacerbate forgetting and plasticity. The paper targets robust value-function learning in CRL.

Method: Propose Qreg+NWLU for DQN with two modifications: (1) continuous data rehearsal that dynamically collects and updates stored Q-values during training, (2) No-Wait (NW) regularization that applies the regularization immediately rather than after the first task. They compare against Qreg and conventional CRL baselines in a value-function approximation setting.

Result: The proposed approach yields improvements in learning efficiency, forgetting mitigation, and knowledge transfer over Qreg and conventional CRL methods within the value-function approximation context.

Conclusion: Continuous data rehearsal combined with immediate regularization enhances stability-plasticity in value-based CRL, addressing both forgetting and transfer in multi-cycle environments; promising for broader adoption in value-function approximations beyond policy-gradient CRL.

Abstract: Data rehearsal has emerged as a leading approach for mitigating catastrophic forgetting in Continual Reinforcement Learning (CRL). However, existing work remains confined to policy gradient frameworks, regularizing only actors due to the performance degradation incurred by critic regularization. This actor-centric approach overlooks the potential of data rehearsal for value function approximation. Moreover, existing evaluations in CRL rarely consider multi-cyclic environments where task sequences repeat, a critical real-world scenario that exacerbates forgetting and plasticity. We investigate data rehearsal for Deep Q-Networks using Q-value regularization in multi-cyclic settings and propose Qreg+NWLU which introduces two simple modifications: (1) continuous data rehearsal that dynamically collects and updates stored Q-values throughout training, and (2) "No-Wait" regularization that applies immediately rather than after the first task. Together, these modifications yield improvements in learning efficiency, forgetting mitigation, and knowledge transfer over Qreg and conventional CRL methods within value function approximation settings.

</details>


### [370] [Lost in Tokenization: Fundamental Trade-offs in Graph Tokenization for Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22471)
*Maya Bechler-Speicher,Gilad Yehudai,Gil Harari,Clayton Sanford,Amir Globerson,Joan Bruna*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Graph transformers' expressivity hinges on how graphs are tokenized. Three building-block tokenizations—spectral, random-walk, and adjacency—impose distinct depth regimes, with random-walk being lossy and spectral being ill-conditioned for local tasks. Limited-depth transformers cannot generally convert between tokenization families. Theoretical lower bounds coupled with experiments show task-specific tokenizations are preferred, and combining tokenizations can leverage complementary signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether and how the choice of graph-to-token mapping affects the representational power and practical performance of transformer models on graph-structured data, and to identify fundamental limits of single-tokenization approaches.

Method: Theoretical analysis of three tokenizations (spectral, random-walk, adjacency) to establish depth regimes, lossiness, losslessness, ill-conditioning, and impossibility of inter-tokenization conversion under finite depth; derivation of lower bounds; controlled experiments on synthetic and real-world tasks to validate predicted separations and explore benefits of combining tokenizations.

Result: - Random-walk tokenization is lossy for any walk length, making exact graph recovery impossible in general. - Spectral tokenization is lossless but ill-conditioned for local tasks. - Limited-depth transformers cannot, in general, convert between tokenization families. - Lower bounds and impossibility results show unfavorable tokenizations can hinder recovery of better structural representations. - Experiments on synthetic and real-world data validate the theoretical separations and show that different tasks prefer different tokenizations; combining tokenizations yields complementary signals that transformers can exploit.

Conclusion: Tokenization choices are a fundamental determinant of graph transformer expressivity. Different tasks benefit from different structural representations; a multi-tokenization approach can harness complementary information and mitigate the limitations of any single tokenization.

Abstract: Transformers have become a central architecture for graph learning, but their application to graphs requires first choosing a tokenization: a graph-to-token map that determines which structural information is exposed at the input. In this work, we show that this choice is a fundamental component of transformer expressivity. We examine three tokenizations that serve as building blocks for many existing graph tokenizations: spectral, random-walk, and adjacency tokenizations. We prove that different tokenizations induce distinct depth regimes: the same graph computation may be realizable by a shallow transformer under one tokenization, while requiring substantially larger depth under another. For example, we prove that random-walk tokenization is lossy for any walk length, making it impossible in general to recover the graph from it, and that while spectral tokenization is lossless, it is ill-conditioned for local tasks. We further show that although both random-walk and spectral tokenizations are derived from adjacency information, it is impossible for a limited-depth transformer to convert between tokenization families in general. In particular, we establish lower bounds and impossibility results showing that unfavorable tokenizations may preclude the efficient recovery of more suitable structural representations. Finally, we complement our theory with controlled experiments on synthetic and real-world tasks, validating the predicted separations and showing that different tasks favor different structural views, and combining complementary tokenizations allows the transformer to leverage distinct signals from each representation.

</details>


### [371] [Winner-Take-All bottlenecks enforce disentangled symbolic representations in multi-task learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22472)
*Julian Gutheil,Simon Hitzginger,Robert Legenstein*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A WTA bottleneck in deep nets can enforce extraction of categorical latent factors under certain conditions, yielding highly symbolic representations where individual neurons (or groups) encode single abstract features; empirically observed to improve generalization even when assumptions are not fully met, suggesting a link between symbolic and subsymbolic AI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how winner-take-all (WTA) activations influence latent-factor extraction in complex, nonlinear factor models and whether they foster symbolic-like representations that aid generalization and multi-task learning.

Method: Provide a theoretical proof of conditions under which a WTA bottleneck induces categorical latent-factor extraction; demonstrate empirically on two datasets that the emergent representation is symbolic and robust to deviations from the theoretical assumptions; evaluate generalization benefits and applicability to architectures not fully aligned with the theorem.

Result: The WTA bottleneck yields highly symbolic representations, with individual neurons or neuron populations encoding the presence of a single abstract feature (e.g., object, color, position); empirical results show improved generalization and that symbolic encoding persists even when model assumptions are relaxed.

Conclusion: WTA components can enhance generalization and induce interpretable, symbolic latent-factor representations in deep networks, offering a possible interface between symbolic and subsymbolic AI and informing multi-task learning designs.

Abstract: Winner-take-all (WTA) networks constitute a central circuit motif in cortical networks of the brain. In addition, WTA-like activations are abundant in modern deep learning models in the form of the softmax activation for example in attention layers of transformers. While their role in the extraction of latent factors has been studied for relatively simple generative models, their role in the context of highly non-linearly entangled latent factors has remained elusive. In this article, we show that a WTA bottleneck within a deep neural network can enforce under certain well-defined conditions the extraction of categorical latent factors of the data in a multi-task learning setup. In particular, we prove that the representation that emerges in the WTA bottleneck is highly symbolic, where a single neuron or a population of neurons encodes the presence of a single abstract feature such as a specific object, color, or position. We furthermore show empirically on two datasets, that this also holds for architectures and setups that do not fully comply with the assumptions of our theorem and demonstrate the advantages of the acquired symbolic representation for generalization. Our proposed model provides insights into the generalization capabilities of deep neural networks with WTA-like components and may serve as an interface between symbolic and subsymbolic AI systems.

</details>


### [372] [Structured-Sparse Attention for Entity Tracking with Subquadratic Sequence Complexity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22476)
*Hangyue Zhao,Paul Caillon,Erwan Fagnou,Alexandre Allauzen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Blockwise, subquadratic resolvent-style operator for entity tracking that preserves exact within-block interactions and routes cross-block coupling via a reduced system; achieves O(n^{4/3} d) complexity (or O(n^{7/3}) when d ~ n) with accuracy matching dense operators and 12–29% wall-clock speedups versus a dense Transformer; limited when the number of concurrently evolving properties exceeds attention heads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense attention in long sequences is computationally expensive for entity tracking. The paper seeks to exploit empirical structure in learned attention to yield a more efficient, scalable evaluation without sacrificing accuracy.

Method: Identify that attention mass concentrates in local block-diagonal neighborhoods with a light cross-block residue. Develop a blockwise evaluation of a resolvent-style operator that keeps within-block interactions exact and uses a reduced system to model cross-block interactions, producing subquadratic complexity. Perform ablations on block size and model capacity and assess limitations.

Result: On controlled tracking benchmarks, the method matches the dense operator's accuracy while reducing wall-clock time by 12–29% and achieving up to 2.4× speedups versus a compact dense Transformer at comparable exact-match accuracy.

Conclusion: Block-structured attention and resolvent-style blockwise evaluation enable efficient long-sequence entity tracking with subquadratic complexity. The approach has practical speedups but may fail when the number of simultaneously evolving properties exceeds the number of attention heads.

Abstract: Entity tracking requires maintaining and updating latent states for entities and attributes over long sequences. Recent task-specific attention operators can compress deep Transformer stacks into a few layers by performing multi-hop state propagation within a single layer, but their dense evaluation remains expensive. We show that in this setting, learned attention is strongly structured: most mass concentrates in local block-diagonal neighborhoods with a light cross-block residue. Exploiting this, we derive a blockwise evaluation of a resolvent-style operator that keeps within-block interactions exact and routes cross-block interactions through a reduced system. The resulting evaluation is subquadratic in sequence length $O(n^{4/3}d)$ (and $O(n^{7/3})$ when $d\approx n$). On controlled tracking benchmarks, our method matches the dense operator's accuracy while reducing wall-clock time by $12-29\%$ under a standardized measurement protocol, and is up to $2.4 \times$ faster than a compact dense Transformer at comparable exact-match accuracy. We further provide ablations over block size and model capacity, and identify a limitation: performance collapses when the number of simultaneously evolving properties exceeds the number of attention heads.

</details>


### [373] [Implicit Regularization of Mini-Batch Training in Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22480)
*Clement Wang,Antoine Vialle,Robin Vaysse,Thomas Bonald*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Random Node Sampling (RNS) is an effective, theory-grounded approach for scalable GNN training, matching or surpassing full-graph training on most datasets and offering strong implicit regularization via gradient variance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand why a simple, non-structure-preserving sampler can outperform or match sophisticated structure-aware samplers in GNN training; characterize the implicit bias induced by mini-batch SGD on graphs.

Method: Theoretically analyze SGD on graph mini-batches via backward error analysis to show a regularizer term proportional to mini-batch gradient variance; empirically compare RNS with full-graph training and structure-aware samplers across 8–10 datasets, measuring loss alignment and gradient variance.

Result: RNS yields comparable or better accuracy than full-graph training on 8 of 10 datasets, with reduced wall-clock time and memory usage; the expected loss under RNS is closer to the full-graph loss, and per-batch gradient variance is lower, due to implicit regularization.

Conclusion: Sampling in graph mini-batch SGD acts as implicit regularization; RNS is a strong, theoretically grounded, scalable choice for GNN training.

Abstract: Mini-batch training of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) is fundamentally different from training on i.i.d. data: sampling a subgraph alters the topology and introduces boundary effects, leading prior work to develop structure-aware samplers that preserve local connectivity and reduce embedding variance. Surprisingly, we demonstrate that the simplest possible scheme, Random Node Sampling (RNS), training on the induced subgraph of uniformly sampled nodes, matches or outperforms full-graph training on 8 of 10 datasets at a fraction of the wall-clock time and memory. To explain this, we apply backward error analysis to graph mini-batch Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) and show that it implicitly minimizes the sampled loss plus a regularizer proportional to the mini-batch gradient variance, a quantity directly shaped by the sampler. Although RNS discards local structure, it produces mini-batches whose expected loss is closer to the full-graph loss, and whose per-batch gradients have lower variance, yielding a better implicit objective. Our analysis reframes the choice of graph sampler as a form of implicit regularization, and identifies RNS as a strong, theoretically grounded method for scalable GNN training.

</details>


### [374] [When Stronger Triggers Backfire: A High-Dimensional Theory of Backdoor Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22481)
*Donald Flynn,Hadas Yaron Goldhirsh,Jonathan P. Keating,Inbar Seroussi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: In high-dimensional GLMs on Gaussian mixtures, stronger training triggers can raise clean accuracy while backdoor success peaks at finite trigger strength; the most damaging trigger aligns with the data covariance's minimum eigenvector; results are derived in closed form for squared loss and extended to convex losses, with a finite-sample noise floor driving (i). Empirical results on CIFAR-10 and networks corroborate the theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explain and quantify the counterintuitive, non-monotonic behavior of backdoor poisoning attacks in high-dimensional settings and to identify the role of data geometry and finite-sample noise in shaping defender-attacker tradeoffs.

Method: Theoretically analyze regularised generalized linear models on Gaussian-mixture data in the proportional regime (p/n → κ). Derive closed-form results for squared loss; extend to general convex GLM losses via a Gaussian-proxy fixed-point system. Identify a finite-sample noise floor proportional to κ. Validate with CIFAR-10 experiments on Gaussian surrogates and ResNet-18.

Result: Three phenomena are established: (i) clean test accuracy increases with training trigger strength α; (ii) attack success peaks at a finite α and then declines; (iii) the most damaging trigger direction is the minimum eigenvector of the data covariance. These are proven in closed form for squared loss; extended to general convex losses; empirical results closely match theory.

Conclusion: High-dimensional effects and data covariance geometry fundamentally shape backdoor vulnerability. A finite-sample noise floor explains the nonmonotonic defense-attack dynamics, and the minimum-covariance-eigenvector is a worst-case trigger direction. The results generalize beyond convex settings and have practical implications for designing robust defenses.

Abstract: Backdoor poisoning attacks behave counter-intuitively in high dimensions: stronger training triggers can help the defender. We study regularised generalised linear models on Gaussian-mixture data in the proportional regime ($p/n \to κ$), varying the training trigger strength $α$ against a fixed test trigger. Three phenomena emerge: (i) clean test accuracy increases with $α$; (ii) attack success peaks at a finite $α$ and then declines; and (iii) the most damaging trigger direction is the minimum eigenvector of the data covariance. We prove all three results in closed form for the squared loss, and extend (i) and (ii) to general convex GLM losses via a Gaussian-proxy fixed-point system. We identify a finite-sample noise floor proportional to $κ$ as the mechanism behind (i), invisible to classical $n \gg p$ analysis. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and Gaussian surrogates match the theory closely; ResNet-18 experiments show the same phenomena beyond the convex setting.

</details>


### [375] [Represented Is Not Computed: A Causal Test of Candidate Algorithmic Intermediates in a Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22488)
*Ishita Darade,Sushrut Thorat*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformer learned to compute the base-B digit-extraction task exactly, with evidence of intermediate representations and partial causal separation between representation and use; probes suggest staged computation, but causal routing contradicts this, highlighting divergence between probe interpretations and actual causal dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how a network implements structured instruction-like tasks and whether intermediate computations align with a clean, closed-form solution. Arithmetic offers precise algorithmic targets; this enables probing and causal interventions to reveal internal representations and routing strategies, and to test whether probes reflect causal use.

Method: Train a Transformer on a base-digit extraction task: given N, B, D, output floor(N/B^D) mod B (i.e., the D-th digit in base B). Use three random seeds to assess reliability. Evaluate exact accuracy on held-out number-base intersections. Use linear probes to decode intermediate quantities corresponding to the closed-form intermediates. Perform causal tests to assess whether representation passes to the output stream via a localized route dependent on D-related communication. Conduct sparse circuit searches to identify routing of N, B, D information, checking whether routes are separate and whether they operate late vs. staged. 

Result: The model achieves 99.83% exact-answer accuracy on held-out number-base intersections. Linear probes successfully decode the arithmetic intermediates, supporting a plausible staged computation. Causal tests show that, within the localized route from the D-input stream to the output positions, behavior depends on early D-selective communication, independent of N and B. Sparse circuit search reveals mostly separate N, B, and D routes that combine late rather than via the staged route suggested by the probes. This indicates the model does form the intermediates needed for the closed-form solution, but the localized causal route does not transmit them to the output. 

Conclusion: Probe-based interpretations can diverge from causal observations even when explicit algorithmic hypotheses are available. Care is needed when linking decoded intermediates to causal pathways to outputs.

Abstract: Structured prompts require integrating components according to task-relevant relations. How a network implements this integration is often hard to judge in language or vision, where those relations are rarely specified precisely enough to define a candidate internal algorithm. Arithmetic offers a cleaner setting. We study a Transformer trained on base-digit extraction: given $N$, $B$, and $D$, it must report the coefficient of $B^D$ in the base-$B$ expansion of $N$. The closed-form solution, $\lfloor N/B^D \rfloor \bmod B$, provides explicit candidate algorithmic intermediates. Across three seeds, the model reaches 99.83% exact-answer accuracy on held-out number-base intersections, establishing reliable task competence. Linear probes decode the intermediates, making staged arithmetic computation plausible. Causal tests then separate representation from use: within the localized route from the stream with $D$ as input to the output positions, behavior depends on early $D$-selective communication, independent of $N$ and $B$. Relatedly, a sparse circuit search finds mostly separate $N$, $B$, and $D$ routes that combine late rather than the staged route suggested by the probes. Thus, the model represents the intermediates that make the closed-form solution plausible, but the identified localized causal route does not transmit them to the output stream. This case shows that probe-based conclusions can diverge sharply from causal observations, even when explicit algorithmic hypotheses are available.

</details>


### [376] [The Signal in the Noise: OOD Detection Through Goodness-of-Fit Testing in Factorised Latent Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22496)
*Philipp Bomatter,Jack Geary,Henry Gouk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Signal in the Noise (SITN), a single-sample out-of-distribution detector based on continuous normalising flows (CNFs) that maps OOD inputs to atypical noise under a noise prior, avoids requiring OOD data, reduces complexity bias of likelihood-based methods, and offers strict false-positive control with low overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Likelihoods produced by deep generative models are unreliable for distinguishing in-distribution from out-of-distribution data. By exploiting the diffeomorphic and mass-preserving properties of CNFs, inputs can be analyzed to reveal atypical mappings that likelihood alone fails to capture, enabling robust single-sample OOD detection.

Method: Build on continuous normalising flows that are diffeomorphic and mass-preserving to characterize how OOD samples map to noise-like representations. Propose SITN as a single-sample OOD detector that operates without access to OOD data and with minimal computational overhead, enabling strict control of false-positive rates.

Result: Comprehensive evaluations on standard benchmarks and synthetic perturbations demonstrate SITN’s effectiveness and show an absence of the complexity bias commonly associated with likelihood-based methods.

Conclusion: SITN provides a practical, single-sample OOD detection approach that avoids reliance on likelihoods, requires no OOD samples, and offers controlled false-positive rates while maintaining low overhead.

Abstract: Deep generative models offer a natural foundation for out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, yet prior work has shown that their assigned likelihoods are notoriously unreliable indicators for in- vs out-of-distribution data. In this paper, we address this problem by leveraging the diffeomorphic and mass-preserving properties of continuous normalising flows. Our analysis shows that OOD samples are mapped to noise samples that are highly atypical under the noise prior in ways not captured by the likelihood. Based on this observation, we propose a new method -- Signal in the Noise (SITN) -- for OOD detection on the single-sample level. SITN requires no access to OOD data, incurs minimal computational overhead, and provides strict control of false positive rates. Comprehensive evaluations through standard benchmarks and synthetic perturbations highlight the method's effectiveness and the absence of the complexity bias inherent to likelihood-based methods.

</details>


### [377] [The Neural Compiler: Program-to-Network Translation for Hybrid Scientific Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22498)
*Lucas Sheneman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Translates symbolic physics programs into differentiable PyTorch modules; exact physics encoding with learned corrections; strong composability and data efficiency, outperforming large PINN baselines in several domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In scientific ML, integrate known physics with data-driven corrections without hand-coding equations or using soft penalties.

Method: A compiler translates a Scheme-like expression language into frozen, differentiable PyTorch modules that match the source program to floating-point precision and provide autograd gradients. It supports 51 primitives (vector/matrix algebra) and enables PDE discretizations and hybrid models. The system emphasizes composability and automatic generation of modules from symbolic specs.

Result: Compiled modules numerically match hand-coded PyTorch for single equations. With 1–4 trainable parameters, models recover physical constants to <1% error in most cases, while PINN baselines with >8,500 parameters show 7–93% error. Compiled modules compose with zero error; they generalize composition cleanly. Supports six experiment domains including PDEs and dynamics.

Conclusion: The primary value is systematic composability: automatically generate correct, differentiable modules from symbolic specs without rewriting each equation by hand. This provides a natural target for LLMs to translate scientific descriptions into executable modules.

Abstract: Scientific machine learning often requires combining known physics with unknown parameters or correction terms learned from data. Existing approaches either ignore known structure, encode it as a soft penalty, or require hand-written PyTorch code for each equation. We present The Neural Compiler, a system that translates programs written in a first-order Scheme-like expression language into frozen, differentiable PyTorch modules. These modules match the source program to floating-point precision and provide gradients through autograd. In hybrid models, the compiled module encodes known physics exactly while learned components model the unknown remainder. We evaluate the compiler across six experiment domains: Feynman physics equations, Lotka-Volterra dynamics, a damped pendulum, a one-dimensional heat equation, three-dimensional vector mechanics, and compositional generalization. Compiled modules match hand-coded PyTorch implementations numerically for single equations, showing no accuracy loss from compilation. With only 1 to 4 trainable parameters, compiled models recover physical constants to less than 1 percent error in most cases, while standard PINN baselines with more than 8500 parameters show 7 to 93 percent error. Compiled modules also compose with zero error, while neural approximations can accumulate large errors in deep composition chains. The main value of the compiler is not improved accuracy over hand-coded equations, but systematic composability: it generates correct, differentiable modules from symbolic specifications without rewriting each equation by hand. The system supports 51 primitive operations, including vector and matrix algebra, enabling PDE discretizations and hybrid scientific models. This string-in, module-out interface also provides a natural target for large language models that translate scientific descriptions into executable differentiable modules.

</details>


### [378] [Generative Modeling by Value-Driven Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22507)
*Pablo Moreno-Muñoz,Adrian Müller,Gergely Neu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A discrete-time stochastic-control framework for measure transport yields a linear-programming dual that encodes the optimal value function. A simulation-free primal-dual algorithm computes approximate value functions and derives value-driven transport (VDT) policies, which produce straight transport paths, fast simulation, and robust performance with easy extensions (conditioning, guidance, unpaired translation). Experimental results indicate strong performance and scalability potential.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Develop a scalable, efficient generative-modeling framework based on measure transport that overcomes limitations of flow-, diffusion-, and Schrödinger-bridge-based methods by optimizing transport policies via an LP dual and a fast primal-dual algorithm.

Method: Formulate the transport problem as a discrete-time stochastic control problem, recast as a linear program whose dual variables are the optimal value function; develop a simulation-free primal-dual algorithm to compute approximate value functions and the corresponding value-driven transport policies (VDT).

Result: VDT policies yield straight transport paths, enable fast and robust simulation, and support enhancements analogous to diffusion/flow models (e.g., conditional generation, classifier-free guidance, unpaired data-to-data translation). Empirical results show strong performance and potential for scalability.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a principled, scalable approach to generative modeling via measure transport, delivering practical advantages and flexible extensions compared with existing flow/diffusion/Schrödinger-bridge methods.

Abstract: We propose a new framework for generative modeling based on a discrete-time stochastic control formulation of measure transport. Adapting classic results from control theory, we formulate our problem as a linear program whose dual variables correspond to the \emph{optimal value function} of the control problem, which directly encodes the optimal control policy. Exploiting this LP formulation, we develop an efficient simulation-free primal-dual algorithm for computing approximately optimal value functions and the associated \emph{value-driven transport} (VDT) policies which approximate the true optimal policy. We show that well-trained VDT policies enjoy numerous favorable properties in comparison with other state-of-the-art methods based on flows, diffusions, or Schrödinger bridges: they lead to straight transport paths which can be simulated quickly and robustly, and can be enhanced in all the same ways as diffusion and flow-based models (e.g., conditional generation, classifier-free guidance, unpaired data-to-data translation are all easy to incorporate). We evaluate our methodology in a range of experiments, with results that indicate strong performance and good potential for scalability.

</details>


### [379] [Stabilising Explainability Fragility in Cybersecurity AI: The Impact and Mitigation of Multicollinearity in Public Benchmark Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22529)
*Ioannis J. Vourganas,Anna Lito Michala*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Formalizes and demonstrates that multicollinearity inflates attribution variance in IDS explainability, introduces Explanability Fragility Score, and offers two mitigation methods (CAA-Filtering and SHARP) with empirical validation on UNSW-NB15 and four model families.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explainability robustness under correlated features is critical for trust and reproducibility in security-critical intrusion detection systems; existing post-hoc tools (SHAP/LIME) do not account for multicollinearity, potentially yielding unstable explanations.

Method: The paper presents a formal theorem showing attribution variance inflation due to multicollinearity. It validates this theorem experimentally on the UNSW-NB15 dataset across four model families (linear, tree-based, kernel, neural) using full and pruned feature sets selected by VIF and correlation thresholds. It introduces Explanability Fragility Score and proposes two mitigation methods: CAA-Filtering (groups attributions to stabilize explanations) and SHARP (training-time regularisation penalising attribution instability). Stability is quantified with Kendall's tau across bootstrapped explanations, and predictive performance is evaluated to ensure no loss.

Result: Theoretic claim is supported by experiments: multicollinearity indeed destabilizes explanations and makes attributions non-identifiable. The Explanability Fragility Score correlates with instability, and the proposed mitigations (CAA-Filtering and SHARP) stabilize explanations while maintaining performance, with monotonic improvement possible as integration complexity varies.

Conclusion: The work enhances trustworthiness and reproducibility of XAI in security-critical settings, advocating the inclusion of multicollinearity mitigation in IDS pipelines and providing practical guidelines and two concrete mitigation strategies for practitioners.

Abstract: This paper investigates a unexplored yet impactful vulnerability in AI explainability used in intrusion detection (IDS): multicollinearity-induced instability. Despite extensive reliance on post-hoc explainability tools such as SHAP or LIME, the impact of correlated features on explanation robustness is not evaluated. We introduce a formal theorem stating that multicollinearity inflates attribution variance. This demonstrates that explanations and feature importances are non-identifiable under multicollinearity. A suite of comprehensive experiments validates the theorem on a representative benchmark dataset, UNSW-NB15. Four widely used families of models are evaluated, including linear, tree-based, kernel, and neural, across full and pruned feature sets based on VIF and correlation thresholding. We propose the novel metric of Explanability Fragility Score and two novel methods to mitigate it with variable integration complexity. CAA-Filtering focuses on stabilising explanations by grouping attributions of trained models.
