SHARP is a novel training-time regularisation framework that penalises attribution instability, enabling controllable and monotonic improvement of explainability stability. The findings support stable predictive performance, using Kendall's τ to quantify instability across bootstrapped explanations. This work has direct implications for the trustworthiness and reproducibility of XAI in security-critical contexts, and motivates incorporating multicollinearity mitigations into the IDS pipelines, providing a set of guidelines for practitioners.

</details>


### [380] [Disentanglement Beyond Generative Models with Riemannian ICA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22531)
*Edmond Cunningham*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Riemannian ICA (RICA) as a local geometric alternative to global ICA, introducing a pointwise (second-order) disentanglement tensor built from the Hessian of the data log-likelihood and the model’s Ricci curvature; shows source recovery across manifolds and highlights limitations of ICA baselines depending on coordinate choice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridging the gap between classical ICA-based disentanglement (global generative models) and modern representation learning, by providing a theory of disentanglement that does not rely on a global generative assumption.

Method: Adopts Riemannian geometry to replace a global generative model with local geometric structure. Introduces radial curves from a data point that map to axis-aligned latent representations, and defines a disentanglement tensor based on the Hessian of the log-likelihood and Ricci curvature. Demonstrates source recovery in controlled experiments on multiple manifolds.

Result: In controlled, ground-truth source recovery, RICA recovers sources across several manifolds, whereas ICA baselines’ success depends on the chosen coordinate representation of observations.

Conclusion: Provides a theoretical basis for local, pointwise disentanglement without requiring a global generative model, thereby extending disentanglement theory beyond traditional ICA frameworks.

Abstract: There is a gap between the theoretical foundations of disentanglement and the practice of modern representation learning. Existing theoretical frameworks, particularly Independent Component Analysis (ICA) and its nonlinear variants, assume a generative model with statistically independent latent variables underlying the data so that disentanglement amounts to identifying the latents that could have generated the data. This generative framework is interpretable and theoretically justified, but its strong assumptions make it difficult to apply to modern representation learning. Modern pretrained encoders often learn features that exhibit disentangled properties without making generative assumptions, yet there is no general theory for interpreting these features as independent factors of variation. We take a step toward such a theory by introducing Riemannian ICA (RICA), which replaces ICA's global generative model with local geometric structure. RICA is founded on the observation that in ICA, the factors of variation underlying a data point can be understood through radial curves emanating from the point that map to axis-aligned lines in the latent space. We formalize this perspective using Riemannian geometry and introduce our theory in a way that is consistent with the existing generative approach. Our main contribution is the disentanglement tensor, which encodes a second-order notion of disentanglement that we call pointwise disentanglement. This tensor depends on the Hessian of the data log likelihood as well as the Ricci curvature induced by the model. In a controlled source recovery setting with known ground-truth sources, RICA recovers sources across several manifolds, while the success of ICA baselines depends on the coordinates used to represent the observations. Our work provides a theoretical basis for studying local disentanglement without assuming a global generative model.

</details>


### [381] [Relational Linear Properties in Language Models: An Empirical Investigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22532)
*Giovanni Valer,Luigi Gresele,Marco Bronzini,Emanuele Marconato*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a KL-divergence–based probe for relational linearity in language models, testing whether, for a fixed relation, the unembedding of an object can be predicted from its subject via a linear map. Finds model- and layer-dependent variation, layer-wise patterns aligned with known linguistic information, and sensitivity to how the relation is phrased; more efficient than prior Jacobian-based methods across four datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess the validity and practicality of the relational linearity hypothesis (Marconato et al., 2025) in LM representations, and develop a scalable, efficient probing method to evaluate it across layers and paraphrased queries.

Method: Introduce a probing method based on Kullback-Leibler divergence to quantify relational linearity. Evaluate how well a linear map from subject embeddings predicts unembeddings of objects across layers and paraphrased relation phrases. Compare efficiency and results to the Jacobian-approximation approach used in Linear Relational Embeddings (Hernandez et al., 2024). Conduct experiments on four datasets.

Result: Relational linearity varies across models and follows layer-wise patterns consistent with known linguistic information in representations. It is differently affected by changes in how the relation is phrased, and the proposed method is more efficient than prior Jacobian-based approaches.

Conclusion: The relational linearity phenomenon is model- and layer-dependent, with reliable yet nuanced behavior across layers. The KL-divergence probing method provides an efficient, scalable tool for evaluating relational linearity and reveals sensitivity to relation phrasing, suggesting avenues for deeper interpretability studies and robust cross-model comparisons.

Abstract: Linear properties are ubiquitous in the representations of language models; however, testing them experimentally remains a challenging task. This work focuses on relational linearity: the hypothesis that, for a fixed relation (e.g., "plays"), the unembedding of an object (e.g., "trumpet") can be predicted from the embedding of its subject (e.g.,"Miles Davis") by a linear map. We present an experimental method to test the formulation of relational linearity by Marconato et al. (2025). Specifically, we introduce a probing method, based on Kullback-Leibler divergence, to evaluate this property and examine its variation across layers and paraphrased relational queries. It is also more efficient than previous work; for example, it avoids the crude Jacobian approximations used in Linear Relational Embeddings by Hernandez et al. (2024). Our findings across four datasets show that relational linearity varies across models, exhibits layer-wise patterns consistent with prior observations about linguistic information in model representations, and is differently affected by changes in how the relation is phrased.

</details>


### [382] [F-TIS: Harnessing Diverse Models in Collaborative GRPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22537)
*Nikolay Blagoev,Oğuzhan Ersoy,Wendelin Boehmer,Lydia Yiyu Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Filtered Truncated Importance Sampling (F-TIS) for GRPO-like RL with heterogeneous, decentralized LLM models. Demonstrates that off-policy samples from diverse models can be leveraged without harming convergence, and may improve out-of-distribution generalization (up to 12%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address practical decentralization where collaborating models differ in compute and architecture, causing heterogeneous samples. Off-policy data in such settings typically harms GRPO convergence; need a method that preserves convergence while enabling heterogeneity.

Method: Introduce F-TIS within a GRPO-style training loop. Allows embedding off-policy samples from heterogeneous models by filtering and truncating importance weights to reduce variance and bias. Emphasizes communication efficiency and parallel inference across nodes, enabling scalable, decentralized collaboration.

Result: Empirical evaluation across various heterogeneous settings shows convergence identical to on-policy training. In certain configurations, the approach improves generalization on out-of-distribution tasks, with performance gains up to 12%.

Conclusion: F-TIS enables heterogeneity in RL fine-tuning of LLMs without sacrificing convergence and can enhance OOD generalization in some settings, making decentralized collaborative RL more practical.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning methods such as GRPO have seen great popularity in LLM post-training. In GRPO, models produce completions to a set of prompts, which are rewarded, and the policy is updated towards the relatively high reward completions. Due to the auto-regressive nature of models, the generation phase of such style of training can be extremely time consuming. As a solution, prior work has sought to distribute the inference step across many nodes, working parallel. These works assume primarily homogeneous models in the training in order to keep samples as close to on-policy as possible. This assumption may be impractical in decentralized systems, where parties with various computes and preferences may wish to collaborate on the same task. Thus, decentralized training requires an approach that can handle heterogeneous models - different models collaborating on the same tasks. However, this leads to highly off-policy samples presented during training, which prior work has identified that off-policy samples can hurt GRPO convergence. To enable heterogeneity, we propose Filtered Truncated Importance Sampling (F-TIS) - a GRPO-style training paradigm that can use off-policy samples to improve local model's learning. Our framework allows various models to collaborate in the same RL training run while being communication efficient. We extensively evaluate F-TIS in various heterogeneous setups and we show that it exhibits identical final model convergence to purely on-sample training. Furthermore, we observe in some setups better generalization on out-of-distribution tasks than on-policy training, increasing model's performance by up to 12\%.

</details>


### [383] [ImplicitTerrainV2: Wavelet-Guided Spatially Adaptive Neural Terrain Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22556)
*Haoan Feng,Xin Xu,Leila De Floriani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ImplicitTerrainV2 delivers a compact, frequency-aware implicit terrain representation with wavelet-guided adaptivity and derivative-aware supervision, achieving high PSNR with much smaller models and effective post-training compression, competitive with DEM codecs and offering off-grid queries and derivative evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Digital elevation models in raster form need interpolation and finite-difference analyses; previous terrain INRs lack explicit frequency control, struggle with gradient fidelity, and are costly to train. There is a need for a compact, derivative-friendly, deployment-friendly INR for terrain.

Method: Introduce Wavelet Complexity Field (WCF) to derive spatially adaptive frequency masks from wavelet coefficients; use these masks for complexity-aware adaptive sampling; apply gradient matching supervision to enforce smooth terrain manifolds; employ post-training mixed-precision quantization and entropy coding; facilitate off-grid queries and derivative evaluation; achieve resolution-independent reconstruction.

Result: On 50 Swiss terrain tiles, PSNR 66.25 dB end-to-end, +5.70 dB over prior work; 3.2x fewer parameters; training time 55 s per tile on a single GPU; compressed format with 1.23 bpp and 0.28 dB PSNR drop; competitive RD with established DEM codecs; supports off-grid queries, closed-form derivatives, and resolution-free reconstruction.

Conclusion: The approach yields a practical, scalable terrain INR that preserves derivative fidelity, enables efficient storage and inference, and broadens GIS applications by providing a compact, flexible representation with potential for wider deployment.

Abstract: Digital elevation models (DEMs) underpin terrain analysis in Geographic Information Systems (GIS), but in their common raster form, they rely on interpolation for off-grid sampling and finite-difference operators for derivative-based analysis. Implicit neural representations (INRs) offer a continuous alternative, but prior terrain INRs lack explicit frequency control, neglect the gradient structure of terrain, and remain too large and costly to train for practical deployment. We present ImplicitTerrainV2, which advances terrain INRs toward a compact, efficient neural terrain data format by combining a spectral control mechanism with wavelet-guided spatial adaptivity, derivative-aware supervision, and post-training model compression. At its core, a wavelet complexity field (WCF) derives spatially-adaptive frequency masks from analytically computed wavelet coefficients, localizing high-frequency capacity to complex terrain regions. The same field guides complexity-aware adaptive sampling that concentrates training in high-complexity regions, while gradient matching applies extra supervision to enforce the smooth manifold structure of terrain DEMs for improved derivative fidelity. Post-training mixed-precision quantization and entropy coding reduce storage to 1.23 bpp with a 0.28 dB PSNR drop. On 50 Swiss terrain tiles, ImplicitTerrainV2 reaches 66.25 dB end-to-end PSNR, improving over the prior work by 5.70 dB while using 3.2x fewer parameters and training in 55 s per tile on a single GPU. Our compressed neural format is competitive with several established DEM codecs in rate-distortion performance, while additionally supporting off-grid point queries, closed-form derivative evaluation, and resolution-independent reconstruction, which may benefit many downstream GIS applications.

</details>


### [384] [Neural Flow Operators can Approximate any Operator: Abstract Frameworks and Universal Approcimations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22557)
*Shuang Chen,Juncai He,Xue-Cheng Tai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A two-structure neural flow framework unifies continuous-depth models for networks and operators, proving well-posedness and universal approximation (including infinite-dimensional spaces) and showing discretizations recover ResNet-like and plain architectures for FC/conv layers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridging finite-dimensional neural networks and infinite-dimensional neural operators; obtaining universal approximation guarantees and a unified architectural perspective via continuous-depth flows.

Method: Define two continuous-depth models: composition and separation structures; establish well-posedness; prove universal approximation for these flows (including infinite-dimensional spaces); extend to convolutional neural flows; derive time-discretization schemes that yield ResNet-type and plain architectures; cover both fully connected and convolutional linear layers.

Result: First universal approximation result for flow-based models between infinite-dimensional spaces; universal results for convolutional neural flows; discretizations yield ResNet-type (composition) and plain architectures (separation); unified route to residual and plain architectures for neural networks and neural operators with FC or conv layers.

Conclusion: A unified, theoretically grounded framework linking residual and plain architectures in finite- and infinite-dimensional settings, enabling robust universal approximation results for neural networks and neural operators and guiding practical discretizations.

Abstract: We introduce an abstract neural flow framework for neural networks and neural operators. The framework contains two continuous-depth models, namely neural flows with composition and separation structures, and covers both finite-dimensional function approximation and infinite-dimensional operator approximation. We prove well-posedness and universal approximation properties for the corresponding neural flows, including, to the best of our knowledge, the first universal approximation result for flow-based models between infinite-dimensional spaces. We also obtain universal approximation results for convolutional neural flow models. Through suitable time discretizations, the composition structure recovers ResNet-type architectures, while the separation structure, via a splitting-based discretization, yields plain architectures. This gives a unified flow-based route to both residual and plain architectures for neural networks and neural operators with fully connected or convolutional linear layers.

</details>


### [385] [Regret-Based $(ε,δ)$-optimal Stopping Criteria for Bayesian Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22561)
*Haowei Wang,Jingyi Wang,Qiyu Wei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes tighter instantaneous regret bounds for GP-UCB and a probabilistic stopping criterion for Bayesian optimization with Gaussian processes, guaranteeing ε-optimality with high probability (1-δ) and showing empirical efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: BO often terminates after a fixed budget, which can be wasteful and lacks optimality guarantees. A theoretically sound stopping rule is needed to ensure near-optimal solutions with minimal evaluations.

Method: Derives improved instantaneous regret bounds for GP-UCB, then designs stopping criteria based on these bounds that ensure ε-optimality with probability at least 1−δ upon termination. Validates the approach with numerical experiments.

Result: Tighter regret bounds for GP-UCB are established; the proposed stopping criteria achieve ε-optimal solutions with high probability and reduce unnecessary evaluations, as demonstrated by experiments.

Conclusion: The work provides a theoretically justified stopping mechanism for GP-UCB in Bayesian optimization, balancing solution quality and evaluation cost. Further work may explore robustness to model misspecification and applicability to other acquisition functions.

Abstract: Bayesian optimization (BO) is a widely used iterative black-box optimization method that utilizes Gaussian process (GP) surrogate models. In practice, BO is typically terminated after a fixed evaluation budget is exhausted, which can incur unnecessary cost and provides no optimality guarantee on solution quality. Recent research in developing a practical stopping criterion has made empirical progress, yet a theoretically sound stopping criterion remains a work in progress. In this work, we present provably tighter instantaneous regret bounds for GP upper confidence bound (GP-UCB) at any given iteration. Then, we propose stopping criteria for GP-UCB based on this tighter bound that ensures an $ε$-optimal solution with high probability $1-δ$ upon termination. Numerical experiments are performed to validate and demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of our stopping criteria.

</details>


### [386] [GraphFlow: A Graph-Based Workflow Management for Efficient LLM-Agent Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22566)
*Ao Li,Shangpeng Yang,Fahao Chen,Tianheng Xu,Peng Li,Zhou Su*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified graph (wGraph) represents workflows as a set of atomic operations; GraphFlow dynamically instantiates task-specific workflows from wGraph and uses two designs (adaptive workflow generation and workflow state management) to integrate workflows into LLM-based agent serving, achieving higher performance (~4.95 pp) and ~4x memory reduction across five benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing workflow-assisted agent systems rely on predefined templates and shallow matching, which fail to capture deep semantic relationships and generalize to unseen tasks. A unified, graph-based workflow representation can encapsulate semantics, reuse, and adaptability.

Method: Represent workflows as wGraph where each node is an atomic operation; dynamically instantiate task-specific workflows from wGraph. GraphFlow integrates workflows via two designs: (1) adaptive workflow generation that builds workflows from wGraph based on task semantics and constraints, and (2) workflow state management that uses wGraph structure to efficiently manage Key-Value caches and reduce redundant computation during serving.

Result: Empirical evaluation across five benchmark datasets shows GraphFlow consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, with an average improvement of about 4.95 percentage points and around a 4x reduction in memory footprint.

Conclusion: wGraph provides a unified substrate for flexible, semantically-rich workflow representations; GraphFlow demonstrates efficient integration and execution for LLM-based agents, improving generalization to unseen tasks and serving efficiency.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents demonstrate strong reasoning and execution capabilities on complex tasks when guided by structured instructions, commonly referred to as workflows. However, existing workflow-assisted agent serving systems typically rely on predefined templates and shallow matching mechanisms, which limit their ability to capture deep semantic relationships and generalize to previously unseen tasks. To address these limitations, we propose a new workflow management paradigm that represents workflows using a unified graph, termed wGraph, where each node corresponds to an atomic operation. wGraph serves as a shared substrate from which task-specific workflows are dynamically instantiated. Building on wGraph primitives, we introduce GraphFlow, a system that efficiently integrates workflows into agent serving through two key designs. First, adaptive workflow generation dynamically constructs workflows from wGraph based on task semantics and constraint requirements. Second, workflow state management exploits wGraph structure to efficiently manage Key-Value (KV) caches, reducing redundant computation during agent serving. Extensive experiments across five benchmark datasets show that GraphFlow consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, yielding an average performance improvement of approximately 4.95 percentage points, while achieving an approximately 4$\times$ reduction in memory footprint.

</details>


### [387] [A Tutorial on Diffusion Theory: From Differential Equations to Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22586)
*Jiayi Fu,Yuxia Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified differential-equation view of diffusion models that links forward processes (ODE/SDE) with backward dynamics (reverse SDE and probability-flow ODE), shows that training objectives align with score matching, and explains sampling methods and the relation between DDPM and DDIM within this framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a clean, mathematically unified framework for diffusion models that clarifies the connections between forward ODE/SDE formulations, reverse-time dynamics, training objectives (score matching), and sampling schemes (including guidance and DDPM/DDIM).

Method: Derive the conditional Gaussian forward process with both ODE and SDE representations; marginalize over the data to obtain forward ODE/SDE transporting p0 to p1; derive reverse-time SDE and reverse probability-flow ODE driven by the marginal score ∇ log p_t(x); show the standard noise-prediction objective is equivalent to score matching up to a parameter-independent constant; discuss sampling methods (e.g., DPM-Solver, classifier guidance, classifier-free guidance); compare DDPM and DDIM within the reverse SDE/ODE framework, showing shared training objective and mapping DDPM to discrete reverse-SDE and DDIM to reverse-ODE.

Result: A cohesive, unified framework that ties forward ODE/SDE formulations to their reverse-time counterparts via the marginal score; establishes the equivalence between the noise-prediction objective and score matching up to a constant; clarifies sampling algorithms (DPM-Solver, guided/classifier-free guidance) and their interpretation as reverse SDE or reverse ODE steps; demonstrates that DDPM and DDIM share the same training objective and correspond to discrete reverse-SDE and reverse-ODE respectively.

Conclusion: The diffusion-model paradigm can be understood as a dual forward–reverse differential-equation process, where forward dynamics (ODE/SDE) are marginalized to connect data to a Gaussian prior, and backward dynamics are governed by the marginal score. This unifies training objectives and sampling schemes, and clarifies the relationships among DDPM, DDIM, and modern solver-based sampling approaches.

Abstract: This tutorial develops diffusion models from the viewpoint of differential equations. We begin with the conditional Gaussian forward process and show that this path admits both an ordinary differential equation (ODE) representation and a stochastic differential equation (SDE) representation. Averaging the conditional process over the data distribution then yields marginalized forward ODE and SDE formulations that transport the data distribution $p_0=p_{\mathrm{data}}$ to a Gaussian prior $p_1=\mathcal{N}(0,I)$. We next derive the corresponding reverse-time dynamics, namely the reverse SDE and the reverse probability-flow ODE, both of which are governed by the marginal score $\grad\log p_t(x)$. This leads to a training objective for score estimation and shows that the standard noise-prediction objective is equivalent to score matching up to an additive constant independent of the model parameters. We then discuss sampling methods for the learned reverse dynamics, including DPM-Solver, as well as guided sampling through classifier guidance and classifier-free guidance. Finally, we compare DDPM and DDIM with the reverse SDE/ODE framework and show that they share the same training objective, while DDPM sampling corresponds to discrete reverse-SDE sampling and DDIM sampling corresponds to reverse-ODE sampling.

</details>


### [388] [Do Deep Ensembles Actually Capture Uncertainty in Graph Neural Networks?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22593)
*Pedro C. Vieira,Pedro Ribeiro,Viacheslav Borovitskiy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deep ensembles offer limited benefit for uncertainty quantification in message-passing GNNs, with gains mainly from stabilizing optimization noise rather than improving epistemic uncertainty; independent trainings collapse to similar predictions due to functional convexity, undermining ensemble diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess whether standard deep ensembles, successful in vision, provide meaningful uncertainty estimates for graph-structured data and to understand the mechanism behind any observed gains.

Method: Benchmark seven diverse graph datasets across varied tasks; evaluate standard deep ensembles for message-passing GNNs; perform aleatoric-epistemic decomposition; analyze prediction diversity and ensemble disagreement; examine functional vs weight-space convexity and its impact on diversity.

Result: Ensembles yield little improvement over a single model; marginal gains arise from stabilization of optimization noise in point predictions rather than better uncertainty estimates; independently trained networks converge to overly similar predictions (epistemic collapse); lack of diversity neutralizes the core advantage of ensembles.

Conclusion: Deep ensemble success does not readily transfer to graph machine learning; epistemic uncertainty remains poorly captured due to paradigm-induced collapse of diversity, suggesting the need for alternative uncertainty quantification strategies for GNNs.

Abstract: While deep ensembles are widely considered to be the default method for uncertainty quantification in deep learning, their effectiveness for graph-structured data is often simply assumed based on successes in domains like computer vision. We investigate standard deep ensembles specifically for message-passing graph neural networks. Benchmarking across seven datasets representing varied tasks and complexities, we reveal that ensembles provide surprisingly little improvement over a single model. Instead, the observed marginal gains stem primarily from stabilizing optimization noise in point predictions rather than yielding meaningfully better uncertainty estimates. Through an aleatoric-epistemic decomposition, we identify epistemic collapse: independently trained networks consistently converge to overly similar predictions. Because disagreement is the fundamental mechanism through which ensembles capture epistemic uncertainty, this lack of diversity neutralizes their key advantage. Analyzing this phenomenon further, we suggest this collapse is driven by functional rather than weight-space convexity, where distinct parameter solutions induce almost identical behavior. Our results suggest that deep ensemble success does not seamlessly transfer to graph machine learning.

</details>


### [389] [Factored Diffusion Policies:Compositionally Generalized Robot Control with a Single Score Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22596)
*Sayan Mitra,Ege Yuceel,Noah Giles,Abhishek Pai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A single shared diffusion policy factorizes scoring across task factors via per-factor dropout, enabling scalable, near joint-performance with a theoretical score bound and a closed-loop trajectory certificate; validated on drone racing with strong generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome combinatorial data requirements when tasks are described by multiple discrete factors (e.g., objects, obstacles, colors) by enabling composition without training separate models per factor combination.

Method: Train a diffusion model with per-factor null-token dropout; at inference, sum the factor-wise scores assuming approximate conditional independence given action-observation. Derive a uniform error bound on the joint score; propagate this through reverse-time ODE to a trajectory-tube certificate; use a contracting controller to bound state trajectories; evaluate on drone racing tasks.

Result: Theory: a bounded uniform error for the joint score; a trajectory-tube certificate linking score error to closed-loop tracking. Empirically, on state-based multi-gate racing: 90% held-out gates vs 3% with a K-network baseline; on vision-based single-gate traversal: +11.7pp success-rate gain, 2.4x fewer crashes, zero-shot transfer to unseen venue.

Conclusion: Factored diffusion policies with a single shared network provide data-efficient, generalizable policy learning with provable bounds and practical closed-loop guarantees, outperforming multi-network compositional baselines and enabling better transfer.

Abstract: Robotic tasks are typically specified by a tuple of factors, such as the object to be grasped, the obstacles to be avoided, the color of the target, and so on. Collecting expert demonstrations for every combination of factor values grows combinatorially. We present factored diffusion policies: a single shared diffusion network trained with per-factor null-token dropout, whose score decomposes additively across factors at inference. Under approximate conditional independence between factors given the action-observation pair, this composition approximates the true joint score with a bounded uniform error, reducing the training-task budget from a product of factor cardinalities to a sum. A trajectory-tube certificate chains this score-level bound through the reverse-time sampling ODE and a contracting tracking controller into a closed-loop state-trajectory tube whose radius factors into an ODE-sensitivity constant and a per-factor score-error budget. Unlike compositional-diffusion methods for control that combine separately trained networks, we use one shared network. Drone racing experiments confirm both the generalization bound and the certificate. On state-based multi-gate racing, the factored policy passes 90% of held-out gates -- matching an oracle -- while a K-network composition baseline collapses to 3%; on vision-based single-gate traversal, it transfers zero-shot to an unseen venue with +11.7pp success-rate gain and 2.4X crash-rate reduction.

</details>


### [390] [The Double Dilemma in Multi-Task Radiology Report Generation: A Gradient Dynamics Analysis and Solution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22635)
*Erjian Zhang,Yatong Hao,Liejun Wang,Zhiqing Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A backbone-agnostic optimizer (CAME-Grad) is proposed to fix gradient dynamics failures in multi-task radiology report generation by analyzing linear scalarization with an SDE framework; it uses conflict-averse direction rectification, magnitude-enhanced energy injection, and adaptive gradient fusion to improve robustness and achieve consistent gains across eight RRG methods (2.3% on MIMIC-CXR, 1.9% on IU X-Ray).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-task radiology report generation relies on coarse linear scalarization, which poorly balances discriminative supervision and smooth report generation. Gradient dynamics under linear scalarization cause drift and diffusion issues, leading to suboptimal solutions. A backbone-agnostic optimizer is needed to enforce geometric validity and enhance optimization.

Method: Gradient-dynamics analysis via stochastic differential equations (SDE) to identify a 'Double Dilemma' of drift term deviation and diffusion term decay under linear scalarization. Propose CAME-Grad with: (1) conflict-averse direction rectification to align gradients with feasible directions; (2) magnitude-enhanced energy injection to strengthen productive updates; (3) an adaptive gradient fusion mechanism to balance the theoretical optimal direction with task-specific inductive biases. It is backbone-agnostic and plug-and-play across RRG methods.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows substantial, consistent improvements across eight diverse RRG methods. Average gains of ~2.3 percentage points on MIMIC-CXR and ~1.9 percentage points on IU X-Ray, demonstrating improved clinical efficacy.

Conclusion: CAME-Grad provides a universal, plug-and-play optimization strategy that mitigates gradient dynamics issues in multi-task RRG, preserves geometric validity, helps avoid poor local optima, and yields consistent performance gains across datasets and methods.

Abstract: While multi-task learning based automatic radiology report generation (RRG) is widely adopted to ensure clinical consistency, most focus on architectural designs yet remain limited to coarse linear scalarization strategies. These strategies cannot effectively balance the hard constraints of discriminative clinical supervision with the smoothness requirements of report generation. To address these problems, we analyze the failure mechanism of linear scalarization from the perspective of gradient dynamics, utilizing the stochastic differential equation (SDE) framework to characterize it as a "Double Dilemma" of drift term deviation and diffusion term decay. Based on this, we propose a backbone-agnostic optimizer named Conflict-Averse Magnitude-Enhanced Gradient Descent (CAME-Grad). Through conflict-averse direction rectification and magnitude-enhanced energy injection, the algorithm not only ensures geometric validity, but also avoids local optimal solutions. Then, the adaptive gradient fusion mechanism is used to establish a dynamic balance between the theoretical optimal direction and the task-specific inductive bias. Experiments show that as a universal plug-and-play optimizer, CAME-Grad brings substantial and consistent improvements across eight diverse RRG methods, elevating overall clinical efficacy performance by an average of 2.3\% on MIMIC-CXR and 1.9\% on IU X-Ray. Our code is available at https://github.com/vpsg-research/CAME-Grad.

</details>


### [391] [Benchmarking Machine Learning Architectures for Antimicrobial Stewardship in Pediatric ICUs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22611)
*Niklas Raehse,Luregn J. Schlapbach,Daphné Chopard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Target-driven, dataset characteristics trump model complexity in PICU antimicrobial stewardship ML predictions; sequence models help precision-recall at 24-hour granularity but calibrations suffer, while simpler tabular models have better calibration. Multi-task learning offers limited gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate diagnostic uncertainty and broad-spectrum antibiotic use in pediatric ICUs by applying ML to EHR data, extending beyond adults, and systematically evaluating temporal representations and targets in a unified framework across cohorts.

Method: Systematic benchmarking across a public dataset and a private institutional cohort. Four proxy targets for antibiotic exposure (intravenous-to-oral switch, de-escalation, discontinuation, short-course therapy). Compare tabular, sequence-based, and graph-based temporal models at multiple temporal resolutions under a unified evaluation framework. Assess calibration and precision-recall, and explore multi-task learning.

Result: Performance is driven mainly by target prevalence and dataset characteristics rather than model complexity. Sequence models improve precision-recall at coarse (24-h) resolution versus tabular approaches, but finer temporal modeling yields limited additional benefit. Gains come with poorer calibration; simpler tabular models provide more reliable probability estimates. Multi-task learning yields only marginal improvements, suggesting limited shared structure across targets. Design choices around target definitions, temporal representation, and calibration are crucial for reliable pediatric AMS decision support.

Conclusion: The study underscores the importance of target design, temporal representation, and calibration when applying ML to pediatric AMS. It provides practical guidance on model selection and evaluation for developing reliable decision support systems in PICU antimicrobial stewardship.

Abstract: Antimicrobial stewardship (AMS) is critical in pediatric intensive care units (PICUs), where diagnostic uncertainty often drives broad-spectrum antibiotic use, increasing antimicrobial resistance and potential long-term harms. Machine learning offers a promising approach for identifying patient-level opportunities for stewardship interventions from electronic health record data, yet prior work has focused largely on adult populations and static tabular representations. We present a systematic benchmarking study of AMS intervention prediction in the PICU across a public dataset and a private institutional cohort. We define four clinically relevant proxy targets for reducing antibiotic exposure: intravenous-to-oral switching, de-escalation, discontinuation, and short-course therapy. Under a unified evaluation framework, we compare tabular, sequence-based, and graph-based temporal models at multiple temporal resolutions. We find that predictive performance is driven primarily by target prevalence and dataset characteristics rather than model complexity. Sequence models improve the precision-recall trade-off over tabular approaches at coarse (24-hour) resolution, while finer temporal modeling provides limited additional benefit. However, these gains come at the cost of poorer calibration, with simpler tabular models yielding more reliable probability estimates. Multi-task learning produces only marginal improvements, suggesting limited shared structure across stewardship targets. Our findings highlight the importance of target design, temporal representation, and calibration in clinical machine learning, and provide practical guidance for developing reliable decision support systems for pediatric AMS.

</details>


### [392] [Evolutionary Multi-Task Optimization for LLM-Guided Program Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22613)
*Halil Alperen Gozeten,Xuechen Zhang,Emrullah Ildiz,Ege Onur Taga,Tara Javidi,Samet Oymak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EMO-STA enables evolutionary multi-task optimization for LLM-guided program discovery by evolving a shared pool of executable programs across task families and then adapting candidates to individual tasks, yielding better cross-task generalization and compute efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Related-task reuse of structure in program discovery; reduce overfitting and compute cost of independent single-task evolution.

Method: Two-stage framework: 1) evolve shared archive across a family; 2) adapt selected shared programs to each target task using various strategies (warm-start from shared, best average, or task-specific best). Evaluate across eight task families (continuous optimization, geometric construction, modeling, algorithmic optimization); study compute-allocation between shared evolution and task adaptation.

Result: EMO-STA generally improves over matched single-task evolution; STA Best-Local best for in-distribution adaptation; STA Best-Shared robust for unseen tasks; allocating substantial budget to shared evolution helps; balanced budgets often optimal; shared evolution reduces overfitting in low-data settings (ARC tasks, time-series feature engineering).

Conclusion: Shared multi-task evolution enhances generalization and transfer across task families, offering compute-efficient program discovery and mitigates brittle artifacts by leveraging cross-task structure; adopt EMO-STA with hybrid budgets for robust performance.

Abstract: Recent LLM-guided evolutionary search methods have shown that iterative program mutation can discover strong algorithms, but they typically optimize each task independently, even when related tasks share reusable structure. We introduce Evolutionary Multi-Task Optimization (EMO) for LLM-guided program discovery, and propose EMO-STA (Shared-Then-Adapt), a two-stage framework that first evolves a shared archive of executable programs across a task family and then adapts selected shared candidates to each target task. Within EMO-STA, we explore multiple adaptation strategies, including warm-starting from the shared archive, adapting the best average shared program, and adapting the shared program that performs best on each target task. Across eight task families spanning continuous optimization, geometric construction, modeling, and algorithmic optimization, EMO-STA improves over matched-compute single-task evolution in most settings, with STA Best-Local providing the strongest in-distribution adaptation and STA Best-Shared yielding robust transfer to unseen tasks. Compute-allocation experiments show that allocating a substantial fraction of the family-level budget to shared evolution is consistently beneficial, with roughly balanced shared and adaptation budgets often being optimal. Beyond compute efficiency, we show that shared evolution can mitigate overfitting in low-evidence settings (e.g. few training data), including ARC tasks and time-series feature engineering, by favoring programs that generalize across all tasks rather than exploiting task-specific brittle artifacts.

</details>


### [393] [Two is better than one: A Collapse-free Multi-Reward RLIF Training Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22620)
*Shourov Joarder,Diganta Sikdar,Ahsan Habib Akash,Binod Bhattarai,Prashnna Gyawali*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a multi-reward RLIF framework with two internal rewards (cluster-voting answer-level reward and token-wise self-certainty completion-level reward), plus GDPO normalization and KL-Cov regularization, improving stability and robustness on math reasoning and code-generation benchmarks to approach supervised RLVR without external supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of RLIF that rely on a single internal reward, which can cause reward hacking, entropy collapse, and degraded reasoning structure; seek scalable unsupervised alternatives with stable long-horizon reasoning.

Method: Decompose training signals into two complementary rewards: (1) answer-level reward via cluster voting; (2) token-level completion reward via self-certainty. Apply GDPO-based normalization to align reward scales. Introduce KL-Cov regularization to maintain higher-entropy token distributions and prevent late-stage collapse, preserving exploration.

Result: Empirical evaluation on mathematical reasoning and code-generation benchmarks shows improved stability and robustness over prior unsupervised RL approaches, with performance approaching that of supervised RLVR methods.

Conclusion: Complementary internal rewards combined with targeted regularization can sustain stable long-horizon reasoning without external ground-truth supervision; code release pending.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has substantially improved the reasoning ability of LLMs, but often depends on external supervision from human annotations or gold-standard solutions. Reinforcement learning from internal feedback (RLIF) has recently emerged as a scalable unsupervised alternative, using signals extracted from the model itself. However, existing RLIF methods typically rely on a single internal reward, which can lead to reward hacking, entropy collapse, and degraded reasoning structure. We propose a multi-reward RLIF framework that decomposes the training signal into two complementary components: an answer-level reward based on cluster voting and a completion-level reward based on token-wise self-certainty. To combine these signals robustly, we apply GDPO-based normalization to reduce reward-scale imbalance. We further introduce KL-Cov regularization, which targets low-entropy token distributions responsible for disproportionate entropy reduction, preserving exploration and preventing late-stage collapse. Across mathematical reasoning and code-generation benchmarks, our method improves stability and robustness over prior unsupervised RL approaches, while achieving performance close to supervised RLVR methods. These results show that complementary internal rewards, combined with targeted regularization, can support stable long-horizon reasoning without relying on external ground-truth supervision. Code will be released soon.

</details>


### [394] [A note on convergence of Wasserstein policy optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22622)
*David Šiška,Yufei Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Wasserstein Policy Optimization (WPO) is analyzed in a continuous-space RL setting, arguing that, under entropy-regularized MDPs and mean-field/Wasserstein gradient-flow theory with log-Sobolev inequalities, the gradient flow converges linearly to the global optimum of the value function, given a sufficiently regular solution and energy dissipation along the flow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide theoretical convergence guarantees for WPO in continuous state-action spaces, addressing a gap between empirical success and theory.

Method: Model WPO as a gradient flow in Wasserstein space for an entropy-regularised objective; use mean-field analysis and log-Sobolev inequalities to establish energy dissipation and a local log-Sobolev inequality; assume existence of a sufficiently regular solution to the gradient-flow equation and derive linear convergence of the value function.

Result: The argument yields linear convergence of the value function to the global optimum under the stated assumptions, via energy dissipation and a local log-Sobolev inequality.

Conclusion: WPO can achieve linear convergence in continuous spaces within entropy-regularised MDPs, supported by gradient-flow and mean-field analysis; practical realizability depends on verifying regularity and local LSI conditions.

Abstract: Wasserstein Policy Optimization (WPO) is a recently proposed reinforcement learning algorithm that leverages Wasserstein gradient flows to optimize stochastic policies in continuous action spaces. Despite its empirical success, the theoretical convergence properties of WPO in environments with continuous state and action spaces have yet to be fully established. In this note, we argue that WPO within the framework of entropy-regularised Markov Decision Processes converges linearly. This is done by leveraging recent advances in mean-field analysis for convergence of gradient flows using log-Sobole inequalities. Assuming existence of sufficiently regular solution to the gradient flow equation we demonstrate monotonic energy dissipation along the flow and establish a local log-Sobolev inequality. Ultimately, these properties allow us to argue that the value function should converge linearly to the global optimum.

</details>


### [395] [Why SGD is not Brownian Motion: A New Perspective on Stochastic Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22644)
*Igor Ignashin,Anna Radovskaya,Andrew Semenov,Egor Lopatin,Stanislav Potapov,Aleksandr Kovalenko,Andrey Veprikov,Aleksandr Shestakov,Andrey Leonidov,Aleksandr Beznosikov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a discrete, master-equation-based reformulation of SGD dynamics that diverges from Langevin predictions at O(eta^2); reveals a regime separation along Hessian directions, with logics for diffusion in flat directions and confinement in sharp directions, supported by neural network experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard SGD analyses often replace minibatch noise by Brownian motion in a continuous-time Langevin framework, which is only accurate in a strict limit. Finite learning rates and discrete updates can alter the noise structure and dynamics, especially near critical points.

Method: Derive a master equation from the discrete SGD update, yield a discrete Fokker–Planck equation; perform eigenbasis decomposition with respect to the mean Hessian; analyze behavior near critical points and identify distinct regimes for different Hessian directions; provide empirical validation on NN models.

Result: Discrete FP differs from Langevin at order eta^2; nearly-flat directions lack a stationary distribution with variance growing over time, indicating diffusion along valleys with a rate proportional to the learning rate; empirical data show a qualitative separation between confined and diffusive modes in vision and NLP models.

Conclusion: A discrete, non-equilibrium picture of SGD captures qualitative dynamical features missed by Langevin approximations, with implications for generalization and training dynamics; invites further experimental validation and exploration of optimization strategies that exploit the mode-dependent diffusion behavior.

Abstract: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is commonly modeled as a Langevin process, assuming that minibatch noise acts as Brownian motion. However, this approximation relies on a continuous-time limit and a sqrt(eta) noise scaling that does not match the discrete SGD update at finite learning rate. In this work, we propose an alternative formulation of SGD as deterministic dynamics in a fluctuating loss landscape induced by minibatch sampling. Starting directly from the discrete update, we derive a master equation for the parameter distribution and obtain a discrete Fokker--Planck equation that differs from the standard Langevin form at order eta^2. Using this framework, we analyze SGD dynamics near critical points of the loss. We show that the behavior decomposes along the eigenbasis of the mean Hessian into qualitatively distinct regimes. In particular, nearly-flat directions do not admit a stationary distribution: the variance grows over time, corresponding to effective diffusion along valleys with a coefficient proportional to the learning rate. We provide empirical evidence supporting these predictions on neural network models in computer vision and natural language processing, observing a clear qualitative separation between confined and diffusive modes.

</details>


### [396] [Posterior Collapse as Automatic Spectral Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22691)
*Johannes Hirn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Posterior collapse in beta-VAEs acts as automatic spectral pruning; latent modes collapse when their reconstruction contribution falls below the beta cutoff, yielding a cascade as beta changes; in the linear Gaussian case, collapse aligns with PCA; tested on WorldClim dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why posterior collapse occurs in beta-VAEs and provide a unifying spectral-pruning mechanism; connect to stability analysis and an invariant order parameter to rank and inspect latent modes.

Method: Landau stability analysis of the VAE loss; define a latent-rescaling-invariant order parameter; derive collapse thresholds; analyze linear Gaussian case where collapse spectra coincide with PCA; compare predictions with WorldClim data.

Result: Equilibrium solutions show a cascade of latent-mode collapses as beta varies; a mean-field law governs each collapse; in the linear Gaussian setting, collapse, utility, and PCA spectra coincide; predictions validated on WorldClim dataset.

Conclusion: Posterior collapse is interpretable as automatic spectral pruning governed by an invariant order parameter; this framework ranks active latent modes and predicts collapse thresholds, with linear-Gaussian cases aligning with PCA and practical validation on real data.

Abstract: We show that posterior collapse in $β$-VAEs implements automatic spectral pruning. A latent mode collapses if its contribution to reconstruction is below the cutoff set by $β$. Equilibrium solutions with different $β$ thus reveal a cascade of collapses as latent modes decouple from least to most useful.
  We derive this as a consequence of the loss via a Landau stability analysis. We define a latent-rescaling-invariant order parameter that ranks active latent modes and whose collapse thresholds identify which effective variables to inspect first.
  In the linear Gaussian case, the collapse spectrum, utility spectrum, and normalized PCA spectrum coincide, and each collapse follows a mean-field law. We test these predictions on the WorldClim dataset.

</details>


### [397] [Clipping Bottleneck: Stabilizing RLVR via Stochastic Recovery of Near-Boundary Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22703)
*Shuo Yang,Jinda Lu,Chiyu Ma,Kexin Huang,Haoming Meng,Qihui Zhang,Yuyang Liu,Bolin Ding,Guoyin Wang,Li Yuan,Jingren Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hard clipping in GRPO-style RLVR objectives discards near-boundary signals; NSR introduces near-boundary stochastic retention, recovering informative signals and stabilizing training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate training instability caused by rigid hard clipping in RLVR by exploiting informative signals just beyond the clipping threshold.

Method: Systematically analyze clipping-based GRPO-style objectives to identify the boundary information loss; propose Near-boundary Stochastic Rescue (NSR): a minimal plug-and-play approach that stochastically retains tokens just beyond the clipping threshold; interpret as implicit gradient decay in expectation; compare with deterministic decay via ablations; conduct extensive experiments across 7B–30B models and both dense and MoE architectures; benchmark against DAPO and GSPO.

Result: NSR consistently improves training stability and yields gains across model sizes (7B–30B) and architectures (dense and MoE); outperforms strong baselines such as DAPO and GSPO; its boundary-local stochastic rescue is more effective than deterministic gradient decay.

Conclusion: Near-boundary Stochastic Rescue (NSR) is a simple, plug-and-play remedy for clipping bottlenecks in RLVR, enabling more robust optimization and reliable gains across diverse models and configurations.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a central paradigm for scaling LLM reasoning, yet its optimization often suffers from training instability and suboptimal convergence. Through a systematic dissection of clipping-based GRPO-style objectives, we identify the rigid clipping decision induced by hard clipping as a key practical bottleneck in the studied RLVR setups. Specifically, our analysis suggests that informative signals can lie in the near-boundary region just beyond the clipping threshold, and are therefore discarded by the standard hard-clipping rule. Notably, once this bottleneck is precisely identified, even simple stochastic perturbations at the boundary can recover meaningful performance gains. Building on this finding, we propose Near-boundary Stochastic Rescue (NSR), a minimal, plug-and-play modification that stochastically retains these slightly out-of-bound tokens to recover lost signals. While NSR, via stochastic sampling, can be interpreted as inducing an implicit gradient decay in expectation, our ablations reveal that its stochastic, boundary-local rescue mechanism is consistently more effective than deterministic gradient decay. Validated by extensive experiments across model sizes from 7B to 30B and both dense and MoE architectures, as a plug-and-play solution, NSR substantially improves training stability and delivers consistent gains over strong baselines such as DAPO and GSPO.

</details>


### [398] [Abstraction for Offline Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22711)
*Clarisse Wibault,Alexander Goldie,Antonio Villares,Maike Osborne,Jakob Foerster*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes relativised options and multi-representation hierarchies to enable absolute abstraction in goal-conditioned RL; introduces two learning algorithms and demonstrates gains in offline GCRL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Markov Decision Processes often exhibit redundancy due to symmetries across state-goal pairs in real-world GCRL. While hierarchical policies have been used for horizon reduction, there is potential for absolute abstraction to reuse experience across similar contexts, improving data efficiency in offline settings.

Method: Introduce relativised options and distinct representations for different hierarchy levels. Present two simple algorithms for learning relativised options and for abstracting from the absolute frame of reference.

Result: Empirical evaluations show that the proposed inductive biases significantly improve performance in offline GCRL.

Conclusion: Hierarchical structures with relativised options enable absolute abstraction and better knowledge reuse, offering a promising direction for improving offline GCRL performance.

Abstract: Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) often exhibit significant redundancy due to symmetries and shared structure across state-goal pairs in real-world Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning (GCRL). While hierarchical policies have been motivated for horizon reduction via temporal abstraction in offline GCRL, we demonstrate that hierarchy also enables absolute abstraction. By introducing relativised options as well as distinct representations for different levels of the hierarchy, we demonstrate how an agent can reuse experience across similar contexts of the state-space. Based on this framework, we introduce two simple algorithms for learning relativised options and abstracting from the absolute frame of reference. Our experiments show that such inductive biases significantly improve performance in offline GCRL.

</details>


### [399] [Reading Task Failure Off the Activations: A Sparse-Feature Audit of GPT-2 Small on Indirect Object Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22719)
*Mahdi Nasermoghadasi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A reproducible audit identifies many sparse-autoencoder features in GPT-2 small that correlate with IOI task failure; strongest correlate is feature 17,491 tied to prompts involving 'the keys', but causal ablation shows it is not a sufficient cause; the main contribution is an interpretable audit pipeline and data/code release rather than a single causal feature.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: to surface interpretable, model-agnostic correlates of IOI failure and evaluate whether such correlates are causal.

Method: 300 prompts; layer-8 residual-stream SAE features (24,576 features) from Bloom (2024); Holm-corrected significance; Cohen's d for effect size; three controls: (i) causal ablation of feature 17,491 across all tokens for the 45 keys prompts; (ii) representation baseline via logistic regression on the raw 768-dimensional residual stream; (iii) seed-robustness across five random seeds; evaluation on accuracy, AUC, p-values.

Result: GPT-2 small achieves 79.7% accuracy on 300 prompts. 146 of 24,576 SAE features pass Holm-corrected significance; 105 reach |d| > 0.8. The strongest correlate is feature 17,491 (d=+2.93; Neuronpedia label 'cryptographic keys'), predominantly active when the transferred object is 'the keys', where failure is 93.3% vs 7.5% on other objects (Fisher p = 8.79e-33). Causal ablation shows zeroing 17,491 does not restore accuracy (6.7% -> 4.4%), indicating correlation rather than sufficiency at this layer. A logistic regression on the raw 768 residual-vector achieves ROC AUC = 0.929, matching the SAE top features (0.927); the SAE basis adds interpretability but not predictive power. Across five seeds, the keys-related failure persists (75.0%–93.3%), but 17,491 is top-|d| in only 1 of 5 runs. The paper emphasizes the audit pipeline as the main contribution rather than a single causal feature, and releases code, data, and scripts. The pipeline runs on a typical laptop (Apple M3 Max).

Conclusion: The study provides a model-agnostic, interpretable audit workflow that surfaces correlated features but does not establish causal mechanisms; the strongest feature identified is not causally sufficient, underscoring the value of the pipeline for robust interpretability and reproducibility over claims about individual features.

Abstract: We report a small, reproducible audit of which sparse-autoencoder (SAE) features of GPT-2 small fire differently on failed versus successful trials of the Indirect Object Identification (IOI) task. On 300 prompts, GPT-2 small reaches 79.7% accuracy; 146 of the 24,576 features in the layer-8 residual-stream SAE release of Bloom (2024) clear a Holm-corrected significance threshold and 105 reach a large effect size (|Cohen's d| > 0.8). The strongest single correlate of failure -- feature 17,491, d=+2.93, Neuronpedia label 'cryptographic keys' -- is essentially silent except when the prompt's transferred object is 'the keys,' on which GPT-2 small fails 93.3% of the time vs. 7.5% on the other seven objects (Fisher exact p = 8.79 x 10^-33). We put this correlate through three controls that a mechanistic claim should pass. (i) A causal ablation: zeroing feature 17,491 in the residual stream across all token positions of the 45 keys prompts does not restore accuracy (6.7% -> 4.4%); the feature is a correlate, not a sufficient cause at this layer. (ii) A representation baseline: a logistic regression on the raw 768-dimensional residual stream reaches 5-fold ROC AUC = 0.929, matching the top-100 SAE features (0.927); the SAE basis adds interpretability, not predictive power. (iii) A seed-robustness check: across five random seeds the keys-subset failure rate stays in 75.0--93.3% (the behavioural effect is real), but feature 17,491 is the top-|d| feature in only 1 of 5 runs. The methodological contribution is therefore the audit pipeline (cheap, model-agnostic, surfaces named correlates) rather than any single feature found through it. We release the code, the 300-prompt corpus, the 300x24,576 activation matrix, the ablation and baseline scripts, and the figures. The full pipeline runs on a laptop (Apple M3 Max, no discrete GPU).

</details>


### [400] [The Value of Covariance Matching in Gaussian DDPMs and the Lanczos Sampler](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22723)
*Md Sahil Akhtar,Aymane El Gadarri,Vivek F. Farias,Adam D. Jozefiak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Full posterior covariance matching reduces the path-KL error in Gaussian DDPMs from O(1/T) to O(1/T^2), and is made practical by the Lanczos Gaussian sampler (LGS), a training-free, matrix-free method that samples from the optimal reverse covariance using covariance-vector products. With only a few Lanczos steps (e.g., 3), this approach outperforms strong diagonal baselines on standard image benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Path-space KL error between the exact reverse and learned reverse in Gaussian DDPMs limits sampling quality, especially under classifier guidance which uses the entire reverse trajectory. Isotropic/diagonal reverse covariances incur an unavoidable O(1/T) error that hinders scaling to more steps.

Method: The authors prove that matching the full posterior covariance eliminates the 1/T barrier, yielding an O(1/T^2) path-KL. They introduce Lanczos Gaussian sampler (LGS), a training-free, matrix-free sampler that draws from the optimal reverse covariance using only covariance-vector products (accessible via Jacobian-vector products of the posterior mean). LGS is lightweight, avoids dense covariance storage, and relies on the Lanczos iteration, with each step needing one Jacobian-vector product.

Result: Theoretically, path-KL improves to O(1/T^2) with full covariance matching. LGS approximation error decays exponentially with the number of Lanczos steps. Empirically, using only three Lanczos steps yields better sample quality than strong diagonal-covariance baselines (including OCM-DDPM) on standard image benchmarks.

Conclusion: Full covariance matching is both theoretically valuable and practically feasible for fast DDPM sampling. LGS provides a training-free, matrix-free route to realize optimal reverse covariance sampling with exponential convergence in Lanczos steps.

Abstract: A central error measure in Gaussian DDPMs is the path-space KL divergence between the exact reverse chain and the learned Gaussian reverse process. This quantity is especially relevant for procedures such as classifier guidance, which perturb the entire reverse trajectory rather than only the terminal sample. Prior analyses show that standard isotropic reverse covariances suffer an unavoidable $Ω(1/T)$ path-KL error as the number of denoising steps $T$ grows. We show that matching the full posterior covariance breaks this barrier, yielding an order-wise improvement that reduces the path KL to $O(1/T^2)$. To make full covariance matching practical, we introduce the Lanczos Gaussian sampler (LGS), a training-free, matrix-free method for sampling from the optimal reverse covariance using only covariance-vector products, which are available through Jacobian-vector products of the posterior mean. LGS avoids dense covariance storage and auxiliary covariance models. We prove that LGS approximation error decays exponentially in the number of Lanczos steps, where each Lanczos step requires a single Jacobian-vector product. Empirically, using only just three such steps improves sample quality over strong diagonal-covariance baselines, including OCM-DDPM, across standard image benchmarks. This identifies full covariance matching as both theoretically valuable and practically accessible for fast DDPM sampling.

</details>


### [401] [Multiple Neural Operators Achieve Near-Optimal Rates for Multi-Task Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22724)
*Adrien Weihs,Hayden Schaeffer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multi-task operator learning with shared representations achieves near-optimal bounds and shares the asymptotic cost with single-task learning; MNO and concatenated-DeepONet have similar worst-case rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess how multi-task sharing affects approximation and generalization for operator-learning architectures; compare MNO with DeepONet in worst-case complexity.

Method: Derive upper bounds for approximation and statistical generalization for Lipschitz multiple operator maps; prove minimax lower bounds; compare architectures.

Result: Near-optimal upper bounds; minimax lower bounds showing curse of parametric complexity; shared representations do not increase overall cost; MNO and multi-task DeepONet share asymptotic rates.

Conclusion: Multi-task operator learning obeys the same scaling laws as single operator learning; architecture has limited effect on asymptotic rates; practical design choices may impact finite-sample performance.

Abstract: We study the approximation and statistical complexity of learning collections of operators in a shared multi-task setting, with a focus on the Multiple Neural Operators (MNO) architecture. For broad classes of Lipschitz multiple operator maps, we derive near-optimal upper bounds for approximation and statistical generalization. On the lower-bound side, we establish a curse of parametric complexity and prove corresponding minimax rates. Together, these results show that shared representations across tasks do not increase the overall cost: multi-task operator learning follows the same scaling laws as single operator learning. We also compare MNO with a multi-task extension of DeepONet based on concatenated task inputs and show that, from a worst-case approximation-complexity perspective, both architectures satisfy essentially the same asymptotic rates.

</details>


### [402] [Proxy-Based Approximation of Shapley and Banzhaf Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22738)
*Santo M. A. R. Thies,Hubert Baniecki,R. Teal Witter,Eyke Hüllermeier,Maximilian Muschalik,Fabian Fumagalli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ProxySHAP computes exact interaction indices for tree ensembles in polynomial time via a residual-corrected proxy framework, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome speed–accuracy trade-offs in higher-order Shapley/Banzhaf interaction estimators and bypass exponential dependencies on tree depth while ensuring consistency.

Method: Introduce ProxySHAP: combine tree-based proxy models with residual correction. Provide a polynomial-time generalization of interventional TreeSHAP to compute exact interaction indices for tree ensembles. Analyze residual adjustment via Maximum Sample Reuse (MSR) and derive conditions under which MSR corrects proxy bias without exponential variance growth.

Result: Theoretical generalization enabling exact interactions in polynomial time; empirical benchmarking shows ProxySHAP surpasses ProxySPEX and KernelSHAP-IQ in approximation quality, scales to thousands of features, and improves downstream explainability tasks.

Conclusion: ProxySHAP establishes a new state-of-the-art for interaction estimation in tree ensembles, achieving high accuracy without sacrificing scalability, and provides practical guidance for MSR-based residual correction.

Abstract: Shapley and Banzhaf interactions capture the complex dynamics inherent in modern machine learning applications. However, current estimators for these higher-order interactions trade off between speed and accuracy. To overcome this limitation, we introduce ProxySHAP. ProxySHAP reconciles the high sample efficiency of tree-based proxy models with a principled path to consistency via residual correction. On a theoretical level, we derive a polynomial-time generalization of interventional TreeSHAP to compute exact interaction indices for tree ensembles, successfully bypassing exponential tree-depth dependencies in prior methods. Furthermore, we formally analyze the residual adjustment strategy, characterizing the specific conditions under which Maximum Sample Reuse (MSR) corrects proxy bias without its variance scaling exponentially with interaction size. Extensive benchmarking demonstrates that ProxySHAP sets a new state-of-the-art standard for approximation quality, including in large-scale applications with thousands of features. By achieving the lowest error in both small- and large-budget regimes, ProxySHAP significantly outperforms the prior best estimators ProxySPEX and KernelSHAP-IQ, while also delivering superior performance on downstream explainability tasks.

</details>


### [403] [Cyber-Physical Anomaly Detection in IoT-Enabled Smart Grids Using Machine Learning and Metaheuristic Feature Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22749)
*Adis Alihodžić,Eva Tuba,Milan Tuba*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GA-assisted feature selection with Extra Trees achieves strong anomaly detection on the MSU/ORNL dataset, reducing features from 112 to ~27 and improving macro-F1 and ROC-AUC; tree-based models outperform baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Smart-grid expansion with dense measurements increases vulnerability to cyber-physical disruptions. The goal is to distinguish physical incidents from malicious actions and to determine if a compact, physically informative set of PMU/IED measurements suffices for reliable detection.

Method: Evaluate multiple classifiers (logistic regression, RBF-SVM, XGBoost, Random Forest, Extra Trees) on the MSU/ORNL Power System Attack Dataset. Apply genetic-algorithm-based feature selection to identify a reduced feature subset. Compare full-feature baselines with GA-reduced subsets, focusing on detection accuracy and interpretability.

Result: Tree-based ensembles are most effective; Extra Trees provides the strongest full-feature baseline. GA + Extra Trees reduces features from 112 to ~27.4 (avg) and improves macro-F1 from 0.9118 to 0.9212 and ROC-AUC from 0.9791 to 0.9837.

Conclusion: Many synchronized electrical measurements are redundant; a compact subset of phasor-based features can achieve accurate, interpretable anomaly detection in smart grids.

Abstract: Modern smart grids rely on dense measurement infrastructures, communication links, and intelligent field devices. Although this improves supervision and control, it also increases vulnerability to cyber-physical disruptions. Operators must distinguish physical incidents, such as faults or line disturbances, from malicious actions, such as false data injection or unauthorized command execution. This chapter investigates this problem using the well-known MSU/ORNL Power System Attack Dataset. The proposed method combines machine learning with genetic-algorithm-based feature selection. The objective is twofold: to classify attack and natural events accurately, and to determine whether a reduced set of physically informative PMU/IED measurements can support reliable detection. Several baseline models are evaluated, including logistic regression, RBF-SVM, XGBoost, Random Forest, and Extra Trees. The results show that tree-based ensemble models are the most effective for the considered dataset, with Extra Trees providing the strongest full-feature baseline. After feature selection, the GA + Extra Trees model reduces the clean PMU feature space from 112 attributes to an average of 27.4 attributes over five runs, while increasing macro-F1 from 0.9118 to 0.9212 and ROC-AUC from 0.9791 to 0.9837. These results indicate that many synchronized electrical measurements are redundant. A compact subset of phasor-based features can still provide accurate and interpretable anomaly detection in smart grids.

</details>


### [404] [Ternary Decision Trees with Locally-Adaptive Uncertainty Zones](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22740)
*William Smits*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A ternary decision tree with an uncertainty zone around each split improves decision accuracy by probabilistic routing; five local delta-estimation methods outperform standard CART across 72 OpenML-CC18 datasets, with the margin method often best and no extra hyperparameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard decision trees use hard binary thresholds and assign identical confidence to distant and boundary instances. Introducing a local uncertainty zone (delta) around the optimal split threshold enables weighted blending of child predictions for boundary cases and explicit boundary uncertainty signaling, without requiring external noise specifications.

Method: Augment each split with an uncertainty zone of half-width delta centered on the optimal threshold. Delta is estimated locally from statistics available during CART split finding. Five delta-estimation methods are proposed: quality-plateau, class-overlap, gain-ratio, node-bootstrap, and margin. Predictions in the zone are produced by weighted blending of the two child subtrees and flagged as boundary-uncertain. Evaluations use probabilistic routing. Extensive experiments include 72 OpenML-CC18 datasets with 5-fold CV, analyses on Breiman synthetic benchmarks, and real-world medical/financial datasets (e.g., mammography).

Result: All five delta-estimation methods with probabilistic routing significantly outperform standard CART on decided accuracy (Wilcoxon signed-rank, p < 0.001). The margin method achieves the best efficiency (0.104 accuracy gain per unit boundary-uncertain flagging rate), wins on 42 of 72 datasets, and requires zero additional hyperparameters. In Breiman benchmarks, margin is self-calibrating on clean data while node-bootstrap and quality-plateau track theoretical irreducible error. Practical gains are shown in medical/financial data: e.g., in mammography, node-bootstrap yields +0.71% decided accuracy with 10.8% boundary-uncertain cases.

Conclusion: Ternary decision trees with a locally computed uncertainty zone and boundary-uncertain signaling deliver practical accuracy gains and interpretable uncertainty. The margin-based delta estimation stands out as a robust default due to calibration and zero hyperparameters; boundary-uncertain flags can guide downstream handling of predictions.

Abstract: Decision trees partition the feature space using hard binary thresholds, assigning identical confidence to instances far from a decision boundary and to those directly on it. We introduce ternary decision trees, which augment each split node with an uncertainty zone of half-width delta centered on the optimal threshold. Instances in this zone receive predictions formed by weighted blending of both child subtrees and are flagged as boundary-uncertain, signaling that downstream applications may treat these predictions differently. Crucially, delta is computed locally at each node from statistics already available during standard CART split finding, requiring no external noise specification. We propose and evaluate five delta-estimation methods: quality-plateau (plateau width of the split criterion curve), class-overlap (empirical class-distribution overlap), gain-ratio (split quality relative to split entropy), node-bootstrap (threshold variance under node-level resampling), and margin (SVM-inspired distance to the nearest cross-class training example). Evaluated across 72 OpenML-CC18 datasets with 5-fold cross-validation, all five methods with probabilistic routing significantly outperform standard CART on decided accuracy (Wilcoxon signed-rank, p < 0.001). The margin method achieves the best efficiency (0.104 accuracy gain per unit of boundary-uncertain flagging rate), wins on 42 of 72 datasets, and requires zero additional hyperparameters. Analysis on three Breiman synthetic benchmarks reveals that margin is self-calibrating on clean data while node-bootstrap and quality-plateau best track theoretical irreducible error.
