Experiments on four medical and financial datasets demonstrate practical value: on mammography, node-bootstrap achieves +0.71% decided accuracy by flagging 10.8% of screening cases as boundary-uncertain.

</details>


### [405] [CogAdapt: Transferring Clinical ECG Foundation Models to Wearable Cognitive Load Assessment via Lead Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22774)
*Amir Mousavi,Mohammad Sadegh Sirjani,Erfan Nourbakhsh,Mimi Xie,Rocky Slavin,Leslie Neely,John Davis,John Quarles*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CogAdapt adapts clinical ECG foundation models to wearable cognitive load assessment using LeadBridge (3-lead to 12-lead transformation) and ProFine (progressive fine-tuning) to achieve subject-independent performance, with macro-F1 of 0.626 (CLARE) and 0.768 (CL-Drive) under LOSO, outperforming training-from-scratch baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current cognitive load assessment from ECG is hampered by limited labeled data and poor cross-subject generalization. Clinical ECG foundation models offer rich representations but misalign with wearable sensor configurations and tasks. A domain-adaptive approach could leverage pre-trained models for wearable cognitive load estimation.

Method: LeadBridge: a learnable adapter that maps 3-lead wearable ECG to anatomically consistent 12-lead representations. ProFine: progressive fine-tuning that gradually unfreezes encoder layers to minimize catastrophic forgetting. Evaluation uses two public datasets (CLARE, CL-Drive) with leave-one-subject-out cross-validation, comparing to baselines trained from scratch.

Result: CogAdapt substantially outperforms baselines trained from scratch, achieving macro-F1 scores of 0.626 on CLARE and 0.768 on CL-Drive, demonstrating improved subject-independent cognitive load assessment from wearables.

Conclusion: Foundation-model adaptation for ECG enables effective transfer from clinical to wearable sensing for cognitive load estimation, showing promise for real-time, subject-generalizable HCI applications.

Abstract: Real-time cognitive load assessment is essential for adaptive human-computer interaction but remains challenging due to limited labeled data and poor cross-subject generalization. Recent ECG foundation models pre-trained on millions of clinical recordings offer rich representations, but cannot be directly applied to wearable devices due to sensor configuration mismatch and task differences. In this paper, we propose CogAdapt, a framework that adapts clinical ECG foundation models to wearable cognitive load assessment. CogAdapt introduces LeadBridge, a learnable adapter that transforms 3-lead wearable signals into anatomically consistent 12-lead representations, and ProFine, a progressive fine-tuning strategy that gradually unfreezes encoder layers while preventing catastrophic forgetting. Evaluations on two public datasets (CLARE and CL-Drive) under leave-one-subject-out cross-validation show that CogAdapt substantially outperforms baselines trained from scratch, achieving macro-F1 scores of 0.626 and 0.768. These results demonstrate the promise of foundation model adaptation for subject-independent cognitive load assessment from wearable sensors.

</details>


### [406] [SeqLoRA: Bilevel Orthogonal Adaptation for Continual Multi-Concept Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22743)
*Javad Parsa,Enis Simsar,Amir Joudaki,Thomas Hofmann,André M. H. Teixeira*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SeqLoRA is a bilevel-optimized, sequentially regularized LoRA method for continual adaptation of diffusion models, enabling multi-concept compositionality without costly fusion and with theoretical guarantees on convergence and forgetting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In text-to-image diffusion models, composing multiple user concepts is hampered by representation interference. Existing modular methods either require expensive post-hoc fusion or freeze adaptation subspaces, limiting expressiveness and fidelity. A method that jointly learns adaptable components across concepts while controlling interference is needed.

Method: SeqLoRA jointly optimizes both LoRA factors via bilevel optimization within a constrained continual learning framework, enabling sequential updates. The authors model residual layer activations as a matrix sub-Gaussian process to derive high-probability bounds on forgetting, and prove that learning the LoRA basis from data reduces residual interference energy more effectively than frozen-basis methods. They provide convergence guarantees for the algorithm.

Result: Empirical results show improved identity preservation and scalability across up to 101 concepts, while avoiding post-hoc fusion and reducing attribute interference in composed generations.

Conclusion: SeqLoRA offers a scalable, theoretically grounded solution for sequential concept adaptation in diffusion models, achieving high-fidelity concept composition without expensive fusion and with provable control over catastrophic forgetting.

Abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning enables fast personalization of text-to-image diffusion models, but composing multiple custom concepts remains challenging due to representation interference. Existing modular methods either rely on expensive post-hoc fusion or freeze adaptation subspaces, which limit expressiveness and concept fidelity. To address this trade-off, we propose Sequential regularized LoRA (SeqLoRA), a constrained continual learning framework that jointly optimizes both LoRA factors via bilevel optimization. Theoretically, we establish strong convergence guarantees for our algorithm and model the residual layer activations as a matrix sub-Gaussian process to derive high-probability bounds on catastrophic forgetting. We further prove that learning the LoRA basis from data minimizes residual interference energy more effectively than frozen-basis methods. Experiments on multi-concept image generation demonstrate that SeqLoRA improves identity preservation and scalability across up to 101 concepts, while avoiding costly fusion and reducing attribute interference in composed generations.

</details>


### [407] [MambaGaze: Bidirectional Mamba with Explicit Missing Data Modeling for Cognitive Load Assessment from Eye-Gaze Tracking Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22775)
*Amir Mousavi,Mohammad Sadegh Sirjani,Erfan Nourbakhsh,Mimi Xie,Rocky Slavin,Leslie Neely,John Davis,John Quarles*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MambaGaze enables real-time cognitive load estimation from eye-tracking signals using XMD encoding and bidirectional Mamba-2, achieving real-time performance on edge devices and outperforming standard baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address frequent data missingness from blinks/tracking failures and capture long-range temporal dependencies in eye-tracking-based cognitive load assessment for safety-critical tasks.

Method: 1) XMD encoding: augment features with observation masks and time-deltas to model uncertainty; 2) bidirectional Mamba-2: temporal modeling with linear complexity.

Result: LOSO evaluation on CLARE and CL-Drive yields 76.8% and 73.1% accuracy; outperforms CNN, Transformer, ResNet, VGG by 4–12pp; Jetson edge benchmarks: 43–68 FPS and <7.5W.

Conclusion: MambaGaze demonstrates feasible real-time wearable cognitive load monitoring with improved accuracy and efficiency, validating the proposed encoding and model design.

Abstract: Real-time cognitive load assessment from eye-tracking signals could potentially enable adaptive human-centered-AI such as safety-critical applications such as driver vigilance monitoring or automated flight deck assistance, yet two challenges persist: handling frequent data missingness from blinks and tracking failures, and efficiently modeling long-range temporal dependencies. We propose MambaGaze, a framework that addresses these challenges through 1) XMD encoding, which augments raw features with observation masks and time-deltas to explicitly model data uncertainty, and 2) bidirectional Mamba-2, which captures temporal dependencies with linear computational complexity. Experiments on CLARE and CL-Drive datasets under leave-one-subject-out evaluation show that MambaGaze achieves 76.8% and 73.1% accuracy, respectively, outperforming CNN, Transformer, ResNet, and VGG baselines by 4-12 percentage points. Edge deployment benchmarks on NVIDIA Jetson platforms demonstrate real-time inference at 43-68 FPS with power consumption below 7.5W, confirming feasibility for wearable cognitive load monitoring.

</details>


### [408] [Plug-in Losses for Evidential Deep Learning: A Simplified Framework for Uncertainty Estimation that Includes the Softmax Classifier](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22746)
*Berk Hayta,Hannah Laus,Simon Mittermaier,Felix Krahmer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a plug-in loss for Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) to approximate Dirichlet-based uncertainty without complex Dirichlet objectives; proves the approximation error decays with more evidence across common losses; shows softmax is compatible under the evidence-to-Dirichlet mapping; validates on Google Speech Commands with performance on par with classical EDL but with simpler training; first to report coverage-accuracy trade-offs for speech tasks using EDL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty estimation in real-world sensor-based systems needs reliable yet computationally efficient methods. EDL offers single-pass uncertainty via Dirichlet probabilities but involves computationally heavy Dirichlet objectives, hindering analysis and deployment. A simpler, well-behaved surrogate could retain performance while easing implementation.

Method: Approximate the first-order empirical risk minimization objective of EDL by evaluating a plug-in loss at the Dirichlet mean. Prove that the approximation error decays under mild conditions for a broad class of losses (e.g., mean-squared error, cross-entropy). Show that the evidence-to-Dirichlet mapping can recover softmax under a special case. Implement with standard deep learning losses and pipelines.

Result: Empirical validation on Google Speech Commands shows predictive accuracy and selective prediction (coverage-accuracy) comparable to classical EDL while using simpler losses and training setups. Demonstrates the feasibility of efficient uncertainty estimation for speech recognition tasks using EDL.

Conclusion: The work justifies using plug-in Dirichlet-mean losses as a practical surrogate for EDL, including recovering softmax behavior under a particular mapping. It enables simpler, computationally efficient uncertainty estimation with comparable performance and opens avenues for broader empirical analysis like coverage-accuracy trades in speech tasks.

Abstract: Real-world sensor-based learning systems require uncertainty estimation that is both reliable and computationally efficient. Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) provides single-pass uncertainty estimation by modeling the class probabilities via Dirichlet distributions, where the Dirichlet parameters are predicted by a learned neural network mapping. However, this approach can lead to computational challenges, as Dirichlet expected objectives are more complex than standard supervised learning losses, complicating their analysis and implementation. We address this issue by approximating the objective of the first-order empirical risk minimization problem induced by EDL with a plug-in loss evaluated at the Dirichlet mean and show that, under mild assumptions, the approximation error decays with growing evidence for a broad class of loss functions, including mean-squared error and cross-entropy loss. As a special case, our analysis provides justification for the use of softmax in the context of uncertainty estimation, since under a particular evidence-to-Dirichlet mapping, our framework includes the standard softmax classifier. We validate the proposed simplified objectives on the Google Speech Commands dataset and show that they achieve predictive accuracy and selective prediction performance comparable to classical EDL, while being simpler to implement using standard deep learning losses and training pipelines. To the best of our knowledge, this empirical analysis is the first to obtain coverage-accuracy trade-offs for speech recognition tasks through EDL.

</details>


### [409] [SDPM: Survival Diffusion Probabilistic Model for Continuous-Time Survival Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22776)
*Stanislav R. Kirpichenko,Andrei V. Konstantinov,Lev V. Utkin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SDPM is a diffusion-based, continuous-time survival model that directly models P(T, δ | x) without discretizing time or assuming hazard structure; it uses a transformed target space and Kaplan-Meier post-processing to estimate survival functions, achieving competitive performance on real datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing survival methods either impose parametric/hazard structure or discretize time, which limits flexibility and incurs approximation errors. A flexible, nonparametric, continuous-time approach is needed.

Method: A conditional denoising diffusion model for P(T, δ | x) called Survival Diffusion Probabilistic Model (SDPM). It operates in a transformed target space with standardized log-times and a continuous Gaussian-mixture representation of δ. Conditional samples are mapped to survival estimates via the Kaplan-Meier estimator. No discretization or parametric event-time assumptions are required. The model is evaluated on 10 real datasets against five baselines; ablation studies validate the target-space transformations; code is publicly available.

Result: SDPM achieves competitive performance across C-index, integrated time-dependent AUC, and integrated Brier score. On synthetic Cox-Weibull data, SDPM better recovers the underlying continuous distribution than a strong nonparametric baseline when many samples are generated. Ablation confirms the benefits of the target-space transformations for calibration and valid-time generation. Public code is provided.

Conclusion: SDPM offers a flexible, discretization-free, nonparametric framework for continuous-time survival analysis, leveraging diffusion modeling and a transformation-based approach to enable accurate and well-calibrated survival predictions; it broadens the toolkit beyond traditional parametric and discretized methods.

Abstract: Survival analysis aims to estimate a time-to-event distribution from data with censored observations. Many existing methods either impose structural assumptions on the hazard function or discretize the time axis, which may limit flexibility and introduce approximation errors. We propose the Survival Diffusion Probabilistic Model (SDPM), a generative approach to continuous-time survival analysis. SDPM models the conditional distribution of the survival outcome, represented by the pair of observed time and censoring indicator, $\mathbb{P}(T,δ\mid \mathbf{x})$, using a denoising diffusion model. Under the assumption of conditionally independent censoring, conditional samples generated by the model can be transformed into survival function estimates using the Kaplan-Meier estimator. This formulation avoids parametric assumptions on the event-time distribution and does not require a discretization of the output time space. The model operates in a transformed target space, using standardized log-times and a continuous Gaussian-mixture representation of the censoring indicator. We evaluate SDPM on ten real survival datasets and compare it with five strong baselines, including tree-based, boosting-based, and neural survival models. Results show that SDPM achieves competitive predictive performance across C-index, integrated time-dependent AUC, and integrated Brier score. A study on synthetic Cox-Weibull data demonstrates that SDPM can recover the shape of an underlying continuous survival distribution more accurately than a strong nonparametric baseline when sufficiently many samples are generated. An ablation study confirms the importance of the proposed target-space transformations, which improve event-rate calibration, reduce invalid generated times, and provide consistent gains in predictive discrimination. Codes implementing the proposed model are publicly available.

</details>


### [410] [The Matching Principle: A Geometric Theory of Loss Functions for Nuisance-Robust Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22800)
*Vishal Rajput*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified statistical framework: deployment nuisance covariance estimation and matching regularizers unify robustness, domain adaptation, invariance, and compositional generalisation; many existing methods are estimators of the same object.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To connect disparate robustness methods under a single principled objective and derive falsifiable theory.

Method: Linear-Gaussian analysis yielding closed-form optima, range-coverage necessity for Jacobian penalties, deep-minima range-dichotomy; trajectory deviation index (TDI) as a label-free probe; thirteen pre-registered blocks across ML tasks testing the predicted geometry/order; 7B-scale experiments to assess Style-PMH behavior.

Result: Interprets CORAL, adversarial training, IRM, augmentation, metric learning, Jacobian penalties, and alignment constraints as estimators of the deployment-nuisance covariance; 12/13 blocks conform to the predicted matched→isotropic→wrong-W order; Office-31 fails due to an eigengap issue; at 7B scale, matched style-PMH improves selective honesty and preserves Style TDI even when standard DPO degrades it.

Conclusion: The deployment nuisance covariance is the central object; the regulariser must target its range; the paper provides a falsifiable theory with closed-form results in the linear-Gaussian limit rather than universal performance claims.

Abstract: Robustness, domain adaptation, photometric and occlusion invariance, compositional generalisation, temporal robustness, alignment safety, and classical anisotropic regularisation are usually treated as separate problems with separate method families. This paper argues that much of their shared structure is one statistical problem: estimate the covariance of label-preserving deployment nuisance, then regularise the encoder Jacobian along a matrix whose range covers that covariance (the matching principle). CORAL, adversarial training, IRM, augmentation, metric learning, Jacobian penalties, and alignment-style constraints are different estimators of that object, not independent robustness tricks.
  In the linear-Gaussian model we prove closed-form optimality (Theorem A), including cube-root water-filling within the matched range; necessity of range coverage for quadratic Jacobian penalties (Theorem G); the same range dichotomy at deep global minima; and two falsification controls (Lemma C; Corollaries E), with seven conditional consistency lemmas (D1-D7) for estimation under standard identifiability assumptions.
  We introduce the Trajectory Deviation Index (TDI), a label-free probe of embedding sensitivity when task accuracy or Jacobian Frobenius norm is insufficient.
  Thirteen pre-registered blocks from classical ML through Qwen2.5-7B test the predicted matched, then isotropic, then wrong-W ordering on geometry and deployment drift; twelve pass, and the sole exception (Office-31) is an eigengap failure named before the run. At 7B scale, matched style-PMH improves selective honesty and preserves Style TDI where standard DPO degrades it.
  The contribution is naming the deployment nuisance covariance, stating what the regulariser must do, and supplying a closed-form falsifiable theory once that object is identified, not universality on every leaderboard.

</details>


### [411] [Lumberjack: Better Differentially Private Random Forests through Heavy Hitter Detection in Trees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22756)
*Christian Janos Lebeda,David Erb,Tudor Cebere,Aurélien Bellet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Lumberjack is a DP random forest that uses deep decision trees with privacy-preserving pruning and a new DP heavy-hitter detector, delivering state-of-the-art utility under differential privacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Differential privacy often degrades learning utility on sensitive tabular data; enabling deeper, more expressive trees under DP via careful pruning and a hierarchical heavy-hitter detector can improve performance.

Method: Construct large random trees, apply aggressive privacy-preserving pruning to retain sufficiently populated nodes; introduce an ε,δ-DP heavy-hitter detection algorithm for hierarchical data with error O_{ε,δ}(√log h), enabling deeper trees without excessive privacy loss; empirically evaluate on benchmark datasets.

Result:  Lumberjack consistently outperforms prior DP random forest methods, achieving a new state-of-the-art and substantially improving the privacy-utility trade-off at practical privacy budgets.

Conclusion: Carefully designed DP random forests can substantially close the utility gap under DP; this direction shows promise for future research and broader applicability.

Abstract: Random forests are widely used in fields involving sensitive tabular data, but existing approaches to enforcing differential privacy (DP) typically degrade performance to the point of impracticality. In this paper, we introduce Lumberjack, a differentially private random forest algorithm that achieves substantially higher utility by constructing large random decision trees and then applying aggressive, privacy-preserving pruning to retain only sufficiently populated nodes. A key component of our approach is a novel $(\varepsilon,δ)$-DP heavy hitter detection algorithm for hierarchical data, whose error is $O_{\varepsilon,δ}(\sqrt{\log h})$ for trees of height $h$ and may be of independent interest. This favorable scaling enables the use of significantly deeper trees than in prior work, leading to improved expressiveness under privacy constraints. Our empirical evaluation on benchmark datasets shows that Lumberjack consistently outperforms prior DP random forest methods, establishing a new state of the art. In particular, our approach yields substantial improvements in the privacy-utility trade-off for practical privacy budgets. Our findings suggest that carefully designed DP random forests can close much of the utility gap, highlighting a promising and underexplored direction for future research.

</details>


### [412] [Vector Policy Optimization: Training for Diversity Improves Test-Time Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22817)
*Ryan Bahlous-Boldi,Isha Puri,Idan Shenfeld,Akarsh Kumar,Mehul Damani,Sebastian Risi,Omar Khattab,Zhang-Wei Hong,Pulkit Agrawal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Vector Policy Optimization (VPO), an RL method that trains LLMs to output diverse solutions by optimizing over vector-valued rewards, improving performance in inference-time search and enabling tasks unreachable by scalar-RL baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scalar reward post-training often yields low-entropy, non-diverse outputs, hindering performance in inference-time searches that use multiple, possibly conflicting reward signals. In practice, rewards are frequently vector-valued (e.g., per-test-case accuracy, multiple personas).

Method: VPO replaces the GRPO advantage estimator with a vector-valued objective, training the model to produce a set of solutions that specialize to different trade-offs across the vector reward space. It treats downstream rewards as a vector and optimizes for diversity in the solution set, serving as a drop-in replacement for GRPO. Evaluation across four tasks shows improvements in test-time search metrics (pass@k, best@k) and enables problems solvable by evolutionary search that GRPO cannot.

Result: VPO matches or surpasses the strongest scalar RL baselines on test-time search; gains become more pronounced with larger search budgets; in evolutionary search, VPO unlocks problems GRPO cannot solve.

Conclusion: Diversity-aware post-training objectives may become the default as test-time search becomes more standardized, with VPO offering a practical approach to produce diverse, high-performing solutions.

Abstract: Language models must now generalize out of the box to novel environments and work inside inference-scaling search procedures, such as AlphaEvolve, that select rollouts with a variety of task-specific reward functions. Unfortunately, the standard paradigm of LLM post-training optimizes a pre-specified scalar reward, often leading current LLMs to produce low-entropy response distributions and thus to struggle at displaying the diversity that inference-time search will require. We propose Vector Policy Optimization (VPO), an RL algorithm that explicitly trains policies to anticipate diverse downstream reward functions and to produce diverse solutions. VPO exploits that rewards are often vector-valued in practice, like per-test-case correctness in code generation or, say, multiple different user personas or reward models. VPO is essentially a drop-in replacement for the GRPO advantage estimator, but it trains the LLM to output a set of solutions where individual solutions specialize to different trade-offs in the vector reward space. Across four tasks, VPO matches or beats the strongest scalar RL baselines on test-time search (e.g. pass@k and best@k), with the gap widening as the search budget grows. For evolutionary search, VPO models unlock problems that GRPO models cannot solve at all. As test-time search becomes more standardized, optimizing for diversity may need to become the default post-training objective.

</details>


### [413] [Uniform Diffusion Models Revisited: Leave-One-Out Denoiser and Absorbing State Reformulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22765)
*Samson Gourevitch,Yazid Janati,Dario Shariatian,Umut Simsekli,Eric Moulines,Eric P. Xing,Alain Durmus*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UDM underperforms MDM due to mismatch between plug-in ELBO and cross-entropy denoising; leave-one-out posterior and absorbing-state reformulations align training and inference, improving generation without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify why Uniform Diffusion Models (UDM) diverge from Masked Diffusion Models (MDM) and identify the correct target for the reverse process to unify parameterization and training objectives.

Method: Theoretically relate denoiser, leave-one-out posterior, and score; derive exact conversions; introduce an absorbing-state reformulation of UDM; implement an informed predictor-corrector sampler and temperature sampling; evaluate on language modeling with leave-one-out parameterizations.

Result: Leave-one-out posterior is the effective plug-in target; exact conversions between denoiser, leave-one-out posterior, and score derived; inference improved without retraining via predictor-corrector and better temperature sampling; absorbing-state decomposition preserves UDM joint law with simpler denoisers; language modeling shows leave-one-out helps UDM, absorbing construction matches/surpasses MDM.

Conclusion: The empirical gap between MDM and UDM stems from parameterization and sampling design rather than marginals; adopting leave-one-out targets and absorbing-state reformulations aligns objectives, yielding practical improvements and closing the gap.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion models are often trained through clean-data prediction, but the prediction can be used in different ways to define the reverse dynamics. In Masked Diffusion Models (MDM) these choices largely coincide, whereas in Uniform Diffusion Models (UDM) they do not. We show that the standard plug-in bridge parameterization for UDM is not optimized by the denoising posterior, but by a leave-one-out posterior that predicts each clean token without using its own noisy observation. This identifies a mismatch between the plug-in ELBO and the usual cross-entropy denoising objective. We characterize the leave-one-out target and derive exact conversions between the denoiser, the leave-one-out posterior, and the score. These conversions allow us to disentangle parameterization and training objective. Our results also lead to inference improvements without any additional training through an informed predictor-corrector sampler and improved temperature sampling based on the leave-one-out predictor.
  We further introduce an absorbing-state reformulation of uniform diffusion that preserves the UDM joint law while decomposing it into masked-diffusion-like sampling operations, with simpler denoising posteriors, carry-over unmasking, and a natural remasking mechanism. On language modeling, leave-one-out parameterizations consistently improve UDM generation, while the absorbing construction matches or surpasses masked diffusion. These results suggest that the empirical gap between masked and uniform diffusion is driven less by the choice of marginals themselves than by parameterization and sampling design. The code and models can be found at https://github.com/samsongourevitch/rev_udm.

</details>


### [414] [Remember to be Curious: Episodic Context and Persistent Worlds for 3D Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22814)
*Lily Goli,Justin Kerr,Daniele Reda,Alec Jacobson,Andrea Tagliasacchi,Angjoo Kanazawa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A curiosity-driven RL approach for photorealistic 3D exploration uses a persistent online 3D world model and an episodic RGB sequence policy, enabling effective exploration, zero-shot generalization, and transfer to downstream tasks, trained purely on intrinsic rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse rewards and long-horizon exploration in photorealistic 3D environments; existing curiosity methods fail due to lack of spatial persistence and episodic context; requires a persistent world model plus episodic trajectory history to drive exploration and navigation.

Method: Combine online 3D reconstruction as a persistent world model with a policy parameterized as a sequence model over RGB observations to retain episodic context; train end-to-end using intrinsic rewards (curiosity); deployment uses RGB frames only; trained on HM3D.

Result: Agent outperforms RL-based active mapping baselines on HM3D; generalizes zero-shot to Gibson and AI-generated worlds; end-to-end policy enables efficient adaptation to downstream tasks (e.g., apple picking, image-goal navigation); outperforms from-scratch baselines.

Conclusion: Persistent world modeling paired with episodic context mitigates exploration failures in complex 3D environments, enabling robust curiosity-driven exploration and transfer to downstream tasks with RGB-only deployment.

Abstract: Exploration is a prerequisite for learning useful behaviors in sparse-reward, long-horizon tasks, particularly within 3D environments. Curiosity-driven reinforcement learning addresses this via intrinsic rewards derived from the mismatch between the agent's predictive model of the world and reality. However, translating this intrinsic motivation to complex, photorealistic environments remains difficult, as agents can become trapped in local loops and receive fresh rewards for revisiting forgotten states. In this work, we demonstrate that this failure stems from a lack of spatial persistence and episodic context. We show that effective curiosity requires a model of the world that is persistent and continuously updated, paired with an agent that maintains an episodic trajectory history to navigate toward novel regions. We achieve this using an online 3D reconstruction as a persistent model of the world, while the agent policy is parameterized as a sequence model over RGB observations to maintain episodic context. This design enables effective exploration during training while allowing the agent to navigate using solely RGB frames at deployment. Trained purely via curiosity on HM3D, our agent outperforms RL-based active mapping baselines and generalizes zero-shot to Gibson and AI-generated worlds. Our end-to-end policy enables efficient adaptation to downstream tasks, such as apple picking and image-goal navigation, outperforming from-scratch baselines. Please see video results at https://recuriosity.github.io/.

</details>


### [415] [Integrable Elasticity via Neural Demand Potentials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22820)
*Carlos Heredia,Daniel Roncel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ICDN is a demand-first neural model for multiproduct retail demand that learns log-demand as a context-conditioned smooth function of log-prices, enabling exact elasticity extraction from the learned surface; on Dominick's beer data it outperforms a directed log–log baseline and yields more stable, plausible cross-price elasticities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve multiproduct demand estimation by integrating a neural demand surface from which elasticities can be derived analytically, enabling better generalization and more stable cross-price effects.

Method: Introduce Integrable Context-Dependent Demand Network (ICDN), modeling log-demand as a smooth function of log-prices conditioned on context; elasticity computed by differentiating the learned surface; empirical evaluation on Dominick's beer dataset; comparison against a directed log-log benchmark.

Result: Better out-of-sample generalization than the directed log-log baseline; elasticity estimates are more stable and economically plausible, particularly for weakly identified cross-price effects.

Conclusion: ICDN demonstrates a viable demand-first neural framework for multi-product demand with exact elasticity extraction, improving generalization and elasticity stability on a real dataset; suggests value for practical demand estimation and policy analysis.

Abstract: We propose the Integrable Context-Dependent Demand Network (ICDN), a demand-first neural model for multiproduct retail demand. The model learns log-demand as a smooth, context-conditioned function of log-prices, allowing elasticities to be derived exactly from the learned demand surface. On the Dominick's beer dataset, ICDN improves out-of-sample generalization over a directed log-log benchmark and yields more stable, economically plausible elasticity estimates, especially for weakly identified cross-price effects.

</details>
